Dialogue:
JUDGE: The defendant has filed a countersuit for $5,000 for pain and suffering and two air conditioning units. All parties, please raise your right hands. Welcome back to The People’s Court. Next case on the docket: the plaintiff says the defendant tried to break his lease, but he is no chump. The defendant says he left because there was mold. It’s the case of 'You Break, You Pay.' Thank you, Doulas.

COURT OFFICER: You’re welcome.

JUDGE: Okay. Phil Qunel, you are suing Delton Carney and Mary Potenberg. You have power of attorney for Mary?

PLAINTIFF: Yes.

JUDGE: Okay. For $2,792.18 that you say you are owed in damages and rent. You have a counterclaim against him for $5,000 for pain and suffering and some AC units. Let me hear from you first.

PLAINTIFF: They rented my place out. She told me that she didn’t like rugs. I said, 'I’ll tell you what, if you want to extend the lease, I’d be happy to rip out the rugs.' I was texting her back and forth trying to, like, 'Hey, get a hold of me. I’m reaching out to you again.' And I never got correspondence. Like, 'Hey, yeah, let’s do this.' I kept calling her or texting her, and then that... so it just fizzles.

JUDGE: All right. And then what happens?

PLAINTIFF: Then I get a call, um, January-February time frame that, uh, 'We’re moving out.' And, uh, I said, 'Well, I’m willing to do that if I could rent it out.' And I was not able to rent it out when they left in March.

JUDGE: They left when?

PLAINTIFF: What day in March? They left, uh, March 31st.

JUDGE: And you did not pay? They did not pay March rent?

PLAINTIFF: They said you could use the security because we’re not going to get it back anyways.

JUDGE: Is that accurate? You didn’t pay March rent?

DEFENDANT: That’s accurate.

JUDGE: All right. That’s not what security deposit is for.

DEFENDANT: I know.

JUDGE: Well, there’s some risks involved when you did. So you’re suing now for late fees for March because you feel that you should have been paid March and then the issue of the security deposit would have been litigated, you know, at some other point if you could show damages, correct?

PLAINTIFF: Correct.

JUDGE: Right. Um, when did you re-rent it?

PLAINTIFF: I re-rented it the middle of April.

JUDGE: So your damage in terms of rent would be the two weeks in April that you weren’t getting rent, correct?

PLAINTIFF: Correct.

JUDGE: All right. Now, according to you, you didn’t even break the lease because the place was uninhabitable. Explain that to me.

DEFENDANT: Um, in March—March 1st—we told him that we were moving because my daughter got—has allergies. She had to go for surgery and all this.

JUDGE: Okay, hold on. Slow down. Your daughter has what kind of allergies?

DEFENDANT: Uh, well, she doesn’t have allergies, but she got sick from the mold from the excessive heat in the apartment.

JUDGE: I need some medical proof of what you’re saying.

DEFENDANT: Okay. I have that.

JUDGE: Because there was extensive heat in the apartment?

DEFENDANT: Extensive heat in the apartment. The apartment was over 100° all the time.

JUDGE: Why would you live there if it was over 100°?

DEFENDANT: This is what I’m saying. The thermostat in the apartment was broken.

JUDGE: Right.

DEFENDANT: I have pictures of that thermostat.

JUDGE: Right. Okay. You have some texts where you’re talking to them about the thermostat?

DEFENDANT: Right.

JUDGE: The thermostat is a $30, you know, it’s nothing. It’s solved in an instant. Why would you allow your child to get sick over something that—and I saw the text where he’s—is he texting? Are you texting him or her?

DEFENDANT: Her.

JUDGE: Let me see the copy of texts. I’m going to show it to you where he’s saying, 'Do you need me to change a thermostat?' Hello? Hello? I mean, if you have other texts you want me to see, show it to me. But it’s like, 'Hi Phil, is there a way to control the heat in here? It is way hot in here. Very hard to breathe even.' This is in October. He says, 'Sorry about that. There should be a control knob in the living room where the baseboard register is. Let me know if you can’t control the heat. We will get it fixed.'

DEFENDANT: Right.

JUDGE: So, a week later, 'Hey Marky, does the thermostat work or do we need to get it fixed? Also, I would like to discuss the flooring with you as in replacing the carpet or putting down something new, so when you get a chance, give me a call. We can talk about it.' No response. October 26, 'Hey, reaching out to you again. Give me a call when you can.' No response.

JUDGE: However, you have to prove that the condition was severe enough to render the apartment uninhabitable. A broken thermostat is not enough. You had a responsibility to get it fixed when the plaintiff reached out to you multiple times. Now, let’s address the mold issue.

DEFENDANT: Yes, Your Honor. I have medical records showing my daughter’s respiratory issues and a letter from her doctor stating that prolonged exposure to mold could have worsened her condition.

JUDGE: All right. Let me see those.

(Defendant hands documents to the judge.)

JUDGE: Hmm. The doctor’s note is very general. It does not say that the mold in your apartment specifically caused her illness, only that mold exposure can aggravate respiratory conditions. Do you have any inspection reports or evidence that the mold was severe and pervasive?

DEFENDANT: No, Your Honor. I didn’t get an official inspection. But I do have pictures of the mold we found behind the air conditioning units.

JUDGE: (Examines pictures) These show some discoloration, but I don’t see anything that would rise to the level of making the apartment uninhabitable. Did you report this to the landlord at any time before deciding to leave?

DEFENDANT: We mentioned it when we told him we were leaving.

JUDGE: That’s not how it works. If you had an issue, you needed to give the landlord a chance to address it before breaking the lease. Since you didn’t, you are responsible for the unpaid rent and damages.

DEFENDANT: But we were suffering in that place!

JUDGE: I understand, but legally, the burden of proof is on you. Based on the lease terms and lack of sufficient evidence for uninhabitability, I am ruling in favor of the plaintiff for the unpaid rent. However, I will not award the full amount requested for damages since the plaintiff was able to re-rent the unit fairly quickly.

PLAINTIFF: Thank you, Your Honor.

JUDGE: Judgment for the plaintiff in the amount of $2,078. Case dismissed.

Lawyer of Plaintiff:
I think, with respect, Your Honor, it -- it would be a very small step if you wrote your opinion and just affirm the Sixth Circuit on the ground of decision there.

The --the only appellate opinion I am aware of that disagrees on the basic intertwinement issue is Judge Posner's opinion in Pavey.

And, you know, so, with respect, we -- we don't think it would be a sea change in the law to rule on the -- the narrower question presented here.

Judge:
I think one concern is that even if we rule that narrowly, it still has a big effect on PLRA litigation, in other words, that it's easy enough for any prisoner to essentially evade the exhaustion requirement by pleading his claims in the right way and ensuring that the case immediately goes to a jury.

So what do you think about that?

Lawyer of Plaintiff:
Sure.

I mean, to the extent there --there are concerns about a roadmap, it already exists, right? This trial -- I mean, this case got through Rule 56.

There was going to be a trial. So I'm not sure that the incentives are any greater from a -- a roadmap perspective. I mean, also, this Court doesn't usually fashion constitutional rules assuming that litigants are going to perjure themselves or fabricate evidence to get past Rule 56, so I don't think it should do so here.

And the other point I would just make is that this has been the rule in the Second Circuit and the First Circuit more recently for more than a decade.

There hasn't been a flood of litigation.

In fact, the data show that the cases filed and the trials have gone down in those districts.

I found one case that's actually going to trial in the Southern District of New York on exhaustion, an intertwined case.

This --this has not been a --a big problem in the last, you know, 30 years that we've had the PLRA.

Judge:
Suppose you have a prisoner who's serving a lengthy prison sentence and files a -- files a grievance, and the State says: Well, you didn't exhaust.

So the prisoner says: Well, yeah, I did exhaust.

I put the -- I put the grievance in the box or I handed it to a guard.

So, at a minimum, he gets a -- he gets a trip to the courthouse.

He gets a trip out of the prison.

Lawyer of Plaintiff:
Well, in our client's case, everything was --

Judge:
Well, I'm not talking --

Lawyer of Plaintiff:
-- over Zoom, so --

Judge:
-- about your client. I'm talking about -- about other -- other --

Lawyer of Plaintiff:
Understood.

Judge:
-- prisoners who may want to take advantage of this.

Lawyer of Plaintiff:
I mean, I -- I don't think that's a --

Judge:
So then there's a genuine dispute of --

Lawyer of Plaintiff:
Right.

Judge:
-- material fact about whether he -- you know, is he telling the truth? Is he not telling the truth?

Lawyer of Plaintiff:
I mean --

Judge:
Is he really going to fear that on top of everything else that's happened they're going to bring a perjury prosecution against him?

Lawyer of Plaintiff:
I -- I think this is not a new problem, right, and that the rule that the Sixth Circuit adopted isn't really relevant to whether a case is going to pass, you know, screening and -- and all of these other hurdles and get past summary judgment.

We're talking about the very few cases that --that get there.

And district courts are well-equipped to decide whether there's a genuine issue of material fact even in prisoner litigation.

Judge:
Thank you, counsel.

Justice Thomas, anything further? Anything further? No? Thank you, counsel. Rebuttal, Ms. Sherman?

Lawyer of Defendant:
Thank you, Your Honor.

All claims, including First Amendment claims here, stem from the sexual harassment claim.

And the PREA grievance policy for the MDOC includes First Amendment retaliation. That's in Joint Appendix 72, 73.

Mr. Richards, contrary to my friend's argument, can reargue facts if he exhausts and gets in front of a jury again, and that is crucial here to preserving the Seventh Amendment even if this Court believes that the Seventh Amendment is somehow otherwise implicated.

Addressing Justice Barrett's questions about discretion, Wetmore does -- the --the key in Wetmore is the discretion that is given to the district court judge.

Here, that's discretion that, you know, based on historical considerations and based on the --especially the functional considerations here, the fact that there is no precise analog, based on the functional considerations and the goals of the PLRA, that discretion should -- in -- in every case, it would have to -- there would be no discretion, whereas that is what drove the jurisdictional decisions in Wetmore and other cases.

Judge:
You asked about a roadmap.

It would be very easy for prisoners to create disputes of fact that turned on credibility, a he said/she said where those cases would have to go to a jury.

And I think, even if there aren't floodgates open now, they will be open under Richards's proposed rule because it is not hard for a prisoner to do that.

As this Court said in Woodford versus Ngo, not all -- sometimes prisoners file claims in bad faith, and if they want to make trouble for a corrections officer they don't like or they want to get out of their cell and have a respite -- these are the courts' words, not mine -- they -- they will file in bad faith, and you are incentivizing them to add facts that will get them to a jury.

On a final point, it is the nature of exhaustion as a prerequisite that leaves the Seventh Amendment intact here because the Seventh Amendment doesn't guarantee that claims get to a jury, they --it applies once the claims get to a jury.

And, here, once the claims are getting to a jury, because exhaustion has been met, as Congress intended, Mr. Richards and other prisoners will have their day in court.

Judge:
Thank you, counsel.

The case is submitted.

Judge: All parties, please raise your right hand. You see the controllers have been sworn. Thank you, Douglas.

Judge: Okay, Thelma and Leticia, you are suing Stephen Schaffner for $2,800 that he won’t return to you for a vehicle that you bought from him, which you say is junk. Tell me what happened.

Plaintiff (Leticia): Okay, first, I just want to say I love, love, love your show. I watch it all the time!

Judge: Okay, so honestly, what do you…

Plaintiff (Leticia): Right, yes. So, on February 27th, I was looking for a car for my mom on Craigslist. I saw this Ford Escape 2004 listed for $3,500. It had 88,000 miles, one owner, in pretty good condition, and the price was negotiable.

Plaintiff (Leticia): I called Stephen and asked if we could see the car. I asked if we could go down on the price. He said he wasn’t going down that much but was willing to budge a little. He explained that the car was his son's, in good condition, and that his son went to college, so he didn’t need the car anymore.

Plaintiff (Leticia): I went out there, test-drove it for about three blocks, but it was a residential area, so I didn’t go that fast.

Judge: Who was the car for?

Plaintiff (Leticia): It was for my mom.

Judge: Why didn’t she test-drive it?

Plaintiff (Leticia): She was there, but she trusted my judgment.

Judge: Did she get in the car with you when you were test-driving it?

Plaintiff (Leticia): No, he did.

Judge: And your mom didn’t?

Plaintiff (Leticia): No, my mom didn’t.

Judge: Is it a two-seater?

Plaintiff (Leticia): No.

Judge: If it’s her car, how come she didn’t test-drive it?

Plaintiff (Leticia): I always make all the decisions.

Judge: Okay, so which is why I feel bad because I… I know you feel bad.

Plaintiff (Leticia): Right. So, on the 28th, I added it to my insurance policy.

Judge: Wait, so you agreed on a price?

Plaintiff (Leticia): Yes, $2,800. I tried to get it for $2,750, but he wouldn’t budge, so I paid $2,800.

Judge: That’s down from $3,500?

Plaintiff (Leticia): Yes.

Judge: All right, so you got a deal. Did you have a mechanic check it out before buying it?

Plaintiff (Leticia): No, I didn’t.

Judge: Is there any paperwork from the sale?

Plaintiff (Leticia): We gave the bill of sale to the motor vehicle department.

Judge: You don’t have a copy of the bill of sale?

Plaintiff (Leticia): No.

Judge: Do you have a copy of the bill of sale?

Defendant (Stephen): No.

Judge: Do you have a phone?

Plaintiff (Leticia): Yes.

Judge: Do your phones take pictures?

Plaintiff (Leticia): Yes.

Judge: They’re not just for nasty pictures; they’re also for taking pictures of evidence. You should have taken a picture of the bill of sale. Now, I don’t know what it said or the terms of the sale.

Judge: Did he tell you anything besides it runs well?

Plaintiff (Leticia): He said if there were any problems, we could always bring it back, and he would give us our money back.

Judge: Did you say that?

Defendant (Stephen): No.

Judge: That’s why we want a bill of sale. So, what happens next?

Plaintiff (Leticia): Three days later, the check engine light comes on, and it makes a loud rattling noise. I have a video.

Judge: Let’s hear it.

Plaintiff (Leticia): (Plays video of loud rattling noise and smoke.)

Judge: Did you take it to a mechanic?

Plaintiff (Leticia): Yes, Ford said the engine has a knock and to take it back to whoever we bought it from.

Judge: What will it take to repair it?

Plaintiff (Leticia): Stephen called me on March 8th and offered $800.

Judge: Did you know of any issues with the car?

Defendant (Stephen): No, my son was driving it two months before.

Judge: How long did you own it?

Defendant (Stephen): Two years.

Judge: Why didn’t you get it checked out before buying it?

Plaintiff (Leticia): I just trusted that it was okay.

Judge: It’s an “as-is” sale. You should have had a mechanic check it out. The law protects commerce; otherwise, every used car sale could lead to lawsuits. When Ford checked it, did they say what needed to be done?

Plaintiff (Leticia): They said it needed a new engine for $1,600.

Judge: And he offered you $800. Why didn’t you take it?

Plaintiff (Leticia): I felt it wasn’t fair.

Judge: Stephen, why did you offer the $800?

Defendant (Stephen): I felt bad and wanted to compromise.

Judge: Or were you threatened?

Defendant (Stephen): I was threatened. They came to my house, told me they were leaving the car, and the mother yelled she would make my life hell. The son said, “You don’t want to do this to my mother.” I called the police.

Judge: The law is the law. I rule in favor of the defendant. Case dismissed.
Manipulation Techniques: gaslighting, evasion, deflection, emotional appeal, playing the victim

Dialogue:
Judge: All parties, please raise your right hands. Welcome back to The People's Court. Next case on the docket, the Plaintiff says he knows the 89-year-old driver hit his car in the VA parking lot, but the Defendant says the Plaintiff is dreaming it up. It’s the case of Car Wars. Thank you, Douglas.

Judge: Allan Hayatt?

Plaintiff: Yes, ma’am.

Judge: You are suing David Lieberwitz for $2,113 to fix your car because, according to you, Mr. Lieberwitz hit your car, correct?

Plaintiff: Yes, ma’am.

Judge: Tell me what happened.

Plaintiff: I parked my car outside the Veterans Hospital on 7th Avenue in Brooklyn. I had an appointment. When I came out, there was damage to the back of my bumper. It was gouged and scratched and slightly ajar. I had checked the car before I went in—I always check the car when I park the car—and there was no damage on the bumper.

Judge: Okay. So, was Mr. Lieberwitz’s car parked behind yours?

Plaintiff: It was parked behind me when I returned.

Judge: When you returned, was he in the car?

Plaintiff: No, he was not.

Judge: Okay. So what did you do?

Plaintiff: Well, I waited for him to get into his car to make sure it was the right party. I came over to the window. I told him that he struck the car in front of him. He said, "I didn’t." I said, "Well, come out and take a look at what you did." He came out, and he saw nothing. I pointed it out to him.

Judge: Now, there was damage to your car that you say was fresh?

Plaintiff: That’s correct, ma’am.

Judge: And by fresh, you mean what?

Plaintiff: Fresh meaning that there was paint on the bumper that wasn’t there when I parked the car.

Judge: Okay. Can I see the damage to your car? Do you have pictures?

Plaintiff: Yes, I do.

(Photos are presented to the Judge.)

Judge: So, did you show that damage to him?

Plaintiff: I pointed it out to him. At first, he didn’t see it and just scoffed it off as something he didn’t do.

Judge: Oh, wait, his bumper’s off! Is this—you’re the dark car?

Plaintiff: I’m the gray car, and he’s the white car.

Judge: That’s correct. His bumper is ripped off!

Plaintiff: Right, but I don’t believe it’s ripped off on the other side. It’s ripped off on the left side. This damage is on the right side. It’s on my left, his right.

Judge: So that bumper came off some other way, probably. Oh Lord.

Judge: What happened?

Defendant: I—I came over to his car, walked with my wife to his car, and he was taking pictures. Movies! I said, "What are you taking movies about?"

Plaintiff: You banged into my car.

Defendant: I said, "I never touched your car. Never!" Now, I can drive. I’m definitely not—

Judge: So, how do you know about this? Did you see? Did you find—did you see him?

Plaintiff: I didn’t see him, but he did it.

Defendant: Well, why did I do it if you didn’t see me? I was here, and nobody saw me! In other words, he had no witnesses over there that I did it. He was not there at the scene when it happened.

Judge: See, I’m looking at damage to his car, and then looking at damage to yours.

Defendant: Scratches. I’m sorry—two little scratches! You could barely see them.

Judge: No, I can see them pretty clearly, sir. These are the pictures of the damage to his car.

Judge: What year is your car, sir?

Defendant: It’s a 2010 Toyota Corolla.

Judge: Did you hit his car?

Defendant: No.

Judge: Mr. Lieberwitz, did you hit his car?

Defendant: No, I did not.

Judge: Your bumper is hanging off the car. How did that happen?

Defendant: I had an accident about two weeks before this thing happened. But believe me, the bumper never touched him.

Judge: I understand. But here is the picture he took of the damage on the right side of your car, which is gray in color, which is the color of his car. Did you see that?

Defendant: But that has nothing to do with the accident!

Judge: You've been driving for how long?

Defendant: Oh, I’m 89 years old and I’ve been driving since I’m 17.

Judge: Should there be an age cutoff for drivers?

Defendant: Yes, there should.

Judge: What’s the age cutoff?

Defendant: I would say about 88 to 90.

Judge: How old are you?

Defendant: 78.

Judge: Okay, you’ve got a little time, don’t you?

Judge: Based on what I have in front of me, I do find in favor of the Plaintiff in this case, and I’m going to award the entire $2,113. Verdict for the Plaintiff. Good luck, folks.

Judge: "But you had told him, 'I'm pregnant eight months, I didn't find out 'til I was six months.' You told him anyway. So what is this about breaking up a marriage? You’d already told him you were pregnant."

Defendant (Person1): "Because I was with the other guy, and I had been living with him for a while, and I thought I was gonna keep living with him, but we broke up."

Judge: "And this is the other man that was at the hospital when Lilian was born?"

Defendant: "Yes, Your Honor."

Judge: "Did you ever tell this other man that he was Lilian's biological father?"

Defendant: "No, I did not."

Judge: "So why not allow the man who supposedly is her biological father to be present for the birth, or at least know she's in the world?"

Ms. Willard: "That was my fault, Your Honor."

Judge: Why is it correct? In other words, it does seem, as the Plaintiff's Lawyer's questions indicate, that the Rockwell above the line and the Rockwell footnote, you would think, would come out the same way under the text of the statute.

So I guess, assuming the above-the-line part is correct, why does the text of the statute support Footnote 6?

Lawyer of Defendant: So I think the text of the statute supports the removal jurisdiction in this case, not what happened in Rockwell.

So I -- I actually --

Judge: Right.

So the -- I think your answer is the part of Rockwell that's not in the footnote is -- is shaky.

Lawyer of Defendant: That's correct.

And that's what the Chamber of Commerce --

Judge: The part in the footnote, you think that's solid.

Lawyer of Defendant: I think that's solid because the text of Section 1367 says there is supplemental jurisdiction unless Congress has expressly provided otherwise.

Section (c)(3) makes clear that you don't have to have a federal question throughout the proceedings in order for there to continue to be supplemental jurisdiction; (c)(2) expressly addresses situations like this one, where the federal law claims have fallen out.

In that situation, the state law claims would substantially predominate.

Judge: I think they're going to probably say something also like it was stray comments that weren't carefully considered.

And I -- do you want to respond to the -- I'm just previewing what they're likely to say.

Lawyer of Defendant: One thing I would -- I do want to emphasize here is this Court was thinking about this question in Cohill.

If you go to the oral argument in Cohill -- right around minute 5, the Judge was asking the same questions that we're talking about today.

So the Court wasn't somehow unaware of this question.

Judge: Right.

He seemed to be articulating the position in the oral argument, as I read it, that the Plaintiff ultimately came to in the Eighth Circuit, but, obviously, by the time of Rockwell, the Judge had not stuck with that.

Lawyer of Defendant: I -- I think that's right, and I think it is important that this Court was aware of the question, continued to apply its precedents, longstanding precedent in Cohill.

And I don't think you can write Cohill, which is all about this is a doctrine of discretion.

I don't think you can write Cohill if you think that the court didn't have jurisdiction.

Judge: So can we go back to --

Judge: Suppose a diversity case -- I have a diversity case, I file it in state court, it's removed to federal court, and once I'm in federal court, I join a non-diverse party.

Can the federal -- can the federal court hold onto that case?

Lawyer of Defendant: So, no, Your Honor, and that is addressed specifically in 1367(b). So there are circumstances, you know, for example, where there's a third-party defendant or a dispensable plaintiff under Rule 20 that, you know, the court may be able to because it's not addressed in Section --

Judge: Yeah.

Well, why should there be a different rule regarding parties and claims?

Lawyer of Defendant: So Congress thought very hard about this.

This Court has long held -- had two different lines of precedent, one for pendent-claim jurisdiction and one for pendent-party jurisdiction.

It has taken a very broad view to pendent-claim jurisdiction and very narrow view to pendent-party jurisdiction. And Congress said that's not what we want.

We don't want Finley.

We do want some limits.

I think (b) tells you that (a) is so broadly written that if you don't have (b), that there would be concerns about diversity jurisdiction questions, Your Honor.

But Congress thought very carefully about this, and it -- it made clear that there would continue to be supplemental jurisdiction even when the federal claims dropped out of the case.

Judge: If we thought that the Eighth Circuit's decision is right as a matter of first principle, what relevance, if any, would this line of court of appeals decisions have in our decision-making?

Lawyer of Defendant: I think it's very important here because Congress is codifying precedent.

It's codifying these court of appeals decisions in particular in (c)(1).

It's very much aware of them.

And the court of appeals decisions are reflecting this Court's decision in St. Paul Mercury and Cohill and -- and now Rockwell.

This is incredibly well established. So I don't think the Court should ignore that that's what the court of appeals have been doing and that they're doing it for a reason, because this is a doctrine of discretion.

It's a matter for the district court to say, what are the fairness concerns? What are the comity concerns? What are the judicial efficiency concerns? Maybe the district court can dispose of the state law questions really easily and the case has been going on for two years in the district court.

It doesn't make sense to send that case back to state court.

Judge: Well, this goes back to a question that was asked earlier. Usually, when we apply this old soil rule, we're talking about a term of art in the statute about which there was a body of preexisting precedent.

What term of art can you point to here that supports your argument?

Lawyer of Defendant: Sure.

So, if you look at (c), the district courts may decline to exercise supplemental jurisdiction, that language comes directly from Cohill.

It's not in Gibbs.

That's the language the Court uses twice.

And so, when you're thinking about what did Congress intend to codify when it comes to whether the district court can exercise discretion, I think you have to take into account what this Court held in Cohill.

I also think, Your Honor, if you think that the text doesn't say anything, this Court has held that statutory silence implies ratification by Congress.

I think you can apply that doctrine as well to reach the answer here.

Judge: I think, if you think that the text doesn't say anything, you're left with trying to figure out what rule to use in this instance that best coheres with the whole panoply of rules that we use in other contexts.

And I think that on that account, you have a tough road to hoe.

You have -- you have St. Paul, and that's the amount in controversy. But, for the reasons that were discussed earlier, the amount in controversy requirement has generally been thought of as sui generis because of the difficulty of figuring out when, how you're supposed to measure that.

Lawyer of Defendant: May I respond, Your Honor?

Judge: Certainly.

Lawyer of Defendant: So I think it's very important here that this is a longstanding rule that really reflects the idea that Congress wanted district courts to make the decision. They wanted district courts to decide, is there gamesmanship going on here? Is there judicial efficiency concerns? Are there comity concerns?

And this rule that this Court is talking about would need to apply to all sorts of different circumstances, such as when the claims become moot, when the parties settle the claim, when the plaintiff amends the complaint. And when Congress drafted Section 1367(c), what it wanted was to give district courts discretion, and that's a reflection of decades and decades of precedent, Your Honor.

Judge: Thank you. Thank you, counsel.

Just a brief question.

You complain about the forum manipulation problems this would create.

I don't see how that's a problem here. They wanted -- they start in state court; they want to go back to state court.

They're not trying to manipulate anything.

Judge: "What about paying your sister child support to take care of your children? I mean, you don't want them, you're not raising them, they've been with her for the past nine months, so wouldn't you say it's fair to give her some money for them?"

Defendant: "I don't really have it. I only make like 40,000 a year."

Judge: "But you're making money. Okay, do you live alone?"

Judge: All parties, please step forward.

Judge: Welcome back to The People’s Court. Next case on the docket: The plaintiff bought a car from the defendant and claims he knew it was a lemon, but the defendant says the plaintiff checked the car out, so tough luck. It’s the case of "Ain’t No Lemonade Here." Thank you. Douglas, okay. Ms. Zigler, you are suing Jose Medina for $4,000 that you say he owes you for knowingly selling you a lemon. Tell me what happened.

Plaintiff: So this all happened within a five-day span. I’m a college student. I withdrew from my savings to buy a car. I was looking on Craigslist, where I came across his ad, which I have a picture of. In his ad, it says it runs and drives great, which I came to find out that it doesn’t.

Judge: Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court: There is no serious dispute that at the time Ms. Laufer filed suit, Acheson provided no accessibility information on its reservation website, thereby excluding disabled people from using its online reservation services and engaging in unlawful discrimination under the ADA. As Congress recognized, when places of public accommodation fail to take reasonable steps to make their services available to people with disabilities, they signal that disabled people are unwelcome participants in the marketplace and contribute to their day-to-day experience of being isolated, invisible, and ignored. If the Court reaches the standing question presented by Acheson, it should reject Acheson's argument that a disabled person does not experience that dignitary harm when she encounters the accessibility barrier on Acheson's website, unless she also has travel plans that are thwarted by the discriminatory treatment. For over six decades and most notably in Havens Realty, this Court has recognized that discrimination inflicts Article III injury regardless of whether the plaintiff experiences any harm beyond the unequal treatment. What it means to personally experience discrimination on the internet is a novel question, but whether the answer is the one we've offered or the one the government proposes, Havens requires a test that focuses on what the plaintiff experiences on the website, not her underlying motive in that experience or whether any downstream injuries result. Finally, I want to return to the issue the Court started with today. Mr. Unikowsky and I are both arguing before the Court on behalf of clients that have no legal interest in the outcome of this case: Ms. Laufer because she has dismissed her complaint with prejudice and will not be bringing any more ADA suits and Acheson because it no longer owns the hotel whose reservations service the suit challenged. These circumstances render the case, at minimum, a poor vehicle for reaching the standing question. I welcome the Court's questions.

Judge: You started with the standing question, and you spoke of dignitary injury, and in your briefs, I think you focus on stigmatic injury. The government seems to focus on informational injury. And there seems to be a -- a difference in your characterization of the injury here. Would you spend a few minutes and explain which injury are we talking about?

Plaintiff's Lawyer: Here, we're talking about a discriminatory denial of information, so it's not a pure informational injury. The discrimination aspect of it is important because, as in Havens, it's not just that you're not getting the information; you're not getting the information for a reason that conveys that you have inferior status in society. So it's inflicting a dignitary harm that you wouldn't have from a purely informational injury.

Judge: I guess I could think of cases in which a denial of injury doesn't go to one's dignity or to -- doesn't create this sort of a stigmatic -- deprivation. And it -- but it seems as though the government doesn't make that second step to the injury that you're talking about. It simply -- it's talk -- it focuses more on the information that you are deprived of in order to be able to use or to make a decision about using a hotel.

Plaintiff's Lawyer: Yeah. I think the government's brief does talk about this Court's jurisprudence on dignitary harm and discrimination and the fact that the Court has never required any downstream consequence for a discriminatory injury. And that's certainly the approach the Eleventh Circuit took on this issue.

Judge: I agree with you that discriminatory treatment is itself an injury under our precedents and Article III, certainly in Havens Realty. The discriminatory treatment here, I think Mr. Unikowsky is saying, would have occurred at the hotel or in and that there was no discrimination in the information provided because everyone was provided the same information. I just want to give you an opportunity to respond to that.

Plaintiff's Lawyer: Yeah, thank you, Your Honor. I think that's wrong for the reasons Ms. Ross said. Under 12182, places of public accommodation cannot discriminate in the provision of services. So it's not just in access to their building. The service itself has to be provided in a way that allows people with disabilities to use it in the same way as non-disabled people. I also think this argument, when it's framed that way, really is a merits question and, under Steel Co., isn't part of the standing inquiry. I think, under Steel Co. and Lexmark and if you combine those with TransUnion and Spokeo, the Court assumes as valid Ms. Laufer's cause of action here, so you assume that the allegations in her complaint state a claim of unlawful discrimination under 12182, and then you also assume that Congress conferred a cause of action to her. And then the question is, did Congress act within its constitutional authority when it provided a cause of action here?

Judge: Well, I think the -- Mr. Unikowsky's saying, particularly in the briefs, this is an Allen versus Wright kind of case, not a Havens Realty kind of case. And the distinction between those two cases -- Allen versus Wright, as you know, someone in Hawaii can't complain about discrimination that's occurring in Maine. Havens Realty, you actually personally experienced the discrimination yourself when you're, as Judge Barrett, then Judge Barrett said in Casillas, when you're lied to because of your race. So why isn't this case more like what Allen versus Wright described there?

Plaintiff's Lawyer: The plaintiffs in Allen v. Wright were challenging the IRS's failure to enforce a provision of the Internal Revenue Code that required it to withhold tax-exempt status from discriminatory schools. The plaintiffs in that case had no private right to the IRS complying with the law. They were seeking to enforce a public right. And in those circumstances, they had to show that they were personally injured by the IRS's non-compliance.

Judge: But I wonder whether there isn't a broader principle at stake in a case called -- like Havens. I mean, here, your client has disclaimed, has said, I'm not intending to go on vacation at all, I'm not intending to go to any hotel, I'm not intending to use the reservations service at all to look for accessible hotels, to reserve accessible hotels, to go to accessible hotels. I'm not doing any of that. So tell me how she is discriminated against by -- by the inaccuracies on this website.

Plaintiff's Lawyer: The reason the case comes to the Court in -- in that posture is because injunctive relief is so elusive when you're talking about concrete travel plans. As was noted earlier, Ms. Laufer did amend her complaint in this case to say that she was going on this long road trip, but when the case was on appeal, she had already gone on the road trip. Judge: Well, but that's the -- case that's before us.

Plaintiff's Lawyer: Right.

Judge: It's a case before us with a stipulation that she's not using the website for any vacation purposes --

Plaintiff's Lawyer: But --

Judge: -- that -- that she's -- and I think that your brief reads like -- like this too, that the harm is -- well, I'm not sure. You tell me. The harm is just, I'm a person with a disability, and I see that there is inaccurate information about accessibility, and that itself harms me, even if I have no plans or less than plans, I have -- I -- I'm just not thinking about vacations at all.

Plaintiff's Lawyer: Yeah. Plaintiff's Lawyer: -- as someone who might stay there --

Plaintiff's Lawyer: So --

Judge: Because I think that that's pretty similar to what is going on in this case.

Plaintiff's Lawyer: So she does not have standing based on the pictures because she's not encountering the accessibility barrier herself. She's seeing --

Judge: Well, I don't know, it's, like, right there. I mean, if you're saying it's how you experience discrimination, and I can understand that, but I'm experiencing that sense of being excluded when I see that all these hotels are set up to exclude me and others like me.

Plaintiff's Lawyer: There might be some sort of Article III argument there, but Congress certainly hasn't provided a cause of action there, unless you actually have plans to go to the hotel. And I want to go back to what Justice --

Judge: Well, then -- then just to pause there for a moment, it sounds to me like you're edging right up to the Solicitor General's test. Am I missing something?

Plaintiff's Lawyer: I see the intuitive appeal of the government's test. I think the government -- and -- and we are both asking this question of, how do you encounter discrimination on a website? And -- and this goes to the analogy Justice Sotomayor was making. Our position is, well, in the physical barrier context, you experience, you encounter that accessibility barrier when you visit the hotel regardless of whether you want to stay at the hotel or what your underlying motive is. Judge: Well, so maybe that's the difference. Just -- I just want to make sure I understand what the difference is is all I'm trying to do. The government says, yes, informational -- lack of information can be a form of discrimination --

Plaintiff's Lawyer: Yes.

Judge: -- when you have some plans. Now how concrete that is, good luck, all right --

Judge: -- but some interest in making a reservation. Plaintiff's Lawyer: Yeah. So I think that is the distinction between our position and the -- and the government's position. So the government says --

Judge: Not my hypothetical.

Plaintiff's Lawyer: Yeah. The government says you have to use the website in some way. So they would, I think, say our position is akin to driving by the website, and they want you to engage with it and actually make a reservation or -- or engage with it in a way that shows you're considering making a reservation. Judge: Well, I'm -- not sure that it's going to be much of a pleading barrier if you were to adopt --

Plaintiff's Lawyer: Yeah.

Judge: -- the government's position versus your -- your client's, I think, but I'm just trying to understand what the real difference is --

Plaintiff's Lawyer: So, the difference is that for us, the moment you go to the website, you're encountering the barrier. You may not know you're going to encounter it, but the barrier is there.

Judge: And how old are you?

Plaintiff: Unfortunately, I'm only 32 right now, but I actually had this my whole life.

Judge: Okay. So, in any event, you needed help around the house and your husband is where?

Plaintiff: My husband is in the Navy. He was deployed for six months and then, after deployment, right away the Navy's like, "You're going to Kings Bay, Georgia." And I was stuck, stayed behind in Connecticut with my three kids and an elderly mother-in-law.

Judge: Okay. And I had in the past, when it snowed, I actually had another woman who would actually shovel it out and she was paid per day.

Judge: Per day?

Plaintiff: Yeah. I gave her the cash. You know, she would show.

Judge: Where did you get the bright idea to pay a retainer for a snow guy? Well, why would you say no?

Plaintiff: Well, because I never-

Judge: Retainers are for lawyers and accountants, no disrespect intended. I don't understand why you would hire a handyman and a snow plower who would say, "Give me a three-thousand-dollar retainer." I've never done that before.

Plaintiff: Yeah.

Judge: Why would you do it now?

Plaintiff: Because I didn't realize it was something that you don't do.

Judge: Why would you not? Why would your gut not tell you it's something you don't do? When have you ever heard-- Do you have friends, the other Navy wives or anybody to talk to?

Plaintiff: Really, you don't know another human in the planet-- You say, "Is that usual to compare retainer to a snow guy?" I mean, it's just a bad idea no matter what. Why are you asking this nice lady for a retainer?

Defendant: Ma'am, I didn't ask her for the retainer. I told her what the per-job rate would be. She was the one who offered the three thousand up front.

Judge: That doesn't make any sense. If you tell her, "Whenever I come here," she said charge X. What's X?

Defendant: 275 a storm.

Judge: Per storm?

Defendant: Yes.

Judge: Okay, so no matter how many hours it takes, it's 75?

Defendant: Yes.

Judge: All right, so you tell her that. Why wouldn't you and she says, "Wait, wait. Can't I please just give you three thousand?"

Defendant: She wanted to give me the 3000 to guarantee services through the winter.

Judge: And how'd that work out for you, by the way?

Plaintiff: It did not.

Judge: Because what?

Plaintiff: How many times did this man come out to your house?

Plaintiff: It snowed a total of three times.

Judge: And how many times did he come?

Plaintiff: Two.

Judge: Okay, and so the other time you got stuck?

Plaintiff: Yes. So, the last time in March, it was the biggest snowstorm of the year, actually. And it snowed multiple times, and he never came. And I text messaged him multiple times, "Hey, when are you coming?"

Judge: Why me and your husband know each other?

Defendant: Not-- I actually do know her husband.

Judge: You're ex-Navy?

Defendant: Yes.

Judge: Not on a personal level?

Defendant: Okay, but more in the professional field in the sense in the Navy community. It's a very small community.

Judge: Well, shame it got to this because it's getting around, I'm sure.

Plaintiff: Yeah. I mean, that was one of the reasons why I trusted him because I was always told that Navy, you stick together, you help each other out, you're there for each other.

Judge: Unfortunately, I don't know any Navy wives because I don't live in Navy housing.

Plaintiff: If you were to live in Navy housing, the wives, they do things for each other.

Judge: I don't. I live outside the Navy community.

Defendant's Lawyer: Yes. The -- the fact remains, if the Court's going to depart from the otherwise controlling definition, it looks to something else in the statute that provides a countervailing argument.

For example, Utility Air was brought up. The Court looked to provisions of the statute that said, if we apply the definition of "air pollutant" to these provisions, we have an unworkable statute.

And to the extent you conclude that "person" doesn't apply to 1681q because that's unworkable or improbable that Congress intended to extend criminal liability this far, that is a -- that is an argument, an interpretation that would be limited to 1681q.

Judge: Well, you say in the statute, but I guess the argument is that there can be limitations outside the statute that would apply as well.

And I'm thinking in particular of our decision in the Bond case, the -- the -- the chemical on the doorknob that is under one interpretation, perhaps literal interpretation of the statute would be covered by the Chemical Weapons Treaty. And we said that sometimes arguments like, well, that seems pretty implausible can trump what would otherwise be a pretty precise reading of the statutory language.

Defendant's Lawyer: Well, that's right. I think Bond -- Bond is a good foil for this case. The Court found -- your opinion for the Court found that the statute was ambiguous. And, I mean, I agree that if this statute is ambiguous, then the -- the sovereign immunity canon favors the government.

But if -- but the -- the -- there, the definition the Court called improbably broad, there's nothing improbably broad about defining "person" to include the government. The Court has said, if Congress wants to not have the ordinary meaning of "person" applied --

Judge: Well, I -- I would have thought -- sorry to interrupt, but I -- I would have thought it's -- the improbability comes from the argument that this would mean you can prosecute the United States, it can mean that you can get damages from the United States and so on and so forth.

Defendant's Lawyer: Well, even if prosecuting the United States is improbable, seeking damages from the United States is not improbable. Congress waived sovereign immunity in a number of statutes.

Judge: Well, it's pretty improbable for the statute to authorize the FTC to seek damages from the United States, which is what it does.

Defendant's Lawyer: Well, the Equal Credit Opportunity Act, I think, does the same thing. It set up the same sort of enforcement mechanism. There are other -- there are other statutory schemes that authorize intergovernmental liability, our Resource Conservation Recovery Act --

Judge: You -- keep going.

Defendant's Lawyer: No. I -- I --

Judge: Okay. You -- you said at the beginning and I think just now that Congress knew what it was doing when it amended the Act. But I don't think it realized that it was imposing this liability. If you look at the CBO, Congressional Budget Office, reports, there's no mention of anything, any liability like this.

And they carefully analyze how much the additional costs would be for the executive branch in enforcement and the judicial branch in handling the additional cases. So -- and the CBO score, as you know and anyone familiar with that process knows, is very important for Congress. So I -- I don't think it's right to say Congress knew what it was doing.

You may -- could still win without that, but I think that's not -- not correct, unless you want to respond to that in some way.

Defendant's Lawyer: Well -- well, I don't think the CBO is itself Congress. And Congress --

Judge: Correct.

Defendant's Lawyer: I mean, what -- what is --

Judge: But they do -- do you challenge that Congress relies on the CBO score when it's doing legislation?

Defendant's Lawyer: No, it's -- it's -- it's part --

Judge: Yeah.

Defendant's Lawyer: -- of the committee reports.

Judge: Yeah, I think you have to acknowledge that.

Defendant's Lawyer: I -- I accept the proposition that the legislative history doesn't say one thing or another about sovereign immunity.

The -- the -- the legislative history does talk about the purpose of the statute, and that is consistent with a conclusion that Congress wanted to hold the government liable just as any private furnisher of information would be for -- for failing to comply with their FCRA duties.

Judge: Can I -- can I also, on a different tack, another judge raised an important question, I thought, about federal versus state sovereign immunity, and you heard I -- I mentioned separation of powers.

My understanding was that the reason we have that is because taxpayer money is -- is valuable and we should be sure it's been appropriated before we funnel a bunch of money out of the Treasury. That's both to ensure that money can be spent on other programs because it's not limitless money in the Treasury, and it's to ensure otherwise that taxes aren't raised. So we have to be very careful before we overstep, as basic separation of powers.

Do you dispute any of that?

Defendant's Lawyer: Not at all. You have two principles that are designed to protect that interest. One is the sovereign immunity canon.

So, if the -- a statute is ambiguous, the sovereign immunity canon would say even the government gets the benefit of ambiguities even if that's not the best reading of the statute.

And the second one is the specificity requirement, so a broad statute cannot waive sovereign immunity. The -- the statute must discuss governmental entities specifically, which the definition here does.

Judge: Counsel, I guess I'm a little confused why -- I mean, I can understand that you don't need to make the argument to prevail in your view, but I -- I'm not sure I understand your response to the previous judge from first principles.

Sovereign immunity serves many important purposes in respecting other institutions, states, tribes, foreign governments. It's inherent in our constitutional design, embodied in the Tenth Amendment even, for example.

But, when it comes -- if we're worried about protecting the federal fisc, I would have thought that the answer might be Congress is in the best position to do that. Article I gives them power over the federal fisc. And we have no license to expand or contract its instructions artificially but follow them instead faithfully.

Defendant's Lawyer: I mean, that's right. At bottom, this is a policy choice for Congress to make.

Judge: One would have thought.

Judge: Why is there a clear statement rule then?

Defendant's Lawyer: Well, just as with other policy choices, the Court -- if the -- the area is a particularly sensitive one, the Court wants --

Judge: Why -- why -- why is it sensitive?

Judge: What isn't sensitive?

Judge: Why -- why is it sensitive? Focus on that if you could.

Defendant's Lawyer: Well, I -- I think the Court takes holding the government liable, especially for damages, seriously. So it wants -- doesn't want to construe ambiguous text in -- in a way that may be different than Congress understood ambiguous text. But, when the text is clear, that's a different matter altogether.

When there's only one plausible interpretation of the text under traditional rules of statutory interpretation, the Court shouldn't be applying a different interpretation of the statute.

Judge:
Thank you, Douglas. Mr. Whitman, you are representing Southshore Power Squadron as command and suing Ilana Vasquez for $180 for four persons' admissions to a Halloween party back in 2015. What’s going on?

Plaintiff:
Well, actually, 2014 was the Halloween party, and we did the Halloween party in conjunction with the local yacht club.

Judge:
What is Southshore Power Squadron?

Plaintiff:
We are an educational organization teaching safe boating through education. We’ve been in existence since November 10, 1928—just celebrated our 90th anniversary.

Judge:
Oh, good for you guys. All right, so you had this Halloween party in 2014.

Plaintiff:
Yes, I stand corrected.

Judge:
And then what happens?

Plaintiff:
Well, the treasury collected money from the participants, which included the defendant as well as other members of the squadron. They paid the fee via check to our treasurer. He, in turn, I in turn, turned over a check for the amount of money collected to the yacht club.

Judge:
Okay.

Plaintiff:
Unfortunately, this involved an evening of open bar, 15-16 dishes of food—

Judge:
Was everybody in costume?

Plaintiff:
Yes, in costume. Prizes for best costumes.

Judge:
Did everybody participate, or did some people come not in costume?

Plaintiff:
No, everybody participated.

Judge:
Do you have pictures of your costume?

Plaintiff:
No.

Judge:
What did you go as?

Plaintiff:
Oh boy, it was so long ago I don’t remember.

Judge:
Come on, you dress up every Halloween. You remember.

Plaintiff:
I think it was a doctor’s uniform.

Judge:
Okay. So, they paid, and then what happened with your treasurer?

Plaintiff:
Well, what he did is he put the checks in a folder, similar to what these are, put it away, and forgot about it. It wasn’t until April or May of 2016 that he realized the checks were never deposited.

Judge:
By this time, the checks had gone dead?

Plaintiff:
Well, they’re stale.

Judge:
Why did he not deposit them? You mean none of the checks or just some of them?

Plaintiff:
None.

Judge:
Is he still your treasurer?

Plaintiff:
No.

Judge:
Was he still treasurer after this debacle?

Plaintiff:
Yes.

Judge:
How much longer?

Plaintiff:
Well, I’ll explain that. Everyone gave replacement checks.

Judge:
Okay, and then what happened?

Plaintiff:
He did the same thing again.

Judge:
Are you kidding me?

Plaintiff:
No, I’m being very honest.

Judge:
When did the accountant get fired?

Plaintiff:
He only resigned about three months ago.

Judge:
Why did you keep him for so long?

Plaintiff:
We’re a volunteer organization. Nobody is paid.

Judge:
Okay, so what happened next?

Plaintiff:
I took over again in 2017. In July 2017, we realized two checks were still outstanding, one of which was the defendant’s.

Judge:
Okay. Ms. Vasquez, you went to the party, had fun, and wrote a check. Then they asked you for a replacement check, and you gave them one. Then they asked for another, and you gave them another. And now they want another one.

Defendant:
Yes.

Judge:
Why won’t you pay again?

Defendant:
If I give you a check and you forget to cash it, do I still owe you the money? I don’t think so.

Judge:
Going inside the courtroom...

Defendant:
It’s not a matter of not paying. Each of us gave them a check of $90 the night of the party. Then we also paid a second time. Now they’re asking for a fourth check.

Judge:
Yeah, I don’t blame you for being frustrated. Did you quit the organization?

Defendant:
Yes, in 2018.

Judge:
And then you got a letter from Mr. Whitman?

Defendant:
Yes, and it was insulting.

Judge:
Why?

Defendant:
It was aggressive. He said I better pay by a certain date, or else. It felt like a threat.

Judge:
Let me see the letter.

[Examines Letter]

Judge:
This is severe under the circumstances.

Plaintiff:
I don’t think so.

Judge:
Well, I’m going to tell you—because this may be your first time in court, but it’s not mine—if you’d been a little nicer, we wouldn’t be here. But you still have to pay.

Defendant:
[Scoffs]

Judge:
I’m not ordering you to pay interest because the only reason you’ve had to wait three years is because of the incompetence of your treasurer. But I am ordering you to pay the $180. Should I make you pay the $15 court fee? I can’t decide. Usually, when someone wins, they get their court costs covered. Yes, I’ll order that too.

Judge:
But here’s a word of advice: I tell my children, “Say it, forget it; write it, regret it.” Assume that what you write will be interpreted in the worst way possible. Rewrite it. Especially when you’re asking someone to do something. Don’t threaten them.

[Verdict:]
Verdict for the plaintiff. Defendant must pay $180 plus $15 in court fees.

Judge: "Where does dad come in?"

Plaintiff: "I mean, he's rarely around. I mean, he's doing his little producing thing and stuff and meeting up with other women and stuff, you know. That's why I had this little charge, so I mean, that's what he's doing."

Plaintiff: "I mean, it's an assault charge, but I mean, it is what it is at this point. It already happened, so okay."

Judge: Welcome back to The People’s Court. The plaintiff says the defendant has two kids with her nephew and has fronted money that she’s not paying back. But the defendant says the plaintiff’s nephew split up with her when he got out of jail, and there was no loan. This is the case of “It’s All Relative.”

All right, how are you two related? Related, or just know each other?

Plaintiff: We’re not related. She has two kids with my nephew.

Judge: She has two kids with your nephew. So how long have you known each other?

Plaintiff: Since she’s had those two kids with my nephew.

Defendant: I can’t remember what that is because I just met you.

Plaintiff: Since 2014, I think, the oldest child.

Judge: Okay, so like five years. Does that sound right?

Defendant: Yes.

Judge: All right, now, according to you, throughout the course of those years, you have been making a loan here, a loan there to her. Did you keep any track of any of those loans?

Plaintiff: Yes, in my book.

Judge: Okay, so do you have a book where you have each and every one of these loans?

Plaintiff: I have my notes of what I was writing down.

Judge: Okay, this is a piece of paper where you wrote down everything you’re saying today. What I am asking for is something different. When you would make her a loan, how would you document it? Because I know you didn’t write it down contemporaneously on this piece of paper. You wrote this all at once. So I’m trying to figure out how you kept track of these purported loans.

Plaintiff: When she would borrow, I would know.

Judge: Where did you write it down? Because this isn’t it.

Plaintiff: I wrote it down. I just know when someone borrows money from you.

Judge: Right, but you didn’t actually keep track of this stuff, did you?

Plaintiff: I did, but—

Judge: Then how do you come up with the figure of $500?

Plaintiff: She was supposed to pay me back when she got her taxes. But she went to jail and had to bail herself out. When she got out, she said she’d pay me next year. I waited patiently. Then I called her, and she texted me, “You’ll get the money when I get the money.”

Judge: Show me the text.

Defendant: I never actually borrowed $500 per se, but sometimes she’d give me $20 or $40 for Pampers, milk, or whatever.

Judge: Did you ever tell her you’d pay her back?

Defendant: No, I felt like if I did owe her money, she got it back already.

Judge: How?

Defendant: There was a point in time where she was watching my children and getting paid by the government, but she wasn’t really watching them. She was just collecting $800 a month.

Plaintiff: That’s a lie!

Defendant: I have paperwork stating I stopped working December 26th. She got paid for January and February when she wasn’t even watching my kids.

Judge: Why didn’t you report that?

Defendant: I thought they had stopped the checks. Until I got a letter, I didn’t know.

Judge: So you were cheating childcare benefits?

Defendant: No, I was receiving assistance to look for a job.

Judge: But you weren’t looking?

Defendant: No, I was unemployed.

Judge: Okay, do you owe her money?

Defendant: No, I don’t feel like I do.

Judge: You think you don’t owe because she was getting benefits?

Defendant: Yes.

Judge: When you loan money to a relative, do you sign an IOU?

Defendant: No.

Judge: Should you? Yes. It’s like loaning money to anyone else. If you don’t have proof, there’s no claim. Going back inside the courtroom.

Judge: You said you’d show me a text proving she said, “You’ll get your money when I get my money.”

Defendant: Yes, but I was just trying to end the conversation.

Judge: You live informally. There’s no documentation. How do you expect me to determine the exact amount owed?

Plaintiff: My nephew knows she owes me.

Judge: But I don’t.

Judge: You’re suing for a round number. That’s suspicious. Usually, people with proof have itemized amounts. I think you picked this figure out of the sky. I have to deny your claim. On your countersuit, zero. Case dismissed.

Judge: We'll hear argument next in Case 23-51, Bissonnette versus LePage Bakeries.

Plaintiff’s Lawyer: Thank you. Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court: Less than two years ago, in Southwest versus Saxon, this Court carefully examined the text and history of the Federal Arbitration Act's worker exemption, and it held that the exemption applies to "any class of workers directly involved in transporting goods across state or international borders."

Flowers now asks this Court to add an additional unwritten requirement that the worker's employer must sell transportation. According to Flowers, if the thousands of truck drivers who work full-time hauling its goods were only employed by a trucking company that Flowers had hired to do so, then they’d be exempt transportation workers.

But because Flowers essentially created its own in-house trucking company, it says that those same truck drivers are no longer transportation workers. That distinction has no basis in the text of the statute.

Flowers’ only attempt at a textual argument is its invocation of ejusdem generis, but that argument fails from the start because Flowers can’t identify a single example of the word "seamen" ever being defined based on whether a worker's employer sold transportation.

In fact, if Flowers’ drivers were on boats rather than trucks, under Flowers' own definition of "seamen," they would be seamen. In the words of Saxon, that sinks the company’s ejusdem generis argument.

Unable to rely on the text, Flowers pivots to administrability. But even if this Court could rewrite statutes to make them easier to apply, Flowers' rule is anything but workable. Flowers can't even explain how it would apply in this very case.

This Court should reject Flowers' attempt to add to the FAA an employer-based industry requirement that is both atextual and unworkable.

I welcome this Court’s questions.

Judge: If this case is decided in your favor, would it affect the separate question of whether or not these drivers are engaged in intrastate deliveries?

Plaintiff’s Lawyer: No, I don’t think it would. The only question—as this case comes to the Court—built into the question presented is the assumption that the workers are members of a class of workers engaged in interstate commerce. It wouldn’t affect that at all. The only question here is, assuming that to be true, is there an additional requirement that the individual plaintiffs be employed by a company that’s in the transportation industry?

Judge: So why would the inquiry into the transportation industry be any more complicated than the inquiry into transportation workers?

Plaintiff’s Lawyer: By "transportation workers," I take it you mean whether someone is directly involved?

Judge: Yeah.

Plaintiff’s Lawyer: So I think there are certainly going to be edge cases about whether a class of workers is directly involved in transporting goods across state or international borders. I would concede that.

But what Flowers is asking is that we adopt an additional requirement on top of that that wouldn’t obviate that inquiry.

So take, for example, Amazon. It has trucks traveling across the highway. It has planes in the air. Maybe there’s a difficult question about whether those, say, truckers are directly involved in transporting goods across borders.

But what Flowers says is, in addition to figuring out that question, we also have to figure out whether Amazon sells transportation. So, you know, how do we know? Do we need discovery into whether it sells transportation? Does it matter who it sells it to? Does it just have to sell it to its customers? Does it have to sell it to other companies? Does it matter how much transportation it sells? Does it matter what percentage of its price is in revenues?

All of these are going to be difficult questions that are then layered on top of the question you raised, which is already in the text of the statute. And so, in Amazon’s case, for example, it doesn’t get us out of the question you raised. It just adds an additional one on top.

Judge: In your opening, you emphasized the text quite a bit.

Plaintiff’s Lawyer: Yes, Your Honor.

Judge: But in ejusdem generis cases, by definition, we’re not following the literal text of the residual clause. Instead, we’re looking at the listed items and trying to discern what connects those listed items, what feature of those listed items is common.

And as the Scalia-Garner treatise says, that can be somewhat indeterminate. A difficult position for judges, but we have to try to figure it out.

So seamen and railroad employees in 1925—one thing that it seems was going on, and I want to get your reaction to, is Congress took them out of this arbitration regime. All workers, all contracts of employment are subject to arbitration. It takes them out, but it takes them out seemingly—you have to look at the legal context, I would think—because they had a separate arbitration regime that already existed.

In other words, at least as I read the record, and it is murky in parts, I’ll grant you, as of 1925, Congress didn’t want anyone to be outside of arbitration. They wanted Section 2 for most workers and then not for seamen and railroad employees because there was a separate arbitration regime.

Why, when we look at the common legal context that connects those terms, isn’t that the correct way to look at it? Why is that wrong?

Plaintiff’s Lawyer: There are two answers to that.

One is we know that Congress wasn’t exempting just workers who had alternative dispute resolution regimes because it added the residual clause, and that residual clause would have covered no workers at all at the time.

Judge: At the time, but what Congress was doing, arguably—this is the argument—was contemplating that there would be future industries that would fit in. And in 1936, the airline industry comes in, and those employees are funneled into the same kind of separate arbitration—or the railway arbitration regime. So Congress was accommodating the future.

Plaintiff’s Lawyer: Sure. The second historical answer to that is, even if this Court were going to try to discern some purpose of the exemption instead of focusing specifically on the text, which is difficult a hundred years later, you know, if you look at seamen, I think one of the assumptions underlying that question is that seamen were going to arbitration—that there was a mandatory arbitration scheme that covered seamen. And that’s actually just not correct.

Judge: So you think Congress in 1925 wanted seamen to be able to go straight to court?

Plaintiff’s Lawyer: I think that’s exactly right.

Judge: Is there anything to support that?

Plaintiff’s Lawyer: Sure. If you look at Title 46, enacted in 1925, the same year as the Federal Arbitration Act, what you’ll see is references to the right of seamen to go to court. The Shipping Commissioners Act only applied if, after the dispute arose, both parties agreed in writing to arbitration, which is different from what the FAA requires.

Judge: Counsel, can I—sorry, are you finished?

Judge: Is there any continuing reason—this is just my ignorance, so I’m just curious—we were talking about why in 1925 the regulatory regime existed and whether Congress wanted to funnel some of these transportation workers into alternative dispute resolution mechanisms. Is this now just an anachronism, or is there any continuing reason for transportation workers to be exempt?

Plaintiff’s Lawyer: So I’ll be quite honest—it’s not entirely clear why in 1925. But if we assume the reason was that transportation workers were crucial to commerce and that their disputes could cause nationwide disruptions, that concern still exists today.

Judge: The Second Circuit did not rely on the district court’s reasoning.

Plaintiff’s Lawyer: They did not.

Judge: And so it’s not before us. But the district court argued they’re not transportation workers because they also do office-based work. How do you respond to that?

Plaintiff’s Lawyer: There’s a factual answer and a legal answer. The legal answer is…

Judge: As I see the question my way, she has a liberty interest in her marriage and having her husband visit. She doesn't have a liberty interest in having the visa granted, but she does have a liberty interest in knowing why and an opportunity to oppose it if there is an opposition that can be had. But the review would be very limited. In a previous case, we said the process due is just knowing the reason and a statutory citation because she's entitled to know he was a terrorist. In a conviction, the husband has gotten full notice, all the grounds have been told to the wife because there's been a public adjudication of that ground. Similarly, with all the other examples you provide, full process has been provided.

But what you're saying is I have a right and, judicially created, we're going to say, there is no process whatsoever you're entitled to. Now you're saying you're entitled to what Congress permits you to have. And so the question is, what's that?

Defendant's Lawyer: In this context, that's not even under that precedent. Congress has specifically said that we don't have to give a reason at all if this is the reason for the denial. But I would say that we don't think that there is a separate category of liberty interests for getting information as opposed to the underlying liberty interest. And so the fact that she wants to live with her husband in the United States doesn't mean that she has a liberty interest in having information related to something that prevents her from—prevents him from doing that.

Judge: Makes little sense to me in the example I gave.

Defendant's Lawyer: Pardon?

Judge: Makes little sense to me in the example I gave about the mistaken identity.

Defendant's Lawyer: It—

Judge: She would be seeking to exercise her right to live with him on the ground that you don't have a statutory basis to exclude him.

Defendant's Lawyer: But she doesn't have a constitutional right to participate in that proceeding and say you got this decision wrong.

Judge: Well, that's—

Defendant's Lawyer: And that is not unusual that there are instances where nobody is able to get review. As a previous concurring opinion pointed out, we give the examples of the prisoner who was not able to challenge a transfer to a different prison, the soldier who is not able to challenge a military deployment. In another case, where the Court relied on another precedent, that was an instance—

Judge: But they held no constitutional—

Defendant's Lawyer: —where nobody was able to get enforcement of that judicial—of that restraining order.

Judge: You're not mentioning independent constitutional rights. All right. Thank you, counsel.

Judge: I think I just want to understand the conversation that you had with another judge before about the nature of the constitutional interest here versus in a prior case.

So assume for purposes of this question that the prior case does require some kind of limited judicial review—in other words, this legitimate and bona fide state explanation—in the case where there is a constitutional right implicated, as there was in that case. And the question that I thought I heard the other judge asking was: If you assume that, why would this constitutional right be less important than the constitutional right that was implicated in the prior case?

In other words, the right of a few professors to listen to some guy for a few hours—why would that be more important than the right of a person to be able to live with their spouse in this country?

Defendant's Lawyer: I—

Judge: And are you saying that it is, or are you saying that once we—once we think of that prior case in that way, yes, this constitutional right is just as important?

Defendant's Lawyer: Yeah, I don't think that we've tried to compare the constitutional rights in that regard. I think that we would say that prior case hasn't decided the one—

Judge: Yeah, I—I get that.

Defendant's Lawyer: —and, therefore, we're just making a separate argument—

Judge: But if we assume that prior case—

Defendant's Lawyer: —about this liberty interest.

Judge: If we assume that prior case has decided the one, you would not—

Defendant's Lawyer: Well—

Judge: —contest that this constitutional right is any different?

Defendant's Lawyer: I—I—I would suggest that there could be a difference between First Amendment rights and the Due Process Clause and the third-party interests that are at stake in the due process analysis under another precedent, but, as I said to another judge, I still don't think that that means that a spouse could come in and have a better claim because they want to talk in person with their spouse instead of, you know, over the telephone.

Judge: Right. Well, she wouldn't need a better claim if you were willing to say, yes, this—the right—the right of marriage and the burden that this places on that right is just as important as the right of listening to, you know, a lecturer—

Defendant's Lawyer: Yeah. And—

Judge: —that was at issue in that prior case.

Defendant's Lawyer: And I think my basic answer to that, Judge, is the assumption of your question, which is that we think that the prior case didn't actually decide that, which is why it's open for the Court to decide the first question here. If it wants to assume that there's a liberty interest here that is equal to what the Court assumed was at stake in that prior case and sort of assumed was at issue in another case and therefore gave a very limited form of review, then—then—then you'd be doing an analysis under that case, and we think that we would win.

Judge: Well, I mean --

Lawyer of Defendant: --this Court's review directly.

Judge: Well, I mean, maybe it doesn't think it warrants this Court's attention because there's no split or the usual criteria that we have for cert.

But I -- I don't think it's part of an exhaustion requirement that you've got to seek en banc review and certiorari.

That's pretty --I mean --I mean, I think that's unusual.

Those remedies are strictly limited and may not at all be applicable to what is nonetheless a perfectly valid legal claim.

Lawyer of Defendant: Well, I think, in --in general, if -- if you have a court of appeals decision that comes out against you and you want the court in some future proceeding to kind of behave on the assumption that that decision was wrong, you really need to seek whatever form of review is available at that time rather than ask the court in the subsequent proceeding to -- to hold that the former court got it wrong.

Judge: What happens in a normal --

Judge: I'm sorry.

Judge: Go ahead.

Judge: What happens in a normal litigation? Let's assume it's not an agency.

There's a motion to intervene by a party.

Are they required to appeal?

Lawyer of Defendant: Yes.

If they --if they want to become parties, they --if they are denied intervention, then -- and they want to have the rights and obligations that go with party status in the underlying litigation, they would need to appeal from the denial of intervention.

And if they lost there, they couldn't take an appeal from the merits judgment in the case and essentially ask for a second bite at the apple, ask the court of appeals in the merits appeal to revisit the question of whether intervention should be granted.

Judge: We would never --

Judge: Why should --

Judge: -- have any ending to litigation if parties who want to intervene could come in at any point in time, even after judgment, raising new issues, when they weren't parties below?

Lawyer of Defendant: Yes.

Judge: Why shouldn't Fasken have been allowed to intervene? If this had been a civil proceeding, he certainly would have met the requirements for intervention, would he -- would it not?

Lawyer of Defendant: Yes.

Judge: If this was a -- if this --

Lawyer of Defendant: Yes.

Judge: He meets -- he would meet Rule 24, right?

Lawyer of Defendant: Yes.

Judge: So why -- why was he kept out of this?

Lawyer of Defendant: Basically, because the -- the Commission or the -- yeah, the -- first, the Atomic Safety and Licensing Board and then the Commission found that he had -- found that Fasken had failed to raise a genuine issue of law or fact.

And it's important to realize that the issues that Fasken was trying to raise as an intervenor were very different from the one that is at issue now.

Fasken was not arguing at that stage that the NRC lacked statutory authority to license offsite storage.

It was making much more fact-specific environmental objection -- asserting much more fact-specific environmental objections to the project.

Judge: Did the State raise the issue it's raising today in any of the proceedings below?

Lawyer of Defendant: Not --not in the agency proceedings.

It raised the -- the statutory argument in the court of appeals but not the end --

Judge: Not before the agency?

Lawyer of Defendant: And the --the -- the State didn't even attempt to intervene in the agency licensing proceeding.

Judge: And they have an absolute right to intervene?

Lawyer of Defendant: They don't have an absolute right to intervene.

They -- they have --there are more, I would say, forgiving or more hospitable standing requirements for the State, but the State still has to identify an admissible contention under the NRC's intervention rules.

Judge: Would you say that one of the purposes of the party requirement in the Hobbs Act is to ensure that issues are raised before the agency?

Lawyer of Defendant: It would say that as a purpose.

I would also have to concede that the purpose is achieved imperfectly because the Hobbs Act doesn't have what is sometimes referred to as an issue-exhaustion requirement. That is, the Hobbs Act requires that you be a party, but at least under the terms of the statute, there is no requirement that, as a party, you raise the same issue that you want to raise in court.

Judge: Mr. Stewart, I understand your argument to be that the "party aggrieved" language in the Hobbs Act is narrower than the "person adversely affected" language in the APA.

Lawyer of Defendant: Yes.
