-- you don't appreciate a difference between the downline impacts of having more train traffic in certain areas versus what is being carried on those trains and what then happens to that cargo?

Lawyer of Defendant:
So I think the standard is foreseeability for both, but I do think that it will be much more rare for the trains to be carrying a single commodity for refining in a single -- for consumption in -- in a single location.

That, I think, is what makes this case, you know, a particularly unrepresentative example of -- of the foreseeability standard because, in many cases, as the D.C. Circuit acknowledged, the -- whatever the commodity is, whether it's on a train or in a pipeline, it will go into some national distribution network and no one will know where it will go, what will -- how it will be consumed, whether it will displace other goods.

And I think the D.C. Circuit made -- made that point in distinguishing one of its own precedents involving natural gas. But, in this case, the -- the entire purpose of the project is to carry waxy crude oil, and the record before the agency shows that every train that leaves the Uinta Basin is going to be carrying waxy crude oil, every one.

And the Board was able to quantify both the amount of oil that would be necessary to make the project financially viable and to identify where it would go. So, if the Board has essentially tracked the oil out of the basin onto the trains and to the refineries, what the D.C. Circuit pointed out is that since the agency was also assuming that all of the oil would be refined, it needs to -- it needs to taut up the environmental consequences at the refinery. In many cases, that will not be the case, but that's -- that's why the reasonable foreseeability standard is met here.

And the --

Judge:
Do -- do -- do you think that the agency could turn down the project on that basis? I mean, I -- I take it you must, because you were assuming that the agency couldn't mitigate the harms by saying you can't carry this particular product. Do you think that the agency can turn down the product? Is that the sort of assumption that's underlying what you're saying?

Lawyer of Defendant:
So I think that is the assumption on which the D.C. Circuit decided the case.

But that's not a NEPA question obviously. It is a question of the -- the authority under the ICCTA.

Judge:
I mean, it seems related to a NEPA question because, if the agency can't mitigate the harm and it can't turn down the entire project, one wonders what all this fuss and bother is about.

Lawyer of Defendant:
Right.

I think the D.C. Circuit understood that these were the types of -- the types of considerations and certainly the downline impacts, I -- I -- I think probably the upstream impacts as well, were the kinds of considerations that -- I mean, they did motivate at least one member of the Board to dissent from -- to dissent from the decision. So, as the case came to the Court and as the question presented is framed for you, that -- that is not the question.

That -- that's -- that's taken as a given.

Judge:
Can --

Lawyer of Defendant:
And I take --

Judge:
-- can I ask about foreseeability? Because, I -- I mean, foreseeability is certainly part of the inquiry, but I'm having -- you're -- you're -- you're seeming to make it the entire thing. And if it were the entire thing, Public Citizen couldn't have come out the way it came out, where it was perfectly foreseeable what environmental effects were going to flow from those trucks. So I guess I'm wondering, it's got to be more than that, right, Mr. Jay?

Lawyer of Defendant:
It is more than that.

So the government has this mantra in its brief about attenuated, speculative, contingent, or otherwise insufficiently material. We actually agree with a lot of that, that -- that things that are too attenuated or speculative for the agency to -- to look at, those are excluded by the foreseeability standard. And then setting it even separate and apart from the foreseeability standard, this is almost sort of the -- the flip side of -- of Mr. Clement's position.

Mr. Clement seems to be saying that if somebody else has authority to look at it, this agency shouldn't.

So our -- our position is, if the -- if this agency doesn't have authority to look at it, it is not required to. And then the third point is that they obviously have to be environmental in nature. That was the thrust of Metropolitan Edison, that some things are not environmental consequences at all because they don't bear -- there 's no causal relationship between the -- the agency action and the effect on the physical environment. So I think each of those is -- is part of the analysis, but quite a -- quite a bit of it is accomplished by the reasonable foreseeability standard.

And I -- I think that there -- there -- it's important to distinguish between two questions.

One is how much process should NEPA -- should the agency give the things that are within what NEPA tells it to look at? And much of Mr. Kneedler's presentation focused on that. And we agree a hundred percent that agencies have a lot of discretion to say we're going to prioritize these effects over others. And we think that's the necessary concomitant of the -- of the BUILDER Act and the 150-page limit and the time limit, that agencies are going to have to prioritize some effects over others. They will get deference if they explain briefly what their reasoning is for doing so.

That could have to do with their statutory mission. It could have to do with their expertise. But what Congress did not do is say that it was going to attack this -- the problem of NEPA being too -- having too broad a compass by saying -- by changing the reasonable foreseeability standard. And that's the problem with what Mr. Clement is proposing, is that he's saying that even where the agency finds something reasonably foreseeable and says, we should study that, and in that study, it makes a basic AP error -- APA error, like the one that the district court -- sorry, that the court of appeals in this case found to be utterly unreasoned. Mr. Clement says: No problem, harmless error, because NEPA didn't require you to study that at all. And that, we think, is the wrong way to -- to attack the problem because the reasonable foreseeability standard is in the statute, and -- and this sort of alternative "not my problem" standard is not.

Judge:
Why do you say that the agency here didn't do anything? I thought there were 50 pages of their EIS that was dedicated to the environmental consequences of new oil and gas drilling and refining.

Lawyer of Defendant:
So the -- the -- the upstream development, I -- I -- I think it's -- it's a curious case, Justice Jackson, because the -- as you say, the agency took quite a bit of time looking at certain aspects of upstream development.

Judge: All right. So getting back to the question about the Barker test, I thought that Barker was about timing and that there were various species of due process claims. One is about how quickly or slowly the government has acted to provide the procedure it has promised, and another is about contesting the procedures themselves. I understood Barker to apply to the former, concerning timing, while Mathews v. Eldridge applies to the latter. Am I oversimplifying this, or is there a reason to apply Barker to a situation that is not about timing?

Plaintiff's Lawyer: I think you're looking at it correctly, Judge. While the timing question in $8,850 tracks the Barker test, our claim is different.

Judge: So why is it different?

Judge: I didn't understand that.

Judge: Yes. Why is it different?

Judge: The only reason you're asking for a retention hearing is to get the car back sooner. That’s a timing question. Barker established one timing rule, and the claimants here want another, more generous one. This isn't about process for process’s sake—it’s about people wanting their property back sooner. So isn't this still a timing issue?

Plaintiff's Lawyer: You can view it as a timing question in a broad sense, but that doesn’t mean the issues are the same. Consider the criminal context as an analogy. The Barker speedy trial right is about reaching a final determination quickly, while a separate right under Gerstein ensures a probable cause determination within 48 hours. If a trial happened within 48 hours, the probable cause hearing might be unnecessary, but that doesn’t mean the rights aren’t distinct.

Similarly, here, there’s one interest in getting a timely ultimate determination—that's what was at issue in Von Neumann and $8,850. But there's also a separate interest in retaining property during the time it takes to reach that determination. If a final ruling came in 48 hours, we wouldn’t need to worry about an interim hearing. But in reality, it takes much longer.

Judge: Counsel, it seems odd that we’re debating which precedent applies rather than focusing on what the Due Process Clause requires. It’s a strange framing of the question.

Also, while I recognize that some jurisdictions use civil forfeiture as a funding mechanism, where recovering property requires navigating difficult bureaucratic hurdles, that doesn’t seem to be the case in Alabama. Your client waited 13 to 18 months before filing for summary judgment. Why not file on day one? It’s an innocent owner defense—they knew the facts of their ownership. What delayed them, and how is that delay attributable to the state?

Plaintiff's Lawyer: I believe this is the right case for the Court to decide how to approach the question of whether a hearing is required. Whether we’re talking about the facts in Alabama, Wayne County, or hypothetical cases like you mentioned, the core methodological question remains the same.

Judge: But even if a hearing were required, couldn’t your client have secured one by filing a summary judgment motion immediately? If so, what are we doing here?

Plaintiff's Lawyer: There’s no guarantee that a summary judgment motion filed on day one would have been considered on an expedited basis. There’s no evidence that the court would have acted quickly.

Judge: But there’s no evidence either way, is there?

Plaintiff's Lawyer: Under Mathews, this raises the question of whether additional process would have had value. If, on remand, the state could show that summary judgment motions in Alabama are routinely granted in a matter of days, then perhaps an additional hearing wouldn’t be necessary.

Judge: Okay.

Defendant's Lawyer: And -- and I think the Court has addressed a similar context in the prison-type cases. Previous cases have recognized an underlying substantive constitutional interest, for example, in not being forced to take psychotropic drugs or not being removed to a mental facility, and yet it -- the way it balanced those interests was by providing a minimal level of procedural due process. And I think one of the points that is important to consider thematically here, Your Honor, is consular officers work very hard. They have very heavy caseloads. They're working under very difficult conditions. And what we're asking for is for them to give us enough information to help them make a decision that's going to foster the government's --

Judge: So, Mr. -- Mr. Lawyer --

Judge: But what if a -- just let me --

Judge: No, go ahead.

Judge: If I could follow up. What if an American citizen wants to have a child who is a non-citizen admitted to the United States? Would the same thing apply there? Or an American citizen wants to have a non-citizen parent admitted or an American citizen who doesn't believe in marriage wants to have a non-citizen admitted to the United States so that person can live in an intimate relationship with that person in the United States? What about all those situations?

Defendant's Lawyer: So the only person that's going to have both the -- and I want to take a half step back in answering that and try and define the interest precisely as well. The only person who's going to have an interest directly deprived is going to be a spouse. You have to have both an underlying liberty interest, which we get from the Constitution, and you have to have a direct deprivation, which we get from the statute. It totally distinguishes the facts here from previous cases, where the home care patients had no statutory role to play whatsoever in the certification process, et cetera. But -- so the point I'm trying to make is that -- and the Court understands that immigration law is quite complicated, I don't want to go on too long on this, but citizens can only petition immediate relatives and citizens have to be adults to petition immediate relatives. So the Court -- so all those other floodgates concerns are exaggerated because the only person who's going to both be statutorily involved enough to have been directly deprived and have an underlying liberty interest in marital cohabitation --

Judge: Well --

Judge: Mr. --

Judge: Well, is there a liberty -- do those people have a liberty interest? And if they do, then how can it be deprived by statute? How can it be taken away by a statute?

Defendant's Lawyer: So, for example, a grandparent and a grandchild, they certainly would have an underlying liberty interest. But they wouldn't be able to petition each other. So my point is that you have to have both these elements. And -- and that's going to be my answer to all the floodgates concerns that Your Honor posits because you need to both -- and there is no -- the Court has not recognized a substantive --

Judge: It's not a flood --

Defendant's Lawyer: -- liberty interest in adult cohabitation.

Judge: We just read your text, so you're a liar, and you lied to the police about that because I just read you your texts.

Defendant: Yeah, but I never said that—like, I stole her—like, I never even told her I stole your money.

Judge: I mean, well, you don’t need to say you stole your money. You were caught stealing her money, at which your text clearly reflects that she never gave you the money. So that means you went to the cookie jar, grabbed it, and took it.

Defendant: She did know, you know. She did know that I took it.

Judge: All parties, please raise your right hands. Next case, in the matter of the Plaintiff, Sandra Kwar, who is suing the Defendant, Dr. Ronald Juvas, a dentist, for dentures, claiming they do not fit. The Defendant states that he has been practicing for 37 years and believes the dentures are properly made. It is the case of the dentist who broke the mold.

Judge: Thank you. You're welcome. Sandra Kwar?

Plaintiff: Yes.

Judge: You are suing your dentist, Ronald Juvas, for $1,567, the amount you paid for dentures that you say do not fit.

Plaintiff: Yes.

Judge: What happened?

Plaintiff: I joined a dental plan back in January of 2011, and the Defendant was a participant on the plan. I contacted him because his location was close to where I live and work. On my first visit, we discussed the dentures and the price. I told him I have a very small mouth and asked if he could make the new dentures the same size as my current ones. He said no problem. That would never be a problem. But unfortunately, they are not.

Judge: Did you bring him the dentures you were wearing?

Plaintiff: I was wearing them.

Judge: So, he took them out and looked at them?

Plaintiff: Yes, I assume he did a mold of my mouth. He did two impressions, which he said was his standard practice to ensure accuracy. However, I feel the mold he used was too large for my mouth. When he took it out after the plaster settled, it did not seem correct.

Judge: What happened when you received the dentures?

Plaintiff: I went back at least five times. Each time, I told him I couldn’t wear them. He filed them down, but they were still too thick. The gum part was thicker than my previous dentures. He kept insisting I would adjust, but I couldn't. I felt like I had a mouthful of marbles. I was gagging when I ate, and there was no room for food in my mouth. My jaw didn’t close properly.

Judge: Did he acknowledge the issue?

Plaintiff: He said they were only off by two or three millimeters, which he claimed was within an acceptable range. But even a small difference in the mouth feels exaggerated. He kept saying I would adjust, but I never could.

Judge: Did you ever ask for your money back?

Plaintiff: No, because I felt it would have been a dead issue. He was convinced he had made a perfect set of dentures.

Judge: Do you have the dentures he made?

Plaintiff: Yes. What prompted me to do something about it now, a year later, is that I ran into a nurse who used to work in his office. She asked if I could wear the dentures. I told her no, and she said she never liked the forms he used to take impressions.

Judge: Can I see those dentures? Did you get new ones made by someone else?

Plaintiff: No, I am still wearing my old ones.

Judge: Okay. These are in a plastic bag. The ones with the red are the new ones?

Plaintiff: Yes. I also brought my old set for comparison.

Judge: Doctor, what’s going on?

Defendant: I followed all proper procedures, using the guidelines I teach at my residency program and the ADA recommendations. The Plaintiff came back for adjustments, which is normal. Dentures take time to adapt to.

Judge: How many times did she come back for adjustments?

Defendant: Five times.

Judge: In your answer to the complaint, you stated that she never complained about the dentures. Yet, she came back five times for adjustments. How is that possible?

Defendant: When fabricating dentures, there are many variables, including jaw size and a patient’s tolerance for change. Patients who have had prior dentures are the most difficult because they get used to their old ones, even if they are not in the proper position.

Judge: These dentures are much bigger. Why?

Defendant: When a patient has a lack of bone, we try to maximize retention. Adjusting too much too soon can create long-term instability.

Judge: Could you have done something different?

Defendant: I could have used a tissue conditioner, which is a gel-like material that adjusts to muscle movements. But she never asked for it.

Judge: Could you still fix them now?

Defendant: Yes, but she hasn’t worn them in over a year, and tissue changes over time.

Judge: Courts in recess.

(After recess)

Judge: She came back five times, and they still don’t fit. Should she get her money back?

Defendant: There are still adjustments that can be made.

Judge: But she has not gone to another dentist. That makes it hard for me to determine whether the dentures were improperly made or if she simply cannot adjust to new ones. There’s no independent verification that these are defective.

Judge: Are you willing to make further adjustments?

Defendant: Yes, but she must be willing to go through the process without wearing her old dentures during that time.

Judge: On your lawsuit to recover the full price of the dentures, I rule in favor of the Defendant. The original contract and guarantee still stand. You have the option to return to the Defendant for adjustments.

Judge: That is my verdict. Good luck.

Judge:
General, can I ask you a question about the state of medical evidence at the present time? In your petition, you made a sweeping statement, which I will quote: "Overwhelming evidence establishes that the appropriate gender-affirming treatment with puberty blockers and hormones directly and substantially improves the physical, psychological well-being of transgender adolescents with gender dysphoria." That was in November 2023.

Now, even before then, the Swedish National Board of Health and Welfare wrote the following: They currently assess "that the risks of puberty blockers and gender-affirming treatment are likely to outweigh the expected benefits of these treatments," which is directly contrary to the sweeping statement in your petition. After the filing of your petition, of course, we saw the -- the release of the Cass report in the United Kingdom, which found a complete lack of high-quality evidence showing that the benefits of the treatments in question here outweigh the risks.

And so I wonder if you would like to stand by the statement that you made in your petition or if you think it would now be appropriate to modify that and withdraw the statement that there is overwhelming evidence establishing that these treatments have benefits that greatly outweigh the risks and the dangers.

Lawyer of Defendant:
I, of course, acknowledge, Justice, that there is a lot of debate happening here and abroad about the proper model of delivery of this care and exactly when adolescents should receive it and how to identify the adolescents for whom it would be helpful.

But I stand by that there is a consensus that these treatments can be medically necessary for some adolescents, and that's true no matter what source you look at.

You mentioned both the Cass report and Sweden --

Judge:
Well, can be --

Lawyer of Defendant:
-- but neither of those jurisdictions --

Judge:
-- can be medically necessary for some minors.

But, for the general run of minors, do you dispute the proposition, in fact, that in almost all instances, the judgment at the present time of the health authorities in the United Kingdom and Sweden is that the risks and dangers greatly outweigh the benefits?

Lawyer of Defendant:
I --

Judge:
Do you dispute that?

Lawyer of Defendant:
-- I do dispute that because, if you actually look at how those jurisdictions are addressing this issue, they have not outright banned this care.

The Cass report says at multiple points that this care can be medically indicated for some transgender adolescents.

And, of course, it's true that they have called for a more individualized approach to these issues and have questioned whether it should be readily applied to all adolescents as a matter of course.

Judge:
Is it not --

Lawyer of Defendant:
But what that supports --

Judge:
-- is it not true that in England -- I -- I'm sorry to interrupt --

Lawyer of Defendant:
Yeah.

Judge:
-- but I -- time is running out -- that the National Health Service some months ago limited the prescription of puberty blockers to adolescent males who are over the age of 16 and are already on estrogen, but, for those who are under the age of 16, it's allowed only for experimental purposes? Is that not true?

Lawyer of Defendant:
So the approach in the U.K. right now is to allow hormone therapy for anyone 16 and older, and, with respect to puberty blockers, the U.K. has restricted new prescriptions outside of research settings.

But the Cass implementation plan itself makes clear that if a medical team determines that these medications are necessary for a particular patient, they will be provided.

And that is a --

Judge:
The restriction that I mentioned was imposed by the British government some months ago.

It was reaffirmed by the current Labour government, was it not? It was upheld by the High Court of Justice as based on sufficient medical evidence.

Isn't all of that true?

Lawyer of Defendant:
I believe that all of that's true.

It's outside the record in this case, and so I -- I haven't myself confirmed everything that you just cited, which wasn't before the district court in this case.

But let me make a couple of additional points.

To the extent that you think that this needs to be taken into account in the application of heightened scrutiny, there's a time and a place for that, and it's with record evidence on remand.

We think the Court here just needs to recognize the sex-based classification in this statute and send the case back.

If the Court wants to go ahead and look at what's happening in Europe, the U.K. has not categorically banned this care.

Sweden, Finland, and Norway, the other jurisdictions that my friends point to, have not banned this care, and I think that's because of the recognition that this care can provide critical, sometimes life-saving, benefits for individuals with severe gender dysphoria.

Judge:
Thank you, counsel.

Justice Thomas, anything further? Justice?

Judge:
In your opening brief, you did not mention any of these European developments.

And in your reply brief, is it true -- is it not true that you just relegated the Cass report to a footnote?

Lawyer of Defendant:
So, Justice, with respect to the developments, there has been no change in the law that I'm aware of in Sweden, Finland, and Norway.

Each of the medical authorities in those states has called for an individualized approach to care.

They've said it shouldn't be routinely applied.

But they have not changed their laws to do anything like what Tennessee is doing here, which is to categorically ban it no matter the need.

With respect to the Cass report, that isn't in the record in this case, but we have discussed that report in our reply brief, and, as I just noted, it likewise recognizes the need for this care on -- in individual cases.

The U.K. has not banned the care, and -- and Hilary Cass was not calling for such a ban.

Judge:
Your primary argument in the -- in your oral presentation this morning is based on Bostock-like reasoning, is that not correct?

Lawyer of Defendant:
I think that's incorrect.

Our primary argument is that this statute on its face says you can't have medications inconsistent with sex.

And no matter what you think about transgender discrimination generally, that's a sex-based line.

It's no different than saying you can't dress inconsistent with your sex.

My friends concede on page 25 of their brief that's obviously a facial sex classification.

But our primary argument is SB1 is worded exactly the same way and it works exactly the same way.

Judge:
Well, you have a Bostock-like argument, and you say that a -- a girl who wants to live like a boy cannot be administered testosterone, but a boy who wants to live like a boy can be administered testosterone.

So -- and that -- and that's one of your major arguments.

I take that to be a -- a Bostock-like argument. So my question is: Why should we look to Bostock here? Bostock involved the interpretation of particular language in a particular statute.

And this is not a question of statutory interpretation.

It's a question of the application of the Equal Protection Clause of the Fourteenth Amendment, and the Court has addressed the -- the question of how an equal protection claim should be analyzed when the law in question treats a medical condition or procedure differently based on a characteristic that is associated with just one sex.

And that was Geduldig in 1974, reaffirmed in Dobbs in 2022.

And neither Bostock nor Dobbs saw any connection between the Bostock reasoning and the Geduldig/Dobbs standard.

Bostock did not mention Geduldig, and Dobbs did not mention Bostock.

So why should we -- we look to this Bostock-type reasoning here?

Judge: All parties, please raise your right hands. Welcome back to The People’s Court. Next case on the docket: the plaintiff agreed to rent an apartment with her disabled son, but then the defendant said she couldn't move in with the son, and now he won't return her security deposit. The defendant says the plaintiff's the one who changed her mind. It’s the case of ‘Don’t Be Cruel.’

Judge: Thank you, Douglas. Francia Aosta, you are suing Martin Kagi for $1,765 in a security deposit plus a bounced check fee because he never let you move into the apartment, according to you. You say she changed her mind. Let me hear from you. Tell me what happened.

Plaintiff: This is the way it started. I was living at 20 Madison, and I had to move from there because they had to sell the house. So at the end of my lease, I told the landlord that I needed to look fast for an apartment. I started looking and found in the Penny Saver this gentleman. They were fixing the apartment. I gave him $1,000 and asked for a receipt, and this is what he gave me.

Judge: Okay.

Plaintiff: Right away, I sent a letter to my Section 8 housing office informing them that I wasn’t going to stay there and that I had found a place in Ridge. This is the paper proving that.

Judge: Okay, but did he know that you were on Section 8?

Plaintiff: Yes.

Judge: Did you know that she was on Section 8?

Defendant: Yes, I did.

Judge: So you were working with Section 8 to do the inspection or whatever else would have been necessary?

Defendant: That is correct.

Judge: Okay, go on.

Plaintiff: So I gave him $1,000, and later in the process, I gave him $700. When I met him for the first time, I told him that I had a disabled son who is bipolar and very sick.

Judge: On medication?

Plaintiff: Yes, he is.

Judge: How old is your son?

Plaintiff: He’s 24 now.

Judge: Okay.

Plaintiff: He agreed that it wouldn’t be a problem for my son and me to move in. After they did all the paperwork, the inspection had to be done in the place, and the inspection didn’t pass. I have proof of that.

Judge: Okay, and then what happened?

Plaintiff: Then it took like two months for paperwork.

Judge: How many months from the time that you wanted to move in to the time everything happened?

Plaintiff: It was a while.

Judge: That wasn’t my question. How many months?

Plaintiff: From March to June.

Judge: What happened in April?

Plaintiff: The process continued.

Judge: Okay, and then what happened?

Plaintiff: A second inspection was done, and it passed.

Judge: When was that?

Plaintiff: Probably in June.

Judge: Okay.

Plaintiff: After the inspection passed, he started asking about my son. He asked if I thought it was a good idea for my son to move in because he’s bipolar. He questioned me about my son’s condition. I told him the truth, that when my son is low on medication, he can get angry, but he is a sweet and clean person. Then he told me he was going to talk to ‘the commission.’

Judge: What commission?

Plaintiff: The board of directors at the condos.

Judge: Did your son have a history of violence?

Defendant: She told me he was locked up once or twice, put holes in walls, and we were really concerned.

Judge: When did you learn that?

Defendant: After about two months.

Judge: But why did you continue for four months?

Defendant: Because I liked her. I thought she would make a great tenant.

Judge: So what changed?

Defendant: The violence concerns.

Judge: If you changed your mind, why not return her money?

Defendant: I returned $1,500 of it but stopped after that.

Judge: Why?

Defendant: She wanted to take me to court over $250, so I told her to take me for the whole amount.

Judge: So she’s suing you for $1,765 plus a $15 stop-payment fee.

Judge: Can a landlord deny someone based on disability?

Defendant: I don’t think it’s right, but they can.

Judge: Actually, they cannot. That’s against the law. Verdict for the plaintiff, $1,765.

Defendant: I thought returning $1,500 was fair.

Judge: But you were the one who changed your mind, not her. Pay the full amount.

Court Officer: Case closed.

"Judge: This is the plaintiff, Elena Taria. She says she parked her car on the street and it got hit by the defendant's car at 5:00 in the morning. The defendant is claiming her car was stolen, but she believes the defendant was driving the car and is responsible for the damage to her car. Elena is suing for $5,000, the cost of her total car.
[Music]
Judge: This is the defendant, Lacy. She says her car was, in fact, stolen and evidently involved in an accident with the plaintiff's car. She's very sorry her car got damaged, but she wasn’t anywhere near the car. She’s tried to explain this to the stubborn woman, but she still insists on coming after her.
[Music]
Judge: All parties, please raise your right hands.
[Music]
Judge: Welcome back to the People's Court. Next case: the plaintiff, Elena, parked her car and claims the defendant smashed it at 5:00 a.m., but the defendant says her car was stolen, so it’s not her fault. It’s the case of ""I'm Smashed, Thanks to You."" Thank you, Elena Taria. You are suing Lacy for $5,000 for a car accident that you say was her fault. Tell me what happened.
Plaintiff (Elena Taria): I live in Far Rockaway, and I went to Astoria to my son after the mega storm because he didn’t have electricity.
Judge: You mean after Hurricane Sandy?
Plaintiff: Yes, I left my car there because there was a problem with parking due to flooding in Far Rockaway. On November 16th, my son called me at 8:00 a.m. and told me, ""Mama, your car is completely destroyed.""
Judge: Did he say how it was destroyed?
Plaintiff: Yes, another car, a Ford, struck the car in front of me and my car. The other car, a Honda, was completely destroyed, and the Ford went on the sidewalk and crushed a pole across the street.
Judge: How did you know all this? Did your son hear the accident?
Plaintiff: He heard a noise, but he didn’t go outside until the morning. When he saw the accident, he called me, then we went to the police in Astoria, and they gave us the police report.
Judge: Can I see the police report?
Plaintiff: Yes.
[Judge reviews the police report.]
Judge: According to the police report, the Ford hit your car and the car in front of you. The driver of the Ford left the scene of the accident and left the car behind.
Plaintiff: Yes, that’s right.
Judge: The Ford was gone by the time you got there, and the Honda was gone too. What year was your car?
Plaintiff: It was a 1992 BMW 525.
Judge: And how did you come up with $5,000 as the Blue Book value of that car?
Plaintiff: The value of my car is $2,500, and the stress I got from this accident is more than $5,000, but I couldn’t ask for more than $5,000.
Judge: So you understand that the Blue Book value of your car is not $5,000, right?
Plaintiff: No, I understand.
Judge: What happened here, Lacy?
Defendant (Lacy): My car was stolen.
Judge: When was your car stolen?
Defendant: I parked my car on November 15th. I live in Jamaica. On November 16th, around 4:00 a.m., I came out and my car was not there.
Judge: Where did you park it?
Defendant: I parked in a neighborhood driveway.
Judge: What does ""neighborhood driveway"" mean?
Defendant: It was just a driveway in my neighborhood. It’s hard for me to find parking because I have an expedition.
Judge: Were you parking in somebody else’s private parking lot?
Defendant: No, I was blocking half of someone’s driveway.
Judge: So the person who lives there knows you park there?
Defendant: Yes, she’s seen me park there before, but I always move it before 5:00 p.m.
Judge: So, you’re saying your car was stolen but it really wasn’t. Is this suspicious to you?
Plaintiff (Elena): Yes, definitely. She’s lying. I think it was towed because she was parked illegally.
Judge: How does that affect your credibility?
Plaintiff: It ruins it.
Defendant: I didn’t park illegally, but yes, I wasn’t parked in a proper space.
Judge: So, when you go outside and it’s gone, what happens?
Defendant: I called 911, and they told me it was too early to file a stolen car report. They said it was probably towed.
Judge: And then what happened?
Defendant: I called again, and they said my car was picked up by the sheriff's department.
Judge: Wait, were the Marshals or sheriffs involved?
Defendant: No.
Judge: It wasn’t stolen?
Defendant: No, it was picked up for illegal parking.
Judge: So, you're telling me that some mystery person was driving your car and crashed it, but it wasn’t you?
Defendant: Yes, that’s what happened.
Judge: Why didn’t you file a stolen car report right away?
Defendant: I didn’t know what to do. I thought it was a misunderstanding.
Judge: I find it suspicious that there’s no stolen car report until later.
Plaintiff: Exactly. If my car was stolen, I’d report it immediately.
Judge: Lacy, why didn’t you report it immediately?
Defendant: I didn’t know what happened until later.
Judge: The police report and the way things happened lead me to believe that you were involved in the accident. You didn’t file a stolen car report, and your story doesn’t add up.
Judge: I am ruling in favor of the plaintiff for $2,500, the Blue Book value of your car."

Judge:
Darcy and Heather, we're on a first-name basis. Darcy, you're suing Heather, your former landlord, for $1,400 that you want returned to you—the money that you paid for July rent for part of it, plus the last month which you paid at the beginning of going in there. You are counterclaiming against her for the remainder of July, the remainder of the last month, because according to you, she left after that, plus damages.

How long did you live in that apartment?

Plaintiff (Darcy):
I originally moved in on January 2017 on the third floor, but then I ended up moving down to the first floor.

Judge:
When did you move down to the first floor?

Plaintiff:
August of 2017.

Judge:
Okay, so you had been—and when did you move out?

Plaintiff:
End of July.

Judge:
You spent about a year down on the first floor, correct?

Plaintiff:
Yes.

Judge:
Alright, and why did you move from the third floor?

Plaintiff:
Because the third floor was infested with bed bugs and it had mice as well.

Judge:
When did it get infested?

Plaintiff:
Shortly after I moved in.

Judge:
You moved in in January and you moved to the bottom floor in August. How did you let her know that there were bed bugs?

Plaintiff:
I texted her.

Judge:
Okay, do you have the text from January of 2017 to show me that you texted her?

Plaintiff:
No.

Judge:
Do you have any emails?

Plaintiff:
No.

Judge:
When did she let you know there were bed bugs?

Defendant (Heather):
She probably let me know when she moved into number five, which was downstairs in August, but I would say probably a couple months after that.

Judge:
Okay, so according to you, she had never complained about bed bugs in January of 2017 when she was upstairs?

Defendant:
No, she did not.

Judge:
Do you have texts with her in your phone?

Defendant:
I have plenty of texts from her.

Judge:
Let's see if that’s from 2017 or 2018.

Defendant:
I mean, she did notify me of the bed bugs but not till much later.

Judge:
So these texts go back to September of 2017. Did you have a bed bug problem the entire time that you were living there?

Plaintiff:
Yes.

Judge:
The whole time?

Plaintiff:
Yes.

Judge:
How come you're not talking about it in your texts to her?

Plaintiff:
Some of it was verbal. I also have a letter from a state worker that came to my house from March of 2017 till August 20th of 2018 about what she witnessed while she visited my house weekly.

Judge:
Why were there state workers?

Plaintiff:
My daughter has behavioral issues, so I asked for them to come in and help with my family.

Judge:
How old is your daughter?

Plaintiff:
She’s nine.

Judge:
Do you know why she wanted to leave?

Defendant:
I just assumed it was because she had a child and she needed a bigger place to stay.

Judge:
Did she say why?

Defendant:
She told me July, I believe 12th, that she was leaving that month.

Judge:
Did she say she was moving out August 1st or August 2nd?

Defendant:
Yes.

Judge:
Was she on a month-to-month lease at that point?

Defendant:
Yes, she's always been on a month-to-month lease.

Judge:
When did you notify her that you were moving?

Plaintiff:
July 12th, 2018.

Judge:
And you left? You didn’t give her 30 days' notice?

Plaintiff:
No, I did not.

Judge:
But your feeling is you still shouldn’t have to pay rent because it was unlivable?

Plaintiff:
Yes.

Judge:
Okay, so let’s talk about the bed bugs. Who was living there?

Plaintiff:
Myself, my husband, and my two daughters, right before my son was born.

Judge:
Did you have an exterminator go over?

Defendant:
Yes, I did.

Judge:
May I see the receipt from the exterminator?

Defendant:
Yes, they went in March. They went six times.

Judge:
Do you have pictures?

Plaintiff:
Yes, I have pictures of bites and bed bugs.

Judge:
Were you there when the exterminator came on July 16th?

Plaintiff:
Yes.

Judge:
Did you show him any bed bugs?

Plaintiff:
Yes, and he said we had an infestation.

Judge:
You are coming to court today because you feel that the $500 you paid for July you shouldn’t have to pay?

Plaintiff:
Yes, she told me she was waiting for an equity loan to pay for the extermination, so I gave her $500 to help with that.

Judge:
Do you have an equity loan?

Defendant:
I don’t know about an equity loan. I was just trying to get rent from her.

Judge:
Did she leave damages?

Defendant:
Yes, she left a bed, a table, a mirror, and the refrigerator was dirty.

Judge:
Do you have pictures?

Defendant:
Yes.

Judge:
All right. I find that you are entitled to $200 for trash removal and $750 for painting, totaling $950. I find that you, Darcy, are entitled to $1,400. Subtracting the $950, that means a net judgment in your favor for $450. That is my verdict. Good luck, folks.

Judge: Justice Kagan?

Lawyer of Plaintiff: Elena Kagan.

And just on that, if I could get you to focus specifically on Mr. Clement's test and say what you think is wrong with it.

And, as I understood Mr. Clement's test, and he will tell me if I'm wrong, but that it's a kind of two-part test. And if it is both remote in time and place, space, and another agency has regulatory authority, then you can't fault the agency for not looking at it.

And so, again, Mr. Clement will tell me if I'm making his test more stringent than he would like it to be, but it's two parts.

If both are satisfied, you can't fault the agency. What's wrong with that?

Lawyer of Defendant: So two things.

Let me start with the other agency's authority piece first. I -- I think Justice Barrett brought out in a -- in a colloquy at the beginning about -- about whether agencies sometimes have concurrent authority.

And the answer is yes, they absolutely do in a lot of cases. And -- and therein, I think, lies the problem, that Mr. Clement's rule would say Agency Number 1 passed the -- passed the buck to Agency Number 2.

It's not clear that Agency Number 2 would then say we're going to do the study rather than pass the buck to someone else. And I think that that's directly in the teeth of NEPA and the -- the provisions for timely and unified federal review, like the one document provision at new Section 107(b), that all the agencies with expertise or jurisdiction, either one, are supposed to be participating in the -- in the NEPA review and to coordinate.

So that's one piece. The second piece you asked me about is the remote in time and -- and space point. And -- and I think, obviously, our test likewise -- the statutory test would likewise rule out some things that are remote in time or space, but just as foreseeability doesn't have a strict geographic line -- I think Mr. Clement's rule, by making it strictly geographic, that's not really tied to the concept of the project. Projects in our large federal government, you know, especially multi -- multibillion-dollar projects like this one, may well have effects that are felt beyond just next door.

And I think that the fact that Mr. Clement says that the only rail line between these -- between this railway and the Texas refinery, so, in other words, every railcar going to Texas or Louisiana, they're all going to come out of this railway, they're all going to turn left, and they're all going to go down the Union Pacific line, Mr. Clement says, well, that's -- that's too remote in time or space for our test to -- to bring it in. We think that the foreseeability test makes that a really good example.

It is very, very foreseeable that the rail cars that come off of Mr. Clement's 88 miles of track and turn left are going to cross that rail line.

And the agency itself foresaw that about once a year there's going to be an accident with an oil-laden car and about one out of every four of those is going to result in an oil spill.

Lawyer of Plaintiff: Thank you.

Judge: Justice Kavanaugh? Justice Barrett? Justice Jackson? Thank you, counsel.

Lawyer of Defendant: Thank you, Your Honor.

Judge: Rebuttal, Mr. Clement?

Lawyer of Plaintiff: Thank you, Mr. Chief Justice.

Just a few points in rebuttal. First, Justice Kagan, you understand our test perfectly.

And the reason it has both elements is to deal with this situation of concurrent jurisdiction or to deal with the situation where another agency has information that bears directly on the project. And that's why, if you look at the mitigation measures that were adopted, both the voluntary ones and the ones imposed, they talk about the other agencies that are involved. And other agencies were directly involved here.

The most obvious is the -- is the Forest Service that provided a right-of-way. And all of that took place in the cooperation and consultation process.

Twenty-seven agencies were consulted, and nobody's complaining about that.

That's why it's a disjunctive -- or that's why we have to have both elements there. But, if it's remote in time and space and it's in another agency's -- you don't put them in the consultation process, and you let them deal with the process. The second point I'd like to make is just to be specific about what's wrong with Sabal Trail, in addition to its decision to disregard FERC's judgment that the EIS there was enough. I mean, first of all, it decouples reasonable foreseeability from environmental effects, and that's what the Respondents do as well.

As long as it's reasonably foreseeable, it's foreseeable that it goes down the tracks 500 miles, never mind that once it gets there, the safety regulation is the job of a different agency; the tracks are no longer operated by my clients; the trucks, the -- the tanker trucks on there are owned by a third party. It's got nothing to do with us.

If there was an accident there, FRA would come in. They would talk to lots of people and they wouldn't talk to us because we have nothing to do with that down -- that downline accident. So that's one thing that's wrong.

The other thing that's wrong is it has this formula that if you think about it even a little bit, then it's foreseeable, then you have to study it to death.

That creates all the wrong incentives. The last point I would make is just about what's before you.

I think the entire environmental impact statement and whether or not you have to consider effects that are not proximate and are in the jurisdiction of another agency is properly before you. The question presented doesn't distinguish between downline and downstream.

I don't even know what that exactly means. There's not a stream here. So I would think you look at all of the things that -- that the D.C. Circuit faulted this agency for and the downline impacts are not materially different.

Indeed, both the refining in the Gulf and the extra traffic on the train tracks in Colorado depends on the idea that there's going to be more upstream development in the basin. This basin is not some small area. It's as big as the State of Maryland.

And all of that is speculative.

If a project happens and there are concerns with the vegetation or whatever, then either the Ute tribe or Utah can make a determination, they can impose their own mitigation measures. Just the last thing I'll say is, if you're worried about sort of trying to provide some additional detail to what's remote in space and time, I do think that the scope of the reasonable alternatives and the scope of the mitigation measures provides a good guide, but this is a common carrier agency. The one thing it could not do is say let's mitigate by saying no waxy oil in these trains.

It just doesn't make a lot of sense to make that agency responsible for combustion in the Gulf. Thank you, Your Honor.

Judge: Thank you, counsel. The case is submitted.

Defendant: "Well, because I just feel like if he can afford the psychic, then maybe he can afford the insurance, and I—I guess I feel that that is money that could have gone towards my education while we were married."

Judge: "That has nothing to do with me. I mean, you should have addressed that in your divorce, obviously. And whatever he pays a psychic, if he chooses to do that, that has nothing to do with your financial responsibility to your daughter."

Lawyer of Defendant: But I -- but I think it's quite clear, though, that if you're saying to a company: You have to stop talking unless somebody else does something, and that's imposed by the force of law, it directly affects that company's speech. That's --

Judge: Well, it's -- it's --it's --again, I don't -- I don't know if it's directly affecting the company's speech or the speech of third parties. And I'm not sure what -- you know, where your -- your emphasis is. But, again, I'm not sure there's another case where we've said that regulating a company has --should be --others' expression should be treated as direct imposition on their speech in terms of a standard of review, for example, when it's based on derivative regulation of corporate structure of somebody else.

Lawyer of Defendant: Well, Your Honor, I think that it's -- I --I would concede that this is a pretty unprecedented case. I'm not aware of any time in American history where the Congress has tried to shut down a major speech platform. But I -- I think that if a law imposes a -- a direct regulation on a third party that, in turn, results in shutting down somebody else's speech, and they do it for content-based, viewpoint-based reasons, and, in particular, on this record, because the speaker that is ultimately being shut down, they don't like the speech of that particular platform, that's a real problem. So --

Judge: Well, it may be a real problem or it may not. But I just am wondering if there's any precedent where we have that same connection and that it affects the standard of review. For example, you would treat it as a direct restriction on expression. Even the only thing the law does is say, in this case, somebody other than the Chinese government has to own the platform.

Lawyer of Defendant: So -- so -- so we don't have any direct precedent along the lines that you're citing, but we do have precedents. We have cases like Arcara, and what Arcara says is, if the law is totally speech-neutral, then that's one thing. We have cases like O'Brien, which say, if the law doesn't care about speech but happens to draw in speech, that's another thing. Both of those cases make clear, however, is that when the law is concerned with the content of the speech, when the justification is based on the content of the speech -- that's cases like Reed too -- then you do trigger strict scrutiny --

Judge: So then I think your argument comes down to: Is this direct concern with speech, or is it concern with the potential for foreign interference with the level of interference in --indirectly? In other words, they're not coming back --the foreign government -- the platform doesn't care what the people are saying on the platform. That's not the -- the concern. The concern is that they are regulating a particular channel of communication. And I just wonder if there's any precedent for that type of thing. They're not saying: We're going to restrict this content and that content but not this. They're just saying: We're going to be in a position where we can control what happens, whether it's based on expression, whether it's based on anything else.

Lawyer of Defendant: So, Your Honor, I disagree. And I think, if you take a step back and look at this record, I think it is quite clear that it is focused on both current and potential future content on the platform. Here, you don't have just an act that is based on speakers and speech. It's triggered by speech. It's focused on a single speech or the platform itself.

Judge: Thank you. Justice, anything further? Justice?

Justice: What if Congress -- if there were nothing in this Act about content moderation or covert manipulation? What if it was just about preventing what Congress viewed as an enormously powerful, popular application from gathering an arsenal of information about citizens, and they said: This is the worst offender and we're going to require divestiture by this offender? Would there be a First Amendment problem there? And if you think there would be, what would the level of scrutiny be?

Lawyer of Defendant: Yes, there would be a First Amendment problem if you had a law like this that was only focused on speakers, those who use user-generated content, other than product, travel, or business reviews, and --

Justice: Well, Congress concludes that this particular entity is the worst, this is the worst offender, and it happens to be an entity that is involved with speech.

Lawyer of Defendant: If all you had --so I want to make sure I understand the hypothetical. The only provision you have is one that says: This company has to shut down --

Justice: Right.

Lawyer of Defendant: -- because of data security.

Justice: Right.

Lawyer of Defendant: I would have a different set of arguments. I think it would still implicate the First Amendment, particularly where you have strong evidence that they were being targeted in part at least because of their speakers and speech. Suppose Congress passed the law that you posited --

Justice: Well, all right, but you're changing the hypothetical by -- by injecting congressional concern about the content of the speech.

Lawyer of Defendant: Okay. Well, I'll put that to the side.

Justice: So what would your argument be? It would be an equal protection argument --

Lawyer of Defendant: No. No. I'd still be saying --

Justice: -- based on rational basis? What --

Lawyer of Defendant: -- I'd still be saying that Arcara itself makes clear that where a law disproportionately burdens just a speaker, we have to subject that to scrutiny to suss it out, to suss out whether the asserted interest is the actual interest. There, the asserted interest is in data security. I think I would have a couple of arguments under whatever form of scrutiny you wanted to apply, whether it is strict scrutiny or intermediate scrutiny, in that context. I would say first that that law is dramatically under-inclusive because it categorically exempts e-commerce apps that this record shows have comparable ties to the foreign country --

Justice: All right. You say -- you say -- I don't want to prolong this too much. You --you say this is not like Arcara, I think primarily because you say that divestiture requires the new company to cease using the algorithm, right?

Lawyer of Defendant: No. I think it's not like Arcara for a much more fundamental sense. Arcara involved a totally speech-neutral law. It didn't go after speakers at all. If you had a law in Arcara that said we're going to prohibit prostitution in bookstores only, then I think that Arcara would have come out differently. There would have at least been, you know, some kind of intermediate scrutiny, potentially strict scrutiny.

Justice: All right. Well, you're -- you're continuing --

Lawyer of Defendant: That's the law that I think is your hypothetical.

Justice: -- you're continuing to walk away from the hypothetical that --

Lawyer of Defendant: I don't think so, Your Honor.

Justice: -- I proposed for the purpose of narrowing in on what your -- on what your argument is. My -- I understood you to say that it -- this -- that would not be a -- a solution to the problem because one of Congress's motivations was -- was the content -- was based on the content of the platform. Am I wrong in that? Did I read your argument incorrectly?

Lawyer of Defendant: Well, I think the -- I want to make sure I understand what you're saying. I certainly think that because one of the motivations was content, that is an enormously important fact. I was trying to answer your hypothetical where we were trying to take that out of the mix. And the reason why Arcara is different is because Arcara didn't just simply say no prostitution in bookstores. That's what your hypothetical effectively says. It says no data security problems in speakers or in this particular speaker. And I think that that would trigger at the very least intermediate scrutiny.

Justice: All right.

Lawyer of Defendant: And then --

Justice: Thank you.

Lawyer of Defendant: Thank you.

Judge:Norberto Valz, you are suing Mark Kintana, your former friend, for $48.50 that you say he owes you in various loans that you made to him throughout your friendship, that he apparently refuses to repay. According to you, tell me what's going on.

Judge: But you loaned him because?
Plaintiff (Norberto Valz): I loaned him because he needed a job and a security license to get the job.
Judge: So, he came to you and asked for money?
Plaintiff (Norberto Valz): Yes. He said if I fronted the money, he would pay me back when he got the job.
Judge: And did he get hired?
Plaintiff (Norberto Valz): No, because my boss didn't hire him. He said he needed to pay child support, and that turned my boss off.
Judge: Why?
Plaintiff (Norberto Valz): Because he was behind on child support.

Defendant (Mark Quana): Not behind, I wasn’t on child support yet.
Judge: So why did you mention it?
Defendant (Mark Quana): I said it because I was about to be put on it.
Judge: But you're paying money to your baby mom, right?
Defendant (Mark Quana): Yes.
Judge: But you're not paying child support.
Defendant (Mark Quana): No.
Judge: Why don’t you have a driver’s license?
Defendant (Mark Quana): I have a bad driving record.
Judge: How bad?
Defendant (Mark Quana): I had 18 points in less than three months.
Judge: You must have ignored the summons or didn’t pay the tickets.
Defendant (Mark Quana): Yes, that's about right.
Judge: How do you get 18 points?
Defendant (Mark Quana): By being irresponsible.
Judge: You’re still working, though.
Defendant (Mark Quana): Yes, for a pool company my family owns.
Judge: Do you drive?
Defendant (Mark Quana): No, I get picked up.
Judge: So, you didn't get hired.
Plaintiff (Norberto Valz): He didn’t get hired, and he didn’t pay me back.
Judge: But then you continue to loan him more money.
Plaintiff (Norberto Valz): Yes, because he promised to pay me back once he started working.
Judge: But he didn’t pay you back the last time. Why trust him again?
Plaintiff (Norberto Valz): Because I trust people.
Judge: But that’s how you get burned.
Plaintiff (Norberto Valz): I have a good heart.
Judge: Trust is good, but you should protect yourself, too.
Plaintiff (Norberto Valz): Yes, you're right.
Judge: Now, he needed $105 for child support.
Plaintiff (Norberto Valz): Yes, I loaned him that.
Judge: So, now we're up to $350.
Plaintiff (Norberto Valz): Yes.
Judge: And you gave him $20 for gas, $10 for beer, and $10 for cigarettes.
Plaintiff (Norberto Valz): Yes, he asked for it, and I gave it to him.
Judge: You loaned him money to drive him to meet girls.
Plaintiff (Norberto Valz): Yes, I did.
Judge: Were you socializing?
Plaintiff (Norberto Valz): No, I stopped hanging out with him after he started harassing me.
Defendant (Mark Quana): It started when my friend, his cousin's niece, introduced us. He promised me a job, and when I didn’t get it, I couldn't pay him back.
Judge: So, you didn’t get the job?
Defendant (Mark Quana): No.
Judge: And he gave you money for that?
Defendant (Mark Quana): Yes. He fronted the money for a security license.
Judge: Did you get the job?
Defendant (Mark Quana): No, because of my driving record.
Judge: Why didn’t you get your license back?
Defendant (Mark Quana): I didn’t deal with it properly.
Judge: You’ve seen this situation before, haven't you?
Defendant (Mark Quana): Yes, but I needed the help.
Judge: You owe him the money.
Defendant (Mark Quana): I feel bad, but I don't owe him all the amount he claims.
Judge: You have no proof of what you owe.
Defendant (Mark Quana): No, I never kept track of the loan.
Judge: The plaintiff has written it all down.
Defendant (Mark Quana): I’m sorry, I wasn’t organized.
Judge: Well, based on the evidence, I believe the plaintiff’s version.
Judge: I find in favor of the plaintiff in the amount of $48.50.
Defendant (Mark Quana): No reaction.
Judge: Don't you think he’s been through enough?
Defendant (Mark Quana): I guess so.
Judge: What do you think now, Norberto?
Plaintiff (Norberto Valz): I'm happy. I learned my lesson and I’m never loaning money again.
Judge: What about Pay It Forward?
Plaintiff (Norberto Valz): It didn’t work for me, but good luck.
Judge: When you loan or borrow money, keep a ledger, or you’ll lose.
Judge: That will do it for this case. Next case on the way into the courtroom.

Judge: Okay, Janice, you've asked to be referred to only as Janice?

Plaintiff: Yes.

Judge: You are suing Albert George Schubert II?

Plaintiff: Okay, sorry. So what happened, um, well apparently what happened was I bought this car.

Plaintiff: So, it was like, first, it was like between me and my mother because I recently came from Puerto Rico due to the Hurricane Maria disaster.

Plaintiff: So I was like in a very desperate situation, like I was like, "Whatever car for like the amount of money I have, like, I want."

Plaintiff: So my mom reached out to me—I came to PA and everything.

Plaintiff: My mom reached out to me. She's like, "Oh, I talked to this guy, like he seems very legitimate. He seems like he, you know, is selling this car."

Plaintiff: It was a 2003 Pontiac Aztek.

Plaintiff: And, um, the original price for it was like $2,800.

Plaintiff: I'm 23 years old.

Judge: Okay. So you buy the car. Did you have it looked at by a mechanic before you bought it?

Plaintiff: Yes, ma'am.

Plaintiff: Yes, ma'am.

Plaintiff: No, ma'am.

Judge: Was this your car?

Defendant: Yes.

Judge: The rules don't change because you feel desperate at that moment.

Judge: You know, it's just how it is.

Judge: So, you buy the car, and what's the problem?

Plaintiff: That—talking about—it has to do with, um, him practically selling me the car.

Plaintiff: Like, this is the proof that he sold—

Defendant: I've never even seen that until—

Judge: Al Schubert declares the vehicle, Pontiac Aztek 2003 sold, has no transmission or motor issues.

Judge: Yes.

Defendant: There was a problem with brakes. I bought her brakes.

Defendant: She never showed up.

Manipulation Techniques: evasion, emotional appeal, playing the victim

Dialogue:
Judge: Okay, Chen Paul, correct?

Plaintiff: Yes, correct.

Judge: Okay. You’re suing Trey for $200 that you say you loaned to him. He was your bartender at your restaurant. You have a Thai restaurant, correct?

Plaintiff: Yes.

Judge: Okay, and where is that?

Plaintiff: That is in Massachusetts.

Judge: Okay, and he was your bartender, and you made him a loan, and he never repaid the loan. You have a counterclaim against him for your last two weeks’ paycheck, which he wouldn’t give you because he says you didn’t pay the loan. What happened here? Why were you loaning him money?

Plaintiff: He approached me, asking for some help. He said he was short on money. So instead of giving him cash, I wrote him a check. The first check was for $160, and later he said he needed a little more, so we agreed on another $40, making it a total of $200. And he promised to pay me back after he got his refund from the IRS.

Judge: Okay, and I do see that there are some checks written to you. Did you receive that?

Defendant: Yes, Your Honor.

Judge: And were you supposed to pay that back?

Defendant: Yes, Your Honor.

Judge: And did you?

Defendant: No, I did not.

Judge: All right. So, you worked there until when?

Defendant: Until May.

Judge: And what happened in May?

Defendant: In May, we decided to go our separate ways.

Judge: Why is that?

Defendant: Because he did not give me the benefit of the doubt after the six to seven months of goodwill and hard work I put in—being on time, being professional, not just being a bartender but a mixologist. I did a lot for this man, and he promised me. The bar manager actually promised me that I’d get busy nights—Thursday, Friday, and Saturdays. I never got them.

Judge: Okay.

Defendant: I complained to her about it. The bar manager said she would take care of it. She never addressed it.

Judge: Who was getting the busy nights?

Defendant: The women.

Judge: Okay. Because the theory is that the men are drinking there, and they’ll buy more drinks from a woman?

Defendant: That’s right.

Judge: Okay. So, you approached the plaintiff and let him know how you felt?

Defendant: Yes. He wrote on a piece of paper, "Is it pride, or is it money?" I said, "It’s always about the money. Pride doesn’t pay bills."

Judge: Okay.

Defendant: So, he said he would give me $30 extra on top of my hourly rate and gratuities for every Sunday I worked.

Judge: Meaning he’d give you another $30 every Sunday?

Defendant: That’s right.

Judge: Is that accurate?

Plaintiff: That was not accurate.

Judge: What is accurate?

Plaintiff: It was not for Sundays.

Judge: What was it for?

Plaintiff: I forgot what it was. It was a long time ago.

Judge: All right. So, according to you, you were supposed to get another $30 every Sunday. Were you getting it?

Defendant: The first Sunday I got it.

Judge: Okay.

Defendant: The second Sunday, I saw it coming down the pike—he started to change up. He got a little funky with me.

Judge: How so?

Defendant: He said, "I’m not going to pay you at the end of the shift. You have to wait until the next bartender comes on to make sure the bar is left clean."

Judge: Okay.

Defendant: The next time I got my Sunday pay, at the end of the night, I went to serve him a beer. I grabbed one out of the cooler, and by accident, I tipped one of the beers over, and it fell and broke. He charged me $10 for the beer.

Judge: Get out of here.

Defendant: I swear on everything I love.

Judge: You charged an employee $10 for a beer? You didn’t even charge customers that much?

Plaintiff: It happened one time only because I didn’t like what he had been doing at the restaurant.

Judge: What was he doing?

Plaintiff: He left customers stranded without drinks for quite some time after appetizers and food had been served.

Judge: And did you say something to him?

Plaintiff: I couldn’t say anything because he ran away from me.

Judge: You’re the owner of the restaurant—you can find him.

Plaintiff: Yes, but it wasn’t the right time. But later, I had enough, and I let him go.

Judge: Oh, you let him go?

Plaintiff: Yes, I let him go.

Judge: Okay. So, according to you, the first Sunday you got paid. What happened the second Sunday?

Defendant: The second Sunday, he gave me $20 because he charged me $10 for the beer.

Judge: And then what happened the third Sunday?

Defendant: Didn’t get any money after that.

Judge: How many Sundays did you work after that?

Defendant: Seven Sundays.

Judge: And during those times, were you asking, "Hey, where’s my $30?" What did he say?

Defendant: He said he wasn’t giving it to me.

Judge: Okay. So when did he change his mind about giving it to you?

Defendant: Halfway through.

Judge: When did he change his mind? Do you remember or no?

Defendant: He never actually came and said no. The bar manager told me, "We’re not paying you extra money."

Judge: Okay. And when did the bar manager tell you that?

Defendant: The third or fourth Sunday.    Judge: Okay. So at that point, why did you keep working Sundays if you weren’t going to get the extra money?

Defendant: Because I loved my job, Your Honor. I loved being a mixologist. I loved creating drinks.

Judge: But you were mad about not getting paid what you thought you were owed?

Defendant: Yes.

Judge: And you claim you worked a total of seven Sundays that should have been paid at an extra $30 per Sunday?

Defendant: That’s right.

Judge: And that’s why your counterclaim is for $210?

Defendant: Yes, plus the $15 he deducted for the beer, making it $225.

Judge: Okay, let’s get back to the plaintiff’s claim. You agree that you took the $200 as a loan and that you never repaid it?

Defendant: Yes.

Judge: Okay, so why shouldn’t you have to pay it back?

Defendant: Because I believe that he owes me the money from the Sundays I worked, and that offsets the amount.

Judge: Okay. So basically, what you’re asking me to do is to say, "You owe me, and I owe you, so let’s call it even."

Defendant: Yes, Your Honor.

Judge: Let’s talk about the Sundays. Did you ever agree to pay him an extra $30 per Sunday?

Plaintiff: No. I did not make that agreement.

Judge: So you’re saying he was working Sundays at his regular pay?

Plaintiff: Yes.

Judge: Do you have any evidence—texts, messages, anything—where he acknowledges this extra payment arrangement?

Defendant: No, Your Honor. It was a verbal agreement.

Judge: But you do acknowledge you took a loan and didn’t repay it?

Defendant: Yes.

Judge: Okay, well, here’s the problem. You have no proof that this extra $30 per Sunday was ever promised. It’s just your word against his. But there is written proof—your checks—that he loaned you $200, and you admitted you haven’t paid it back.

Defendant: Yes.

Judge: So, I have no choice but to rule in favor of the plaintiff for the $200.

Defendant: What about my counterclaim?

Judge: You didn’t prove the extra pay agreement, so I’m denying your counterclaim.

Judge: Judgment for the plaintiff in the amount of $200.

Judge: Welcome back to the People's Court. Next case on the docket, the plaintiff says the day after she bought a car from the defendant, it died. But the defendant says it was a 1999 Cougar and in fine shape. It's the case of the Cougar that...

Defendant: I told her it was costing $400 to repair the starter. I told her I sold the car to her at cost and if she wouldn’t mind paying 50% of it.

Judge: And she said?

Defendant: And she said, "Okay, she will do it."

Judge: Did she ever say to you, "No, I guess I just want my money back"?

Defendant: No.

Judge: Did you ever say to him, "Forget it, I don’t want to go through with the deal"?

Plaintiff: I said to him, "What are my other options?"

Judge: That’s not what your complaint says, though. Are you trying to make the case better now?

Plaintiff: No, ma'am.

Judge: Okay. So go on from there. You decide you're going to pay half the cost of the starter, and then what happened?

Plaintiff: I picked up the car Monday the 6th from the auto mechanic. We drove it. On the 7th, the 8th, on Thursday, the check engine lights come on. I called him and I said, "The check engine lights are coming on, there’s a grinding sound when I start the car." He’s like, "Okay, you know there’s a one-year warranty on the starter, bring it to me if there are any problems."

Judge: And did you bring it back?

Plaintiff: I parked the car in my driveway Friday. I go to start the car, and the car does not start at all. I call him, leave a message at 10:00 at night expecting to get a phone call back from him the following day. It’s 1:00 in the afternoon, no phone call, nothing. I call him. "Jeff, the car won’t start." And he’s like, "Well, what’s wrong with it?" I said, "I’m not a mechanic." He’s like, "Well, what do you mean it won’t start?" I said, "Exactly, it does not start."

Judge: Did you have it towed back?

Plaintiff: Yes, on the 13th.

Judge: And what happened next?

Defendant: I had a mechanic look at it, checked everything, and the car was fine. I returned it to her house.

Judge: Did you tell her you were dropping it off?

Plaintiff: No. He left my keys in my mailbox.

Judge: Did you hear back from her after that?

Defendant: Yes, she left a note listing new complaints about the car.

Judge: And?

Defendant: The transmission slipping, a rumbling sound, check engine light. I checked, added antifreeze, drove it up the block, no issues.

Judge: The state law requires certain warranties for used cars, but with over 100,000 miles, only safety at the time of sale is required. Did you have any additional warranty?

Plaintiff: No.

Judge: Then your claim for a refund doesn’t hold. You assumed risks with an old car. Case dismissed.
Manipulation Techniques: gaslighting, evasion, deflection, playing the victim

Dialogue:
Narrator: This is the plaintiff, Allan Hayatt. He says he parked his car outside the VA hospital, and when he came out, his bumper was damaged. The car behind him had a damaged bumper too, and it was white—just like the scratches on his car. The 89-year-old defendant bumped into him when he was parking his car but won’t pay him the $2,113 worth of damage he caused, so he’s suing.

[Music plays]

Narrator: This is the defendant, David Lieberwitz. He says he was visiting the VA hospital with his wife, and when he came out, the plaintiff was taking pictures of his car—and then started taking pictures of him! The guy thinks his car is the only white car in the world and, therefore, he must have caused the damage. He did no such thing and thinks the judge will concur. He’s accused of bumping a guy the wrong way.

[Music plays]

Bailiff: All parties, please raise your right hands.

Narrator: Welcome back to The People’s Court. Next case on the docket: The plaintiff says he knows the 89-year-old driver hit his car in the VA parking lot. But the defendant says the plaintiff is dreaming it up. It’s the case of Car Wars.

Judge Marilyn Milian: Allan Hayatt?

Plaintiff (Allan Hayatt): Yes, ma’am.

Judge Milian: You are suing David Lieberwitz for $2,113 to fix your car because, according to you, Mr. Lieberwitz hit your car.

Plaintiff: Correct.

Judge Milian: Tell me what happened.

Plaintiff: I parked my car outside the Veterans Hospital on 7th Avenue in Brooklyn. I had an appointment. When I came out, there was damage to the back of my bumper. It was gouged and scratched and slightly ajar. I had checked the car before I went in—I always check the car when I park it—and there were no damages on the bumper.

Judge Milian: Okay. Was Mr. Lieberwitz’s car parked behind yours?

Plaintiff: It was parked behind me when I returned.

Judge Milian: When you returned, was he in the car?

Plaintiff: No, he was not.

Judge Milian: Okay, so what did you do?

Plaintiff: Well, I waited for him to get into his car to make sure it was the right party. I came over to the window and told him that he struck the car in front of him.

Judge Milian: What did he say?

Plaintiff: He said, "I didn’t." I said, "Well, come out and take a look at what you did." He came out, and at first, he saw nothing. I pointed it out to him.

Judge Milian: Now, there was damage to your car that you say was fresh?

Plaintiff: That’s correct, ma’am.

Judge Milian: And by fresh, you mean what?

Plaintiff: Fresh meaning that there was paint on the bumper that wasn’t there when I parked the car.

Judge Milian: Can I see the damage to your car? Do you have pictures?

Plaintiff: Yes, I do.

[Plaintiff hands over pictures.]

Judge Milian: So, did you show that damage to him?

Plaintiff: I pointed it out to him. At first, he didn’t see it and just scoffed it off as something he didn’t do.

Judge Milian: Oh, wait—his bumper’s off. Is this your car?

Plaintiff: I’m the gray car. He’s the white car.

Judge Milian: His bumper is ripped off.

Plaintiff: Right, but I don’t believe that it’s ripped off on the other side. It’s ripped off on the left side. This damage is on the right side—on my left, his right.

Judge Milian: So that bumper came off some other way.

Plaintiff: Probably.

Judge Milian: Oh, Lord.

Defendant (David Lieberwitz): I came over to my car, walked with my wife to my car, and he was taking pictures, movies. I said, "What are you taking movies about?" He says, "You banged into my car."

Judge Milian: What did you say?

Defendant: I said, "I never touched your car. Never."

Judge Milian: Did you see him hit your car?

Plaintiff: I didn’t see him, but I know he did it.

Defendant: He didn’t see me, but I did it? How does that make sense?

Judge Milian: I’m looking at the damage to his car and then looking at your car… scratches.

Defendant: Two little scratches! You could barely see them.

Judge Milian: No, I can see them pretty clearly, sir. These are the pictures of the damage to his car. What year is your car?

Defendant: 2010.

Judge Milian: 2010 what?

Defendant: Toyota Corolla.

Judge Milian: Did you hit his car?

Defendant: No!

Judge Milian: Sir, your bumper is hanging off. How did that happen?

Defendant: I had an accident about two weeks before this happened, but believe me, the bumper never touched him.

Judge Milian: Here’s a picture he took of the damage on the right side of your car, which is gray in color—the color of his car.

Defendant: But that has nothing to do with this!

Judge Milian: So you've been driving for how long?

Defendant: I’m 89 years old and I’ve been driving since I was 17.

Judge Milian: Okay, do you think there should be an age cut-off for drivers?

Spectator: Yes, I would say about 88 to 90.

Judge Milian: What about you?

Spectator 2: I agree, 88 to 90.

Judge Milian: Anybody think there should be no age cut-off?

Spectator 3: My aunt is 94, still driving a 1969 El Camino just fine. The problem is she doesn’t know where she’s driving!

[Laughter]

Judge Milian: Sir, you have to admit that as people get older, their reflexes aren’t the same. If I have his car with no damage, and I have no reason to disbelieve him, and I have fresh paint on both cars, what am I supposed to conclude?

Defendant: Throw the case out!

Judge Milian: That’s not happening today. Maybe you should retake the driving test. My dad did. Then he walked into the house, slammed his new license on the table, and told me to shut up!

[Laughter]

Judge Milian: Based on what I have in front of me, I find in favor of the plaintiff for the full $2,113. Verdict for the plaintiff.

[Music plays]

Reporter: Sir, the judge ruled against you. What do you think?

Defendant: She’s wrong! There were no witnesses. Nobody saw it.

Reporter: Should you still be driving?

Defendant’s Wife: I can’t say anything…

Reporter: What about taking the driving test again?

Defendant: … (inaudible)

Reporter: Alright. Thank you.

Reporter: Sir, you won the case. How do you feel?

Plaintiff: I feel justice was served.

Defendant's Lawyer: As to the different occasions-type question before the Court in this case, there were zero states before 1929 that allowed a judge, instead of a jury, to make such a finding. Amicus has gathered four states that argue against overruling Almendarez-Torres, along with a handful of other states with minor findings that are not related to offense-related conduct, which is the issue here.

Amicus’s only substantial argument relates to determining the date of a prior conviction to establish that it was a second conviction or similar. However, that pertains to the inherent nature of the conviction, not the offense-related conduct. This distinction makes this case straightforward under Descamps and Mathis, whether based on statutory interpretation or historical principles.

Now, regarding Descamps and Mathis, the Court has been clear. In Mathis, the Court stated that one reason for applying the categorical approach was to address "serious Sixth Amendment concerns." In fact, the Court went even further in Mathis, stating unequivocally that any facts beyond the elements of the prior offense—when related to the conduct of that offense—must be determined by a jury and cannot be decided by a judge.

Judge: May I ask—

Judge: Mr. Counsel, could you address the history and tradition supporting your argument? A previous question suggested it would be unfair to place the burden on amicus to establish history and tradition. Could you elaborate on the jury’s role in fact-finding regarding recidivism?
