Defendant's Lawyer: Certainly. As outlined in our brief, even for prior convictions themselves, the prevailing practice was for juries to make these determinations. This was the established common law rule, and I don’t believe there is any dispute over that.

For any other facts that amicus identifies, the same principle applies. Additionally, the occasions inquiry in this case emerged from recidivism statute reforms in the 1960s, meaning there is no direct historical precedent for it. This makes it amicus, rather than us, who is deviating from tradition by allowing a judge to determine this fact rather than a jury.

Judge: One more question. Do you agree with the government’s amicus that a harmless error analysis would apply?

Defendant's Lawyer: Yes, I believe Neder dictates that harmless error would apply in these cases. While this case may affect some pending cases in lower courts, once this rule is established, I don’t foresee it being a major issue going forward.

Judge: May I ask—

Judge: Please, go ahead.

Judge: If there is a historical tradition of juries determining recidivism-related facts, as your response to the earlier question suggested, then what is the justification for the Almendarez-Torres carveout? Why does it exist?

Defendant's Lawyer: I believe the Court’s jurisprudence justifies it on two grounds. First, in Almendarez-Torres, the Court referenced a tradition of judicial fact-finding in cases involving prior convictions. However, that tradition is relatively recent and does not align with the type of historical precedent the Court typically considers today.

Second, as the Court explained in Apprendi, when dealing solely with the fact of a prior conviction—the elements and nothing more—there are procedural safeguards, including the right to a jury trial during the original adjudication. These safeguards do not apply to other types of fact-finding, like the one at issue here.

Judge: Thank you, counsel.

Defendant's Lawyer: Thank you, Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court: As counsel has made clear, Petitioners view the Section 1 exemption as encompassing any worker directly involved in a good's interstate journey, from the plant worker who loads goods for shipment to the store clerk who unloads them and shelves them.

But, in Circuit City, this Court said that the Section 1 exemption should be read narrowly and should be interpreted with reference to the ejusdem canon, context, and history, all three of which demonstrate that the exemption is limited to transportation industry workers.

After all, in 1925, seamen and railroad employees were defined by the industry in which they work. And that commonality should carry through to the residual clause.

Context and history tell you why this line makes sense. By 1925, Congress knew that labor disputes involving transportation industry workers were different. They were unique. They could cause famines in Chicago.

And in response, Congress passed two and only two federal arbitration statutes, one governing railroad employees in the rail industry and one governing seamen, who, under the Shipping Commissioners Act, were limited to those in the shipping industry.

Petitioners can't provide a "why" for the enumeration. They can't explain why you would pair railroad employees and seamen together.

And they advocate a definition of "seamen" that is so broad, it's flatly inconsistent with the notion of a transportation worker and this Court's holding in Circuit City.

The result: a poor fit. And Petitioners show by example.

Petitioners buy Flowers' bread. They pay Flowers for the product. Then they take title to the bread, and it is only after they take title to the bread that they then move it intrastate in order to sell it to retailers for a profit.

They are under no personal obligation to move anything. They look nothing like railroad employees or seamen. I welcome the Court's questions.

Judge: We have looked at the performance of the workers in Saxon, and wouldn't it complicate matters now to look at the entire industry, as certainly the district court did and the Second Circuit did?

Defendant's Lawyer: I don't think so, Your Honor.

Judge: And don't you think—I mean, I thought we foreclosed that.

We said that we won't look. The argument—part of the argument in Saxon was, well, she, Saxon, is in the transportation industry, therefore.

And as I hear you, you're saying, well, Petitioner here is not in the transportation industry, therefore.

And we foreclosed that, I thought, in Saxon.

Defendant's Lawyer: So two points, Your Honor.

The first was that you have to read those holdings in Saxon in light of the background fact that Ms. Saxon was an airline transportation industry worker.

The Court presupposed that fact. And as was read from the oral argument, that was accepted fact and part of the background on which the holding was made.

The second point is the industry-wide holding, and in that part of the Court's opinion, the Court was rejecting Ms. Saxon's argument that it was sufficient for her to fall within the Section 1 exemption just because she was a transportation industry worker.

And our argument is not that it's sufficient.

We think that you have to do the Saxon analysis, but the first question is, is being in the transportation industry necessary?

And the answer to that should be yes, because, ever since 1972 in the Second Circuit, the background rule has been that you have to be in a transportation industry.

That's the Erving decision that predates Circuit City and was on the winning side of the Circuit City split.

Judge: So we have cases from the 1920s in which you didn't have to be in the transportation industry in order to be counted as a seaman.

So how do you square your position with that?

Defendant's Lawyer: First, I think Saxon informs what it means to be a seaman, but those cases aren't dealing with the limit here, which is you already have Circuit City, and Circuit City has already held that ejusdem—because of the ejusdem canon, there are implied limits.

And one of those implied limits is it's not a limitless seaman.

It's the seamen who are transportation workers.

And I think that's where Petitioners' definition gets in trouble because Petitioners freely admit that their seamen are pirates, they're enemy ship folks, they're on recreational boats—

Judge: I understand that.

Judge: But can I—

Judge: But how do you square that with cases where we have actors aboard a ship being counted as seamen, for example?

Defendant's Lawyer: Most of those are Jones Act cases, and—

Judge: Well, why does that matter, when Congress was using the word "seamen" as I'm sure it was understood at the time that statute was passed?

Defendant's Lawyer: Two reasons.

The first is the Jones Act has a broad remedial purpose, and this Court has repeatedly recognized in the Jones Act context that it's reaching to the outer limit of seamen.

The second is that there's no other federal statute that uses railroad employees and seamen together, and Circuit City says that that list has meaning and that list means that Section 1 seamen are different from other seamen.

They share a commonality with railroad employees.

And this Court held in Circuit City that that commonality is transportation workers.

Judge: Well, but commonality can get very complicated, as the other side said.

I mean, where did the price structure and revenue approach come from?

Defendant's Lawyer: That was part of the Second Circuit's decision and—

Judge: Yeah, but where did they get it?

Defendant's Lawyer: I think the Court was looking to characteristics of folks in the transportation industry and giving a more granular approach to what are common characteristics on the facts of this case.

And, again, these facts aren't disputed.

Judge: No, no, but I mean, they're not—they're trying to figure out what the transportation industry is.

Defendant's Lawyer: Mm-hmm. Right.

Judge: Okay, all parties please raise your right hands. Welcome back to The People's Court. Next case on the docket, the Plaintiff says her former boyfriend is stiffing her on furniture and other items she bought for him. To add insult to injury, he's using this stuff with his new girlfriend. But the Defendant says he still loves the Plaintiff. It's the case of No Love with a Love Seat.

Judge: Hope Flanigan, you are suing your former boyfriend, Michael Pendergrass, for $496.29. You say he owes you for numerous things you bought for your apartment with him and that he has. Tell me what's going on.

Plaintiff: We met in July of 2018.

Judge: How did you meet?

Plaintiff: On Facebook, through mutual friends.

Judge: Uh-huh.

Plaintiff: And it was love at first sight. And then we moved in in August of 2018.

Judge: You moved in with a guy you'd known for how long?

Plaintiff: A month.

Judge: Seriously?

Plaintiff: Yeah.

Judge: Do you do that often?

Plaintiff: No.

Judge: Okay, how'd it work out for you?

Plaintiff: We broke up in December.

Judge: Oh, so not too well, right? And did you move from somewhere?

Plaintiff: Yes, ma'am. I moved from Georgia.

Judge: You didn't just move in together. You moved from one state to another state to move in together, correct?

Plaintiff: Correct.

Judge: So you move in together.

Plaintiff: And I acquired household furniture and items.

Judge: But didn't you have stuff?

Plaintiff: No, I did not.

Judge: But where were you living before that?

Plaintiff: With my parents.

Judge: Okay, had you never moved out? You never had your own stuff, or you just didn't at this time?

Plaintiff: I was married before.

Judge: Oh, okay. What happened?

Plaintiff: Well, it was a volatile situation.

Judge: What was a volatile situation?

Plaintiff: He had jealous rages.

Judge: He does?

Plaintiff: He does. Because of his previous marriage.

Judge: Okay, so?

Plaintiff: And he was going through my phone and causing problems.

Judge: So how was he causing problems?

Plaintiff: He was accusing me of cheating.

Judge: Okay.

Plaintiff: When I wasn't cheating. And I found a friend that I was friends with in high school 25 years ago, and we were talking about our old high school friends. But there was no cheating involved whatsoever.

Judge: But that friend crossed the line by saying some things he shouldn't have said?

Plaintiff: Oh, you mean he found some nasty texts or something?

Judge: No?

Plaintiff: Uh, yes.

Judge: Oh, okay. So he gets mad, and then you guys break up. Tell me about the furniture. What happens? Do you move out of the apartment?

Plaintiff: I did, yes, in the first week of December of 2018.

Judge: Did you take everything with you?

Plaintiff: My clothes. I didn't have anything to bring my furniture with.

Judge: Did you ever say, "Hey, I want the furniture?"

Plaintiff: I don't think so.

Judge: Okay. So then how long did you live there after the breakup?

Defendant: She moved out the first week of December. I stayed December, January. I just moved last week.

Judge: Now, according to her, you bought everything on credit. You didn't have any cash to buy anything. And why didn't he buy stuff?

Plaintiff: I do not know. Because we were in such a hurry to play house, right, that we had to set up a whole place. In fact, I bought a fireplace, like a portable fireplace.

Judge: I did, yes. Right? Like one of those, you know.

Plaintiff: He picked it out.

Judge: Oh, that's lovely. But you put it on your credit card, right?

Plaintiff: Yes.

Judge: So you have like $5,000 in debt now?

Plaintiff: Yes.

Judge: And he has everything because you had nowhere to put it, right?

Plaintiff: Right.

Judge: All right, so where's the stuff?

Defendant: In Leesburg, Florida, where I moved to.

Judge: So you took it with you to the next place?

Defendant: Yes, ma'am, I did.

Judge: All right, so now tell me about your defense. Let's talk about the stuff.

Defendant: When I lived in Georgia, I had my own apartment, had my own everything. She asked me, "Why bring all that stuff? Just sell it, and we'll start fresh. I don't want something some other woman has had." So I sold everything and moved here.

Judge: What did you do with the money?

Defendant: We got the apartment with the money.

Judge: Who was paying the rent?

Defendant: I did.

Judge: How much was the rent?

Defendant: $1,200 a month.

Judge: And why didn't she pay any rent?

Defendant: Because she's a woman. It's not the woman's job to pay the rent. It was mine.

Judge: Oh wow. God bless you. All right, so because of her gender, she doesn’t have to pay rent? You hear that? I want to make sure everybody heard it in case you suckers were paying rent in places that you were living.

Judge: You didn't pay rent, so she wants you to pay off all of her balances because, according to her, you owe it. Why would he owe it?

Plaintiff: Because he used everything.

Judge: Okay, so let’s settle this. I'm going to order the Defendant to pay the $297 in the electric bill because he says that's his to pay. As for everything else, we will make arrangements with Officer McIntosh for the furniture to be picked up by the Plaintiff. You will pay for the pickup and transport. It’s your furniture, and you can keep paying for it. Case dismissed.
Manipulation Techniques: guilt tripping, deflection, playing the victim

Dialogue:
Judge: What is your response to the request for a psychological evaluation?

Defendant: Not doing it, and you cannot force me to do it. You cannot physically make me go and show up and partake in anything that I do not want to partake in. Yes or no? Do you agree or not agree?

Judge: Uh, you don't get to ask me questions right now.

Judge: I understand that. That's a different case. What I'm interested in is we had this exact issue in a previous case. That would have seemed to have been the occasion for deciding exactly what you're talking about. But we did not get a court for that. I can think of no other case where the right to have your spouse immigrate to this country has been considered. That's what I'm interested in.

Plaintiff's Lawyer: I see. To clarify, that's not the procedural due process right that we're advancing. We do not claim that the petitioner has a right to do what she wants, live with her husband in the U.S., even if her husband is inadmissible. We're merely asking for a reasonable and workable solution, which is that some basis for the denial be given so that we can correct the possibility that there was a mistake.

Judge: No, I'm interested in the liberty interest that requires the procedural right, the underlying liberty interest that we could not find in the previous case. That's all I'm interested in.

Plaintiff's Lawyer: I see. In a number of other substantive due process cases, precedent has acknowledged the substantive right to raise a family and establish a home, which certainly has a cohabitational element. The issue of the right to daily personal intimate association was recognized by this Court as a substantive right. Even in the immigration context, at the peak of restrictive immigration policies, the Court acknowledged that a noncitizen had the right to bring their spouse to the country even without the required documentation.

Judge: Well, none of those address the central issue, which is the principle of consular nonreviewability, which seems to be an entirely different order of magnitude than the cases you've cited.

Plaintiff's Lawyer: The government raises consular nonreviewability as an exception when the rights of citizens are implicated. If a U.S. citizen has been living with their spouse for years and has tried to go through the legal process, but a denial is given without any explanation, that is the type of case where some level of review is necessary. The Court has previously considered claims asserted by U.S. citizens regarding violations of their personal rights allegedly caused by the government's exclusion of particular foreign nationals. We do not believe the Court would be breaking any new ground by acknowledging that consular nonreviewability does not apply when the liberty interests of U.S. citizens are implicated.

Judge: What more would you be entitled to than what you've already received? I understand the question of the timeliness of the explanation, but what else do you think you're entitled to?

Plaintiff's Lawyer: What we received, what the lower court said was sufficient, is far better than vague explanations.

Judge: But as you said, you’ve already received that.

Plaintiff's Lawyer: That's correct, after the one-year deadline had passed.

Judge: So you think you're entitled to more just because the deadline had passed?

Plaintiff's Lawyer: No, but we think we're entitled to a reasonable opportunity to respond to the allegation with enough information so that we're not trying to fight back with our hands tied.

Judge: In court?

Judge: Can't you—I'm sorry.

Plaintiff's Lawyer: No, but if I may, this certainly came up in the earlier discussion.

Judge: I mean, what happens in court then? You say reasonable opportunity to contest. So you can force the government to provide more evidence behind the reasoning, or what happens?

Plaintiff's Lawyer: No, Your Honor. What we believe is the most appropriate way to proceed is to follow the process used in the domestic context, where a general factual summary of the basis for denial is provided, excluding sensitive national security material. This would allow these cases to be resolved at the agency level instead of leading to prolonged litigation.

Judge: But, counsel, you got that. The lower court said that’s exactly what you received. So why are we here?

Plaintiff's Lawyer: Because what we're asking for now is the chance to go back to the consulate and try to overcome the denial with the information we now have.

Judge: Okay. Okay. So you're not asking for further judicial process?

Plaintiff's Lawyer: That's correct, Your Honor.

Judge: One level, this is a very large case about fundamental liberty interests. At another level, I wonder if it's a small case. We talked about remedies a little bit a moment ago, you and I, and I'm just curious, what remedy does the plaintiff seek here? I know they wanted originally a declaration that there wasn't a bona fide reason, seeming to take Mandel as given. I'm looking at page 13 of their complaint. But they got that. They got that reason. They got the Mandel reason. Then they want a declaration that the statute's unconstitutionally vague. That's not before us. Then they want their costs. That's not before us. And I guess, you know, of course, the usual catch-all, grant such further relief, but if a district court can't order the husband into the country, what other relief remains that's available for a court to issue?

Defendant's Lawyer: We don’t think there is any. I think that they've asked for declaratory judgment, that the reason that was given was not good enough. I think that now they would say that if this Court—

Judge: I mean, if they wanted—

Defendant's Lawyer: —says that the Ninth Circuit is wrong about what we did give, then—then—

Judge: Well, the—

Defendant's Lawyer: —they would be entitled to more, I guess, is what they would be saying on remand.

Judge: So—so how about this, that, you know, the declaratory judgment would be you got the Mandel reason because that's what the Ninth Circuit found. It just wasn't timely, period.

Defendant's Lawyer: Well, the Court didn’t grant review on the third QP. We think that the timeliness analysis is wrong and we think it's particularly odd to say that—

Judge: Fine.

Defendant's Lawyer: —that this lawsuit needs to churn along at this point.

Judge: Would the government oppose a declaratory judgment that it wasn't timely in its Mandel statement?

Defendant's Lawyer: Well, I—

Judge: And what harm would that do?

Defendant's Lawyer: —without knowing what the consequences of that are, I—I—

Judge: Yeah.

Defendant's Lawyer: —I understand your point. And—and so I—I don't know what else they would get out of a declaration that it's untimely. The Ninth Circuit obviously wanted there to be further proceedings and they wanted there to be more information that was provided, and I understand my friend to be asking for more information even now.

Judge: But you wouldn’t know what—well, if the court’s correct in the Ninth Circuit that they got all the information they were due at least at the Mandel stage and the Ninth Circuit’s remanding to pierce that to conduct some sort of due process analysis about whether it was good enough, what could the Ninth Circuit or district court do at the end of that proceeding that would be lawful?

Defendant's Lawyer: We—we don’t think anything, but I don’t think that’s a reason to affirm what the Ninth Circuit said here. I think that would be a reason to reverse and say that—that this case is—

Judge: Well, counsel, I'm not fighting you. I'm just asking some questions.

Defendant's Lawyer: Yeah, and—and I agree with you, that I—I don’t know what they—I don’t think there is anything that the court ought to be able to do on remand.

Judge: Yeah. Thank you.

Judge: So, counsel, I had understood the government—this goes to the threshold question. I had understood the government to essentially be asking us to take a certain position, but based on some of your colloquies with my colleagues today, I’m thinking that maybe what the government’s first-order position is, and I had not gotten this from the brief, I thought you just mentioned it in a footnote, is to say that Mandel should never have assumed that there was an exception if there was a fundamental right implicated. Is that right?

Defendant's Lawyer: Well, I—I think that the point of a prior plurality opinion would be that there wouldn’t be an exception to consular nonreviewability that applied anyway, so I think it would get there one way or the other.

Judge: No, I agree, but it's different. I mean, I think another Justice called it a much more dramatic holding. It would be different. I—I guess I see you potentially asking us to do one of two things. One would be to say there’s no fundamental right here, she doesn’t have a fundamental right in having her spouse live with her in the United States, and so the Mandel exception isn’t triggered and she’s not entitled to anything.

Defendant's Lawyer: That is our—that is our front-line position, I agree.

Judge: Okay.

Defendant's Lawyer: And—

Judge: So you’re not asking us to say and, by the way, people shouldn’t have an opportunity to show that there’s a fundamental right implicated because, after all, Mandel had just assumed that. It didn’t say that you are entitled to get a Mandel explanation if you can point to the existence of a fundamental right? Do you see what I’m saying?

Defendant's Lawyer: That’s right. And I was—I was saying for purposes of the assumption in a prior question that if—if we assume that there is a constitutional liberty interest that is sufficient to trigger that review in Mandel, why isn’t this one equally good?

Judge: Right.

Defendant's Lawyer: And our position is that this is not, as the—

Judge: Yeah.

Defendant's Lawyer: —plurality concluded in a prior case, not a sufficient liberty interest to trigger any exception to consular nonreviewability that would look like Mandel review. So we think you don’t get to Mandel review one way or the other.

Judge: No, no, and I understand that, but I guess I’m—I’m still not quite sure what your position is. I understand that. I’m just asking you, are you asking us to say, to go still further than that and say and, by the way, Mandel just assumed that there would be an exception to consular nonreviewability if there was a fundamental right implicated, but we think there’s no such exception at all. So not only is this one not good enough, there’s—are you asking us to do that?

Defendant's Lawyer: I—I don’t think you need to do that. I think the question presented is based on the idea that there is the background of—

Judge: Okay.

Defendant's Lawyer: —consular nonreviewability and the exception is not triggered here—

Judge: That is what I—

Defendant's Lawyer: —for that reason.

Judge: Okay. So your answer is that the non-citizen cannot be found removable unless the government shows by clear and convincing evidence that the notice was provided and that he is removable, and you're saying that there's no way to show that he's removable if the NTA has not been served? So at least that much information would have to be there?

Defendant's Lawyer: If there's no document that contains charges against the non-citizen, there's no way to show that they're removable.

Judge: Okay. So it doesn't eliminate the hypothetical of the right to counsel not being included, but it would eliminate the hypothetical of the entirely blank document?

Defendant's Lawyer: I think that's fair.

Judge: Okay. Thank you.

Judge: Can we go back to the distinction, if any, between the stop-time rule and in absentia removal? I understood you to respond by saying that the government's position is that the stop-time rule gives the government a procedural advantage, the ability to thwart the accrual of time, and so that's why the government has to dot all the I's and cross all the T's with respect to that. But I see this as exactly the same thing, because, ordinarily, a non-citizen would be entitled to a hearing where they could make an argument and advocate for themselves about removal, and the statute allows the government to get around that by obtaining a removal order in the absence of adversarial presentation. So why would we have a statute that requires the government to give notice in order to be able to get an in absentia removal order, but would allow that notice to be deficient?

Defendant's Lawyer: I have a couple of responses to that. First, we agree that non-citizens get the opportunity to present their case, and they get that opportunity by showing up at the hearing. In these cases, we told the non-citizens when and where to show up to present their case, and they failed to do that.

Judge: But the statute doesn't say, if the non-citizen doesn't show up, the government gets the removal order. The statute says, if the non-citizen doesn't show up and the government proves by clear and convincing evidence that they got notice and that the person is removable, then the government gets the order. So the government can't rely on the fact that the person didn't show up as the basis for the validity of their removal order. My question remains: If the government doesn't actually prove that they gave the notice required by the statute, why should the government be entitled to getting the removal order when it wouldn't have been able to get the stop-time order under prior precedents?

Defendant's Lawyer: My answer is similar to the one I gave before. For in absentia removal, two notices are relevant and can be a basis for in absentia removal.

Judge: No, you're just saying that the notice isn't deficient. Let's assume that the notice is deficient. If the notice is deficient, why should the government still get the removal order, when in the stop-time rule scenario, the government wouldn't be able to stop the time? Are you asking us to implicitly overrule prior decisions?

Defendant's Lawyer: No, we are asking you to take prior decisions seriously when they stated that they were narrow decisions about the intersection between the stop-time rule and the notice to appear requirements. We are not saying that if a non-citizen gets no notice whatsoever, they can be ordered removed in absentia. In these cases, while the notice to appear lacked certain information, that missing information was supplied by the notices of hearing that the non-citizens received.

Judge: But you don't dispute that the missing information in this case is the same missing information in the prior cases, correct? We're not talking about two different kinds of missing information that might allow you to make this distinction?

Defendant's Lawyer: I don't dispute that the information is the same. I dispute that the relevance is the same because, in prior cases, there was concern that if we never told the non-citizen when and where to show up, they wouldn’t be able to figure out what to do. Here, the information was provided.

Judge: Let me ask you two quick things. One, is in absentia removal the only way the government can remove a non-citizen?

Defendant's Lawyer: Correct. If a non-citizen goes to their removal proceeding, they can be found removable. So I didn't mean to suggest that in absentia is the only way.

Judge: Or if you find them and arrest them and bring them for removal, they can be removed, right?

Defendant's Lawyer: That’s the way an ordinary removal proceeding works, and that’s the way these removal proceedings were initiated as well. What’s different about these cases is the non-citizens just didn’t finish out the process.

Judge: And finally, regarding the catastrophic nature of a ruling in favor of the other side, you said that the Ninth Circuit finds automatic reopening. Couldn’t we agree with the Ninth Circuit's holding on the deficiency of the notice but disagree that there's automatic reopening for all the people who have previously had this problem?

Defendant's Lawyer: The question of what is the remedy and whether it’s automatic or not is not before you. I don’t think it’s fairly included as part of the question presented.

Judge: But if we were to hold that the notice was deficient, it's not necessarily the case that we would have this catastrophic result because we could separately decide the remedy question.

Defendant's Lawyer: That’s true; you could have a later remedy case. But even if it's not automatic, a significant number of these motions are likely to be granted because the non-citizens claim they did not get notice. If they are granted and reintroduced into the immigration system, that will have significant impacts.

Judge: Thank you.

Judge: Thank you, counsel.

Defendant's Lawyer: Thank you.

udge: Welcome back to The People's Court. Next case on the docket: The plaintiff helped the defendant find two kittens, and the defendant won’t pay the adoption fee. But the defendant says there was never an adoption fee agreement. It’s the case of kitty galore.

Bailiff: You're welcome.

Judge: Okay, Mary Chirichella, you are suing Here Kitty Kitty Incorporated, represented here by Leslie Diamond. You’re the president?

Defendant: Yes.

Judge: Okay, for $180, the amount that you say they owe you as an adoption fee for two kittens, right? That they received and kept rather than giving you the adoption fee?

Plaintiff: Yes.

Judge: All right, so tell me what’s going on.

Plaintiff: Okay, in July of [year], I rescued two tortoiseshell kittens—

Judge: Where did you rescue them from?

Plaintiff: From a street in a busy area.

Judge: Okay.

Plaintiff: They were about 10 to 12 weeks old. It was a location where I’d been doing cat rescue for 12 years. I had been feeding there and had seen the kittens before.

A friend of mine, Eileen, agreed to foster them. I provided the food and litter. Eileen took care of them daily. I created a flyer for Natasha and Nadia, my phone number was on it, and Eileen and I posted it in various locations.

It was a slow summer. The economic crisis was in full swing, and I wasn’t getting calls I was happy with.

Judge: What kind of donation were you looking for?

Plaintiff: Originally, just to cover my costs.

Judge: How much was that?

Plaintiff: At the time, I would have taken $75 for them.

Judge: For each or both?

Plaintiff: For both. At that point, they had only one vet visit, which cost $131.

Judge: Okay, so what happens next?

Plaintiff: When Eileen took the kittens to the vet, someone there suggested she take them to Petco on Route 3. Miss Diamond’s group, Here Kitty Kitty, showed cats for adoption there on weekends.

Judge: Do you work for Petco?

Defendant: No, but we show our cats there. I’ve been doing rescue for 20 years. Here Kitty Kitty will celebrate its 9th anniversary in September.

Judge: Okay, so what happens next?

Plaintiff: Eileen brought them to Petco and left them with her. Eventually, someone completed an adoption application. I spoke with the adopter and then with Leslie. I asked, "What is the adoption fee?" She said, "$180."

I told her I would need that fee to cover my costs. She asked how much I had spent. I said a few hundred dollars. She said, "I can’t give you that." I replied, "I don’t expect you to, but I will need the adoption fee."

Defendant: Nobody talked about an adoption fee before this.

Judge: Is that true?

Defendant: Mary knew our adoption fee. Because the adopters were in their 60s, we have a program sponsored by Purina that covers the adoption fee.

Mary only brought up the adoption fee after the adoption. In all my years doing this, I sometimes split the fee with someone or write a tax letter.

Judge: So the cats were adopted, and then what?

Plaintiff: Leslie agreed to mail me the adoption fee but said I’d have to wait for the money from Purina. I said that was fine. A few months went by, and I started to suspect something was wrong.

I called her, but she didn’t return my calls. One night, I spoke to her husband, and he said she’d get back to me. Eventually, I went to see her in person.

Judge: Why were you avoiding her?

Defendant: I took all her calls. The one night she called, I was very sick, and my husband picked up the phone.

Judge: According to you, how many messages did you leave?

Plaintiff: Three.

Judge: So you went to Petco in person?

Plaintiff: Yes. As soon as I arrived, Leslie said, "I don’t have your money."

I asked why. She said, "I had a couple of veterinary emergencies, and I had to use it."

I asked when she would have it. She said, "I don’t know."

She made no attempt to work something out. Then she told me, "If you want your money that badly, take me to court." So I did.

Judge: All right, can someone ask for an adoption fee after the fact?

Audience Member 1: No.

Judge: Why?

Audience Member 1: Because an adoption should be about helping animals, and if there was no agreement beforehand, it’s not fair.

Audience Member 2: There was no agreement, so why should someone have to pay an adoption fee after they think it’s free?

Judge: Fair enough.

(Back inside the courtroom.)

Judge: You did get paid by Purina, correct?

Defendant: Yes.

Judge: And at some point, you told her you would send the adoption fee?

Defendant: I never actually said that. I never signed anything. I said I would try to see what I could do.

Judge: Both of you operate through adoption fees, so I have to determine whether there was a contract. You didn’t set a price, so I have to set one. I’m going to split it. I order you to pay her $90.

For the future, if you’re in this situation, specify the money part upfront. Good luck.

Narrator: Let’s talk to the parties.

(Outside the courtroom.)

Reporter: Are you satisfied with the judge's decision?

Defendant: It was very fair.

Reporter: Is Mary a good cat rescuer?

Defendant: She does a great job at trap-neuter-return.

Reporter: Why didn’t you get something in writing?

Defendant: I will next time.

(Reporter turns to Plaintiff.)

Reporter: Any problems with how the judge ruled?

Plaintiff: The judge based her decision on what Leslie said, but what she said wasn’t true.

Reporter: Is Leslie a good cat rescuer?

Plaintiff: I suppose so.

Judge: We will hear argument this morning in Case 23-5572, Fischer versus United States.

Defendant's Lawyer: Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court: Congress enacted 1512(c) in 2002 in the wake of the large-scale destruction of Enron's financial documents.
The statute therefore prohibits the impairment of the integrity or availability of information and evidence to be used in a proceeding.
In 2002, Congress hedged a little bit and added Section (c)(2) to cover other forms of impairment, the known unknowns, so to speak. It was, after all, the dawn of the Information Age.
Until the January 6th prosecutions, Section 1512(c)(2), the "otherwise" provision, had never been used to prosecute anything other than evidence tampering, and that was for good reason.
This Court has said that "otherwise," when used in a criminal statute, means to do similar conduct in a different way.
The government would have you ignore all that or disregard all that and instead convert (c)(2) from a catchall provision into a dragnet.
One of the things that that dragnet would cover is Section (c)(1).
Our construction of the statute at least leaves (c)(1) and (c)(2) to do some independent work.
The January 6th prosecutions demonstrate that there are a host of felony and misdemeanor crimes that cover the alleged conduct.
A Sarbanes-Oxley-based, Enron-driven evidence-tampering statute is not one of them. I welcome the Court's questions.

Judge: How do we determine what these two provisions have in common? Do we look after the "otherwise" or before, and why?

Defendant's Lawyer: We -- you look at before, Your Honor, and you look at the kinds of manner in which documents and records are to be impaired, and then you look after to see what the effect is.
But I would submit that the effect is the same, right, in order to cause the impairment of the integrity of the evidence that's to be used in a proceeding or to prevent its availability.
So we look back and we look forward.

Judge: Wouldn't it be just as easy to look at (c)(2) and then ask what it has in common with (c)(1) and use (c)(2)'s provisions as a basis for that similarity?

Defendant's Lawyer: No, because (c)(2) speaks to the effect of the actions that the "otherwise" clause covers.
So, in other words, we look at (c)(1) and we see that Congress is concerned about documents and records and other objects and things that are done to those to impair the integrity of those, and the effect of that is to obstruct.
And so (c)(2) omits that object and verb section.

Judge: But you could just as easily say that Congress is really concerned about things that obstruct, influence, or impede proceedings.
And that's (c)(2). So why isn't that the basis for the similarity?

Defendant's Lawyer: Well, because of the presence of the "otherwise" provision.
So "otherwise," as I mentioned -- and "otherwise," this Court has said, means to do similar conduct in a different way.
So what we've got here is -- is the impairment of evidence being done in a different way.

Judge: I'm sorry. I thought it was, yes, doing it in a different way, so let me give you an example.
There is a sign on the theater: You will be kicked out of the theater if you photograph or record the actors or otherwise disrupt the performance.
If you start yelling, I think no one would question that you can be expected to be kicked out under this policy, even though yelling has nothing to do with photographing or recording.
The object that the verb is looking at is the obstruction. It's not the manner in which you obstruct; it's the fact that you've obstructed.
Isn't that the structure of this provision?

Defendant's Lawyer: It is, Your Honor.
It -- it's -- it's in part the structure of the provision.
But what -- what your hypothetical omits is that there is a specific reticulation, I guess it's called, of all of the different sorts of things that might be done to evidence to begin with.

Judge: Except that --

Defendant's Lawyer: There's a long --

Judge: What's fascinating about (1), which is not about (2), is that (1) doesn't require you to have actually impeded the proceeding. (1) requires you to have that intent, but you don't actually have to accomplish the intent.
(2) requires you to accomplish the intent.
And so that's a very different articulation of what the object of (2) is.
The object of (2) is the actual disruption of the proceeding.

Defendant's Lawyer: Well, I would respectfully disagree because both --

Judge: Well, why? Look at the language.

Defendant's Lawyer: Yeah.

Judge: "Alters, destroys, mutilates, or conceals a record." I do that in my home, and I do it anticipating that it might be needed.
All I have to do is have the intent to impair.
By that very language, I don't have to have an actual proceeding that I've impaired.
On (2), you need an actual proceeding to impair.

Defendant's Lawyer: I guess I'm -- I guess I'm a little confused, Your Honor, because, as I read this, I would think that the government would say that any attempt at (1) is also covered by the statute, and I'm not sure that I would disagree.
So I'm not -- I don't think that there has to be an actual impairment.

Judge: Mr. Miller.

Plaintiff's Lawyer: Mr. Chief Justice, may it please the Court: Maybe I'll begin with Justice Jackson's question about page 51.

We have six programs here, including the EMS program, which is a major feature of the government's motion to dismiss that is the subject of this case. The third-party revenue spending has to be anchored to those six programs. It cannot be spent on a dental program. We don't see dental there. It cannot be spent just on general health. Even interpreting that term broadly and generously in light of the Indian canon, it still has to be anchored in these six programs.

With regard to these six programs, when the Indian Health Service awarded a contract to the San Carlos Apache Tribe—and just visualize this, we're talking about 1.8 million acres, about 29,000 square miles, a thousand miles of roadless area, a couple of hours east of Phoenix—the Emergency Medical Service program was severely underfunded.

So, on page 101, the Indian Health Service demands that the tribe agree to a clause that says, in running the EMS program, it will maintain an efficient billing system to maximize third-party revenues. Why? Because IHS knew that there was no way this program could be run at even a moderate level without third-party revenues coming into the program and going out into the program, coming in from program income and going out into services.

So that's why this clause is such a pivotal element of the contract.

Now we rely on (m)—(m)(1), and (m)(1) was enacted in 1994, but that was not the first time the concept that program income goes into the contract was invented.

First of all, it is a standard government contracting clause in the OMB regulations. You can go to 2 C.F.R. 200.307, I think it's (e)(2).

But, more specifically, the Indian Health Service, in the sample contract that the Indian Health Service had in the 1980s, required that all program income be spent to provide additional services and benefits under the contract.

So this was an old concept, not a new concept, that Congress put in there in 1994. Well, why did Congress put it in 1994? I mean, there it was in the sample contract that IHS had because Congress decided that IHS could not be trusted to administer this program at all.

That was the positive provision, that program income comes into the contract, but there were a lot of negative provisions in the old sample contract. There were a lot of negative provisions in regulations that IHS had adopted, proposed to adopt in January '94.

So Congress comes in, clears the decks, says we are declaring what the master contract is going to say, we are prohibiting a raft of things that the agency proposed in these regulations, but we are cementing the good things.

And one of the good things was (m)(1), that all program income had to go to further the general purposes of the contract.

So too the question about (a)(2) and (a)(3) and how they relate.

They require the administration—the overhead has to be in connection with the expenditure of—of—of—excuse me, with the administration of the contract.

And the contract, as we just saw, requires the spending of program income.

So we satisfy (a)(3).

It's, of course, part of the federal program that we discussed earlier. The federal program includes direct service spending from the secretarial amount and also program income spending.

So—and IHS spends both tranches of money. The tribe spends both tranches of money.

Judge: You've heard—

Plaintiff's Lawyer: So it's indisputably part of the federal program.

Judge: You've heard questions about a situation where the tribe may be spending money on services that go primarily to non-tribal members. What is your principle for limiting that, if there is one?

Plaintiff's Lawyer: Right.

Well, of course, as was pointed out earlier, it's not presented in this case, but I will answer the question.

The issue of the government's obligation with respect to services to non-beneficiaries has been litigated in another setting.

Contract support costs are not the only mandatory spending under the Act. There's also leasing that is mandatory spending.

If a tribe uses a tribal facility to run the federal program, then the federal government needs to pay the leasing costs.

That issue has been litigated, and the courts have held in the Jamestown case that there's a reasonableness limitation to the leasing costs.

Incidentally, reasonableness comes up twice, once in (a)(2), once in (a)(3). So there's a reasonableness limitation on the overhead also.

So that's just thing one.

Judge: Well, but that presumably doesn't have anything to do with the allocation between tribal members and non-tribal members.

Plaintiff's Lawyer: Oh, no.

With respect, Your Honor, it would mean in this situation that if there were substantial overhead costs associated with services to non-beneficiaries, then you would discount that element, and IHS would not be responsible for reimbursing it.

And I should note that IHS has a mechanism already for doing this.

IHS does this in the leasing arena.

If I go to IHS and ask for a lease to compensate for the use of a tribal facility, IHS will ask for data, how many non-beneficiaries do you serve?

Zero.

Then they cover the whole lease.

Fifty percent, which is unheard of, but there are examples, I suppose, then IHS will whack off a part of the leasing cost to be sure that they are only supporting services to Indians because this is an Indian healthcare program.

Judge: Can I ask—on the question of whether the tribe can use this money for something that IHS itself couldn't use the money for? If that happened with the use of these third-party income funds, overhead costs, your answer?

Plaintiff's Lawyer: Right.

Thank you, Your Honor.

We don't have a separate appropriation for construction. IHS does.

That's how they get their construction money—a quarter of a billion dollars, but—

Judge: That is not an answer to the statutory question that was posed earlier. That’s a real-world answer, which is a good real-world answer, but I don’t think it’s a great statutory answer. So keep going.

Plaintiff's Lawyer: I agree with Your Honor that if a construction activity serves the general purposes of, let's just take the EMS program, then it's under the umbrella.

Let's consider it.

So building a garage to house the ambulances or to do maintenance work on the ambulances—yes.

To build a new facility where the ambulance crews were sleeping because they were decrepit and there were holes in the walls and it was not an acceptable place for ambulance crews to sleep in between calls—yes, reconstructing a new facility to house the ambulance crews, absolutely.

Are those construction activities things that the tribe can do with the third-party revenue money but that IHS cannot do because it needs permission from Congress? Yes. That is a fact.

Judge: Yes is the answer to that?

Plaintiff's Lawyer: Yes is the answer to that question.

Judge: Would you ever—

Judge: And the overhead—the overhead for that, just to follow up on that, the overhead for that, or maybe you already answered this, I just want to—

Plaintiff's Lawyer: No, but thank you for asking the question.

So the overhead on that, just as if you and I hire a—

Judge: That’s what you’re seeking, right?

Plaintiff's Lawyer: Yes, but there would be no overhead on it, is what I was about to say, Your Honor.

Judge: Are you going to include any overhead as contract support?

Plaintiff's Lawyer: No.

Judge: Thank you.

Plaintiff: I rented some commercial space to the defendant, who opened a hair salon. Almost immediately, the cops were called due to marijuana odors emanating from the place. I filed for an eviction, and the defendant trashed the place before vacating. I'm suing for $3,220.74 for damages, rent, and attorney’s fees.

Narrator: This is the defendant, Ashley Packard. She says everything was fine for the first year, but a new tenant moved in upstairs, and that’s when the problems started. She says the plaintiff started harassing her workers and clients, so she moved out, left the sinks and chairs because they belonged to the place, put her trash on the street, and is now being sued.

Judge: All parties, please raise your right hand.

Narrator: Welcome back to The People's Court. The plaintiff rented commercial space to the defendant, who opened a hair salon. The plaintiff says the defendant was heavy into weed and trashed the place, but the defendant claims the problems were caused by another tenant. It’s the case of the salon that gave everyone a buzz.

Judge: Thank you, Douglas. A68 LLC, represented by Ms. Drab, you’re the owner?

Plaintiff: Yes.

Judge: You’re suing Ashley Packard for $3,220.74 in rent and utilities because, according to you, she violated a stipulation the two of you had entered into in landlord-tenant court. You own a piece of property that is a salon?

Plaintiff: Yes.

Judge: And when did you start renting it?

Plaintiff: In December.

Judge: How long was she there in total?

Plaintiff: A year and a half.

Judge: And what went wrong?

Plaintiff: The reason we're here is that she did not meet the stipulations of the agreement.

Judge: How did you end up in landlord-tenant court? What was the first thing that went wrong?

Plaintiff: The rent was not paid for July and August.

Judge: July and August of what year?

Plaintiff: 2018.

Judge: Did you pay July and August?

Defendant: No, because I received a notice to quit.

Judge: Were you in the middle of a lease?

Defendant: Yes.

Judge: So she stops paying rent. Why did you give her a notice to quit? Had you received complaints?

Plaintiff: The reason was that she was constantly late with rent. Also, the electricity had to be paid, and sometimes she would pay it late. There were also other reasons—I spoke to her about bringing in people to do acrylic nails, and the fumes were offensive. The lady upstairs complained, and I spoke with her about installing a ventilation system, which never happened.

Judge: What was the deal with the nail technicians?

Defendant: My girls, who worked with me, could be a little mouthy, but they stood their ground.

Judge: What were they standing their ground about?

Defendant: Gina tried to get a hold of me one day, but I was at a class. My workers told me she came in, screaming at them to stop what they were doing, demanded to call me, threw her purse down, and started going through my drawers and the salon.

Judge: Did they record her?

Defendant: No, they were in the middle of doing nails.

Judge: In this day and age where everyone records everything, there's no video?

Defendant: No, but they told me what happened.

Judge: So, there was no rent paid in July or August. You file for eviction and end up in landlord-tenant court. There was a stipulation that the defendant agreed to pay $1,665.28 by September 5th. Did you make that payment?

Defendant: Yes, and I have proof.

Judge: How did you make the payment?

Defendant: By check.

Judge: Did you mail it or deliver it?

Defendant: I drove to her house and put it in her mailbox.

Judge: Is this your mailbox?

Plaintiff: Yes.

Judge: That picture is dated September 5th at 10:00 PM. Technically, she complied, even if you didn't see it until the 6th.

Plaintiff: But she didn't remove all her belongings or leave the premises broom-clean.

Judge: What did she leave behind?

Plaintiff: There was trash everywhere. She left a chair outside, garbage in the sinks, alcohol bottles, clutter, nail polish, and stuff in the back room and stair landing. The city wouldn't pick up the garbage, and I had to make private arrangements.

Judge: Did you notify her?

Plaintiff: Yes, but she never responded.

Judge: Why did you leave all the trash behind?

Defendant: That’s where we always put trash.

Judge: That’s where you put one bag of trash on trash day, not an entire pile. She could get fined.

Defendant: I had no one to help me move.

Judge: That’s a you problem, not a her problem. If the deal was two months of free rent only if you left the place clean, and you didn’t, she can sue you for that rent.

Defendant: I understand.

Judge: What’s this black thing in the picture?

Plaintiff: It's a pile of garbage.

Judge: You know you’re under scrutiny in court. Why not just take the trash to the dump? Instead, you left it there and created this issue. Now she’s suing you for July and August rent because she says you violated the stipulation.

Judge: You had a good deal—you didn’t have to pay July or August rent, just leave the place clean. Instead, you left it a mess. If I were you, I’d make sure the place was spotless to avoid being sued.

Defendant: I understand.

Judge: Do you think the landlord has the right to sue for two months' rent?

Audience Member: Yes, because the defendant didn’t fulfill her part of the agreement.

Judge: Agreed. Going back inside the courtroom.

Judge: There’s alcohol left behind? What kind?

Plaintiff: A bottle of wine.

Judge: Clutter in the back room, nail polish in a small room, and leftover pedicure supplies. She didn’t clean up.

Judge: Ms. Packard, you were given a deal, and you didn’t hold up your end. I find in favor of the plaintiff for $3,220.74.

Judge: Judgment for the plaintiff.

Judge: Kareem P, you are suing Patricia Hacker for $5,000 for pain and suffering and the cost of the dog. You say your dog was attacked by her dog and that it's her fault. Tell me what happened. I'm so sorry about the loss of your dog.

Plaintiff: Okay, when this happened, I wasn’t in that moment with my kids. Okay, it was my husband who came up. What happened? That happened February 24 at night. I was walking up the same way when I drive in, my son, my first son, he was away from me by 10 feet, and I was walking with my second son. My first son had his puppy. He was, how old is your first son?

Judge: 10 years old. How does he have your puppy? Explain.

Plaintiff: Okay, he was walking with the leash.

Judge: A leash?

Plaintiff: Yes, I have the leash. You have the leash. Let me see it.

Plaintiff (showing leash): So, my son, my first son, was 10 feet ahead of me walking the dog. And what happens then? The puppy stopped.

Judge: Who's the puppy? My Coco? How old is your dog?

Plaintiff: Okay, it's a teacup poodle. Five pounds, no more than that. It’s a teacup. I have the pictures if you want.

Judge (looking at pictures): Yeah, let me see it.

Plaintiff (showing pictures): Okay, so what happens? Coco, he was doing his business, and in one moment, I saw another dog coming in the same direction where my son was. My second son spoke to me in that moment, and I looked at my son, and that was the last time I saw Coco on the floor. My son screamed and ran to me. Then I see her dog.

Judge: What kind of dog do you have?

Plaintiff: It's a Pitbull.

Judge: Okay, go ahead.

Plaintiff: My Pitbull grabbed my Coco. I tried to kick the dog, but then he let go of the puppy. The first time I saw Coco, I knew he was already in bad condition.

Judge: The dog was alive though when you took the dog to the vet?

Plaintiff: No, in that moment, we didn’t have the key to go back to the garage. Some neighbors heard my son screaming because he was begging for help. My little son came to me, and he said, "Oh, the dog killed him," and I thought it was my son. Then I saw my dog, and he looked at me and passed away. So, we thought we could do something, so we took the dog with a neighbor’s car to the hospital. When we got there, I gave the dog to the doctor, and he said, "I’m sorry." I saw my clothes with blood and everything. It’s very sad because it’s not only the first time this happened. The same dog attacked another dog, and I have proof. Yes, I have the pictures. When I went to make the report to the police, the police were very sad for me, but unfortunately, when a dog is killed by another dog, you can’t do anything.

Judge: Well, that’s not true. You’re doing something, but go ahead. I want to see the pictures. Do you have a statement from that person?

Plaintiff: Yes, I have it.

Judge: Okay, let me see that.

Defendant: Okay, I was walking my dog, and there was something that I thought at first was a cat, you know, because there wasn’t anyone on the street. The dog was by itself. Then I realized that it wasn’t a cat, that it was actually a dog. You say the dog was by itself. Was the dog on a leash?

Defendant: Absolutely not. The dog wasn’t on a leash, and neither were the two boys. There was one boy.

Judge: Okay, I have an affidavit for a witness that was directly across the street.

Defendant: No, no, she’s keeping me company. She’s my sister.

Judge: Okay, go on.

Defendant: The dog was by itself when it started running at me. May I step out?

Judge: Yeah, okay.

Defendant: The dog just came running at me. So, I screamed. No, okay, the dog kept coming, and by that time, he was right at me. I stuck my foot up, and it hit the bottom of my foot. It ricocheted off, crashing into my shin. I had a bruise for at least two weeks on my leg. The dog bounced back, leaped on my dog’s face, and bit him.

Judge: Okay, I have pictures of his face.

Defendant (showing pictures): Let me see. I immediately stuck my hand in the choker, and I told him, "No, no." It’s a baby now. I know he knows the word “baby,” so he immediately put the dog down on the ground.

Defendant: Then simultaneously, I look and there’s a man kneeling in front of me, and I hear the voice of a boy next to me. The man went down, took the dog up, and was doing this to the top of the head. I said to him, "Is he okay?" He said, "He’s fine, he’s fine."

Judge: Okay, so you went across the street and called again.

Defendant: Yes, I asked, "Are you sure? Is he okay?" He said, "He’s fine, he’s fine."

Judge: The attack killed the dog. Why would an owner of a dog say everything’s fine?

Defendant: He wasn’t saying, "Everything’s fine." He said, "He’s fine." Did you think he was fine?

Judge: I know, but it had to be a pretty serious attack. Was there blood?

Defendant: I didn’t see any blood, no.

Judge: Was there blood on the spot?

Defendant: Yes, of course.

Judge: According to you, he goes, “Yeah, everything’s fine,” and then the dog dies.

Defendant: He didn’t say like that. He said, “He’s fine.”

Judge: Okay, go on.

Defendant: I walked across the street to where the witness was standing. He said to me, “Was that you that was screaming?” I said to him, “Yeah, there was a dog across the street with no leash.” I was screaming at the dog to stop. The witness said, “I heard the commotion and saw a man with a tan and white dog crossing the sidewalk. The dog was wearing a double collar and was on a leash.”

Judge: What’s this person’s name?

Defendant: I can’t even read the signature. Well, it’s Joe Mango. Do you know that person?

Judge (reading the statement): “I saw and heard a dog running from the underground garage up the driveway. A few minutes later, I saw a woman with a male tan and white dog crossing the sidewalk. Her dog was wearing a double collar and was being held on a leash. She was recounting an incident with another dog across the street moments before. She told me her dog was just attacked by another unleashed dog.”

Judge (pondering): What do I have here? I have the plaintiff, who has the burden of proof in my courtroom of proving that things happened the way she says. And I have some question whether things happened the way she says. And unless you're able to persuade me by a preponderance of the evidence that they happened your way, my verdict has to be for the defendant.

Judge (after considering): And it is. Good luck, folks.

Judge:
Angela and Michael Gorbach, you are suing Emil S. for $1,200, $450 of it to fix a window you say he negligently broke and $750 for the inconvenience and time to get the window fixed. Okay, you're the father and the car is under your name?

Plaintiff (Angela):
Yes.

Judge:
Okay, sit down, relax. You weren't there that night, right?

Plaintiff (Michael):
No, but I spoke to him afterwards.

Judge:
Okay, but we’ll get your testimony in a minute. You can sit down and relax because I’m going to talk to the person who was there, and that’s you, Angela. How old are you?

Plaintiff (Angela):
19. 10 days, I’m turning 22 in October.

Judge:
Okay, when did this happen?

Plaintiff (Angela):
Around May 31st, at the end of May of this year.

Judge:
Okay, what happened?

Plaintiff (Angela):
I was at my friend’s house. She invited three guys over. One of them was with them. I’ve seen them around before, I didn’t hang out with them, but I have seen them before. We were just hanging out. No parents were home. We started drinking.

Judge:
You were drinking?

Plaintiff (Angela):
No, I wasn’t drinking. I was driving that night.

Judge:
But they were drinking?

Plaintiff (Angela):
Yes, they started getting a little rowdy. They were being kind of rude to us, like indirectly—not really saying our names, but they just started looking at us and laughing about things. I said, “What’s going on?” and they said, “None of your business.”

Judge:
Who’s house is it?

Plaintiff (Angela):
My friend’s. We’re not friends anymore after this happened. It was my ex-best friend’s house. It’s her father’s house. She lived with her mother, but she went there because her father went away to Russia, so she had nobody home and wanted to have people there.

Judge:
Right. What happened after that?

Plaintiff (Angela):
I went to the kitchen. It’s a small house, so everything’s crowded. I came up to her and said, “I’m not really comfortable here. Either you come with me to your mom’s house, or I’m leaving by myself.”

Judge:
Who was driving that day?

Plaintiff (Angela):
Me.

Judge:
They had their car, and you had your car. What time of the night was it when you first told her you wanted to go?

Plaintiff (Angela):
It was around 1:30—late enough to go already.

Judge:
Okay.

Plaintiff (Angela):
She said, “Hold on, I don’t feel comfortable telling them to leave right now. Just give it a little bit of time.” Nothing changed, so I said, “Let’s go.” She came with me. Their car was still there. Then I reversed back because she said she forgot to lock the door. It was only about three houses away from the stop sign. So, I reversed back. I looked into my mirror and over my shoulder. Nobody was in the street, it was dark, and then out of nowhere, I heard the loud crash. The glass shattered all over me and my friend when he threw his phone.

Judge:
Where were you when you saw him throw the phone?

Plaintiff (Angela):
I was in my car. I was reversing back, looking over my shoulder. It wasn’t too far from where he was. He was standing on the sidewalk, not in the street.

Judge:
And what did he say to you?

Plaintiff (Angela):
He said, “I thought you were going to run me over.”

Judge:
How far was he from you?

Plaintiff (Angela):
About two to three feet.

Judge:
How do you know?

Plaintiff (Angela):
When I got out, he was standing very close to where he had thrown the phone.

Judge:
Okay. And he said, “I thought you were going to kill me?”

Plaintiff (Angela):
Yes.

Judge:
What did you say to him?

Plaintiff (Angela):
I said, “You were nowhere near. You were on the sidewalk. I was looking right over my shoulder.”

Judge:
Okay. What happened next?

Defendant (Emil):
We were hanging out at her friend’s house. We were all drinking. Everybody was drinking in the house. It was about 4:45 in the morning when they were about to go home.

Judge:
What time was it when this happened?

Defendant (Emil):
It was about 4:45 AM. I remember because after I broke her window, I told her, “Give me two hours, at 7:00 AM when all the glass shops open, I’ll pay for your window.”

Judge:
What else did you do besides drink?

Defendant (Emil):
We just socialized, drank, smoked cigarettes, and smoked weed.

Judge:
Did you try to come on to her?

Defendant (Emil):
No, I didn’t try to come on to her.

Plaintiff (Angela):
He tried to kiss me later.

Defendant (Emil):
I didn’t force myself on her. We came onto each other.

Plaintiff (Angela):
He locked me in the bathroom, pressed himself against me to kiss me.

Defendant (Emil):
I didn’t force myself on her. We kissed back and forth, but I didn’t force anything.

Judge:
Wait, you locked her in the bathroom?

Defendant (Emil):
Yes, to try to calm her down after she was upset.

Judge:
Okay, let’s get back to the window incident. You threw your phone at her window. Why?

Defendant (Emil):
I was startled. She was backing up and I thought she was going to hit me. I threw the phone to get her attention.

Judge:
Why didn’t you just jump out of the way?

Defendant (Emil):
It was a first reaction. I couldn’t control it.

Judge:
Well, you have to work on your first reactions, pal, because that could have ended badly for you.

Plaintiff (Michael):
The police were called right away.

Defendant (Emil):
Yes, I know. They came, and she was drinking.

Judge:
Where’s the police report from that day?

Plaintiff (Angela):
I filed the police report on August 15th, three months later.

Judge:
You filed a police report three months after the event?

Plaintiff (Angela):
Yes, because I wanted to have the full details of the incident.

Judge:
I find that $450 is a reasonable amount to fix the window, and I’m going to order that the defendant pay the plaintiff that amount. However, I’m not going to award $750 for inconvenience because you got into that situation being out at 5 AM with three guys you didn’t know, and your refusal to leave made things worse.

Plaintiff (Angela):
Okay.

Judge:
That’s my judgment: $450 for the window.

Judge: All parties, please raise your right hands. Welcome back to The People’s Court. Next case: The plaintiff says the defendant’s dog attacked his cat and killed it, then put the cat in the trash. But the defendant says the dog was on his own property. It’s the case of "dog got your cat." Thank you, Douglas. Lenny Rivera, you are suing Alan Esterine for $5,000—$375 for the cost of three cats and $4,000 something for emotional distress. Tell me what happened.

Plaintiff: Okay, um, I was actually at the Mets baseball game. I received a phone call from my wife and my brother indicating that the dog next door had eaten my cat.

Judge: Okay. Is this the brother?

Plaintiff: Yes, my brother and my daughter, because they were both at home when this happened.

Judge: Okay. Can I see the brother? Can you come up to the microphone? Did you actually see what happened?

Plaintiff’s Brother: No, I heard the cat screaming.

Judge: And where was—did you run out when you heard the cat scream?

Plaintiff’s Brother: I ran out, but the cat, um, already was—the pit was already on her stomach.

Judge: Was it in your yard, or was it in their yard?

Plaintiff’s Brother: It happened—the cat was in our yard, and the dog put his head out and took the cat.

Judge: Put his head out of what?

Plaintiff’s Brother: The fence. It pulled the cat.

Judge: Through the fence? What kind of fence is it?

Plaintiff’s Brother: It’s like, um—my brother has pictures.

Judge: Yeah, let me see it.

Plaintiff: I have numerous pictures that I want to show.

Judge: What kind of dog is it?

Plaintiff: A pit bull.

Judge: What is the fence that we’re talking about? This iron one?

Plaintiff: Yeah, that one.

Judge: When you see it, where’s the dog, and where’s the cat?

Plaintiff’s Brother: The cat was already in his gate.

Judge: When you first see it, the cat is in his gate?

Plaintiff’s Brother: Yes.

Judge: How do you know the cat didn’t just jump in?

Plaintiff’s Brother: I don’t know that.

Judge: Oh, well, that’s important, though.

Plaintiff’s Brother: But that dog—you can’t even go through the gate or anything because that dog wants to attack you.

Judge: Are you supposed to go through the gate?

Plaintiff’s Brother: No.

Judge: So just for the record, this dog’s very vicious?

Plaintiff’s Brother: This dog—my kids can’t even come outside to play because his dog is constantly in the gate, sticking his face out, attempting to bite my children. I have three children—an eight-year-old, a six-year-old, and a four-year-old. They can’t even play on my premises due to this dog.

Plaintiff: This dog actually, over the summertime, bit a crossing guard and required 18 stitches.

Judge: Did the crossing guard report it?

Plaintiff: No, because she said they’re friends, so she didn’t want to.

Judge: Have you filed a vicious dog report?

Plaintiff: Yes.

Judge: What happened? Were you there?

Plaintiff: No, I was at work at the time.

Judge: How about you? Were you there? Come on up. Who are you to him?

Plaintiff’s Girlfriend: Girlfriend.

Judge: Okay. What happened?

Plaintiff’s Girlfriend: The cat—from what we saw, the dog was chasing the cat around the yard and caught the cat in the front of the yard.

Judge: Did you actually see it happen?

Plaintiff’s Girlfriend: I got it on video.

Judge: You got what happened on video? Why were you taking a video instead of solving the problem?

Plaintiff’s Girlfriend: Well, I mean, I just got it off the video, you know, for what was going on.

Judge: Why are you taking video with your camera instead of running out there and calling the dog?

Plaintiff’s Girlfriend: This is after—this is off my surveillance camera.

Judge: You should’ve just said that. So, you were videoing—it was on your surveillance camera, and you brought it into your phone?

Plaintiff’s Girlfriend: Yes.

Judge: Let me see it. (Reviewing footage) Okay, I see. The cat is running around, and the dog is chasing it. That means the cat was already inside the yard. You have no evidence that the dog dragged the cat in.


Judge: After reviewing all testimonies and evidence, the law does not punish animals for acting like animals. The law punishes irresponsible behavior on the part of owners. You own a cat. You can't stop cats from jumping around into other people’s yards. You have no evidence that the defendant’s dog pulled the cat into the yard. Based on what I’ve heard, my verdict is for the defendant.

Judge: Okay, Jonathan Fuentes and Joselyn Fuentes, you are suing your former landlord, Horizon Investors Corp., represented here by—you are an owner also?

Defendant: Yes.

Judge: Okay, good. And your name is Oreo Cardona?

Defendant: Yes.

Judge: And you are the Executive Vice President and landlord?

Defendant: Correct.

Judge: Okay. You are suing him for $5,000 for moving expenses, a security deposit that wasn’t returned, and loss of wages. But basically, you’re really mad about this security deposit not being returned. Tell me what happened.

Plaintiffs Explain Their Case
Plaintiff: Basically, it was a long winter, so there was a lot of snow and a lot of stuff accumulating.

Judge: So you folks move into—is it an apartment or a house?

Plaintiff: It’s an apartment.

Judge: Okay. And the apartment is owned by him?

Plaintiff: Yes.

Judge: And you had a very bad winter. Why?

Plaintiff: Well, there were pools of water on the roof, and we had very bad leaks.

Judge: Did you complain to your landlord about the leaks?

Plaintiff: Yes.

Judge: Were the leaks fixed?

Plaintiff: No. They said they couldn’t address it because it was dangerous conditions on the roof—there was ice and whatever.

Judge: So when the ice melted, did they address it?

Plaintiff: They addressed it in the spring, but by that time, mold had accumulated. We emailed them, we called them, we let them know—like, hey, you know, we have two children here.

Judge: So you were seeing mold on the ceiling?

Plaintiff: Yes.

Judge: Do you have pictures of it?

Plaintiff: Actually, we have a video.

Judge: A video?

Plaintiff: Yes.

Judge: Hold on, let me see.

(Plaintiff plays the video.)

Judge: Oh, I can actually see the dripping water. That’s horrible.

Plaintiff: That’s a pot catching the water.

Judge: Inside the apartment?!

Plaintiff: Yes.

City Declares Apartment Illegal
Judge: So at some point, you get frustrated and call the city?

Plaintiff: Yes, we reported it.

Judge: And when you called the city, what happened?

Plaintiff: They came to inspect and found the apartment illegal.

Judge: Illegal?

Defendant: The city changed the zoning laws, not us.

Judge: That’s not what this says. Do you have the document given to you by the city?

Plaintiff: Yes, here.

Judge: Let’s see.

(Judge reads the document.)

Judge: Illegal apartment. Two legal Class A apartments were subdivided into four.

Defendant: But that whole city block was torn down! The only building left was mine, so the situation changed.

Judge: You expect people, in the event of a fire, to climb up to the roof and jump to the next building like Superman?!

Defendant: In New York City, people—

Judge: Just stop. The bottom line is that the city said what you did was illegal. That’s why they had to leave.

Eviction and Moving Expenses
Judge: When did you get told to vacate?

Plaintiff: The 16th.

Judge: And you left on the 16th?

Plaintiff: Yes.

Judge: Where did you go?

Plaintiff: The YMCA. Then a shelter.

Judge: And you were in the system for how long?

Plaintiff: Six to seven months.

Judge: At some point, you tried to go back?

Plaintiff: Yes, but they refused.

Security Deposit Issue
Judge: Now, why didn’t they get their security deposit back?

Defendant: Damages to the apartment.

Judge: What damages?

Defendant: The banister was broken.

Judge: Do you have an estimate for the banister repair?

Defendant: $2,000.

Plaintiff: It was an $8 banister from Lowe’s!

(Judge checks the price online.)

Judge: I see the exact banister here for $64.

Judgment
Judge: Here’s what I’m going to do. I am deducting $150 for the banister.

Judge: The plaintiffs are entitled to the security deposit minus the banister cost.

Judge: $1,250 for the deposit.

Judge: Plus, I am awarding loss of wages for two days—$247.20.

Judge: So, total judgment in favor of the plaintiffs for $1,497.20 plus court costs and prejudgment interest.

Judge: Good luck, folks.

Judge: We will hear argument this morning in Case 23-1300, Nuclear Regulatory Commission versus Texas, and the consolidated case.

Lawyer of Plaintiff: Thank you, Your Honor, and may it please the Court: First, the petitions for review should be dismissed because neither Texas nor Fasken was a party to the NRC licensing proceedings. Texas did not try to intervene in the agency adjudication.

Lawyer of Plaintiff: Fasken moved to intervene, but its request was denied, and the court affirmed the denial.

Lawyer of Plaintiff: And there is no sound basis for the court's ultra vires exception to the Hobbs Act's "party aggrieved" requirement.

Lawyer of Plaintiff: If the Court reaches the merits, it should reverse the court of appeals' judgment. The Atomic Energy Act prohibits the unlicensed possession of spent nuclear fuel's constituent parts while authorizing the Commission to license private interim storage of those substances.

Lawyer of Plaintiff: The Nuclear Waste Policy Act left that scheme intact.

Lawyer of Plaintiff: And, since 1980, the NRC's regulations have provided for both onsite and offsite storage.

Lawyer of Plaintiff: That system allows a substantial role for private market responses to the country's nuclear waste storage issues, subject to Commission oversight to ensure that storage is safe and consistent with statutory requirements.

Lawyer of Plaintiff: I welcome the Court's questions.

Judge: What does it take to be a party in these proceedings?

Lawyer of Plaintiff: In an adjudication, you would need to intervene, and the Commission's rules set out the process for intervention.

Judge: So when can a party -- when can an interested person intervene?

Lawyer of Plaintiff: The Commission's rules set out two requirements.

Lawyer of Plaintiff: One is that -- basically, a standing requirement, and that is, essentially, that you be an interested person, that your interests be affected by the outcome. And, second, the Commission's rules require what's called an admissible contention.

Lawyer of Plaintiff: And the rules were changed in 1990. The changes were upheld by the court. And, basically, the problem the Commission had confronted was that it would get requests to intervene accompanied by very vague assertions.

Judge: But -- so, aside from the substance, when can you intervene? When can an interested person intervene?

Lawyer of Plaintiff: Essentially, the Commission will issue notice that a licensing proceeding is under way or will soon be under way, and then it will give a certain amount of time for part --

Judge: How much? How much time?

Lawyer of Plaintiff: I think it was 60 or 90 days to give notice of your intent to intervene. And then there were written submissions.

Lawyer of Plaintiff: The Atomic Safety and Licensing Board passed in the first instance on various requests to intervene. And then there was an appeal available to the full Commission.

Lawyer of Plaintiff: And then Fasken sought judicial review of the Commission's denial of its request to intervene.

Judge: The regulation that you cited, how is that supported by the statutory language?

Lawyer of Plaintiff: The court in the case Union of Concerned Scientists that I referred to a moment ago, I think it's in 920 F.2d, said that this was an aspect of agencies' traditional broad authority to regulate their own procedures.

Lawyer of Plaintiff: And as -- as I --

Judge: Does it go -- it seems to go beyond the statutory language itself.

Judge: Is that correct? Do you agree with that?

Lawyer of Plaintiff: I agree that the statutory language in itself would not impose this requirement.

Lawyer of Plaintiff: And the Commission's prior rules had not done so.

Lawyer of Plaintiff: The -- the --

Judge: And in looking at the statutory language, it says that any person who requests a hearing and their interests are affected shall be admitted. That's a mandatory obligation as I read it.

Lawyer of Plaintiff: Yes.

Judge: And Fasken, their interest is affected and they requested a hearing.

Judge: Those -- those things are undisputed, right?

Lawyer of Plaintiff: Yes.

Judge: So --

Lawyer of Plaintiff: I -- I --

Judge: -- help me with the previous question.

Lawyer of Plaintiff: I guess I would say were, first, when Fasken appealed to the court from the denial of its request to intervene, it didn't make the argument that the Commission's rules were invalid or it had a statutory right to --

Judge: Well, it said it had a statutory right to intervene.

Judge: And I read the court opinion.

Judge: It didn't address that argument at all.

Lawyer of Plaintiff: It has said that it has a right to intervene, but it was asserting that right under the Commission's own rules.

Lawyer of Plaintiff: And I guess the second thing I would say is, at most, the argument you're suggesting would imply that Fasken ought to have been allowed to intervene, it ought to have been made a party.

Lawyer of Plaintiff: But the fact is it wasn't --
