Judge: He could have thought it was futile given the court precedent on the question, yes?

Lawyer of Plaintiff: It -- well, it -- it could have thought -- the arguments that Fasken made were actually that it was entitled to intervene under the Commission's own rules. It was not arguing that the rules imposed an invalid extra-statutory requirement.

Lawyer of Plaintiff: So it had no reason to think that --

Judge: Well --

Lawyer of Plaintiff: -- that that was invalid, but your --

Judge: -- actually, I -- I -- I've got it before me, and it says that they're entitled -- that they act -- NRC abused its discretion and acted arbitrarily and capriciously in an excess of statutory jurisdiction by not admitting them.

Judge: It -- and it goes on to talk about the policies and regulations, but it cites the statute in its petition for review.

Judge: And, again, the court didn't address it.

Lawyer of Plaintiff: I guess the other thing I would say is they could have sought en banc review.

Lawyer of Plaintiff: They could have sought certiorari review.

Lawyer of Plaintiff: And what they are in essence doing --

Judge: All parties, please raise your right hands. Welcome back to The People's Court. Next case on the docket: the plaintiff loaned the defendant money for a big warehouse party, but the cops shut it down because it was too noisy, and the defendant stiffed him. But the defendant says the party was on 420, and he didn't know about the whole pot day thing. He says the plaintiff actually stole all the money. It's the case of "Let's Be Blunt." Thank you, Douglas.

Judge: Michael, you are suing Ankit Patel for $2,590 that you say he owes you on the loan portion of a business agreement that you had with him. According to you, you do not owe that money to him because it was entirely an investment, and both of you lost money. Okay, what happened?

Plaintiff (Michael): Ankit and I met in late 2011 in the fall. We were introduced through a friend, and we started talking about possibly doing this nightclub-style event together. This event would be dancing, drinking, live DJs, etc.

Judge: And why were you talking about that?

Plaintiff (Michael): I had previously been involved in a similar type of event. Ankit approached me because he wanted me to help manage his event.

Judge: And the event you had been involved in, was it to make money or just to have a party?

Plaintiff (Michael): Kind of both.

Judge: And did you make money?

Plaintiff (Michael): Yes.

Judge: How much?

Plaintiff (Michael): About $1,000.

Judge: Okay, go ahead.

Plaintiff (Michael): So we were talking back and forth for several months. Ankit was trying to figure out the correct time to do it and was getting everything together. We were just trying to sort everything out. After several months of negotiations, eventually, on April 5th, we met.

Judge: Originally, it was going to be in December of 2011, but then you couldn’t get it together in time?

Plaintiff (Michael): Correct.

Judge: So then the date for the party was chosen to be April 20th?

Plaintiff (Michael): Yes, which happens to be my brother’s birthday.

Judge: And 420... What is it? Pot day?

Plaintiff (Michael): Yes, pot day.

Judge: National pot day or worldwide pot day?

Plaintiff (Michael): I’m going to go with worldwide. I don’t know.

Judge: I don’t know either. All right, you both probably know and won’t tell me. Okay, go ahead.

Plaintiff (Michael): So we met, signed a contract, and I gave him a check for $3,000. In the stipulations of the contract, it was agreed that if the party was not profitable, Ankit would pay me back the initial loan, as well as 10% interest. If it was profitable, we would split the profits 20% to 80%.

Judge: Now that doesn’t sound like a very good business deal for you.

Plaintiff (Michael): I gave out the loan for three reasons: one, because I liked Ankit and wanted to help him; two, because there was a significant chance we would make some money; and three, because it was fun.

Judge: Okay. Let me see the contract.

Plaintiff (Michael): (Hands over the contract.) I also have the copy of the check here.

Judge: (Reads contract.) You should always put “loan” on the memo line if it’s a loan. All right, then what happens?

Plaintiff (Michael): The night of the event, about an hour in, the police responded to a noise complaint and shut it down.

Judge: Why did they shut it down?

Defendant (Ankit Patel): We had liquor without a license. We had a full bar and were charging people an entrance fee.

Judge: Okay, so they closed you down because you weren’t allowed to sell liquor without a license. How many people showed up?

Defendant (Ankit Patel): 30-50 people.

Judge: And how many were you expecting?

Defendant (Ankit Patel): 600-700.

Judge: What did you do when the police came?

Plaintiff (Michael): I left with everyone else but came back 20-30 minutes later when the coast was clear.

Judge: How much money was at the door?

Plaintiff (Michael): $410.

Judge: Ankit, you said there was more?

Defendant (Ankit Patel): I wasn’t handling the money. Someone else was, but I don’t remember who.

Judge: That’s not evidence. Do you have proof the amount was wrong?

Defendant (Ankit Patel): No.

Judge: Then why aren’t you paying him?

Defendant (Ankit Patel): Because I felt like he didn’t do as much as he said he would. Also, he took all the money and left.

Judge: But you signed a contract stating the $3,000 was a loan. Why aren’t you paying him?

Defendant (Ankit Patel): I thought he would help more with organizing things. He left during the police raid and only came back for his projector.

Judge: But the contract is clear. You owe him the money.

Defendant (Ankit Patel): I just don’t think it’s fair.

Zero on your counterclaim against her also. Zero.

Judge:
Well, we have a tie in the People's Court and nobody wins anything. I don't know if this is going to settle the dispute between you two. What do you think?

Plaintiff (Anita):
I'm not going to say nothing to her. She's still a crackhead and I'm still going to live my life, and I'm still going to be there. And if she say anything or do anything, she’s just going to get beat up again.

Judge:
Okay, you might be back in court again, who knows?
Manipulation Techniques: gaslighting, guilt tripping, evasion, deflection, playing the victim

Dialogue:
Judge: The lawsuit, Leon Nron, you are suing Robert at Nikki for $364.71, a cost to repair a door that you say he damaged on purpose. Tell me what’s going on here.

Plaintiff (Leon): On May 10th, 3:00, we get off work. I usually give this gentleman a ride at least twice a week down to the bus stop.

Plaintiff (Leon): And you do that for him because you’re friends, coworkers, and not—uh, he’s a human being. Out of hospitality, I just, you know, I try to explain to them that it really takes me out of my way but, okay, I got to do it.

Judge: All right, so you do it. And what happens?

Plaintiff (Leon): So what happens is we’re going to the bus stop. The bus stop apparently—the bus leaves and I see the bus leaving, so we go to the next bus stop, which happens all the time, more or less. And I park three feet from the curb next to the—it’s a testing station for the water department. It’s actually like a fire hydrant, but a testing station. As he gets ready to get out of the vehicle, I see his foot going towards the door panel. As soon as I see the foot touch the door panel, I yelled at him, "Watch my door!"

Judge: So he’s going to push the door open with his foot?

Plaintiff (Leon): Yes.

Judge: Okay, go on.

Plaintiff (Leon): So before I even finish the door sentence, it went bang.

Judge: How open was the door when you heard "bang"?

Plaintiff (Leon): All the way. 'Cause if the hinge could’ve gone more, it would have. It went all the way, it just couldn’t go anymore, and apparently, that’s when it hit.

Judge: Do you have a picture of the damage to the door?

Plaintiff (Leon): Yes, I do, Your Honor.

Judge: Where is the damage on the door?

Plaintiff (Leon): In the middle of the door, in the tip of the edge.

Judge: Where is it? Close to the edge by the hinge?

Plaintiff (Leon): Okay, let me see.

Judge: This is where the door opens?

Plaintiff (Leon): Yes.

Judge: Okay. My confusion is—you're saying by the hinge, but if I’m sitting as a passenger in a car and I’m opening up the door, the open part is here. The hinge part is up front here. All right, and then it opens, opens, opens, opens, and then I walk out. Correct?

Plaintiff (Leon): Yes.

Judge: Okay, where is the damage? Close to this part or close to the hinge? Those are two opposite parts.

Plaintiff (Leon): Close to that part where the door opens.

Judge: So not close to the hinge, close to the opening part?

Plaintiff (Leon): Okay.

Judge: All right, what happened?

Defendant (Robert): Your Honor, what he said so far, almost correctly, almost. But it happened on May 10th. It was on a Thursday. He pulled me up to the bus stop, as he says, we’re friends and whatnot. And I said goodbye to each other. Right when I went to open the door, I was like, "Clank!"

Judge: What was that?

Defendant (Robert): I looked down, and it was in front of door H. He parked actually three feet away from the fire hydrant. I’m like, "What was that?" Then he’s like cursing, whatever, blah blah blah. He—yeah, but why didn’t you see it? You’re the guy opening the door. Why weren’t you more careful?

Defendant (Robert): He drives an SUV, and the fire hydrant was maybe about three feet up like this. I have a picture of the fire hydrant. Does anybody have a picture of the actual—?

Judge: I appreciate that you took such a close-up for me to be able to see the detail of the damage. I would love to see it in perspective to the car if you have any more pictures. Don’t hide them now’s a really good time. I don’t know who you brought them for, if not me.

Defendant (Robert): A repair door.

Judge: Excellent. Oh, no, that’s fine. I don’t really care about the repaired door. This is a picture you’re showing, damage or no?

Defendant (Robert): That’s the repaired door. There’s no damage on it.

Judge: Okay, on all of these, it’s repaired.

Defendant (Robert): Got it.

Judge: Is this the thing that was hit, or is this the thing that was hit?

Defendant (Robert): One is a little sort of like a pipe coming up that I guess is used. That’s where it hit. It’s the shorter of the two that it hit.

Judge: Wait, what?

Defendant (Robert): Here’s what I don’t understand. There’s that thing. There’s this thing, which is practically smacking you in the face. How do you not see it when you open the door?

Defendant (Robert): Ma’am, I’m sorry.

Judge: I mean, you’re the—let me ask you a question. When he came up to you and he said to you, "I want you to pay for this damage," your answer was this: "It’s not my fault. You’re the guy who pulled up here because your theory is you have eyeballs and you pulled up, so it’s your fault. Do you think it’s at all your fault?"

Defendant (Robert): That’s a tough one, but I’d say probably the driver for parking too close. Parking too close. Anybody think the passenger is responsible?

Defendant (Robert): Yes, the passenger needs to watch where they’re opening the door.

Judge: You think the passenger is responsible?

Defendant (Robert): Yes, ditto.

Judge: Why?

Defendant (Robert): Same thing he said. Responsible, you know. You have to be responsible for your own action. Fair enough.

Judge: Going inside the courtroom, I take partial. I do take partial of it.

Defendant (Robert): Well, you didn’t at the beginning.

Judge: No, I did, ma’am.

Defendant (Robert): No, you didn’t.

Judge: At the beginning, you’re telling him, "No, no, this is your fault." And then as a good—I'm going to quote you from your answer to the complaint: "Because I’m a good Christian, I decided to offer him half." So you weren’t doing it because you felt you were culpable. You were doing it because you’re so incredibly magnanimous. That’s how your complaint, your answer to the complaint read. Am I wrong? Did you from the beginning say, "Oh, I feel so bad, but you know, you pulled up, but I opened the door, maybe we should split it"?

Defendant (Robert): Yes. That’s what I said. I said to him, "I’m sorry." That’s the first thing I said to him. First off, when that happened, he peeled off cursing. I didn’t—and this happened on Thursday. Friday I called out sick.

Judge: Did you ever come out of the car and see the damage?

Defendant (Robert): I was already hurt. I didn’t want to see no more hurt. So this is my—I’m sorry. So, um, um, I’m sorry. This happened on Thursday, right? I don’t see the gentleman until Monday. Now Monday morning, I see him. His exact words—word for word, he says: "I said, I told him again, ‘Leon, I’m sorry for—you know, don’t worry about it, forget about it at the garage, because we pull out our trucks from the garage.’"

Defendant (Robert): So I said, "I’m sorry, don’t worry about it." Now I get in my truck to start my truck up. He pulls—he comes over, walks over to my driver’s side. And, you know, we start talking. He’s like, "Show me your license." We start laughing for what, three, four times? "Show me your license." I’m not thinking, I gave him my wallet to show him my license. What he does, he pulls out his cell phone—clank! He takes a picture of my license.

Defendant (Robert): Then he comes up, he goes, "I said, first off, what’s that for?" He goes, "Uh, we have two choices: you could pay me $300 or we could go to court." Then that’s when I said, "Your Honor, I’m not paying you nothing. Your fault. You should not pull up in front of the fire hydrant, three feet like that. Open the door, bang! What’s wrong with you?" Then, Your Honor, during the day, I said, you know what, in my mind as I’m driving, yes, it’s his fault. But, like, and like you said, Your Honor, I should’ve looked when I got out.

Defendant (Robert): So I took that into mind. At the end of the day, when I met him, I said, "Leon, listen, I’ll give you $150." I never saw the bill. I never saw the damage. Let me see the bill. He peeled off. I never saw the damage of the bill. Monday morning comes, his car was already taken care of over the weekend. It was done, so I didn’t see nothing.

Judge: Isn’t there something inside you that says, man, the guy was doing me a favor? The guy was dropping me off at the bus stop so I wouldn’t have to walk, but—and then not only that, that bus took off, so now he’s taking me to the next bus stop. Geez, like, regardless of what your legal obligation is, like why isn’t there like a little Christian thing inside you that said, that the guy was doing you the favor?

Defendant (Robert): I don’t know. Just—I'm just pondering.

Judge: Can I say something? So here’s a real question, okay? It’s a very simple case. What it’s about is whose fault is this? Is it—you say, well, it’s entirely his fault because he opened the door. And there’s a certain beauty to that theory. That’s your opinion. It’s okay. Yeah, it’s my fault, but it’s his fault too, Judge. So we need to split it. Why? 'Cause he’s the guy who pulled up to it. And there’s a certain beauty to that theory too.

Judge: However, when I look at this and I look at the pictures of the damage and where the damage is at the door, you could have not hit that item for a long way. You know, there’s many, many ways to open the door. One of them is your hands still on the door and then you get out. I realize you’re a big guy, but that way wouldn’t have hit.

Judge: He didn’t pull up an inch from the thing. He pulled up in a way that a human being could get out. It’s just not the way that human being got out. So I am going to find you totally liable for the full amount, not half and half, and order you to pay the plaintiff the $364.71. Good luck, gentlemen.

Judge: Karen Jefferson, you are suing your former landlord, Olay Deaayi, for $2,250—$1,920 of it being your security deposit, and according to you, $330 in interest on the security deposit. We'll talk about that in a second. What happened here?

Plaintiff: Prior to Olay, the previous owner rented the apartment through a management company.

Judge: And how long did you live there?

Plaintiff: One year.

Judge: One year. And what happened?

Plaintiff: I was offered a job position in Florida, so I got the job offer. I notified the plaintiff that—I mean, the defendant—that—

Judge: So she bought it during your one-year tenancy, right?

Plaintiff: Right.

Judge: So you said, "I'd like my security deposit back." And her answer was?

Plaintiff: When she went through closing, she said that she asked about it. Prior to that, I had talked to her realtor, and he called me asking if I had a security deposit. I said yes, and he asked me how much. I told him $1,950, and he said, "Okay, I'll get that during the closing."

Judge: So then when you talked to her personally?

Plaintiff: When I talked to her physically on the phone, she said she had asked about the deposit, and nothing was said. She said she had to take out a personal—I guess—ass suing the old landlord for the money.

Defendant: I bought the house around August. At the closing table, I asked for the security deposit, if there was any security deposit.

Judge: Did you ask that?

Defendant: Oh, I asked at the closing table.

Judge: You asked at the closing table?

Defendant: Yes, when the checks were already prepared.

Judge: Wait a second. What about a security deposit? Did you have a lawyer?

Defendant: Yes, I did.

Judge: Why didn't your lawyer ask that?

Defendant: He was there. I asked if there was any security deposit, and he said yes. Then I asked for the check.

Judge: Okay. And he said he's going to be giving it to you?

Defendant: Who?

Judge: Who is "he"? The lawyer? Your lawyer? Or their lawyer?

Defendant: My lawyer said he asked for it already. The seller's lawyer said it was going to be given to me the following week.

Judge: Next?

Defendant: I think like five or six days after, the seller of the house disengaged the flowing of the water in the house even before we moved in, even before the keys were given to me.

Judge: So what happened?

Defendant: Because Karen was there, I was like, "No, we can't do this." So I had to go to the water corporation, then I paid.

Judge: So your lawyer—

Defendant: I paid for the water, and there was a back bill that the prior owner hadn't paid.

Judge: Why isn’t your lawyer finding all this stuff out before the closing? Because that's kind of the lawyer's job.

(Cut to a discussion outside the courtroom)

Interviewer: So is the defendant responsible for giving the plaintiff the security deposit back, whether the defendant got it or not?

Bystander: No, he's not.

Interviewer: Why?

Bystander: Because he wasn't there for the initial contract or anything else like that.

Interviewer: So the defendant bought the house, right?

Bystander: Yeah, but, you know, he wasn’t in contract, like he said, with the original owner. So he didn’t get any money or anything.

(Back inside the courtroom)

Defendant: My lawyer promised to give me the check for the security deposit and the water bill—about $600-something dollars—but they have to pay me back.

Judge: They're not paying you back. They're calling it a credit.

Defendant: That’s what they'll— I don’t know.

Judge: Maybe they will. My water company wouldn’t pay me. They're just going to call it a credit. And then you have to fill out all kinds of forms if you want it back. You have to hand them your firstborn.

Defendant: Yes.

Judge: You know, I cannot believe how many people are treating you wrong. Unbelievable. But here's the thing—you have all the power at the closing table. And you kind of lose it after that. Now you have to chase people.

I have to tell you, there's only one person here who is not going to chase anybody, and that's her. And I'm going to explain why.

I believe every word you're saying. I believe you never saw the security deposit from the prior landlord. It just doesn’t matter.

When you buy a property, you buy it with all its assets and its liabilities. And this is one of its liabilities.

Defendant: I never knew it was going to be like this.

Judge: I know. I believe you with every fiber of my being. I know.

Defendant: Yes.

Judge: So, you are going to have to file a small claims case against— You have two choices.

One, you tell your lawyer, "Hey, how did this happen? I’m going to file a bar complaint against you if you don’t help me." So hustle up, get off your chair, and get some— you know—help me to get this money. Because your lawyer is sitting back, filing his or her nails instead of worrying about you. And I don’t like it.

Because this is part of the job at closing, to make sure that his client gets everything they’re supposed to get.

Defendant: Yes.

Judge: Okay. So that’s one. Or two, if you're sick of your lawyer, you can always file a small claims case yourself. But you have to do something to get both these monies back now.

And I know you didn’t bank on this, but I'll tell you who really didn’t bank on chasing anybody, and that’s her.

She did not destroy the place. She’s entitled to her money. I'm going to order you to pay her money.

When you leave here, I’m going to have a written order for you, so that you can show it to the next judge and say, "Look, I had to pay her back the money."

Because you should definitely get your money back from that seller. And your lawyer should definitely help you to get it back.

Because I can’t believe everybody’s sitting around the closing table going, "Yep, yep, yep, there’s that security deposit," and nobody knows that there's money due to utilities and everything else. This is really bad for you.

Plaintiff: Yes.

Judge: You’re not entitled to that interest. I don’t know how you calculated the interest.

Plaintiff: Well, it was the traveling back and forth and the—

Judge: Yeah, that's not how it works. You're entitled to the entire security deposit, plus the statutory interest on your security deposit, which is not going to be anywhere near the amount you calculated.

But you are entitled to those things, plus your court costs, of course. You should be made completely whole, and that’s what’s going to happen here today.

Plaintiff: Thank you.

Judge: Good luck to you. Verdict for the plaintiff
Manipulation Techniques: evasion, framing the narrative, dismissal, deflection, minimization, playing the victim

Dialogue:
Judge:
This is the plaintiff, Wallace Tillman. He says he met the defendant on a bus ride to a casino, and she asked him if she could borrow $200 to get her car out of the impound. He loaned her the money, and then she disappeared, thinking he wouldn’t be able to track her down. Well, you need to get up pretty early in the morning to outrun him. He’s here suing the defendant for $525, which includes the loan plus interest.

This is the defendant, Sandra. She says the plaintiff’s a married flirt who loaned her $200. When she was ready to pay him back, he told her he didn’t want the money; what he wanted was to rent a hotel room and have sex with her. She refused. He disrespected her, and that’s the only reason he’s suing.

Judge:
All parties please raise your right hands. You may be seated. Thank you. Mr. Tillman, what’s going on?

Plaintiff (Wallace Tillman):
Well, your honor, I met this young lady on a bus ride to the Sands in Pennsylvania, which is a casino. After that, once we came back home, I heard from her again. She told me her car was pulled over and that she had to go to the DMV to renew her driver’s license and registration because they were suspended. She also said her car was impounded and needed $200 to get it out. I felt sorry for her and loaned her the money.

Judge:
So you loaned her how much?

Plaintiff (Wallace Tillman):
$200, and I put it in writing. The $200 was going to become $535 if she didn’t pay me back on time.

Judge:
When was the loan made?

Plaintiff (Wallace Tillman):
On February 5th.

Judge:
And when was it supposed to be paid back?

Plaintiff (Wallace Tillman):
By February 8th.

Judge:
So she was supposed to pay you back, and she didn’t?

Defendant (Sandra):
No, I didn’t have the money that day. I texted him and told him I couldn’t give him the money before work, but I would have it later.

Plaintiff (Wallace Tillman):
She didn’t have the money on February 5th, and she didn’t have it when we saw each other on the bus. She lied and said she would give it to me after the bus ride, but when I went to meet her, she still didn’t have it.

Defendant (Sandra):
I didn’t have the cash at the time, but I told him I would get it later. I tried to pay him, but when I gave him the money, he said he wanted to go to a hotel room and have sex instead of getting the $200.

Plaintiff (Wallace Tillman):
That’s not true. I never asked her for that. I asked for my $200. She kept avoiding me.

Judge:
What happened when you met at the casino?

Defendant (Sandra):
When we met, I gave him the $200. But he told me that he and the bus driver had rented two rooms upstairs and said he wanted to go upstairs and have sex with me instead of taking the money. I refused, and I walked off.

Judge:
Did you feel disrespected?

Defendant (Sandra):
Yes, I did. He propositioned me, and I didn’t appreciate it.

Judge:
So, what happened after that?

Plaintiff (Wallace Tillman):
After I didn’t get the money, I went looking for her with the bus driver. We finally found her at the casino. I asked for my money again, and she got upset. She didn’t want to pay me. That’s when I said I was adding extra fees.

Defendant (Sandra):
That’s not true. He never told me he was adding extra fees. He just kept insisting I should give him the money.

Judge:
Let’s take a look at the texts. It appears there was quite a bit of communication. (Judge looks at text messages on the phone.) The messages show a series of texts from you, Mr. Tillman, asking for the money. You texted her multiple times, asking for your money, and then you said this needed to happen today. But there’s no mention of you being upset about the sexual proposition. Why didn’t you mention that in these texts?

Defendant (Sandra):
I was upset, but I couldn’t bring it up in the texts. My phone was broken, and I couldn’t retrieve all the texts.

Judge:
But you didn’t mention anything about him propositioning you in these texts, even though that’s your defense. You should have texted him, saying how dare you proposition me, but you didn’t. Instead, your texts just ask about paying back the money.

Defendant (Sandra):
I didn’t feel it was the right time to mention it. But I’m not a prostitute, and I didn’t want to give him anything after that.

Judge:
I’m not feeling much sympathy for you right now. You lied about the money, and you lied about the sexual proposition. I believe you owe him $525, which includes the $200 loan and the extra fees you agreed to.

Plaintiff (Wallace Tillman):
Thank you, your honor. I’m glad this is over.

Judge:
$525 for the plaintiff, plus court costs. This is what happens when a $200 problem becomes a bigger issue.

Defendant (Sandra):
I’m a little embarrassed. I know what I said happened, and I’m upset about the judgment.

Plaintiff (Wallace Tillman):
I was shocked when I heard her story. I never did anything like that. But I’m glad I got my money.

Judge:
Congratulations on winning the case, Mr. Tillman. This concludes the case.

Judge: Okay, Mr. Sporza, you are suing Ron. You've asked us not to refer to your last name. Ron has brought a posse of people. You are suing for $2,573.59 that you say he owes you for all you are out regarding a lemon that he sold you. Tell me what's going on.

Plaintiff: Good day, Your Honor. I was referred to Ron because he had a truck for sale. In speaking with Ron, I believe he misrepresented the reliability of the truck. I did test drive it. I had some concerns. The price started at...

Judge: What year truck was this?

Plaintiff: It's a 1903 Chevy—2003 Chevy. I'm sorry. Did I say 1903?

Judge: 2003 Chevy?

Plaintiff: Yes, a 2003 Chevy.

Judge: So, it’s 15 years old. 162,000 miles. Did you have a mechanic look at it before you bought it?

Plaintiff: I did not.

Judge: Okay. So, how did you know each other?

Plaintiff: He is roommates with a friend of mine for 30 years.

Judge: All right, go on.

Plaintiff: I did trust Ron when he expressed to me that he knew the vehicle, having worked on it for the company that owned it before, because he's in the automotive business. He stressed to me that he uses this mechanic that is really reliable and really knows his work. I drove it for just under 150 miles, and an oil coolant line blew, totally seizing the motor. Ron had stressed to me that he had changed the oil coolant line on the truck. Come to find out, there are two oil coolant lines. One is hidden underneath a total enclosure under the motor. Come to find out, the mechanic he had been using—who was supposed to come look at the truck when the oil line blew—had taken out a customer’s vehicle, smashed it, came back all bloody, and ended up in prison for some reason. I don’t know the exact reason why. This was all explained to me by Ron.

So, I feel that he used a very unscrupulous mechanic. Ron’s brother mentioned that when you change an oil coolant line, you should change both of them because they are a pair. He did not.

Judge: Did you ever take the car to a mechanic?

Plaintiff: No, I did not.

Judge: Even after it blew, you didn’t take it to a mechanic? Where’s the car now?

Plaintiff: It’s in a driveway at my boss’s property.

Judge: But you must have taken it to a mechanic who told you the engine was blown.

Plaintiff: The defendant told me that.

Judge: How does he know the engine’s blown?

Plaintiff: Because he came and did the testing and figured out that it is blown.

Defendant: Your Honor, I do auto body work.

Judge: Okay, we’re in court, and nobody has seen fit to have an actual mechanic diagnose this and figure out how it happened and why it happened?

Plaintiff: I know how it happened because I purchased the oil coolant line. Ron and his brother showed me that the oil coolant line blew.

Judge: And who am I supposed to get the answer from? That both should be changed at the same time?

Plaintiff: Ron’s brother told me that.

Judge: That’s not evidence. That’s hearsay. I need an expert.

Defendant: He told me he was coming out of work, it was pouring rain, and he was going up Southington Mountain, a steep hill. He saw the oil light come on, then he saw the oil pressure gauge go down to zero. He continued to drive the truck.

Judge: But your knowledge of whether he continued to drive it after the lights turned on is based on what?

Defendant: That’s what he told me.

Judge: You guys are fascinating. You take up my time in court, but you don't think to bring an expert witness to confirm what happened?

Plaintiff: Ron told me that the engine was seized.

Judge: You need a mechanic’s report saying the cause of failure was X. Do you have that?

Plaintiff: No, I don’t.

Judge: You’re suing for the money you paid for the truck, brake pads, registration, oil, and lost wages. What was the day of purchase?

Plaintiff: I believe it was the 7th.

Defendant: It was a good running truck when I had it. I feel really bad, but I don’t feel guilty. The line blew, and he ignored the gauges on the dashboard.

Judge: Did you sell it “as-is?”

Defendant: Yes.

Plaintiff: No, the bill of sale doesn’t say “as-is.”

Judge: Does the bill of sale have a warranty?

Plaintiff: No, it doesn’t.

Judge: The default position in every state is that a used vehicle sale is “as-is” unless otherwise stated. You, as the plaintiff, have the burden of proving your case. You have to show that the failure was due to something the defendant did wrong, not just that the car broke down.

Judge: My verdict is for the defendant. Good luck.
Manipulation Techniques: persuasion, evasion, framing the narrative, deflection

Dialogue:
Judge:
Peter, you are suing Victor and Scott—that's how everybody wants to be referred to, just by their first names—for $2,670, which you say it will cost you to remove a boat that Scott gave to Victor and Victor dumped on your property without your permission.

Plaintiff (Peter):
That's correct.

Judge:
What happened? How did this come to light here? Actually, you know what—put a pin in that for a moment because really, I want to know what happened here.

Judge (to Scott):
You had a boat that you got—how?

Defendant (Scott):
I had a boat that was given to me, which I then put up on a website for free. Victor got a hold of me and said he wanted the boat.

Judge:
How did you get the boat from point A to point B?

Scott:
I had it on a trailer, and I brought it to his property.

Judge:
What kind of property is this?

Plaintiff (Peter):
I have two commercial buildings with a big parking lot between them. It’s a parking lot for two businesses.

Judge (to Victor):
So, who gave you permission to leave it in the parking lot of two businesses?

Victor:
Well, um… I assumed it was a friend of mine’s property.

Judge:
Wait—whose property did you assume it was?

Victor:
Um… another friend of TAA.

Judge:
And you know her how?

Victor:
Through my brother, who has, um… you know, friends.

Judge:
I’m trying to understand how you go about thinking that it would be okay to dump a boat that you received for free in someone’s parking lot.

Judge (sternly):
I need you to put your big boy pants on now and explain to me why you thought that was possible. Who gave you permission to do that?

Victor:
Uh… I was given permission from, um… TAA to drop it off on her property. I believed she said that was her property.

Judge:
No—what did she say exactly?

Victor:
She had told me that the part where I left the boat wasn’t her property.

Judge:
Where’s her property?

Victor:
It’s right behind—kind of right together. It’s just separated by a fence.

Judge:
Why haven’t you moved the boat since realizing it’s in the wrong place?

Victor:
Well, um… when we put it there, before it was officially off the trailer, someone came to us and was on the phone with Peter. Peter had agreed that I could leave the boat there for $50.

Judge (to Peter):
Did that happen?

Plaintiff (Peter):
Not quite that way.

Judge:
What way did it happen?

Peter:
I got a call from a friend of mine who has a motor home. He stays in it once in a while and was around the corner of the building. He heard something going on and came out to take a look.

Then he calls me.

Judge:
And then you get on the phone with Victor?

Peter:
Yes. Victor said, “I need to leave a boat here on the trailer, and I’ll come by in the morning, and we’ll discuss what needs to be done.”

Judge:
Why wouldn’t you say no? Why didn’t you say, "Get your boat off my property"?

Peter:
Well, I try to accommodate—

Judge (interrupting):
No, no, no. That sounds so weird. Why didn’t you just say, "Get your boat off my property"?

Peter:
The next day, I came into work and saw a boat up on three piles of concrete blocks with some wood. Right away, I knew I’d been duped.

Judge:
When you say duped, why?

Peter:
Well, I know a little bit about boats—not much, but enough. I knew when a boat is not seaworthy and really should be disposed of. When I saw it up on blocks, I said, “This is trouble.”

Then, when Victor showed up, I found out that he had been duped too.

Judge (to Victor):
Did you go up and see the boat?

Victor:
No.

Judge:
How old are you?

Victor:
Twenty-two.

Judge:
Okay, you’re an idiot.

Victor:
Yeah…

Judge:
I cannot tell you the many ways that you are, because what you did was take his headache off his hands and make it your headache.

Then, by taking this behemoth off of a trailer and putting it on blocks, you have no idea the headache you’ve created. It is so monumental.

Judge:
How is it going to leave there?

Victor:
I didn’t get to that.

Judge:
Yeah, yeah—you’ve got to connect the dots, Victor. But in the meantime, what’s your plan? Because you can’t leave it there.

And two months have passed. You have no plan.

Judge:
Do you know what it’s going to take to get that boat off now that it’s off the trailer?

Victor:
You just hook up the winch—

Judge:
A winch on what?

Victor:
On the front of the trailer. The trailer has a crank that cranks boats on and off.

Judge:
You need the trailer.

Victor:
Yes.

Judge:
Do you have the trailer?

Victor:
I don’t have the trailer anymore.

Judge:
Liar.

Victor:
I don’t.

Judge (to Scott):
Do you have the trailer?

Scott:
No, I do.

Judge:
Show me proof you sold the trailer.

Scott:
I don’t have it with me.

Judge (to Peter):
So, you're suing him—why?

Peter:
Because I have to dispose of the boat.

Judge:
No, no—I know why you’re suing Victor.

Are you kidding me? Did it sound like I didn’t know you were suing Victor?

You’re suing Scott—why?

Peter:
Because I felt Scott took advantage of an unsuspecting victim.

Judge:
That may be true, but how does that give you a right to sue Scott?

There’s a concept in the law called standing. It means I can't just be a social justice warrior and sue people because I think something is unfair.

You have the right to sue Victor because Victor trespassed on your property and dumped something there.    Judge (to Peter):
You have the right to sue Victor because he trespassed on your property and dumped something there.

But you don’t have the right to sue Scott unless you can point to some reason why Scott has a duty to you.

Peter:
Scott misled Victor.

Judge:
That may be true, but he misled Victor—not you.

Victor could sue Scott, but you cannot sue Scott for misleading Victor.

So, your case against Scott is dismissed.

Your case against Victor? You’re 100% right—he dumped something on your property that now costs you money to remove. That’s his problem, not yours.

Judge (to Victor):
So, I am finding in favor of the plaintiff for the full amount of $2,670.

Victor—you need to get your life together. I’m telling you now: you need to start thinking.

Victor:
Yes, Your Honor.

Judge:
Good luck.
Manipulation Techniques: persuasion, evasion, framing the narrative, minimization

Dialogue:
Judge: Douglas Bottomly, you are suing Felix Ortiz, your former roommate, for five thousand dollars. That includes two thousand dollars for a car you claim he stole from you, 2400 for cash he allegedly took, and 600 for a security deposit he won’t return. Tell me what happened.

Plaintiff: I moved in with him in November of 2012. Everything was fine at first. The first incident happened—

Judge: Did you two know each other beforehand?

Plaintiff: No, I met him through a friend of mine. It was a quick decision—I needed a place, and I moved in.

Judge: Okay. Go on.

Plaintiff: The first incident happened in March of 2013. He took the keys to my truck—a Ford Explorer. I drive a tow truck, and he took all my equipment. He took my booster box, which was worth 150. I had just bought it. He took my toolbox, worth 500. A brand new radio I installed—he took that, too. He took my GPS.

Judge: How do you know he took it?

Plaintiff: He called me on December 15th of 2015 and admitted that he stole my money two years ago.

Judge: Wait—two years earlier?

Plaintiff: Yes, two years ago. He told me that he had stolen my car.

Judge: When did you stop living there?

Plaintiff: In 2015.

Judge: So it wasn’t until 2015, when things escalated, that he called and admitted to stealing your truck?

Plaintiff: That’s correct.

Judge: Let’s talk about the truck first. You eventually found it, correct?

Plaintiff: Yes. The truck was recovered. I drive a tow truck, and my company got a call from the police department.

Judge: That’s coincidental. So your tow truck company actually got the call to recover your stolen truck?

Plaintiff: Yes.

Judge: How long after it was stolen was that?

Plaintiff: About eight months later.

Judge: And how was it found?

Plaintiff: It was sitting on the street, unmoved.

Judge: So it sat there for eight months, and no one reported it?

Plaintiff: That’s right.

Judge: Was it accumulating tickets?

Plaintiff: No, it was parked legally.

Judge: What did you do with the truck once it was recovered?

Plaintiff: I didn’t have the title or keys anymore. It was gone so long I assumed it was chopped up. I threw them out, so I had to junk the truck.

Judge: Let’s talk about the money now. You claim that in August of 2014, money was stolen from you. Tell me about that.

Plaintiff: I had $2,400 because I was looking to move out. I mentioned to him that I had the money. The next morning, it was gone.

Judge: You mentioned to him that you had the money? Why?

Plaintiff: It was a mistake.

Judge: But why would you do that if you didn’t trust him?

Plaintiff: I thought that if I told him where it was, he wouldn’t take it. I figured if he knew I knew where it was, he wouldn’t steal it. But it disappeared anyway.

Judge: Did anyone else live in the house?

Plaintiff: No.

Judge: Did either of you have guests?

Plaintiff: He had guests.

Judge: Did you call the police when the money was gone?

Plaintiff: I went to the precinct, but they wouldn’t take a report because I couldn’t prove who took the money.

Judge: Did you confront him?

Plaintiff: I didn’t mention it until I was moving out. When I did, I told him, “Just admit you took the $2,400.” He didn’t say anything.

Judge: And then in December of 2015, he called you and admitted to taking your money and truck?

Plaintiff: Yes.

Judge: Felix Ortiz, what’s your response?

Defendant: I work hard. I take care of myself. I never steal from anyone.

Judge: What was the living situation? Were you a renter?

Defendant: Yes, I was paying rent. We lived in a two-bedroom apartment. He had a room, and I made the living room my room. We shared the kitchen and bathroom.

Judge: Did you get along at first?

Defendant: Yes, we were fine at first.

Judge: When did things change?

Defendant: About six months after he moved in, he started acting strangely. He would curse a lot. I had to call the ambulance for him twice—if I hadn’t, he could have died.

Judge: Did you steal his car?

Defendant: No.

Judge: Did you admit to stealing his car?

Defendant: No.

Judge: Did you steal his money?

Defendant: No.

Judge: Did you admit to stealing his money?

Defendant: No.

Judge: Why do you think he’s suing you?

Defendant: He’s also asking for his security deposit back, but he didn’t pay rent for February or March before moving out. I kept the deposit to cover that.

Judge: Do you have proof that you paid rent for February and March?

Plaintiff: I paid him, but I don’t have receipts. The only receipt I have is from when I first moved in.

Judge: If you’re paying cash, it’s on you to get a receipt. How are you going to prove you paid?

Plaintiff: I don’t have proof.

Judge: Do you have any proof that he admitted to stealing your truck or money? Any texts, emails, witnesses?

Plaintiff: No.

Judge: Based on what I’ve heard, I have no choice but to rule in favor of the defendant.

Plaintiff: This isn’t fair. An ex-con wins again.

Judge: This case is dismissed.

[After the verdict, outside the courtroom]

Reporter: Mr. Bottomly, you didn’t have enough evidence to prove your case. Any comments?

Plaintiff: I’ll take him back to court and do my homework next time.

Reporter: Mr. Ortiz, your thoughts?

Defendant: I tried to help him, but people take advantage. I told the truth, and the judge saw that.

Reporter: Thank you. The case is closed.

Judge: All parties, please hit your radiance. Welcome back to the People's Court. Next case on the docket: the plaintiff rented an apartment with all sorts of problems, including electrical, so she bailed. But the defendant says the plaintiff is such a hoarder, she couldn't even come in to see if there were problems. It's the case of "Hoarder We Hardly Knew Her." Thank you, Douglas. Monica, you are suing Jennifer. Neither of you want your last names mentioned for $1,550 of a security deposit that you want returned to you because you feel that Jennifer is unfairly keeping it. You feel that she breached the contract, and therefore, you're entitled to talk to me first. Okay?

Plaintiff: When I plug my electrical appliances in, everything was chipping out.

Judge: What was chipping out?

Plaintiff: If I'm in this room and I turn on the light in another room, this one would chip out.

Judge: So what's "chip out"? You mean the electricity?

Plaintiff: Yes.

Judge: Okay, go on.

Plaintiff: So because another apartment is downstairs, I think the electricity needed was to upgrade it to like a 220. My fan wasn't working. The refrigerator, if the microwave is turned on, it chips out the light in the other room.

Judge: Okay, so what did the landlord tell you?

Judge: Wait, okay, so that the kitchen... that is the kitchen, right? What's that white thing?

Defendant: That's right. So that's the before. This is the kitchen after she moved her stuff in, and you can see the china closet is covering the window, which is a clear fire hazard. So that's the after.

Defendant: But also, you can see all the items that are there, which is the fire hazard as well. So that was just one facet of the fire hazard. If you go to the next page, that's the before room, and then you can look at the actual... no, you can't even walk in there. Good Lord.

Judge: Okay, that's a fire hazard in the sense of what you have declared, and I realize you're the daughter of a New York firefighter, but you have declared that having all that stuff is a fire hazard, and I'm not sure... I'm not sure like she's not storing combustibles.

Plaintiff: I moved with it, not to keep it there because I have an outdoor...

Nobody could... forget about it, as they say. You couldn't do anything in there because they say here... so then, after I discovered that, I said I needed to come and inspect. I gave her a certain amount of time to correct.

Defendant's Lawyer: The Ninth Circuit holds that Rule 704(b) prohibits only explicit opinions that the "particular defendant had the state of mind required to convict." The government appears unwilling to endorse that full test. It seems to agree, contrary to Ninth Circuit precedent, that Rule 704(b) forbids an expert from testifying that people in the defendant's position always have the requisite mens rea.

Defendant's Lawyer: Such testimony never explicitly mentions the defendant, of course, but as the government seems to recognize, it is clearly in the rules of -- in the words of Rule 704(b), an opinion about whether the defendant had the requisite state of mind. In other words, it establishes a class of individuals, including the defendant, and it assigns a mens rea to that class.

Defendant's Lawyer: Agent Flood's testimony here was no different. He testified that in "most circumstances," people like the defendant know they have drugs in the car when they cross the border. The only difference between that testimony and absolute class-wide testimony is that he posited that people like the defendant usually, instead of always, have the requisite mens rea. But any such distinction is immaterial.

Defendant's Lawyer: Imagine an expert took the stand and said: I believe the defendant in this case probably or maybe 80 percent of the time -- or maybe there's an 80 percent likelihood had the requisite mens rea. Obviously, Rule 704(b) would prohibit that testimony. And the testimony here is exactly the same.

Defendant's Lawyer: It expresses an opinion, a probabilistic opinion instead of an absolute opinion, but an opinion about whether the defendant had the requisite mens rea.

Defendant's Lawyer: So that leaves the government's final objection, that Rule 704(b) should not be construed to prohibit what the government calls inferentially relevant testimony. And we agree with that proposition.

Defendant's Lawyer: The rule that I'm advocating today has been employed by the Fifth Circuit for over 20 years, and it makes clear that modus operandi evidence, for example, things like drug traffic organizations use couriers to transport drugs across the border, that they -- that drugs are extremely valuable, are perfectly legitimate.

Defendant's Lawyer: The rule in the Fifth Circuit is that testimony from which a jury might infer mens rea is perfectly permissible, but testimony that assigns a mens rea to the defendant or people in her class is not okay. And that's what Agent Flood did here, and for that reason, we'd ask the Court to reverse.

Judge: You put quite a bit of weight in your argument on the preposition "about." And that was a stylistic change, certainly non-substantive. Do we normally put that much emphasis or that much weight on stylistic changes to statutes?

Defendant's Lawyer: Well, I think what I would say, Your Honor, is the Court obviously puts a lot of weight on the text of the statute. And so I start with the current text of the statute that is current law.

Judge: Okay. So, even with that, it says "about defendant." It doesn't say "about" someone else. So you're saying that even if it's indirect, it's about this defendant?

Defendant's Lawyer: Let me say two things, Your Honor. First, to finish my answer about the word "about," what we say is that preposition is important, just like the prior preposition "as to" before the stylistic amendment.

Judge: Yeah.

Defendant's Lawyer: Because it makes clear the Ninth Circuit's clear statement rule requiring an explicit opinion is too narrow. So that's what the preposition "about" does.

Defendant's Lawyer: Now you also asked me about "the" defendant. And our position there is very simple. It is that that word covers either a direct statement about the particular defendant or a statement about a class of people, including the defendant.

Defendant's Lawyer: I don't think the government disagrees that the rule covers a statement covering -- I'm sorry, that the rule covers a class of people including the defendant as long as it's stated in absolute terms.

Judge: Well, I thought the argument there was that if you say "all defendants," all people in this class do this, then it obviously would include the defendant.

Defendant's Lawyer: I think that's exactly right, Your Honor. So the only move left from there is to say, if the expert were to testify people like this almost always, virtually always, 99 percent of the time, probably, in most circumstances, all of those are still opinions about whether the defendant had the mens rea.

Defendant's Lawyer: They're just not absolute opinions. But they're still stating an opinion about whether a class of people including this defendant have the requisite mens rea. And I think that's why the text of the rule, opinions about whether the defendant had the mens rea, covers this exact case.

Defendant's Lawyer: So let me say the rule and the reason why I gave you the answer I did. Remember, the rule talks about not just any mental state or not facts that might lead to a mental state, but it talks about the mental state of the crime -- of the element of the crime to convict.

Defendant's Lawyer: So, here, that mental state is knowing, and that's what the expert expressly said.

Judge: Yeah. How about if I say --

Defendant's Lawyer: -- all kinds of those.

Judge: How about if I said something like, in my experience, the organization always informs the courier of the nature of the scheme and doesn't allow the courier to set off until she verbally assents?

Defendant's Lawyer: Right. I think, at some point, you get so close that that may well be a statement about mens rea. But what I want to emphasize, Your Honor --

Judge: I guess what I'm suggesting -- and it's really the same point that another judge was making, I think -- is that it just seems at that point a kind of game as to how you frame the testimony so that it falls just over the line, you know, or, instead, you can argue that it's just inside the line, but in the end, the government is going to get this testimony in.

Defendant's Lawyer: Well, if you think that's too formalistic, you could, of course, make the rule broader. All I'm saying is that there is a core of Rule 704(b) which is statements about the defendant's mens rea, and that's unquestionably what you have in this case.

Defendant's Lawyer: And how far out that goes and whether it covers your hypothetical would just be a different case on different facts.

Judge: But why is your statement --

Judge: What about --

Defendant's Lawyer: But it has to mean something.

Judge: What about your own -- oh, sorry.

Judge: Why is it a statement about the defendant's mens rea? That's the part that I'm not understanding.

Judge: So, you know, if the expert in a situation says 80 percent of the time, you know, when these conditions exist, the person knows. Why isn't there still an inference to be drawn as to whether or not the defendant is in the 80 percent or the 20 percent?

Defendant's Lawyer: Because the expert would not -- the expert is just giving a less-than-absolute opinion in that regard.

Judge: No, I understand, but there's --

Defendant's Lawyer: It's still an opinion about whether.

Judge: Right.

Defendant's Lawyer: And it's still about people in the defendant's class. Let me put it to you this way, Your Honor.

Judge: No, the question is whether the defendant is in the class. That's what I'm saying. The inference that remains from a testimony that is not a hundred percent.

Defendant's Lawyer: Yeah.

Judge: -- is whether or not the defendant is in the class that the expert has identified.

Defendant's Lawyer: Well, the defendant is certainly in the class, just to use the facts of this case, because the class is people crossing the border with a large quantity of drugs in their car. So the defendant is in that class.

Judge: Thank you. Douglas John Aramini and Jacquelyn Garcia, you’ll be representing John?

Plaintiff: Yes, he’s my fiancé. He’s the owner of the car, but I was the one driving.

Judge: All right. You are suing for $1,422, um, the defendant, because according to you, she damaged your car by hitting a motorcycle, which then landed on your car. Tell me what happened.

Plaintiff: Okay, so on June 20th, I went to my doctor’s appointment. I parked my car on Casin Boulevard next to a Starbucks, left my car, went to my doctor’s appointment, came back maybe 20 minutes later—well, I was only about a half an hour, hour. I came back and I was on the phone with my girlfriend. I see Starbucks, which is fully windowed out. I see a whole bunch of people waving their arms, and I didn’t think it was me. So, I closed the door, and a young kid, 16 or 17 years old, knocked on my window. I said to my girlfriend, “Let me call you back.” I opened the door, and he was like, “The motorcycle is on your hood.” And I said, “Oh God!” It was actually on my hood, the handlebars were on the right side of my car.

Plaintiff: Yeah, it was indenting. Did you take a picture?

Plaintiff: Yes, I have pictures.

Judge: Let me see it.

Plaintiff: Oh, goodness.

Judge: And you hadn’t noticed it when you got in the car?

Plaintiff: No, I didn’t notice it. I came from behind. Maybe that’s why. And I didn’t realize it was in my car. I didn’t think about it. You know, bikes are also on a tilt. I didn’t realize it was in my car. Plus, I didn’t notice it when I turned the key. Honestly, I didn’t notice it right away.

Judge: Okay, let’s go over this a second. So, when they called your attention to it, you actually got out of the car and saw that the motorcycle was leaning into your car at that moment. Did the motorcycle person come after you?

Plaintiff: No, there was no damage to his motorcycle.

Judge: So, what happens? When do you meet up with the defendant?

Plaintiff: So, they said to me, “We saw the Sentra.” There was a nice lady who came out, and she was my witness. She wrote for me. She had noticed Isabelle was trying to get into the spot. As she was parallel parking, it was clearly a tight spot, and they saw her bump it. It fell onto my car. They told her, and she tried to act like she didn’t know. They said to her, “We’re going to take down your plate.” She said, “Okay, I’ll pay for it.” I come back about 10 minutes later. When she showed up, she was walking slowly, and she said, “I’m so sorry. I tried very carefully to park, and I understand accidents happen.”

Plaintiff: I told Isabelle that I’ve had an incident like this before and didn’t get paid for the damage, so I’m still going to call 911. It was really hot that day. She invited me into Starbucks and bought me an iced coffee, and I started feeling really bad. So I called my brother and said, “Should I forget about the cops and just take her information?” My brother said, “No, you won’t get paid.” So I called 911.

Plaintiff: The police never came, but two officers happened to pull across the street. I asked them to come over. They were so nice and did. She proceeds to tell the officer that she would pay for the damage, and the officer said there was no reason to make a report.

Judge: Did you hit the motorcycle?

Defendant: No.

Judge: Thank you. And now we have a reason to write the report.

Plaintiff: She told the officer she would pay, but it’s not happening now.

Judge: Ms. Suie, what happened?

Defendant: Well, that whole thing was really funny. I didn’t hit the motorcycle. That day, I was in a rush. I was just looking for a parking spot. I saw a motorcycle in the way, and I was careful not to hit it. I parked carefully. Then I thought, “Great, I made it!” Then the motorcycle fell onto her car.

Defendant: I ran to the grocery store. I picked up something for my husband and went home. I didn’t talk to anyone. I didn’t know about the accident until I was told.

Judge: Why did you take her for iced coffee then?

Defendant: Because I felt bad for her. I didn’t know what had happened.

Judge: So you’re admitting you didn’t hit the motorcycle but still apologized?

Defendant: Yes.

Judge: Did you ever say, “I didn’t hit your motorcycle”?

Defendant: No, I didn’t say that. I did apologize, though.

Plaintiff: This is my witness statement. I was in Starbucks when I saw a woman driving a silver Sentra parallel parking. As she reversed into the parking spot, she hit the motorcycle, which then fell onto the hood of the car. I gathered my belongings and approached Jacquelyn Garcia to inform her about the damage.

Judge: Let me see all the estimates you have.

Plaintiff: I have the estimate from Allstate when the adjuster came out. I called her insurance company, then I had the estimate from the body shop I went to.

Plaintiff: So I called her before I went to the body shop, and she said she was okay with it. But when I got there, she didn’t want to pay.

Judge: Did she ever say she’d pay?

Plaintiff: She did not say she’d pay, but she did apologize.

Judge: She didn’t want to deal with insurance?

Plaintiff: Yes. She said she would pay out of pocket, but later said she could only give me $400.

Judge: And what did she say when you told her it was $1,300?

Plaintiff: She said she could only give $400 because she was unemployed. But she didn’t want to pay.

Plaintiff: So, I called Allstate. Allstate came out, but they denied my claim.

Judge: Isabelle, you didn’t admit you hit the motorcycle?

Defendant: No, I did not.

Judge: The Allstate adjuster said you admitted to hitting it, but you said you were afraid of getting hurt by the kids nearby.

Defendant: I didn’t say that.

Judge: You feel bullied by the kids?

Defendant: I don’t know about the kids.

Judge: I don’t believe you. I think you hit the motorcycle and tried to avoid responsibility. The kids didn’t bully you. I believe the plaintiffs and the witnesses.

Judge: I’m finding in favor of the plaintiff for $1,116.79 plus court costs.

Judge:
Thank you, Douglas. Okay, Angelo Carion, you are suing Axel Rodriguez, doing business as the Auto Doctor, for $3,455. What you say is the Blue Book value of a 1998 GMC truck. You have a counterclaim against them for $2,315 impound fees that you paid on his behalf that you want reimbursed and storage fees from the time of your dispute until today. Tell me what is going on, gentlemen.

Plaintiff (Angelo Carion):
Okay, how about we try this in English now? Let's try it in English. Thank you for accepting my case. Okay, I took my truck to Mr. X ride for clutch repair. He completed the clutch repair, and when I took the truck out, there was a rattling noise inside where he fixed the clutch. It sounded almost as if there was a nut or a washer in there. I took it back to him to fix. That's when the whole issue started, where on December 12th, like the truck... What day did you take it back to him?

Defendant (Axel Rodriguez):
I want to say the 8th of December.

Judge:
Okay, and do you agree with that, that it's the 8th of December when he brings it back to you, he has it towed there?

Defendant (Axel Rodriguez):
Yes, he has it towed there.

Judge:
Now, the 8th of December, when he has it towed there, why is it being towed there?

Defendant (Axel Rodriguez):
He's bringing it for me to sell. He’s not bringing it for you to repair.

Plaintiff (Angelo Carion):
No, he says that the transmission went on it. He wants to sell the vehicle, and I told him I can't sell the vehicle without a title.

Defendant (Axel Rodriguez):
Is that accurate?

Plaintiff (Angelo Carion):
I thought about selling it, but I never agreed to sell it. I never agreed to sell.

Judge:
What is the truck there for, to be sold or to fix?

Plaintiff (Angelo Carion):
To fix.

Judge:
To fix, okay. What is the quote he gave you to fix it?

Defendant (Axel Rodriguez):
Let me see the paperwork.

Plaintiff (Angelo Carion):
He didn’t give me a quote. He said it was there for me to sell.

Defendant (Axel Rodriguez):
Yes, not to fix.

Plaintiff (Angelo Carion):
No, if you see...

Defendant (Axel Rodriguez):
How much did he agree for you to sell it for?

Plaintiff (Angelo Carion):
He wanted to try to get $1,000 for the truck.

Defendant (Axel Rodriguez):
Did he give you the title so you could sell it?

Plaintiff (Angelo Carion):
No, that’s what the issue is. I couldn’t take the truck because he never showed me ownership of the vehicle. He never had a title for it. If you notice...

Defendant (Axel Rodriguez):
What did you do with my property for four days? He left it outside. It was out there for... I don't know how long.

Plaintiff (Angelo Carion):
Where did you park it?

Defendant (Axel Rodriguez):
He parked it on a street parking outside the shop.

Plaintiff (Angelo Carion):
Is that not on my property?

Defendant (Axel Rodriguez):
I never took possession of it.

Judge:
Wait a second, he had it towed?

Defendant (Axel Rodriguez):
Yes, he had it towed. If he had it towed, how can it be parked outside?

Plaintiff (Angelo Carion):
Yeah, they left it. They parked it in a parking spot outside on the street.

Defendant (Axel Rodriguez):
No, Your Honor.

Judge:
Okay, so what happens? Four days later, you get a call from the police because your car has been stolen. The truck was stolen?

Plaintiff (Angelo Carion):
December 12th, 6:30 in the morning, I’m notified by the lieutenant of the Marcus Police Department that my 1998 GMC was in the custody of the Lower Southampton police department, and then I even signed a vehicle release report. With this release, he was able to recover the truck.

Judge:
So who ends up taking it out of the impound?

Plaintiff (Angelo Carion):
You do, upon his permission.

Defendant (Axel Rodriguez):
I did.

Judge:
But why, if he parks his car on the street and it gets towed, why are you taking it out of impound?

Plaintiff (Angelo Carion):
The tow company that we used, that dropped off his car, is the same company I use to go down. I told him about the expense, and I texted him about the amount. He agreed to get it out. When I come out of work, I’ll reimburse you for it.

Judge:
Is that in the text somewhere?

Plaintiff (Angelo Carion):
That’s in my text.

Judge:
Show me the text.

Plaintiff (Angelo Carion):
While you're looking for that, he takes it out of impound. He pays $650 to take it out of impound. What day do you take it out of impound?

Defendant (Axel Rodriguez):
A day before Christmas.

Plaintiff (Angelo Carion):
The day before Christmas, he takes it out of impound. The car is brought over to his place of business, right? He has it now. You go to pick it up, and what happens?

Plaintiff (Angelo Carion):
When I go to pick up the vehicle, I look at the vehicle. Both of the mirrors are busted, the ignition’s busted, the glass inside is busted. You have pictures of the vehicle?

Defendant (Axel Rodriguez):
He wouldn't let me...

Plaintiff (Angelo Carion):
Let me tell you why I couldn’t take pictures. Even with my antique phone... but I said to Alex, you know what, Alex, forget it. I'm just going to take the vehicle, and I'm going to take it with me. So he says, okay. He goes in his office, comes back with a piece of paper, hands it to me, and the paper says, “You owe me $650.” I’m like, for what? He says, “I paid for the towing and the storage and the days that I delayed in picking up the vehicle.” So he says to me, “You’re not taking the vehicle until you pay me.” And I’m like, well, I’m calling 911. I go outside. I call 911. They’re going to send a police officer, and all I hear is Alex saying, “That’s it. Shut the place down.” The gates go down, the locks go in, the keys go to lock, and Alex... I said, “Are you leaving?” I just called the police. He said, “I’m out of here.” He’s out of there, and I’m in there like an idiot with my phone. Finally, I wait half an hour. The police arrive, and at the end of the day, I do have a Philadelphia police report because that was a requirement to get the vehicle released from Southampton. The police tell me, “Well, you already have a police report, it’s recovered. You know where it’s at. This is a civil matter. We’re investigating murders and robberies and everything else. We can’t do anything else.”

Judge:
Okay, so here's what happens. I see that on... there's a couple of texts that don't really matter to me, and then at 12:19, the car has not been removed yet. You say to him, “I don’t have $500 to invest in your truck the week before Christmas.” Sorry. He answers to you, “You still have not picked up the truck.” Well, this is not an investment. Alex, you were recovering a vehicle that was in your possession. Please don’t take my kindness for weakness. I work for some very powerful attorneys, and I am politically tied in. I will work with you and put up half. Need an answer now. Let's stay on good terms and out of court. Your response is, “I’m having it picked up tomorrow. Can’t sell without the title.” He then says, “I hope so. Alex, you are an excellent mechanic. I value this relationship. I will bring you the title. Let’s see how bad the damage is. Hope truck is not totaled. Is the ignition busted? It’s amazing how good it looks just like you left it.” Did you...?

Plaintiff (Angelo Carion):
It looks terrible.

Judge:
Looks terrible. You have pictures of the truck?

Plaintiff (Angelo Carion):
Yes, ma'am.

Judge:
What is your position that he agreed to pay half of the impound fees?

Plaintiff (Angelo Carion):
Yeah, but that's not your position. That's not what you said in here, and that's not what he says.

Defendant (Axel Rodriguez):
When he came to pick up the car, I told him to pay me $650.

Judge:
Why did you take the $650 if it wasn't your agreement?

Defendant (Axel Rodriguez):
Because he jumped in the car and started pulling it out without paying.

Judge:
When did you tell him to pay you $325?

Defendant (Axel Rodriguez):
I agreed to $325 plus the title so that when I sell the car, I can recover the other half.

Judge:
None of that is in your texts.

Defendant (Axel Rodriguez):
No, I agreed to the $325 plus the title.

Judge:
Why don't you say that in the texts? Why don't you say that in court?

Defendant (Axel Rodriguez):
I will... I was trying to explain that I agreed to $325 plus the title.

Judge:
Okay, you’re making the situation confusing now. But listen. I am going to order you to pay $325 for the impound fee because that’s what you agreed to. That’s what you acted on to get the truck out. You’re not entitled to any other damages because he left the vehicle there. No further storage.

**Judge:** This is the plaintiff Dawn Harper. She says she rented an apartment from the slumlord defendant who didn't tell her the house was in foreclosure. When the new owners came in, they told her they were going to kick her out. The unscrupulous defendant hasn't returned her security deposit. She's not going to let this guy get away with stealing her money and is suing him today in this court for the $1,600 she's owed.

**Judge:** This is the defendant DeMarco. He says he sold the place the plaintiff lived in and let her live out the last month of rent with her security deposit, which she did. How she thinks she’s still owed this money is comical, he thinks, and soon the judge will think the same. He’s accused of trying to rip off a tenant.

**Judge:** All parties, please raise your right hand. Welcome back to The People's Court. Next case on the docket: the plaintiff says her landlord secretly sold the house she was renting, and the new landlord won't give her security deposit back. But the defendant says they're even. It’s the case of insecurity deposit.

**Judge:** Ms. Harper, you are suing your former landlord, Mr. DeMarco, for $1,600 in a security deposit that he refuses to return. What's going on?

**Plaintiff:** Well, he says that I lived out my security, and I didn’t.

**Judge:** Let’s back up. You rented a place from him for how long?

**Plaintiff:** Two years.

**Judge:** And you lived where?

**Plaintiff:** Upstairs on the second floor, and he lived underneath on the first floor.

**Judge:** And when was the first time you realized something had gone terribly wrong?

**Plaintiff:** I was notified in the mail by the new owners of the property. They told me I needed to contact them for any further information.

**Judge:** So what happened, Mr. DeMarco?

**Defendant:** I sold the property.

**Judge:** You sold it, or you got foreclosed on?

**Defendant:** I sold the property.

**Judge:** A short sale?

**Defendant:** Yes.

**Judge:** If it was a short sale, you were already in foreclosure proceedings with the bank, right?

**Defendant:** That’s correct.

**Judge:** Did you ever let her know about that?

**Defendant:** Yes.

**Judge:** Did he ever let you know about that?

**Plaintiff:** No.

**Judge:** Did you have a lease?

**Plaintiff:** Yes.

**Judge:** When did you do the short sale?

**Defendant:** June 20th, 2018.

**Judge:** So she says you owe her. You’re no longer living there, right?

**Plaintiff:** No.

**Judge:** So she wants her security deposit back. What’s your defense?

**Defendant:** On May 1st, I explained to her that she no longer has to pay rent. I was going to sell the property in June, and when that happens, she should start packing her things and find a new place.

**Judge:** So did she pay June rent?

**Defendant:** She only paid for May.

**Judge:** So you’re saying you didn’t accept June rent?

**Defendant:** No.

**Judge:** You told her she didn’t have to pay June rent?

**Defendant:** Yes, she didn’t have to pay June rent. She had to find a place to move.

**Judge:** Ms. Harper, did you pay June rent?

**Plaintiff:** I paid up until the day I left. When I got the notice in July, I paid him July rent. They asked me if I paid the rent, and I said yes.

**Judge:** So you paid him June and July rent?

**Plaintiff:** Yes.

**Judge:** Are the new owners coming after you for July rent?

**Plaintiff:** No.

**Judge:** What did the property manager say to you? Are they going after him for the July rent?

**Plaintiff:** Yes, they went after him for it.

**Judge:** Mr. DeMarco, did the new owners ask you for July rent?

**Defendant:** No, ma’am.

**Judge:** So is it your position that you didn’t take July rent from her?

**Defendant:** I did not.

**Judge:** How do you pay your rent, Ms. Harper? Cash or some other way?

**Plaintiff:** Money order.

**Judge:** So you have proof?

**Plaintiff:** Yes, I have proof of my security deposit and rent payments.

**Judge:** Fabulous. So why aren’t you paying her back her security deposit?

**Defendant:** As I said before, on May 1st, I agreed with her that she didn’t have to pay June rent or any rent as of June 20th.

**Judge:** But you hadn’t sold the place yet.

**Defendant:** No.

**Judge:** So how would you know to say June 20th?

**Defendant:** I told her in advance that I was selling the property in June.

**Judge:** But you didn’t know it would be June 20th, right?

**Defendant:** No, but—

**Judge:** So how could you possibly say that to her on May 1st?

**Defendant:** I don’t know.

**Judge:** How much was your rent, Ms. Harper?

**Plaintiff:** $1,611.

**Judge:** Mr. DeMarco, you collected her rent not once, but twice when you had no right to. Actually, you might have had a right to June rent, but you collected in July as well. Every answer you give me is evasive. You can’t answer directly because you have no defense. Pay the lady her money. Judgment for the plaintiff, $1,600.

**[Judge]:** Paul Green, you are suing Paige Clark for $3,692.38. You say she owes you and refuses to pay. Tell me what's going on.

**[Plaintiff]:** Yes, ma'am. My daughter came to me and said that she had a friend that was looking for a car and asked if I would sell her the Mustang I had.

**[Judge]:** What kind of Mustang did you have?

**[Plaintiff]:** A 2006 limited edition Mustang.

**[Judge]:** Okay. Was your Mustang for sale coincidentally, or did she just want it?

**[Plaintiff]:** It was going to be for sale in a week or two.

**[Judge]:** Okay. So you show it to her, she likes it, and what happens?

**[Plaintiff]:** Showed it to her, asked my daughter if she had the money. My daughter said no, but she would pay half down. Talked to Paige. Paige said she’d give me half down and make payments on the rest. I said okay. She got the car, we went to the title place.

**[Judge]:** Why did you sign over the title if she hadn’t paid the rest yet?

**[Plaintiff]:** Because she signed a promissory note for the balance.

**[Judge]:** So, does she ever pay you anything else?

**[Plaintiff]:** No.

**[Judge]:** All right. So why, then, do you buy her tires?

**[Plaintiff]:** That was only about a week later.

**[Judge]:** But why would you buy her tires?

**[Plaintiff]:** Well, eventually when we ordered the tires, she was going to pay for them.

**[Judge]:** How do you order tires without paying for them?

**[Plaintiff]:** A friend of mine runs a dealership, and we were getting them through the dealership at a discount. So, I said they’ve already been ordered, and they’re going to take it out of my friend’s paycheck if we don’t get them. I said I’d go ahead and cover them on my credit card for 30 days.

**[Judge]:** And what did she say?

**[Plaintiff]:** She said, "I don’t really want to do that." And then she came back with a text and said, "Let’s go ahead and get the tires. I’ll pay you back for it."

**[Judge]:** Do you have the text?

**[Plaintiff]:** Yes, ma’am.

**[Judge]:** May I see it?

**[Plaintiff]:** Yes.

**[Judge]:** While he’s looking for that, can I ask you some questions, Paige?

**[Defendant]:** Sure.

**[Judge]:** So, you are a friend of his daughter’s?

**[Defendant]:** Yes, ma’am.

**[Judge]:** Are you still a friend of his daughter’s?

**[Defendant]:** Yes, ma’am.

**[Judge]:** All right. So what happens here? You buy the car, and you’re supposed to pay money, and he says you didn’t. Why not?

**[Defendant]:** Well, after meeting him and giving him the down payment, I got a call from his daughter stating that he liked me, thought I was cute, and wanted to take me out.

**[Judge]:** What’s the age difference between you two?

**[Defendant]:** I don’t know.

**[Judge]:** You never asked?

**[Defendant]:** I don’t know.

**[Judge]:** I’m kind of curious. Anybody else curious? What’s your age?

**[Defendant]:** I’m 28.

**[Judge]:** And how old are you, Mr. Green?

**[Plaintiff]:** I’m 57.

**[Judge]:** So, the tires. Wait—you don’t go from "he likes you, he wants to ask you out" to "the tires." He likes you, he wants to ask you out—what do you tell his daughter?

**[Defendant]:** I said, "Sure, absolutely. I’ll go out on some dates with him."

**[Judge]:** And then what happened?

**[Defendant]:** During those dates, we were discussing that if things worked out, I wouldn’t have to pay anymore on the car—that those payments would be null and void.

**[Judge]:** Was it your idea or was it his idea?

**[Defendant]:** His idea.

**[Judge]:** That if you dated him, you wouldn’t have to pay it back?

**[Defendant]:** Right.

**[Judge]:** No, if it worked out or if you dated?

**[Defendant]:** If it worked out and we continued dating.

**[Judge]:** And all you had to do was sign your life over? I mean, what does "it worked" mean? And what does "dating" mean?

**[Defendant]:** I don’t know.

**[Judge]:** It’s your agreement. I’m asking you to specify what you meant.

**[Defendant]:** I mean, we didn’t make a contract on how long or anything like that.
