Judge: I understand. But it’s not exactly running. It’s not like he’s a fugitive.

Defendant (Frank McGaan): Oh, no. He was running. He was ducking. He wouldn’t get in touch with me.

Judge: How much did you pay him to do the repair?

Plaintiff (Douglas Brenson): I paid him $100.

Judge: And when you picked up the car, why didn’t you complain then?

Plaintiff (Douglas Brenson): It was at night, and I didn’t see the white.

Judge: Why didn’t you complain the next day?

Plaintiff (Douglas Brenson): I tried to talk to Frank numerous times about it. "Hey Frank, the headlight turned white. Can we do something about it?" He blew me off. He blew me off, then he got out of the area for years, and then what happened?

Plaintiff (Douglas Brenson): How did you come to sue him now?

Plaintiff (Douglas Brenson): I was at a mechanic shop. I needed some more work done, so I went out of my area where I live at and went to the mechanic shop to get the work done. I was browsing around, and I looked over and I seen Frank.

Defendant (Frank McGaan): First of all, I fixed his headlight about two, three years ago. He had an accident. It wasn’t crazy glue. It was glue that we used in the body shop. And I did him a favor in front of my house. It wasn’t at no shop or anything.

Judge: Did he pay you $100?

Defendant (Frank McGaan): No, I never got any money from him. Matter of fact, he still owes me for the brakes he paid me for that he didn’t pay me for.

Judge: And it’s his bumper that’s on the vehicle? The vehicle is in the parking lot?

Defendant (Frank McGaan): Yes. He brought the vehicle to the parking lot. The serial numbers on the parts will match the VIN number to the vehicle. So I want to know how that—that’s his headlight.

Plaintiff (Douglas Brenson): I had to bring it because the camera wouldn’t take a clear picture because you couldn’t tell.

Judge: Let’s go look at it.

Plaintiff (Douglas Brenson): You never put any tape on the lens.

Defendant (Frank McGaan): Wait, wait, wait. Is what they...?

Judge: I want to see the vehicle. And I never put any tape on the lens. Stay... let’s go. You got the key?

Defendant (Frank McGaan): Yes.

(They head to look at the vehicle.)

Judge: The plaintiff says the defendant screwed up his headlight repairing his car, but he waited three years to sue. Plus, if the plaintiff was so upset, why did he come back to get other repairs?

Defendant (Frank McGaan): The headlight was already like that before I put the tape on. Where’d you put the glue? If you open the hood, I’ll show you where the glue was. The brackets that hold the headlight in place were broken. He needed a new headlight, but he didn’t have money for a new headlight, so he had me repair that headlight. Did he pay you for the repair? No, he didn’t.

Plaintiff (Douglas Brenson): No, I did not. That’s his original headlight.

Defendant (Frank McGaan): Well, you did.

Plaintiff (Douglas Brenson): Where is it that you glued it?

Defendant (Frank McGaan): Behind the grill, the tabs are all the way down there. So I don’t see how any glue got on this lens.

Plaintiff (Douglas Brenson): Why didn’t you just use the hardware you’re supposed to use?

Defendant (Frank McGaan): Because he never supplied me any hardware.

Plaintiff (Douglas Brenson): Didn’t my house realize you’re an A1 mechanic?

Defendant (Frank McGaan): You brought it to my shop. I work at a very reputable shop.

Judge: Well then why didn’t you just?

Defendant (Frank McGaan): Because he didn’t have the money for that, and how much could it cost to put the hardware? He needed to buy a whole new light. It was broken.

Plaintiff (Douglas Brenson): I told him it was a whole new light he needed, but the headlight was my light. You want to see my light?

Defendant (Frank McGaan): Come on right now.

Judge: Wait, wait, wait. Stay... I want to see the vehicle.

Plaintiff (Douglas Brenson): No problem. Matter of fact, I will hold the headlight in place, and this is why you need a new headlight.

Defendant (Frank McGaan): Gentlemen, hold on.

Judge: Okay, let’s go back in.

Judge: Welcome back to the People’s Court. Harvey Levin here. Is waiting three years in a situation like this too long?

Defendant (Frank McGaan): Yes, it is.

Judge: Well, I think three years is a tough case to try to prove for something of this magnitude. Glue? I mean, three years later, most glue would probably have lost its adhesiveness by then.

Plaintiff (Douglas Brenson): But what about the notion that it created a milky film? Wouldn’t that have shown up three years ago?

Judge: Three years is too long. Definitely too long. I mean, anything could’ve happened. The guy could’ve messed with the lens on his own.

Defendant (Frank McGaan): That’s a good point.

Judge: And you're going to get the last word.

Defendant (Frank McGaan): He probably spilled the milk.

Judge: You spilled milk on it and that’s what happened?

Plaintiff (Douglas Brenson): I know that if there’s anything I know right now, it’s that you feel he did you wrong 100%.

Plaintiff (Douglas Brenson): But your feeling that he did you wrong is not as good as proof. And when you wait three years, you’ve basically lost your proof. You have nothing. You have three years of passage where a million things could have happened.

Defendant (Frank McGaan): No, no. We’re done, Mr. Brinson. We’re done.

Judge: My verdict is for the defendant. Good luck.
Manipulation Techniques: persuasion, emotional appeal, playing the victim

Dialogue:
Judge: This may be a frolic and a detour, but have there been real cases in which the United States has criminally prosecuted itself?

Defendant's Lawyer: I am not aware of any.

Judge: I mean, if such a case came here, what would—it's Monday morning.
(Laughter.)
I'm having trouble getting a grasp on this. How would this work? You would be arguing on one side, and one of your colleagues would be on the other side, and you'd be arguing against each other?

Defendant's Lawyer: I think that's right. I mean, it's a pretty incongruous idea. We think that if Congress wanted that result, it would have made it much clearer than just using the word "person."

Again, though, the reason that it matters why Congress uses just "person" or instead addresses the sovereign in the cause of action itself is that when Congress uses references to the sovereign itself in the cause of action, the only purpose those words can serve is to authorize recovery against the sovereign.

And so, in Financial Oversight and Management Board, the Court said that when Congress has expressly authorized suits in that way, it would effectively negate the statutory language to allow the assertion of a sovereign immunity defense. And in that circumstance, the Court has been willing to infer that Congress must have intended to waive sovereign immunity.

Judge: Well, it does negate the statutory language if you do the normal thing that we do in interpreting statutes, which is plug in the definition into the provision that uses the defined term.

So, here, plug in the definition to n and o, and then it negates the statutory language in the same way that it does in all those other cases.

So I've said that before. Here's what I really want to ask: What does "person" mean if it doesn't mean that?

Defendant's Lawyer: So we think that, just as we think in 1681q it means its ordinary definition, so too in n and o, we think it carries its ordinary—

Judge: What is its ordinary definition? Does it include individuals?

Defendant's Lawyer: It includes individuals.

Judge: Does it include partnerships?

Defendant's Lawyer: It includes partnerships.

Judge: Corporations?

Defendant's Lawyer: Yes.

Judge: Trusts?

Defendant's Lawyer: Yes.

Judge: Estates?

Defendant's Lawyer: Yes.

Judge: Cooperatives?

Defendant's Lawyer: Yes.

Judge: Associations?

Defendant's Lawyer: Yes.

Judge: Other entities?

Defendant's Lawyer: Yes, but not governments.

Judge: Just not government. I mean, that's a strange way to read a defined term, right? We'll take every part of the defined term and plug it in but not plug in this last listed thing before you get to the residual term.

Defendant's Lawyer: So that's what we think Congress did in 1681q, and we think it's plausible to think that it did the same thing in 1681n and o.

But, to the point you made before that, I mean, yes, if we—

Judge: Well, how about the point I'm making now?

Defendant's Lawyer: That is my answer, that we think that it carries a meaning other than its defined one, just as "air pollutant" in Utility Air carried a meaning other than its defined one, just as "person" in 1681q carries a meaning other than its defined one.

Judge: But we're taking the entire definition, except we're striking "government." We're taking the entire definition, except for one word, two words, "governmental subdivision or government," four words.

Defendant's Lawyer: Yes, because that is the ordinary meaning of "person." It is—

Judge: Okay. But—

Judge: Why—why are you doing that? I mean, why—put aside the ordinary meaning of "person."

Defendant's Lawyer: We're doing that because we think it's plausible to read the civil liability provisions in the same way that it would be plausible to read the criminal liability provisions.

Judge: Well, I thought you were doing that because of sovereign immunity, and it's important to protect the fisc of the United States against ambiguous derogations of sovereign immunity.

Defendant's Lawyer: So we're also doing it for that reason. We think that that's where the—

Judge: But Congress can waive sovereign immunity, and the question is whether Congress has done so.

Defendant's Lawyer: Yes. And nothing in the statute—may I finish?

Judge: Sure.

Defendant's Lawyer: Nothing in the statute says anything about sovereign immunity. You can only do that from implication. And we think, logically, the fact that Congress didn't specify sovereign defendants in the cause of action itself means that implication is not available here.

Judge: We'll afford you a couple of minutes for rebuttal.

Defendant's Lawyer: Thank you.

Judge: Thank you, counsel.

Judge: I have a few questions about how trials would be conducted if you prevail here. So what -- how would the government be able to go about proving that an offense -- that -- that a series of offenses occurred on the same conviction? I -- I assume they can introduce the judgment of conviction in all of those offenses, right?

Defendant's Lawyer: Yes.

Judge: Can they introduce the charging documents, which would typically say, on or about March 27, 2024, so-and-so broke into a house and burglarized it?

Defendant's Lawyer: I think the charging documents are harder, Your Honor, because they might be hearsay or the like. I mean, they might -- so, for the truth of the matter asserted in those charging documents, I think the government might have a problem, but --

Judge: Well, what if they're --

Defendant's Lawyer: -- most of these cases -- sorry.

Judge: What if they're not introduced for the truth of the matter asserted, they are introduced for the truth of the fact that this is what the person was charged with?

Defendant's Lawyer: I -- I think -- I think perhaps. That's something I've tried to research and just haven't found much law on. Something else I would add, though, is plea colloquies are going to be --

Judge: Plea colloquy --

Defendant's Lawyer: -- obviously, most of these cases are pleas --

Judge: Plea colloquies would -- they would be admissible?

Defendant's Lawyer: Yes.

Judge: And jury instructions would be admissible, so if the jury is instructed on Count 1, you must find that on or about March 27, blah, blah, blah --

Defendant's Lawyer: I think jury --

Judge: -- that would be admissible?

Defendant's Lawyer: I think the jury instructions might be admissible.

Judge: It's a court document?

Defendant's Lawyer: I -- I -- I think -- I think -- and, basically, what I would tell the Court is, you know, the Federal Rules of Evidence have many provisions about official records and court records and prior testimony and the like, and so, you know, those rules and precedent are readily available to administrate this rule.

Judge: Okay. What about the question of how the jury would be instructed on the question of whether prior offenses occurred on the same occasion? That was a vexing issue in a previous case, and I think the Court's opinion was well-crafted and nuanced, but I would be hard-pressed to reduce it to an instruction that would be easily intelligible to a jury. It's a multi-factor question.

Defendant's Lawyer: I think the jury instruction --

Judge: Have you given any -- could you give us a model jury instruction on this or do you have some idea how a jury could grapple with this question?

Defendant's Lawyer: I think those exist, Your Honor, and they just mostly track the language in the Court's opinion.

So the question for the jury overall, of course, is whether these prior offenses were committed on different occasions, which, as the Court put it in that case, turns on whether it was a single criminal episode or not. Then there are the factors: temporal proximity, geographic proximity, and the nature and relationship to the offense.

I think it's similar to other kinds of qualitative elements that juries sometimes find. Mens rea can sometimes be highly qualitative, materiality in a fraud case can be multi-factored in certain ways.

Judge: But those are not multi-factored determinations. Mens rea, you're asking the jury to determine what is in the defendant's mind. People make judgments about what is in the mind of other people all the time. That's a common experience.

Defendant's Lawyer: I -- I think what you had --

Judge: Materiality, I can't think of something offhand -- maybe you can -- that's quite as multi-dimensional and nuanced as this.

Defendant's Lawyer: Well, I think maybe one way to think about it, Your Honor, is you have a top-line finding that needs to be made, which is different occasions or a single criminal episode.

And then you have subsidiary facts that feed into that ultimate finding. And that's just -- you know, that's like most of the things, I think, we were just talking about, which is a top-line finding and then subsidiary facts.

And just so you have an opinion in the previous case that makes -- you know, kind of lays out those various facts, the jury could be instructed to consider those things.

Judge: So the judge says the temporal factor -- I don't want to dwell too much on this, but it will turn out to be important if you prevail. Temporal proximity is important.

And so then the jury says: Well, what does that mean? They had to occur on different days, different weeks? And what's the judge supposed to say?

Defendant's Lawyer: I don't think --

Judge: Well, that's up to you.

Defendant's Lawyer: I think that's right. And I think the judge would say --

Judge: It's up to you?

Defendant's Lawyer: In that situation, something like, the ultimate question you're asking is whether this is a single criminal episode or not when you consider these three prior offenses.

Judge: So then they say, well, what is a criminal episode? How do you define a criminal episode?

Defendant's Lawyer: I think we're repeating the same argument here.

Judge: I know. That's the problem. (Laughter.)

Defendant's Lawyer: Well, I think an episode involves sort of a single coherent plan or experience or event.

Judge: Like a whole criminal enterprise? That's a single criminal episode?

Defendant's Lawyer: I don't think so. I think there are limits temporally, but I don't think -- as the Court itself went back and forth on in the previous case, I don't think it's necessarily a single day or a single place.

I think the qualitative nature of a single episode allows for a little bit more than that.

Judge: Thank you.

Judge: Tisia Ingram, you're suing Sharice Caldwell, your dear old friend as you called her, for two trips that she took that you organized as well as a skirt she won't return. What happened? How long have you two known each other?

Plaintiff: Approximately 13 years.

Judge: Wow. Okay, and what happened?

Plaintiff: Over the course of our relationship, we've had a very rocky friendship with borrowing and loaning and being there for each other. Just so happens last year, I gave several bus trips, and she—

Judge: What do you mean by you gave several bus trips?

Plaintiff: I give bus trips. We go—

Judge: You said "give," but do you mean "organize?"

Plaintiff: I organize bus trips where we go to different amusement parks, food eateries, things like that.

Judge: Okay.

Plaintiff: So I gave three bus trips last year, and she agreed to go on two of them, bringing her family.

Judge: And according to you, the price of the first one that she owes is $365?

Plaintiff: Yes, ma’am.

Judge: And she went on that one? Did she have family members on that one?

Plaintiff: Yes.

Judge: And the price of the second one was $255?

Plaintiff: Yes.

Judge: And did she have family members on that one?

Plaintiff: Yes.

Judge: Okay, so she owes you $620, and she's never paid a penny of it. When was the first trip?

Plaintiff: The first trip was June 17, 2017.

Judge: And the second one?

Plaintiff: August 12, 2017.

Judge: And so what's happened between then and now? What kind of discussions have the two of you had about this?

Plaintiff: We've had verbal discussions, we've had telephone discussions, and even text messaging in regards to the money owed. We've even done a payment plan.

Judge: If you said that you had a rocky relationship with her in terms of borrowing and lending, why is it that you loaned—why did you front the money for the trip for her anyway?

Plaintiff: She's a friend. We've done things for each other. We've been there for each other.

Judge: So the fact that you've had a rocky relationship in exactly this kind of situation wasn't a red flag? That if you wanted to keep your friend, rather than have to sue her, perhaps you guys shouldn't engage in this kind of behavior of borrowing and lending?

Plaintiff: Exactly.

Judge: What are you nodding about? You're the one who borrowed.

Defendant: Okay, well, let's talk about the trips.

Judge: What do you say these trips were?

Defendant: Okay, basically, I'm here, Your Honor, because I didn’t say yes to the dress.

Judge: We’re going to get back to that later.

Defendant: This is the dress—this is my daughter's prom dress.

Judge: Prom dress?

Defendant: Yes, this is the reason why I’m here.

Judge: Okay.

Defendant: This is the dress—this is why I’m here. Because I didn’t say yes to this dress. I did not ask to go on these trips with Miss Ingram. Miss Ingram begged me to go on these trips. Miss Ingram doesn’t get along with a lot of her family members, and she begged me to go.

Judge: I'm sorry, are you suggesting that she put a gun to your head and made you incur this debt?

Defendant: No. That’s my friend.

Judge: Okay, but you’re supposed to pay her back, right?

Defendant: No, she told me that—please go because I can then pay her whenever.

Judge: Okay, so the idea, though, is not that she's going to fund your trip. You're supposed to pay her whenever you can?

Defendant: Yes.

Judge: Okay, so you're supposed to pay.

Defendant: No.

Judge: So you made a decision to go on the trip knowing that you would have to pay at some point?

Defendant: Yes.

Judge: When is "some point"? How many years have to pass before "some point" is ripe?

Defendant: Well, Miss Ingram knew of my ailments. One being, now, I just had surgery. I haven’t worked.

Judge: When you look at a judge and you say, "I'm not working," do you think that's a defense? It’s not a defense.

Defendant: I told Miss Ingram in the beginning that I could not afford the trips. I told her I couldn’t afford it.

Judge: Okay, but you still went.

Defendant: She begged me to go. She asked me to recruit my mom. She asked me to recruit my friend. And they also can be witnesses to her saying that I can pay her whenever.

Judge: We’re going to go with your version—you can pay her whenever. The problem is, when you two enter into an arrangement like this, and there is no clear deadline, it becomes my job as a judge to determine what is reasonable. And a year is past reasonable.

Defendant: But I haven't been working!

Judge: I don't care. Go get a job. You seem very able-bodied.

Defendant: I haven't worked in a year.

Judge: That is a "you" problem, not a "me" problem. A year, not a month, not a day, not a week. A year! That is a "you" problem.

Defendant: I have zero.

Judge: I don’t care! You still can’t figure out how to pay 20 bucks? Would we be here if you had been paying her a little bit here and there?

Plaintiff: Of course not.

Judge: Your total sum of payment is zero.

Defendant: She never asked me for it.

Judge: Don't talk. You don’t look good in this. Shut your mouth very soon. Verdict for the plaintiff in the amount of $620. And you still have the skirt.

Defendant: That’s because it was due whenever. If I had it, I would have given it to her. I didn’t have it.

Judge: I don’t buy that. Case closed.
Manipulation Techniques: gaslighting, evasion, deflection, minimization, emotional appeal, playing the victim

Dialogue:
Judge: All right, Bettyanne Pajura, you are suing John and his business, the farm where he kept your horses, because you claim he mistreated them by not nourishing them properly. Tell me what happened.

Plaintiff: I was at a farm in East for about 12 years. I’ve owned horses for most of my life and have never had issues before. The farm where I was boarding was investigated for compliance issues with shelters and horse care.

Judge: Were your horses affected by that?

Plaintiff: No, my horses were well taken care of at that farm.

Judge: Then why did you move them?

Plaintiff: There was a rumor that if the farm was shut down, the state could seize all the horses present on the property.

Judge: So, you moved them to John’s farm out of precaution?

Plaintiff: Yes.

Judge: Did you move one horse or both?

Plaintiff: I rode one horse over, but I boarded both within a few days.

Judge: What was the payment agreement?

Plaintiff: I initially agreed to pay $1,100 per month for both horses.

Judge: For how long did you pay that?

Plaintiff: Two months. Then, I negotiated a lower price of $850, which John accepted.

Judge: How long did you board your horses there?

Plaintiff: From January 28th through September 9th.

Judge: How often did you visit?

Plaintiff: On weekends only.

Judge: Did you notice any issues with the horses while visiting?

Plaintiff: Yes. I had repeated confrontations with John and different barn managers. I told him my horses weren’t getting enough water, adequate feed, or quality hay. He always assured me he’d fix it, so I trusted him.

Judge: When you saw them the following week, did they appear malnourished?

Plaintiff: I could see their weight fluctuating.

Judge: Did they lose weight consistently from January to September?

Plaintiff: Yes.

Judge: Then why did you leave them there for nine months?

Plaintiff: They weren’t severely underweight at first. When Megan, a western rider managing the barn, was there, my horses were well cared for. She rode them and fed them properly.

Judge: When did Megan leave?

Plaintiff: End of May or early June.

Judge: So, from June to September, your horses were losing weight?

Plaintiff: Yes.

Judge: Why didn’t you remove them sooner?

Plaintiff: I wasn’t fully aware of how bad it had gotten until I took one to a horse show. People in the horse community came up to me, shocked at how thin my horse looked.

Judge: But you didn’t notice before then?

Plaintiff: Compared to the other horses at John’s farm, mine didn’t seem as bad. But at the show, I realized how underweight they were.

Judge: What did you do next?

Plaintiff: I confronted John and removed my horses.

Judge: Did he refund you for the last month?

Plaintiff: Yes.

Judge: So, you’re suing for the entire nine months’ worth of boarding fees?

Plaintiff: Yes, including the time Megan was there.

Judge: John, were the horses being adequately fed?

Defendant (John): Yes, Your Honor. We followed the feeding instructions provided by Bettyanne.

Judge: Do you have records of what was fed?

Defendant: Yes, I have an affidavit from Jane Lyman, the only person who mixed the grain. She couldn’t be here today because her husband recently passed away.

Judge: I’m sorry to hear that. What does the affidavit say?

Defendant: It states that the horses were given fresh hay three times a day and that in June, Jane recommended increasing their grain, which Bettyanne agreed to.

Judge: Bettyanne, did Jane ever approach you about increasing the grain?

Plaintiff: No, that never happened.

Judge: Jane says you agreed to it.

Plaintiff: I only spoke to John about feed, and I was often in tears because he kept blaming his staff.

Judge: John, did Bettyanne repeatedly complain to you about the feeding?

Defendant: No, not until September when she confronted me after the horse show.

Judge: Bettyanne says she brought it up multiple times over nine months.

Defendant: That’s not true.

Judge: The issue here is that you were fine paying rent for nine months, then took the horses to a show, got questioned about their condition, and only then decided they were too thin.

Plaintiff: No, I repeatedly expressed concerns.

Judge: Do you have vet records?

Plaintiff: Yes, from both before and after moving them.

Judge: What do the records say?

Plaintiff: My vet saw them in the spring while they were at John’s farm and again in the fall after I moved them. He noted mild weight loss.

Judge: That’s not severe malnourishment.

Plaintiff: But my vet saw them four weeks after they had already been moved and started recovering.

Judge: Did you take them to a vet immediately after removal?

Plaintiff: I called right away.

Judge: Do you have medical evidence proving significant malnourishment?

Plaintiff: I have emails recommending increased feeding.

Judge: Emails aren’t enough. You’re claiming $7,200 in damages because you believe your horses were mistreated to the point you shouldn’t have had to pay board. But I need concrete proof.

Plaintiff: I have photos.

Judge: Let me see them.

(Photos are reviewed.)

Judge: These show weight loss, but I still don’t see evidence justifying full reimbursement. You visited every weekend, rode the horses, and didn’t take drastic action until September.

Plaintiff: I trusted John when he said he would fix the issues.

Judge: But you’re the one who waited nine months before taking serious action. Your vet only noted mild weight loss, and you were still actively showing the horse.

Plaintiff: But—

Judge: My ruling is for the defendant. Case dismissed.

Judge: Can I ask before you—before you go on, I'm wondering whether some of the problem here might be that we are not really focusing in on the fact that there are different kinds of experts in different kinds of testimony.

And so to the extent that this rule says that it relates to an opinion about whether the defendant did or did not have a mental state or condition, and it was generated in the context of a dispute about testimony related to the psychiatric or, you know, physical condition of a particular defendant, I'm just wondering whether the problem might be that this is really directed at not this kind of testimony.

The kind of testimony that comes from an expert who hasn't examined this defendant, doesn't know anything about this defendant, and is talking about framework or, you know, general modus operandi, whereas this rule seems to be targeting the kind of expert who has examined or knows something about this defendant's mental state.

What do you think about that?

Defendant's Lawyer: Well, I think that's correct as a historical description of what Congress had in mind here when it enacted 704(b), but I think the logic of that intuition supports our position in this case, not Plaintiff's, and that is because when Congress enacted Rule 704(b) against the backdrop of robust, public debate about what appropriate testimony—what appropriate expert testimony—should be offered in criminal trials involving the insanity defense, it was clear to everyone at the time that Congress was not eliminating all expert testimony in insanity trials.

Judge: No, of course not.

Defendant's Lawyer: The—the text of the rule—

Judge: I mean, isn't—isn't the line—I thought in the text had to do with whether it's an element of—of the offense or—or the defense.

So, for example, in Hinckley, an expert could still get up and say and opine on Mr. Hinckley's mental state. To the extent he has schizophrenia, he can say that.

And what they can't say is: And, therefore, Mr. Hinckley—or the—or the—any other defendant—did or did not have the mental state required to either convict or to make out an insanity defense.

That's the line, isn't it?

Defendant's Lawyer: Yes. And I think that is just an articulation of our position in this case.

And—and—to return to our earlier exchange, and I think this again goes to your question as well—

Judge: And so—and so—so if that's the case—

Defendant's Lawyer: —everyone understands—

Judge: If that—if that's the case, counsel, what's the difference between saying:

A) Mr. Hinckley didn't understand right from wrong.
B) A hypothetical person who meets all—looks just exactly like Mr. Hinckley couldn't have understood right from wrong.
C) All persons with the—with the characteristics of Mr. Hinckley do not understand right from wrong.

What is the difference between those three statements?

Defendant's Lawyer: If—if I've understood the examples that you have offered, those are all just variations on the expert taking the stand and saying that the defendant, in fact, met the legal standard. And, again, if I could step back—

Judge: And so they should all be—they're all impermissible, aren't they?

Defendant's Lawyer: I think those all could be impermissible, if—if I've understood the three examples.

But, again, earlier I think one of—one of—one of our exchanges was about, well, isn't it only—the fact that it is coming into court at all shows that it's being offered to prove the defendant's mental state.

And I think the Hinckley example shows that can't be the correct understanding of Rule 704(b).

Everyone understands when you're calling your examining expert in a trial involving the insanity defense to offer that expert's diagnosis of the particular defendant's mental state, the reason it is relevant is because you want the jury to draw some inference about whether the defendant does or does not meet the insanity defense—

Judge: So—

Defendant's Lawyer: —the—the legal standard for insanity.

That doesn't mean it's prohibited on 704(b) grounds.

Judge: If we think about the insanity argument, I mean an expert can come up to the stand and say a person with these sorts of behaviors or sorts of symptoms, I would say that that person has schizophrenia.

That's fine, right?

Defendant's Lawyer: Yes.

Judge: Okay.

And then could the person then say, I think that all people with schizophrenia have the necessary capacity to form an intent as to this crime?

Defendant's Lawyer: I don't think that that would be objectionable on 704(b) grounds.

Judge: Okay.

Defendant's Lawyer: I doubt it would be reliable, but it wouldn't be objectionable on 704(b) grounds.

And—and—and—

Judge: Because that to me—okay—is—is what the Hinckley controversy was about and why Congress passed this rule, that it didn't want the expert to go from the step of saying: Looks to me like this person has schizophrenia, to the conclusion that, okay, once we say that, we can say that this person has the necessary intent because all people with schizophrenia can form—you know—have the necessary intent or not.

Defendant's Lawyer: Yeah, I think the problem with the particular testimony that Congress was targeting in Rule 704(b) is that the expert is drawing an explicit link between the defendant and the legal requirement that the jury has to find.

That's what Congress said, no more of that.

Judge: Well, that—

Defendant's Lawyer: It created a public—

Judge: We'll hear argument this morning in Case 23-477, United States versus Skrmetti.

Lawyer of Plaintiff: Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court: This case is about access to medications that have been safely prescribed for decades to treat many conditions, including gender dysphoria.

But SB1 singles out and bans one particular use.

In Tennessee, these medications can't be prescribed to allow a minor to identify with or live as a gender inconsistent with the minor's sex.

It doesn't matter what parents decide is best for their children.

It doesn't matter what patients would choose for themselves.

And it doesn't matter if doctors believe this treatment is essential for individual patients. SB1 categorically bans treatment when and only when it's inconsistent with the patient's birth sex.

Tennessee says that sweeping ban is justified to protect adolescent health.

But the State mainly argues that it had no obligation to justify the law and that SB1 should be upheld so long as it's not wholly irrational.

That's wrong.

SB1 regulates by drawing sex-based lines and declares that those lines are designed to encourage minors to appreciate their sex.

The law restricts medical care only when provided to induce physical effects inconsistent with birth sex.

Someone assigned female at birth can't receive medication to live as a male, but someone assigned male can.

If you change the individual's sex, it changes the result.

That's a facial sex classification, full stop, and a law like that can't stand on bare rationality.

To be clear, states have leeway to regulate gender-affirming care, but, here, Tennessee made no attempt to tailor its law to its stated health concerns.

Rather than impose measured guardrails, SB1 bans the care outright no matter how critical it is for an individual patient, and that approach is a stark departure from the State's regulation of pediatric care in all other contexts.

SB1 leaves the same medications and many others entirely unrestricted when used for any other purpose, even when those uses present similar risks.

The Sixth Circuit never considered whether Tennessee could justify that sex-based line.

Because the Equal Protection Clause requires more, this Court should remand so that SB1 can be reviewed under the correct standard.

I welcome the Court's questions.

Judge: Much of your -- the latter part of your opening statement suggests that the -- well, seemed to suggest that there's an outright ban on this treatment.

But that's not the case.

It's really for minors.

So why isn't this simply a case of age classification when it comes to these treatments as opposed to a ban, as you suggested in your opening statement?

Lawyer of Plaintiff: It's certainly true, Justice, that the statute classifies based on age, but it packages that age classification with a sex restriction and says that for all adolescents, you cannot take these medications if they're inconsistent with your sex.

So I acknowledge that the State so far has not banned this care for adults, although I think that the arguments it's making that this isn't a sex-based line in the first place would equally apply in that context.

But the Court has likewise made clear that when you classify on the basis of multiple characteristics, you can't avoid heightened scrutiny just because you have a non-protected characteristic that accompanies the protected one.

And if you look at it from the standpoint of the plaintiffs who are actually affected by this law, the reason I'm calling it a categorical ban is because the State has left no out for those patients to obtain these medications when there's a showing of individualized medical need, and that is, I think, a -- a stark departure from how the State ordinarily handles issues related to measuring risks and benefits even in the pediatric context.

Judge: Well, is there no difference in the -- if a girl takes testosterone or if a boy takes testosterone?

Lawyer of Plaintiff: So the district court specifically considered this question in detail and found that with respect to the risks that the State had identified, it was not substantiated that there would be unique risks associated with --

Judge: No.

I --

Lawyer of Plaintiff: -- a cross-sex use of the hormones.

Judge: Is there no physiological difference?

Lawyer of Plaintiff: Certainly, I understand that there are biological differences between males and females, but when it came to the specific risk factors that the State was focused on, what the district court found is that many of those risk factors would exist regardless of the birth sex of --

Judge: Well, I'm more --

Lawyer of Plaintiff: -- who was taking those medications.

Judge: -- interested in whether or not there is a difference in testosterone and its reaction in a male as opposed to in a female --

Lawyer of Plaintiff: So --

Judge: -- and vice versa for estrogen.

Lawyer of Plaintiff: So, if you take hormones, they will prompt the development of secondary sex characteristics, and -- and whether you're a male or a female, if you take testosterone, you might develop a deeper voice register, you might have facial hair growth, and, in fact, that's one of the intended effects of these treatments because that can be critical to helping manage gender dysphoria that transgender adolescents would ever -- would otherwise experience.

But I think, when it comes to the question of whether that creates unique risks, the district court found that for the most part, the State had not substantiated those risks and that it leaves regulation of medication unrestricted even in contexts where these same medications or others would pose a comparable set of risks.

Judge: Counsel --

Judge: Counsel, you rely very heavily in your briefing on cases like Morales-Santana, which was about the distinctions between men and women when it came to adoption and things of that sort.

Here, it seems to me that the medical issues are much more heavily involved than in many of the cases that you -- you look to, including -- I understand there's a dispute between both sides on how extensive any evolution or increase in uncertainty in Europe has been and elsewhere.

And, of course, we are not the best situated to address issues like that, unlike in, you know, like Morales and Craig v. Boren and some of the other ones, where it doesn't strike me that they're intensely affected by medical considerations.

And if that's true, doesn't that make a stronger case for us to leave those determinations to the legislative bodies rather than try to determine them for ourselves?

Lawyer of Plaintiff: So let me respond to that concern with a couple of different points, Mr. Chief Justice.

I certainly take the point that you might think that states should have a lot of leeway to regulate when it comes to medical uncertainty.

And we're not arguing otherwise.

If the State is not restricting access to medications on the basis of a protected characteristic, that is only going to be rational basis review from the outset, and it's only in a circumstance where the State is saying your access to drugs depends on your birth sex or your sex generally that the Court would apply heightened scrutiny.

But, even at that stage, I don't think it's necessary for the Court to step in and suggest that states have no ability to draw those kinds of lines.

And I think this relates to my point in colloquy with Justice Thomas as well.

We, of course, recognize that if there's a lot of medical uncertainty or differential risk, and if the State can actually come forward and show that it has an important reason to restrict access based on sex, that can be taken into account in heightened scrutiny, and it wouldn't provide a basis to displace the state legislatures altogether from weighing this evidence.

But I think it would be a pretty remarkable thing for the Court to say that just because we're in the space of medical regulation, you are not going to apply the traditional standards that ordinarily are applied when there's a sex classification.

Judge: In this case, there is evidence from which one could infer that the magistrate would not have issued an arrest warrant were it not for the felony charge as to which there was no probable cause. But it does strike me that in many other cases, it will be very difficult—and I don't know how a plaintiff would prove that the charge for which there was no probable cause was the one that resulted in the decision to arrest as opposed to just issuing a summons.

Defendant's Lawyer: I appreciate that it may be difficult for the plaintiff to make that showing, and since it's an element of the claim, the failure to make the showing would mean that the plaintiff would lose. But I could point out a few ways in which a plaintiff might be able to make that showing.

First, if there's a bail determination and the judge sets higher bail because of a felony charge that was added to a misdemeanor charge, but it turns out that the felony charge was fabricated, that might be a circumstance where it's possible to show that the felony charge was the cause of the detention.

In addition, state law might provide that certain minor offenses result only in a citation or a summons, but more serious offenses can result in pretrial detention. That's another way in which the plaintiff could make that showing.

Ultimately, however, it would depend on the facts and circumstances of the case.

Judge: Presumably, the plaintiff would not be able to depose the magistrate and ask the magistrate, "What would you have done?"

Defendant's Lawyer: That's right. We see this kind of inquiry in other areas of criminal procedure. For example, in an ineffective assistance of counsel claim, you ask what the court would have done but for counsel's error, or in a Brady claim, what the jury would have done but for the suppression of particular evidence.

And you don't have people deposing the judge or the jury. You just ask objectively what would have happened in those circumstances. We envision a similar inquiry here.

Judge: So, to make sure that I understand your response, because I was trying to figure out from your brief exactly what your position was and if it was distinct from the respondent’s.

You see it as kind of a but-for inquiry, like you don't see it as asking, "Would it have been reasonable to detain him, say, for four days based on these two misdemeanors?" You think the right inquiry is to say, "Would he, in fact, have said four days is sufficient?"

Defendant's Lawyer: Would the magistrate, in fact, have authorized the additional detention?

And the bail example might make that particularly clear, what the distinction between us and the respondents would be. We would say if the magistrate says, "I'm issuing this higher bail," which it turns out the defendant can't pay because of the felony charge, that should be enough to show that that's an unreasonable seizure if the felony was a fabricated charge.

But I take the respondents to be saying that if the magistrate could have simply denied bail outright as a matter of federal constitutional law, then there's no Fourth Amendment violation.

Judge: But just to make sure I have this right, you do not think that we should engage on that issue?

Defendant's Lawyer: Correct.

Judge: And why not?

Defendant's Lawyer: It's not been the focus of the briefing, and it's not what the court below decided.

In addition, the unreasonable seizure question raises a variety of nuanced issues, for example, distinguishing between a fabricated charge that was presented to the magistrate and a fabricated charge for which there was probable cause that wasn't presented to the magistrate.

Distinguishing between the first 48 hours after the arrest and the pretrial detention that happens after, between bail and other procedures that might happen during the pretrial procedure.

It's just too complicated to get into without briefing.

Judge: And as to the two questions that you think we should address, the one that the plaintiff’s lawyer agrees with, and then you added that, of course, this would have to cause a seizure—but there's no disagreement on that one either, is there?

Defendant's Lawyer: I take the plaintiff’s lawyer to be suggesting that it's also possible to bring the Fourth Amendment malicious prosecution claim under the warrant clause based on the issuance of a warrant that's never executed and when no seizure was—

Judge: I see. I took the plaintiff’s lawyer to say something like, "We should just leave that to the side." And you think we shouldn't leave that to the side?

Defendant's Lawyer: I think that the Court should say that to bring this particular type of claim—the Fourth Amendment malicious prosecution claim—an unreasonable seizure is required. That’s in Thompson itself.

Of course, there may be a separate claim under the warrant clause that's cognizable under 1983, but that may have a different common law analog. It may be that the common law analog for that isn't malicious prosecution.

There's a separate tort that I found preparing for this case called malicious procurement of a warrant. Maybe that's the appropriate analog. That's why we suggested you limit your focus to the seizure provision.

Judge: But you're saying we should not—just to piggyback on the last question—you would have us stop at just saying that for this kind of claim, the baseless charge has to have caused an unreasonable seizure, but not get into how one would go about proving that?

Defendant's Lawyer: That's correct. That would be enough to resolve the circuit split.

Judge: I took the plaintiff’s lawyer to be agreeing with all of that. So maybe I'll just put in a request with the plaintiff’s lawyer to say on rebuttal whether they agree with all of that.

Judge: Thank you, counsel.

**Judge:** This is the plaintiff, Kurt Moer. He says he was stopped at a red light with his wife and two kids in the car, and out of nowhere—Boom! The defendant rear-ended him and claimed he dozed off at the wheel. He didn't want to go through insurance, he didn't want to call the cops, and now he doesn't want to pay for the damage he caused. Well, today is judgment day because he's suing him here and now for every penny of the $1,943 he's owed.

**Judge:** This is the defendant, Oomi Atiti. He says he may have dozed off for a minute, took his foot off the brake, and tapped the plaintiff's car with his. The plaintiff's car has a spare tire on the back. There was no scratch, no dent, no damage to the car. The opportunist is just trying to get a payday over a little mishap. He's accused of napping and tapping his way into court.

**Judge:** All parties, please step forward.

**Judge:** Welcome back to The People’s Court. Next case on the docket: The plaintiff says the defendant dozed off at the wheel and rear-ended him, but the defendant says it was a tap and there was no damage. It’s the case of "Tap That."

**Judge:** Mr. Moer, you brought your brother, your brother-in-law, and your son-in-law. Were you in the car too?

**Plaintiff:** He wasn’t in the car. I was in the car. He wasn’t in the car. He owns the car.

**Judge:** Got it. You are suing Oomi Atiti for $1,943 in damages to the rear end of the vehicle. What happened?

**Plaintiff:** It's pretty simple, Your Honor. We were at a stoplight on our way home, and we got rear-ended by Mr. Atiti and his vehicle. After checking and making sure my girls in the back were okay—

**Judge:** How old are they?

**Plaintiff:** They are fine. There were no injuries.

**Judge:** How old were they?

**Plaintiff:** Uh, two and four.

**Judge:** Were they crying?

**Plaintiff:** No, no.

**Judge:** So it wasn't a particularly hard hit?

**Plaintiff:** It wasn't a hard hit, no. I had a little neck soreness, but it wasn't anything that made me want to go to the hospital. I didn’t want to escalate it to that. So, we made sure everybody was okay, got out of the car, and I said, "Oh, you hit my car." He said, "Oh, I’m sorry. I fell asleep momentarily and I apologize. Is everyone okay?" I said, "Yeah, everyone’s okay, but let’s pull off to the side." And he’s like, "Well, let's not call the police because I just got insurance. I don’t want my insurance to go up." He said, "I’ll take care of whatever damage is there. I have no problem doing it." He was very nice.

**Judge:** Was it a good idea or a bad idea not to call the police?

**Plaintiff:** In hindsight, it was a bad idea.

**Judge:** Yeah, that hindsight is where we are, right?

**Plaintiff:** Right.

**Judge:** Because as it turns out, you have since learned several things: One, he won’t pay for the damage. Two, he didn’t even have insurance according to you.

**Plaintiff:** Correct. Well, he said he did at the time. I found out later he didn’t.

**Judge:** And you trusted him?

**Plaintiff:** Yeah.

**Judge:** Okay. It’s a risk when you don’t call the police that exactly this is going to happen. So what happens? You take a picture of his license?

**Plaintiff:** I took a picture of his license, I took a picture of his car, and a picture of the registration sticker.

**Judge:** Whose car is this?

**Plaintiff:** That’s his.

**Judge:** Okay. Is there any damage to his?

**Plaintiff:** Yeah. I can see the bent plate a little bit. I think that’s a bent plate right there. The hood, I think, has a crack.

**Judge:** All right. And then you also took a picture of the damage to your car, correct?

**Plaintiff:** Correct. The damage actually is underneath. I had two estimates done. There’s a reinforcement bar underneath that was bent.

**Judge:** Mr. Atiti, what happened?

**Defendant:** I did not know actually what happened. All I heard was a bump, you know, on the car.

**Judge:** What happened? You took your foot off the brake? What happened?

**Defendant:** I did not even recollect because I was asleep. We were by the red light, and all I heard was the bump.

**Judge:** Had you fallen asleep?

**Defendant:** I’m not sure. I don’t even know.

**Judge:** Did you tell him you thought you fell asleep?

**Defendant:** Well, I did not. I didn’t know what I said at that time. And as soon as the thing happened, he came down and said, "What happened?" I came out too to find out what happened, and I saw that there was really no problem. Whatever.

**Judge:** So did you have insurance when this accident occurred?

**Defendant:** I had insurance. What happened was they were about to terminate my insurance.

**Judge:** So did you have insurance that would cover this?

**Defendant:** I had insurance.

**Judge:** So why didn’t you just have your insurance pay?

**Defendant:** At that time, since we both checked our cars and did not find anything, he was ready to let go.

**Judge:** Then he calls you and tells you he has $1,943 worth of damages. What was your response?

**Defendant:** Less than an hour later, he called and said a shop checked his car and found something was rattling under the trunk. He needed to fix it, and it was going to cost $900. I said, "What? Now we just left the scene, and there was nothing."

**Judge:** All right. I’m ruling in the plaintiff’s favor but not in the amount he is asking for. I’m awarding $500 in damages. That is my judgment.

**Defendant:** I think it’s fair enough. I didn’t really think there was much damage, but he just wanted to get some money.

**Plaintiff:** How do you believe someone that lies about insurance? I came in good faith with proper estimates, and I think she ruled incorrectly.

**Judge:** The judgment stands. Case closed.

Lawyer of Defendant: I—I didn't mean to be suggesting a—a clear statement rule.

It's a structural point about the INA.

In the INA, throughout the statute, when Congress wanted to preclude review of categories of claims, we see it over and over that it does so with express language.

And here amicus is not pointing to any express language precluding review of CAT claims by aggravated felons.

Instead, he's pointing to what is really just a generic 30-day filing deadline as a way of arguing that Congress indirectly or implicitly precluded judicial review of these claims.

And I just think that, as a structural matter, that's hard to square with the INA, its context and its structure.

Judge: And haven't we also said in Guerrero-Lasprilla that there's a presumption of judicial review, that, you know, we not foreclose the possibility of judicial review over agency action unless there's clear and convincing evidence that Congress intended that result?

Lawyer of Defendant: That's right, Your Honor.

We don't think you actually need to get to the presumption in this case because we think the statutory structure and the context are sufficiently clear to show that Congress wanted review of these claims, particularly Section 1252(a)(4), which is what I referenced in my introduction.

But we don't deny that if the statute were truly ambiguous, that the presumption would apply here.

We just don't think you need to get to that here.

And just to get back to the Section 1252(a)(4) point, Your Honor, that is express textual evidence that Congress wanted there to be judicial review of CAT claims.

And I think it stands to reason—and then we also have Nasrallah, which says that CAT orders are reviewable together with removal orders.

And so I think it really does stand to reason that a CAT order must become final at the same time as a removal order so that a person can consolidate challenges to both orders into the same petition for review, which is what the zipper clause contemplates.

Another way of seeing the same point is that it's unlikely that Congress would have given express textual evidence that CAT claims are reviewable but at the same time written a deadline so short as to render many of those claims unreviewable.

Normally, Congress doesn't give with the one hand and then take with the other in that sort of manner.

Judge: Under your position, is it the case that an order that is final is rendered non-final by the filing of the subsequent application?

Lawyer of Defendant: No, that's not our position.

Our position is that when a person has a withholding-only claim, the Section 1228(b) removal order does not ever become final until the withholding-only claim is resolved. It's not that it is final, then becomes non-final.

It's that it never became final in the first instance until those proceedings are resolved.

And that's because—

Judge: Okay.

What is the time requirement for filing a withholding-only claim?

Lawyer of Defendant: The regulations contemplate that there—that the claim is raised when with the agency before the Section 1228(b) removal order is issued.

And here it was—it was raised immediately after. So if there were a situation where a person didn't raise it for 30 days after the entry of the Section 1228(b) removal order, we would agree that at that point, the order would be final and the petition for review timeline would have run.

But here, and as—as is usually the case under the regulations, these claims are brought contemporaneously with the entry of the Section 1228 removal order.

The problem is that the resolution of those claims takes much longer because these are often very fact-intensive claims, as opposed to a Section 1228(b) removal order, which has very little process because it's meant to be expedited.

Judge: And how long—do you know, by any chance, how long on average it takes to resolve CAT claims?

Lawyer of Defendant: We don't have exact numbers, but my sense is it usually takes several months if it's a real—if it's a serious claim.

If it's a meritless claim, these can be resolved fairly quickly because the asylum officer will say that there's no negative—or there's no reasonable fear.

Then the immigration judge will sustain that.

And both of those officers have to make those decisions within a 10-day span.

So they can sometimes be resolved relatively quickly.

But here, when it's a serious claim, here the IJ actually found that he was entitled to CAT—to CAT relief, and then that was reversed by the BIA.

When it's a serious claim like that, it could sometimes take several months to even over a year.

The dissent in Guzman Chavez pointed to some instances where they took multiple years, I believe.

Judge: So as between the two—in light of that, as between these two options, which one is preferable and why? The first would be the requirement that a prophylactic petition for review be filed, and the second would be your position that there's no need to do anything until after the CAT claim is resolved.

Do you think that your—that there's a risk that your position would lead to greater delay?

Lawyer of Defendant: I don't think it would lead to greater delay because people are going to have incentives to bring these withholding-only claims no matter what because they can always bring the claim before the agency, and the removal order will be stayed pendency—pending the agency's resolution of the claim.

So these claims are going to be brought either way if there's a plausible reason to bring them.

The only additional delay, under our interpretation versus amicus's—and I'm setting aside the protective scheme which I've already discussed—but the delay here would be the time needed for the court of appeals to review the withholding-only determination.

And I also want to just point out, in terms—in terms of the—any public safety concerns about giving this additional time or additional process to aggravated felons, it's important to bear in mind that they can be detained throughout the entirety of the agency proceedings, as well as subsequent judicial review proceedings.

And this Court affirmed that power in Guzman Chavez.

Judge: So just as a matter of government practice, when you have the order of removal but the CAT proceedings have not yet been concluded, what does the government feel itself free to do with the alien?

Lawyer of Defendant: So we—the removal order would be stayed as to the designated country of removal.

So we would not be able to remove the person to that country.

There is this issue of third-country removal.

And in that circumstance, I want to first point out that under Title 8, DHS does not pursue third-party—third-country removal while the withholding-only proceedings are ongoing.

But if it were to, the way it would work is this—

Judge: So let me—let me make sure I understand that.

You think you have the—the—the legal right—

Lawyer of Defendant: That's right.

Judge: —to—to send the non-citizen to some other country, where he doesn't have a CAT claim, but, in fact, the U.S. government does not exercise that right?

Judge: All parties, please raise your right hand.

Welcome back to The People’s Court. Next case on the docket: The plaintiff bought a car from the defendant and is now having issues with the title. The defendant claims the title is fine, but the plaintiff changed his mind. It’s the case of Every Lawsuit Has a Title.

Defendant: That would probably be the first person that the car was purchased from, I believe.

Defendant: Cherry Street.

Judge: Is your name on that title?

Judge: Is your sister’s name on the title?

Plaintiff: No, it’s not.

Judge: So what happened here? You tried to register it, and what did they say?

Plaintiff: They said it’s not filled out correctly, so I have to go back to the seller and ask them to request a new title with their name on it. They have to just re-sign it back. It’s called "jumping title," and it’s a problem.

Judge: So did you contact her and say, "Hey, we need to figure this out, I need to ask for a new title?"

Plaintiff: I did.

Judge: And what did she say?

Plaintiff: She indicated that she would help me with that, but then she texted me later that she was running into some personal problems and couldn’t help me anymore.

Judge: Is that accurate?

Defendant: That’s accurate to a certain extent. At the time, I was in the process of moving, and I did tell him that. I asked if he could be a little more patient with me.

Judge: So did you have any further contact with her after this?

Plaintiff: No, but once she told me she was moving, I went into panic mode and went to the court system because I was afraid of losing contact with her.

Judge: So what you’re suing for is the $1,500 that you paid, correct?

Plaintiff: Correct.

Judge: See, when you sell a car, it’s an "as-is" sale. The only thing you can’t do is sell something that isn’t legally transferable. If he can’t register it, he can’t drive it. If you do something funky with the title or refuse to help, that becomes a problem. And now he gets to rescind this contract.

Judge: Do you have anything to say in your defense that you haven’t already said?

Defendant: Does he get to keep the $1,500 and the car?

Judge: No, the car goes back to its rightful owner. You’re not the owner, and you don’t have a way to prove it.

Judge: Do you have a bill of sale that shows that you bought it?

Defendant: I should have one at home.

Judge: Wow. I don’t know what we’re going to do. Here’s what we’re going to do—because you don’t get to keep it if you want the $1,500. We’re going to make arrangements for you to surrender it to the police station. Let somebody else figure it out.

Defendant: That sounds reasonable.

Judge: How are you going to prove ownership?

Defendant: I should have proof at home.

Judge: The proof I’m talking about is a bill of sale.

Defendant: Yes, I understand.

Judge: If you show me the bill of sale and it doesn’t look fake, I will make arrangements through the courthouse for the car to go back to you. On your claim against her, I’m ruling in your favor for $1,500.

Judge: Judgment for the plaintiff. Case closed.

**Plaintiff:** This is the plaintiff, Tanisha May. She says she hired the defendant to drive her to work, and they got into an argument in the parking lot. **Defendant:** This is the defendant, Victor Janua. He says the plaintiff opened her door while he was driving and slammed it into a concrete pillar, then started a ruckus about her hurt hand. If anything did indeed happen to the plaintiff's hand, it was her own fault. He did nothing wrong.

So, I'm angry, so I made a statement about the car being cheap, and then he was like, "Oh, but you're walking though." Like, he was just going back and forth with me for no reason. Well, not for no reason, but nothing that I was saying was directed towards him, but he was taking it personally.

**Judge:** The car was cheap? Okay, go on.

**Plaintiff:** So, we dropped the first passenger off. As we're driving to my—

**Judge:** Driving to your car?

**Plaintiff:** I'm sorry, driving to my job. I'm sorry.

**Judge:** Your job. Okay. And you work where?

**Plaintiff:** Target.

**Judge:** Okay.

**Plaintiff:** So before we got to my job, I couldn’t tell him that I needed to get cash from the ATM.

**Plaintiff:** Sure.

**Judge:** Mr. Janua, can you tell me what happened?

**Defendant:** Yes, Your Honor. Like she said, it all started when I picked her up. She was complaining that she was late for work. I explained that I take people from point A to point B. Judge: But this would be the first case, right, because it didn't happen in Robinson itself, where we required a pre-enforcement challenge on the basis of the Eighth Amendment to the criminalization of certain conduct, putting policemen in this situation, right?

Defendant’s Lawyer: But I suppose in Robinson itself, if the person had been arrested once, been arrested a second time, and then he's arrested a third time, I would think he could bring a pre-enforcement challenge because the way the police were interacting with him was not respecting the Robinson principle with respect to Robinson himself.

Judge: How does the federal government do this? So in the brief, you talked about clearing the encampment at McPherson Square. Can you just describe briefly, if you can—do police then make individualized inquiries? How does this work?

Defendant’s Lawyer: Well, what happened there was, I think, the gold standard of the way this should be done, and larger cities have this ability.

The Park Service cooperated very closely with the District government. The Park Service does not have the sort of social services that a municipality has in D.C., and so that function is split.

These are special National Park properties, but the National Park Service relies, as the federal government does, on the federal protective service for buildings elsewhere, cooperating with the local government.

The local government's social service workers or nonprofits went out and interviewed everybody in the encampment in McPherson Square and informed them about the services available.

There was advance notice given that the encampment was going to be cleared within—I think it was 30 days. People were warned 30 days in advance. They were warned the night before and the day before, so they could collect their belongings.

Some just moved elsewhere, while others accepted the city's offer and went into shelters. That is typically how shelters—excuse me—encampments are cleared, particularly in cities that have provided amicus briefs explaining the problem.

This is not the scenario we've been discussing, where a law enforcement officer encounters a person for the first time. Smaller cities don’t have that capability, but Grants Pass does have outreach workers who carry on the dialogue. That’s how the encampment was cleared.

Judge: And so, given that experience and the fact that Martin has actually been the law since 2018, we don't really have to speculate as to how this works, right? I mean, this is happening—this is the law right now in the Ninth Circuit.

Defendant’s Lawyer: The Robinson principle is.

Judge: The Robinson principle as adopted in Martin. My understanding is, for example, California says that’s the law, they comply with it, and that’s how it is.

Defendant’s Lawyer: Yeah. They are not asking for Robinson to be overruled. What they are objecting to are the injunctions that go well beyond that.

Judge: Yes, I understand. I'm just responding to some of the questions you've gotten about how this rule works—whether it can work—because some seem to suggest that this isn't already happening on the ground in these places.

Shelters and workers are aware of what is available. People are being advised. The principle from Martin, at least in the Ninth Circuit, states that as long as there are more homeless individuals in a jurisdiction than available beds, the jurisdiction cannot prosecute homeless individuals for sitting, lying, or sleeping.

This is not a new rule. That is the law right now in that situation, correct?

Defendant’s Lawyer: Yeah, that’s what Martin says. I don’t want to say that the clearance procedures work perfectly in every case or that they’re always available, but—

Judge: No, I just mean we don’t have to speculate about how the rule works.

Defendant’s Lawyer: Or—yeah, how—

Judge: It's not a new thing being asked for today.

Defendant’s Lawyer: How it’s supposed to work.

Judge: Yes.

Defendant’s Lawyer: All I’m saying is that there may be imperfections—

Judge: All right, and then what happened?

Judge: And what happened?

Plaintiff: They agreed that it would be okay—a verbal agreement—that it would be okay to keep it there.
