Plaintiff: Mhm. All right, this is Motor Parkway. He's coming straight across as I'm coming in this way. I see him out of the corner of my eye. I blow the horn.

Judge: So you see him out of the corner of your eye. What does it look like he's going to do—go behind you or go in front of you?

Plaintiff: He crosses the lane I had been occupying and continues to the second left-hand lane. As I moved over, I stopped so he could come across.

Judge: So what happens now? He's in front of you, and what do you do?

Plaintiff: I come around him because I do not want to stay behind him because of his previous actions.

Judge: Why?

Plaintiff: I just want to stay behind him and not move for a while until he's a few blocks up.

Judge: Because he's crazy? If he did everything you're saying, he's crazy. So what do you do?

Plaintiff: I moved back to the outer left lane.

Judge: What was your game plan? To pass him?

Plaintiff: No, I did not want to pass him.

Judge: You just wanted him to proceed?

Plaintiff: I didn’t want him in front of me.

Judge: So what happens?

Plaintiff: As I pull up, I look at him, and I expect—

Judge: Wait. Is this the part where you turn around and engage with the guy who might have a gun in his glove compartment?

Plaintiff: No, I didn't engage.

Judge: Go back over there. Go back over there and then tell me. So you look over.

Plaintiff: So I look over, and I just go [gestures]. I'm expecting him to say at least go like this [gestures] and say, “Thank you for being aware enough to see what I’m doing and avoiding a collision with me.” I'm expecting some thank you.

Judge: And what do you get?

Plaintiff: And he just—

Judge: Gives you the finger.

Plaintiff: Gives me the finger. I say, “Okay, fine.”

Judge: Okay, fine.

Plaintiff: When the light turned green, I see him look in the rearview mirror. He cuts—no signal, no nothing—cuts right back to the outer lane that I'm in.

Judge: Okay, fine. I had enough room and enough time to actually hurry up and move back to this lane.

Judge: So what are you guys doing? Racing? Putting the rest of us all in danger while each of you playing chicken?

Defendant: We are going slow.

Judge: Not your turn yet.

Defendant: Okay.

Judge: And it's close to where I live. 10 to 2 is the ending of—see, I understand. I’m from Miami. I understand road rage. I feel it. I understand that other people have road rage. I just try to say, “Okay, how am I going to get away from this guy?” The easiest way to get away from a lunatic is to just drive at 5 miles an hour until the lunatic has gone. They're out of your way.


Judge: Please describe the events leading up to the accident.

Plaintiff: I was driving within the speed limit, and as I approached the intersection, the defendant suddenly swerved into my lane. I had no time to react.

Defendant: That's not true, Your Honor. I had my turn signal on, and the plaintiff was speeding. I was already halfway into the turn when their car hit mine.

Judge: (looking at the police report) The report indicates that there were no skid marks from the plaintiff’s car. This suggests no attempt to brake. Plaintiff, why didn’t you brake?

Plaintiff: I didn’t have enough time. It all happened so fast.

Judge: (to the defendant) The officer’s notes state that you did not yield the right of way. Can you explain?

Defendant: I thought I had enough space. The plaintiff came out of nowhere.

Judge: (reviewing further) The report also mentions a witness who stated that the plaintiff had the green light. Did either of you see the traffic signal change?

Plaintiff: Yes, I had the green light.

Defendant: I wasn’t looking at the light at that moment.

Judge: (pauses) Very well. I’ll review the full statements and evidence before making a decision.

Judge: The insurance companies found this 50/50. What is wrong with you guys? Is this how you lead your lives normally, or was this just a hot moment for both of you?

Defendant: Perhaps it was a hot moment for both of us.

Judge: Or perhaps you live your life normally like this.

Defendant: No.

Judge: Let me tell you something: don’t come down to my neck of the woods and drive.

Judge: Case dismissed. Both of you were equally responsible.

Judge: So this is more like the sort of initial hypothetical of criminalizing eating outside, not that you'd be doing something that was otherwise criminally culpable?

Defendant’s Lawyer: Yeah. Yes. I mean, I suppose there could be ordinances that the city would have about where you can -- you know, you can't eat at -- can't consume --

Judge: That is time, place, and manner.

Defendant’s Lawyer: Yes.

Judge: Final question. You mentioned with respect to states doing this. Why isn't the federal government arguing this case is moot in light of 195.530? This is the Oregon recently passed statute that I mentioned earlier. Why doesn't the government read that law as I do to prevent Grants Pass from enforcing its ordinances to block sleeping outdoors at all places and all times?

Defendant’s Lawyer: Yeah, no, I certainly agree there appears to be a pretty stark inconsistency between that state law and the ordinance. It hasn't been applied. It has to be objectively reasonable, I think --

Judge: So would the federal government --

Defendant’s Lawyer: -- but this isn't time, place, and manner at all.

Judge: Right. So what would your position be if the Court decided that as a matter of constitutional avoidance or whatever else that we don't need to hear this or reach this decision in this case, given this new state ordinance?

Defendant’s Lawyer: That -- that would be one possibility. It wouldn't answer the core Robinson principle point and the limitations on that point that has triggered the amicus briefs.

Judge: Right. But our typical rule is that if there's some other way, we don't necessarily comment on constitutional issues, correct?

Defendant’s Lawyer: Right. And yes. And that would be one course to see how what time, place, and manner meant under state law and how the Eighth Amendment could accommodate that or take it into account.

Judge: Thank you.

Judge: Thank you, counsel.

Plaintiff’s Lawyer: Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court: Robinson v. California holds that status-based punishment schemes are categorically cruel and unusual under the Eighth Amendment. The challenged ordinances inflict status-based punishment in both effect and purpose.

Although the City describes its ordinances as punishing camping on public property, it defines a campsite as any place a homeless person is while covered with a blanket. The City interprets and applies the ordinances to permit non-homeless people to rest on blankets in public parks while a homeless person who does the same thing breaks the law.

The ordinances, by design, make it physically impossible for homeless people to live in Grants Pass without facing endless fines and jail time.

The only question under Robinson is whether there's any meaningful difference between a law that says being homeless is punishable and a law that says being homeless while breathing or sleeping or blinking is punishable.

In other words, does adding a universal human attribute to the definition of the offense make the punishment conduct-based instead of status-based? The answer is no.

The purpose and effect of the second statute is exactly the same as the first, to make people with a status endlessly and unavoidably punishable if they don't leave Grants Pass.

Indeed, all the ordinances do is turn the City's homelessness problem into someone else's problem by forcing its homeless residents into other jurisdictions.

The injunction below leaves the City with an abundance of tools to address homelessness.

It can impose time, place, and manner restrictions on when and where homeless people sleep. It can ban tents and clear encampments. It can enforce a sleeping ban against homeless people who decline shelter. And it can fully enforce laws prohibiting littering, public urination, defecation, drug use, and violent or harassing behavior.

The only tool the City wants that it doesn't have is the authority to impose a 24/7 City-wide sleeping ban that forces its homeless residents to either move to another jurisdiction or face endless punishment.

The state police power is broad, but it does not include the power to push the burdens of social problems like poverty onto other communities or the power to satisfy public demand by compromising individual constitutional rights.

I welcome the Court's questions.

Judge: In Robinson, there was a statute that outlawed -- that said that "to be addicted" is a crime. Is there an ordinance here that says "to be homeless" is a crime?

Plaintiff’s Lawyer: So the language "for the purposes of a temporary place to live" bakes homelessness into the definition of the offense. Justice Sotomayor was talking about that earlier.

So when you combine that language with the definition of camping, what you have is an ordinance that says being homeless, while sleeping with a blanket, is punishable.

And as I just said earlier, the question becomes: when you attach the status to the universal attribute of sleeping, does it then transform the offense into conduct-based punishment instead of status-based punishment? And I think the answer is no.

Judge: A number of us, I think, are having difficulty with the distinction between status and conduct.

You'll acknowledge, won't you, that in those terms, there's a difference between being addicted to drugs and being homeless?

In other words, someone who's homeless can immediately become not homeless, right, if they find shelter.

Someone who is addicted to drugs, it's not so easy.

It seems to me that in Robinson, it's much easier to understand drug addiction as an ongoing status, while here I think it is different because you can move into and out of and into and out of the status, as you would put it, of being homeless.

Judge: Ms. Katherine David, you are suing Dorene Kho for $415 that you are out as a result of what you say was a sick puppy that she sold you. Tell me what happened.

Plaintiff (Katherine David): That is correct. On January 15, 2017, I answered an ad in The Long Island Newsday for golden retrievers. I contacted the phone number, which turned out to be Dorene Kho. I made arrangements with her after speaking with her on the phone to visit the puppies. I visited them that day, and I was very happy with the puppy I saw—or rather, the puppies I saw. We discussed adoption, and I left a $200 deposit because the puppy was not ready to come home yet.

I waited the recommended period of eight weeks to pick up the puppy, which I did on February 18. I gave her the remaining amount of $600.

Judge: So the total purchase price was $1,800, correct?

Plaintiff: Correct.

Judge: Is this your first golden retriever?

Plaintiff: No, this is my third golden retriever. I’ve had two prior golden retrievers, which I also adopted through Newsday, and I felt very confident applying to this ad.

Judge: So you bring the puppy home, and what happens? What day do you bring the puppy home?

Plaintiff: I brought the puppy home on a Saturday, which was February—

Judge: February what?

Plaintiff: February 18, which was a long weekend. I made arrangements for the dog to see my vet on Monday, February 20. The dog went to my vet on February 20, was examined, and I left a fecal sample with the vet to be tested.

I was informed the following day that the sample showed the dog had Giardia and Coccidia, which are parasites.

Judge: Was the dog showing any symptoms?

Plaintiff: Yes, she was, as a matter of fact.

Judge: What symptoms?

Plaintiff: She was showing a lack of appetite and having very loose, watery stools—consistently. In and out of the house, which is normal with a puppy, but the fact that she wasn’t eating, and just that something didn’t seem right, made me worried. That’s not what a puppy should be doing at this early stage.

Judge: So you bring the dog back to the vet?

Plaintiff: Yes.

Judge: And what does the vet do?

Plaintiff: The vet prescribes an antibiotic for five days, with a recheck thereafter, along with another fecal sample to make sure the parasite is gone.

Judge: Okay.

Plaintiff: The dog did not respond to the medications, so I had to go back to the vet again.

Judge: Did you go back five days later, or before that?

Plaintiff: I went back five days later.

Judge: That was your checkup time?

Plaintiff: Yes.

Judge: And what happened?

Plaintiff: The same thing. Loose stools, not eating. The vet then gave the dog an antibiotic injection to try to help. He also suggested I change her diet to a prescription diet.

Judge: And did you do that?

Plaintiff: Yes, I did.

Judge: Did it help?

Plaintiff: No, to no avail. The dog continued with the same symptoms.

Judge: So then you took the dog to a veterinary neurology specialist?

Plaintiff: Yes.

Judge: And what happened?

Plaintiff: They gave her intravenous fluids to sustain her while she was there. She had several seizures.

Judge: What did the specialist tell you?

Plaintiff: Dr. Bilderbank called me and told me what they had observed overnight. She said the seizures were something the puppy was not going to recover from. The dog was walking in circles, drooling at the mouth, and was not responsive.

She said they could do further testing, but even if I had a Black American Express card, she could not guarantee that the results would provide a solution.

Judge: So the puppy was put down?

Plaintiff: Yes.

Judge: Did you notify the defendant?

Plaintiff: Yes, two days before. I called her the morning the specialist told me about the condition and asked if she wanted the puppy back.

Judge: And what did she say?

Plaintiff: She said, “Do not bring that dog here.”

Judge: Ms. Kho, let me talk to you. How long have you been breeding dogs?

Defendant (Dorene Kho): Approximately 40 years.

Judge: Wow, that is a long time.

Defendant: Yes, it is.

Judge: When you first heard about the issue, what did you do?

Defendant: I was in shock. This was an amazing litter—

Judge: How many puppies were in the litter?

Defendant: Seven.

Judge: Did any of the other dogs have issues?

Defendant: Her neighbor owns the brother of the puppy that passed.

Judge: Did that dog have issues?

Defendant: That dog also had Giardia, but it survived.

Judge: Did another puppy from the litter die?

Defendant: Yes. I kept one of the puppies, and she passed away at my home.

Judge: So two from the litter had issues?

Defendant: Yes.

Judge: Then what’s your defense?

Defendant: I had no idea this would happen. I sent the puppy to Cornell University for a necropsy, and they confirmed it was a virus.

Judge: The law is clear. If the animal is unfit for purchase at the time of sale, the breeder is responsible for reimbursement.

Judge: I’m ruling in favor of the plaintiff for the full amount of $3,600.

[Gavel bangs]

Judge: Verdict for the plaintiff.

Judge: I want to articulate your rule. And you're right, it's different. But you keep saying it's a Fifth Circuit rule. I'm not sure it's the Fifth Circuit rule because the Fifth Circuit has criticized evidence with respect to what the drug cartel's knowledge is. So I think you're breaking from the Fifth Circuit, as the plaintiff's side breaks from the Ninth Circuit.

Judge: Everybody's trying to find that happy medium. But the Rules of Evidence don't say, even if you can get something in a different way, your mistake is—you can do it this particular way all the time.

Judge: You know, I'm thinking about—we have a lot of hearsay that you can't get in under hearsay, but you can get it in for other reasons that don't go to the ultimate guilt of people. Rules are rules, and there's a reason for them.

Judge: And I think what you're arguing is this rule says you can talk about modus operandi of drug traffickers generally, but you can't talk about a particular defendant or class of defendants and what their mental state is because that's what the rule tells you you can't do.

Judge: So it might feel like an exercise without a point, but it is an important point. Isn't that what you're trying to say?

Defendant's Lawyer: I think that's right. I think we are defending pretty much what happens in the Fifth Circuit. And the reason why that's the rule is because of the importance of mens rea.

Defendant's Lawyer: It's—as the court itself has highlighted in cases in recent years, it is the heart of our criminal law, and it's the heart of a jury's function to make that moralistic qualitative determination.

Defendant's Lawyer: So, if it seems formalistic when I say you're drawing a line, a protective barrier around mens rea, it's because that's what the right of jury trial is about and that's what the drafters of this rule recognized.

Judge: Has the federal government had any trouble convicting drug traffickers in the Fifth Circuit?

Defendant's Lawyer: Not to my knowledge. And, of course, the plaintiff's side has had 20 years to bring this issue up if it didn't like the Fifth Circuit law.

Defendant's Lawyer: So I think it works pretty well in the Fifth Circuit.

Defendant's Lawyer: But let me say something that would lead—if you agreed with the plaintiff's concession now that saying people in this position always have the requisite mens rea, you're going to have to draw some very difficult lines.

Defendant's Lawyer: What if the expert testified and said people like this virtually always, 99 percent of the time?

Defendant's Lawyer: What if the expert said maybe it's hypothetically possible a defendant—take the 83 out of 83, and the expert said maybe, maybe it's possible somebody could not have known, but I've never seen such a case?

Defendant's Lawyer: You know, would that count? What if the expert said, like in this case, there's only three situations I can—I'm aware of where an expert—I'm sorry—where the defendant doesn't have the mens rea, and those three situations are all different from this case?

Defendant's Lawyer: And then take it one step further in closing argument when defense counsel tried to argue, well, Agent Flood admitted there are a few situations where defendants don't have the mens rea. The plaintiff objected and said, well, you can't argue that other situations might be possible because, here, you named three.

Judge: But those all sound to me like cases in which Rule 403 might well come into play.

Defendant's Lawyer: Well, remember, what the Ninth Circuit says about Rule 403 and Rule 401 is that this testimony goes to the heart of a blind mule defense, like in this case.

Defendant's Lawyer: So it's the exact opposite of being irrelevant or unduly prejudicial.

Judge: Well, it's relevant, but it has a highly prejudicial value, and maybe it would be excluded by a lot of trial judges.

Defendant's Lawyer: Well, I think—the plaintiff's position is in line with the Ninth Circuit, and we concede there's force to it, that this evidence is damaging because it's so directly relevant to the defendant's mens rea. That's the problem with it.

Judge: Thank you, counsel.

Judge: Justice, anything further?

Judge: Justice, I—Justice is inviting, I think, even more chaos among courts.

Judge: If you're going to rely on 403 or 702 or any individualized decision-making by judges, you're just throwing this up into the air with no clarity.

Defendant's Lawyer: I think that's right, Judge.

Defendant's Lawyer: One way to think about this is you have Rule 403, which is maybe the broadest, most flexible of the Rules of Evidence to deal with prejudicial evidence.

Defendant's Lawyer: You have the next narrower rule, which is Rule 702, which is just about expert evidence and reliable bases for that evidence.

Defendant's Lawyer: And then you have the narrowest of them all, Rule 704(b).

Defendant's Lawyer: I'm only asking you today to rule—to apply Rule 704(b), which is—

Judge: I mean, once you're asking courts to go into asking every police officer how many of these cases have you had, when am I going to decide that 10 cases are not adequate, but 83 might be?

Judge: When there are 550,000 drug arrests, is 83 enough? I don't know the statistical answer to that.

Judge: Your rule is much simpler, isn't it?

Defendant's Lawyer: I think it—

Judge: It's—if you're going to talk about a defendant and what a defendant is thinking, that's off limits. If you're talking about modus operandi of what others do or don't do, that's okay?

Defendant's Lawyer: Right.

Defendant's Lawyer: So my rule has two components. One is, is it about mens rea? Unquestionably yes here because the expert used the word "know." And is it about the defendant?

Defendant's Lawyer: And our rule there is very simple as well—directly about the defendant or a class of people including her.

Defendant's Lawyer: That's the full scope of that.

Judge: And what is permissible, as you said, is if an expert says drug traffickers prefer to hire people that the traffickers know and trust rather than hide the drugs in a stranger’s car.

Judge: Or drug traffickers want to know where the car goes, so they're going to use a GPS or something like that.

Judge: That's modus operandi.

Defendant's Lawyer: Descriptive modus operandi evidence that does not speak to mens rea directly is perfectly fine.

Defendant's Lawyer: And let me add one thing to what you were talking about because I think it goes back to the Chief Judge’s question about cross-examination and maybe another Judge’s question about Rule 702 as well.

Defendant's Lawyer: If the expert testified either on direct or cross, look, this is my experience across most cases, but I can't tell you anything about this particular defendant because I don't know anything about this defendant—whether or not she had the mens rea—then you would have a real objection under relevance or Rule 702.

Defendant's Lawyer: Of course, you had nothing like that in this case, you know, so the plain implication to the jury was not just most people like this have the mens rea, but I believe this defendant has the mens rea

Judge: Angela Gaston?

Plaintiff: Yes, ma’am.

Judge: You are suing Ray Miller and L&M Auto for $5,566.18, the amount that you have put into a car that you paid him for. What happened?

Plaintiff: Yes, ma’am. I purchased a car last year, July 3rd, which was the day before the 4th of July.

Judge: So, the very same day—as it often is.

Plaintiff: Yeah, but by the same token, it was closed the next day, so I couldn’t contact him. The car started leaking.

Judge: The car started leaking? What—from where?

Plaintiff: Could you tell it was power steering?

Judge: You couldn’t call?

Plaintiff: So, when I did catch up with him, he told me to go ahead and get it fixed, and he said he would pay for it.

Judge: He did take care of that?

Plaintiff: He did. Yes, he did.

Judge: Okay.

Plaintiff: So then, I said, “Wow, I still hear the same sound.”

Judge: Well, you never told me about a sound. Where was the sound?

Plaintiff: Well, you know when something is wrong, you feel it. When I shift, there's a little drag. So anyway, I called Ray. I said, “Ray, I still got a problem. I’m still hearing the same thing.”

Judge: What did he say?

Plaintiff: He said, “Bring it in.” When I got there, the mechanic was there. Then, a few days later, the next time I got there, he didn’t have the plug that you plug in to tell you exactly what it is.

Judge: The computer diagnostic tool?

Plaintiff: Correct. So, he told me to come back. The next time, he still didn’t have it.

Judge: Okay.

Plaintiff: Anyway, maybe two or three weeks later, he went to Vegas. He came back. By then, the 45 days was over.

Judge: What 45 days?

Plaintiff: The 45 days he told me he would guarantee the transmission and the motor.

Judge: Do you have that in writing?

Plaintiff: No, I don’t, but that’s what he does. Like, if you finance it, he says, “Don’t worry about it. I’m telling you this is what I do to all my cars.”

Defendant's Statement
Judge: Mr. Miller, did you tell her verbally that you would cover it for 45 days?

Defendant: I did tell her that. But she never got in touch with me within those 45 days.

Judge: She says she did but couldn’t catch you.

Defendant: The reason she didn’t contact me within those 45 days, Your Honor, is because there was nothing wrong with the car. She contacted me after the 45 days. And I still honored the warranty—I even gave her half the money on the transmission.

Judge: What happened next?

Defendant: She told me the issue happened just before the 45 days was up, and she just couldn’t catch up with me. So I agreed to help her.

Judge: What did you do?

Defendant: The actual transmission repairs cost $550—I gave her $275.

Judge: So what work was done?

Defendant: The transmission was actually replaced.

Judge: It was replaced?

Defendant: Yes, that got done. And then, some electrical stuff was wrong with the vehicle.

Judge: Okay.

Defendant: Then, she contacted me and told me I had to pay for that too. I said, “I’m not going to pay for that.”

Judge Requests Evidence
Judge: Can you prove to me what’s wrong with the car and why he should have to pay for it?

Plaintiff: Let’s assume there is a 45-day warranty.

Judge: Yes.

Plaintiff: We are well past that. We’re almost a year later.

Judge: Where’s the car right now?

Plaintiff: Would you believe the car is still in the shop with the same problem?

Judge: What’s wrong with it?

Plaintiff: The mechanic says to fix this properly, he has to drop the motor and seal the seal around the transmission.

Judge: Was a new transmission put into your car?

Plaintiff: Yes, but the guy did not fix it correctly.

Judge: I need to see evidence. Do you have any proof from your mechanic that the prior repair wasn’t done correctly?

Plaintiff: Is it possible I can get him on the phone?

Judge: Let’s try that.

Mechanic Joins the Call
Judge: Hi, Rick.

Mechanic (Rick): Hello.

Judge: How long has the car been in the shop this time?

Mechanic (Rick): I think about five weeks.

Judge: When did she bring it to you?

Mechanic (Rick): When they put the transmission in originally, the problem wasn’t actually the transmission—it was the alternator.

Judge: So, what happened now?

Mechanic (Rick): We took the transmission out, put in a front seal—

Judge: Because the transmission was pouring fluid out?

Mechanic (Rick): Yes.

Judge: Did that have anything to do with the way the original transmission was installed?

Mechanic (Rick): They probably didn’t change the seal.

Judge: Are they required to?

Mechanic (Rick): Well, that’s a gray area. Some shops do, some don’t.

Judge: What percentage of mechanics would have changed the seal?

Mechanic (Rick): About 50%.

Judge: So, not an industry standard?

Mechanic (Rick): No.

Judge: How much did that job cost?

Mechanic (Rick): $678.

Judge: Thank you, Rick.

Final Verdict
Judge: So, according to Rick, the prior mechanics didn’t take the extra precaution of changing the seal. But only 50% of mechanics do, meaning it’s not an industry standard.

You don’t have a written warranty.

The defendant helped you after 45 days.

The transmission was replaced.

The issue now is a seal—not necessarily the defendant’s fault.

Judge: My verdict in this case is for the defendant.

Judge: All parties, please raise your right hands. Welcome back to The People’s Court. Next case on the docket: The Plaintiff says the Defendant was a creepy landlord who charged him every time he had someone spend the night and even spied on him. But the Defendant says the guy only moved because he tried to save his marriage. It’s the case of ‘Who’s Been Sleeping in My Bed?’

Judge: Alexander Petrov, you are suing DeAndre Zephyr, your former landlord, for $1,840, representing the return of your security deposit, some extra utilities you feel you shouldn’t have had to pay for, and pain and suffering. You are counterclaiming against him for the rest of his lease because, according to you, he broke it early without reason. Talk to me—what happened?

Plaintiff: I moved in in January of this year. What happened was, he originally told me that utilities were included. But as time went on, he decided he could charge me extra for them.

Judge: Why do you agree if they were—what did your lease say?

Plaintiff: According to the lease, they were included.

Judge: Really? Because I looked at your lease, and it says electricity paid by tenant and owner, right? Go look at it.

Plaintiff: Well, I don’t have a copy of the lease here.

Judge: Then how do you know what ‘according to the lease’ it says?

Plaintiff: In the agreement that we talked about, he told me it was included as part of the agreement. There were four people who lived there, including himself, and $160 a month is too much for a single room.

Judge: You mean in rent?

Plaintiff: No, for the utilities. Just for the utilities.

Judge: Do you have a copy of your lease?

Plaintiff: I do not.

Judge: Where’s your copy of the lease?

Plaintiff: I only have receipts for the things that he charged.

Judge: Do you have a copy of the lease?

Defendant: Yes. May I see it?

Judge: (Reading) ‘Owner will provide services which are required by law. The following services and utilities are the responsibility of the renter unless otherwise indicated below: electricity—owner/tenant, gas—owner, water—owner, cable TV—owner, telephone—tenant, air conditioner—tenant.’ It’s all actually spelled out in the lease. So, he comes to you for your half or whatever, a portion you felt was too big?

Plaintiff: When I confronted him about it, I told him, ‘This is too much to be charged for a single month for a single room.’ He said, ‘Can I just pay him the $160, or he would kick me out.’

Judge: But why don’t you just look at—do you have the electricity bill?

Plaintiff: No, I don’t.

Judge: What were you charging him for?

Defendant: The bill was $400. It was split evenly among the household members. His portion was the same as everyone else’s—$60.

Judge: You didn’t want to get kicked out. Were you going through a divorce or separation?

Plaintiff: Yes, Your Honor.

Judge: So, you didn’t want to relocate, and you paid the money. What else was a problem?

Plaintiff: He made me do chores around the house—mopping, sweeping the common areas, washing the bathroom—even though other roommates didn’t have to do anything.

Judge: Did you have a whip or something?

Defendant: No, Your Honor. We all had rotating chores. Everyone cleaned on weekends.

Judge: Go on.

Plaintiff: He would knock on my door and say, ‘Hey Alex, why didn’t you take out the garbage? Why didn’t you do this?’ Even when I had guests over.

Judge: You and your wife are back together?

Plaintiff: We’re working on it, but I’m living with her.

Defendant: He told me he wanted to reconcile with his wife. I asked why he signed a lease if he was trying to reconcile. He signed a lease for a year. After the year, I had no issue with him leaving.

Judge: So, from the beginning, he told you he was trying to reconcile?

Defendant: After 20 days, he changed his mind.

Judge: What happened that made him leave?

Defendant: One day, I saw his father in the house when he wasn’t there. That was the first incident. The second time, I said we should go our separate ways.

Judge: So, you said that to him?

Defendant: Yes, ma’am.

Judge: Then why do you have a counterclaim against him?

Defendant: After he moved out, he accused me of stealing his camera and called the police. That’s when I counter-sued.

Judge: But you found your camera, right?

Plaintiff: Yes.

Judge: Would you like to apologize?

Plaintiff: I regret saying he stole my camera.

Judge: If you told him to leave, you can’t sue for rent. I’m ordering the return of $750 for the security deposit. As for utilities—no. As for pain and suffering—no. And for your counterclaim—zero. Good luck, folks.

Reporter: Mr. Zephyr, you lost the case.

Judge: Okay. How? Financially or some other way?

Defendant’s Girlfriend: 21.

Judge: How old are you?

Defendant: 20.

Judge: Lord above. All right. So what happens?

Plaintiff: The mother calls me and says to me that they tried to get some money to help get a second car for Connor’s girlfriend, Mary. So I said okay. Judge: Okay.

Judge: Did you know her?

Plaintiff: I knew her just for a little bit.

Judge: Okay.

Plaintiff: So if a 20-year-old I barely knew asked me for $4,000 for a car, I’d be like, um, no. I’ve gotten beaten up by my family, friends, everything else. It’s just the way I am.

Judge: But why did you do that?

Plaintiff: I did it for the mother because she did help me, and I really thought that getting these young kids a second car would really help them out a lot.

Judge: Seriously?

Plaintiff: It did.

Judge: The person it didn’t help was you because, according to you, that was a loan. That’s how you were helping them out—giving them a loan.

Plaintiff: Absolutely.

Judge: And according to you, Mr. Lucas, what?

Defendant: Well, our assumption was that it was a gift because—well, the reason we accepted was because we weren’t in a position to pay anything. We are very paycheck to paycheck.

Judge: Okay.

Defendant: We have a little girl.

Judge: How old?

Defendant: Three years old.

Judge: Who’s how old?

Defendant: Three.

Judge: Oh, for the love of God.

Defendant: So we really aren’t in a position—

Judge: Right, you are the kind of people we call bus takers, or public transportation takers, right? You don’t get to have the $4,000 car until you’ve saved up for it, right?

Defendant: Uh—

Judge: Or you have to drop a—You know, that’s the kind of thing it is.

Defendant: Yeah.

Judge: And so when—it’s not—I’m not sure why you would think a virtual stranger was going to rain four grand on you.

(Commercial Break)

Judge: Well, was there any discussion about when the payback would be?

Plaintiff: Your honor, I—I—I had to go to the bank Monday. They didn’t know that I had to go borrow the money to cover a check if he wrote it out.

Judge: So you didn’t even have the money?

Plaintiff: No.

Judge: You took it out of what?

Plaintiff: My home equity.

Judge: You put your house up as collateral?

Plaintiff: I had to borrow the money.

Judge: Okay. Who are those folks?

Plaintiff: These are my sister and her husband.

Judge: Oh, they must have been the ones who gave you the most grief. Because they have a bobblehead going while I’m talking—the two of them. All right. So let me—listen, there are worse things, right? So let me ask you a question. When you wrote the check, did you have them sign a promissory note or anything else?

Plaintiff: We were at breakfast. I took them out for breakfast on a Sunday morning.

Judge: You paid?

Plaintiff: Of course, I did, your honor. I paid.

Judge: They know what a nice person you are.

Plaintiff: I know. Listen, kids—he was sitting right next to me, Mary was across with her daughter, we were having breakfast. I took the check out of my pocket, and I handed it to him. And I said, “Listen, you better make sure you look at the check before you fill it out and cash it.” And that was the last of the conversation.

Judge: And on that check, in the memo line, you had written “Personal Loan for Kids.”

Plaintiff: Yes, your honor.

Judge: And you had left the payee blank because you didn’t know if he was going to sign it to himself or to an auto agency?

Plaintiff: That’s exactly right.

Judge: And as it turns out, you wrote it to yourself, right? Like, you filled your name in and then you got the money out. And what’d you do with the money?

Defendant: Well, the money was deposited into my bank account, and then once we found a vehicle—

Judge: You did buy a car?

Defendant: We did buy a car.

Judge: Yeah? How’s that working out for you?

Defendant: Very well.

Judge: Good. Yeah, of course. So did you not read the memo line?

Defendant: No, your honor. I filled the check out in the car.

Judge: Because where you fill out your name with your hand is a half-inch from the memo line. What did the memo line say?

Defendant: I—I do not remember.

Judge: Okay, you’re 21. You’re not blind. What did the memo line say?

Defendant: I don’t recall.

Judge: The memo line said “Personal Loan.” What would make you think it was ever going to be a gift? Did you ever say it was going to be a gift? Did the word loan or payment plan or anything like that come up at breakfast?

Defendant: No.

Judge: Okay. Months later—months—months later, you say, “Hey, you ever going to pay me back for that?” And what happens?

Plaintiff: They thought it was a gift.

Judge: And you said?

Plaintiff: What?!       Judge: So, according to you, this was a loan. And according to you, this was a gift. Now, let me ask you a question, Mr. Lucas. If someone gives you $3,500, wouldn't you at least ask, "Are you sure?"

Defendant: We assumed that because there was no discussion about repayment, it was meant to help us out.

Judge: Right. But common sense tells you that a stranger isn’t just going to give you that kind of money with no strings attached. Did you ever think to clarify?

Defendant: Well, we just went with what we understood at the time.

Judge: Uh-huh. Let me ask you this—when the plaintiff asked you to pay him back, what did you say?

Defendant: I told him we were struggling financially, and that we wouldn’t be able to pay him back.

Judge: Okay. But did you say, "Oh, I thought this was a gift," or did you just say you couldn’t pay him back?

Defendant: At first, I told him we didn’t have the money. And later, when he kept asking, I said that I believed it was a gift.

Judge: And you never thought to call him and say, "Hey, we’re really grateful for this—what are the terms of this?"

Defendant: We just assumed.

Judge: Right. You just assumed the best-case scenario for yourself. You know what that sounds like to me? Convenient.

Defendant: I understand, your honor.

Judge: Now, Mr. Petron, I understand you didn’t have a contract, but you do have a check that clearly states “Personal Loan for Kids” in the memo. That is evidence of intent. It’s not ironclad, but it’s strong. You also asked for repayment, which further supports that this was not a gift.

Defendant: But we genuinely believed—

Judge: No, no, no. I don’t believe that. I believe that you took the money, spent it, and then when you realized there were consequences, you backpedaled and decided it was a gift. But the law doesn’t work that way.

Defendant’s Girlfriend: Your honor, if I may—

Judge: No, you may not. Your boyfriend accepted the check, deposited it, and benefited from it. If you wanted to clarify, you should have done that at the time.

Defendant’s Girlfriend: But we—

Judge: No “but.” You’re young, you’re struggling, and I get it, but that doesn’t make it right to take advantage of someone’s kindness.

Plaintiff: Thank you, your honor.

Judge: Here’s my ruling. Mr. Petron, you made a very bad decision in giving this money without an ironclad contract. That being said, there is enough evidence to support your claim that this was a loan. I am ruling in favor of the plaintiff for the full amount of $3,500.

Defendant: But your honor—

Judge: Case dismissed. Pay the man his money.

Thank you, Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court: This is the third time the government has come before this Court and asked to be relieved of the consequences of flouting the plain text of the INA. For a third time, the government says the notice it gave is good enough. Just as it did in previous cases, this Court should reject the government’s argument here. Indeed, the government's position in these cases is more extreme than before.

In a prior case, the government argued that as long as the non-citizen received all the information listed in paragraph (1), the format did not matter. In these cases, the government now seems to argue that if a paragraph (2) notice is given, it does not matter if the non-citizen never receives the information in paragraph (1).

To illustrate, imagine a scenario where the government sends a non-citizen a blank piece of paper but provides the immigration judge with details about the charges against them. In that circumstance, the government may prove removability by clear and convincing evidence, while the non-citizen remains unaware of the charges. In a previous case, the argument was that a deficient NTA could still stop the clock for discretionary relief; here, the government argues that the same deficient NTA is enough to remove a non-citizen without allowing them to present their case.

Because the statute does not allow for such an outcome, and because it requires a complete and valid paragraph (1) notice in every case while treating paragraph (2) notice only as a supplement, this Court should reject the government's arguments again. I welcome this Court’s questions.

Judge: What’s your best textual argument for your last point?

Defendant’s Lawyer: Your Honor, we believe there are two main textual arguments. The first concerns the centrality of the NTA. The statute requires that it be issued in every removal proceeding, includes key information not found in paragraph (2), and states that a hearing cannot be held less than 10 days from the issuance of an NTA. This establishes a presumption in favor of the NTA.

The second argument comes from the language of 1229 paragraph (2), which states that a notice of change must be given only when there is a change in the time and place of the proceeding. The combination of these two points— the importance of the NTA and the interpretation of "change"—supports our position.

Judge: I think the statute says "any change," and that language seems broad enough to cover the change here.

Defendant’s Lawyer: Your Honor, that "any change" language was interpreted in a prior case, and we do not believe it is broad enough to apply here. Consider the example from the government’s reply brief about voter registration. If a person fills out an invalid voter registration form, no number of party affiliation change forms will correct that. Until the voter registration form is valid, any subsequent changes are meaningless. The same applies here. Because the initial NTA was invalid due to the "TBD" language, later notices cannot correct that deficiency. Until a valid NTA is issued, there is nothing to change.

Judge: Could I clarify something? Suppose the non-citizen receives a paragraph (1) notice, and there is no issue with the date and time, but the government omits details about the charges or the right to counsel. Later, a paragraph (2) notice is issued because the date and time need to change. Could the non-citizen reopen the case?

Defendant’s Lawyer: No, Your Honor, and that’s for the reason I just articulated. In this case, the issue with the "change" language is twofold. First, we argue that changing from "TBD" to a specific date is not actually a change. Second, the statute specifies that changes pertain to the time and place of the proceeding. If there was never a valid NTA setting a time and place, then there is nothing to change.

Judge: I’m assuming there was no problem with the time and date in the first notice. There was an issue with something else, like the charges or the right to counsel. Would the non-citizen still have a right to reopen?

Defendant’s Lawyer: Yes, Your Honor. Returning to the voter registration analogy—if the initial document is invalid due to missing required information, simply filing a change notice does not remedy that. The NTA is invalid if it lacks required information from paragraph (1), meaning it does not function properly. A proper, valid NTA must be issued before any changes can be made.

Judge: So your position is that there is simply no NTA, meaning paragraph (2) does not apply?

Defendant’s Lawyer: That’s correct, Your Honor. In this case, the issue is further reinforced by the "TBD" date and the previous Court ruling interpreting similar language.

Judge: Do you dispute that, in ordinary language, a change can occur from an indeterminate time or place to a determinate one?

Defendant’s Lawyer: Yes, Your Honor. We believe that ordinary speakers do not use "change" to describe moving from an undefined time to a defined one. For example, if a bride announces she is getting married but does not specify a date, when she later sends out a card with the date, it is called a "Save the Date" rather than a "Change the Date."

Judge: But if a friend asks the bride for the date, and she responds that she doesn’t know yet, and the friend later says, "Let me know if there’s any change," do you think that is an unusual use of language?

Defendant’s Lawyer: Your Honor, I don’t know if it’s completely unreasonable, but when combined with the precedent from prior cases and the structural importance of the NTA in this statute, our interpretation of "change" is the stronger one.

Judge: And so the expert that the government puts on the stand says: "In my expertise, people who have this kind of material in these quantities in their apartments have it because they’re manufacturing drugs that they intend to sell."

Judge: In or out?

Judge: At this point, we've jumped over several preliminary questions. Before your time is up, could you say a little bit more about them? For one thing, do you think that the Respondent has a cause of action and, if so, where does it come from?

Defendant’s Lawyer: I don't know where it comes from. The complaint seems to assume that there's probably a constitutional cause of action.

Judge: Well, do they think it's an Ex parte Young cause of action?

Defendant’s Lawyer: That’s what is in—I mean, I think that’s their reasoning. The lower court has obviously countenanced that, and so it hasn't been previously explored in this case. We don't think that—they also assert an APA claim. We think that we have good arguments that the APA does not provide a cause of action here, that this is excepted from judicial review in two different rationales that are explained in decisions that we cite in the reply brief in different circuits. So I don't know where the cause of action is, unless it is an implicit Ex parte Young-like action that they're asking for declaratory judgment on.

Judge: Well, I'll ask the other side about that. If there was a protected liberty interest in Mandel, wouldn't it follow that there's a protected liberty interest here? If the Respondent had not said, I want to live with my husband in the United States, but had said, he has a lot of interesting things to say, and I wish he were here so I could listen to what he has to say and therefore exercise my First Amendment right to receive information, would that be sufficient?

Defendant’s Lawyer: I don't think so. I don't think that the marriage cases can be repleaded as First Amendment cases and satisfy Mandel. Again, I would say that Mandel didn't even say that there is a constitutional right there. And here, we think that the point of this is that we cite O'Bannon for the premise that in the due process context, an enforcement action taken against one person doesn't create a liberty interest in a third party who is indirectly affected by that.

So she is obviously affected by the decision that her husband can't come into the United States. The same thing would be true if this were a removal proceeding, removing him from the United States, and you wouldn't say she has a due process interest in intervening in that case and making arguments that he can't make or arguments that he can make. Either way, she doesn't have an independent due process interest there.

Judge: You did ultimately provide a lot of information to the Respondent, and the lower court didn't fault that. It found that you gave plenty of information in the end. It just said it wasn't timely, and, therefore, it remanded to the district court to conduct further proceedings. I'm not clear what those are, what that would look like, and I'm just curious what you understand would follow on remand. And if there's a suggestion here perhaps that there's no harm, no foul because everybody knew exactly what was going on here—everybody knew, it appears, that the Respondent filed a petition for rehearing based on an assumption that the denial was due to gang membership, for example.

So there's a lot packed in there, but if you could help me with that.

Defendant’s Lawyer: Yeah, I—I don’t think my opposing counsel would say that the lower court correctly concluded that we provided enough information. I don't read their brief as acknowledging that.

Judge: Let's put that aside.

Defendant’s Lawyer: And I'm not sure what the lower court thinks is going to happen on remand. This is sort of terra incognita for the courts to say that giving that much information is enough, but, nevertheless, we want there to be a full-bore review.

The lower court seemed to suggest that there's going to be some type of independent judicial confirmation that the government was correct about its reasons. I think that goes far beyond anything that any other court has countenanced in the context of Mandel-type review. And so, at the end of the day, we don't think that the district court would be able to order the issuance of a visa.

And the complaint doesn't ask for anything other than a declaration that the reason that had been given before we provided additional details about gang membership was not legitimate and bona fide.

Judge: Thank you, counsel. Anything further?

Judge: What exactly does the doctrine of consular nonreviewability do?

Defendant’s Lawyer: We think that it prevents a non-citizen outside the United States from challenging a decision to deny a visa and also prevents a third party from attacking that decision.

To the extent that there's an exception that the Court wants to acknowledge, that would then get into whether there is a constitutional liberty interest, and that's the first question here. But otherwise, it protects the political branch's ability to protect the nation's borders and decide who is going to be admitted to the United States without judicial oversight for cases involving foreign citizens who are outside the United States.

Judge: So, without more, it is simply a doctrine that prevents all judicial review of these decisions?

Defendant’s Lawyer: Yes. All judicial review that Congress has not provided for.

Judge: you shouldn't have a problem paying the additional child support amount based on your income.
Defendant: well what if what if I lose my job do you know?
Plaintiff: what if I lose mine? For me. I've got three kids.
Defendant: well it feels like I have three kids from time to time you know. I went, I was there last month, I took them for an entire weekend.
Judge: and oh you took your children for one long weekend? That's called parenting. Uh, I mean you're not gonna get a medal.

Judge: So it seems to me that your causation rule eviscerates the kind of plaintiff dynamic that I explored with Ms. Anand.
And maybe you can help me understand why that's not the case. So I said we have a person who is a criminal, and he's committing crimes.
And he has got three or four of them absolutely dead to rights, but one of them, no.
One of them, he says, this additional thing, whatever it is, I didn't do it.
And because I'm, you know, a career criminal, the police officer knows me from way back when, and he actually tacked that on after he made stuff up and he -- this charge is a malicious prosecution.
It seems to me that if we buy your rule of causation, so, first of all, we don't say, you know, any charge, he still gets to go forward, just because there are these other charges, he still gets to go forward on that basis, but you're knocking him out basically for the same reason on the causation prong.
You're saying because you have these other charges for which you could have been arrested, and perhaps he even agrees to that, you're not allowed, because you can't say that the baseless charge caused. And I guess I don't understand why that's the case.
Why shouldn't he still be able to make his claim on the civil docket with respect to the malicious prosecution of that one charge?

Defendant's Lawyer: I think you've absolutely highlighted the distinction between our positions.
I think Williams versus Aguirre, which Petitioner embraces, would hold that you could make out a per se constitutional violation for that alleged malicious prosecution. And we think that's incorrect.
And we think that's because of the operation of the Fourth Amendment. The correct rule is a Fourth Amendment rule.
How could you have a per se constitutional violation for an unreasonable charge, which the Fourth Amendment does not forbid, without an unreasonable seizure? And -- and we think it's the Fourth Amendment that does the work there.

Judge: So what if the other crimes are all misdemeanors and he can show -- we have this coming up in other cases -- that no one would ever have been prosecuted or held or detained for the three other things.
Yes, he says, I did those things, but those are not things for which people get detained. It's the one they made up.
And that's, in fact, why they made it up because they wanted me to go to jail.
Does he go forward or no under your causation prong?

Defendant's Lawyer: We still think it is the overlay of existing Fourth Amendment precedent that asks about that objectively.
And -- and that means that if those misdemeanor charges, regardless of local practice, even if it hadn't been local practice to arrest for those, the Fourth Amendment says you can arrest for even a fine-only offense.
That's Atwater versus City of Lago Vista. And the Fourth Amendment also allows an arrest, even if it would be unlawful under state law.
That's Virginia versus Moore.
Those don't violate the Fourth Amendment, so you can hold those individuals.
Now, it would be a different case if the detention is for 18 months, for example.
And the only probable cause that supports it are for these very minor misdemeanor offenses.

Judge: Well, it doesn't matter to you at all if there's evidence that he puts forward that no one would ever -- that this magistrate, in fact, wouldn't have or didn't, you know, go forward with respect to this -- authorizing this detention but for the baseless charge that -- that was put there in order to get him to go to jail?

Defendant's Lawyer: That's right.
We think that is exactly the kind of analysis that the Fourth Amendment forecloses.
And I can point to Whren, Virginia versus Moore, that all say the Fourth Amendment reasonableness does not depend on local practice.
It doesn't depend on state law.
It doesn't vary from place to place and from time to time.
And that is just settled Fourth Amendment law.

Judge: Counsel, let's say I -- I understand all that and maybe -- maybe even am slightly sympathetic to it.
But what about the Fourteenth Amendment and what about the common law malicious prosecution claims, which didn't depend upon showing that all the charges against me were false?

Defendant's Lawyer: I think those standards could definitely be different.
Under those, I think under state law, there is no requirement that you show a seizure, so the analysis would be different.
That claim has been abandoned by this stage in the litigation. And, yes, under the Fourteenth Amendment, you wouldn't have this requirement to show a seizure.
I think the analysis would be different if the Court were to recognize it in the future.

Judge: Thank you, counsel. Justice Alito? Justice Sotomayor, anything more? Justice Kagan? Justice Gorsuch? No? Thank you, counsel.

Defendant's Lawyer: Thank you.

Plaintiff’s Lawyer: But the claim itself, Your Honor, comes from the Fifth Amendment not just in Texas but in states nationwide.
And I think this is an important point. Take Oregon, for example.
Oregon signed on to the state's amicus brief in support of Texas, but the reason that Oregon pays just compensation for takings under the Fifth Amendment is the Oregon courts, citing First English, have said it must pay just compensation.
And so answering the Question Presented—

Judge: If we don't read First English the way you do—I mean, I think that footnote's pretty difficult to decipher—do you lose?

Plaintiff’s Lawyer: No, Your Honor.
I—I don't think it's just the footnote in First English.
I think it's the broader holding that the remedy is required.
But I think there's no dispute here that there is an entitlement to relief.
And, certainly, by the time of the ratification of the Fourteenth Amendment, courts across the country had converged on how that kind of entitlement would be enforced.
And it's enforced by a lawsuit directly against the entity that took the property that takes the form of saying, you have this duty to provide just compensation, you have not fulfilled it, and I'd like the court to order you to fulfill it.

Judge: Can I have just a small point of information? Your case was dismissed in federal court.
Did you ask for a remand on your claims under the Texas Constitution?

Plaintiff’s Lawyer: No, Your Honor.
The district court is keeping pendent jurisdiction over the claims under the Texas cause.

Judge: So you—you have a pending suit on the state law claim?

Plaintiff’s Lawyer: Yes, Your Honor, but there is a dispute about the scope of the takings law that governs that question.
Texas has taken the position in the lower courts that the Texas Constitution has a narrower definition of what counts as a taking than the federal courts.

Judge: Well, then First English comes in too because First English was about a state court claim and when it started, whether a temporary claim was a taking or not, and we said yes, it's a taking, and so the state court had to pay for that taking.
How is it different than First English in that respect?

Plaintiff’s Lawyer: I—I don't think it's different from First English, Your Honor, except that, here, it was removed into federal court and then the Fifth Amendment aspect of the case was dismissed on the merits.

Judge: Oh, I—I—I don't disagree with you, but First English is about what the substantive law of Texas is and what Texas has to pay.
And so that issue should be resolved even in the district court, correct?

Plaintiff’s Lawyer: I—I don't think so, Your Honor, because the backstop in First English is the Fifth Amendment that—that says that the met—the just compensation—

Judge: No, the backstop in the Fifth—yes, it's the Fifth Amendment that provides the substantive law, but not necessarily—we didn't address whether it provides a cause of action.

Plaintiff’s Lawyer: I—I think the Court did, Your Honor.
The United States' amicus brief—

Judge: All right.
We're—we're going to—we're going to go into—

Judge: Can I just ask—I—I mean, this is similar to what [previous judge] was just getting into.
Are you saying that we don't have three separate concepts—right, remedy, and cause of action?
I thought those were three different things, and perhaps First English only covered two of them?

Plaintiff’s Lawyer: I—I'm not sure they're distinct concepts, Your Honor.
I think the simplest way to understand cause of action is an entitlement to a particular remedy, which is why it's coherent to say someone might have a cause of action for an injunction.

Judge: I thought it had to do with the forum, that you have a cause of action that is recognized in the judicial forum as opposed to, say, going to the legislature through—through private bills.

Plaintiff’s Lawyer: Well, Your Honor, I—I think, to the extent that's the definition of "cause of action," we would have a cause of action under the clear import of the history that the—

Judge: Not—not the history.
I guess I'm just trying to understand, is there—does it make sense to think about the Fifth Amendment as providing the right and the remedy but not speaking to where you're going to get that remedy from or what is the enforcement mechanism?
That's how I sort of am conceptualizing this, and—and I think we differ about that, so I'd like to hear your opinion on it.

Plaintiff’s Lawyer: I—I'm not sure that's a correct reading of the Fifth Amendment, Your Honor, in part because I think that reading—everyone agrees there are some judicial remedies for the Fifth Amendment.
As I understand my friend's argument, we'd be entitled to sue for injunctive relief or for ejectment in the absence of a—a path to a Fifth Amendment compensation remedy.
So everyone agrees there's some judicial remedy, and I think the form of that judicial remedy depends on the scope of the government's obligation.
There are two visions of the Fifth Amendment.

One is that the Fifth Amendment just provides a precondition.
The government is required to pay, and it can be enjoined from taking the property if it doesn't pay.

The other vision that's adopted in First English, that's reiterated in Knick, is that the Fifth Amendment creates an obligation to pay just compensation.

And if that's the ongoing obligation—the government has taken property, it owes just compensation today, will owe just compensation tomorrow—courts are empowered to cure that ongoing obligation.
It's not a question of damages for a past violation.
It's a question of the government's obligation as it stands in court today.

**Judge:** Mr. Goldar, what happened?

**Plaintiff (Seth Goldar):** Well, I brought my computer in, Judge, to have the keyboard replaced, and when I went back later that day, it was a non-functioning computer.

**Judge:** Did you receive any phone calls from him in the meantime?

**Plaintiff:** I did. He called to ask me if it was a working computer when I brought it in.

**Judge:** And what did you say?

**Plaintiff:** Why would I put a new keyboard on a non-working computer and not mention it? I imagine that was the long and short of it.

**Judge:** Yeah. And then what does he say?

**Plaintiff:** He was sympathetic. He tried going back through the computer to see what was wrong with it, and when he realized it wasn’t working at all, he offered to send it somewhere else to have it fixed on his dime.

**Judge:** So he sends it somewhere else, and then what does he tell you?

**Plaintiff:** About a month later—three weeks or so later—it came back with the diagnosis that it was completely fried. Several parts were beyond repair, and they were unable to get parts to replace them.

**Judge:** Okay, so how do you two leave it? What happens? What do you say to him?

**Plaintiff:** It was kind of a circular conversation. I asked him how we could fix this, kind of alluding to the idea that he would help out in some way for damaging the computer. And he just kept running circles around me.

**Judge:** And saying what?

**Plaintiff:** He was flabbergasted and astonished at how something like this could happen. And he’s never had anything like this happen in his 25 years of business.

**Judge:** And how did you guys leave it?

**Plaintiff:** I left the computer with him. I went back a few weeks later—a week or so later—and picked it up after I kind of cleared my head and decided what to do.

**Judge:** And what was it you decided to do?

**Plaintiff:** Sue him, ultimately. Yes.

**Judge:** Right. All right, so now, Mr. Johnson, tell me your version of these things.

**Defendant (Kent Johnson):** Oh, he brought in the computer. He said it was a keyboard trouble, and he brought in the keyboard with it. I replaced the keyboard. When I put it back together and hit the button to turn it on, it didn’t turn on.

**Judge:** Uh-huh.

**Defendant:** So I called him, and he said no, it was working when he brought it in. Well, I don’t know what I could have possibly done that did this. So I took it back apart, and I checked every connection. And I—I have no idea how it didn’t work. So I offered to send it out to someone who could figure it out. I figured it must be a fuse or something somewhere. Those fuses are microscopic. I can’t do them here.

**Judge:** Uh-huh.

**Defendant:** And they said no, the board was bad. And not only that, but the LCD was bad, which—I have no idea how that could have happened.

**Judge:** Well, didn’t you—I mean, when someone brings in a computer, don’t you have them power it up? I know that’s what the—

**Defendant:** Yeah, I didn’t do that.

**Judge:** Yeah, no, you didn’t do that.

**Defendant:** That’s—I did not check it before I took out the battery. I just started the whole thing, started the process.

**Judge:** Are you sure you took out the battery?

**Defendant:** I’m sure I took out the battery, yeah.

**Judge:** So you then end up sending it out because you feel bad, and you pay to have it sent out and investigated. And what—you know, when they tell you—

**Defendant:** Not really.

**Judge:** Okay, so you didn’t pay?

**Defendant:** I paid shipping.

**Judge:** You’re suing, Mr. Goldar, for the cost that you paid for it, plus the cost of the keyboard, plus tax of $100—which I don’t know where you got that. Remember, it’s the value of the item at the time of the loss. And you are talking about a laptop that was by then—how old?

**Plaintiff:** Yes, Your Honor.

**Judge:** You know, this isn’t a criminal case. I don’t have to absolutely know what happened. I just have to look at the facts. Judge: And this is what's being claimed here.

Do you think that this is a slightly different case also because they had been extending a benefit that they then took away?

Lawyer of Plaintiff: I think that goes to an animus-based claim.

Judge: But there's still --

Lawyer of Plaintiff: I don't have a different answer.

I would just boil it down to this, which is those other sources of law may well be useful in enforcing promises that an employer makes.

But the problem here, the alleged problem here, is that the employer made one promise to people without disabilities and a different, worse promise to people with disabilities.

And simply enforcing that other less good promise isn't going to remedy the alleged discrimination in the complaint.

Judge: Thank you, counsel.

Judge: Next question?

Judge: So, I just want to make sure that I understand what you would like us to do.

Judge: And, as I understand it, it's because the Plaintiff was employed, was holding a job, for a period of time that you would like us to go off on that basis and, you say, you know, they sued within the 300 days, and the consequence of that is that we never have to reach this qualified individual provision.

Is that correct?

Lawyer of Plaintiff: I think because everyone agrees the Plaintiff was a qualified individual during that time.

Judge: So we never have to figure out what the qualified individual provision means with respect to somebody who is retired, not in a job.

Judge: And, you know, whether we should think of that as precluding a suit for some later person. And you talked about why you shouldn't think of these as forfeited.

Lawyer of Plaintiff: I would say that the issue defined at the right level -- and I'm defining it as the text of the statute defines it -- is whether there was discrimination against a qualified individual.

I do understand the Court to have granted cert on that question.

Then it's just a matter of the fact that these arguments, while they were pressed below not only by us but by the Plaintiff, weren't addressed below.

But I've always understood that pressed or passed-upon requirement to apply to issues and not arguments but also to be phrased in the injunctive such that if you did want to apply that test to the precise arguments here, it would be satisfied because these arguments were pressed below.

Judge: Thank you.

Judge: Any further questions?

Judge: Well, I thought we were deciding whether the Eleventh Circuit's understanding of the law was correct given the arguments that it considered.

Is that not what we -- you don't think that's what we should do?

Lawyer of Plaintiff: I think the Eleventh Circuit found various ways to reject the arguments that go to the overarching issue.

Some of those ways of rejecting those arguments did involve addressing those arguments on the merits, and others involved determining that they were forfeited.

But I think all of that is before this Court.

Judge: Okay.

Judge: Any further questions?

Judge: So I just wanted to clarify the conversation that you had with the other Judge about whether the elements of disparate treatment have been plausibly alleged here.

That -- you don't consider that to be within the question presented in this case?

Judge: So it's more like whether or not the Plaintiff's status as a former employee precludes them from making this claim? Lawyer of Plaintiff: Correct.

Judge: And you're saying the Plaintiff is because there's evidence that they were discriminated against, evidence, not -- it hasn't -- whether it's proven or not, evidence that they were discriminated against during the period of their employment, and that should be enough to allow for their case to go forward?

Lawyer of Plaintiff: That should be enough.

We think even if they couldn't have pointed to that existence of discrimination while they were employed that they would still have a claim, like many others do in other situations, where they cannot point to a precise moment in time in which they were both qualified and disabled.

But I'll acknowledge that those are trickier issues, and this Court's usual practice is not to decide issues more broadly than it needs to.

Judge: Thank you.

Judge: Thank you, counsel.

Next speaker.

Judge: And -- and you say that that's a kind of underinclusion problem. And, you know, it strikes me that on formal equal protection analysis, it is, unless the State can come forward with some piece of medical evidence that says that the risks are greater in the one area than in the other area, which you say Tennessee has not done.

I guess what I'm asking is, like, isn't the -- the structure of these laws going to mean that all of them are going to have to be struck down once you get to heightened scrutiny? Because you seem to want to say: No, you can do heightened scrutiny, but you can also make certain deferential moves towards the legislature.

And I guess I'm pressing you on whether that's really true.

Lawyer of Defendant: So I think it is true.

To be sure, we think that a categorical ban like this one is severely underinclusive and also severely overinclusive, which is an important ingredient here, and so should be invalidated.

And if other states likewise have this kind of sweeping ban, then they would fail under heightened scrutiny.

But I don't think that means that heightened scrutiny ties the hands of the states in this regard.

One of the problems with the State's approach here is that although it has targeted this gender-affirming care for disparate treatment on the basis of sex, as we say, it has left these exact same medications entirely unregulated for all other purposes and also turned its back on how it handles the risk/benefit calculus with respect to all other pediatric treatments.

But we do think there is a real space for states to regulate here, and I point to the example of West Virginia.

West Virginia was thinking about a total ban, like this one, on care for minors, but then the Senate majority leader in West Virginia, who's a doctor, looked at the underlying studies that demonstrate sharply reduced associations with suicidal ideation and suicide attempts, and the West Virginia legislature changed course and imposed a set of guardrails that are far more precisely tailored to concerns surrounding the delivery of this care.

West Virginia requires that two different doctors diagnose the gender dysphoria and find that it's severe and that the treatment is medically necessary to guard against the risk of self-harm. The West Virginia law also requires mental health screening to try to rule out confounding diagnoses.

It requires the parents to agree and the primary care physician to agree.

And I think a law like that is going to fare much better under heightened scrutiny precisely because it would be tailored to the precise interests and not serve a more sweeping interest like the one asserted here in having minors appreciate their sex.

Judge: Thank you, General.

Judge: Justice Gorsuch? Justice Kavanaugh?

Judge: First, I want to ask about our role here and pick up on the Chief Justice's questions at the beginning, who decides.

You've put forth forceful policy arguments to allow these medical treatments, and Justice Sotomayor's questions elaborated on -- on that.

But the 20-plus states on the other side put forth very forceful arguments against allowing these medical treatments for minors. So it seems to me that we look to the Constitution, and the Constitution doesn't take sides on how to resolve that medical and policy debate.

The Constitution's neutral on the question.

At least that's one way to look at it.

I want to get your reaction to that.

You know, if the Constitution doesn't take sides, if there's strong, forceful scientific policy arguments on both sides in a situation like this, why isn't it best to leave it to the democratic process?

Lawyer of Defendant: Well, I do think that the Constitution takes a position that individuals are entitled to equal protection of the law.

And I totally understand the force of your intuition that states need space to regulate and to try to take into account concerns like adolescent health.

We're not denying that that's an important interest here.

But, when you look at how this law actually operates, what it is doing is denying individual plaintiffs the ability to access medications on the basis of their sex.

And that doesn't mean that the states are disabled from taking into account the actual biological differences between males and females, but that has to be channeled to the heightened scrutiny stage.

And I think that there would be a real danger in this Court saying --looking ahead, essentially, and saying: We think there might be benign justifications here, or we think that states should have some flexibility in this regard to overlook the facial sex classification in the statute.

If you are concerned, Justice Kavanaugh, about moving too fast in this space and maybe restricting the ability of states to take a close look at these issues, I think the Court could write a very narrow opinion in this --in this case, and -- and the Court could say simply that when you prohibit conduct that's inconsistent with sex, that is a sex-based line, so you do have to apply heightened scrutiny.

But the Court has made clear that that's an intermediate standard, and if the State can come forward with an important interest and substantiate that it needed to draw those sex-based lines to substantially serve the interest, that's going to be okay.

Judge: Just on --keep going, sorry.

Lawyer of Defendant: Well, and the final point is then you can send it back and let the Sixth Circuit grapple with this in the first instance.

Judge: On the sex discrimination point, I guess picking up on Justice Kagan's questions, the -- the way you would think about this is, I guess, it prohibits all boys and girls from transitioning using certain medical treatments, and it doesn't say only boys can do so or only girls can do so.

Lawyer of Defendant: Well, I think the -- the problem with trying to put that "transitioning" label on it as a basis to avoid the sex classification is that transition itself is inherently tied to sex.

In other words, the prohibited purpose here are those treatments that would allow a minor to live and identify inconsistent with sex, and the statute would permit anyone to have those treatments for the non-prohibited purpose, which, again, is when it's consistent with sex.

The Court has said many times that labels don't control in this space.

And I think, when you have that kind of purpose that's expressly defined using sex-based line-drawing, you have to recognize that for what it is.
