All of the courts of appeals that have considered that issue and the Board when it has considered that issue have said that the requirements, both the statutory requirements and the regulatory requirements, regarding the notice to appear and the information it has to contain are claims processing rules, they are not jurisdictional rules, so they do not divest the immigration court of jurisdiction if there is information missing from the notice to appear.

Judge: So is your position—let me try to state how I—I don't think you say it quite this way in your brief, but this is what I take from your brief, and I want to see if I'm understanding it correctly. A notice of hearing presupposes that there has been a notice to appear because, otherwise, well, both because of the word "change" and, otherwise, there would be no way for the alien to receive it because you have to know what address to send it to or have personally served it, correct?

Defendant’s Lawyer: That's correct.

Judge: Okay.
So is it your position that once the notice of hearing arrives telling the non-citizen where and when to show up, even if there's some information lacking—and let's say it's information that's even maybe more significant than the date and time that the government has been omitting and it's maybe an incomplete statement of the grounds for removal—that the alien, by virtue of the in absentia provision, has an obligation to show up because he's on notice? It's not like he's not aware that he is in contact with Immigration and that there are removal proceedings underway.
But he has an obligation created by the notice of hearing to show up and at that point to register any objections he may have to the incomplete NTA?

Defendant’s Lawyer: That is our position, Your Honor.
I want to make sure that I'm clear, though, that we think there are a number of safeguards that would prevent the result that you're talking about or the blank document hypothetical that the other side has raised, and if I could go into those protections.

Judge: Sure.

Defendant’s Lawyer: The first is the regulatory requirement that the notice to appear contain the charging information.
So, if we had a blank document or a document that was missing the charges against the non-citizen—

Judge: Don't you need—sorry, don't you need the protection from the statute itself? Because the regulatory requirement can be subject to change.
So I think your better argument comes from the clear and convincing evidence requirement in the statute itself.

Defendant’s Lawyer: That was my second response, Your Honor, which is we bear the burden by clear and convincing evidence to prove that the non-citizen both received notice and that they are removable as charged.
And so, if you have a document that lacks charges, we could not prove that the non-citizen actually is removable. And then the third—

Judge: Suppose—I'm sorry.

Defendant’s Lawyer: Well, the third protection I was going to refer to is the fact that in absentia removal is not automatic. There is an immigration judge who is sitting on the other side of the bench and who has to review the documents and decide whether in absentia removal is appropriate.
And so I think it is highly unlikely that an immigration judge, in the circumstances with a blank document or a document without charges, would enter in absentia removal.

Judge: All parties, please raise your right hands. Welcome back to The People’s Court. Next case on the docket: The plaintiff bought a horse for her disabled daughter, and it didn’t look anything like the horse in the pictures—and it was injured to boot. But the defendant says the plaintiff agreed to buy Lucky sight unseen. It’s the case of ‘My Horse is a Nightmare.’

Judge: Okay, Michelle Fowler Weiss, you are suing Bethany McCurley for $750. $500 is a deposit that you put on a horse and $250 is for travel expenses. According to you, the horse was not as advertised. Tell me what happened.

Plaintiff: My daughter has been doing barrel racing, so we were on Craigslist—

Judge: Yes.

Plaintiff: Her horse is currently 17, so we were looking for a younger horse to continue to grow on. So I was looking through the Craigslist ads and looking at a lot of different things when I came upon Bethany’s ad.

Judge: Okay.

Plaintiff: And I read it—

Judge: How old is your daughter?

Plaintiff: She is 19.

Judge: Okay.

Plaintiff: And I read through the ad. It had everything that we were looking for.

Judge: Do you have the ad?

Plaintiff: I do. May I see it?

Judge: Okay. So what do you do?

Plaintiff: Earlier in March, my daughter had been videotaped on TV because of her disability and riding.

Judge: What’s the nature of her disability?

Plaintiff: She was born with spina bifida, so she walks with crutches and she can’t just get on a horse like a normal person. She needs a mounting block.

Judge: Okay.

Plaintiff: So I sent her the video. I sent a lot of people through the Craigslist ads the same video and said, ‘Okay, your horse sounds awesome. Now, these are the limitations of my daughter. Would your horse fit that?’ Because it’s a long travel distance. And I sent it to Bethany as well.

Plaintiff: And she said, ‘Yeah, everything would be fine.’ So the pictures show a very strong, stout-looking horse, similar to the one that she currently has.

Judge: Do you have the picture?

Plaintiff: Yes.

Judge: Okay. And if you go to the next...

Plaintiff: Okay, there we go.

Judge: Oh my gosh, our paintings do look like an elephant! That’s funny. An elephant with a ball. Alright. So, she texted—I text-messaged her about price and stuff. And so, at the end of the day, you agree as follows: there’s a $500 non-refundable deposit. And that’s very clear, right? Those were the terms of the agreement.

Plaintiff: Yes.

Judge: And then a $250 travel charge because you guys are how far?

Plaintiff: We are about half the state—almost half the state.

Judge: Yeah, about four hours?

Plaintiff: Yeah, four-hour drive.

Judge: Alright. Now, how old are you?

Defendant: 17.

Judge: Okay, so I assume you were involved in all this?

Defendant: Yes.

Judge: But it’s your horse?

Defendant: Yes.

Judge: Alright. So the horse is brought over. What happens?

Plaintiff: She gets her out of the trailer. In the photo, she looks very stocky, and she gets out of the trailer and she’s thin—a lot thinner than the photos—and she looks smaller. But then she gets out and you can see that on her withers—

Judge: What are withers?

Plaintiff: It’s like our shoulder blade.

Judge: Just point to where it is on the horse.

Plaintiff: Okay, got it.

Judge: So what happened when the horse stepped out of the trailer?

Plaintiff: Well, right away, we noticed she was much thinner than in the photos, and when she walked, she was visibly stiff. We figured it might just be the long trailer ride, but when my daughter tried to lead her around, she wouldn’t move properly.

Judge: Did you call a vet at that point?

Plaintiff: Yes, we called our vet, and he came out later that afternoon. He examined her and said she had a problem with her hip—something that didn’t just happen from the ride.

Judge: Did you relay this to the defendant?

Plaintiff: Yes, I texted Bethany immediately, and she told me the horse was “just fine” and that she had “no issues” before. But that’s not what our vet said.

Judge: Alright, Ms. McCurley, what’s your response?

Defendant: The horse was perfectly healthy when we loaded her up. I made it very clear in the ad that there were no known health issues.

Judge: But the issue is that the horse, as delivered, was not as advertised, correct?

Defendant: No, Your Honor. I believe the stiffness was due to the travel. It was a long ride, and horses get stiff. My vet checked her before we listed her for sale.

Judge: Did you offer to take the horse back?

Defendant: No, because the deposit was non-refundable, and I knew she was healthy. I told them to give her a few days to adjust.

Judge: But they got a vet’s opinion. Did you ask for a second opinion?

Defendant: No, because I didn’t think it was necessary.

Judge: Okay. Here’s the problem: when you sell an animal under certain conditions—especially a working horse for a disabled rider—it needs to match the description provided. In this case, the plaintiff relied on your statements and photos. When the horse arrived and was deemed unsuitable, you refused to return the deposit. However, I find that the contract was effectively breached by misrepresentation, whether intentional or not.

Defendant: But—

Judge: $750 verdict for the plaintiff.

Judge: Counsel, you've been talking about this costing a lot. It may well be. And I'm interested in the other side's response to the series of questions that are going on now about that limitation. But I understand that in terms of tribal healthcare, it's about one-third of what is spent by the average American on their own healthcare. So it's not as if all of this money is bringing us a luxury healthcare spa. It's actually bringing us to a fairly minimal level of healthcare for tribal members. I still don't understand how your interpretation makes any contract support system costs, or many of them, recoverable at all because you seem to be saying that if you're providing services with a—you have services with a third party like Medicare or Medicaid, why would that then include contracts with a consultant who comes in to do the other services that the contract requires for the government? There's always third-party contract—contract supports that are reimbursed by the government. Why is this any different? They're not providing the service because of Medicaid or Medicare. They're providing the service because of their agreement with the government. This is only a reimbursement. It's not a required service agreement.

Defendant's Lawyer: So I do want to make sure to respond to the point that Your Honor raised in the beginning of your question about the underfunding of Indian health, and IHS, you know, agrees with that and is seeking additional appropriations from Congress but just doesn't believe that upending the funding scheme in this way, in an open-ended way, is what—

Judge: That assumes the answer to the question, which is that it's upending, if it's clear by the text that if you provide services, you'll be reimbursed for them and for contract support. It's the contract doing that, not upending it.

Defendant's Lawyer: Yes, but I—to respond to Your Honor's question about 5326 and the two prohibitions there, so you were, I believe, referencing the second prohibition, which is that IHS funds cannot be spent to pay costs associated with any contract that's not with IHS. We understand that prohibition to be limited to contracts by which the tribe receives funds, which is consistent with Congress's—what we understand to be Congress's motivation to not have IHS's appropriated funds be used to subsidize other funding schemes. But, even if you disagree with me about that, I would point you to the first prohibition in 5326. That is the one saying that IHS's appropriated funds cannot be used to spend on—to reimburse costs that are not directly attributable to ISDA contracts. And we think that that language squarely applies here because attributable is asking for a causation.

Judge: The contract requires them to seek reimbursement. How can it not be attributable? As previously pointed out, before, they didn't have to do it. Now they're forced to do it by contract.

Defendant's Lawyer: Because we think attributable is calling for—I mean, the word means capable of being produced by or brought about by or caused by. And then you add directly, which is an adverb that doesn't appear the other times that ISDA—the three other times that ISDA uses the word "attributable." And that is—

Judge: Okay.

Defendant's Lawyer: There's an extended—

Judge: Thank you, counsel.

Judge: Counsel, can I—this is a complicated statute and so I have a question about how it actually works. It seems to me there's an argument that these costs should be included in the secretarial amount. Am I right that you don't dispute that the costs of collecting the Medicare and Medicaid fall within the secretarial amount?

Defendant's Lawyer: That's correct.

Judge: Okay. When IHS collects the money, does IHS then spend it?

Defendant's Lawyer: Yes. It has to first dedicate it to the facility that earned its compliance with Medicaid and Medicare, but yes.

Judge: Right. So, if the tribe is standing in IHS's shoes, why shouldn't the secretarial amount—I don't understand the tribes—and the plaintiff's counsel can address this point—I don't understand the tribes to be saying it should be part of the secretarial amount, but if the secretarial amount includes the costs of collection, it's not apparent to me why it wouldn't cover the costs of expenditure in the same way that you're saying IHS spends that money.

Defendant's Lawyer: Because the—well, there are some—I'm not sure this is Your Honor's question. There are some overhead administrative functions that are included in the secretarial amount if they're the kind of thing that the Secretary could have allocated to that tribe. But, no, the costs of spending income from third parties is not part of the secretarial amount because that is limited to the funds the Secretary otherwise would have provided for the operation of the program. And then the contract support cost obligation is itself tied as funds to support that amount.

Judge: Except my confusion is you're saying that the secretarial amount does include the costs of collecting the third-party income, right?

Defendant's Lawyer: Yes, because—yeah.

Judge: Yeah. And you incur overhead costs when you spend that third-party income as well, which I take to be the entire dispute here, is whether they get coverage for that, right?

Defendant's Lawyer: Right, but I guess I would say the costs of collection and billing, that is associated with providing the services using the secretarial amount itself. These later costs of deciding how to spend those funds, which may not even happen during the same contract period, that is not tied up with the services being provided in the first instance. So the billing function, we think, comes over with the secretarial amount because it's tied up with providing the services and getting the funds in return but not with the later decisions about how to spend this money, which could be spent on building a new facility, starting a new kind of healthcare program the Secretary didn't previously run on the tribes' behalf, that kind of thing.

Judge: I think the answer, though, if I understood it correctly, is that when IHS does collect third-party payments, it spends that money to advance Indian health, and that includes some overhead costs?

Defendant's Lawyer: Yes, that is correct.

Judge: And then—just back to where we were earlier, you said that the plaintiff's counsel didn't raise an argument. I've got page 27 of the brief in front of me. "At a minimum, contract support costs are recoverable when program income is used to fund enumerated services within the contractual scope of work." And on page 29, they state that every penny of program income was, in fact, spent for activities enumerated in the contractual scope of work.

Defendant's Lawyer: So I don't understand that to be an argument along the lines Your Honor was suggesting about—

Judge: Why not? My argument was—my question was, aren't they obliged under (m)(1) to spend on general purposes, that's Indian health, and aren't they also obligated to spend in accordance with the contractual services that they've agreed to provide for the government?

Defendant's Lawyer: So I—

Judge: That is exactly what's laid out in those pages.

Defendant's Lawyer: I don't think they're distinguishing between serving eligible Indian beneficiaries and those that the tribe is eligible to serve once they make a determination that they can.

Judge: How do you square that view and that interpretation with the rule of construction that the statute gives us, which says—I'm looking at 5321(g)—that each provision shall be liberally construed for the benefit of the Indian tribe participating in self-determination?

Defendant's Lawyer: Because we believe that statutory language is calling for courts to apply the Indian canon, the common law Indian canon that has been applied in this Court's cases.

Judge: Thank you, counsel.

"JUDGE: This is the plaintiff, Christa Burkhart Vigil.
She says she put down a $500 deposit on an old lamp she saw at the defendant’s store, and when she went back to inquire about the lamp's age and history, the defendant’s salesperson got snooty with her. She was outraged, demanded a refund, and is suing for the return of her $500.
ANNOUNCER: This is the defendant. He says the plaintiff put a deposit down on an imitation Tiffany lamp, and when he couldn't give her the history of the lamp, she got all crazy on him. All sales in his store are clearly marked final sale, no refunds, and the shopaholic plaintiff, who had a change of heart, isn't getting any money back from him.
He's accused of being a pain in the glass.
BAILIFF: All parties, please raise your right hands.
JUDGE: Welcome back to The People’s Court.
Next case on the docket: The plaintiff put $500 down on a lamp, but when the sales clerk got shady, she wanted her money back and can’t get it. The defendant says it was an imitation Tiffany lamp, and she knew it.
It’s the case of You Don’t Light Up My Life.
Thank you, Douglas.
Christa Burkhart Vigil?
PLAINTIFF: Yes.
JUDGE: You are suing the defendant’s store. You've asked us not to mention your last name or the store’s name, so we will refer to you as ""plaintiff"" and ""defendant."" You are suing for $500, a deposit you put down on a lamp that you no longer want to buy.
Tell me what happened.
PLAINTIFF: I went by the store and saw a lamp I liked very much. I went in a couple of days later and wanted to buy it. The lamp I originally saw was too tall for our apartment, so I saw another one that was just perfect. The salesperson showed it to me, and I said I’d take it.
I came back the next day—it was pouring rain, and my husband couldn’t really find a parking spot on Austin Street in Kew Gardens. So, I went in, gave them a $500 deposit—
JUDGE: Because you wanted to make sure they didn’t sell the lamp?
PLAINTIFF: Right. And then, I said I’d come back the next day. Now, I work as a tour guide in the city, and I have a crazy schedule, so I couldn’t come back the next day. I came back a couple of days later, and the salesperson I had spoken to wasn’t there. Richie, another salesperson, was also not in.
So, I did not want to buy the lamp that day because nobody could really help me with it. We came back on the 4th of January—
JUDGE: Okay, just a few days after you put the deposit down.
PLAINTIFF: Yes. I had the money in my pocket to pay the difference, but I had been thinking about it. There was no evidence on the lamp—like where it was manufactured or where it came from—so I asked.
They told me, ""We never do that."" Then, they put the owner of the store on the phone. I asked him about the lamp’s history, and he said, ""I’ll write you down anything you want,"" but there was no printed evidence.
So I said, ""I’m not spending $900 on something I have no background on.""
JUDGE: Well, it sounds like you had already decided to spend $900 on something you didn’t know the background of because you had already paid a $500 deposit.
PLAINTIFF: I did, but I was a little impulsive. I get carried away very easily.
JUDGE: So, she wants to invoke the ""carried away"" exception to deposits at your store.
Tell me what your store policy is.
DEFENDANT: My store policy—can I show you?
JUDGE: Sure, approach.
DEFENDANT: Thank you.
We have a lot of impulse buyers—
JUDGE: What kind of store is this?
DEFENDANT: It started as a gift shop, but I expanded into furniture and clothing. We sell all kinds of art and new stuff.
JUDGE: New stuff? No used?
DEFENDANT: No, it’s all new. Once in a while, I get a vintage piece of furniture I love, but everything is new.
JUDGE: Okay.
DEFENDANT: She came in and fell in love with the lamp.
JUDGE: Was that lamp something you came into, or something you bought from a vendor?
DEFENDANT: We purchased a whole lot of lamps from a vendor who was manufacturing them in Mexico. The first day she came in, I explained to her that the glass is from Germany, and it’s manufactured in Mexico.
Originally, the price was $1,800. But since I figured she was an impulse buyer, I said, ""$800 takes it.""
She was shaking for it, but her husband said, ""Let’s go have dinner first."" Then, when she came back, I wasn’t there. Later, she called and my manager said she was upset and wanted a warranty.
So I said, ""I’ll give you any warranty you want—it’s a lamp, what can go wrong with it?""
She asked, ""How much warranty?"" I said, ""Is one year good enough?""
Then, she changed the subject. She wanted to know where it was made and asked for a printed story. I told her, ""It’s not a real Tiffany lamp.""
JUDGE: Did she think it was a Tiffany lamp?
DEFENDANT: You can ask her.
JUDGE: Did you think it was a Tiffany lamp?
PLAINTIFF: No.
JUDGE: Well, now what?
PLAINTIFF: I would like my money back.
JUDGE: Of course you would, honey, but you don’t get your money back because the receipt where you put your deposit specifically says ""no refunds.""
PLAINTIFF: But this is not a layaway. This was a deposit.
JUDGE: I don’t care. What do you think a deposit means?
A deposit means you’re paying him $500 so that he doesn’t sell the lamp to anybody else. And he didn’t. He did his part. Why should you just be able to take it back?
PLAINTIFF: I don’t know if the lamp is still there.
JUDGE: Is the lamp still there?
DEFENDANT: Yes, it’s still there.
JUDGE: Okay, go visit it—it’s your lamp.
Can she use the $500 to buy something different?
DEFENDANT: Your Honor, we are a neighborhood store, and I love my customers. I want them to be happy, especially her—she’s very dramatic, like an actress.
JUDGE: He says he loves his customers. You have a $500 credit there. You can either buy the lamp or use the $500 to buy something else.
But he has no legal obligation to give you back the cash. Your deposit is non-refundable, as your receipt clearly says.
Next time, don’t be an impulse buyer.
PLAINTIFF: Okay.
JUDGE: Verdict for the defendant. Good luck, folks.
BAILIFF: Thank you."

Judge: If we thought that a reasonable way to deal with this really quite difficult situation is for the government to do what it does on the merits part of the cases and go in and tell the judge in camera why the person was listed and why the person was taken off and what it has to say about why the person won't be taken off, do you know anything about how that process works? Is counsel part of that process typically when it's a substantive issue? And do you want to say anything about whether counsel has to be part of that process?

Plaintiff’s Lawyer: Sure, Your Honor.
A few thoughts. The government, generally speaking, does provide some kind of explanation as to the reasons for a person's watchlisting publicly even.
And so this is an exceptional case where they provided no information at all. In other watchlist cases, the government, for example, in a prior case, provided an extensive series of ex parte in camera declarations to supplement some of the public descriptions, and I believe there was a mixture of access that was provided to counsel in that case. But some of the information that gets disclosed ends up being designated as sensitive security information.
We've gotten cleared on that basis. But, you know, there are all those options, but the government had all those options before they got to this Court.
And so, before 2018, they decided— in the first application of voluntary cessation doctrine to this case, they didn't provide any declaration. After the first application, they provided this declaration. And so we think that the government had the opportunity to muster what evidence it wanted on the mootness question, and it's provided this to the Court.

Judge: Have you suggested any of these alternatives or offered to reach an accommodation with the government in this case in this fashion?

Plaintiff’s Lawyer: I believe there is a protective order in place currently at the— or there was—

Judge: Beyond a standard protective order, have you made any attempts to settle this case with the government?

Plaintiff’s Lawyer: Yes, Your Honor, we have. We've done—I think, before the appellate court's decision, there was a mediation, and the mediation was in part about what the government was willing to say, and it turns out that the government is not willing to say a word more than what's in the declaration they provided.

Judge: Worth a try. (Laughter.)

Judge: Can we go back to an earlier question? What's the remedy you're seeking and how do you have standing for that remedy?

Plaintiff’s Lawyer: Yes.

Judge: Let's clarify. Are you seeking an injunction? Are you seeking just a declaration? And why would the declaration not violate the law that the other side claims it would?

Plaintiff’s Lawyer: Yes, Your Honor.
We're seeking an injunction and a declaratory judgment. The injunction is the same injunction that we saw at the beginning of the case to prevent the government from using the unlawful reasons that it did to list him and from using the same unlawful procedures that it did previously. And the reason that injunction is still viable and live is because the voluntary cessation doctrine isn't exactly an exception to mootness.
It's when mootness exists.
And so, here, the plaintiff has an interest in the government not returning to its old ways, and so he maintains the interest in the injunction. But there's even more concrete things. The government, by standing by its prior decision, indicates an interest in continuing to use his past No Fly List status in the future. Among many of the things that the government is likely to consider about the plaintiff when it runs into him in the future is the fact that the government for a period of five years put him on the No Fly List.
And so the Court, this Court or another, lower courts, can order the government to not use his past No Fly List status for any particular purpose. Annotate the records—that's something that the government has been ordered to do by a court in a prior case after the only No Fly List trial in this nation's history.
The trial judge ordered the government to annotate the No Fly List record that still existed, expunge the ones that they could.
And so we think that there's plenty for the Court to do on the injunctive side. On the declaratory judgment side, the plaintiff, the reason he brought this lawsuit was so that he could go about his everyday life, and that is what ran him into the No Fly List.
A decision, a declaratory judgment spelling out what the government's authorities are, what the plaintiff's rights should be, will have meaning to him.

Judge: I still don't understand exactly what you want.
You wouldn't be satisfied simply with an injunction that says the government is enjoined from using evidence that would be in violation of the First Amendment? That wouldn't satisfy you, right?

Plaintiff’s Lawyer: I don't think so, Your Honor.
I think we would have to get very specific.

Judge: All right.
So suppose one of the reasons why he was put on was that he traveled to a certain country, and suppose the passage of time convinced the government that the fact that he traveled there at some point—I'm just speculating, this is not connected to the real facts—but let's suppose that that's the case. The passage of time means that having traveled there in the past was no longer much—carried much probative weight and, therefore, he didn't deserve to continue to be on the No Fly List.
So what do you want? Do you want, like, an advisory opinion, if you go back there, you might get back on, or, if you go to any other country about which there might be some suspicion, if you go to another flagged country, maybe that'll put you over the top? What exactly do you think could possibly be done?

Plaintiff’s Lawyer: Yeah, it's—I think it's a difficult fact-intensive question about what injunction would be appropriate, but the injunction might be at a higher level of generality.
Perhaps it's the government being required to make a showing of criminal conduct as part of a person's listing. So I think that there are possibilities—

Judge: Well, that would be a huge change.

Plaintiff’s Lawyer: That would be a change.
And I'm just doing that—

Judge: That would be dramatic. Not to interrupt.

Plaintiff’s Lawyer: Yeah.
And I'm just doing that as an illustrative change of something that the government could do that would not be specific to this plaintiff's case. But the opposite is also true.
The government could do something that's only specific to this plaintiff.
For example, if the procedural safeguards that the Court put in place— that the government put in place—were not generally applicable but were only specific to this plaintiff, this Court could look at those procedural safeguards and say, hey, before the government relists him, they're going to call this lawyer and they're going to call—it's going to go to this committee and they're going to make sure that the terms of the declaration are implemented.
So there's a way for the government to do it specific to this plaintiff.
There's a way for the government to do it program-wide.

Plaintiff's Lawyer: Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court: The Ninth Circuit holds that Rule 704(b) prohibits only explicit opinions that the "particular defendant had the state of mind required to convict." The government appears unwilling to endorse that full test. It seems to agree, contrary to Ninth Circuit precedent, that Rule 704(b) forbids an expert from testifying that people in the defendant's position always have the requisite mens rea.

Such testimony never explicitly mentions the defendant, of course, but as the government seems to recognize, it is clearly in the rules of -- in the words of Rule 704(b) an opinion about whether the defendant had the requisite state of mind. In other words, it establishes a class of individuals, including the defendant, and it assigns a mens rea to that class.

Agent Flood's testimony here was no different. He testified that in "most circumstances," people like the defendant know they have drugs in the car when they cross the border. The only difference between that testimony and absolute class-wide testimony is that he posited that people like the defendant usually, instead of always, have the requisite mens rea. But any such distinction is immaterial.

Imagine an expert took the stand and said: I believe the defendant in this case probably or maybe 80 percent of the time -- or maybe there's an 80 percent likelihood had the requisite mens rea. Obviously, Rule 704(b) would prohibit that testimony. And the testimony here is exactly the same. It expresses an opinion, a probabilistic opinion instead of an absolute opinion, but an opinion about whether the defendant had the requisite mens rea.

So that leaves the government's final objection, that Rule 704(b) should not be construed to prohibit what the government calls inferentially relevant testimony. And we agree with that proposition. The rule that I'm advocating today has been employed by the Fifth Circuit for over 20 years, and it makes clear that modus operandi evidence, for example, things like drug traffic organizations use couriers to transport drugs across the border, that they -- that drugs are extremely valuable, are perfectly legitimate.

The rule in the Fifth Circuit is that testimony from which a jury might infer mens rea is perfectly permissible, but testimony that assigns a mens rea to the defendant or people in her class is not okay. And that's what Agent Flood did here, and for that reason, we'd ask the Court to reverse.

Judge: You put quite a bit of weight in your argument on the preposition "about." And that was a stylistic change, certainly non-substantive. Do we normally put that much emphasis or that much weight on stylistic changes to statutes?

Plaintiff's Lawyer: Well, I think what I would say, Your Honor, is the Court obviously puts a lot of weight on the text of the statute. And so I start with the current text of the statute that is current law.

Judge: Okay. So, even with that, it says "about defendant." It doesn't say "about" someone else. So you're saying that even if it's indirect, it's about this defendant?

Plaintiff's Lawyer: Let me say two things, Your Honor. First, to finish my answer about the word "about," what we say is that preposition is important, just like the prior preposition "as to" before the stylistic amendment --

Judge: Yeah.

Plaintiff's Lawyer: -- because it makes clear the Ninth Circuit's clear statement rule requiring an explicit opinion is too narrow. So that's what the preposition "about" does. Now you also asked me about "the" defendant. And our position there is very simple.

It is that that word covers either a direct statement about the particular defendant or a statement about a class of people, including the defendant. I don't think the government disagrees that the rule covers a statement covering -- I'm sorry, that the rule covers a class of people including the defendant as long as it's stated in absolute terms.

So, if I understand the government's position correctly, it agrees you need not mention the explicit defendant. If you talk about a class of people that includes the defendant and say those people always have the requisite mens rea, I think the government agrees, contrary to the Ninth Circuit, that Rule 704(b) applies.

Judge: Well, I thought the --

Plaintiff's Lawyer: And all we're saying -- sorry.

Judge: Well, I thought the argument there was that if you say "all defendants," all people in this class do this, then it obviously would include the defendant.

Plaintiff's Lawyer: I think that's exactly right, Your Honor. So the only move left from there is to say, if the expert were to testify people like this almost always, virtually always, 99 percent of the time, probably, in most circumstances, all of those are still opinions about whether the defendant had the mens rea.

They're just not absolute opinions. But they're still stating an opinion about whether a class of people including this defendant have the requisite mens rea. And I think that's why the text of the rule, opinions about whether the defendant had the mens rea, covers this exact case.

Judge: You have to file with the state to claim the benefits to start with, correct?

Lawyer of Defendant: Correct.

Judge: And so you have to be there.

So how and when can you go to federal court?

Lawyer of Defendant: Well, I think --

Judge: That -- that's what I don't understand from the other side, but do you understand it?

Lawyer of Defendant: I think you can go to federal court at any time, and your claim might lose on the merits if you haven't tried out the process from the state court, but you can still go.

It's just the ground for dismissal wouldn't be a lack of exhaustion.

Judge: It might be a lack of ripeness perhaps, though?

Lawyer of Defendant: Right.

In principle, a federal court could dismiss for lack of ripeness, yes, but it just wouldn't be an exhaustion dismissal.

Judge: So what's --

Judge: So you're really stuck in a state process with a loop that you can't get out of?

Lawyer of Defendant: Well, our view is that if we went to federal court in this case, the -- the federal court would not dismiss for lack of exhaustion under Patsy and would -- it would entertain our due process claims on the merits.

Judge: Did you try a futility exception argument?

Lawyer of Defendant: I don't -- no, I don't think we --we made that argument below.

Judge: Do you --do you have any reason to believe that Alabama doesn't recognize a futility exception requirement?

Lawyer of Defendant: Well, I -- I mean, we argued below that it would be absurd.

In our brief --

Judge: Yeah.

Lawyer of Defendant: -- we argued it would be absurd to --

Judge: You argued it would be absurd and Catch 22 and --

Lawyer of Defendant: Right.

Judge: -- Heller was trotted out.

And we -- we hear that from time to time, but that's what a futility exception exists to -- to address.

And I'm just curious whether you know whether Alabama law permits a futility argument, and -- and --and you're saying, as I understand it, that none was attempted here in any event?

Lawyer of Defendant: So I don't know the answer to the question.

Alabama does say this is a jurisdictional limitation, which implies to me that there aren't equitable exceptions like futility, but I -- I'm not sure.

Judge: You don't know?

Lawyer of Defendant: I don't know.

Judge: Okay.

And then, if -- if one were challenging a benefits decision by the federal --by the federal government, there -- there's almost assuredly going to be an exhaustion requirement, Social Security disability benefits or whatever, in -- in federal court, and I'm just curious what you might say about the incongruity of refusing to allow a state to have a similar exhaustion requirement that the federal government imposes on Americans.

Lawyer of Defendant: Well, I don't think it's incongruous at all because I think the practical effect of our rule is that most claims would be dismissed in state court if you're just seeking benefits for --for lack of merit because you've gotten enough process.

In other words, if you -- if you just file a claim in a state agency saying, I want benefits, and then you get a bad decision from the hearing examiner, and then you just go straight to federal court and say, well, that was a due process violation, I didn't get a good hearing before the examiner, you would lose in federal court.

The federal court would say there's no due process violation.

You have plenty of process in --

Judge: All parties, please raise your right hand. You may be seated.

Judge: Litigants have been sworn. Your honor, this is a car case, right? What's the bird doing here?

Plaintiff: The bird is a witness. She was in the car when we developed a series of issues.

Judge: The bird is a witness? Okay, I have seen it all. For real. The bird is—do you feel the bird is a wit—did you swear the bird in? Oh, raise your right wing. Did you put this one, including the bird? Oh, for the love of all that's holy! I thought I had taken the wrong case out. Okay, let's get to it, Mr. Hess.

Judge: Who is she?

Plaintiff: This is my fiancée.

Judge: Was she in the car with you?

Plaintiff: Yes, she was.

Judge: All right, tell me what happened here.

Plaintiff: Well, on February 15th, I brought my truck into Prestige Auto. It was sputtering and having problems driving.

Judge: What kind of truck is this?

Plaintiff: It's a Ford F-150, a 1991.

Judge: Oh wow, it’s in very good condition.

Plaintiff: It’s actually an art car.

Judge: Yes, it’s also old enough to vote. Okay, and—so what does sputtering mean?

Plaintiff: Sputtering means it was having fuel problems. I was pressing the gas, and it wasn’t really going.

Judge: Okay.

Plaintiff: I brought it into Prestige Auto. I have dual fuel tanks, and I never use the front tank. I only use the rear tank, and it’s been working that way for ten years. I brought it into Prestige Auto to have them diagnose the problem and fix it.

Plaintiff: They changed the fuel filter. Then they said they wanted to do a full oil change. So I agreed to the oil change and expected the car to work. They gave it back to me the next day, and I drove it. As I'm driving to work on the West Side Highway, it breaks down again.

Plaintiff: So I had them tow it back. Then they said it wasn’t the fuel filter or the fuel lines—it was the fuel pump.

Judge: Well, did they say that, or did they say, “Now your fuel pump went”?

Plaintiff: No, they said it was a fuel pump problem. They never mentioned that anything was wrong with it before the first time I brought it in.

Judge: Did you say to them, “Well, I don’t want to pay extra,” or did you pay that second time?

Plaintiff: I paid both times.

Judge: Right. Okay. So the first time you went in there, what was the repair? What did they do?

Defendant: The first time he came in, he asked us to just replace the fuel filter, do an oil change, and replace the neck for the tank that was not working. Those were his notes.

Judge: Can I see the evidence that he’s telling you how to fix it instead of you telling him what needs doing? Isn’t that customary for you to tell the customer?

Defendant: He just wrote notes when he came in. He said he wanted an oil change, to see if we could fix the tank that was not working.

Judge: What tank was not working?

Defendant: The front one.

Judge: But he doesn’t even use the front.

Defendant: Yes, but he asked us to see if we could put it to work again.

Judge: Did you see if you could put it to work again?

Defendant: We couldn’t because the car is older; most of the parts—

Judge: So what did you charge him for that first time?

Defendant: A fuel filter and an oil change. That’s it.

Judge: Okay. Then he comes back the second time and says what?

Defendant: The second time, he was towed in.

Judge: How long after?

Defendant: Two days later.

Judge: So it was towed in, and what does he say?

Defendant: We checked the car; there was no fuel pressure. We diagnosed that the fuel pump wasn’t working. We called him and told him he needed a fuel pump, and we put it in.

Judge: And then was the car working?

Defendant: The car was working fine, yes.

Judge: How do you test a car to make sure it’s working fine before calling the customer?

Defendant: First, we leave it on the lift to make sure there are no leaks while it’s running. Then we leave it outside for a little bit and take it for a test drive.

Judge: So you pick up the car. How long does the repair last you that second time?

Plaintiff: It lasted until I drove home to pick up my tools, then picked up my fiancée and the birds, and we started driving again. On the Grand Central Parkway, people were screaming at us that something was wrong with our car. We smelled gas. We pulled over, and gas was gushing out of the bottom of our truck.

Judge: Gushing?

Plaintiff: Gushing. We called Prestige Auto, and they said they couldn’t do anything until the morning.

Judge: Did you take it to another mechanic?

Plaintiff: Yes, we did.

Judge: Do you have anything in writing from that mechanic?

Plaintiff: Yes, I do.

Judge: Did that mechanic say Prestige did something wrong?

Plaintiff: Yes, they said the problem was a rubber grommet on the front fuel tank, and they also found a faulty relay valve switch. Prestige should have checked these.

Judge: There’s no evidence that the defendant failed to install a fuel pump correctly or predict another issue with a 20-year-old car. My ruling is for the defendan

Judge: We'll hear argument this morning in Case 22-448, Consumer Financial Protection Bureau versus the Community Financial Services Association.

Plaintiff's Lawyer: Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court: The Fifth Circuit's decision in this case is the first time any court in our nation's history has held that Congress violated the Appropriations Clause by enacting a statute providing funding. This Court should uphold the CFPB's funding statute because it is firmly grounded in constitutional text and in historical practice dating back to the founding.

The text of the Constitution shows that when the Framers wanted to limit Congress's appropriations authority, they did so expressly. And while the Framers restricted appropriations for the Army to two years, they applied no similar limits on appropriations for any other agency. History confirms that point.

Since the founding, Congress has consistently funded agencies through standing appropriations that are not time-limited and that provide significant discretion over how much to spend. The first Congress did exactly this with the very first agency it created, the Customs Service, and the same is true for other founding-era agencies, including the Post Office, the National Mint, the Patent Office, revenue officers, and the National Bank.

Congress has used this kind of appropriation as the default when funding financial regulators, including the Federal Reserve Board, the Office of the Comptroller of the Currency, the Federal Deposit Insurance Corporation, the National Credit Union Administration, the Farm Credit Administration, and the Federal Housing Finance Agency. The CFPB's appropriation fits squarely within this unbroken line of historical practice.

In fact, Congress exercised significantly more discretion and control over the Bureau's funding by capping its annual appropriation in an amount that is far lower than many other agencies' budgets.

Defendant's Lawyer: Respondents argue that the combination of features in the CFPB statute violates some previously unrecognized constitutional line. But the Bureau's appropriation is materially identical to the numerous funding statutes just listed, and Respondents concede those statutes are constitutional.

This Court should reject Respondents' attempt to gerrymander a rule to fit the CFPB alone without providing a coherent theory about how to interpret and apply the Appropriations Clause.

Judge: General, other than passing an appropriations law, are there any limits on what Congress can do?

Plaintiff's Lawyer: At the outset, as your question touches on, the term "appropriation" itself contains some essential requirements. That term is defined to mean a law that provides for funding and that specifies both the source and the purpose of the funding.

That's how an appropriation was defined at the founding, it's how it's always been understood, and that's still how it's defined today in sources like the GAO Red Book, which is Congress's own appropriations treatise. So we think that at the outset, it's not just any old law but a law that contains those features that constitutes an appropriation.

Then I think the question becomes, are there other limits out there on how Congress can structure funding for particular types of activities or government functions? We know, obviously, from the Army Appropriations Clause that the answer is yes. With respect to that particular government function, there's a durational limit, and it's based on the nature of the activity because the Framers were specifically concerned about having a standing Army.

But I think the Army Appropriations Clause itself demonstrates that there are not otherwise constraints in the Appropriations Clause that would limit Congress in how it decides to structure the funding.

Judge: So, beyond the initial almost skeletal requirements of duration and purpose, there isn't—if I hear you right, there is no other condition?

Plaintiff's Lawyer: We don't think that the Appropriations Clause places those limits on Congress itself when it enacts a funding statute, but I do want to emphasize that our argument here also relies heavily on history.

So, if you have in mind some kind of funding statute or arrangement that's never been done in all of this nation's history, I think the Court could take that into account in a future case.

Here, though, we have a specific type of appropriation, a capped lump-sum appropriation that’s standing for a single agency, and our historical argument is that this is nothing new or unprecedented. All of the agencies previously listed had similar appropriations.

Judge: Is there any role that appropriations play in the separation of powers? It seems that, at least when I was in the executive branch, Congress exercised appropriations to check the executive branch to some extent. In the case of the CFPB, how would that play out?

Plaintiff's Lawyer: I do agree and think that, of course, it's a critical element of—

Judge: Yeah.

Plaintiff's Lawyer: —the separation of powers that Congress has control over the purse.

Judge: Yeah.

Plaintiff's Lawyer: Our argument here is that Congress has exercised that power. This Court, of course, is looking at a statute that Congress itself enacted that set up this funding mechanism for the CFPB, which is similar in kind to the way that Congress has funded other financial regulatory agencies.

Judge: On that score, though, the Ninth Circuit at least held that the explanation you provided later was sufficient. It just wasn’t timely. The information the government gave, though redacted, didn’t provide much. You could simply say, “We think he’s a member of a gang based on our contacts with law enforcement,” period. How would that implicate any serious governmental interest?

Defendant’s Lawyer: Well, it would go beyond any of the Court’s previous cases and beyond what is often given in similar cases, beyond what Congress requires in cases involving denials under (a)(2) and (a)(3).

Judge: No.

Defendant’s Lawyer: And—

Judge: Of course, it does, but we are asking what due process and Mandel may require. If the Ninth Circuit says it requires more than a statutory citation but less than revealing intelligence, then a sentence would suffice—which is what they said. Do you have any objection to that? Is that wrong?

Defendant’s Lawyer: We do think that the statutory citation ought to be enough for the reasons explained in previous rulings.

Judge: But that was a—

Defendant’s Lawyer: And—

Judge: —different statute. A totally different statute. The statute in that case contained the kinds of sentences that I think we are discussing here, relating to terrorist activities. It didn’t just say “terrorist activities, period.” Identifying a subsection provided some factual basis for the determination. But that’s not happening here.

Defendant’s Lawyer: That would be true if we had identified particular subsections of the terrorist activities bar, but in that case, we just cited the entire terrorist activities bar.

Judge: But there were six—

Defendant’s Lawyer: —Justice Breyer’s dissent talked about—

Judge: —there were six discrete terrorist acts described. Here, the statute only states “any other unlawful activity.” It’s a generalist catch-all for “security and related grounds.” Your brief even suggests that the ground must be tied to security, but we don’t know what security means here. The way it’s written, you could deny entry based on something as minor as a jaywalking conviction or stealing a piece of bread as a child. Some countries even criminalize children for such acts. How does a citation to unlawful activity tell anybody anything? At least in the D.C. Circuit case, you stated the person was a member of a criminal organization. That tells you something.

Defendant’s Lawyer: That does tell more, I agree.

Judge: Well, but—

Defendant’s Lawyer: We have not applied the statute in cases involving jaywalking, and this wouldn’t just cover past criminal offenses. It requires a reasonable belief that the person will commit unlawful activity upon entry into the U.S. That is why gang membership has been considered an indicator since 1965.

Judge: Right, but—

Judge: The point is, saying, “We think you will commit some kind of criminal activity,” does not tell a person anything. But saying, “We think you’re an MS-13 member,” does. If someone isn’t actually a member, they would know how to contest that finding. There’s a big difference between these two statements. Both are a single sentence, but one provides a way to challenge the determination, and the other does not.

Defendant’s Lawyer: I understand that point, but I don’t think that’s the point of Mandel review. The purpose of Mandel review isn’t to scrutinize the agency’s reasoning or improve their decision-making. It’s simply to confirm that the government had a reason that was facially legitimate and bona fide. It’s more of a spot check.

Judge: But how does simply stating “unlawful activity” fulfill that purpose?

Judge: Mr.—

Judge: How does saying, “We think you committed unlawful activity,” meet that requirement?

Defendant’s Lawyer: We think the person is—

Judge: Going to.

Defendant’s Lawyer: —going to commit unlawful activity.

Judge: Excuse me. So if the purpose of Mandel review is what you say it is, how does merely citing that one broad section fulfill that goal?

Plaintiff's Lawyer: So it's interesting, we today understand addiction as an immutable status. In Robinson, the Court suggested that someone might be recovered and no longer have the status of addiction. So the Robinson court wasn't thinking about addiction as something that couldn't change over time.

Judge: Well, that may limit the applicability of Robinson to a different situation, but what is the -- I mean, what is the analytic approach to deciding whether something's a status or a situation of conduct?

Plaintiff's Lawyer: So the question is, a status is something that a person is when they're not doing anything. So being addicted, having cancer, being poor, are all statuses that you have apart from any conduct.

Judge: Having cancer is not the same as being homeless, right? I mean, maybe I'm just repeating myself because homelessness can -- you -- you can remove the homeless status in an instant if you move to a shelter or situations otherwise change. And of course, it can be moved the other way as well if you're kicked out of the shelter, whatever. So that is a distinction from all these other things that have been labeled status, isn't it?

Plaintiff's Lawyer: I -- I don't think so because, you know, a cancer patient can go into remission, they no longer have that status. I don't think -- I mean, I don't think there's any question that being poor is a status. It's something that you are apart from anything you do. It's a status that can change over time and at that point you wouldn't be a part of the class but I don't think it changes the fact that it is a status. And what Robinson found so offensive about statuses is --

Judge: Well, I guess is -- is being a bank robber a status?

Plaintiff's Lawyer: No, because being a bank robber means you rob banks. So -- so the definition and the conduct --

Judge: Violating this ordinance means upon being asked to leave you don't leave.

Plaintiff's Lawyer: Violating this ordinance means you're homeless. So again, homelessness is not something that you do. It's just something that you are. And so the question becomes, when you attach the universal human attribute of sleeping or breathing to that status, does it make the punishment conduct-based instead of status-based? And I think the answer is --

Judge: Counsel, Edwards v. California in 1941 struck down a law that made it a crime to transport an indigent person, correct?

Plaintiff's Lawyer: Yes.

Judge: Indigency is a not -- is a condition that can change over time, but the law was aimed at the transport of a person who wasn't morally reprehensible.

Plaintiff's Lawyer: Yes. I think that's notable because our history and tradition as a country is to emphatically reject any sort of local legislative scheme that has the effect of pushing the burdens of poverty or indigency into other communities. It's woven throughout our Constitution. So Edwards located it in the Dormant Commerce Clause. We have Saenz v. Roe which locates it in the Privileges of Immunities Clause; Papachristou addresses that status-based punishment in the context of procedural due process. What Robinson held is that when that expulsion is effectuated through status-based punishment, it violates the Punishments Clause.

Judge: How do you define a community? So when another judge was describing how New Jersey has so many tightly woven municipalities close together and here, you know, the Chief Justice was asking about whether if Grants Pass, if there was a new homeless shelter with lots of beds right across the border 10 minutes away, you know, could that be taken into account? And I think there was some back and forth and not necessarily agreement on that. What is your position? How do you define a community? Take that example of a homeless shelter right outside the limits of Grants Pass.

Plaintiff's Lawyer: Yes. So to -- so to answer that hypothetical first, I'm not concerned -- I don't have any problems with saying that a homeless person in Grants Pass has legal and physical access to a shelter that's just over the lines, if that's, in fact, true. Lots of jurisdictions limit their homeless shelters to people who are residents. So -- and just to be clear, there was no suggestion in the record here that there were any shelters available outside of Grants Pass.

Judge: Understood. But so community doesn't need to be determined by jurisdictional lines, is what you're telling me --

Plaintiff's Lawyer: No.

Judge: -- as a matter of -- because, let's see, I'm asking all of this because, in response to another judge, you were pointing out that our -- you know, our nation has a history and tradition of not saying you can shunt homeless people or the poor out of your jurisdiction and on to others. So -- or out of your community and on to others, is I think how you -- how you phrased it. So I'm asking, how do we know what those lines are? And you're saying it doesn't have to be jurisdiction-specific.

Plaintiff's Lawyer: No. I think jurisdiction matters because that tells us kind of the lines in which the -- whatever ordinance or statute applies. Judge:

I just want to make sure you acknowledge there is, as a factual matter, some group of people?

Lawyer of Plaintiff:
Yes, yes.

We're certainly not denying that some people might detransition or regret this care, but all of the available evidence shows that it's a very small number.

Judge:
Then, to pick up on the Chief Justice and Justice Alito's questions, it's a obviously evolving debate.

I mean, just in the last couple years in Europe, there's big changes in terms of how they're thinking about it and how they're thinking about these risks and benefits that you and I have just been talking about and you've been elaborating.

If it's evolving like that and changing and England's pulling back and Sweden's pulling back, it strikes me as, you know, a pretty heavy yellow light, if not red light, for this Court to come in, the nine of us, and to constitutionalize the whole area when the rest of the world or at least the people who -- the countries that have been at the forefront of this are, you know, pumping the brakes on this kind of treatment because of concerns about the risks.

Lawyer of Plaintiff:
We certainly are not asking the Court to set forth some bright-line constitutional rules in this space that is going to -- to really take further debate and evaluation of regulatory options away from states.

We think, as I mentioned, that the Court really only needs to decide the first-order question here of whether this law classifies based on sex. I think that's entirely distinct from some of the concerns you've identified about what justifications the State has.

Judge:
Do you think that West Virginia law you mentioned is constitutional?

Lawyer of Plaintiff:
I think it would likely satisfy heightened scrutiny.

It hasn't been subject to adversarial testing because I don't think anyone has sued to challenge it, so I haven't looked at the record that West Virginia would build, but I do think that there is room here for states to enact tailored measures to try to guard against the kind of risks that you're concerned about and that the State has identified.

Judge:
And last topic, on the heightened review -- and you -- you say all we need to do is do heightened review and that's kind of a minimal approach -- step, I mean, I'm not sure, really, that the follow-on effects of that could be pretty significant.

I think Justice Kagan alluded to that in her question or at least raised that as a question.

And I want to ask in particular about one thing.

If you prevail here on the standard of review, what would that mean for women's and girls' sports in particular? Would transgender athletes have a constitutional right, as you see it, to play in women's and girls' sports, basketball, swimming, volleyball, track, et cetera, notwithstanding the competitive fairness and safety issues that have been vocally raised by some female athletes seen in the amicus brief of the many women athletes in this case? So can you explain how intermediate scrutiny would apply to women's sports?

Lawyer of Plaintiff:
Yes.

And -- and just as a threshold clarifying point, I want to be clear that when it comes to access to sex-separated spaces, like sports and bathrooms, courts already recognize that those are facial sex classifications that trigger heightened scrutiny.

So it's actually not the question teed up here about how to classify the law in the first place or how to identify whether it's a sex classification.

Instead, that's taken as given in that litigation.

And the entire focal point of the disputes in those cases has been, well, does the state have an important governmental interest and does it need to draw the lines to exclude --

Judge:
If we -- if we --

Lawyer of Plaintiff:
-- transgender people.

Judge:
Right.

But how would it --how would intermediate scrutiny, which we may not -- if we went to intermediate scrutiny, there' s a possibility we would apply it here.

How would it apply to, in your view -- and maybe you don't have fully informed views, which would be fine -- but how do you think they would -- it would apply to sports?

Lawyer of Plaintiff:
So courts have split on that issue, and I hesitate to -- to try in -- you know, in a vacuum without an actual factual record to try to opine on the State's justification and whether it will satisfy that standard.

It's obviously a different set of governmental interests that are being asserted there, and those would have to be analyzed in their own right.

But I think that this Court, if it wants to preserve space to make clear that nothing here should be understood to affect the -- the separate questions that are arising there, the Court could very well do so.

And we would have no objection --

Judge:
Do you think --

Lawyer of Plaintiff:
-- to explicit language saying this decision does not in any way or should not be understood to affect the separate state interests there that have to be evaluated on their own terms.

Judge:
Okay.

But looking ahead, do you think it's logically possible as a matter of constitutional decision-making to say that laws like the ones at -- the one at issue here do not satisfy intermediate scrutiny, but laws that restrict women's and girls' sports in a way that transgender athletes cannot participate would satisfy intermediate scrutiny? Is that logically possible?

Lawyer of Plaintiff:
Oh, yes, definitely.

So we do think intermediate scrutiny applies in both contexts, but there are a different state of --a different set of state interests at play.

And I think one readily apparent difference is that in the context of sports, there are arguments made that that affects the rights of cisgender women and that the ability to allow transgender women to compete on those teams is going to be other regarding in the sense of having those external impacts.

There's nothing like this here. Allowing transgender individuals who have carefully thought about this and consulted with their parents and their medical team to access these medications that have health benefits recognized here and abroad in no way affects the rights of other people.

And so I think the Court could well understand the statute here to fail intermediate scrutiny even if it would survive there.
