Dialogue:
Judge: We'll hear argument next in Case 23-3, Coinbase versus Suski. Counsel.

Defendant's Lawyer: Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court: The Federal Arbitration Act requires courts to enforce arbitration agreements according to their terms.

Respondents and Coinbase agreed to arbitrate any disputes about Coinbase services and to delegate to an arbitrator any threshold disputes about whether specific claims were subject to arbitration.

Despite this delegation clause, the parties have spent nearly three years disputing this threshold issue.

That's because, instead of enforcing the delegation clause, the courts below came up with rationales to evade it and to instead answer the question of arbitrability for themselves.

The Ninth Circuit did so by characterizing Respondents' challenge as one of contract formation, asserting that contract formation can never be delegated and sidestepping the delegation clause.

The issue, as the court of appeals saw it, was whether the parties formed a contract to arbitrate these claims.

If that approach were correct, courts in every case could ignore delegation clauses by just characterizing an arbitrability dispute as a question of whether the parties formed a contract to arbitrate the claims at issue.

Respondents do not defend that reasoning. They agree the contracts here were formed and that they were in effect. Their argument is that the scope of the otherwise applicable arbitration agreement was narrowed by a later contract to exclude their asserted claims.

That question is one of arbitrability, and the parties agreed an arbitrator would decide it.

Respondents blur three distinct legal questions in a case like this one.

The first is the merits of the claims the Respondents assert.

The second is whether the merits of those claims should be arbitrated.

And the third, which is the only question that was before the courts below and is before this Court, is who decides whether the merits should be arbitrated.

This third question is antecedent to the other two.

I welcome the Court's questions.

Judge: Isn't the problem the confusion that results from the difference between the official rules and the agreement?

Defendant's Lawyer: So, Your Honor, at some level, there is some confusion that the Respondents have argued results from looking at these two together.

But the only way there is confusion is if you conflate those different layers of questions because the delegation clause that's in the arbitration agreement answers directly the who decides.

Judge: So what is the source of the disagreement here? Doesn't it come from the rules?

Defendant's Lawyer: Certainly.

The source of the disagreement is that there is a forum selection clause in the official rules that says something about controversies regarding the promotion.

And the Respondents have argued that, by using that language, the second contract somehow carved out of the arbitration agreement the claims they want to assert here.

Judge: Well, couldn't this have been solved in drafting by simply either having an express arbitration provision in the rules or by referring back to and incorporating the user agreement?

Defendant's Lawyer: So, Your Honor, I think those are certainly questions that could be and should be addressed by the arbitrator when this gets there.

But the threshold question is whether the confusion that Your Honor is referencing has anything to do with the who decides issue.

And there's nothing in the rules language --

Judge: Well, I think it goes a little deeper because you can also say, is there actually an arbitration agreement that comes out of the rules?

Defendant's Lawyer: So, Your Honor, I don't think there is an arbitration agreement anywhere in the official rules.

The Respondents' argument is to try to take the rules to create ambiguity about the -- or confusion, as Your Honor said, about what claims are actually subject to arbitration.

Judge: Yeah.

Defendant's Lawyer: But that is a garden-variety arbitrability question.

It doesn't speak to the threshold, gateway who decides question.

Judge: So can I ask you -- oh, go ahead.

Judge: Can I ask you about the who decides question? Because I think I'm following your argument, but I guess I'm questioning whether you're right that the only way there is confusion is if you conflate the questions.

So let me posit this hypothetical.

Suppose we have two contracts, the first of which has a delegation clause that answers the who decides question, and the answer in the first contract is the arbitrator decides.

We have a second contract that answers the delegation question, and the answer in that case is the court decides.

In that situation, where you have these two different contracts with two different clear delegation principles, isn't the question at that point which contract controls?

Which, you know, I take it is another way of the Ninth Circuit saying, well, this contract superseded that one.

But really, it's a question at that point of what did the parties intend, what was their agreement about which of these two contracts controls the situation.

And in that case, I think you go to the court.

Why am I wrong about that? And isn't that the situation that's actually being presented on these facts?

Defendant's Lawyer: So I think you are right about that, not wrong about it.

And I think the distinction between your hypothetical and this issue in this case is a very important one.

You're right about it because, in your hypothetical, you identified a contract with a delegation clause and a second contract that isn't silent about delegation; it specifically addresses delegation and has a different provision.

So a party could come into court resisting arbitration and make a specific challenge to the delegation clause in the first contract that is directed at whether that particular delegation clause has been superseded by the later delegation clause.

It's all at this who decides level.

The problem in this case is that from the very beginning, the Respondents have agreed this arbitration agreement remains in effect.

This delegation clause remains in effect.

It would cover these claims.

Judge: Well, I did want to ask you just about that. I mean, let's say I'm with you on the fact that you can't prohibit being homeless, and because you can't prohibit being homeless, you can't prohibit sleeping outside if you are a genuinely homeless person.

And let's say I'm with you that the fact that this ordinance says, well, but we're prohibiting using a blanket, that can't be right. You know, you're not, like, just, like, get hypothermia and the constitutional problem will go away.

But it does seem as though there are line-drawing issues, as you go up, right? It's a very cold night and somebody wants to make a fire. It's raining and somebody wants to put up a tarp. The city has said you can sleep in particular areas, but it turns out that those areas have a ton of crime.

You know, you could go on and on. And I'm not -- how do you deal with questions like that? These are not, like, gotcha questions. This is, like, how do you deal with questions like that? Where is the line where the city can say our legitimate municipal interests can come in and say, you know, as to that, as to that, you can't do that.

Defendant’s Lawyer: Yeah, so what -- and there are several examples that you have there. With respect to tents and tarps, I guess, you -- you were saying, I -- I -- I'd think there's a difference between what you might need to realistically sleep outside if it's raining, snowing, or something like that, and what you might prefer to have as a structure for long-term camping.

As I mentioned, the city might say you can put up a tent if it's very cold, but you've got to take it down in the morning. That's like being in -- some shelters say you can stay here overnight, but you have to leave during the day and you can come back.

I mean, that might seem gratuitous of the city to do it. It might not want to do it. But -- but we're not saying that the Eighth Amendment would prevent it from doing it, and especially as you say, if there's no alternative and it's, you know, 20 -- 20 degrees.

With respect to fires, there are really important issues on the other side of that question. In an urban area, if you're creating fires, there may be hazards in a park. There might be --

Judge: So how does --

Defendant’s Lawyer: -- there might be fireplaces in a park.

Judge: How does a court make these judgments? Because these are tough judgments, and -- and usually they're the kind of judgments that we think of as municipalities making them.

But you're saying, no, there's a certain level where it's out of their hands and it's in the court's hands. And I guess I want to know what the principle is where those questions go to the courts and why that principle is the right principle.

Defendant’s Lawyer: I think -- I mean, I think there are two principles.

One is that it -- it's the municipality's determination, certainly in the first instance, with a great deal of flexibility how to address the question of homelessness and a time, place, and manner.

And then municipalities should -- should be able to choose the place, should be able to choose the attributes of that place, should be able to say we're not going to allow more than, you know, 20 people or something, you know, to -- to regulate it in that manner.

And I think the -- I think the principle, the Eighth Amendment principle, would be whether the -- the city has effectively prevented sleeping outside because the protections needed from the elements are not available.

And, certainly in Grants Pass, I would think even a blanket would not be enough under some -- but I think that's the -- I think that's the touchstone.

Are you basically -- does it boil down to or is the core principle that you can't criminalize homelessness, which includes not being able to criminalize sleeping outside?

If you can't sleep outside because of lack of protection from the elements, I think that's the principle a court would apply. But the Ninth Circuit, in a number of cases, has gone way beyond that.

And we think that's really the source of the problems that have been identified in the briefs, and not the core principle.

Judge: Thank you.

Judge: Counsel, I want to probe this a little bit further because it does seem to me this status/conduct distinction is very tricky.

And I had thought that after previous cases, the ruling was just limited to status. And now you're saying, well, there's some conduct that's effectively equated to status.

But you're saying involuntary drug use, you can regulate that conduct. That doesn't qualify as status.

You're saying compulsive alcohol use, you can regulate that conduct in public, public drunkenness, even if it's involuntary. That doesn't qualify as status, right?

Defendant’s Lawyer: Right.

Judge: You're saying you can regulate somebody who is hungry and has no other choice but to steal. You can regulate that conduct, even though it's a basic human necessity. And that doesn't come under the status side of the line, right?

Defendant’s Lawyer: Yes.

Judge: Okay. But when it comes to homelessness, which is a terribly difficult problem, you're saying that's different and -- because there are no beds available for them to go to in Grants Pass.

What about someone who has a mental health problem that prohibits them -- they cannot sleep in -- in a shelter. Are they allowed to sleep outside or not? Is that status or conduct that's regulable?

Defendant’s Lawyer: I -- I think the -- the question would be whether that shelter is available.

Judge: It's available.

Defendant’s Lawyer: Well, no, available to the individual?

Judge: It's available to the individual.

Defendant’s Lawyer: Well --

Judge: It's just because of their mental health problem, they cannot do it.

Defendant’s Lawyer: I -- I think there might be -- I mean, that's, the mental health problem --

Judge: Status or conduct?

Defendant’s Lawyer: The mental health situation itself is a status.

Judge: Right, I know that.

Defendant’s Lawyer: But -- but it --

Judge: It has this further knock-on effect on conduct. Is that regulable by the state or not?

Defendant’s Lawyer: I --

Judge: All the -- you know, alcohol, drug use --

Defendant’s Lawyer: Right, right.

Judge: -- that they have problems too, but you're saying that conduct is regulable. How about with respect to this pervasive problem of persons with mental health problems?

Defendant’s Lawyer: I -- I think in a particular situation, if the -- if the -- if the person would engage in violent conduct as --

Judge: No, no, no, don't mess with my hypothetical, counsel.
(Laughter.)

Judge: I like my hypothetical. I know you don't. It's a hard one, and that's why I'm asking it. I'm just trying to understand --

Defendant’s Lawyer: I -- I --

Judge: -- the limits of your line.

Defendant’s Lawyer: I think it would depend on how serious the offense was on the -- on the -- individual.

Judge: It's a very serious effect. The mental health problem is serious, but there are beds available.

Defendant’s Lawyer: What I was trying to say it would depend on how serious being required to go into that facility was on the person's mental -- if it would make his mental health situation a lot worse, then that may not be something that's --

Judge: So that's status -- that falls on the status side?

Defendant’s Lawyer: Well, I -- I guess you could put it that way, but I -- I guess what I'm saying --

Judge: I -- that's what I'm wondering. I don't -- I'm asking you.

Defendant’s Lawyer: Well --

Judge: I really am just trying to figure out --

Defendant’s Lawyer: You could view that as --

Judge: You're asking us to extend previous rulings. I'm asking how far?

Defendant’s Lawyer: Well, what I was going to say, you could -- you could think of it as status, but I think another way to think about it is that place realistically available to that person?

Judge: It is in the sense that the bed is available.

Defendant’s Lawyer: I know that it's --

Judge: -- but not because of their personal circumstances.

Defendant’s Lawyer: Right. Right.

Judge: Can I just return to the point about what the common ground is here? So you agree that the question of whether there is a valid delegation clause is one for the court?

Defendant's Lawyer: The question of whether there is a -- under the severability principle, yes.

Judge: You agree that courts make that decision by clear and convincing evidence?

Defendant's Lawyer: I think courts make that -- they -- they make that decision, yes.

Judge: Okay. And you agree that there is a dispute here about whether there is a delegation of the arbitrability question to the arbitrator?

Defendant's Lawyer: I'm sorry. We agree or disagree?

Judge: I'm asking, do you agree with me that the dispute between you right now is about whether there is a delegation that will send, taking these two contracts together, send the dispute to arbitration on the arbitrability question?

Defendant's Lawyer: So I think we agree that there is a -- I think both sides agree that there is a delegation clause and that, as originally formulated, it would cover this dispute. The opposing party says --

Judge: Right, but -- but we're looking at two -- we're looking at two contracts. So you -- you agree -- let's finish this up very quickly. You agree that there's a dispute about whether there's a valid delegation clause that applies here to send it to the arbitrator?

Defendant's Lawyer: Our position is that there is a valid delegation clause and it should be enforced.

Judge: But the -- no. I'm sorry. Maybe I was unclear. But the question is, do you agree that there -- the question here is about the existence, the validity, of a delegation clause?

Defendant's Lawyer: Absolutely, Your Honor. The -- the validity of the delegation clause, I think, is reserved for a court to resolve, applying the severability principle, which limits the arguments that can be --

Judge: Okay.

Defendant's Lawyer: -- offered to undermine it.

Judge: But you agree on those three questions, though?

Defendant's Lawyer: Yes.

Judge: Thank you, counsel.

Judge: Any change from when I said "could remand"? No? (Laughter.)

Defendant's Lawyer: No change, Your Honor.

Judge: Okay. Thank you, counsel. Counsel for the plaintiff?

Plaintiff's Lawyer: Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court: I'm going to abandon what I planned to talk about and try to answer the question that was raised earlier. Why are they fighting this so hard? And that only occurred to me within the last week or so.

And the answer is there's a strategic reason. They want the Court -- they don't care who decides arbitrability. All they care about is how arbitrability gets decided because that's what goes to liability at the end of the day. And so the plan is we need the Supreme Court to overrule the Ninth Circuit's "existence" language.

Why do they need that and that only? The reason is that "existence" language originated from a case that was cited in our Ninth Circuit opinion. That case was somewhat similar to this case in that there was a preexisting agreement to arbitrate but then a subsequent, more specific agreement to litigate. The Ninth Circuit was looking at that situation and thinking: Shoot, we're bound to apply the old presumption in favor of arbitrability under federal law, but we know that as a matter of contract law, there was no real, mutual intent to arbitrate this dispute under the most recent, most specific agreement.

So they made up a new federal rule. Oh, this is a formation dispute, this is an existence dispute, so that they could avoid the presumption in -- in favor of arbitrability and do the right thing under state law.

We've gone to great pains to argue that non-preempted state laws are what apply here because, at the end of the day, those are the only laws that are capable of accurately discerning the parties' true intentions, no matter what type of contractual mess gets thrown in front of judges. It's impossible to make one federal rule, two federal rules, three federal rules that are going to accurately discern the parties' contractual intentions in -- in an infinite number of business situations. Traditional state law can do that.

And we ask that the Court apply that here and welcome to answer any questions.

Judge: But why wouldn't a later agreement that modifies the earlier contract simply go to what the scope of the agreement is?

Plaintiff's Lawyer: So it goes to two scopes. It goes to the scope of the agreement to arbitrate the merits of the dispute, and our position that we've tried to brief very heavily is that it goes to the scope of the agreement to delegate this arbitrability dispute to the arbitrator.

And our reason for doing that is that under prior rulings, consent to arbitrate is dispute-specific. If that's true in the context of an arbitration agreement, it also has to be true in the context of a delegation agreement.

Judge: General, so I want to go back to the discussion about content discrimination and we're going to shut Joe up. Here, it seems to me like we are saying to ByteDance we want to shut you up.

Judge: So you don't have to stand on that argument that you were having with Justice Alito and Justice Gorsuch to still have your point about content discrimination?

Plaintiff Lawyer: That's right.

Plaintiff Lawyer: And I think, if you're focusing in on the relevant U.S. entities here, TikTok U.S. and the users themselves, this Act isn't regulating them in any way.

Plaintiff Lawyer: It's not trying to dictate the algorithm that TikTok U.S. can use.

Plaintiff Lawyer: And, in fact, Congress, I think, was doing everything it could to preserve access to TikTok in the United States, in recognition that Americans enjoy expressing themselves and building community on the site.

Judge: One last quick question --

Judge: Well, I don't know, General --

Judge: Sorry, just one last quick question.

Judge: No, no, go ahead.

Judge: Justice Gorsuch had asked your friends on the other side whether the new administration on January 20th could extend the deadline.

Judge: What's the -- your position on that?

Plaintiff Lawyer: So I think it tees up a statutory interpretation question of whether there can be an extension after the time period for divestiture has lapsed.

Plaintiff Lawyer: I would think the Court might start with its decision in the HollyFrontier case, which did recognize the ability to get an extension after a lapse like that.

Judge: So it's your position that they could?

Plaintiff Lawyer: We have not run it to ground, in part, because it's simply not presented here, and I'm not prepared to take a position on that statutory interpretation question.

Plaintiff Lawyer: At the outset, of course, Congress was hoping to prompt a divestiture, but I think the more important thing to -- to focus on now is that even if that were to happen, Congress specifically anticipated it and provided authority to lift these restrictions as soon as there's a qualified divestiture.

Plaintiff Lawyer: And the reason for that is because foreign adversaries do not willingly give up their control over this mass communications channel in the United States, and I think Congress expected we might see something like a game of chicken, ByteDance saying we can't do it; China will never let us do it.

Plaintiff Lawyer: But, when push comes to shove and these restrictions take effect, I think it will fundamentally change the landscape with respect to what ByteDance is willing to consider, and it might be just the jolt that Congress expected the company would need to actually move forward with the divestiture process.

Judge: Well, that's --

Plaintiff Lawyer: So it's not irrevocable.

Judge: That -- that's an interesting point, and I hope Mr. Francisco or Mr. Fisher, whoever's delivering the rebuttal, will address it.

Judge: So, if we were to affirm and TikTok were forced to cease operations on January 19th, you say that there could be divestiture after that point and TikTok could again begin to operate the way -- continue to operate?

Plaintiff Lawyer: That's exactly right.

Plaintiff Lawyer: There's nothing permanent or irrevocable that happens on January 19th.

Plaintiff Lawyer: And I think that Congress might have thought that we get in a situation here where a foreign adversary is doing whatever it can to just not comply.

Plaintiff Lawyer: It's hoping the United States is going to blink first through our court system or through the executive branch getting cold feet about enforcing the law.

Plaintiff Lawyer: But Congress set a deadline and I think it thought that deadline would have a forcing function.

Judge: Let me ask you a question about your -- your effort to draw a distinction between ByteDance's speech and TikTok's speech.

Judge: So suppose that the -- the People's Republic of China funds a movie and -- and there is an entity in the United States, a U.S. corporation, that thinks, wow, this is a great movie.

Judge: And while the PRC would not have a First Amendment right to show it in the United States, would you say that the American company would not have a First Amendment right to do that because whatever expression there is in that movie, it's the PRC's expression; it's not their expression?

Plaintiff Lawyer: No.

Plaintiff Lawyer: No, I wouldn't make that argument.

Plaintiff Lawyer: And I want to be really careful --

Judge: I thought that was the argument that was being made.

Judge: No?

Plaintiff Lawyer: No.

Plaintiff Lawyer: So our argument is that this is not a direct regulation of protected speech in the first place, or at most, it would warrant intermediate scrutiny because of the indirect effects that it might have on the American users or on the U.S. subsidiary.

Plaintiff Lawyer: We're not suggesting that if Congress sought to directly regulate and prohibit speech in the United States based on concerns about its content or viewpoint, that's somehow immune from First Amendment scrutiny just because it comes from a foreign source.

Plaintiff Lawyer: Obviously, that kind of law is going to trigger strict scrutiny.

Plaintiff Lawyer: And I imagine it would be a different constitutional analysis because it's hard to imagine the same profound national security harms that would exist in that scenario as compared to what we have here.

Judge: Thank you.

Judge: General, isn't the whole point of the divestiture requirement that the content on TikTok would be different if it was owned by a different company? I'm still struggling with your insistence that this is content-neutral versus content-based when we have that kind of circumstance.

Plaintiff Lawyer: The reason that I am continuing to try to hold the line on that is because there is nothing in the Act that would directly dictate any different mix of content on TikTok.

Plaintiff Lawyer: The U.S. subsidiary could use the same algorithm, show the same content by the same users in exactly the same order.

Plaintiff Lawyer: It's not about trying to interfere with the U.S. subsidiary's exercise of editorial judgment in any relevant sense.

Plaintiff Lawyer: Instead, all Congress was doing was homing in on the problems of having a foreign adversary be able to interject itself and be able to harvest the data or exercise --

Judge: But your friends on the other side say that the motivation for doing that is because the foreign adversary might influence or change the content.

Judge: So content is -- I mean, content matters, doesn't it?

Plaintiff Lawyer: I -- certainly, I think that content was relevant to Congress's concern about an adversary having control over the communications channel.

Plaintiff Lawyer: I think not, again, because of any particular concern about viewpoints or subjects --

Judge: But isn't that relevance --

Plaintiff Lawyer: -- but just that this would be a --

Judge: -- isn't that relevance enough to trigger at least some scrutiny, a heightened scrutiny, from the standpoint of our legal tests?

Judge:
We'll hear argument next in Case 23-971, Waetzig versus Halliburton Energy Services.

Lawyer of Defendant:
Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court: As ever -- every Federal Circuit to have considered the question held until the decision below, Rule 41 voluntary dismissals without prejudice may be reopened under Rule 60(b) because they are final proceedings or final judgments.

To start, a voluntary dismissal is a proceeding or a judgment.

The phrase "judgment, order, or proceeding" in Rule 60(b) was taken in 1937 from Section 473 of California's Code of Civil Procedure, and at the time, California's Supreme Court had interpreted Section 473 to cover all steps in litigation and had --it had specifically applied Section 473 to voluntary dismissals.

These authoritative California decisions were carried into Rule 60(b), and they are consistent with dictionary definitions of the terms "proceeding" and "judgment." Next, a voluntary dismissal is also final.

Some courts, after adoption of the rule, initially read it to constrain their authority to revise interlocutory matters.

And the advisory committee thought this was wrong, so it added the word "final" to confirm that Rule 60(b) comes into play only when a court lacks inherent authority to modify a matter as interlocutory.

But a dismissal terminates a case, so it cannot be modified using a court -- the court's inherent power, and it is, therefore, final for Rule 60(b) purposes.

And that conclusion, again, is confirmed by contemporaneous dictionary definitions.

Plaintiff:
Didn't the Tenth Circuit also treat this as jurisdictional?

Lawyer of Defendant:
The -- the Tenth Circuit treated the decision -- the issue of whether Rule 60(b) could be -- whether the case could be reopened as going to the jurisdiction of the court to vacate the award, and, therefore, there are two, effectively, threshold issues that could be resolved.

And, under Sinochem, of course, there's no sequencing to jurisdictional issues, which is, I think, the point that Justice Gorsuch made in the dissent that we cite in our briefs, where, if there are two jurisdictional issues and the court grant --or two issues that are threshold and go to a court's jurisdiction and the court grants review to decide one of them, it should decide that question, and the other jurisdictional issues or issues going to jurisdiction remain for remand.

Plaintiff:
So do you agree that it's jurisdictional, or is it simply an application of 60(b)?

Lawyer of Defendant:
I --I do not agree that it's jurisdictional.

I think it's a -- it's a -- it's about the application of Rule 60(b), and if relief is granted, then the case is reopened and there is jurisdiction.

But the question that is itself presented I would characterize as a threshold issue in the same way as, in Sinochem, forum non conveniens was a threshold issue which was not jurisdictional.

Judge:
Your -- your friend suggests that the reason that you're going through this process after voluntarily dismissing the case, now trying to revive it, it's an ADEA claim that you lost in arbitration, and it's a way to try to bring a collateral attack on the arbitration award.

Is that --is that what's going on?

Lawyer of Defendant:
Well, there was a motion to -- to reopen the case and to vacate the award, and the case was then sent back to arbitration.

So it was -- the motion was a challenge to the arbitration award.

Plaintiff:
But isn't it a separate motion?

Lawyer of Defendant:
It was -- it was -- it --

Plaintiff:
I mean, there's a motion to vacate, but there's also a motion to reopen, and the court would have to make two independent determinations regarding that, correct?

Lawyer of Defendant:
The district court treated it as two issues, entered two orders.

They are both attached to our petition.

The first order chronologically was to reopen the case under Rule 60(b).

And then later, having reopened the case, the court vacated the arbitration award.

Plaintiff:
And, here, we're just concerned about the propriety of the first issue?

Lawyer of Defendant:
That's right.

Judge:
Well, your friend on the other side is concerned about a little more than that.

He says --I mean, what is the reason for what you're doing if not to collaterally attack the arbitration award?

Lawyer of Defendant:
Well, I think that goes to the -- I agree the motion was filed and the reopening was done to vacate the award.

Those are -- those go to the -- to two different -- they don't go --those issues do not go to the question presented.

They go to, number one, whether the court was right to grant relief under Rule 60(b).

I note that that issue was not addressed by the court itself in the majority opinion, although the dissent would have affirmed the district court's grant of relief under Rule 60(b)(6).

And so, on remand, my friends can argue that the 60(b) ruling, the exercise of discretion by the district court should be vacated as an abuse of discretion. And then, of course, there will be the separate issue on remand, to the extent arguments have been preserved, as to whether the separate order vacating the award should be affirmed.

Judge:
Right.

But, I mean, I'm just trying to get a handle on why we're going through all this.

And the arbitration award has been confirmed in court, right?

Lawyer of Defendant:
It has not been confirmed.

Judge:
Okay.

But there is a proceeding to do that, or are you challenging that independently?

Lawyer of Defendant:
The -- what happened is there was an arbitration award.

Then there was a motion filed by my client to reopen the case and vacate the award.

And then that award was vacated.

And then the matter was appealed.

Judge:
Well, then, so your friend is wrong to suggest that the reason -- I mean, the ADEA claim was resolved in the arbitration.

Now, if it's going to be challenged, it should be challenged in that forum.

No?

Lawyer of Defendant:
Well, there's --there's a motion under -- arbitration awards would be subject to challenge under the FAA.

JUDGE: So can I ask about the dimensions of your argument in two different ways? I'll give you just a couple of hypotheticals.

So one is suppose we take this out of the employment context.

We're not talking about a benefit of any kind.

Is that perfectly okay? Or does that change matters?

LAWYER OF DEFENDANT: I think, Your Honor, if we're talking about case by case, then I think it's time to affirm the Alabama Supreme Court's decision because it was just this categorical argument.

But, second, to get to your question, you need to look to see does this look like a rule of judicial administration or not.

It may be looking a lot more like Felder and thus might be more suspect, but --

JUDGE: I --I don't understand.

What makes it more suspect?

LAWYER OF DEFENDANT: Well, it --it might be what are --like, who is it that's doing the adjudication? What are the sort of requirements that bind them?

JUDGE: Yeah, I mean, it's sort of set up the same way, but there, it's a police board.

You know, police officers are on these boards, and they make you go through three -- three different stages, and we make you do all that for police boards of different levels before you can take the claim to court.

LAWYER OF DEFENDANT: Your Honor, again, if it's particularly stringent, you might run into some problems like in the Brown case from 1949.

If it looks like it's designed to minimize liability, you might run into some Felder problems.

JUDGE: Well, if, if, if. Let's just say it's the same kind of thing, but it's in the police context.

LAWYER OF DEFENDANT: Your Honor --

JUDGE: It's the same time limits.

It's the same everything.

There's no piece of paper that says we're doing this to prevent good claims.

You know, it's -- but -- but it's in a -- it's in a different context, a non-benefits context, a quintessential 1983 context.

LAWYER OF DEFENDANT: The Court has said in Howlett and Haywood that neutral rules of judicial administration are a valid excuse for refusing to entertain a federal claim.

So, if that is a valid and -- and jurisdictional rule that's been set up, then I think that would be okay.

JUDGE: Well, why wouldn't --

JUDGE: -- that look an awful lot like Haywood itself, a -- a rule that evinces hostility to the 1983 claim? Because there's no tradition of requiring this sort of thing when a plaintiff wants to sue -- wants to bring a classic Fourth Amendment 1983 claim.

LAWYER OF DEFENDANT: Well, I think that history could factor in.

But, at the same time, in Haywood, there was no way to get into state court whatsoever.

I took it in Your Honor's hypothetical that you -- you would get to court eventually to raise your claim.

And so -- and this Court has said -- I think this Court's rulings show that merely deferring consideration of a claim is very different than defeating it.

So look at Johnson v. Fankell, where you had a 1983 defense of qualified immunity that was denied at summary judgment stage in the Idaho trial court, and they wanted to take that up immediately to the Idaho Supreme Court and were not able to do so.

1983 did not give them some right of interlocutory appeal.

And I think that's essentially what Petitioners are asking for here, is a right of interlocutory appeal.

JUDGE: Well, I -- I don't want to derail -- Your Honor, I think, had a number of hypotheticals, but --so I -- I don't want to interrupt that.

But then, eventually, I do want to ask you about the Plaintiff’s Lawyer’s narrow argument.

Did you want to --

JUDGE: It -- it doesn't matter which way we do it.

JUDGE: Okay.

I'm not sure I can capture exactly what his narrow argument is. But suppose the narrow argument is that there's no exhaustion requirement in a 1983 case in state court, where it is, as a practical matter, impossible or extraordinarily onerous to get a decision.

How would the state be hurt by that? What would be wrong with that?

LAWYER OF DEFENDANT: I think there might still be some -- some sovereignty interest if you're going to be reading the statute to require state courts to exercise jurisdiction that the state has never given them jurisdiction to exercise.

And that's why the Court has been careful in -- even in Haywood to say that these neutral and -- and truly jurisdictional rules are a valid excuse for declining jurisdiction.

That's the nature of any jurisdictional rule.

That's going to keep some cases out of court, so that can't be the test.

JUDGE: But it --it -- couldn't it be, though, that in some of those cases at least, where it's truly impossible and it really is a Catch 22, that that is evincing hostility to the plaintiffs' claims and a Felder argument might be made there?

LAWYER OF DEFENDANT: Absolutely.

Or even a Haywood argument.

JUDGE: Or even a Haywood argument.

I mean, you -- we just don't know whether that's the case here.

LAWYER OF DEFENDANT: Right.

**Judge:** Miss Jordan, you're suing your home inspector because according to you, he missed that there was mold damage on your house that you bought. Tell me what happened.

**Plaintiff (Lisa Jordan):** Yes, your honor. I moved from Massachusetts after my mother passed away, and I wanted to purchase a home in Florida. I purchased a home on November 7th, 2021.

**Judge:** Okay.

**Plaintiff:** I had contacted the listing agent and asked her if she knew of an inspector, and she gave me Mr. Casten’s phone number. So I contacted him, and he came and did an inspection on November 15, 2021. I came to see him on the day of the inspection, and I had noticed a black circle on the ceiling in the kitchen. I pointed it out to him and asked him what he thought of it, and his response was, "Well, it's probably old and nothing to worry about." On November 18, I received the report via email. He then mentioned in the report that that circle was actually a wet or moist stain.

**Judge:** Mhm.

**Plaintiff:** It was never hinted to me or suggested to me that it could be mold. It did say to get a contractor.

**Judge:** You're not from here, are you? That’s the thing. See, I’m Floridian, and we know how moist we are. So he tells you it's wet and you should get a specialist contractor to look at it, but that didn't alert you? You feel that the report didn’t properly warn you that you needed to get a mold inspection? What did you think it was when you pointed it out to him? Weren’t you worried it was mold?

**Plaintiff:** Well, like I said, when I first asked him about it, he said, "It’s probably dry and old. Don’t worry about it." But then he puts in the report that it’s moist, and he includes a picture of the moisture factor.

**Judge:** And he tells you to see another contractor about this. Do you ever pick up the phone and call him and say, "What type of contractor should I see? Do you have a recommendation on who I should hire?" Do you ever call a mold specialist now that you know the spot is wet?

**Plaintiff:** I didn’t feel I was properly alerted by his report.

**Judge:** Okay. Let me hear from you, Mr. Casten. She says you were deficient in your job. Let me hear from you.

**Defendant (Wayne Casten):** She is correct in everything she says. However, in the State of Florida, you have to have a mold license to start saying the word "mold." Unless I’ve tested it and I know that it's mold, I can't say, "Hey, you have mold." I don’t even want to alert anybody because I don’t want to create a false panic. So we need to get it tested first.

**Judge:** Right.

**Defendant:** I sent her an email and let her know that—

**Judge:** You sent her an email and told her what?

**Defendant:** Even before she paid for the report, I sent her an email. I said, "Look, you know that discussion we had when you were there? I was wrong. After you left, I tested it with two different devices."

**Judge:** So you not only put it in the report, you sent her an email?

**Defendant:** Yes, I did.

**Judge:** *[Reading from email]* "The stain by the kitchen was leaking after all. Inside the cabinet against the wall was wet. A roofer or handyman will need to seal that area of the roof/wall as needed to stop the water intrusion." What does he need to do, hit you over the head with a baseball bat, Miss Jordan? I mean, he even sends you the email telling you that you need to look into this, and you don’t look into it?

**Plaintiff:** I wanted to hear "mold." I wanted to hear, "Hire a mold inspector." I’m not an inspector or a mold specialist.

**Judge:** He’s not allowed to. He’s actually right about that. He’s not allowed to conclude it’s mold and cause a panic. But let’s see what he says. He has a picture of the spot with arrows and the title "Wet Stain Detected." He has a picture of the moisture level detected. And then—where's this wet stain that you have underneath it?

**Defendant:** That was directly below everything above, which is below the cabinets. There was another cabinet at the bottom that you had to open up to see inside.

**Judge:** This was really incumbent upon you to take the next steps. "The cause of the stain should be investigated to determine whether the condition is due to a single event or something that could occur again. Note that the home inspection cannot fully assess the extent of damage as this often involves invasive activities such as removing finishes, etc. Recommend a qualified contractor further evaluate and repair as needed." And you don’t even call him to say what kind of contractor, or anything else? You were fine with it?

**Plaintiff:** I wasn’t fine with it.

**Judge:** Well, if you weren’t fine with it, why didn’t you get another qualified contractor to further evaluate the wet spot?

**Plaintiff:** I contacted Mr. Dry.

**Judge:** Who’s Mr. Dry?

**Plaintiff:** A remediation company.

**Judge:** Right. But that was after you closed, correct?

**Plaintiff:** Right.

**Judge:** So your entire lawsuit is based on being misled, or him doing his job negligently, because if you had only been warned, you would have gotten in a specialist? You might not have bought the house? Because you would then have learned that it would cost you $7,450?

**Judge:** I’m not going to order him to pay for your mold remediation and cabinet replacement. I feel like he did exactly what his job is. In fact, he went above and beyond by emailing you to let you know that a discussion you had wasn’t valid anymore. I find in favor of the defendant in this case.

**Judge:** *[To Plaintiff]* How do you feel after hearing everything?

**Plaintiff:** I still feel I wasn’t properly alerted that it could be mold. I don’t live in Florida—I don’t know that seeing moisture automatically means mold.

**Judge:** Well, you've learned a tough lesson the hard way.

**Judge:** *[To Defendant]* Is it the law that you can’t use the word "mold" even to hint at it?

**Defendant:** From past experience, we don’t want to use the word "mold" unless we know for sure, because it can kill a deal real fast, and I don’t want to be responsible for that.

**Judge:** You did a good job. Case dismissed.

And the agency properly considered not just what was going to happen on the federal land that was being leased but what -- what private development would -- would happen.

Judge: But your -- your answer to the Chief Justice makes it clear, I think, but correct me if I'm wrong, that you would be better off not showing up.

Defendant's Lawyer: I don't think so, Your Honor, because remember, if you don't show up, all the government has to do at that point is say, oh, that's right, I didn't have a proper NTA. Here -- here, I'm printing it out, I'm mailing it to the non-citizen with the time and place. Ten days later I can remove you in absentia. So either way, all you're buying yourself is the government reprinting this piece of paper and waiting 10 days to remove you.

Judge: And --

Judge: But the removal --

Judge: I understood --

Judge: -- in absentia will be -- can't happen under your theory.

Defendant's Lawyer: Well, the removal in absentia can happen if the government prints out the NTA, again, fills in the time and date and then waits 10 days.

Judge: They start the proceedings over again --

Judge: But the removal in absentia can't happen if the person shows up, right? I mean, the reason why we're in absentia world is because they don't show up.
So I guess I'm confused about the Chief Justice's hypothetical and how it relates to this statute or this circumstance that we're talking about. I thought the -- I thought the -- the -- the sort of premise of where we were was we're in a situation in which the person isn't there and the government gets removal in absentia as a result.

Defendant's Lawyer: That's exactly right.
I was just trying to clarify that I don't know how much then is the sort of bizarre incentives point.
First of all, of course, the government can just issue a compliant NTA upfront.
If they haven't and the non-citizen doesn't show up, they can send ICE out to arrest the person. If they choose not to do that, they can print out the compliant NTA then and there, put it in the mail, and then they can get their in absentia.

Judge: Well, that --

Judge: Well, I thought the proposition was simply trying to suggest that your argument leads to an absurd result if what it says is that everything's the same, except you didn't get the notice originally and the fact that you got a later notice, you're saying your argument would still be the same.

Defendant's Lawyer: That's exactly right, Your Honor.
I think that's a function of the government flouting the statute, though, right? The statute was not actually designed for a situation in which the government systematically puts TBD instead of the -- the sort of date and time of the charges.
It was designed for the kind of one-off mistake, you put the wrong person's name on this or sent this to the wrong address. And so, you know, what Congress thought it was doing was coming up with a scheme that put all the cards in the government's hands.
It said we're not going to ask on a case-by-case basis did the non-citizen have enough notice and allow them to say I was confused, I got this document and not this document.
We're just going to give the government all the power.
All you need to do, government, is put these seven pieces of information on one piece of paper.

Judge: But Congress was concerned --

Judge: Maybe --

Judge: Well, as to what Congress thought it was doing, you filed a brief on behalf of Mr. Singh.
Are you able to address the situation of Mr. Mendez-Colin?

Defendant's Lawyer: So, yes, Your Honor.

Judge: All right.
So, in Mr. Mendez-Colin's case, his removal proceeding began in 2001, and after that, he showed up at numerous hearings, the dates were changed. And you say Congress would have wanted him at this late date to be able to reopen his removal proceedings because he didn't get a compliant NTA back in 2001?

Defendant's Lawyer: Yes, Your Honor.
Congress expressly said at any time, in contrast to numerous other provisions allowing --

Judge: Counsel, that -- I think there's a difference between the right to make a motion to reopen than to have it reopened. Are you arguing that if there was a deficient notice in this situation under (a)(1), that the Board has to reopen, or does it have the discretion to consider a forfeiture or a waiver argument?

Defendant's Lawyer: So certainly, Your Honor. There's several provisions outside the scope of the question presented in this case that can deal with any sort of gamesmanship. So, as Your Honor noted, there are equitable doctrines. The government can raise waiver, forfeiture, estoppel.
There are doctrines -- there's statutory provisions about the subsequent hearing.
So, for instance, under 1229a(b)(7), if the -- if the non-citizen had oral notice of the time and place of the hearing, they aren't eligible to apply for various forms of discretionary relief.

Judge: Well, counsel, could you answer the question that I -- I don't think you had -- really had a chance to answer the question that I asked, which is whether -- you talked about what Congress thought it was doing, and my question was, do you think Congress really thought it was doing what you are claiming should be the result in Mendez-Colin's case?

Defendant's Lawyer: I think that Congress didn't anticipate the government would ignore the text of the statute.
The reason that Congress put in the "at any time" hook is because they imagined these would be one-off circumstances where something gets lost in the mail or the government makes a typo and so switches two non-citizens' paperwork. They were not imagining sort of systematically ignoring the statute for many years.
And so, yes, at this point, the statute probably leads to results Congress didn't intend, but that's a function of the government flouting the plain text of the statute and shouldn't be -- shouldn't have any bearing on your reading of the remedial provision.

Judge: Well, as to the plain text of the statute, we've now had, like, an hour and 17 minutes of argument, and virtually nothing has been said about the plain text of 1229a(b)(5)(A).

Pereira and Niz-Chavez were literal interpretations of the statute. Now there's an answer to what I'm going to say, but I'll get to that.

Defendant's Lawyer: Okay.

Judge: But, if you read the provision I just mentioned literally, you lose, right? Any alien who after written notice required under paragraph (1) or (2) has been provided to the alien, okay, notice under (2) was provided, right?

Defendant's Lawyer: So we -- we disagree with that.
So we don't think notice under (2) was provided because (2) is a supplement to a valid (1).
And so, without a valid (1), you can't have a paragraph (2) there.
So our position is that neither notice in accordance with paragraph (1) --

Judge: I'm just talking about the literal language of the statute.
I'm not talking about what was held in Pereira or Niz-Chavez, which concerned very -- a different question.
You keep saying they held this, they held that.
What they held had to do with the stop-time rule.
Just the literal language of this, that's against you, right?

Defendant's Lawyer: So I disagree because we think there's been no paragraph (2) notice.
So you can't have notice required under paragraph (1) or (2) if you've neither gotten paragraph (1) notice -- that's the government's position in this case -- nor paragraph (2) notice, which is our contention about how you read paragraph (a)(2).

Judge: All parties, please raise your right hands.

Judge: Welcome back to the People's Court. Next case on the docket: The plaintiff went to a steakhouse, valeted her car, and when she walked out, she realized her computer was missing. But the defendant says she's such a scammer. She said 35 softball uniforms were stolen, changed to an iPod, and on and on. It's the case of "I have beef with a valet."

Judge: Thank you. Jessica Wolf, you are suing the valet company, represented here by Andres Bazan. Are you the owner, the manager?

Defendant: I'm supervising Westchester County accounts of the company.

Judge: Okay, and you have the authority to represent the company here?

Defendant: Yes, ma'am.

Judge: Okay. You're suing for $3,880 in items that you say the company stole from you when they valeted your car. Tell me what's going on.

Plaintiff: I went to Ruth's Chris Steakhouse on Saturday, November 26th. Earlier that day, I was at the library studying.

Judge: What library?

Plaintiff: The Orangeburg Library in Rockland County.

Judge: Okay.

Plaintiff: I had my laptop, a calculator, and study books and materials in a black tote bag. So I studied, got into the car, put the laptop down. Later that night—

Judge: Where did you put the laptop?

Plaintiff: On the passenger side of the car.

Judge: Where did you put the rest of the stuff you described?

Plaintiff: It was in the bag. Everything was in the bag, and I put it on the passenger seat.

Plaintiff: Later that night, we decided to go to Ruth's Chris Steakhouse for dinner. We had reservations at around 8:00 or 8:30.

Judge: Who is "we"?

Plaintiff: Me and my family.

Judge: Okay.

Plaintiff: I picked up my brother. When he got into the car, the bag was on the passenger seat. He moved the bag to the back seat.

Judge: Okay. What about the computer?

Plaintiff: Everything was in the bag. It was a black tote bag; it had everything in it. He moved it to the back seat.

Judge: Okay.

Plaintiff: We got to the restaurant. I was a little hesitant. I saw the valet. I really didn't want to valet my car—not because I thought something would be stolen, but because I was worried about scratches and things like that.

Judge: What kind of car do you drive?

Plaintiff: A BMW 3 Series.

Judge: What year?

Plaintiff: 2011.

Judge: Okay. Continue.

Plaintiff: We pulled up, and I didn’t realize I had the laptop in the back of the car. My brother had just moved it when he got in.

Judge: But your memory didn't last from then?

Plaintiff: I know, but I wasn't thinking about it.

Judge: Okay, so you come up, and what do you do?

Plaintiff: We get to the valet, I hand him the keys, and we go into the restaurant. He hands me the ticket, and we go inside. Everything was fine. When we came back out, I handed the valet my ticket. He ran to get the car. My brother noticed that the car was parked way in the back, in a secluded area near the woods.

Judge: How did he notice that?

Plaintiff: There were no other cars around the vicinity.

Judge: Maybe you stayed late and most cars were gone?

Plaintiff: No, the restaurant was still busy, but our car was parked alone in the last row of the parking lot.

Judge: Did that strike you as strange?

Plaintiff: Not at the moment. But after we discovered what was missing, it made sense.

Judge: So you get the car. Do you look for your stuff?

Plaintiff: No, because it was late, the car interior is black, and the bag is black. It was dark. I didn’t notice anything until the next morning.

Judge: So you took the car home?

Plaintiff: Yes. I parked in the driveway. The car automatically locks when you walk away.

Judge: And the next morning?

Plaintiff: I went to the car before church and noticed the bag was missing. I called the restaurant, and they said they’d check with the valet company. Later, my mother suggested I check the whole car. When I opened the trunk, it was wiped clean. I had 35 softball t-shirts in there—they were gone.

Judge: Why did you have 35 softball t-shirts?

Plaintiff: I’m a coach. We had just ordered them.

Judge: Defendant, what happened when the plaintiff contacted you?

Defendant: We got a call from her father, not her. He called at noon the next day. We checked with our two valets—they denied touching anything.

Judge: Do you ever conduct polygraph tests in cases like this?

Defendant: Sometimes, but not in this case. These employees have been with us for years with no complaints.

Judge: Do you have a policy regarding valuables in cars?

Defendant: Yes, the valet ticket clearly states we are not responsible for lost items unless checked with management.

Judge: Plaintiff, you left $4,000 worth of items in your car and didn’t check for them until the next day. That makes this case difficult to prove. My verdict is for the defendant.

Judge: All parties, please. Let it get—have you been sworn in?

Bailiff: Yes, Your Honor.

Judge: Okay, Jane. You're suing your ex, Stephen Jacobs, for $3,158.36, half the cost of your son's wedding expenses, which according to you, he agreed to pay. You are counterclaiming against her, I guess, for half the cost of college?

Defendant: No, not half the cost of college—she made no contribution to things like tuition.

Judge: And so how do you pick $7,000 as the amount you're counterclaiming for?

Defendant: Massachusetts law limits the amount I can sue for in this court.

Judge: I see. How much was college in total?

Defendant: I paid about $207,000 for both boys.

Judge: Okay, so you're suing for the most you can sue for. It's a tiny fraction of what you actually paid for college because, according to you, she's supposed to pay for half of that. All right. Let's talk to you first. Tell me about why you're here today.

Plaintiff: We have two sons. The oldest, who's now 29, was married last June. Steve and I had been in conversation about paying for the rehearsal dinner, some flowers to go along with the rehearsal dinner that were going to be repurposed and used at the wedding the following day, and then the day after, there was a brunch held at the bride's family home.

Judge: So that was the agreement you folks reached with the mother and father of the bride?

Plaintiff: Yes, exactly.

Judge: And then what happened?

Plaintiff: I kept Steve in the loop.

Judge: What was he telling you when you told him that?

Plaintiff: "Fine, no problem. You and I will split it. No problem at all."

Judge: So what happens on the day of the rehearsal dinner?

Plaintiff: He had dinner. His mother and father were in from Florida. When I was seeing them down to their car—his dad had fallen the week before and was really bruised up—I helped them down. By the time I got back up to the second floor, he was gone for the evening.

Judge: So he wasn't there when the check came?

Plaintiff: No.

Judge: And then what happened?

Plaintiff: Well, I paid the bill. We had the wedding on Saturday. It was a lovely wedding. It was fabulous. Then the brunch happened the following day.

Judge: Did you say something to him at the wedding? Like, "You weren’t there to help pay" or anything?

Plaintiff: No, I didn't want to start anything then. It wouldn't have been appropriate.

Judge: Did he go to the brunch?

Plaintiff: He did.

Judge: Did you discuss money with him then or did you wait until later?

Plaintiff: I didn't discuss it at the brunch. He said to me that he would be getting back to me about the money. I didn’t have all the receipts yet.

Judge: So when did you give him the receipts, and how?

Plaintiff: I gave him the receipts the following Saturday on July 2nd. I hand-delivered them to his house.

Judge: And what did he do?

Plaintiff: Nothing.

Judge: What did he say? "I’m not paying this?"

Plaintiff: No. For several months, whenever I would either bump into him or speak to him on the phone, he would say, "Yeah, I’m going to get you the money. I’m going to pay you." At one point, I was with my other son, and we saw Steve, and I said to him, "Do you think that you could get back to me on that?" because it was all on my credit card, and it was troublesome. He said he would pay me.

Judge: And he didn’t?

Plaintiff: No.

Judge: When did you learn that he had no intention of paying you?

Plaintiff: In late October.

Judge: What did he say?

Plaintiff: I saw him right before I moved, and he said, "I don’t owe you a cent." Then he turned and walked away. That was the last time we spoke.

Judge: Had you two been fighting about something else before that?

Plaintiff: No, ma’am.

Judge: Why do you think the change happened?

Plaintiff: I don’t know.

Judge: Let's hear from you, Stephen. Did you agree to pay for half of the wedding cost?

Defendant: No. Never.

Judge: Why is she under the impression that you did?

Defendant: Because she doesn't ask—she tells. She kept on telling me many times that this is what we're doing. The first time she brought it up, I told her I had to go to the bathroom—just to avoid the conversation.

Judge: You sure you weren’t just saying "yes" to avoid confrontation and figured you’d sort it out later?

Defendant: No, I was avoiding it.

Judge: And after the wedding?

Defendant: I avoided it until I told her outright, "Forget it. I’m not paying."

Judge: Do you believe you shouldn't have to pay?

Defendant: Yes. She never contributed to any of the college expenses, and our divorce decree states we’d "attempt to agree" on contributions. She contributed zero.

Judge: I’ve read the decree. That phrase doesn't impose financial responsibility. But when you tell someone you'll pay half, and they rely on it financially, that's a contract. I’m ruling in favor of the plaintiff for $3,158.36. The counterclaim is dismissed.

Judge: Mr. Kneedler, I just want to clarify something. So your position in response to, say, the rogue state hypothetical, when you said an injunction is the solution, it's not an injunction to pay money because you said the United States thinks that can't happen. So is it your position that if, say, a state or the United States takes property, refuses to give just compensation for it, that the property owner could get an injunction essentially saying, give me my property back if you're not going to pay, and perhaps get that injunction but not get reimbursed for the temporary taking that happened in between the seizure and the award of the injunction?

Defendant's Lawyer: That is correct. And the same thing would be true, there could be a temporary deprivation of due process, and if you get an injunction preventing the government from doing whatever it did without due process, there is an interim period, but a person can go to court, get a TRO, get a preliminary injunction to prevent that from going on a long time. That's just the nature of litigation and an injunction, but it doesn't lead to the question of damages. And this Court's cases, First English and others, had to do with the calculation of whether interest should be paid, and that's what the Court meant about the Fifth Amendment being a basis for the award of compensation, not that there was a cause of action.

Judge: Justice Jackson?

Judge: And just to clarify from what was just said, the government's position would be that you might be able to have a cause of action, say, under state law or whatnot for that temporary taking. It's not that you would be out the compensation entirely, right?

Defendant's Lawyer: Right. That would depend on state law and the availability of a state cause of action on that. But I'm only talking about the federal causes of action, where there's no basis for an award of money out of the Treasury and overcoming sovereign immunity in federal court for compensation even for that interim period. But the interim period is endemic to litigation, due process violation being held on an indictment, but that is the proper remedy and that's the remedy that existed until the Tucker Act was passed. It was the remedy that this Court said in Knick was the way to vindicate Fifth Amendment rights until the Tucker Act or state constitutions came along and provided a monetary remedy.

Judge: Thank you. Rebuttal, Mr. McNamara.

Plaintiff's Lawyer: Thank you, Your Honor. To begin with Justice Gorsuch's question, I think it's important to remember the procedural posture here. I understood my friend from Texas to say that the City of Baytown decision means that Texas courts hear claims under the federal Constitution. The complaint pleads a claim under the federal Constitution, and to the extent Texas's only complaint with that was that it failed to cite directly to a Texas Supreme Court decision, it's not clear why Texas moved to dismiss it, sought an interlocutory appeal of that decision as a dispositive issue, and then extinguished it on the merits in the Fifth Circuit. To the extent that claim exists, that claim has been extinguished and that warrants reversal.

Plaintiff's Lawyer: To the original meaning, and I think, Your Honor, the rogue state example is not a hypothetical. It's a real example because state after state has looked to federal law and to First English as the thing that prevents the state from denying compensation. That's true in Oregon, as I mentioned, but also New Mexico, South Carolina, Nebraska—the list goes on of states that provide compensation under the Fifth Amendment because they understand the Fifth Amendment to require compensation. And they're correct to understand that, Your Honor.

Plaintiff's Lawyer: The original understanding, as evidenced by writings from James Madison to St. George Tucker, is that the Fifth Amendment creates an obligation to pay, which is why you can sue under the Tucker Act because the Fifth Amendment creates an obligation to pay. Only in the absence of a court of competent jurisdiction to enforce that obligation do the federal courts resort to cases like Meigs v. McClung's Lessee, where the Court ejected the United States military from its own base because it didn't have clean title. That is the last resort in the absence of a court that has the jurisdiction to enforce that obligation.

Plaintiff's Lawyer: That's why, in Maine Community Health, this Court specifically pointed to the Takings Clause as the analogy for what sort of money-mandating inquiry it means to create the obligation to pay. But, more broadly, Your Honor, I think Texas's understanding of the Fifth Amendment would relegate property rights to the status of the poor relation of the Bill of Rights. It would be the only acknowledged ongoing obligation in the Constitution that is entitled to no enforcement, that is left entirely to the discretion of the government entities that are supposedly obligated to pay. But, surely, as evidenced by the writings and by the adoption of the Fifth Amendment itself, the Framers meant for property rights to mean more than that.

Plaintiff's Lawyer: If the Court has no further questions, we'll rest on our briefs.

Judge: Thank you, counsel. The case is submitted.

Defendant: "Do I remember an e-mail to that effect? No, I don't remember that."

Plaintiff’s Lawyer: "But there were emails to that effect."

Defendant: "Not if you say there were. Mr. Waters, I don't dispute that. I don't dispute any of this—that I took money that didn't belong to me, that I misled people. That's simple."

Plaintiff’s Lawyer: "You don't want the answer to be simple. We agreed on that."

Defendant: "I don't necessarily want the answer to be simple. I just want everybody to understand. I do not dispute that I stole money that was not my money, that I misled people to do that, that I misled people that trusted me to do that. And that what I did was terrible. I don't dispute that. It's just the way you're asking these questions. And you know, I mean, there's some things in there that I do take issue with."

Plaintiff’s Lawyer: "OK. Which part of what I just asked you about the pliers do you take issue with? You take issue that y'all didn't conspire to do that, you and Russell?"

Defendant: "Yes."

Judge: Whole parties, please use your right hand. Welcome back to the People's Court. Next case on the docket: the plaintiff brought a car to the defendant for a recall problem, and when it came back, it was making ridiculous noise. But the defendant says he never even touched the engine, so the fact is, the car is a junker. It's the case of Hoop D No Can Do. Litigants have been sworn.

Judge: Sounds like Shamika Hewitt, you are suing Robert D. Gregorris’s dealership. All right, we've agreed not to mention that you are a franchisee of a major manufacturer of cars. You don't want their name mentioned, and we'll respect that. You’re suing his dealership for five thousand dollars for aggravation and money for a new car because, according to you, they broke your car. Tell me what happened.

Plaintiff: I purchased a vehicle February 1st from a private party.

Judge: From a person?

Plaintiff: Yeah, it was a dealer in Delaware.

Judge: A dealer. Okay. And then you drove back to New York?

Plaintiff: Right. Okay. Yeah. The steering wheel was shaking, so I took it to a mechanic.

Judge: Had you test-driven it beforehand?

Plaintiff: Yes.

Judge: It was not shaking when you test-drove it, right?

Plaintiff: No. I had it for a couple of days. The man did tell me I had to change the brakes.

Judge: Who told you that?

Plaintiff: The person who sold it.

Judge: The person, yes. But the steering wheel hadn't been shaking when you bought the car? That was something that happened on the drive down?

Plaintiff: Right. Okay. Yeah. So then I took it to the mechanic when I got to New York.

Judge: And?

Plaintiff: He said I needed control arms and the wheel bearings. So I said okay. When he put it on a lift, he said the frame on the inside of the car was cracking. He looked something up and came back and said, "Did you know that these trucks had a recall on it?" I said no, I had only had it a couple of days. He said, "Yeah, it was about two years ago. They had a recall on it." Before he did the work, he said to have them fix the frame first.

Judge: So you called the dealer. You took it to them, and they fixed it. The recall was for what?

Plaintiff: The recall was for some rusting on the strut towers, where the shocks go into.

Judge: Okay, that's a recall. So he takes care of the repair. You take the car, and then what happens? Do you take it back to the first guy so that you can actually get the repair your car needed?

Plaintiff: No. When I picked it up, the car was barely moving.

Judge: What does barely moving mean?

Plaintiff: It was in four-wheel drive. The oil light was on. There was a loud knocking noise when the man brought it out to me.

Judge: And?

Plaintiff: I said, "What happened to the truck? It wasn’t like this when I brought it to you six days ago." He said, "Oh, I don’t know, I didn’t touch it." So I called inside. He went inside to tell them I could barely move the truck. They said they would send somebody outside. A man came outside, drove the car back and forth to get it out of four-wheel drive.

Judge: Why was it in four-wheel drive?

Plaintiff: I asked him that. He said, "I don’t know, I didn’t touch the car." So I said, "Okay, could somebody find the guy who did touch the car?"

Judge: Defendant, what happened that day?

Defendant: The vehicle was in four-wheel drive because there was a snowstorm that day. The body shop had to put the vehicle in four-wheel drive to move it.

Judge: So they performed the repair, and then she picked it up?

Defendant: Yes. She didn’t know how to return it to two-wheel drive.

Judge: Did somebody take it out of four-wheel drive for you?

Plaintiff: Yes.

Judge: And then what was wrong with it?

Plaintiff: The oil light was on. The man who came out said I didn’t need oil, but the light kept flashing.

Judge: Defendant, was the oil light on?

Defendant: No, it was not.

Judge: You take your car to a repair shop. They fix one thing, but it comes out with another problem. Are they responsible?

(Audience members provide opinions.)

Judge: Plaintiff, did you take it to another mechanic afterward?

Plaintiff: Yes.

Judge: What did they say?

Plaintiff: They gave me this document.

Judge: This looks like a typed note, not an official repair invoice.

Defendant: She purchased this vehicle from an auction. She asked me to help return it. I found out it was sold "as is." The car had a salvage title from fifteen years ago.

Judge: Plaintiff, you bought a twenty-year-old car that turned out to be garbage. You have no evidence they ruined the engine. Verdict for the defendant.

Bailiff: All parties, please rise.

Judge Milian: Welcome back to The People's Court. The plaintiff hired the defendant to fix his car but wanted to watch so he wouldn’t get ripped off. The defendant says he doesn’t give refunds but was ready to do the job. It’s the case of Your Real Dipstick.

Judge Milian: Michael Evans Sr., you are suing Demetrius Callaway for $1,000. That includes $200 you paid him and $800 for inconveniencing you and threatening you. What’s going on?

Plaintiff (Michael Evans Sr.): I contacted him in October about fixing my car.

Judge Milian: Okay.

Plaintiff: But I didn’t get the money to pay him until December.

Judge Milian: So you finally got the money in December. What needed to be fixed?

Plaintiff: I needed camshaft sensors, my power steering pump replaced, and my dipstick holder for the transmission.

Judge Milian: Alright.

Plaintiff: So he told me how much he would charge me—$200. He came over, looked at the car, and I gave him the money.

Judge Milian: Do you deny receiving the $200?

Defendant (Demetrius Callaway): Not at all.

Judge Milian: Okay, go ahead.

Plaintiff: When we texted, he claimed I had no proof I gave him the money.

Judge Milian: (Reviewing) I’ll see that in a moment, but continue.

Plaintiff: So we set up a date—January 3rd—for the repairs. On that day, I texted him to confirm, and he said he was still pricing the parts. When I called him back, I told him I found the parts cheaper than what he was quoting.

Judge Milian: By how much?

Plaintiff: He found a fuel pump for $195. I found it for $127. He also quoted the camshaft sensors for $88, and I found them even cheaper.

Defendant: He’s just trying to save himself some money.

Judge Milian: He has every right to do that.

Plaintiff: So I told him I would be buying the parts myself. Then he tells me the parts won’t be in until 2:00 PM. He said that was too late and that he wouldn’t be able to do the work until Saturday.

Judge Milian: (Confirming) This was on January 3rd, a Thursday?

Plaintiff: Yes.

Judge Milian: That doesn’t sound unreasonable. So what happened next?

Plaintiff: I had a job interview on Friday, and because my car wasn’t fixed, I couldn’t get there.

Judge Milian: Did you try public transportation?

Plaintiff: Cleveland’s public transportation is terrible.

Judge Milian: People use it.

Plaintiff: Yeah, but I’ve had bad experiences with the bus drivers.

Judge Milian: But you could have made it to the interview.

Plaintiff: Well, on Saturday, I told him that wouldn’t work and asked for my money back.

Judge Milian: And what did he say?

Plaintiff: He said he didn’t have the money.

Judge Milian: Demetrius, what’s your version?

Defendant: I never refused to do the work. His schedule just didn’t match mine. He asked for a refund, but I told him I didn’t have the money at that time.

Judge Milian: So why did you spend the money? That was a deposit for future labor.

Defendant: It was right after Christmas. I spent the money on my kids.

Judge Milian: That’s not the plaintiff’s problem.

Defendant: I told him I’d do the work, but he wanted the money back immediately.

Plaintiff: He kept changing his story. First, he said he had no work. Then he said he had too much work.

Judge Milian: Let me see the text messages.

(Both parties hand over their texts.)

Judge Milian: (Reading) "That’s kind of late. I have other cars to work on." Plaintiff responds, "So when will you be able to do it?" Defendant replies, "Not till Saturday."

Judge Milian: Was the work ever done?

Plaintiff: No, I had to borrow money and take my van elsewhere.

Judge Milian: But that was after January 5th. Why not just let him do it on Saturday?

Plaintiff: Because I paid for a specific service on a specific date.

Judge Milian: (Reading more texts) "I definitely would want to watch the work get done." And you respond, Demetrius, "I don’t do supervision."

Judge Milian: You both are acting like a couple of peacocks, strutting around trying to prove who’s in charge. This is so unnecessary.
