That language is identical to Article III, but yet the Court interpreted it to mean something different. And I think that in the Gibbs regime pre -1367, the Court was exercising a pretty free hand in -- in articulating the contours of pendent jurisdiction and ancillary jurisdiction before Congress controlled it. Can you think -- I mean, I think a lot of this case seems to kind of come down to is that just the way we've been treating jurisdictional statutes and do we keep it up with 1367, or in 1367 because, in Finley, the Court kind of said no, look, Congress, there need to be clear jurisdictional rules, expressly invited Congress to address it, which Congress did. Would you say, do you think it's fair to say, or can you think of a counter-example that in 1367, when it comes to supplemental jurisdiction, the Court has tightened its belt and isn't being as free-wheeling, or can you think of other examples where the Court, this Court, has done kind of what the court of appeals seemed to have continued to do in 1367, which is maybe make a little bit more jurisdictional policy than was set out in the text?

Lawyer of Defendant: Yeah, an important question, Judge. I think I would describe the history a little differently. I wouldn't describe it as free-wheeling. I would say it all points in one direction. The Court construed jurisdictional statutes more narrowly than Article III. So that's certainly true with Strawbridge versus Curtiss. We know that there's not a complete diversity requirement because of CAFA. It's the same thing with 1331. Justice Thomas noted this in Grable. From the very beginning of the Jurisdiction and Removal Act of 1875, this Court almost immediately construed the words "arising under" to be not coextensive with Article III.

Defendant: You are hearing this right. I have been dealing with this all my life with this woman. I take the kid out just so I don't have to listen to it.

Judge: You know what, sir? That is very uncalled for.

Defendant: That's all she does is complain.

Judge: Well, she has a legitimate complaint, don't you think? You have not been giving her anything financially for the support of your child.

Judge: "Okay, how old is this child?"

Plaintiff: "He is six months now, six months."

Judge: "Oh, it's a newborn."

Plaintiff: "Yeah, yes."

Judge: "You left your newborn baby with your parents every day?"

Defendant: "Well, I mean, people make mistakes, and so he's a mistake."

Judge: "Wow. You knew what you were going to do."

Defendant: "If I could take it back, I would. Honestly. But—"

Judge: All parties, please raise your right hands. Welcome back to the People’s Court. Next case on the docket: the plaintiff was hired as a mystery shopper by the defendant and says he got stiffed, but the defendant says he scammed, he didn’t shop where he said. It’s the case of "shop till you sue."

Judge: Thank you, Douglas. Okay, Ronald Kerer, you are suing the company that you were working for, represented here by Julia Gwyn. How do I pronounce the last name?

Defendant: Gwyn.

Judge: Gwyn. You've asked us not to mention the name of the company or its clients, and we'll respect that. You are suing them for $1,293 that you say they owe you in reimbursements for the job that you performed. Now tell me, I’m fascinated by this case. Tell me the job that you perform.

Plaintiff: Okay, Your Honor, this is a situation in which I was employed as a mystery shopper.

Judge: Mystery shopper?

Plaintiff: That’s correct.

Judge: And your job description is?

Plaintiff: To visit the business and perform multiple duties. In the case of a supermarket, you have to evaluate the deli counter, the cleanliness of the supermarket, and the produce aisle. In the case of a fast-food restaurant, you have to time the amount of time it takes from when you get to the order line to when you place your order, to when you receive your order, to when you pay your order in nanoseconds. You have to use a digital timer. In a clothing boutique, you have to go in there acting nonchalant like you’re looking to buy a gift and hope they present non-sale merchandise and make recommendations, and then you make a purchase.

Judge: Okay. Who hires you? You work for them, and you get hired by the chains?

Plaintiff: Correct.

Judge: Right. Because they’re trying to see how the franchises are operating or how their own employees are operating. It’s like "Undercover Boss"?

Plaintiff: Exactly right.

Judge: How long have you been doing this?

Plaintiff: I did it for the company for about three or four months.

Judge: You’ve never been a mystery shopper before this?

Plaintiff: That’s correct.

Judge: All right. So what happened here?

Plaintiff: In this particular situation, the criteria from the mystery shopping company is overwhelming, and they are critical when it comes to reporting. In the event that there is a miscalculation or a mistake in the report, or if the procedure isn’t followed, then the mystery shopping company denies payment. So now you’re out your time, you’re out the visit where you should have been paid, you’re out the money you paid for the visit, and you’re also out your expenses, your tolls, and your gas.

Judge: Now is that what happened in this case?

Plaintiff: That’s what happened.

Judge: Okay. Do you have a list of the work that you did that, according to you, was not reimbursed?

Plaintiff: Okay. May I see it?

Judge: All right. What happened here?

Defendant: So we have 600,000 mystery shoppers across the United States. We consider them independent contractors. As part of the application process, they do sign an independent contractor agreement, which I have here. Paragraph five states that they are not entitled to any reimbursements except for those that are set forth in the job posting. What we have is a website that has a bunch of available jobs that the mystery shoppers will take a look at. They’ll see what’s in their area, they’ll see what amount they’re going to get paid for the actual shop, and if there are any reimbursements. Sometimes the fast-food restaurants or even some of the restaurants will just give up to $20 for you to go do a mystery shop at their location, plus you may also get a fee for doing that as well.

Judge: Meaning you can have lunch and get a fee?

Defendant: Right. You might get—the fees are pretty minimal, like $10, $7, depending on the job. But if, like, you were going to go to the burger joint anyway and get a burger, and you were going to go shopping at this big retail chain and buy stuff, you also get the stuff you buy, right?

Judge: Exactly. This is fascinating. It’s a great business model. But hold on one moment. Okay, so I have a list of the jobs that you say you were not paid for. According to this list, there are 24 different jobs, and you’re suing for the visit, the item that was purchased there, and gas and tolls. What is your defense to what he’s saying?

Defendant: We were only able to match three visits out of the 24 to our records, and all three were canceled by Mr. Kerer. In some cases, he claimed he performed work for a client we don’t even have. There are also issues where he submitted visits that seem impossible, such as being in four different locations just a minute apart.

Judge: So you’re saying he falsified records?

Defendant: Yes, and one of our clients is considering prosecution for attempting to return an item he never purchased from their store.

Judge: Mr. Kerer, did anyone tell you that you were being removed from stores due to your behavior?

Plaintiff: No, Your Honor. This is the first I’m hearing of it.

Judge: Your claims are inconsistent and don’t add up. You have a bank listed that isn’t even one of their clients, exaggerated expenses, and job IDs that don’t match. Verdict for the defendant.

Judge: Case dismissed.

Judge: Is there a reason why you don't want to give her any money in child support to pay for your daughter?
Defendant: Well, it's not that I don't want to pay. It's just, I'm not working at all. I'm a full-time student, okay? My tuition is paid for, and I've got a Pell Grant. I get basically $3,200 for a school year to help cover my living expenses as well. I have to maintain grades and stay in school. I have to prove that to be able to continue to receive the grant. So, just trying to further my education. I understand my daughter is important to me. I love her very much, but I'm going back to law. I'm trying to get into law school.
Plaintiff: But I have to bear all the costs alone, and I can't do that. I am only a daycare worker.

Judge: We'll hear argument next in Case 22-529, Cantero versus Bank of America.

Plaintiff's Lawyer: Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court: Section 25b preempts a state consumer financial law only if, as relevant here, it prevents or significantly interferes with the exercise of a national bank's powers. Bank of America argues and the Second Circuit held that this statute preempts any law that controls or otherwise hinders the exercise of a national bank's powers, regardless of whether the law has any significant effect on such powers.

This test conflicts with the statute for four reasons.

First, Section 25b's definition of "state consumer financial law" is incompatible with the control test because it would require that every such law be preempted, nullifying the statute and erecting the very field preemption regime that the statute forbids.

Bank of America's only retort is to concede that state fair lending laws aren't categorically preempted, a concession it doesn't explain and that disproves its own test.

Second, the control test ignores Section 25b's express codification of Barnett Bank's "prevents or significantly interferes with" standard and, in particular, the word "significantly," which Bank of America reads out of the statute.

Third, a control test can't be squared with Section 25b's provisions for OCC preemption determinations, which must assess the impact of a state law and be based on substantial evidence. These requirements would make no sense if a control test were the law.

Finally, and perhaps most fundamentally, adopting a control test would require reading virtually all of Section 25b to have no real-world effect.

With no plausible textual argument, Bank of America turns to policy, claiming that its test is needed to avoid mayhem. But Congress disagreed, and Section 25b has a solution to this concern. The OCC can make the preemption determinations contemplated by the statute. That it has thus far failed to respect the statute's commands grants no license to this Court to do the same.

I welcome the Court's questions.

Judge: I'd be interested in you giving us your explanation as to how Barnett Bank gives us guidance as to how to interpret "prevents or significantly interferes."

Plaintiff's Lawyer: Sure, Justice. So Barnett Bank uses the "prevents or significantly interferes with" standard as a kind of distillation of the rule that emerges from this Court's cases.

Now, of course, the conflict that was at issue in Barnett Bank was a stark conflict. It involved a state statute that said banks may not do X and a federal statute that said national banks may do X, and this Court was able to resolve that as a clear conflict.

But it didn't give much guidance itself in terms of what "significant interferes with" means, but it did articulate that as the standard that emerges from this Court's cases. And the first case that it cited was this Court's decision in Anderson.

And Anderson involved a Kentucky escheat law, and the Court in that case—there was a preemption challenge that was brought to that statute by the national bank, and the Court in that case said that's not a discriminatory statute.

It was the first question the Court asked. It doesn't conflict with any statutory text, and so we examine the law's practical effect.

And in examining the law's practical effect, it distinguished a prior decision from this Court that reached the opposite outcome. And the only way to explain that pair of cases is that the Court examined the practical effect.

And so I think the one thing that we know of the "prevents or significantly interferes with" standard and what it means is that it requires an examination at a minimum of the practical effect of the statute.

And that's clear from the ordinary meaning of the phrase, and it's confirmed by the surrounding text in Section 25b, including the provision that requires that the OCC examine the law's impact based on substantial evidence—

Judge: And what does it—

Plaintiff's Lawyer: —and periodically review.

Judge: —and what exactly does it mean to examine the practical effect? I mean, why don't you talk about this law and say how an analysis of that kind would work with respect to this law and then maybe say anything more general you want, because it seems to provide no guidance at all to courts as to what they have to do.

Plaintiff's Lawyer: Yeah, I will answer that question directly, but I will say that because of the way that Bank of America has argued the case and the way that the Second Circuit decided the case, the only question that this Court has to confront is whether the control test is codified as part of Section 25b or whether, instead, courts must look to the practical effect of the law.

Judge: I appreciate that, but one thing that we should think about at least in considering whether the practical effect test that you're suggesting is the one that's codified and is the appropriate one—

Plaintiff's Lawyer: Mm-hmm. Sure.

Judge: —is what would that—what would that mean? What would it look like? And then we can, you know, consider whether that's what Congress had in mind.

Plaintiff's Lawyer: Yeah. So it might look like the showing that the national bank made in Franklin National Bank, for example, and I would recommend that you look at the trial court decision in that case.

So that case involved a federal statute that granted to national banks the authority to accept savings deposits.

And New York had a statute that didn't prohibit national banks from accepting savings deposits but disabled them from using the word "savings" in their business operation and in their advertisements or any equivalent thereof and reserved to certain state institutions the privilege to use that word.

And what the national bank said in that case, it identified real-world evidence showing the tremendous extent to which that law served as an obstacle to it attempting to accept savings deposits in its business operations, and the trial court in that case found what is effectively significant interference.

And by the time that case got to this Court, this Court, although it resolved its question before it based on statutory construction grounds, emphasizing the statute, the federal statute's use of the word "savings," I think it had confidence based on the record before it that that word mattered in the real world.

Judge: And if that standard had been used here, what would that have meant? What evidence would the parties have put on, and how would the court have addressed the issue?

Plaintiff's Lawyer: So the legal question would be whether there's significant interference. And we think that looks to the practical effect, and Bank of America would have to identify what the practical effect is.

I think it would be particularly easy for it to do so here because we have a statute that's been on the books for 50 years.

State banks have been complying with it. Most federal banks, it's my understanding, have been complying with it.

And, indeed, there was a preemption challenge that was immediately brought, and it failed, and, presumably, national banks were complying with it after that.

And so they could look at the data showing the extent to which this minimum interest requirement has caused banks to not offer mortgage escrow services to—which would be the relevant power—to consumers.

Judge: Well—

Plaintiff's Lawyer: And to—

Judge: Keep going. Sorry.

Plaintiff's Lawyer: And I think it would just be a question of degree at that point.

And I would concede that it's not a bright-line test. Congress didn't want a bright-line test. It had before it various proposals that would have been a bright-line test, including field preemption.

That's administrable, but we know that Congress didn't want that.

And on the other hand, the Department of the Treasury submitted a proposal that would have made preemption determinations turn entirely on whether the law is discriminatory.

That's also administrable, but in the judgment of Congress, that didn't go far enough to provide protection to the banks, and Congress wanted to give banks, as an accommodation, the opportunity on a case-by-case basis to show that there's significant interference.

Judge: Solomon Waserman, you are suing Boro Nof for $1,630 in rent and a sofa you say he took from you, new locks, and leaving you to pack his belongings. You are counter-claiming against him all of the cable, phone, internet bills that you paid during the short time that you folks lived together. Tell me what happened here. How did you two know each other?

Plaintiff: We were members of the same synagogue, Young Israel of Long Beach.

Plaintiff: What happened here? Mr. Boro Nof became a member and he was coming over there, staying in front of me, next bench to me, that's how we got to meet each other. Later on, unfortunately, he had some problem with his wife and they went into the separation and further into divorce proceedings. And then Boro asked me, or I offered, out of a good heart, that he can stay with me because I have indeed a relatively substantial house. I have rooms which were not used, so I offered him.

Judge: Who did you live with in that house when you had him at that time?

Plaintiff: I was alone.

Judge: Right now?

Plaintiff: Now I'm already married.

Judge: Okay, so when does he come into your home?

Plaintiff: I don't remember exactly the months. Probably it's somewhere in April.

Defendant: I remember when I moved in, around March.

Judge: And you moved in because you separated from your wife?

Defendant: Yes.

Judge: So, you moved in in March. Did you agree to pay rent or no?

Defendant: No.

Plaintiff: He said please do not pay me, it's all free, you could use storage. Even before I asked him to stay there myself. But he also, again, reminded me it's out of the goodness of my heart and I would appreciate your company.

Judge: If you're renting a room, how is it the goodness of your heart? You're either renting it or you're not. I mean, what does goodness of heart mean?

Plaintiff: You know, he owes you all this back rent. Indeed, in the beginning, it was a goodness of heart to help him store his stuff. Later on, when he started to live with me in the house, at one point I thought that $250 rent for boarding Boro to stay in my place would be a very reasonable place. That's fine.

Judge: But did he agree to that? Because he was already living there and you weren't charging him rent.

Plaintiff: Yes, he agreed.

Judge: Okay, so now it's not that he was supposed to pay rent from the beginning. It's that in the middle you said to him, I want you to pay $250 a month rent. Did he ever pay $250 a month?

Plaintiff: He suggested because his money were tied up in a lawsuit with his wife that it would be easier for him to use the credit card and pay some of my bills.

Judge: Okay, and then he went, and then he proceeded in May, June, July, August, September, and October. He paid the Verizon bill. Do you have proof of what you paid?

Plaintiff: These are the credit card receipts I have, the credit card receipts matching the Verizon bills.

Judge: How much do you think he paid in the cable bill and all that?

Plaintiff: Some of them were plus dollars. Then I bargained them to come to the cheaper plan, so it started at $88 but I don't remember the charges of $600. It's 21644, I see, I see the proof of that.

Defendant: I dispute the $600, it seems to be outrageous.

Plaintiff: The $600, is everything he paid in July. There's one payment that I'm seeing on his credit card for $540 and then another one for $88. That's how it comes up to $628.30.

Defendant: So the amounts are correct, he did pay $1,264. Is it for my telephone account?

Plaintiff: Yes, it's that account. There's not... he's not paying somebody else's bill. The Horizon account number matches.

Judge: Okay, tell me about the chofar. Am I pronouncing it correctly?

Plaintiff: Yes, chofar. It's a horn from an animal which is used on Rosh Hashanah to blow.

Plaintiff: Okay, so it was stored in my living room. Boro saw it. He goes on Saturday to his father in a community of retired people, and he asked me to borrow it. I gladly gave it to him because it was just collecting dust and he never returned it to me.

Judge: Where is it?

Defendant: I had asked him if I could actually purchase it since it wasn't in use, and he said, no, you can only borrow it.

Defendant: So I borrowed it and returned it promptly the next day.

Plaintiff: Who did you return it to?

Defendant: I took it from his shelf in the living room and I put it right back onto the same exact shelf.

Judge: Did you ever tell him, "Here, I brought it back"?

Defendant: Yes, I said thank you very much.

Plaintiff: Is it on the shelf?

Defendant: Did anybody look on the shelf?

Plaintiff: Is it on the shelf?

Defendant: It's not on the shelf.

Plaintiff: It's not on the shelf?

Defendant: It's not on the shelf.

Plaintiff: What shelf did you put it on?

Defendant: The same shelf I took it from in the living room.

Judge: Okay, so if it was there, you would see it.

Defendant: Yes.

Judge: Somebody's lying.

Judge: Okay, when did you move out?

Defendant: Beginning of January 2015, 2016.

Judge: Why did you move out?

Defendant: One Sunday morning I woke up and he says my wife doesn't like you, you have to leave.

Judge: When did the wife come in?

Defendant: He was living alone when you moved in.

Judge: What happened?

Defendant: He suddenly met a wife and got married two weeks later and she said, wait, wait, he met, he met a woman, he got married two weeks later. So how long were you dating before?

Defendant: How, like two months, three months, something like this.

Judge: That's kind of fast.

Defendant: That's what the Jewish people do. They see, listen, you know, we like each other. We come from the same...

Judge: Is it working out?

Defendant: It's a very heated family relationship but, yes, we're working.

Judge: Why did you ask him to leave?

Plaintiff: I was doing some work for my friend in Connecticut, and my wife told me that Boro brought a lady overnight. It was very inappropriate in our opinion because we are observant. We don't want this kind of thing.

Judge: So that offended your wife, so your wife said he's got to go?

Plaintiff: Yes.

Judge: And then what?

Plaintiff: And then I asked him to leave.

Judge: So where did you go when he asked you to leave?

Defendant: I got a U-Haul that night and I picked up my stuff and I had nowhere to go.

Judge: Where'd you go?

Defendant: I went to a hostel.

Judge: Okay, and you stayed there for how long?

Defendant: No, just for a day or two until I got a room.

Plaintiff: She had actually not let me be in the house on my own without Solomon being there because she did not allow me to be in the kitchen, use the fridge. The bathroom was not working.

Judge: Did you ever agree to start paying rent once you were there?

Defendant: I agreed to help with utilities because.

Judge: Is that something you hadn't been doing until that point?

Defendant: I wasn't in the beginning.

Judge: Did you ever agree to pay him $250 a month?

Defendant: No.

Plaintiff: No.

Defendant: When I moved in, he had begged me not to pay. I would keep him company.

Judge: And did he ever change that and say, "I think you should pay $250 a month"?

Defendant: No.

Judge: No.

Defendant: No.

Plaintiff: What are you suing for, the new lock?

Plaintiff: What's that about?

Defendant: Boro, you have in front of you the... did you take the keys and you didn't give him back the keys?

Plaintiff: No, he changed the locks. So they're no longer working.

Judge: He changed the locks?

**Judge:** This is the plaintiff, Linda Gomez. She says she found the defendants a perfect apartment. Their application was approved, but once they met the landlord, he discovered they lied on their application by stating they had no pets. He felt deceived, and the landlord canceled the contract, and they still owe her the $1,757 commission they agreed to pay. So she's suing.

**Judge:** These are the defendants, Justin and Xandy. Justin says they told the landlord they would give their cat to Justin's grandparents, so it wasn't an issue. The landlord changed his mind and decided not to rent to them. Now the plaintiff thinks she should collect a commission. No way. They're accused of telling a feline fib.

**Judge:** All parties, please raise your right hands. Welcome back to *The People's Court*. Next case on the docket: The plaintiff says she is an excellent real estate agent who found an apartment for the defendants, and now they're trying to stiff her on her commission. But the defendants say the landlord changed his mind about renting to them, and they don't owe this woman a penny. It's the case of *Broker? He hardly knew her!*

**Judge:** Thank you, Douglas.

**Court Clerk:** You're welcome.

**Judge:** All right, Ms. Linda, you are suing Mr. Justin and Ms. Xandy for $1,757 in broker’s fees that you claim you are entitled to and that they refused to pay. Tell me about it. What happened?

**Plaintiff:** Good afternoon, Your Honor. I'm Linda Gomez. On April 21st, Justin reached out to me through email and text, very interested in seeing a unit on Beacon Street in Boston. The next day, I scheduled everything. We met; the property manager and landlord’s agent were there, and he and I conducted the showing. We worked through it. They loved it. It was a perfect location for them since it was a seven-minute walk from their new job, so it was a no-brainer. A few hours later, I sent all the paperwork required to have this apartment taken off the market, and they sent all the applications very promptly, very well filled out. I submitted them with their proof of income, credit reports, and all the applications and brokers' fee disclosure that they signed.

**Judge:** When the landlord reviewed it, was everything fine? What went wrong?

**Plaintiff:** When the lease was produced and Justin and his partner looked at it, they saw that there was a rule that no pets were allowed in the building. They came back to the landlord saying, "Oh, we have a cat. What can we do?"

**Judge:** Wait, wait, wait, wait, wait. Because the timing of stuff is important, and I'm getting confused. They put in an application. Did they mention that they have pets?

**Plaintiff:** No, zero. They actually wrote down zero pets.

**Judge:** Okay. Then you say it’s a done deal. They got the apartment, and they noticed in the lease—but they had already signed the lease?

**Plaintiff:** No, no. At that point, when they wanted the landlord to either change the rule about the pet or let them know what to do, the landlord felt like they lied on the application.

**Judge:** So the landlord backed out?

**Plaintiff:** Yes. The landlord said—

**Judge:** And you feel that they still owe the broker’s fee even though the deal didn’t happen? You feel they still owe the broker’s fee because it’s their fault?

**Plaintiff:** Yes! You shouldn’t lie on an application. If you have a cat, you have a cat. Give the landlord the opportunity to respond to that.

**Judge:** How do you know that they weren’t going to just make other arrangements for the cat?

**Plaintiff:** Because the building association has a rule of no pets.

**Judge:** Okay, let me hear from you, Mr. Justin and Ms. Xandy. Whoever wants to talk first—tell me what’s going on here.

**Defendant (Justin):** Yeah, absolutely, I’ll go first. Everything she said up to this point is true and accurate. We went through everything, all the process. It got to the point where we were presented a lease, and they’re pretty standard. We knew going into Boston—Boston, roughly 20% of landlords allow cats, less than 9% allow dogs, and less than 1% allow big dogs. So we knew going in this could be an issue, so we made plans ahead of time with my grandfather, who’s had cats his whole life.

**Judge:** So when you got the lease, it specifically said no pets allowed?

**Defendant (Justin):** Yes, but also at the end, it said, "Ask for written permission if applicable." So we thought, "Okay, we already made these plans. We have a cat that we love. Why don’t we just try to ask him?" We had a great Zoom interview. They loved us. Why don’t we just try to ask if he could allow a cat?

**Judge:** But my understanding is that the landlord lives in the building and is allergic.

**Defendant (Justin):** I believe they have three locations. I’m not sure if they lived there. They have a place in London, a place in New York, and—

**Judge:** Okay, let’s get to what happened next.

**Defendant (Justin):** We emailed them at 11 a.m. and decided to email the landlord directly. We asked, "Listen, the lease says with written permission I could have a pet, so I’m asking you." The landlord responded, "Absolutely not. I’m allergic to cats. That’s a deal-breaker."

**Judge:** And then what do you say?

**Defendant (Justin):** We immediately responded, "Oh, we’re sorry to hear that. We made plans for our cat to stay with my grandparents."

**Judge:** So they followed the rules. They tried to see if they could get permission. It might have been a yes, but it turned out to be a no. And they said, "Oh, no problem. We’ve already made an arrangement." And then the landlord changed his mind. So why should they have to pay the broker’s fee?

**Plaintiff:** Because the apartment was taken off the market!

**Judge:** But they didn’t back out! The landlord backed out. You only get paid if the deal happens and they back out—not if the landlord backs out because he got a bad feeling. I don’t know what happened here, but what happened isn’t their fault. My verdict is for the defendants.

**Plaintiff:** Can I speak—

**Judge:** Unfortunately, the plaintiff does not prevail. Ms. Gomez, what are you thinking right now?

**Plaintiff:** I feel the judge should have read my broker’s fee disclosure where it says that if the party is approved and a lease is produced, then the broker's fee is owed.

**Judge:** The judge pointed out the deal never went through, though.

**Defendant (Justin):** We found another apartment that allows our cat, and we’re happy about that.

**Judge:** Well, all’s well that ends well. Good luck in Boston.

**Defendant (Justin):** Thank you so much!

Judge: So is this a quasi-govern -- I thought this was a quasi-governmental corporation, the USAC.

Lawyer of Defendant: No.

Judge: It's not?

Lawyer of Defendant: No.

The -- the -- the -- the Administrative Company --

Judge: Yeah.

Lawyer of Defendant: -- is a private company.

It is a private corporation incorporated in -- in -- in Delaware, and -- and -- and we -- we know that it's not a government instrumentality or agent because, if it were, it would run afoul of the Government Corporation Control Act because --

Judge: Thank you.

Lawyer of Defendant: -- the FCC sought permission, and it wasn't provided.

Judge: Thank you, counsel.

Lawyer of Defendant: Thank you.

Judge: Mr. Singh.

Lawyer of Plaintiff: Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court: When Wisconsin Bell requests E-rate funds, the government provides the money.

The administrator pays on the government's behalf using money the government collects and controls to advance a federal program that the government created.

FCC mandates, which the administrator must follow, specify who must be paid and how much they must be paid.

So, when the administrator paid Wisconsin Bell, that was the government providing money through its agent.

Indeed, many government programs, including most notably Medicare, use private claims administrators to provide billions of dollars to beneficiaries of government programs. As Justice Kagan's soup hypothetical, Justice Barrett's proctor hypothetical illustrate, when an entity like the government provides money through agents, we credit the principal in that situation.

So, when Wisconsin Bell receives money, claims money, from the E-rate program, we credit the government with providing it.

FCC mandates also determine carriers' obligations to contribute to the Universal Service Fund.

Those mandates are how the government provided funds to this program, and it doesn't matter that the government routed the funds directly from the carriers to the Universal Service Fund instead of requiring an unnecessary detour through federal Treasury accounts because the False Claims Act's applicability doesn't turn on bookkeeping conventions, which is also a point Ms. Ho made, I think, quite well.

The point is not exactly how did the money move through a bureaucracy, was it routed efficiently or less efficiently.

The point is who is really making the money move, and it is the government.

The government is the only relevant decision-maker at all stages of creating, funding, and then controlling the distributions from this program.

The administrator merely does what the government wants.

Under the plain meaning of the word "provide," the government therefore provides the money Wisconsin Bell claims.

That's true of all the money and especially the hundred million that was discussed more -- earlier.

I welcome the Court's questions.

Judge: Mr. Singh, if this had been an appropriated fund and the administrator had been a federal agency, this would be, of course, a very straightforward case.

We'd know exactly what the government's financial stake was.

But this is private money from private parties to another private party, and it's very difficult to see what the government's financial stake is.

And I think it's -- it -- you might ultimately conclude, or we might, that it is enough to be federal funds.

But it doesn't look like federal funds.

It looks like private funds.

So, in this -- in this instance, would you explain exactly what the government's financial stake is here? It hasn't appropriated any monies, it hasn't given any grants, and it hasn't lost anything.

No matter what happens here, the government's not out of pocket.

Lawyer of Plaintiff: Sure.

So let me -- let me start with part of the premise I'm not sure I agree with.

As Ms. Ho admitted in the first part of the argument, this is treated for budget purposes as a permanent indefinite appropriation.

The appropriation is located in the Telecommunications Act, so it's not a year-to-year appropriation where a specific amount of money is designated, but it is treated for budget purposes as appropriated funds.

And so, to the extent that that's significant, I think it's satisfied here.

Such permanent indefinite appropriations are pretty commonplace throughout the federal budget, and so that piece of the premise I'm not sure I agree with.

But I take the point that there are aspects in which the funding structure of this program is unusual, that there is less government touching the money than there might be for other federal programs.

And, as I said in my introduction, I just don't think that that can be dispositive as to the question whether the federal government is providing the money.

On the specific question of what has the government lost, I think, if you look at this the way that I do, which is to say the government could have ingested this money into Treasury accounts and then moved it to the administrator and out to beneficiaries -- in fact, we know it could have because it did so in 2018.

It relocated all of the funds into Treasury accounts.

Nothing changed about how the program works.

It is still the same -- quintessentially the exact same program except for the flows of funds.

And so I would say what the government loses is all that money coming in because that is money that the government solely controls to achieve its governmental purposes.

And so, when that money is not available because fraud depletes the Universal Service Fund, there is less money on hand to pay the beneficiaries that the government is trying to provide affordable telecommunications services to.

And that is really no different, I think, from any other government spending program.

Judge: Well -- I'm sorry, why don't you finish your answer.

Lawyer of Plaintiff: No, I think that that's what I've got.

Judge: It is a little bit different, and you mentioned this at the beginning.

It -- the GAO calls this back -- backdoor appropriations, which is not -- you know, that's a little bit of a pejorative term, I mean, and the reason I think is because you do get a very large amount of money without going through the normal appropriations process.

And it seems to me that that's a significant question, maybe at too high a level of generality, but if you can take and spend for whatever purposes you would like any amount of money so long as you require a -- a -- a private entity to pay it into a fund and then you exercise whatever authority you want to dispose of it in a particular way, that seems to me a significant exception to the normal appropriations process, and I wonder if that's a concern at all.

Lawyer of Plaintiff: I don't think it's a concern in this instance.

Nobody has made the argument that anytime you have a permanent indefinite appropriation, the False Claims Act just doesn't apply or the program is invalid.

Judge:
How did you guys know each other?

Oh, we've known each other for years. We lived down the street from each other. We grew up on the same block. As we got older, we moved, and we kind of lost touch. So you decide to use her for a trip to—?

Yes, the Dominican Republic.

You and your spouse decide that it’s a great idea. You go on it. What goes wrong during the trip?

Plaintiff:
Oh, everything.

Judge:
Well, first, tell me what went wrong with the flights before the trip.

Oh, it was horrible. We booked the trip on the 11th, and by the 13th, we already had an issue with the flight.

What was the issue?

I got an email saying the flight had been cancelled. But before that, I had actually received a confirmation that we were going on a flight that wasn’t the one we originally agreed to. Okay, but that cancellation sounds normal. She was canceling that flight because it wasn’t the one you wanted. But why would a flight have surfaced that wasn’t the one you picked?

Defendant:
Because I was dealing with a— It was like a third-party transaction. I had a person at the airport.

Who is the she? An employee of yours?

Defendant:
No, she’s just an associate.

Judge:
Okay, go on.

Plaintiff:
The next flight gets cancelled.

Judge:
Why did the second one get cancelled?

Defendant:
That was not what I told her, but, um, the second one got cancelled. The person at the airport told me it was something with the flights to the Dominican Republic—something about the group.

Defendant:
Yes.

Then it’s not something with the trip; it’s something with your methods. Normally, this shouldn’t happen, right? People shouldn’t have their trips cancelled multiple times?

Defendant:
Right, and that’s exactly what I was saying to my person who was booking the flight.

Judge:
Something shady is going on here. What is it? I need to know.

Defendant:
I wasn’t aware of it at first, but later, I found out my associate was using inappropriate credit cards.

Judge:
A stolen card?

Defendant:
Yes, but at the time, I didn’t know.

Judge:
So, let’s fast-forward. You go on the trip. You get to the resort. What happens?

Plaintiff:
I have no confirmation number. I call her. She doesn’t answer. The resort can’t find our reservation. It takes three hours to get a room.

Judge:
And what about the excursions?

Plaintiff’s Spouse:
They were horrible. Our names weren’t on the lists. The driver never showed up to take us to the club.

Judge:
Okay, so the trip is a disaster. You get home. What happens next?

Plaintiff:
I get an email from Southwest's fraud department.

Judge:

Defendant:
Because my associate was using fraudulent credit cards.

Judge:
She paid you how much for the ticket?

Plaintiff:
$520. Then, I had to pay Southwest again—$834.51.

Why wouldn’t Southwest go after the agency?

They said they go after the passenger because we got the benefit of the flight.

Judge:

Defendant:
I wasn’t given the chance to fix it. I would have fixed it.

Judge:
But fixing it would have meant paying the $834, right?

Defendant:
I would have figured out a way.

Judge:
Okay. I’m finding in the plaintiff’s favor for $834.51. However, I am not awarding lost wages.

Verdict for the plaintiff.

I mean, that's in effect the time, manner, and place or -- or similar to that. If state law comes -- comes along and establishes a -- a realistic defense or a realistic approach to how people can remain in the -- in the community, then the courts obviously should defer to that.

Lawyer of Defendant: Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court. A sensible scheme of judicial review would provide a right to review on a petition properly filed, particularly on matters of life and death where errors left uncorrected are so contrary to the fundamental policies of the United States. But it also would not have the courts routinely intrude while the agency is still doing its work.

When the agency is deliberating where a non-citizen can be sent in light of an objection duly raised in the ordinary processes, that work is ongoing. Amicus's proposal would mean that a non-citizen like Defendant is allowed to seek a Court's decision on withholding issues that the agency has barely begun to assess. The INA does not suggest that Congress intended that surprising result. Instead, the statute is consistent with common sense.

Regarding jurisdiction, Santos-Zacaria already explained that Stone was not a holding on that point. The Court should adhere to what it said there. And, certainly, 1252(b)(1) does not rank as jurisdictional under the modern rubric.

I welcome the Court's questions.

Judge: Did Defendant seek review of the order -- removal order?

Lawyer of Defendant: We are seeking -- we are not seeking review of his removability. We are seeking review of the CAT decision, Your Honor.

Judge: But the -- the only jurisdiction we have is over the removal order.

Lawyer of Defendant: I -- I think that's -- I -- I would -- I would question that premise, Your Honor.

(A)(4) does also allow --

Judge: Or a final order. Excuse me, a final order.

Lawyer of Defendant: The -- the court -- the statute allows the court to conduct review within 30 days of a final order from removal, but it also clearly says the Court has jurisdiction to review a CAT order as the Court explained in Nasrallah already.

Judge: Have we ever said that you could do that independent of the final order?

Lawyer of Defendant: I -- not independent in the sense -- and that is exactly the problem, Your Honor. I think that the sensible approach is to wait until the agency has concluded all of its work, and then you have one petition that encompasses removability, the order of removal, and the CAT questions that are at issue.

Judge: Does the CAT question then convert the -- the final order into a broader final order by attaching itself to that?

Lawyer of Defendant: I think what Nasrallah teaches us, like -- certainly, is that it does not convert the -- a CAT order that says -- that -- that denies CAT relief is not converting the final order into something else, except from the timing of it and what gets decided is affecting the finality, because as we explained in the brief, and I believe the government agrees, where you can be sent is something that remains to be decided.

The -- the removal order under the government's regulations has to tell you where you're going to be sent. You have an opportunity to object right -- right then. If the process results in that being -- that -- that where not working, then they are going to tell you where else. And then you might proceed into another. So -- so it does not affect the removability.

Judge: So do you have -- what's your best textual hook for that?

Lawyer of Defendant: I'm sorry, for -- for which, hook for what?

Judge: For your -- for your argument that the CAT order has to be -- it can expand the time limit of the final order.

Lawyer of Defendant: So it -- it turns on what final means, Your Honor. And the -- the statute, we submit, obviously it explains what makes something final in certain circumstances. And aside from that, it's a word that this Court has interpreted in many contexts to mean that the agency's decision-making is full and complete on all issues, that the agency has disassociated itself from the decision-making process. That's the word final.

Judge: So one definition in the statute, at least the courts on amicus's side argued -- or concede that the one issue that defines finality is when the Board concludes its work, correct?

Lawyer of Defendant: When the Board concludes its work, that -- that -- that is correct.

Judge: Okay.

Lawyer of Defendant: And here when the Board concludes its work as you know is --

Judge: Is after the CAT, because the CAT decision --

Lawyer of Defendant: That's right.

Judge: -- can change the final order, correct?

Lawyer of Defendant: Absolutely, correct.

Judge: Because it will change -- the final order says you're -- you are removable, you're going to X country. And the CAT order will say no, make it Y country. So there is an amendment.

Lawyer of Defendant: There -- there -- there is -- there is an amendment, that's right.

Judge: So this is almost like a conviction and a sentence, meaning the conviction is final in a court -- in a district court, until you appeal it, and the -- but you wait for the sentence for the appeal because you want the court to finish with everything at once.

Lawyer of Defendant: I -- I think you have it exactly right, that -- that analogy. And Nasrallah was familiar with that analogy, of course.

Judge: That's the one it gave.

Lawyer of Defendant: That's the one in Nasrallah, that --

Judge: All right. Now, Congress does use the word administrative in a different provision, the detention provision.

Lawyer of Defendant: Correct.

Judge: 1252. So there they said when an order is administratively filed, meaning when the agency has finished on this particular question. But it uses a different word, final order of removal, here. That difference, how does it play into your argument?

Judge: Justice Barrett? Justice Jackson? Thank you, counsel. Rebuttal, Mr.

Plaintiff's Lawyer: Thank you.

Just a few quick points. First, I don't think you heard a persuasive answer to why the Court shouldn't resolve this case on the narrow theory.

And I think we agree with the United States that's the most straightforward way to do it.

And I think the colloquy with Justice Kagan and Justice Alito shows that it's indisputable that she was a qualified individual subject to an allegedly discriminatory policy that reduced her compensation.

The ADA allows her to challenge that policy even after she leaves the job.

And that answers the question and resolves at least a chunk of the circuit split.

But the second point I want to make is I think, while the case can be resolved on that narrow ground, I do want to urge the Court in its opinion to be careful not to foreclose other scenarios that the City's reading would permit, particularly given the City's failure to identify any plausible reason why Congress would have wanted to draw this arbitrary line.

A firefighter who becomes disabled saving people from a burning building could be discriminated against the next month.

A retired firefighter who develops a respiratory condition from years of smoke exposure could lose health coverage.

And an employer could deny privileges that are extended to all other former employees, such as use of the company cafeteria or the attendance at a company retreat, based solely on disability-based animus. And the third and final point I want to make is just on the broader question.

The City's position creates fundamental anomalies that Congress couldn't have intended.

The City concedes that the ADA protects retirement benefits but offers no coherent account of how that protection could be vindicated, as I think the questions with Justice Jackson showed.

The City's extreme position creates perverse incentives for employers to hide discrimination until after retirement, and it would transform clearly unlawful discrimination into perfectly lawful conduct based solely on timing even though Congress expressly protected these benefits and included a safe harbor provision to address legitimate cost concerns.

For race or religion, we would never tolerate a regime under which unlawful discrimination suddenly becomes lawful a minute later.

The City can't explain why Congress would have created such an arbitrary line for disability discrimination alone.

Thank you.

Judge: Thank you, counsel.

The case is submitted.

Judge: What if the other charges on which there was probable cause were also felonies and maybe even more serious felonies than the so-called money-laundering charge? Would you make the same argument?

Defendant's Lawyer: So I don't think we'd be able to make the same argument about why we win under the United States' rule. But I think that the Eleventh Circuit—

Judge: But you think you should win anyway?

Defendant's Lawyer: I think under the Eleventh Circuit's rule that the defense holds. And you can imagine, right—

Judge: Why?

Defendant's Lawyer: I mean, let's say some of them are—

Judge: I mean, why? What if one of the charges is murder and there's probable cause to support the murder charge and then they throw in this money-laundering charge? Or let's say it's an assault, and let's say there's a video when the legitimate owners of the ring came to the store and they were causing a fuss and your client pulled out a gun and he said, "Get out of my store, I'm going to blow your head off?" So he's charged with an assault as well as money laundering. The same result?

Defendant's Lawyer: So just as a preface, I think that this is the reason you should let these rules percolate, right? We should make—we should figure out how these rules actually play out in practice. The reason I think it's the same result, what the Williams Court would say, is there's a reason police officers lied about this charge, right, and so we're going to assume that they lied about it in part because they needed it to be able to detain the person.

Judge: Well, that—I mean, you may be—this is—the facts of this case are disturbing, and you may well win. But we're talking about what the general rule should be. So your rule would apply in a case where the police officers act in good faith, but they just don't have probable cause? It's not limited to a case in which there was perjury or the manufacture of false evidence, right? Your rule is not limited in that way?

Defendant's Lawyer: So that's correct, but we think another element, right, the mens rea element, which this Court hasn't weighed in on yet and on which there's a circuit split, is the proper element to do the filtering that Your Honor is talking about. And the reason why I think the more serious/less serious charge rule—so, to be clear, I think that's a rule we could certainly live with. We win under that rule. There's some evidence in the common law, the Newell Treatise, for instance, seems to suggest that the charge lacking probable cause should be the more serious charge, so we think there's some warrant for it, but the reason we don't think it's the best option is because you can imagine a situation, for instance, where the felony charge is a white-collar offense on which, you know, no one's ever going to be detained. The misdemeanor is a resisting arrest charge that—and the magistrate says, you know, that indicates some kind of dangerousness, that's the reason I'm going to detain him. And so I don't think you can have a kind of categorical more serious/less serious charge rule.

And, again, I think what the Eleventh Circuit's rule does is it says: Look, at the point where this legal process has been tainted, remember, this Court's cases like Gerstein and County of Riverside say, if there was no legal process, right, if there's no arrest or Gerstein hearing, we actually don't care if there's all the probable cause in the world.

Judge: Well, you're talking about being tainted. Now you're—then you're back to the idea that the officer lied, right? But what if he hadn't? I mean, what if there's just a—you know, there's no indication that there was a—that there's a lie, there just wasn't probable cause.

Defendant's Lawyer: Sure, Your Honor. So I think, in that circumstance, first, probable cause, remember, is a low bar, right? It's just a reasonable belief. And, second, qualified immunity will almost certainly protect the officer in a situation where there's no probable cause, but even the judge is confused and thinks there's probable cause.

Judge: Yeah. Well, it'll protect the officer, but it won't protect the municipality.

Defendant's Lawyer: That's true, Your Honor. But I'm having trouble thinking of a situation where a judge signs this thing, right, signs the arrest warrant, signs off on the Gerstein hearing, knowing all the facts the police officer accurately reported and yet there's not even probable cause.

Judge: Can I ask you a question about the Eleventh Circuit rule? I just want to make sure I'm understanding it. I had thought that one distinction—and maybe it doesn't matter, and so you can tell me if it does—is that in Williams, the crime for which he was being detained and for which probable cause had been manufactured was the tainted crime, and the other crime for which there would have been probable cause if a warrant had been pursued was the one that had not had the adequate process. And that's a different situation than we have here, right? Because here, there was a valid warrant for the misdemeanors, right? It was just the money laundering. And so you actually had that process observed for the two misdemeanors in a way that in Williams, it was kind of like, it was blank, right, on the other side, and so there was no valid process holding him.

Does that—why does that not matter? I gather you think it doesn't.

Defendant's Lawyer: So I don't think it does because Williams is very clear. This is at 1165 that Williams will prevail if he establishes a genuine dispute about whether at least one of the two charges against him for attempted murder, right, so, remember, there's attempted murder as to two different officers.

Judge: Mm-hmm.

Defendant's Lawyer: The holding is he only has to prove that one of those lacks probable cause. This is at 1165. So it's true that in Williams, there was also this uncharged offense, but Williams just says you have to prove one of the two charges in the actual arrest warrant was bogus.

Judge: Okay. And then I want to understand a distinction, or what I think is a distinction, between your position and, say, the government's. Do you agree that you have to show a seizure for purposes of making out your claim?

Defendant's Lawyer: So we—we agree that you could just continue to reiterate the Thompson language, which is "resulted in a seizure." We don't think this Court should use this case to go further and say that there cannot be a malicious prosecution claim predicated on, for instance, the unreasonable search clause or the warrant clause. We don't think that this is the case that tees that up.

Judge: Well, the search clause, sure, but, I mean, is it going farther? We've said that before. I mean, you have to make out a Fourth Amendment claim. And here, your claim is for seizure of the person. You didn't make, I don't think, a separate claim about the seizure of his effects being unreasonable?

Defendant's Lawyer: So—so, below, we did argue at the seizure of his effects as well as about the unreasonable search. What's come before this Court is—

Judge: The Court is just this one.

Defendant's Lawyer: Yeah.

Judge: Yeah.

Defendant's Lawyer: And so—

Judge: Yeah.

Defendant's Lawyer: —all we're saying is that you shouldn't—and I think we agree with the government on this.

Judge: Okay.

Defendant's Lawyer: You shouldn't reach out and affirmatively say there can be no other Fourth Amendment malicious prosecution claim.

Lawyer of Defendant:
Well, a couple responses.

One, so is a declaratory ruling. And in the D.C. Circuit, declaratory rulings, it's enough to send a comment.

So their distinction doesn't work on its own terms.

And I would point the Court there to Petition Appendix 18a.

That is where the Fifth Circuit discusses the D.C. Circuit precedent on that point.

But, second, this is a very strange type of adjudication.

By statute, Congress has said that if they're going to do this type of license, they need to open up to NEPA, which is a -- a notice-and-comment process, in the middle of the adjudication.

This is not a normal adjudication.

Judge:
Do you think it's enough for anybody to send in a letter, or does it have to be the governor of a state?

Lawyer of Defendant:
I think it certainly helps that it was a governor of a state.

Judge:
Well, I don't see really how it does help under the statute.

I mean, it's nice that he was the governor of the state, but I don't see how you can make a legal argument on that basis.

If somebody is a party by virtue of sending in a letter under this statutory scheme, anybody is a party by virtue of sending in a letter.

Lawyer of Defendant:
So, again, we think that if you file a comment -- and it wasn't just a letter; it's comments -- as part of the notice-and-comment process, but if they solicit you, which is what happened here -- I'd point the Court to the record on this one.

This was JA 292.

They asked us, they solicited our comments, and then we responded to that.

Even under their -- their best case, this Water Transport case from the D.C. Circuit that they rely on, if the agency solicits your participation and you respond to that, that counts in the D.C. Circuit, their case. That's -- and, again, that's their best case.

So this isn't an ordinary enforcement action or something like that.

Even then, I don't think their argument holds up, but --

Judge:
I mean, the question, General, is what does "party" mean? And it seems to me "party" means somebody who has participated in an agency proceeding with the degree of formality required for that proceeding.

So, if you're in a rulemaking, being a party may very well mean I submitted a comment in a notice-and-comment process.

But -- but that's not the degree of formality that's associated with a proceeding of this kind.

And, you know, you didn't intervene. You didn't even try to intervene, unlike Mr. Frederick's client.

I -- I don't see how we can say that you were a party.

Lawyer of Defendant:
Well, I mean, the word "party," it's the same word in the Hobbs Act that applies to all of these things.

So we have to say that the word "party" is a chameleon.

Judge:
It's not a chameleon. It's like different proceedings might understand who parties are differently.

I mean, that's -- that's -- that's not anything weird.

There are three separate processes, and the way you participate in those three separate processes are -- are different because different rules apply, because the processes are understood to -- parties in -- in adjudications are different from parties in rulemakings.

Lawyer of Defendant:
Okay.

So I guess a couple of responses.

One, I don't agree under the Hobbs Act that you're going to distinguish -- it's --it's one word that has to apply to both.

But say I'm wrong about that.

Judge:
It -- it -- it is one word.

Lawyer of Defendant:
Yeah.

Judge:
It means have you participated with the degree of formality that's necessary for the kind of proceeding it is? And if you are, you're a party.

It's one definition.

Lawyer of Defendant:
Okay.

So say I'm wrong about this.

So I'm agreeing with you for -- for purposes of this answer.

I would still say what we're talking about here is a lot closer to a hybrid between a rulemaking and an adjudication than a pure adjudication.

This is a licensing which Congress said by statute they have to take our comments.

And then we filed those comments in response.

And the argument that we are making today was presented to the agency by Sierra Club, and the agency said no, we're not going to even consider that.

We're not going to take that contention.

So we're in the position here where they've asked for our comments, we've responded to their comments.

The arguments we want to make they've already said they're not going to hear.

It seems very strange to say that the State of Texas is not a party.

We're obviously aggrieved.

And, by their own regulations, they asked for our participation and we participated.

Judge:
Are you defending the Fifth Circuit's ultra vires holding? You're saying, even if you weren't a party aggrieved, we could hear your appeal under the theory of ultra vires? I didn't see you or Fasken spending a whole lot of ink on that in your briefs.

Lawyer of Defendant:
Well, I mean, I think the more straightforward point is that we are a party under the Hobbs Act.

Or, if not, if Fasken is truly a party, then this is all academic for us.

Judge:
Okay.

Judge: Is it not true that he has a long record working for the legislature and he's drawn maps for both Republicans and Democrats?

Plaintiff's Lawyer: Yes, the panel acknowledged he has two decades of experience in which he used race every time he was drawing maps in the past but denied doing so in this case.

Judge: At trial, Judge Gergel -- is that the correct pronunciation of his name, Gergel?

Plaintiff's Lawyer: Yes, it is Judge Gergel.

Judge: Yeah. Judge Gergel had complimentary things to say about him. He said, I know Mr. Roberts. He's a very precise guy. What I want is if that report -- he's talking about a particular report -- isn't accurate, and I'm persuaded, if he tells me it's not, that's good enough for me.

Plaintiff's Lawyer: Yes, and also his honesty in Footnote 9, where he recognized that a year after trial, Mr. Roberts was able to cite with specificity the racial makeup of VTDs, which was completely inconsistent with his non-credible denials that he did not look at race.

Judge: So, the district court turns around in its opinion and says that his testimony rings hollow. So that's a nice way of saying that he lied, right?

Plaintiff's Lawyer: Yes. And that's what this Court acknowledged was the case in Cooper with the three reasons for it, including his in-depth knowledge of the racial demographics of South Carolina.

Judge: Is that damning?

Plaintiff's Lawyer: No, not in and of itself, because race consciousness is not the problem. It's the incredible denial, despite all of the evidence, including his testimony, experts' demonstrations, and the movement of so many people, and the coincidence that they land on the exact same patterns.

Judge: So they say he gave no plausible explanation for that?

Plaintiff's Lawyer: That's correct. In fact, Mr. Roberts admitted he abandoned the core priority of least change in CD1.

Judge: Didn't he say that his aim was to produce a Republican district?

Plaintiff's Lawyer: Yes, the Court accepted that legitimate goal, but the Court recognized in Cooper that using race as a means to get there is constitutionally suspect.

Judge: Isn't that a plausible explanation for all of those things?

Plaintiff's Lawyer: The Court found that the testimony was not as persuasive as the racial movements, and the expert testimony demonstrated otherwise.

Judge: No, but the question is, is it a plausible reason?

Plaintiff's Lawyer: Yes, it is plausible, but the Court is not asked to relitigate the validity of expert testimony that it accepted.

Judge: And the last one is he admitted that his movement of nearly 17,000 African Americans was inconsistent with the Clyburn staff plan for Charleston County that he claimed to be faithfully following, right?

Plaintiff's Lawyer: Yes, the state makes a big defense that their map is consistent with what Representative Clyburn was seeking. However, the record does not reflect that. More importantly, Representative Clyburn’s partial map did not harm West Ashley in the same way.

Judge: Let me come back to the question I asked about why your experts did not produce an alternative map. Dr. Imai produced 10,000 maps, right?

Plaintiff's Lawyer: Correct, for one simulation.

Judge: He never considered politics?

Plaintiff's Lawyer: As Dr. Imai's testimony reflects, he tested for the criteria that the state was saying they were using, including the objective criteria. Dr. Imai and Dr. Duchin's methods were useful in supporting the conclusion that race was a significant factor.

Judge: Well, in a case that's all about disentangling race and politics, how can we possibly give any weight to an expert report that did not take politics into account?

Plaintiff's Lawyer: The district court was confronted with that question and relied upon Dr. Imai's testimony for the findings that it thought were probative. Dr. Ragusa and Dr. Liu provided unrebutted testimony that helped disentangle race.

Judge: Did Dr. Imai run a simulation using political data as well but then decide to shelve it when the results were not favorable?

Plaintiff's Lawyer: That is not what the record reflects.

Judge: It never occurred to him that politics might have something to do with this?

Plaintiff's Lawyer: Each expert is tasked with answering specific questions. Not every expert looks at every factor, but Dr. Ragusa and Dr. Liu focused on disentangling race and showed that race was more predictive than party affiliation.

Judge: Did Dr. Duchin control for politics?

Plaintiff's Lawyer: She did in some of her analyses. She looked at how the maps performed with Black candidates and found that they performed worse in the enacted map compared to generic partisan races.

Judge: Did Dr. Liu and Dr. Ragusa use the county envelope method?

Plaintiff's Lawyer: Yes, both did.

Judge: Is that a sound method?

Plaintiff's Lawyer: Yes, it's a method used in past cases like Cooper and Bethune-Hill.

Judge: Under that method, if two people live in the same apartment building, could one be moved and the other not?

Plaintiff's Lawyer: The county envelope method works at the precinct level, not at the apartment level. It relies on moving entire precincts.

Judge: Does it assume that all precincts could be moved, regardless of their location?

Plaintiff's Lawyer: Only those within the county envelope, which reflects the fact that counties like Berkeley or Beaufort were wholly moved into CD1. It was fair to assume that any VTD in those counties could have been moved into CD1, and where they were not moved in, it involved Black voters.
