Plaintiff: I gave him the money when I picked up the second batch.

Judge: But you're behind on storage fees, correct?

Plaintiff: Yes, but we had—

Judge: How much behind were you?

Plaintiff: One month.

Judge: So now you owe another two months?

Plaintiff: No, because when they took the first nine cars, we renegotiated our deal. There were no longer 14 cars, only five.

Judge: And what did you renegotiate it to?

Plaintiff: $100 a month.

Judge: Is there a dispute about that?

Defendant: Yes, ma’am. It was $400 a month.

Judge: According to you, the charges are $400 a month?

Defendant: Yes, ma’am.

Judge: And you have nothing in writing?

Defendant: No.

Judge: I hate flapping gums. I’m setting the storage fees at $300 a month as an appropriate value. I’m deducting from that the amount he agrees that he got. You don’t have proof of having paid him more. That leaves us with $2,800. By the way, you said July, and then in your lawsuit and counterclaim, you said August, not July. So just a moment—tell the truth there.

Judge: That makes the verdict for the defendant on the storage fees—$2,500. Upon payment of that, you are releasing those cars. You have to have a place to take them. I’m going to give you two weeks of free storage. After two weeks, if you haven’t picked them up, you’ll have a problem because under New York law, he will take your stuff if you don’t pay. Case closed.

Defendant:
"My marriage is past on the line. I don't... I can't..."
"And then he's sitting up here... First, he said it was one time, now it's numerous of times."
"You understand, I knew it when she told me what was in my house that it was numerous of times. And he kept lying."
"It hurts really, really bad. It hurts really, really... I'm sorry. I'm sorry."
"Sorry doesn't fix this. Sorry doesn't fix this."

Judge: This is the plaintiff, Brenda Diggs. She says she gave the defendant a deposit to paint her house. He told her he was going to start in a few days. Weeks went by, and he didn't show, and she's lost faith in him and wants her money back.

Judge: That's right, he can't just steal her hard-earned money. She's suing him for the $300 she gave him and feels she has an open-and-shut case against this guy.

Judge: This is the defendant, Richard Rogers. He says he doesn't know what the problem with this lady is because he started the job, and her husband fired him.

Judge: He scraped her house, primed one side of it, and was then cussed out by the husband. He earned his 300 bucks and then some, and if you ask him, he thinks the judge will agree.

Judge: He's accused of doing nothing.

Bailiff: All parties, please rise.

Judge: Welcome back to The People's Court. Next case on the docket: the plaintiff gave the defendant a deposit to paint her house. The guy took the money and ran, but the defendant says he started the job, but her husband fired him.

Judge: It's the case of Primer He Hardly Knew Her.

Judge: Thank you, Douglas.

Judge: Brenda Diggs, you are suing Richard Rogers, doing business as Creative Touch Painting, for $300, a deposit that you gave him to paint your house, which he never painted. Tell me what happened.

Plaintiff: I hired Mr. Rogers to, um, paint my, um, property, and um—

Judge: When did you hire him?

Plaintiff: I, um, met with him. He, um, signed an agreement.

Judge: What day was the agreement signed?

Plaintiff: October 17th.

Judge: Do you have the agreement with you?

Plaintiff: Yes, I do.

Judge: May I see it?

Plaintiff: Yes.

Judge: Thank you. Are you the one who—how did you get this agreement? When you say you had him sign an agreement, did you take this off the internet or something?

Plaintiff: Uh, yes, I did.

Judge: Good for you.

Judge: Okay, did you read through this and sign it and all that?

Defendant: Yes, ma’am.

Judge: Okay, so go on.

Plaintiff: Okay, um, I, uh, paid Mr. Rogers $300 deposit.

Judge: Mhm.

Plaintiff: He agreed to, uh, to paint the house for $1,500 total.

Judge: Right.

Plaintiff: And, um, he was going to get started immediately, and I never heard back from Mr. Rogers, um, for at least about two and a half or three weeks. I didn’t hear anything from him.

Judge: Mhm.

Plaintiff: So, um, I sent him a text message, um, about five days later, informing him that—

Judge: Wait, five days later or two and a half weeks later?

Plaintiff: Um, before I heard back from him, which was two and a half weeks later, before that, I sent him a text message, and that was five days after the contract.

Judge: Right, and what happens?

Plaintiff: And when he responded, he told me he had been, um, in the hospital and that he had a death in the family.

Judge: Mhm.

Plaintiff: And, um, he asked me if I would give him more time.

Judge: Mhm.

Plaintiff: So, you know, after listening to his circumstances, I agreed to give him more time.

Judge: Okay.

Plaintiff: And, but I told him that he had to get started immediately.

Judge: And this—so this is now, like, about the beginning of November?

Plaintiff: Right.

Judge: And what happened?

Plaintiff: He didn’t get started.

Judge: Did he start on your house at all? Did he do any work on it?

Plaintiff: No.

Judge: No scraping, no priming, nothing?

Plaintiff: No.

Judge: All right, let me hear from you.

Defendant: Uh, your honor, I start—I started—I started the job way before they, uh, even gave me the money.

Defendant: You follow what I’m saying? It was like two days before I started the job. Started scraping.

Defendant: My son and my cousin, we started scraping the house. My cousin went back over there a day later and started priming the, uh, the property.

Defendant: So, I don’t know what she’s talking about.

Judge: Do you have any proof of that?

Defendant: Yeah, I do. I have, uh, um, some pictures.

Judge: Why didn’t you paint the house? You were supposed to paint the house.

Defendant: Because weather permitting—it was October, and it rains, you know. It was cold, so I couldn’t really do anything.

Judge: Do you take people’s money and you say you’re going to paint their house, but October is not a work month?

Defendant: I mean, no, it’s not. I’m not saying—

Judge: Okay, right. So what does that mean? Because it wasn’t just October, it was October, November, December.

Defendant: Right, but she told me don’t come back over to the property.

Judge: After—when did she tell you that?

Defendant: I can’t recall.

Judge: Right. See, the contract that she pulled off the internet—she’s a smart cookie. And the contract that she pulled off the internet actually says time is of the essence.

Judge: That means you can’t just sit back and give all the "the dog died, I was hospitalized, there’s been a death in the family."

Judge: Normally, contractors don’t say "There’s been a death in the family and I was hospitalized" in the same excuse, because then they lose one of those to say later.

Judge: Was there a death in your family?

Defendant: No.

Judge: Did you tell her there was?

Defendant: I never said that.

Judge: Okay. Did he tell you there was?

Plaintiff: Yes, he did.

Judge: Okay. Were you hospitalized?

Defendant: No.

Judge: Did you tell her you were?

Defendant: No.

Judge: Did he tell you he was hospitalized?

Plaintiff: Yes, he did.

Judge: For what reason?

Judge: I have done this for so long. Do you have any idea how many—apparently, no one should go into contracting work, because if they do, their lives will be plagued with "death in the family" and, uh, "hospitalizations," because that’s the excuse all the time for when people don’t do their job.

Judge: Return the $300.

Judge: $300 verdict for the plaintiff.

Judge: You're suing Aaron Flood, your former roommate, for three thousand two hundred and forty-one dollars and sixty-seven cents, a portion of his back rent that you are going to owe the landlord because he was your roommate and left you high and dry. Talk to me, tell me what happened.

Plaintiff: Yeah, um, basically we lived together and we're both on the lease, and there came a point where Aaron stopped coming home and stopped paying his share.

Judge: Okay.

Plaintiff: Well, I was working, uh, I worked like about 12 hours—

Judge: You still got to come home.

Plaintiff: Well, no, I did, I came home every night. It was just like I would come home late.

Judge: Okay, and then you'd leave early?

Plaintiff: Well, I'll leave every morning at four in the morning and get home every night around 10, 11 at night.

Judge: Yeah, okay, yeah. And basically, um, the landlord was calling, saying, "What happened to Aaron? Aaron promised he was going to pay." And I gave the landlord—once, the first time—the landlord accepted half of the rent, which was mine.

Judge: What month?

Plaintiff: It was July.

Judge: Okay, so in July he accepts half the rent, and then what happens? In July, do you pay rent in July?

Defendant: Um, well, we were supposed to pay. The lease ended, I believe, around June. We were on a—I believe it was like a month-to-month. And then once the lease ended, then I had to leave because I was traveling all the time for work to the city, so I moved into the city. And based off then, I was just coming, you know, back and forth to the house.

Judge: Did you hear my question? What was my question?

Defendant: (Pauses)

Judge: You can't say it because you want to say what you want to say instead of answering me. Did you pay July rent?

Defendant: Um, I—I don't quite remember. I know we were supposed to pay half and half. I'm not sure if I did or not, but I know—

Judge: So what happened? When did you stop paying rent and why?

Defendant: When we both—it came to, like, a mutual thing. We—he wasn't paying, I wasn't paying. We both got into, like, an argument, like a, you know, a verbal altercation or whatnot. He owes this, I owe that, and so neither of us paid.

Judge: So neither of you paid?

Defendant: Correct.

Judge: And then the landlord filed a case against you?

Plaintiff: Yes.

Judge: What are the results of that case, or is it still pending?

Plaintiff: It's completely done. I paid everything off, meaning I paid off Aaron's share as well as my own. I have receipts for all of that.

Judge: All right, so what did you pay?

Plaintiff: Well, there was interest accrued for every day, it was 39 cents, and considering I got behind—

Judge: Just tell me what you paid.

Plaintiff: It was like 3,266 dollars at the end of it all.

Judge: Your lawsuit is for 3,241.67, so according to you, the landlord was suing for double that, right?

Plaintiff: Not—well, double minus interest, of course.

Judge: How much did the landlord sue you for?

Plaintiff: (Looks at paperwork) 4,535 dollars.

Judge: Oh, that's very different from 6,400 and something or 5,000-something. See, and when you're suing for 3,241, you're telling me that's his half. Half means that you're being sued for 6,400 and some odd. Now, how much are you actually being sued for?

Plaintiff: 4,535 dollars.

Judge: So why wouldn’t you sue for half of that here? Why are you trying to make money?

Plaintiff: It's not making money; it's the interest accrued that was all on the portion that he owed because I was up to date on my portion.

Judge: Show me the settlement paperwork.

(Brief pause as paperwork is handed over.)

Judge: Now, what did you end up doing? Did you move his stuff?

Plaintiff: Yeah, because we were going to get evicted. So I had to clear out the whole apartment. The landlord kept requesting that we move out so that he doesn’t have to call the sheriffs and have the sheriffs remove everything and then have a big bill for that.

Judge: Right.

Plaintiff: So what I did was start moving all our stuff out. And since Aaron never came home—he would be gone for like a week at a time or two weeks at a time—every time me and the landlord texted him, there was no response. So—

Judge: Okay, what else was I going to do? Get stuck with the bill for all the stuff that was left there? So what did you do with the stuff? You just get rid of it?

Plaintiff: No, I put it all in storage at a friend’s house.

Judge: So you didn’t have to pay storage. And then what happened?

Plaintiff: It stayed at the friend’s house for months, and I never heard from Aaron back.

Judge: And where’s your stuff?

Defendant: Well, the reason is that when I came home one day, I saw half of my stuff was all outside. He sold a bunch of my couch, my table, to one of my buddies who lived in another town.

Judge: Your buddy bought your table?

Defendant: Nothing was sold. He helped him move it into his place in the Bronx.

Judge: So it was given?

Defendant: Yeah.

Judge: You’ve given or sold, but yeah. I went there, and I looked at my buddy’s house, and I saw my couch there.

Judge: All right, I understand the situation. I am ruling in favor of the plaintiff in the amount of 2,267.50 plus pre-judgment statutory interest. Court is adjourned.

Judge:
Okay, Azariah Campbell, you are suing Robert. You’ve asked us not to mention your last name for $5,000. According to you, you’re actually out more than that for lost wages and legal fees for malicious prosecution because, according to you, he had you arrested for something that you shouldn’t have been arrested for. So, talk to me, tell me what happened.

Plaintiff (Azariah Campbell):
Yes, Your Honor. On March 3rd, I was at the taxi stand in White Plains, and while sitting on the stand, I saw two people come to my car and ask me if I could take them to Yonkers. I drove them. Nobody discussed how much it would be. Both of them came into the car. I drove them to their address. I didn’t even know how long it took, approximately 25 or 30 minutes. I drove them to their address, and the gentleman gave me $28. I said to him, "It’s $34 for the fare." And he was getting out of the car, grabbing his bags, and to take out the car. So I decided to get the rest of the remaining money.

Judge:
How? You’re sitting in the front seat and your arm goes back there?

Plaintiff (Azariah Campbell):
We both got out of the car, and I grabbed onto the bags and said to him, “You’re going to pay me my money.”

Judge:
You grabbed the bags that were in his hand?

Plaintiff (Azariah Campbell):
Yes.

Judge:
So did you successfully rip them out of his hand?

Plaintiff (Azariah Campbell):
No, we were struggling over the bag, and he said, “Let go of my bag,” and he used a profane word.

Judge:
What was the word?

Plaintiff (Azariah Campbell):
He said, “Get off my bag.”

Judge:
Okay. So I did not let go, then he punched me in my stomach.

Judge:
Wait, so you didn’t let go, and then he punched you in your stomach? Punched like this?

Plaintiff (Azariah Campbell):
Yes, punched me in my stomach.

Judge:
Okay, and then what happened?

Plaintiff (Azariah Campbell):
I staggered back, and right on my side of my door, there was a Coppertone spray, which I had in my car. A Coppertone... what?

Plaintiff (Azariah Campbell):
Spray, suntan lotion.

Judge:
Yeah, suntan lotion. That’s what you keep for protection?

Plaintiff (Azariah Campbell):
No, I use it for my hands in the summer.

Judge:
Okay, and then what happens?

Plaintiff (Azariah Campbell):
I sprayed him with it in the eyes.

Judge:
Yes. Okay, and then what happens?

Plaintiff (Azariah Campbell):
He was advancing back to me, so I jumped in my car, closed the door, and took off.

Judge:
How advancing back? He’s right on you, and how are you getting in your car if he’s right there?

Plaintiff (Azariah Campbell):
No, I grabbed the spray, then ran back to my car, closed the door, and took off.

Judge:
Okay, go ahead and go back. What happened?

Defendant (Robert):
I got into the cab at White Plains Galleria with my daughter, took a cab.

Judge:
How old is your daughter?

Defendant (Robert):
18.

Judge:
Okay, come on up. So go on.

Defendant (Robert):
We took the cab from the White Plains Galleria to my place of residence. Upon arrival, how much did you usually pay for that ride?

Defendant (Robert):
$28, $30 tops.

Judge:
Okay, so if that’s what it cost, why were you handing the guy $28? Don’t you tip your cab drivers after a 25-minute ride?

Defendant (Robert):
Occasionally, I do.

Judge:
Okay, so go ahead.

Defendant (Robert):
So I told him it was $34. I gave him $30, and then he told me it was $34.

Judge:
You got out of the car?

Defendant (Robert):
Yes, I got out of the car, and he jumped out of the car like a madman, and he’s saying, “Give me my money,” over and over again. Repeatedly, over and over again. Until the point he chased me on my property.

Judge:
According to you, is there a struggle for the bags or no?

Defendant (Robert):
Yes, there was.

Judge:
Was the struggle for the bags close to the car?

Defendant (Robert):
Yes, he was grabbing my jacket and kept grabbing the bag off my jacket, and I told him, “Get off me, don’t touch me, you’re on my property, I’m calling the police.”

Judge:
Wait, wait. You’re not on your property. Come over here. I need everybody to be very specific. I need to understand how this happened. So here you are, and he’s…?

Defendant (Robert):
I got out of the car, and he comes around to me, and we’re arguing here. I told him I’m not giving him any more money, and I proceeded to go to my property.

Judge:
Okay, how far is your property?

Defendant (Robert):
It’s right at the curb.

Judge:
Go ahead.

Defendant (Robert):
I told him, “Get off my property. You’re harassing me.”

Judge:
Did you call the police?

Defendant (Robert):
Yes, I called the police, and I told them I’m having a problem. The cab driver is harassing me. I gave him $30, I paid my fare, and he’s not leaving me alone. There was snow on the side when he grabbed me, and I somehow slipped. It happened very fast. When I got back up, I felt a chemical burn on my face. He must have sprayed me 12 to 14 times in my face, eyes, ears, neck, everything. I was burning.

Judge:
You don’t think it was Coppertone?

Defendant (Robert):
No, it was pepper spray.

Judge:
Do you know what the arresting officer says it was?

Defendant (Robert):
Yes, they said it was pepper spray.

Judge:
Okay. So they arrived a couple of minutes later. What happens then?

Defendant (Robert):
They questioned me, and I went to the hospital. They continued to flush my eyes until the pain went away.

Judge:
And then you went home?

Defendant (Robert):
Yes.

Judge:
Okay. So, you go home, and the police do their investigation, and he ends up getting arrested. The case gets continued five times. Who drops the charges?

Defendant (Robert):
I dropped them. Why? Every time he went to court, his attorney wasn’t ready.

Judge:
Why didn’t you just wait and see the thing through?

Defendant (Robert):
I didn’t want to deal with it. I didn’t want to see him again.

Judge:
You didn’t expect him to sue you right afterward, did you?

Defendant (Robert):
No.

Judge:
Okay. Go ahead.

Defendant’s Daughter:
I’m 18 years old. I remember this night. We took the cab from White Plains Galleria to our place. The cab driver was being aggressive about $4. My dad got out of the car, and the driver jumped out of the car with his hand in his pocket. My dad yelled for me to get out, and then he started cursing at him. The driver was yelling, “Give me my money.” We kept telling him to leave us alone.

Judge:
Did the driver grab your father?

Defendant’s Daughter:
Yes, he grabbed his jacket.

Judge:
So, where does the spraying happen?

Defendant’s Daughter:
It happened about 2 or 3 feet away from our house.

Judge:
Okay. So the police came, and he got arrested. You end up paying a lawyer.

Defendant’s Daughter:
Yes, we paid $1,500 for the lawyer and $3,800 for lost time and court appearances.

Judge:
According to you, the case never went to trial because the attorney wasn’t ready, right?

Defendant’s Daughter:
Yes.

Judge:
Okay.

Plaintiff (Azariah Campbell):
Your Honor, why would we have to be living in a world where you could get mad at someone, turn around, grab something to spray them with, run after them, and spray them, and that you wouldn’t be arrested? That sounds like anger instead of self-defense. Even according to your own story, when you told me what happened, he was right there when you sprayed him, which means you had to grab something and come after him several steps in order to spray him. Why wouldn’t you be arrested for it?

Judge:
It’s not reasonable to reach in, grab something, and walk to spray a guy in the face. I find it is not reasonable. It wasn’t self-defense. I find in favor of the defendant in this case. You do not have to pay him anything.

Judge:
That is my verdict. Good luck, folks.

Plaintiff: My phone is in my name. I'm making monthly payments on that, right? So all I asked was for my phone back, and he didn't wanna give me back my phone.

Judge: And then at some point, you're telling him, "I'll give you Sasha back as soon as you give me back my phone," or—

Plaintiff: I didn't mean it like that because—

Judge: That is what you said.

Plaintiff: Right, I said it, but I didn't mean it in that form because no matter what, I wasn't even gonna give Sasha back, to be honest.

Judge: Oh, so you were just lying when you said, "I'll give her back when you pay me"?

Plaintiff: Well, I said that because he wouldn't leave me alone, and all I did was ask for my phone.

Judge: Ma'am? What do you have to say for yourself? First off, let me ask this question. Is what he just said true?

Defendant: Yes. It's just stuff I be needing to do, and I need somebody to watch the kids, so I take them over there.

Judge: Every day?

Defendant: Yes, every day. I don't see the issue.

Judge: What do you have to do, ma'am, that's so important every day that you cannot have your own kids?

Defendant: I got to make money.

Judge: What do you do for a living?

Defendant: Whatever I need to do, honestly. I do, you know, a little dabble here, a little dabble there, just different stuff.

Judge: What was the last legitimate job that you had?

Defendant: I was a waitress. At a club.

Plaintiff: Is that what you call it? Waitressing?

Defendant: Yes, it was waitressing. What else was I doing?

Plaintiff: Well, she was strolling the streets like a prostitute.

Judge: Every day, your kids are with your dad. When you were working at that club, how much money were you bringing home monthly?

Defendant: Between 7-8,000 a month.

Judge: What I am going to do is base your child support on 7,000 a month. Yeah, you can shake your head all you want to, but that's exactly what I'm going to do, ma'am. This is extremely disappointing that you leave your kids every day with your dad and you're not giving him any type of financial support. What are these kids on, you know, they receive any type of government assistance or anything?

Plaintiff: She gets food stamps every month, you know what I mean? And I don't see any.

Judge: You get food stamps, where the food stamps going?

Defendant: In my house.

Judge: Who lives in your house besides you that need the food? Because your kids over here with your dad. You don't give him any money from those food stamps?

Defendant: No.

Judge: You know what? I'm not about to play with you. Your child support obligation will be 1,377 based on your 7,000 salary that you were receiving. Don't look confused. I don't want to hear it.

Defendant: I can't pay that every month. That's too much. I can't pay that every month. I don't even know make that much money every month, so why I gotta pay that every month?

Judge: I don't know too many women that just take their kids and go drop them off at their parents' house and they have a whole man staying in the house with them.

Defendant: It's my dad. Y'all act like I drop them off with anybody.

Judge: These are your kids. Is there anything further, ma'am?

Judge: Yeah.

Lawyer of Plaintiff: That's right.

Judge: All right.

But -- but I'm asking you, if we were to reach it, what's wrong with that? Do you resist that?

Lawyer of Plaintiff: No --

Judge: And, if so, why?

Lawyer of Plaintiff: There may well be principles of -- of that kind of tracing that might work here.

The -- the only caution I would say is, one, the purposes of the FSIA and foreign sovereign immunity are different from trust law or equitable liens or criminal or civil forfeiture or money laundering or all of the other examples that employ those kinds of tracing rules.

So any tracing rule that might be borrowed from those contexts would have to take into account those purposes.

And then the second is that international law itself has a sort of common law that has developed over the centuries, and that may have something to say about this as well.

And I think those are really complicated issues that nobody has briefed, and that is why we would urge the Court not to -- not to weigh in on that.

Judge: Sounds like good advice all around.

Thank you.

Lawyer of Plaintiff: Well, Judge, if I could just push back one little bit, I think you --

Judge: I think you've pushed back enough.

Thank you.

(Laughter.)

Judge: Justice Kagan?

Judge: You can push back now.

Sorry.

Is it my turn? You can push back now and explain. You -- you obviously want to say more about the burden issue, so have at it.

Lawyer of Plaintiff: The -- the -- the -- the only real point I -- I wanted to say is that if you didn't have, say, the House report and all you had was the statute, I think it would be pretty clear.

In Samantar, I think this Court encountered a very similar proposition.

The pre-FSIA history often treated state officials and actors and individuals as partaking of the sovereign immunity and they too could enjoy immunity.

But, in Samantar, you looked at it and you said, look, the FSIA has displaced what came before it.

Although we interpret the text in light of that history, the clear text always takes precedence over whatever background rules might have applied beforehand.

And the FSIA here says foreign sovereign and instrumentality, it's not defined to include individuals; therefore, the individuals do not partake of that sovereign immunity.

There might be some common law that they might be able to partake of but not FSIA immunity under the text.

And, in that case, obviously, that was another instance where this principle wound up being plaintiff-friendly in that case because the defendant couldn't invoke FSIA immunity.

I think, in this case, it would, you know, happen to work the other way.

And so I think Samantar is a good example of where this Court has really privileged FSIA's text over the background history and certainly over legislative history.

I think Verlinden is a great example of that.

There, the legislative history spoke only about American plaintiffs, American rights, violations of Americans' human rights.

And yet the Court looked at it in Verlinden and said, look, we know that's what the legislative history says, but the statute is not limited to domestic plaintiffs.

Therefore, this foreign plaintiff is allowed to sue.

Again, you're privileging the text of the FSIA where it's clear.

And we think, here, 1330 expressly makes it jurisdictional, and that has consequences, one of which is that the party invoking jurisdiction has to bear the burden of establishing it.

Judge: And you say in the brief, even if we reject commingling and require some kind of traceability, if the burden's on the sovereign to show lack of traceability, that's going to be -- I think you used a burden that could be effectively impossible for the sovereign to meet in cases like this.

Lawyer of Plaintiff: I think that's right.

And that's certainly how the D.C. Circuit understood what it was doing in this case, that -- and it expressly said both in the decision below, I think, and definitely in its 2016 decision in this case that Petitioners would bear the burden to show a lack of tracing. And, you know, when -- when many decades have passed, one would think that, especially given the purposes and the narrow departure from the restrictive theory that the expropriation exception is intended to effectuate, that where the property has essentially been lost, where it's lost its distinct identity, that all that means is that there isn't a U.S. forum available anymore.

It doesn't let the sovereign off the hook.

It just means there's not a forum here to hear those claims.

And it would be, I think, quite perverse to flip it around and say, as soon as it's lost its distinct identity, at that point, when nobody can prove it one way or the other, at that point, U.S. courts are wide open to hear these claims that we --

Judge: And you said earlier you think that this is all in compliance with international law, but it's got to be at the outer -- outer boundaries of that, right?

Lawyer of Plaintiff: I --

Judge: I mean, extending this further would seem to really push us into non-compliance with international norms and law, I would think.

Lawyer of Plaintiff: It would -- it would seriously risk undermining our conformity with international law.

It's a point this Court recognized in Helmerich and Philipp, and I think it applies here as well.

Judge: Did all of those states -- I mean, you know, the Sixth Amendment didn't apply to the states back then. So when you're saying, well, you can't point to a single one in which a court struck it down, were there state analogues to the Sixth Amendment that would be relevant?

Defendant's Lawyer: So I think states did have comparable jury trial rights, and also states in which the supplemental information statutes were enacted, they had grand jury requirements that required all elements to be in an indictment. And so -- and these were challenged on constitutional grounds, and courts uniformly upheld them. This goes all the way back to 1824 and the Massachusetts Ross case that we cite in our brief, all the way through to this Court's decision in Graham. There's no decision that I'm aware of to the contrary. So I do think there is a -- even if it was -- even if it were our burden to show uniform tradition here, I think the uniform tradition was one of legislative discretion when it comes to recidivism.

Judge: What about Mathis and Descamps? You know, it's true they're statutory cases, but, you know, there is some avoidance language in them, which you recognize in your brief. Do you want to talk about that a little about it?

Defendant's Lawyer: Sure. So the language in Mathis and Descamps admittedly not great for my position here. I think --

Judge: We appreciate your candor.
(Laughter.)

Defendant's Lawyer: I do think that the Court just didn't resolve the constitutional question in those cases. They -- they were -- as you said, they were avoidance cases. I think most of what the Court held in those cases was that there is a serious constitutional question about the scope of Almendarez-Torres. And I think that's what this case is about. But I don't think that those cases resolved that question. And I don't think any other decision of this Court has either.

Judge: Can you turn to the theory for a second? You said in your opening that you find the other side's position to be unprincipled. So why is that?

Defendant's Lawyer: So for a few reasons. I think, first of all, their principle in this case, which I think was reiterated in the opening, was this elements-only principle, this principle that judges can only find facts that juries previously found beyond a reasonable doubt. And I just don't think that their theory, their test that they end up articulating, lines up with that principle because they recognize that if the Court were to double down on that elements-only principle, it would blow up the categorical approach because judges, in doing predicate felony determinations, often find facts that are not elements of prior offenses like identity, like the date of the offense, like the sequencing issue in Almendarez-Torres itself. So they articulate -- they have to fall back from their elements-only principle, and they end up articulating standards like the government's standard, for example, facts encapsulated in judicial records that are components of prior convictions. I think that's what the government says. That test is in no decision of this Court. I don't think it's in a decision of any court, as far as I can tell.

And so I think because they are departing from their principle, they are articulating novel tests that really don't have any grounding in this Court. And then the last thing I would say is that I think their test, at least the government's test, is not descriptively accurate, even to -- because the test, facts encapsulated in judicial records, that -- identity is not encapsulated in judicial records. The date of the offense is not a component of the prior conviction. So I think the government's test, and I think the other side's test too, although I'm a little less clear on what it actually is, I think none of them had a principle that actually explains where they end up landing.

Judge: I mean, as I understand that argument, it's really just to say that Almendarez-Torres and Apprendi are in a little bit of tension with each other. And who would deny that, really? I mean, even Apprendi understood that. But there's nothing about that bit of tension that has made the system fail to work. And, you know, why would we allow that bit of tension, which has existed for decades now, to suggest an answer to this question that does not seem the one that all our past precedents point to?

Defendant's Lawyer: So I -- I guess I would say I don't think there needs to be tension between Apprendi and Almendarez-Torres. I think, certainly under the government and the other side's view, there is tension. But I think under my reading of Apprendi and Almendarez-Torres, they drew a pretty clear line between facts about prior crimes, facts about present crimes. I think, again, Apprendi said that multiple times. And so I think if you interpret it that way, the tension sort of resolves itself.

And I think the fact that the Court has -- or courts have found non-elemental facts in doing the predicate felony inquiry suggests that that's really what the line is, is I think my fundamental point.

Judge: Your -- on the tension, I think your point is that the history has two different rules.

Defendant's Lawyer: That's right.

Judge: And -- and --

Defendant's Lawyer: -- Almendarez-Torres recognized that.

Judge: -- and it's rooted in concern about prejudicing defendants.

Defendant's Lawyer: That's right. And I think, in Apprendi itself, the Court demanded a uniform standard as to sentence-enhancing facts about present crimes. And so I think it would be somewhat anomalous not to require an extension of that uniformity down to the different tradition of recidivism facts.

And I think that's exactly what Almendarez-Torres recognized, admittedly before Apprendi, but that there was no such uniform tradition in this different context. And so, in this context, facts about present crimes, those don't need -- there's no constitutional prescription there.

When you tell me that the reason you wanted to move was the health and welfare of your baby and your wife, I think that’s perjury. I think the reason you wanted to move was that there was a friend with an apartment, and it was going to be a better situation for you. I don’t like that.

And you’re asking me to enforce the bonanza of a double penalty? I’m not feeling that warm, all-over glow to do it for you. When this is the situation that presents itself.

However, you don’t get to keep that $1,600 because you can’t collect rent from someone if you don’t have a CO. That’s the law. And if you can’t collect the rent, then you cannot keep the security deposit to collect the rent because you didn’t have a CO.

But it’ll be a snowy day in hell before I allow someone to blatantly breach a contract and get double the security deposit. That’s intended as a remedy for unscrupulous landlords and a tenant who is victimized by it, and that security deposit is wrongfully withheld. That’s not for a situation like this. The security deposit was rightfully withheld. You breached the contract.

But as it turns out, I must return it to him. So it’s not going to be double, but you must return the $1,600 because you can’t collect it as rent. That is my verdict. Good luck, folks.

And he said, "If you ever kick my dog again, I’ll kick your—"

If he hadn’t said that, I probably would have knocked on their door the next day to tell them that there was an injury caused.

But when I went to file the next day at the police department, I described where I was and who was involved, and the cop said, "They know him, and he runs hot."

**Defendant (Deborah Pelts):** That’s not what happened at all. My dog never gets out. Diesel is always on a leash, and I was right there on the porch. The dog was inside. I think Tony’s dog actually provoked Diesel. He’s protective, but he doesn’t just attack.

**Judge:** Did you see the fight start?

**Defendant (Deborah Pelts):** No, I was on the porch, but by the time I looked up, they were already tangled. I ran down immediately.

**Judge:** Who got your dog under control?

**Defendant (Deborah Pelts):** My partner grabbed him, but it was only a few seconds. This was not a vicious attack.

**Judge:** I have the vet bills here, and the wounds on Pepper indicate deep punctures. That suggests a bite force beyond just a scuffle.

**Plaintiff (Tony):** Exactly. That’s why we’re here. The vet said it was a serious bite.

**Judge:** Based on the evidence, I find in favor of the plaintiffs for the full amount of $1,364.44. The defendant’s dog was not properly restrained, and the responsibility falls on her.

**Defendant (Deborah Pelts):** That’s not fair.

**Judge:** That’s my ruling. Judgment for the plaintiffs.
Manipulation Techniques: deflection, emotional appeal, playing the victim

Dialogue:
Judge: All parties ready?

Judge: Mark, you’ve asked to be referred to only by your first name?

Plaintiff: Yes.

Judge: You are suing Rob. Okay, let’s get this straight—you own a company that does what?

Plaintiff: I own a dueling pianos company. We provide rock-and-roll live entertainment for bars and restaurants all throughout the country.

Judge: Do you actually play?

Plaintiff: I am not just the president, I’m also a performer.

Plaintiff: We bring in two keyboards or pianos, two of our roster of over 30 different performers. We do a three-hour non-stop show that’s all-request—from Billy Joel to Bon Jovi, Britney Spears, Bruno Mars, Snoop Dogg, AC/DC. People sing, they drink, they dance—it’s three hours straight every night.

Judge: Oh, God, I love it. All right. Now, you—what is your job?

Defendant: I’m the general manager of the Park Tap Grill.

Judge: Okay, now, you have done business with him many, many times at different venues, right?

Defendant: Yes, probably four or five different venues over eight years.

Judge: Okay, and so you hire him to do work at this venue?

Defendant: Yes.

Judge: And you enter into a written contract? Does anybody have a copy of that written contract?

Plaintiff: Yes.

Judge: Okay, and that written contract covers three months of performances? Was it one time a week?

Plaintiff: One time a month.

Defendant: Four months, one time per month.

Judge: Okay, is that correct?

Plaintiff: Correct, yes.

Judge: Okay, four times, once a month. And how was it going when he would come in?

Defendant: Not great.

Judge: Not great. And do you think that was their fault?

Defendant: No.

Judge: Because you keep hiring them. Do you think it just wasn’t working out, wasn’t what that audience wanted?

Defendant: Yeah, and in this line, you have to give it a few times to build momentum. You know, you never hit a home run right out of the park the first time.

Judge: Okay.

Defendant: But, you know, you build that repetition, always building it up, building it up. And then—it just wasn’t building.

Judge: Okay, so what happens is you perform in the four months of the contract, which are January through April. And what happens?

Plaintiff: At the end of the four-month term, we checked back with Rob to see if he wanted to do another date. He had told us verbally he wanted to add a show for May. We emailed him as per paragraph 7 in the contract, requesting written confirmation, which I also have, and he confirmed.

Judge: Let me see that, please.

Plaintiff: Yes, he wanted us to come in May—he wanted to do one more Thursday in May. So we had that written confirmation, which then comports that date to the rest of the contract.

Judge: What happened in May?

Plaintiff: The day of the show, we got a message that morning that said, and I’m quoting, “We won’t have anyone there to justify having the show,” and he canceled the show because it was raining. He said, “The weather is supposed to be bad all day. I don’t see the point of the show tonight.” And then he went on to say, “Cancel the show, and you can bill me.”

Judge: Let me see that, highlighted in blue.

Plaintiff: And again, as the defendant stated, we’ve done business together for many years, in many different venues. He was well acquainted with our practices and our policies.

Judge: What happened?

Defendant: Like I said, the show wasn’t going well, and the weather was horrible that week. We had been checking the weather back and forth, not just that day, but prior to that as well. I pulled a report from the weather at Lyen Airport, which is about 20 miles away. We had a half-inch of rain coming down the night of the show.

Judge: The question is—do you have to pay them? That’s the question.

Defendant: If we ended up paying him, and I had made him come down, drive an hour, set up, and sit in the corner playing for three hours by himself—

Judge: Right. But we do know when you guys had a written contract for those prior months, it specifically said all performance dates are guaranteed to occur and cannot be canceled by the purchaser for any reason.

Judge: Furthermore, it says all future performance dates—once confirmed in writing, which it was because you sent an email confirming it—are guaranteed to occur and cannot be canceled. The terms of this agreement are clear: if you want to cancel, you have to pay.

Judge: Why shouldn’t they have to pay him?

Defendant: Well, it was mutual—

Judge: Mutual? He’s already hired two people, he’s planning to go, and you’re making him lose $800. How is that mutual?

Defendant: Well, at the same time, I’d rather not lose the money if he came down, spent money, and played for no one.

Judge: But you had a contract. An oral contract is still a contract. And you breached it.

Judge: You said “Bill me.” Where I come from, “Bill me” means “Bill me.” It doesn’t mean, “Oh, I saved you a trip, so you’re going to eat this.”

Judge: I’m ordering them to pay you the $800, plus prejudgment interest since May 17th.

Plaintiff: Thank you.

Judge: Court is adjourned.

Plaintiff : You were cheating on me with another guy. You even hid him in your house when I came over.

Defendant: Well, I didn’t want to hurt you. You have a temper, and I didn’t want a fight to start.

Third Party Witness: She was cheating on him, and I caught her. She even admitted it in the messages.

Defendant: No, it’s not my fault. I didn’t want to hurt you, and this is just being blown out of proportion.

Judge: All parties, please raise your right hand. Welcome back to The People’s Court. Next case on the docket—the plaintiff was the defendant’s boyfriend, but no more. He gave her his credit card for a Lazy Boy. What do you think happened there? She says it was a present, but now that she’s seeing someone else, he’s upset. It’s the case of "You’re Way Too Lazy for Me." Thank you.

Judge: John Cerk, you are suing your former girlfriend, Joellen Reich, for $487.52, the amount that you say you loaned to her. You say he gave it as a gift, and according to you, she's refusing to repay. What's going on?

Plaintiff: Okay, sometime last summer, I think it was July or August, she purchased furniture, and it was one of those deals with little or no interest. Come December, she asked me to loan her the money to pay off the loan. She needed $300, and I was kind of reluctant. She said she'd pay me when she gets her taxes in January. So, I gave her my credit card. I get the bill—it’s for $487.52. Come January, I ask for my money, and she tells me I have to stand in line, you know.

Judge: But this isn't a stranger to you; this is somebody who you dated. How long?

Plaintiff: Oh, probably for 10 years.

Judge: 10 years? Okay, so you were together for 10 years. When did you guys break up?

Plaintiff: Oh, it's been on and off for 10 years. You know, it’s a love-hate relationship, I guess you call it.

Judge: Okay. Right now, are you on, or are you off?

Plaintiff: No, we’re off.

Judge: When was the last time you were on?

Plaintiff: December.

Judge: And who broke up with who?

Defendant: It just happened, you know.

Judge: Who broke up with who?

Defendant: He made me mad, so—

Judge: Say that again?

Defendant: I probably broke up with him.

Judge: He made you mad? How did he make you mad?

Defendant: He was just mean to me. I had just got off work, and he brought up a fight that we had the night before. I had just worked eight hours and saw him, and he was still carrying the same thing from the night before.

Judge: And you guys were living together?

Defendant: No.

Judge: Oh. Okay, so that was it? After 10 years?

Defendant: It’s always something stupid. It’s just the last straw of something.

Judge: All right. He says that he paid this off as a loan to you and that you’re supposed to pay him back. What’s your answer to that?

Defendant: No, ma’am.

Judge: Okay. Tell me your side.

Defendant: He’s helped me for 10 years. When I left my son’s father, he lost his job. I was getting child support, but when my son’s father got fired, I didn’t get child support anymore. Back in the day, John used to give me money—$500 cash. When I moved in August, he gave me his credit card so I could buy new things for the house. That was $300. He’s helped me a lot in the past 10 years and never asked me for anything back.

Judge: And out of curiosity, once you two broke up, he asked for this back. You didn’t want to pay it because you can’t pay it?

Defendant: Well, his claim said that I was supposed to pay him back in 30 days.

Judge: Did you ever tell him you’d pay him back for this?

Defendant: No, ma’am.

Judge: I understand what you’re saying—you’re trying to tell me, "Judge, he’s given me lots of gifts. This was just another gift."

Defendant: Yes.

Judge: All right. Why do you suppose he wants this one paid? What is he mad at you for that would make him say this one wasn’t a gift?

Defendant: I started dating somebody else.

Judge: Ah, that’s the part I was missing, honey. Now I get it. All right. Does this guy get his money back? What do you think? If you make a loan, you have to have it in writing. You’re not getting your money back. Fair enough—it doesn’t feel like a loan to me. Going inside the courtroom—so you are dating somebody else?

Defendant: Yes.

Judge: Are you living with somebody else?

Defendant: No.

Judge: After 10 years, you never lived with him?

Defendant: No.

Judge: Was that an issue, or is that just the way you two rolled?

Defendant: I’ve always wanted— you know, it’s lonely. I’ve been alone for 12 years.

Judge: Apparently, he thinks you rebounded a little quickly.

Defendant: Oh.

Judge: Is he suspicious of you?

Defendant: Oh, I wasn’t cheating on him.

Judge: Okay, but does he think that?

Defendant: I don’t think so.

Judge: You think it’s just that he wants the money because he’s mad that you moved on?

Defendant: Yes.

Judge: Do you have any evidence that this was a loan?

Plaintiff: I have nothing but her word.

Judge: Her word here today is directly contrary to yours.

Plaintiff: I know it is.

Judge: And we just took that oath five minutes ago to tell the truth.

Plaintiff: I’m telling the truth.

Judge: Are you telling the truth?

Defendant: Yes, ma’am.

Judge: When you come to court, it’s really important to have something other than a he said/she said because it becomes very difficult to rule in your favor when you have the burden of proof and can’t meet it. My verdict in this case, I have no choice but to rule for the defendant. Good luck.
Manipulation Techniques: gaslighting, guilt tripping, playing the victim

Dialogue:
Judge: All parties, please be seated. Thank you, Douglas. All right, Ms. Wagan, you are suing Ms. Pigage for $3,000 that you believe she owes you for malnourishing and abusing your horse. Tell me what’s going on.

Plaintiff: Back in October of 2015, I entered into a free lease with Dana and her husband. They had come to my house the first week of October to look at our horse. They agreed that they liked it; they wanted to lease it. It was a free lease—no money was involved.

Judge: So you basically leased the horse to them, and they had to take care of the horse for that amount of time, correct? Do you have a written contract with them?

Plaintiff: I do.

Judge: Let me see. Who’s the rider?

Plaintiff: Our daughter—well, my daughter.

Judge: And how old is your daughter?

Plaintiff: Thirteen.

Judge: Okay, so go ahead. What happens?

Plaintiff: In the beginning, I would go over and check on the horse, you know, the first few weeks. I would ride by once a week, and then—

Judge: How far were they from you?

Plaintiff: About eight miles away. So, you know, as time went on, we’d check on them a little less. Then it got to where I’d just go by the house maybe if I was going grocery shopping. And, you know, the first year everything went great. Last July, I had knee surgery, so I hadn’t been by the house for at least a couple of months. I couldn’t drive for six weeks. Then, um, I would say it was the end of August, early September, John had stopped by our house and said they were thinking about not using the horse anymore. That, um, the daughter wasn’t riding much and was thinking about bringing it back. So we had told him, you know, that’s fine. He asked me again if we could pick the horse up, and I said, ‘Well, I still don’t have a trailer to come get him.’ I said, ‘But you can bring him back anytime. We don’t have to be home. You can just put him in the field, and I’ll see them when I go out to feed.’ So, I didn’t hear from them. And then it was January 16th, I went by with a friend because, actually, she had a trailer and said her daughter would be interested in riding. I said, ‘Well, I think these people are done with the horse. If you can get it back to my house, your daughter can come over and ride.’ So we stopped over to look at him, and he was just skin and bones.

Judge: What’s the horse’s name?

Plaintiff: Cowboy Democracy. And he was about 400 pounds underweight. I do have photos. I brought photos of before and after.

Judge: Hold on. Who is riding?

Plaintiff: That’s—that’s me. And you can just see, he’s got a big chest. He’s not a super tall horse but a typical quarter horse, very healthy there.

Judge: What’s that?

Plaintiff: That’s a photo of the empty trash can that John—the night I found the horse and we got him back to our house. Because even though I didn’t have a trailer that was tagged or had registrations, I came home hysterical. My husband put on his shoes and said, ‘Well, go get him.’ He said, ‘I’ll drive slow.’ So we rode over and got him.

Judge: Wait, wait. Who did you talk to about getting him and everything else?

Plaintiff: Nobody. I just saw the condition and just went and got him. He wasn’t staying there.

Judge: Did you know that she was getting the horse?

Defendant: No, I knew nothing.

Judge: And how did you find out?

Defendant: I came home. I had left the house probably around 3:00 that day, went to the mall with my children. I came home probably around 5:00, and when I came home, Cowboy was gone.

Judge: So when did you get the pictures?

Defendant: I got these pictures two days after I had brought the horse home.

Judge: So you didn’t get the pictures until January?

Defendant: I didn’t get them until January.

Judge: Who got them instead of you?

Defendant: Her husband’s lawyer. He sent them to his lawyer—his divorce lawyer—because the night I got the horse—

Judge: Fighting you for custody?

Defendant: Yes.

Judge: So the premise is, if you can’t take care of a horse, you can’t take care of a kid? How old is the kid you have in common?

Defendant: Six years old.

Judge: But he didn’t send them to her, correct?

Defendant: Well, I don’t know. According to her, she got them and was told in September—

Judge: I’ve seen enough. Based on my calculations, my judgment is in the plaintiff’s favor in the amount of $1,045. Court is adjourned.

Judge: We will hear argument this morning in Case 23-1127, Wisconsin Bell versus United States ex rel. Todd Heath.

Lawyer of Defendant: Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court: The False Claims Act, which casts its shadow over every aspect of the administrative state, has always been trained on guarding the public fisc.

The FCA protects government funds by defining a claim as a request for money the government provides or that's presented to a government agent.

As a result of political branch choices, E-rate reimbursement requests check neither box.

The program could have been funded with public money and administered by a government agency, but the political branches chose private funding and a private administrator to prevent E-rate money from being used to mask budget shortfalls and to avoid the Government Corporation Control Act.

The consequence of those choices is that E-rate reimbursement requests aren't FCA claims.

First, the government doesn't provide any money in the E-rate program.

Text, context, structure, and history all confirm that the government provides money for FCA purposes only if it supplies money from its own funds, putting the public fisc at risk.

That never happened here.

The government doesn't provide money by making one private party pay another private party, and the government doesn't provide money by collecting debts owed to a private party and in which the government has no financial stake.

Second, the program's private administrator, the Administrative Company, isn't a government agent.

By design, it lacks power to bind the government, which in turn lacks control over what matters here: grants of E-rate reimbursement requests.

In fact, we know the company can't be a government agent because, if it were, it would violate the Government Corporation Control Act.

Choices have consequences, and the choices made by the political branches to insulate the E-rate program from the public fisc foreclose applying the FCA.

I welcome the Court's questions.

Judge: Ms. Ho, could you focus just briefly on the $100 million that the government says it contributes or provides? That -- much of that is collected under a debt collection provision that authorizes the government to collect its -- a debt owed to it. That seems somewhat at odds with your argument that it's not the government's money.

How could it be collected under that Act if it is not owed to the government? And if -- if it is owed to the government, then why isn't it the government's money after that?

Lawyer of Defendant: Yes, Justice Thomas, and let me answer both parts of your question.

Let me begin by addressing the debt settlement and restitution.

Those funds are no different than the E-rate contributions themselves.

They are owed to the Administrative Company.

The United States just collects and returns those funds to their private owner.

A -- a -- a good analogy is like child support.

Like a parent provides child support even when the United States withholds from the parent's income and sends to the other parent, the private telecom carriers provide E-rate funds even when the United States collects their delinquent debts, just like when a private litigant uses a sheriff to go after a property to pay judgment.

Now let -- Justice Thomas, let me address directly your question about the Debt Collection Improvement Act.

And those debts don't belong to the government, even under the Debt Collection Improvement Act, for three reasons.

Number one, that's a different statutory scheme with different language. That's the Blanca case from the Tenth Circuit that we cite in our brief.

The FCA limits a claim to money that the United States provides, and the Debt Collection Improvement Act defines "claim" more expansively without regard to its effect on the public fisc.

And, number two, relatedly, the Debt Collection Improvement Act applies to funds that the United States is authorized by statute to collect for the benefit of any person.

That is Section 3701(b)(1)(D).

So money belongs to the person for whose benefit the government collects it.

The government just holds it in trust.

And that's in our brief on page 32.

And my friend, the Solicitor General, admits on page 17 of the Solicitor General's brief that they are obligated, the government is obligated, to transmit funds back to the Administrative Company.

Judge: The -- the --

Judge: Can I ask you a question -- oh.

Sorry.

Judge: I was just -- the government, though, treats this, these funds, as appropriated funds, right? They -- they're -- as I understand it, they're called backdoor appropriations?

Lawyer of Defendant: You're correct, Mr. Chief Justice, that OMB and the President's budget have -- have labeled the E-rate funds as -- I think they're -- I think the -- the bureaucratic term is "permanent indefinite appropriations," but even -- even OMB, in using that term, admitted that -- I think -- I think the language used was that the line between the private sector and government can be murky and that it was including those funds in the budget out of a desire to be -- to be comprehensive. So, ultimately, it is this Court's responsibility to interpret this statute and determine what the nature of the funds are.

So bookkeeping labels aren't dispositive of that question, Mr. Chief Justice.

Judge: But I guess I'm wondering why the government isn't collecting the money here for a public purpose for the funding of this program.

You suggest that the money belongs to the person for whom the government collects the funds, and I thought the funds were being collected to operate this government program.

Lawyer of Defendant: So, Justice Jackson, I take your question to be focused on, you know, isn't -- isn't money that the government collects -- which, of course, we know the government isn't actually collecting the money here.

The private carriers are paying their money --

Judge: Right.

I was just responding --

Lawyer of Defendant: -- to another private person.

Judge: Yeah, I was just -- I was just trying to understand your response to Justice Thomas, who said --

Lawyer of Defendant: Yes.

Judge: -- we have a hundred million dollars that the government is collecting, admittedly, as a result of settlements and debt collection, in the context of this program, but, ultimately, the reason why the money is being collected is because the government has ordered telecom companies to fund this government program.

So the ultimate beneficiary, I would think, is the public, right?

Lawyer of Defendant: So we know, Your Honor, from the text of the False Claims Act and its definition of "claim" that just having a government purpose isn't enough and that the -- the precise language -- and this is 3729(b)(2)(A).

It defines a claim, and it says, "if the money or property is to be spent or used on the government's behalf or to advance a government program or interest," which is what you're asking me about, "and if the United States provides or has provided any portion of the money or property." So it's not enough that --

Judge: So I guess I don't understand your argument about "provides." Can you help me?

Judge: Okay, Ms. Janeski, you are suing Ms. Despo for $725, the cost that you paid for a home inspection on the sale of a home where she ended up backing out of it. Correct?

Plaintiff (Janet Janeski): What happened? Well, in early summer, I began looking for a home for my daughter.

Judge: And you were going to buy it for her?

Plaintiff (Janet Janeski): Yes.

Judge: Okay. She had just gone through a divorce.

Plaintiff (Janet Janeski): Yes. I wanted it in this specific complex, and I was taken to the defendant’s condominium that was for sale. I looked at it a couple of times. I made her an offer to purchase, a written offer to purchase. I paid the fee that goes with the binder.

Judge: And that’s a deposit on the sale?

Plaintiff (Janet Janeski): Correct.

Judge: And you signed a binder?

Plaintiff (Janet Janeski): I signed a binder, which is essentially a contract agreeing to sign a contract later.

Judge: Correct.

Plaintiff (Janet Janeski): Correct. And we negotiated back and forth for several weeks before we came up to a final price. She signed the binder back on October 4th. After that, I had the binder in hand. The only condition that I had was a home inspection and radon test. I retained a home inspector who did the home inspection.

Judge: And that person charged you $725?

Plaintiff (Janet Janeski): Correct.

Judge: Do you have the receipt for that?

Plaintiff (Janet Janeski): I do.

Judge: Can I see that?

Plaintiff (Janet Janeski): Yes, you may.

Judge: And then what happened?

Plaintiff (Janet Janeski): I assume your daughter was all excited.

Plaintiff (Janet Janeski): Yes.

Judge: Was she going to live there alone or with her two children?

Plaintiff (Janet Janeski): Her two children. My grandchildren.

Judge: And was it near you?

Plaintiff (Janet Janeski): It was very close to me.

Judge: And several days later when I got the inspection back, I called my broker and I said, "You know, I’m ready to move forward," and the inspection was alright.

Plaintiff (Janet Janeski): Yes.

Judge: And around the 18th of October, I got a call from my broker that the seller was not going to sell the condo, and she wasn’t going through with the deal. And this is what, October of last year?

Plaintiff (Janet Janeski): Yes, 2011.

Judge: What happened?

Defendant (Barbara Despo): What she said is true, Your Honor. I had the condo up for sale. I had to get out. I was a single parent. I had two teenagers living at home, so I wanted everybody out. I wanted them to become responsible, move on.

Judge: When you say teenagers, I assume you mean over 18?

Defendant (Barbara Despo): Over 18, yes.

Judge: Exactly. Get out! You 13-year-olds—how old are they?

Defendant (Barbara Despo): They needed to go. But they didn’t have their apartments. Nobody was ready to go at that point. My contingency was that the house sold as-is.

Judge: Right.

Defendant (Barbara Despo): Okay. And I asked my realtor, "Look, do I still have time to back out because my kids aren’t set? I’m not set." I had some other issues I was dealing with. But why were you backing out? You had a realtor, you negotiated, you put it up for sale, somebody bit, you negotiated, and you signed a binder. Don’t you think?

Defendant (Barbara Despo): Yep.

Judge: And then I got—I didn’t sign it.

Defendant (Barbara Despo): The original offer was made in August, what happened between August and October? There was negotiations.

Judge: Okay.

Defendant (Barbara Despo): On the price. Back and forth.

Judge: Okay, what happened?

Defendant (Barbara Despo): Then at that time I wasn’t even going to go with it.

Judge: Alright. Binders have always thrown me for a loop anyway because they’re not—it’s sort of the concept is sort of weird. However, let’s look at the binder in this case, and they are, you know, enforceable to some extent. Did you hand me the binder?

Defendant (Barbara Despo): All right.

Judge: In the binder, it says there are three conditions: one is that the contract of sale are to be signed, and the balance of the deposit is to be made before September 4th. Two was scratched out, which is financing, and three was an inspection. But was there a date—did you have like 10 days to do an inspection? How did that work?

Plaintiff (Janet Janeski): I believe it had to be done within seven days.

Judge: Yeah, all right. In any event, in every binder, the intent of the parties is that there’s going to be another contract. That this isn’t all she wrote—that there’s going to be something else. However, that doesn’t mean, if the binder means nothing, then why sign it and why call it a binder? At a minimum, it always has to mean that you’re not going to sell it to somebody else while the binder is in effect. At a minimum, it’s going to mean that you have to hand over the money the binder talks about. Well, what else does it mean?

Defendant (Barbara Despo): I believe that in the circumstances of this case, at a minimum, it means that there’s going to be some good faith sitting at a table because you’ve agreed to agree. You pulled out completely for whatever reasons that you had, and that’s fine. What I don’t understand is why you wouldn’t attempt to recompense her for the money she totally wasted by you pulling out because you know that she had a bill. Did she ever send you the bill? Did anybody ever ask you to pay for the bill, or did you just get sued?

Defendant (Barbara Despo): No. Once I backed out, my realtor called me and said, "Barbara, they’re going to sue you." So I said, “Okay, they’re going to sue me.” So I was waiting for something to come, and then the lawsuit came. Yeah, and then nothing came until my house was just sold, now, like a month ago, and then I guess when they found out I sold the house, it rekindled old memories. So they figured, okay.

Plaintiff (Janet Janeski): So then I finally got the thing in the mail. So I wasn’t—I was going to, you know, try to do the right thing, and yet you’re here.

Judge: Okay. So I find in favor of the plaintiff in the amount of the $725 plus your court costs. Thank you. Good luck.
Manipulation Techniques: framing the narrative, minimization, emotional appeal

Dialogue:
Lawyer of Plaintiff:
So let me address it with two --two points.

The first goes to what the motion below actually did, and the second goes to the sequencing Sinochem point that has been raised.

First is, what did the motion do? It starts at page 24 of the court of appeals joint appendix.

It's a motion to reopen and to vacate the arbitral award.

The only relief it requests is to vacate the arbitral award, and it does not ever request to reopen actually the existing federal claim.

Judge: Thank you, Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court: The only question presented is whether Stanley has alleged discrimination against the qualified individual under the ADA.

The answer to that question is yes.

We agree with Stanley that the most straightforward path to that conclusion lies in the period after she was diagnosed with Parkinson's disease but before she retired.

In that period, Stanley was a qualified individual with a disability.

And there's no dispute that if the alleged discrimination occurred while Stanley was both qualified and disabled, the alleged discrimination was against a qualified individual.

The Court can, and should, decide this case on that narrow basis, but, in doing so, it shouldn't foreclose the possibility of relief in other cases involving plaintiffs who were not both qualified and disabled when the disability discrimination occurred.

We believe the ADA also protects the benefits those plaintiffs earned as qualified individuals, but, because the alleged discrimination here occurred while Stanley was both qualified and disabled, this Court need not address any broader arguments to vacate the decision below.

I welcome the Court's questions.

Judge: Where did Petitioner make those arguments in the district court and in the court of appeals?

That paragraph of the complaint alleges that she became disabled before she retired and also alleges that she continued to work until she retired. We think it's a plausible inference from that paragraph that she was a qualified individual with a disability at some point before she retired.

In the court of appeals, I would look at Romanette 8 and page 10 of her opening brief, pages 4 to 13 of her reply brief, and I would listen to the first five minutes of the oral argument below, in which Stanley herself described the path I'm identifying here as "the narrowest path to a decision in her favor." And then, in this Court, I would -- I would cite the same pages my friend cited in the petition, particularly pages 7, 11, 24, and 25. I would note that the City argued forfeiture in its brief in opposition.

Presumably, this Court considered and rejected that forfeiture contention as a basis for denying review and for good reason, because this Court has said time and again that once a party has preserved an issue for this Court's consideration, that party can make any argument in support of that issue.

And the issue here is whether Stanley satisfies the qualified individual requirement.

I want to emphasize why we think it's a good idea to decide this case narrowly.

First, we think it answers the question presented.

Second, we do think it would go a long way to resolving the circuit split.

That's identified at page 17A of the petition appendix.

And the split implicates two decisions from the Sixth Circuit and the Ninth Circuit, the McKnight decision and the Weyer decision, which both held that former employees categorically cannot sue to enforce Title I.

And so, if this Court were to hold that at least someone in Stanley's circumstances can sue even though she was a former employee when she brought the suit, that would resolve that question that's divided the circuits.

Plaintiff (Ricardo):

Judge:
May I see that?

Judge:

**Plaintiff:** 18.

**Plaintiff:** I ordered a fried fish dish. It had one clam with breading on it. I took a bite of the clam, and there was a red pepper. **Plaintiff:** Actual red pepper pieces. I dug them out for her and put them in her hand. She said, "Oh, well, the chef is sorry. He made a mistake."

**Plaintiff:** She came back and said that.

**Plaintiff:** Yes.

**Defendant:** Yes.

**Defendant:** We just felt that if she had stated "all peppers," we would have—

**Judge:** Hold on. Did you state "all peppers"?

**Plaintiff:** Yes.

**Defendant:** We did our best.

Thank you, Douglas. Mr. Gordano, you are suing Mr. Fauler, your real estate agent, for $5,000, the statutory maximum, because according to you, he got you a tenant that didn’t work out.

Plaintiff: Yeah.

Judge: Okay, tell me what happened.

Plaintiff: This tenant that he guaranteed to me—he suggested I take her as a makeup tenant for the previous tenant that he got me, who destroyed my place half a year earlier. He said I should take her as a makeup because her money is guaranteed. And after five months of paying—

Judge: How is that his fault? Just because he was the broker in the transaction?

Plaintiff: Well, he got me the tenant. He said it was a great young couple guaranteed by the boss, the former landlord. I said okay, and a couple of months later, they were fighting and destroyed my place.

Judge: But how is that his fault?

Judge: Okay, but who pays his fee? You or the tenant?

Plaintiff: I think the tenant does.

Judge: Okay. So then he gets you this tenant in question, and this tenant was somebody who was leaving Section 8, correct? Had you ever dealt with Section 8 before?

Plaintiff: No, but I had heard that they’re good to deal with because their money is guaranteed.

Judge: Well, they could be great. They could be a terrible pain. It just kind of depends. They have to approve it beforehand. If something’s not to their liking, they won’t pay the rent, etc. So you enter into an agreement with both her and Section 8, right?

Plaintiff: Yes.

Judge: And then what happens?

Plaintiff: I prepared the place for this second tenant. It passed inspection, she moved in, and she paid for five months. Then all of a sudden, Section 8 cut off her assistance.

Judge: Why?

Plaintiff: They said they discovered that she has other income, so they stopped paying for her.

Judge: So did she get a job and her circumstances changed?

Plaintiff: She said she got a second job.

Judge: So when they found out she got a second job, they said, ‘You can pay your rent, and we don’t need to help you. There are other people we have to help.’ So they stopped paying. What’s the agreement that you, as a landlord, enter into with Section 8? That they have to pay for a whole year?

Plaintiff: Well, they said that they are—

Judge: Let me see your Section 8 paperwork.

Plaintiff: (Hands document to judge)

Judge: Oh, well, this is a notice of ender payment change or termination. They reduced her payments.

Plaintiff: Yes, they reduced her payments, and so she was supposed to be paying more of the rent.

Judge: And then she said, ‘I can’t afford this place.’ Did she move out?

Plaintiff: No, she squatted for four months. It’s on the second floor of my house, so I observed and heard people coming in and socializing in my second floor as they were squatting. I said, ‘When are you going to pay the rent?’ She said, ‘I’ll pay the rent as soon as I can.’

Judge: Is she still there? Why don’t you just evict her?

Plaintiff: I did. It cost me the sheriff’s eviction expense.

Judge: That’s what everybody has to do. All of us mere mortals have to go to landlord-tenant court.

Plaintiff: I did. I went through the process of evicting her, and that took four months.

Judge: Oh, but you got her out now?

Plaintiff: Yes.

Judge: Okay, that was my question. You’re not helpless. So how do you figure that Mr. Fauler owes you a guarantee on a tenant that he gets you?

Defendant: I never personally guaranteed anything.

Judge: Did you act as a personal guarantor on a tenant for this gentleman?

Defendant: No, your honor.

Judge: Have you ever guaranteed a tenant’s payment?

Defendant: No, your honor.

Judge: Then why does he think that you did?

Defendant: I don’t know, your honor.

Judge: So, Mr. Gordano, you’re saying that because the defendant said ‘Section 8 means guaranteed money,’ you took that as a personal guarantee from him?

Plaintiff: Yes.

Judge: That’s not how it works. Verdict for the defendant.
Manipulation Techniques: persuasion, framing the narrative, playing the victim

No.

So, you’re both claiming you don’t know what’s owed. This is where things get confusing. You didn’t have a contract, you didn’t discuss a price, and now you’re both here arguing about what’s owed.

Defendant (Shawn Davenport):
Yes, it’s been a mess.

Judge:
In this case, the plaintiff has the burden of proof, but there’s no solid evidence for what was agreed upon. It’s very difficult to determine what’s owed or if anything is owed at all.

Judge (to Plaintiff):
You’re the one suing, so it’s up to you to prove that the defendants owe you money. However, you’ve failed to provide proof that they owe you more than what they’ve already paid.

Judge (to Defendant):
You’ve also failed to prove that you paid the full amount. You can’t just hand over cash without a receipt or documentation.

Judge:
The lack of documentation and the informal nature of your agreement makes this case very difficult to resolve. I’m ruling in favor of the defendants.
