Judge: So you're saying there are services available, there's treatment available, so people would ultimately move off the street? Is that what you're saying? Because I think part of the premise of all of this, right, is that there are not enough beds for homeless people to occupy, and so there will be a mismatch, and there are going to be some people who can't be cared for.
Are you saying that if your law is enforced, there is a way for everyone to be cared for?

Defendant's Lawyer: No.
I'm saying that's a policy question that is quite difficult, but these laws are an important part of the puzzle. They're not the only solution.
And we don't believe that they are, but we think they're an important tool.
And without them, we've seen what's happened on our streets. We've seen that people are dying in encampments.
We've seen that cities are being forced to cede all of their public spaces.
So that ultimate question is for the legislature and policymakers to figure out what the right solution, what the right mix of policies is.
But the wrong answer is to do what the lower court did here and to constitutionalize—

Judge: Okay.
Let me interrupt you there.
You're right, it's a very, very difficult policy question.
And I asked you before about whether this was just about blankets or whether it went into having fires or urinating and defecating outdoors and that sort of thing, and another judge pointed out that this particular injunction did carve out those things and was just talking about sleep.
But, you know, other cases have been litigated in the lower court that have gone beyond that, and because the line is things that are involuntary, that are human needs, it can extend—it's difficult to draw the line, and whatever we decide here about this case is about the line.
So can you describe for me some of the things that are difficult to figure out about the line? There's sleeping.
There's sleeping with blankets.
What else?

Defendant's Lawyer: Public urination and defecation, that is a serious problem.
Those are parts of biological necessities of being human.
A court in another jurisdiction addressed that, and the lower court's opinions debated whether its rule would actually reach those things.
I think any rule that we are wondering about and debating whether it would go that far, I think that is a sign that it is not a workable rule.
The slippery slope here is very real. It's not just for camping and conduct that might be a biological necessity, putting aside tents and fires and cold climates.
What other things would be allowed? All of the things that a human needs to survive, for example, potentially come into focus under the lower court’s rule but also in other areas.
Someone could say that my drug use or possession is the other side of the coin because I'm an addict or because a person who violates other laws could say that I had a compulsion to do those things that I couldn't control.
And the plurality opinion in a prior case addressed that very thing and why it's so important to draw the line there.
And when conduct is involved and once the Court gets into deciding which conduct may be excused under the Constitution, it is so far afield of what the Constitution was ever understood to address.

Judge: Okay.
Speaking of status and conduct, you've argued that a previous ruling was wrong and we don't need to overrule it.
And I agree.
I don't think we should overrule it.
You've also been kind of resisting characterizing anything other than the drug addiction that was at issue in that case as status.
So what if the law said it is unlawful and punishable by 30 days in prison to have the status of homelessness? Just go with me.
Just assume that the law defines homelessness as a status and it is a status.
Would that ruling say that law is unconstitutional under the Constitution? Would you concede that?

Defendant's Lawyer: And you're saying that that is a status?

Judge: Yes.

Defendant's Lawyer: Well, yes, and I think it looks a lot like that prior ruling under that hypothetical, but, of course, we disagree that it is—

Judge: I understand you disagree—

Defendant's Lawyer: —a status in that way.

Judge: —but you are accepting that the prior ruling draws a distinction between status and conduct, and you're just fighting about the definition of a status?

Defendant's Lawyer: It draws the line where a law has no actus reus.
So I think that's the easiest line.
I don’t defend the line under the Constitution because I don't think actually that the Court—I know the Court didn't rely on any Constitutional principles or history of—

Judge: But the hypothetical I just gave you had no actus reus either.
The status of homelessness, I mean, it could be, you know, 4:00 in the afternoon and the person is just standing outside the bus stop.
Do you agree that if the law prohibited that, made that a crime, that under the prior ruling, whether it was right or wrong, that under that ruling, that would be a violation of the Constitution?

Defendant's Lawyer: Well, I think the better framework is due process.

Judge: I understand that. Under that prior ruling, do you agree that that would be wrong?

Defendant's Lawyer: Yes.

Judge: Okay. Thank you.

Defendant's Lawyer: Thank you.

Judge: Justice, would you like to ask questions?

Judge: So picking up where the previous question left off, you say that the ordinance here pertains to conduct and not to status, and I'm just trying to figure that out.
I'm not so sure for this reason. It's because all humans engage in the act in question, sleeping.
And yet the statute operates or the ordinance operates to penalize only certain individuals, those who have no choice but to do that act in public.
So it appears, I think, not to be the act that the state or the city in this case finds criminally culpable.
It's instead the act as engaged in by certain people, by people who cannot afford housing and have nowhere else to go.
So why is that the wrong way to think about it? And if that is the right way to think about it, why isn't that a status crime in the way that previous rulings contemplate?

Defendant's Lawyer: It's not because we can look at the law and it has a conduct element.
The conduct is establishing a place—a campsite.
And that is something that a person who has a home or a shelter could do as well.

Judge: But you've just defined away the basic actus reus, right? The actus reus is sleeping out—I guess outside to the extent you put outside in it, but that's the problem I'm talking about.
The actus reus is the sleeping, right? Everybody—that's not a criminally culpable kind of activity.
That's what I think might distinguish it from prior cases and make it worse for you in a way because, in prior cases, at least, to the extent someone had a disease, and the question was, well, are they engaging in otherwise criminally culpable conduct, buying and selling drugs, taking drugs, you know, we look at that kind of category of things.
Here, the actus reus is sleeping, human, universal.
The city adds, okay, but you can't sleep outside.
And I guess what I'm trying to understand is, to the extent that that only happens with respect to a certain category of people who have no other place to go, why isn't that really just punishing the status of being someone who doesn't have any place to go?

Defendant's Lawyer: It doesn't apply only to those people...

Judge: Thank you.

Judge: Thank you, counsel

We'll hear argument first this morning in Case 23-929, Velazquez versus Garland.

Lawyer of Defendant:
Thank you, Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court: The 60-day time period in the voluntary departure statute works like any routine legal time period.

When the last day falls on a weekend or holiday, the period continues to run until the next business day.

Congress would have expected people to read the statute this way.

In 1996, common law principles, case law, established rules and regulations, and years of consistent practice all pointed to that interpretation.

If Congress had meant to deviate from that traditional understanding, it would have said so.

Even the government now acknowledges that other deadlines in the same section of IIRIRA follow the traditional rule in light of a long-standing immigration regulation defining the word "day." So the only way to adopt the government's interpretation is to believe that Congress used two different meanings of the word "day" in the same section of the same statute.

There's simply no indication that Congress took that kind of split approach.

The government's interpretation would also spell chaos for regulatory definitions and deadlines.

In the government's view, even though Section 1001.1(h) provides a single definition for the word "day," that -- the immigration regulations actually use that term to mean different things throughout.

And so the only way to tell which deadline follows which time calculation rule is to trace each deadline's history back through earlier and earlier versions of the code and Code of Federal Regulations.

Answering a question as simple as does my deadline fall on Saturday or Monday shouldn't depend on this kind of complex historical investigation, especially when deportation's on the line.

The default rule for weekends and holidays exists precisely to avoid this kind of case-by-case guesswork. The government identifies no reason and certainly no reason grounded in the text of the statute to depart from that traditional rule.

The Court should read Section 1229c like any other statute and follow the usual rule for weekends and holidays.

Judge: Wow, you’re very relaxed. Go on.

Plaintiff: He said something about hitting his head.

Judge: You—hitting his head? Yes. What happened?

Defendant: No, I—I banged my head March 3rd, prior to his last lesson. So, yes, I was out of the pool for the first two pool sessions. I was present at the pool session, had another instructor do his work with him in a pool.

Judge: Okay. How many times did he schedule ocean—the ocean part that weather didn’t permit?

Defendant: Twice.

Judge: Okay. So the last time—

Judge: And both times, were you present?

Defendant: Yes.

Defendant: Correct.

Judge: Okay. And was he disappointed?

Defendant: Yes.

Judge: Okay. And how—what were the days that we’re talking about? Do you know the days? Does anybody write anything down?

Plaintiff: The 28th, then the 3rd.

Plaintiff: Okay. Went back to the store. Mike wasn’t there. They told me his wife was sick.

Judge: Back to the same thing—phone calls.

Plaintiff: I went to the store.

Judge: So are you telling me that you had ocean dives scheduled, and that you would show up for them, and Mike just wouldn’t show up, wouldn’t call you, wouldn’t cancel?

Plaintiff: Exactly.

Judge: Do you have any proof of that?

Plaintiff: Uh, no.

Judge: Okay, because he runs a dive shop, and I assume you are not the only diver there, correct?

Plaintiff: Correct.

Judge: Are there any ocean dives that you just didn’t show up for?

Defendant: No.

Judge: Now, it becomes obvious that before his trip—when was the last ocean dive appointment?

Defendant: Uh, the 3rd.

Judge: What did you do between the 3rd and the—you went to Mexico when?

Plaintiff: The 13th of May.

Judge: Okay, that’s ten more days. You can do an ocean dive. Why didn’t you schedule it?

Plaintiff: Well, when I went back to the store on the 10th, I told him, "Mike, I’m leaving the 13th. Whatever’s got to happen from today to Saturday can—"

Judge: And today is what? What day do you call?

Plaintiff: Thursday.

Judge: You tell him on Thursday, "We need to have two dives between today and Saturday." And what happened?

Plaintiff: That’s when he pulls out these papers. "With this, you go to Mexico, and with, uh, this other proof of your classes, you will have no problem."
Manipulation Techniques: persuasion, evasion, framing the narrative, dismissal

Judge: The whole family, I think, was meant to be covered by the life insurance policy, but I'm not sure why the company's net worth should drop by half. The purpose of the life insurance policy was to prevent that, right?

Defendant's Lawyer: I think that’s what the parties intended, but intent doesn’t govern here.

Judge: I understand, but it seems odd that the company is suddenly worth 50 percent of what it was the day before his death, even though there was a life insurance policy in place to cover the redemption.

Defendant's Lawyer: Right. Two responses to that. First, this is exactly what a redemption is supposed to do—one shareholder receives cash for their shares, and the other maintains control of a smaller company. Second, if your concern is that this isn’t what the parties intended, you’re correct. What they wanted was for Crown to remain a $3.86 million enterprise while providing $3 million to Michael. That results in a $6.86 million valuation for estate tax purposes because Michael owned that value. His percentage stake determines the fair market value of his shares. This aligns with the petitioner's concerns about continuity of ownership.

There are many ways to ensure continuity in a closely held corporation, but what you can’t do is have $6.86 million in corporate assets, use life insurance proceeds to pay out $3 million to one shareholder, keep the company valued at $3.86 million, and then claim for estate tax purposes that the company wasn’t worth the full $6.86 million.

Judge: What do we—

Judge: Do you—

Judge: Sorry, go ahead.

Judge: Do you dispute the claim that this is a common way for family corporations to maintain continuity of operations? If so, how long has the government overlooked this supposed loophole?

Defendant's Lawyer: That’s not our understanding. We know of only three reported cases—Blount and Cartwright from 1999 and 2005, and this case. That’s it.

We also asked IRS examiners whether they see this often in estate tax returns. They couldn’t find any cases like this. That doesn’t conclusively prove it’s uncommon, but tax advisors likely consider the risks. On one hand, the Ninth and Eleventh Circuits ruled in favor of this structure with minimal reasoning. On the other hand, the Tax Court in Blount I explained clearly why this method is problematic: it subtracts the value of the very thing being priced when determining its value.

Judge: So the Ninth and Eleventh Circuits ruled in favor of this?

Defendant's Lawyer: Yes.

Judge: That would suggest it is a common way for family corporations to maintain continuity.

Defendant's Lawyer: Possibly, but this case comes from the Eighth Circuit. It’s likely that tax advisors in the Ninth and Eleventh Circuits felt safer using this method. However, there are alternative structures—like cross-insurance agreements or trusts—that ensure the life insurance proceeds don’t enter the corporation.

The issue with Blount and Cartwright is that they assume money can enter a corporation without affecting share valuation. There’s no reasoning behind treating the redemption obligation as a liability.

Judge: Well—

Defendant's Lawyer: The assumption is that since the money goes out quickly, it shouldn’t count.

Judge: Exactly.

Defendant's Lawyer: That may seem intuitive, but it’s incorrect. If the money went to a creditor, it would reduce the corporation’s net worth, and therefore, the shareholders’ value. We agree with that. But here, the money went to Michael’s equity stake—he was cashed out. That’s not a debt owed to creditors; it’s an exchange of shares for cash. This does not reduce the company's net worth.

Judge: If the IRS doesn’t see many cases like this, what do families typically do instead?

Defendant's Lawyer: The more common approach is a cross-purchase arrangement. In that setup, two brothers would take out life insurance on each other, keeping the proceeds outside the corporation. This allows one brother to buy out the other without affecting the company’s valuation, avoiding the issues we’re discussing here.

Judge: And the reason companies don’t prefer that method is because, in this case, the corporation is paying the premiums?

Defendant's Lawyer: The two methods are not identical in economic impact. We’re not arguing that this is just a formality. They are structurally different transactions.

Judge: Thank you, Douglas. Michael Miller and Brandy Miller are represented here by Michael Miller. You are suing Kenny Harris and Lake Hamilton Realty Incorporated, represented here by Kenny Harris, for $1,635, comprising the security deposit you believe you're entitled to, plus unused rent, and lost wages in having to come and sue for your security deposit back. Tell me what's going on.

Plaintiff: Well, originally, we had moved to Hot Springs in July 2017, and we had a lease. Since I’m a teacher and had some possibilities of going back into coaching, I needed to be able to get out of the lease in the summer months in July. Okay, and so there are two clauses for termination of the lease. One of them is the tenant reserves the right to give 30-day notice to vacate the home before this lease is expired if we can rent the home immediately, no smoking in the home. And then the other way someone can terminate the lease is for the sale of the property, and this clause says, in the event the property is sold during the period of this residential lease, the tenant hereby agrees to vacate within 30 days' notice by the owner.

Judge: Alright, so what happened?

Plaintiff: So, in June, my wife contacted Heather about—

Judge: Your wife?

Plaintiff: Yes, contacted Heather, who was the actual agent, and said that we had found a place we were looking into, to get out and—

Judge: Listen to me, I’ve read the text. It’s way better for you than that. Hand me the text, please. Do you have it in your phone? I like to see text in a phone. What happens is, she starts communicating with you, yes?

Plaintiff: Yes.

Judge: And she says, “Hey, how are you? Would it be possible if we could get out of our lease if we found something a little bigger? Was just wondering. I know we have like five months left.” You say, “Let me ask them.” They say, “We absolutely love the house. We just want a little bit more room. If not, we will stay until our lease is up. We’re just curious.” You say, “Okay, I think they’re going to try to sell or rent it since you are wanting to find something bigger. Sounds like an okay for them to get out of their lease.” I mean, it sounds to the lay person like that we’re supposed to go look at a house Monday. I hope they’re not upset because your wife is so nice and she says they aren’t. I’ll come by around one Tuesday if that’ll work for you.

Plaintiff: Yes, ma’am, that’ll be fine. Thank you for understanding. You’re welcome. You guys have been so nice to us. Thank you for... I don’t know why I keep reading it in a Southern accent. Where are you guys from?

Defendant: Arkansas.

Judge: That must be it. Thank you. I hope we find something. How long do we have to be out? You answer, “They haven’t said you can stay for the remainder of your lease if you choose to do so. Just let me know when you find something. We will work out the time frame with you.” That, if what you meant to say, was, “You may lose your deposit. It depends on how fast we can rent it or something.” That would be a really good time to say that, rather than making it sound like you’re giving them permission.

Plaintiff: Now, you ended up selling the house, right?

Defendant: Yes, another agent.

Judge: So the house gets sold, and I take it the owner was happy about that because they wanted to sell the house.

Defendant: Yes.

Judge: And the closing date on that house is when?

Defendant: It was August 18th, I think.

Judge: Yes? Okay. But you’re wanting to keep his deposit for August rent when you couldn’t even give him August to live in for those two weeks?

Defendant: There was nothing said that he had to move because the people were purchasing the home.

Judge: No, I understand that. Okay, according to you, you think your lease gives you a right to do what?

Defendant: Once they notified me of the sale, I had 30 days to move out according to the lease.

Judge: You guys feel that he’s not entitled to it back because what?

Defendant: He did not give written notice terminating the lease.

Judge: So according to you, he can’t call his 30 days because he didn’t do it in writing?

Defendant: I know written notice is written notice to your address, right?

Judge: But now what about these texts that I’ve read out loud?

Defendant: I’ve all been through. Thank you so much. Yes, that’ll be fine, and all that stuff because it’s not like they said, “Hey, we’re leaving,” and you go, “Okay.” I never call that acquiescence.

Judge: Okay, is an alternative to shooting someone in the head. Like, what are you supposed to do to show your objection to them leaving? I get that, but this is different. This is permission to leave from her.

Plaintiff: What do we do with that?

Judge: Why are you looking at her? She’s just nice. So the landlord kind of says formally, “Yeah, of course you’re going to move out early.” Does that cancel out the lease, saying that you have to give 30 days' notice if the lease required written notice?

Defendant: He says it.

Judge: Then he—the lease is the lease, and is the lease the lease, or does what the landlord says control the lease?

Defendant: It’s the lease.

Judge: The landlord can change his mind. It’s only your word against his.

Plaintiff: Well, if he agrees, he said it. That’s the question going inside the courtroom. You end up finding a place and letting them know that you found a place when? Around June 26?

Plaintiff: Yes, we just got approved for the house they called you about. We really like that one too. You can advertise it today if you want, but I just had a call from the realtor that showed the other day, and she’s sending me an offer. We may be able to work out a deal with someone to buy it.

Judge: Okay, it’s a nice house. I knew it wouldn’t take long for someone to buy or rent it. So now, June 28th. Mike, that’s you, is going to pay the rent, so it’ll be paid for next month.

Plaintiff: Okay, thank you.

Judge: On June 29th, you say, “Hey, they accepted the offer. Yeah, we were going to give 30-day notice, but as far as I... this wasn’t terminated by me, it’s terminated by the owners when they sold it.”

Plaintiff: So I think it was terminated by you too. I mean, when you tell them in June, we got approved for the house, but the house sold, so you know, hey, everybody wins. It’s all good. You know?

Defendant: Oh, goodness, do we have to pay August rent?

Plaintiff: No, the owner said that you won’t have to pay August rent, but the deposit will be forfeited because of early termination on the lease.

Plaintiff: Oh, okay. Your wife says, “I thought as long as someone was in before it was time for us to pay rent again, and the house was like it was before, then we would get our deposit back. I know the lease was broken, but it worked out perfect for them. They were able to sell it. I’ll talk to them about it. I’m ready to end anything further.”

Judge: Anything further from you?

Defendant: No.

Judge: Alright, you know, I think it’s a tad close, but I don’t feel any doubt about what I’m deciding, and I’m deciding that the security deposit goes back to him. I think that the tenor of the conversations between everybody is that, you know, what, go ahead and look for another place, because we’re going to sell it, which implies that we don’t need you here anymore. If we’re going to sell it, even if that turned out to not be the case, the language between the two parties was not “put your notice in writing like the thing requires in order to kick off the 30 days.” She asked before she even starts looking, “Am I going to be able to break the lease?” It doesn’t sound like “Yeah, but there’ll be penalties.” It sounds like “Yeah, no worries.” So I’m not going to give them a worry. You don’t get the portion of your unused rent just because you moved out early in July. You don’t get lost wages, but you do get that $1,200 and your court costs in order to get those $1,200. Verdict for the plaintiff.

Judge: Maria scarcella, yes you are suing John. You've asked to be referred to only by your first name and not to mention your company name. You're suing John's company for $3,500, the estimate that you have to redo a sidewalk that you say he did in a faulty fashion. Tell me what happened.
Plaintiff: Yes, um basically my brother had hired John to do pavers, a sidewalk, um other brick work around the house but my brother unexpectedly passed away in March of 2008.
Judge: I'm sorry to hear that.
Plaintiff: Thank you. Um so I put the job on hold till about 8-9 months later when I was ready to revisit the job. Um in the interim my mom and I moved into his house and um I wanted to execute his, my brother's wishes so I contacted John and um we started the work December of '08.
Judge: And what was the work?
Plaintiff: We had to do the whole entire driveway. We had to do some work in the back, fix a waterproof, do some stuff around the house.
Judge: No, I, I, if I want to know what the work is, things like "stuff around the house" "some work in the back" is not an answer. I want to know because it was kind of, it was a very good contract for you right? How much was this?
Plaintiff: Uh close to 42,000.
Judge: 42,000. So I'm trying to understand what the work was exactly. What was the work?
Plaintiff: We had to do a whole new sidewalk curb, uh how many feet of sidewalk about uh 40 ft. 40 by 4. Then we had to build a ramp with a new set of stairs. We had a uh fix old staircase, repoint it. We also had to repoint the whole front of the house, waterproof it. We also did some Cobblestone work which is a natural uh Stone pretty much all around.
Judge: Picture of it? Um I have a picture of the um...
Plaintiff: No, of the sidewalks in question.
Judge: I'm just curious if a picture of the other one.
Plaintiff: Yeah no. I have some most of the sidewalk but mainly from you know I have a piece of the driver.
Judge: All right so the work is completed back in sometime in '08-'09 and um when is the first inkling that you have that there's something wrong and that you want him to readdress it?
Plaintiff: Um that was the year we had really bad snowstorms. It was like snowstorm after snowstorm so spring of '09 when everything melted I noticed the sidewalk was chipping and everything else was fine and I'm content with the other work. Judge: Can I go back to the previous question and ask for a point of clarification? I understood Texas law to provide a cause of action for vitiating the federal Fifth Amendment right. I took your answer to be saying that Texas courts define a taking for purposes of the Texas Constitution. Am I wrong in thinking that Texas allows you to bring a state cause of action for the federal Fifth Amendment claim?

Plaintiff's Lawyer: I'm not sure whether that's right, to be honest. And I think two things flow from this. One, if it's true that there is a Texas common law cause of action under which we could vindicate our Fifth Amendment rights, then the Fifth Circuit still has to be reversed because it held that the substantive claim should be dismissed on the merits.

Judge: Okay. Let me just clarify this procedural point. Does Texas provide a state cause of action to vitiate the state takings right from the Texas Constitution?

Plaintiff's Lawyer: Yes.

Judge: Okay. Then it seems that Texas can't discriminate against the federal claim anyway.

Plaintiff's Lawyer: I think that's true, but Texas isn't trying to discriminate against this federal claim. What Texas says, like other state courts, is that it doesn’t create a cause of action through common law analysis. Instead, it conducts constitutional analysis. Texas courts cite the Fifth Amendment and recognize it as creating an obligation. Frequently, they reference prior cases that confirm this obligation.

Plaintiff's Lawyer: As I understand Texas's argument, the complaint we filed in state court was valid and could have been adjudicated under the Fifth Amendment. But once it was removed, Texas moved to dismiss and sought an interlocutory appeal, which extinguished that claim. My concern is that if Texas's argument is adopted, it will send a message to state courts that the Fifth Amendment does not require a remedy, eliminating the federal takings remedy in state courts across the country.

Judge: The language of the Takings Clause is quite similar to the Due Process Clause in the Fifth Amendment, which immediately precedes it. "No person shall be deprived of life, liberty, or property without due process of law, nor shall private property be taken for public use without just compensation." Why should they be read differently with respect to the creation of a cause of action?

Plaintiff's Lawyer: I don't think they have to be read differently. If there's an ongoing due process violation, a plaintiff could bring an action similar to Ex parte Young.

Judge: Not an Ex parte Young action, but a claim for damages?

Plaintiff's Lawyer: Well, I think that’s the difference here. We're not seeking damages; we're seeking just compensation. We're not saying there was a past completed violation of the Constitution that needs to be offset. We're saying that the government has taken property, which creates a present duty to pay just compensation, and we want that obligation enforced. The alternative view, that only injunctions are available, does not align with either the text of the Fifth Amendment or historical interpretations.

Plaintiff's Lawyer: For example, early legal commentators discussed the Takings Clause in terms of the military seizing property. The understanding was not that you could stop the government from taking property, but rather that there was an ongoing duty to provide compensation. Historical court decisions, such as those from the Massachusetts Supreme Court, reflect this understanding as well.

Judge: If the Fifth Amendment confers a right to sue for just compensation in and of itself, is that right unqualified? If not, what qualifications do you recognize?

Plaintiff's Lawyer: I certainly don't think it's unqualified.

Judge: What qualifications do you recognize?

Plaintiff's Lawyer: It requires a court of competent jurisdiction. Congress is free to channel jurisdiction as it sees fit, and Texas is similarly free to establish jurisdiction through its courts. But the core argument is that there is an underlying entitlement to just compensation, and when that entitlement is denied, a court of competent jurisdiction can order that compensation be paid.

Judge: Does it make sense to view the Fifth Amendment as providing a right to sue for compensation, but your ability to vindicate that right depends entirely on Congress's discretionary choice to create lower federal courts and grant them jurisdiction? That sounds like a very weak right if it is subject to such limitations.

Plaintiff's Lawyer: The same could be said of the entire Bill of Rights. All federal constitutional rights depend on Congress to create lower federal courts where these rights can be vindicated.

Judge: Justice Sotomayor?

Plaintiff's Lawyer: That gets to my question, which is Justice -- the Chief Justice asked you whether or not we've ever had a case where pure ownership was at issue and not speech.

And I don't think we've had one like that, you're right, but I don't think that your question --that the question gets to the essence of your argument, is it? The essence of your argument is you're being asked to divest because of speech, correct?

Defendant's Lawyer: Correct.

Plaintiff's Lawyer: All right.

So, if I get past that, if I go to Justice Alito's point, which is I don't think it's just about speech, it's about data control --

Defendant's Lawyer: Mm-hmm.

Plaintiff's Lawyer: Just on the data collection interest, I think Congress and the President were concerned that China was accessing information about millions of Americans, tens of millions of Americans, including teenagers, people in their 20s, that they would use that information over time to develop spies, to turn people, to blackmail people, people who, a generation from now, will be working in the FBI or the CIA or in the State Department.

Judge:
So let me address it with two --two points.

The first goes to what the motion below actually did, and the second goes to the sequencing Sinochem point that has been raised.

First is, what did the motion do? It starts at page 24 of the court of appeals joint appendix.

It's a motion to reopen and to vacate the arbitral award.

The only relief it requests is to vacate the arbitral award, and it does not ever request to reopen actually the existing federal claim.

And the reason we know that is the only other aspect of relief it requests is to appoint a new arbitrator to adjudicate that claim for relief. So the only thing the motion does is to seek to vacate the arbitral award.

It's seeking to use Rule 60(b) as the jurisdictional hook to do that, something that the case itself could not do.

So that's -- that's the first point: What does the motion do? The district court then breaks it apart into two separate orders.

It says: I'm reopening under 60(1) -- 60(b)(1) and (6).

And then it says under the FAA, later, I'm going to vacate the arbitral award.

So you have -- the only request that's ever been made is to vacate the arbitral award. That's the relief that requires subject matter jurisdiction.

The relief that's being requested requires its own form of subject -- its own basis for subject matter jurisdiction.

The only basis presented is Rule 60.

That is not sufficient under Kokkonen.

Lawyer of Defendant:
Second, the sequencing point.

The court of appeals refers to this as a jurisdictional issue, but I don't think that's correct.

We know that because Rule 80 -- under Rule 82, the availability of Rule 60 relief could not expand or contract the district court's statutory jurisdiction.

When the court of appeals was referring to subject matter jurisdiction, what it really was referring to was the long history that -- that a court could not -- lacked power to set aside or otherwise interfere with a plaintiff's voluntary dismissal.

It used a sloppy label in calling it subject matter jurisdiction.

But the availability of Section 60 relief really is like the question in Steel Company whether a plaintiff's particular claim is available under the statute.

So we don't think Sinochem's sequencing actually is available here.

It's more like Steel Company, where the jurisdictional question has to come first.

And there's simply no answer to the fact that --

Judge:
But I don't --

Lawyer of Defendant:
-- the only basis --

Judge:
-- understand -- I don't understand that at all.

I mean, after --I appreciate that the request seemed to merge the two.

But, once the district court broke them apart and issued two separate orders and the rest of the way there was a challenge as to whether or not the district court properly granted a motion to reopen, and that was the thing that this Court took cert on, I don't understand why it necessitates us to care about whether the district court was right with respect to its other order.

Lawyer of Defendant:
I -- Justice Jackson, it's because the motion to reopen had sought no relief on a claim.

Judge:
I understand --I understand what it sought.

What I'm suggesting is that that claim could never have been responded to by the district court until it had the ability to do something.

And so, as a threshold matter, the district court said this is a closed case.

Lawyer of Defendant:
It --

Judge:
I would have to reopen it to give you the relief that you seek.

I'm issuing Order Number 1 reopening the case, and then here's Order Number 2 regarding the relief that you seek.

And from then on, we all were focused on whether or not the district court made a mistake in determining that this case could even be reopened. And, to the extent that that's the issue, that that's what was presented here, that that' s what the courts have split on, I don't understand why we can't just isolate that as requested and answer it.

Lawyer of Defendant:
I'm not afraid of the question presented, but I -- to -- the -- the jurisdictional point is --is important here.

If the motion had requested some form of relief related to the case itself, if it had been I want to relitigate my case and vacate the arbitral award, then at least there would be a request for relief that -- over which the district court originally had subject matter jurisdiction.

Judge:
But, Mr. McGill, on the first one, as I understand the common law history, courts had -- were considered to have jurisdiction over orders, even final ones, judgments, until the expiration of the term of the court, right?

Lawyer of Defendant:
Except with respect to voluntary non-suits, Your Honor.

Judge:
Correct.

Judge: We'll hear argument this morning in Case 22-6389, Brown versus United States, and the consolidated case. Counsel for the Defendant.

Defendant's Lawyer: Your Honor, may it please the Court: Once more we confront the Armed Career Criminal Act, this time with regard to which drug schedules a sentencing court is to consult in order to determine whether a prior state drug crime is a match with those federal schedules and, thus, either is or is not a predicate under the ACCA. We submit that the sentencing court should use the schedules that are current at the time of sentencing.

That is because, at its core, the ACCA is a sentencing enhancement. It is not a crime unto itself.

And this Court has said that the ordinary practice is to apply current law, including at sentencing. There's no reason to deviate from that ordinary practice here.

The statute is phrased in uniformly present terms.

The goal of the ACCA is to incapacitate only the most serious offenders.

And, finally, to do otherwise, as the government suggests, would be to ignore entirely Congress's choice to change those drug schedules with the 2018 Farm Bill. With that, I invite the Court's questions.

Judge: Counsel, didn't we say in McNeill that looking at the statute is a backward-looking exercise?

Defendant's Lawyer: You certainly did, Your Honor.

And McNeill, however, is actually a complement in some sense for this case, not a barrier. The reason why I say that is that McNeill looked at the historical facts of the state crime.

We are now engaged in the present-tense effort to figure out what the federal sentence should be, including a potential ACCA mandatory minimum enhancement. McNeill acknowledged, as I just said, that the statute is phrased in the present tense, but McNeill found a particular problem, and that was that if a state reformulates its criminal laws—different prior state crimes could disappear because a court couldn't figure out under the new formulations what the maximum sentence would be.

Judge: Let me ask you a question, and then I'll let you go. But what if, just say, using your logic and your approach, there was a crime—a state offense that was not on the schedule, it was not included on the controlled substance schedule, but then, subsequently, after the commission of the state crime but before sentencing, it's added? How would that work under your logic or your approach?

Defendant's Lawyer: Well, if the government tried to make that a match, I think the defendant might have the opportunity to claim that that was an ex post facto application of the law.

In other words, it wasn't a match at the time of the offense, but it is now a match at sentencing. And we would say under our approach that, yes, it is a match, but the Ex Post Facto Clause would be a barrier to applying the newer drug schedules there.

And, there, you would shift back to the drug schedules that apply at the time of the federal offense, the 922(g) offense.

Judge: Isn't that an argument why your reading is strained? You're building in an ex post facto problem.

Defendant's Lawyer: Well, respectfully, Your Honor, we're not building in an ex post facto problem because there already is an ex post facto problem.

In other words, we're not avoiding a constitutional question here.

This Court decided in Peugh that if, after the commission of the federal crime, the sentencing range shifted upward, that would be an ex post facto problem, and that was because of the way that the guidelines anchor the sentence. Certainly, here, where we have a statute and not any kind of discretionary exercise, there would be an ex post facto problem potentially with the application of the new drug schedules that had added drugs.

Judge: Can I ask you what purpose your rule has to putting a defendant on notice as to what his potential liability may be at the moment he commits the federal offense? At that point, he has no idea what an enhancement may or may not be based on what conduct he committed in the state offense or even in the federal offense.

I'm not sure what rule of interpretation would counsel that approach.

Defendant's Lawyer: Respectfully, Your Honor, I think that's an odd conception of notice, to be honest with you.

Due process notice problems arise when an offender can't tell where the law is and can't tell what the sentence is.

It doesn't usually arise if the offender gets a break on the way to the sentencing forum. That's what happened in Dorsey. That's what happened in Peugh. That's what happened in Concepcion.

So, if an offender gets a break on the way, the offender gets the opportunity to take advantage of that break to make the argument. We don't say that somebody who is on notice 10, 12, 15 years ago when they committed a state crime should have that crime—

Judge: Except, counsel—

Defendant's Lawyer: —or should have the whole thing—

Judge: —that's the whole fallacy of sentencing enhancements, that somehow, because there's a potential for enhancement, there may be a decision by a defendant not to commit a crime. It's unlikely to ever really happen, but accepting that supposition, your rule doesn't do anything to enhance rejection of a criminal from committing a crime again.

Defendant's Lawyer: Well, I think our rule does do something very important, which is to respect Congress's choice to change the drug schedules and to narrow the types of drugs that are going to go onto the federal schedule.

And that, of course, affects the matching exercise. So our rule respects the change that Congress made in 2018.

And with respect to the prior notice, as I said, I think it's an odd conception to say that you should be culpable for some future act that you had not even committed yet because you were on notice at that time.

Judge: Counsel, can I ask you a question about the distinction between your approach and the time-of-federal-offense approach? Why does it make sense or why would it make sense for Congress to say that two defendants who were convicted at the exact same time should be sentenced differently simply by virtue of when their sentencing happened?

I mean, doesn't the other approach—if we're not going to choose the government's approach—it just seems to me like the time-of-federal-offense approach makes more sense of the scheme.

Defendant's Lawyer: Well, any line-drawing that's done with respect to the applicability or the matching exercise is going to create some arbitrariness. The Court acknowledged that in Dorsey.

And, in fact, the same sort of hypothetical that Your Honor posed was discussed in McNeill and also in Dorsey, and the resolution there was that a time-of-sentencing approach uniformly—although arbitrary in some sense—at least anchors it in a way that is consistent throughout, and, according to the Court in Dorsey, removes some of the arbitrariness.

Judge: And isn't that the standard way it's ordinarily done in the sentencing world? I mean, I understood that under the normal federal sentencing process, a federal judge applies the sentencing law at the time of sentencing.

Defendant's Lawyer: That's correct, Your Honor.

Judge: So, to the extent that we accept that this ACCA is a sentencing statute, then the normal course would be to apply a time-of-sentencing rule?

Defendant's Lawyer: That's correct.

Judge: Welcome back to the People's Court. Next case on the docket, the plaintiffs say the defendant is keeping their security wrongfully, and they think it's motivated by spite. But the defendant says the plaintiffs are lucky they got a penny back. It's the case of a spite suit.

Judge: Ms. Mondeja, Mr. Ocasio, and Lizette Gutierrez, who's Lizette?

Plaintiff (Rosamondejo): I am.

Judge: And what is everybody’s relationship?

Plaintiff (Rosamondejo): This is my daughter, and that's my husband.

Judge: Okay, you are suing David Rivera, your former landlord, for two thousand two hundred and fifty dollars and two cents that you say he owes you—double the remaining amount of the security deposit that you claim he is unlawfully withholding. Tell me what happened.

Plaintiff (Rosamondejo): We moved into his apartment about a year ago and paid twenty-seven hundred dollars for the security deposit, plus we paid five hundred dollars for a pet deposit.

Judge: What kind of pet was it?

Plaintiff (Rosamondejo): A small dachshund.

Judge: Okay.

Plaintiff (Rosamondejo): We signed a lease, and we also signed an agreement that the pet deposit would be returned once we moved out, provided there was no damage to the apartment.

Judge: Correct.

Plaintiff (Rosamondejo): We gave notice to Mr. Rivera in December that we would be vacating the apartment on January 27th.

Judge: Okay.

Plaintiff (Rosamondejo): On January 30th, we met with Mr. Rivera in the apartment and did an inspection. During the inspection, he noted two things. One was that the shower heads were missing.

Judge: Why were they missing?

Plaintiff (Rosamondejo): He claims that the shower heads were there when we moved in, but they were not. I actually purchased the shower heads.

Judge: Why would you rent a place without shower heads? Why weren’t they there?

Plaintiff (Rosamondejo): They weren’t there when we moved in. When we first looked at the apartment, the shower heads were there, but when we moved in, they were not.

Judge: Do you have any record of saying to him, "Hey, where are the shower heads? We need to buy shower heads"?

Plaintiff (Rosamondejo): No, I do not.

Judge: No, you don’t?

Plaintiff (Rosamondejo): No.

Judge: Because otherwise, if you ran out and bought shower heads, it sounds like you beautified the shower heads instead of telling your landlord, "What the heck? I can’t take a shower." You know what I’m saying?

Plaintiff (Rosamondejo): Yeah, we just went out and bought shower heads and didn’t say anything to him.

Judge: It’s unusual.

Plaintiff (Julio Ocasio): In January, with the help of a lawyer, I sent him a letter saying we’d be moving out February 28th and we’d be living out our part of the security.

Judge: Yes, but you can’t do that. I’m surprised a lawyer would tell you to do that.

Plaintiff (Julio Ocasio): Yes, you could, because of the fact that the security—

Judge: No, you can’t. You can’t live out security. Security is for the purpose of security. You’re supposed to pay.

Plaintiff (Julio Ocasio): Everybody does it.

Judge: A lot of people do it, but that’s not legally permissible. Landlords put up with it because, by the time they go to court, three months have passed. But let’s talk about this.

Judge: The day comes when they’re moving out. You live out the security deposit, leaving a remaining $900 in security deposit, correct?

Plaintiff (Julio Ocasio): Yes.

Judge: Plus the $500 pet deposit, which brings us to $1,400, correct?

Plaintiff (Rosamondejo): Yes.

Judge: Then you were demanding the 63% interest, correct?

Plaintiff (Rosamondejo): Yes.

Judge: Instead of getting $1,463, what you got was $462.99 back from him, correct?

Plaintiff (Rosamondejo): That is correct.

Judge: And does he explain why he’s withholding the rest of the money?

Plaintiff (Rosamondejo): He was withholding $45 for the shower heads, $100 for two light fixtures that had to be put back up, and $855 to redo the floors.

Judge: Okay. Defendant, why did you have to redo the floors?

Defendant (David Rivera): They had put up TVs and ripped them off, leaving holes. The floors had water damage from their refrigerator. I have pictures.

Judge: Let me see the pictures.

(Examines pictures)

Judge: That just needs paint, right?

Defendant (David Rivera): No, the floors needed sanding.

Judge: Plaintiff, did you remove the refrigerator?

Plaintiff (Rosamondejo): Yes, but I didn’t see water damage.

Judge: Looking at this, I don’t think this is normal wear and tear. I find that you still owe a remaining $380.01. Verdict for the plaintiff.

Judge: "According to the test result, there is no probability he can be the father."

Defendant: "So first of all, no disrespect to you, but the letter looks a little sketchy. And I don't trust him because his uncle is an attorney, okay? And his uncle can do anything with it, you know what I'm saying?"

Judge: "Wait, I believe at the beginning I asked you all how the test went."

Defendant: "I mean—"

Judge: "You just said—wait, hold on—you said everything went good, right?"

Plaintiff: It's not normal.

Defendant: Susie had a birthday party that cost $2,000.

Plaintiff: So, so you're trying to keep up with Susie?

Defendant: Well, I didn't do $2,000. I could have done $2,000, and I think I kept it at a reasonable cost.

Judge: Wait, can you speak some reason to me?

Defendant: Your Honor, I am more than happy to help our daughter celebrate and live her life, but there comes a point where it's just—you know, it's frivolous.

Judge: All parties, please raise your right hands. Welcome back to the People's Court. Next case and the dog at the Plaintiff says the Defendant side swiped his car and then booked it. But the Defendant says he has no recollection and he says it was a shakedown. It's the case of gun and run. Thank you, Douglas. Okay.

Judge: Javier, you've asked to be referred to only as Javier. You are suing Herbert Lucas. You've asked us not to mention the company you work for. For $950 in damages to your mirror and the driver's side of your car because according to you, he sideswiped you. Tell me what happened on the morning of Monday, August 1st.

Plaintiff: Your Honor, I was driving my car to Upper West Side to help my friend Tim. He had just moved into his new apartment, and we were taking some items to the Goodwill store. So we went upstairs to Tim's apartment and we helped pack.

Judge: Is this Tim?

Plaintiff: This is Dan. He's my friend who was with me, who helped me and Tim.

Judge: Oh, okay. So he was there.

Plaintiff: He was there. He came with me and he was in the car. We parked it, and we went upstairs to help Tim. So we came down with some bags to take into the car. Then we were gonna wait, double parked, while we waited for Tim to bring the rest of the stuff down.

Judge: Okay.

Plaintiff: While we came down around 8:20, we saw the truck drive by driven by Mr. Lucas. We saw him sideswipe. Actually, I heard the sound. I didn't see it as much as I heard it. Then I looked up, and I saw his truck driving. It hit my car and took my mirror off my side view on the driver's side. I saw the side of my car had been hit.

Judge: Can I see a picture of the damage?

Plaintiff: Yes, absolutely.

Judge: Go on.

Plaintiff: Upon realizing that my car had been hit, I kind of chased after the car a little bit on foot. I tried to chase after, but it just kept going. I caught most of the license plates. Then we kind of gathered and wrote down some notes on my phone. We had a license plate number and whatever we could recall. We came back to the car, and I actually found on the windshield a note from a Good Samaritan.

Judge: Who ran after the truck?

Plaintiff: We both did, but Dan was fast enough.

Judge: Sorry, where am I going with that?

Plaintiff: Right there.

Judge: Yes. Sit on my lap, little boy, and tell me what you want for Christmas. No, you don't come up here. Have you never watched court before?

Plaintiff: That was such a beautiful judge.

Judge: All right. What did you see?

Plaintiff's Friend (Dan): Well, we were walking out of the apartment, and that's when we saw the truck go by and we heard the noise. Then we basically started running. I'm pretty quick.

Plaintiff: He's fast. I used to run cross country.

Dan: He's way faster than I am.

Judge: But you couldn't catch up with it?

Dan: Correct. But he got some of the license plate and I got some of it, so we put it together.

Judge: Then when you guys came back to the car, what happened?

Plaintiff: When we came back to the car, we put the bags in the trunk. Then I realized someone had left a note. A Good Samaritan left a note with the information.

Judge: You have the actual note?

Plaintiff: Yeah, I do.

Judge: May I see it?

Plaintiff: Mm-hmm.

Judge: Thank you. The Good Samaritan didn't leave his or her name and number?

Plaintiff: Nope. They actually got the name of the company wrong, but they got most of the plates. That was good. It helped us.

Judge: All right. What happened next?

Defendant: Well, my dispatcher called me around 9:30 on the radio and said Mr. Javier said one of our trucks side swiped his mirror coming down 85th Street. I told him I have no recollection of ever coming down 85th Street. Then he said I have Mr. Javier's number. So I called Mr. Javier.

Defendant: As soon as I talked to Mr. Javier, he tells me, "I'm a lawyer. This is a hit and run. I have witnesses."

Judge: Are you a lawyer?

Plaintiff: Law school.

Judge: I'm sorry, are you a lawyer?

Plaintiff: Yes. I don't practice, but I am a lawyer.

Judge: Do you have a license? Are you a member of a bar? Were you a member at one point of the bar?

Plaintiff: I've graduated law school. I haven't.

Judge: You haven't taken the bar yet?

Plaintiff: Not immediately, no.

Judge: Okay.

[The conversation continues with the back-and-forth discussions regarding evidence, testimonies, and final verdict, leading to the ruling in favor of the Plaintiff.]

Judge: Verdict is for the Plaintiff in the amount of $903 plus court costs.

Judge: It -- it surely would be irresponsible for the government to say we're not going to put him on the No Fly List no matter what?

Plaintiff’s Lawyer: Yes, Your Honor.

Judge: We put him on earlier for some particular reason, we've now found out he has a nuclear weapon, but we said we wouldn't put him on, so we're not going to put him on. I don't see how you can ask them to say anything more than what they've said. Now maybe we'll decide that that's not enough, but you can't really expect them to say more than that. He's not on the list, as far as we know, any other reason he's not -- we're not going to put him on the list for the same reasons.

Plaintiff’s Lawyer: Well, for example, Your Honor, they could moot the procedural due process claim without disclosing any reasons at all about why the plaintiff was put on the list because that just deals with the procedures itself. On the substance -- on the reasons, you're right, Your Honor, that it's -- it's -- it's up to the government to decide whether to disclose or not to disclose. And if the government chooses not to disclose, that doesn't mean that they've lost the case. Instead, it just means that they have to defend it on the merits.

Judge: Well, I appreciate that -- you're right, but that's not what I was saying. I want to know, am I right about the idea that there's no way they could issue -- say anything about what the future would hold in terms of the national security interests? They can't say you've got a, you know, free pass, whatever you want to do, we're not going to put you on the No Fly List?

Plaintiff’s Lawyer: Of course not, Your Honor. And I think the government has made it easy by saying nothing at all about what it'll do in the future, but it could have made a limited promise. It could have said we disclosed -- we put him on the list for a mistake, and now we've addressed that mistake, and we think that this explanation shows that that mistake is unlikely to recur, or they said we disclose the reasons, we identified that they're illegal in X, Y, and Z manner, that's against our policy.

Judge: Well, but you're -- that's where I wonder if you're going back to the situation where you're insisting on a statement to the effect that they were wrong. It was -- in fact, you said it was a mistake or -- or whatever. And mootness has never required that type of determination or that type of assertion. It's just solely -- it's forward-looking and that's the only -- you're not entitled to, to establish mootness, a determination that what they did in the past was wrong.

Plaintiff’s Lawyer: Yes, Your Honor, we agree with that, that repudiation is not required. But repudiation is forward-looking, even though it deals with the past for one. If -- if a party acknowledges that what they did was illegal, was wrong, or perhaps was not what they would want to do or how they want their program to work, that -- that's an example of a government moving away from its prior decision. And the -- the -- the humble fact, the humble point, jurisprudential point of repudiation is that a party that steps away from what it's done in the past is less likely to repeat that behavior in the future.

Judge: Well, but in Already, we said no matter how vigorous the dispute remains between the parties, that's not the question.

Plaintiff’s Lawyer: Yeah -- yes. And in Already, the company had made a promise that covered every shoe that it was currently making, every shoe that it had made in the past, and any future imitation of those shoes. That would be like, instead of the declaration saying currently available information, it would have said we disclose here are the reasons we put you on a list, we promise not to use those same reasons again in the future and enumerated them. That's not what the government did here. And because the government didn't take -- didn't do -- didn't take one step towards Your Honor's position, it -- it -- it -- it simplifies this Court's analysis. There's -- there's simply no repudiation. There's an embrace of their past conduct.

Judge: Does that mean that if you're on the No Fly List, your case is never moot if the government is unwilling to say more than it said here?

Plaintiff’s Lawyer: No, Your Honor. If -- if we were bringing this case today, I think that we wouldn't be able to make out standing. And so this is -- this case deals with a unique situation which, in my colleague's telling, is -- is -- is rare, where a person files a lawsuit and, at the time they file the lawsuit, everybody agrees they're on the No Fly List and there's standing. During the course of litigation, the government removes that person from the No Fly List. That doesn't make the case moot. That triggers the application of this Court's voluntary cessation doctrine to determine whether it is moot or isn't moot. The application of the voluntary cessation doctrine is demanding. It assigns the government -- it assigns the party moving for mootness the burden, and it uses this language over and over and over again, absolutely clear. And I think, Your Honor, that the failure to disclose the reasons for the listing short-circuits this Court's analysis of whether or not they've met their burden.

Judge: You said, if it were filed today, there would be no standing. Can you explain why you think that?

Plaintiff’s Lawyer: I think, Your Honor, we fit within the situation described in Laidlaw where sometimes, when you're making a projection of what's going to happen in the future, you -- the -- the -- the showing that you have to make to demonstrate standing is going to be higher than the showing that you have to make to defeat mootness. Here, in this case, we're -- we think that the -- where the burden is assigned is important and -- and what the case turns on.

Judge: Isn't the answer also that there's just like no basic redressability from the standing standpoint at this point in time? So, in other words, he's off the list now. If he showed up in court tomorrow not on the list, initiating a lawsuit and asking to be taken off the No Fly List as one of his, you know -- or even perhaps a declaration that when he was previously on the No Fly List, that was a problem, I would think there would be a legitimate argument on the government's part that he had no standing to proceed. But what saves you in this case is that he actually initiated this when he did have standing. And the government concedes that. And so now the whole exercise becomes under what circumstance can the government stop the case that was already in motion at the time that it was legitimately -- you know, stop a case that was legitimately started pursuant to Article III. It's on the government then to show that this is now moot as a result of something that they did.

Plaintiff’s Lawyer: Yes, Your Honor, it is on the government. And -- and -- and, here, what -- what underscores Your Honor's point is that the plaintiff doesn't -- he was living his law-abiding everyday life when the government put him on the No Fly List. He still doesn't know why he was put on the No Fly List. And so I think that is -- because the No Fly List operates under that cloak of secrecy, that that creates a problem for them meeting their burden.

Judge: Can I --

Judge: Would you answer --

Judge: -- come back to the question of what might be said in a declaration that would be sufficient to show that it was sufficiently unlikely that he would be put back on the list? Short of repudiation or a change of circumstances, am I correct that your answer is that there must be a disclosure of the reasons why he was on in the first place?

Plaintiff’s Lawyer: For the substantive due process claim, yes, Your Honor, we think that a disclosure of some kind is the only way for establishing a baseline that the court can then compare a promise to.

**Judge:** All parties, please raise your right hands. Welcome back to The People's Court. Next case on the docket—the plaintiff says he was hired by the defendant to do some work around her house, and then she went and stopped payment on his check. But the defendant says the plaintiff was rude to her, didn't wear a mask in her house in the middle of a pandemic, and is owed nothing for his shoddy work. It’s the case of “Mask Up or Pay Up.” Thank you, Douglas.

**Plaintiff (George):** You are welcome. All right, Mr. George, you are suing Ms. Lucy for $785—$390 of it money that you say she owes you and stopped payment on, and the rest of it for pain and suffering and returned check fees. What happened?

**Plaintiff (George):** I received a text from her the Friday before Thanksgiving of last year, and it said that she was a friend of a friend who is a very dear friend of mine who lost his wife last year of 61 years. He is in incredible grieving right now, and I will do anything to help this guy. So when I received the text from the defendant, you know, I went into overdrive because she said she was a friend of Putter’s. She doesn’t know Putter well, as it turns out—she’s a friend of a friend of a friend of a friend of Putter’s or something, apparently. I don’t know.

**Judge:** All right. But so you agree on $65 an hour to do some handiwork, and it’s an actual hourly fee, correct? Like it’s not by the job, it’s by the hour?

**Plaintiff (George):** Yes. You know, I charge a minimum of two hours to do anything, and then it’s $65 an hour for every hour after.

**Judge:** Right. But it’s by the hour, and she agrees to it. And then you go there, and how many hours of work do you do?

**Plaintiff (George):** Six hours.

**Judge:** All right. So she pays you the $65. She pays you by check the $65 an hour for $390. Does she express any concern while she before she pays you? Is she upset about anything?

**Plaintiff (George):** Nothing. She’s complimentary, she’s friendly. She tells me that she actually doesn’t know my friend, but that she got my number from someone else. And that’s when I’m like, what? You know? But no, she expressed no concern whatsoever. And then it was on Thanksgiving Day that I found out that the check had been stopped.

**Judge:** That’s not a great day to have a stopped check. Right. So do you call her, or what do you do?

**Plaintiff (George):** I texted her. And I believe, and I certainly hope, I’ve been professional this whole time. She sent me the world’s longest text, and when she called me a misogynist, that’s when I stopped reading it. I don’t even know what the rest of the text said. I’ve never read it. I just simply realized that I needed to just go to court and file a small claims case.

**Judge:** Let me hear from you, Ms. Lucy. You paid the guy a check for $390 and then you stopped payment on it. Why?

**Defendant (Lucy):** Well, because I realized that he had left a huge mess.

**Judge:** But you realized that the day you were writing out the check. There’s nothing new. That wasn’t a surprise, was it?

**Defendant (Lucy):** Well, I was working when he was in the house, and I was going to let it go. I was just going to deal with the mess that he left. That’s why I wrote him the check. But after he left and I finished my work that I was doing at the dining room table, I went in and I saw the electrical sockets that he hadn’t put back. I saw the shelves, I went to the front, and there was a mess in the front. There was a mess in the backyard. He left such a mess. And I thought to myself, no, that’s not right. I’m not going to pay him the full amount.

**Judge:** So the next morning you texted him and said you weren’t going to pay the full amount but would pay half?

**Defendant (Lucy):** Yes, because he did half the work.

**Judge:** Right. But see, you weren’t paying him by the job. If you had said, “I’m going to pay you $390 to paint these two rooms,” and then you say, “Well, he only got to one of the rooms,” I understand what you’re saying. But your agreement with him was to pay him by the hour, right?

**Defendant (Lucy):** Yes.

**Judge:** And that’s just math. He spent x amount of time doing the work, so you owe him y. That’s it. You just—it’s just math.

**Defendant (Lucy):** But $65 an hour is a lot of money. And I thought that for $65 an hour, I would be completely hands-off. I assumed he was a professional handyman.

**Judge:** All he has to do is tell you, “My time is worth $65 an hour.” And then all you have to do is answer my question: Was he there six hours? If those two things are correct, then don’t rehire him. But you don’t get to say, “You should have worked faster.”

**Defendant (Lucy):** But I told him not to cash the check, and I told him I was going to write him another check.

**Judge:** Yes, but that’s not up to you. Once you let the guy work there under the agreed-upon terms—which are $65 an hour—and you let six hours pass, six times $65 is what the guy gets.

**Judge:** Verdict for the plaintiff. $395—$390 plus a $5 returned check fee. You don’t get pain and suffering in a contract case.

**Defendant (Lucy):** I don’t think it was fair. He should have completed one job properly before starting another. It is what it is.

**Plaintiff (George):** My comment about her dogs was just an observation. I like women. That’s all.

**Judge:** The bottom line is, if you owe someone money, you pay them. Case closed.

Judge: This is the defendant Kenneth Deuchi he says he's been a licensed plumber for 30 years and he performed a gas pressure test at the plaintiff's place of business. The plaintiff's business was in shambles, the gas had been turned off for a decade and the town rejected his test over a technicality. He's done hundreds of tests in the town before without a problem. The town rejected the test because the plate had had the gas turned off for so long and he can't try to blame him and ask him for his money back because he did the required work properly. He's accused of not passing gas. All parties please your rans, welcome back to the People's Court. Next case on the docket, the plaintiff hired the defendant who said that he was allowed to work in the town where he was doing the job, but the town said you don't have a license here. The defendant says it got rejected on a technicality. It's the case of the plumber cracks me up, thank you. Douglas Pasta Machines Incorporated represented here by Michael Wilson, you're the owner, yes? You're suing Kenneth Deuchi, doing business as Ken the Plumber for $591.97 to do some work that was of no use to you. Tell me what happened.

Plaintiff (Michael Wilson): Um, it was in December of 2014. Um, we had had the gas shut off at our building for approximately a year and a half.

Judge: Why?

Plaintiff (Michael Wilson): Um, because I decided to use a wood stove. I purchased a wood stove, very large wood stove, and we're using the wood stove for heat in the winters, because the gas prices were so high at the time. So we're using a wood stove.

Judge: What kind of a business is it?

Plaintiff (Michael Wilson): It's a pasta manufacturing company.

Judge: You make the machines?

Plaintiff (Michael Wilson): Yes, we make the pasta machines. Commercial machines.

Judge: Yes, ma'am.

Plaintiff (Michael Wilson): In any event, um, so as long as you don’t say "yes, sir" when I’m out of the RO. Go ahead.

Plaintiff (Michael Wilson): In any event, the employees were, it was a very bad year that year, the temperature went really, really down low very early in the season. So, you know, I had to get the gas hook back up. I agree.

Judge: Your employees wanted you to get the gas back?

Plaintiff (Michael Wilson): Yes, because it was, you know, cold. The wood stove wasn't cutting the mustard.

Judge: Okay, go ahead.

Plaintiff (Michael Wilson): So, in any event, so uh, we called, I looked on the internet first. I called the gas company and told them to come hook up the gas. The gas company said, “Um, your gas has been turned off for more than a year so we can't bring that...”

Judge: How long had it been turned off for?

Plaintiff (Michael Wilson): According to you by then, about a year and 3/4.

Plaintiff (Michael Wilson): Okay. Yeah, so we said they said you can't get the gas hooked up unless you have someone come in and do what's called a leak test, a pressure test or a leak test, they're both the same thing.

Judge: So the leak test was for the gas company?

Plaintiff (Michael Wilson): Yes, the gas company had to see a leak test performed by and they said it has to be a licensed Master Plumber. That's the only person that can do the leak test for gas. It has to be a master plumber, not just any plumber.

Judge: Okay.

Plaintiff (Michael Wilson): Okay, so I looked on the internet, and I came, uh, you know, I looked at several different people, and I came across a Kender grunt's, um, site. Actually, it was his listing, and it said that, uh, you know, I got a, there were a lot of good reviews. So I said, I picked him.

Plaintiff (Michael Wilson): So, my sec, I had my secretary call him and make an appointment to come and you get a price for the gas test and come and do it so that we could get the gas hooked up, which is, that's what she told him.

Judge: So he comes and he does the leak test. Did you talk to him before he did any tests?

Plaintiff (Michael Wilson): Me personally?

Judge: Right.

Plaintiff (Michael Wilson): No. So whatever conversation he had with your secretary, he comes and he does the leak test. He gives you the results, and what were the results?

Plaintiff (Michael Wilson): We... well, the first time we didn't pass it, then he did something and then the second time he did it we passed.

Plaintiff (Michael Wilson): Okay, okay, so now we said, here, and I said, so this is all we need to, uh, to show to the, to the, um, gas company so that we could get the gas hooked up. He said, yep, that's all you need.

Plaintiff (Michael Wilson): And you had paid him how much?

Plaintiff (Michael Wilson): $591.97.

Judge: And then what happened that all of a sudden that wasn't worth anything to you?

Plaintiff (Michael Wilson): Well, we faxed the copy of the test, the pass where it said passed on it to the gas company, and the gas company said, "Excuse me, he's not on the list of licensed plumbers in the Town of Babylon. We can't accept that. He has to be on the list of licensed plumbers in the Town of Babylon."

Judge: They told you that before then?

Plaintiff (Michael Wilson): No, they had just said Master Plumber.

Plaintiff (Michael Wilson): Yeah, I didn’t call up to find out, you know, who I should pick. They, and besides, they said we don’t recommend anybody.

Plaintiff (Michael Wilson): The gas company said.

Judge: Right.

Plaintiff (Michael Wilson): And then they, and then they tell you, we don’t recommend anybody but no, that one's not good enough.
