Plaintiff's Lawyer: Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court: Everyone in this case now agrees that as the lack of probable cause element of a malicious prosecution claim under the Fourth Amendment, the charge-specific rule governs. That is, a plaintiff may make out a malicious prosecution claim by proving that one charge is not supported by probable cause, even if other charges are, provided, of course, that the plaintiff also makes out the other elements of the claim. As no one appears to dispute, that charge-specific rule is supported, as Chief Judge Pryor put the point, by both centuries of common law and by bedrock Fourth Amendment principles.

That's all this Court needs to address to resolve this case. The defense urges this Court to go beyond that ruling to weigh in on a series of open questions about a different element of the Fourth Amendment malicious prosecution claim, the resulted-in-a-seizure element. No one disputes that the plaintiff must prove that on remand.

But weighing in on the precise contours of that question would require this Court to go beyond the question presented to address issues not aired at all below, that haven't percolated in the courts of appeal, and to do so on the sort of briefing that doesn't have the kinds of text, history, and common law arguments that you'd expect before weighing in on an element of a constitutional tort. As in Thompson, this case involves a narrow dispute over one element of a Fourth Amendment malicious prosecution claim.

This Court should simply hold that a plaintiff can make out a claim by showing that at least one charge lacks probable cause, again, assuming they can make out the other elements of the claim, and remand for consideration of everything else. I welcome this Court's questions.

Judge: Well, what do you have to show, if anything, about causation? If you have a situation where you've got three crimes—three allegations—and two are concededly valid and they justify the seizure, you still get relief? Don't you have to show some kind of causation?

Plaintiff's Lawyer: Yes, Your Honor. So we agree there has to be some kind of causation. We think the formulation in Thompson is correct. You have to show that the malicious prosecution resulted in a seizure. Our position is that if you want to weigh in on precisely what the counterfactual is, the Eleventh Circuit's position is correct—that the correct counterfactual is, could this have been done as a warrantless arrest? The Eleventh Circuit said—

Judge: What's "it"?

Plaintiff's Lawyer: Could the plaintiff have been seized for this length of time or seized with this length of time based on a warrantless arrest?

So the way the Eleventh Circuit articulates it is, look, this legal process was corrupted, we know this legal process was necessary if the detention is longer than 48 hours, right? That's a Fourth Amendment requirement—you've got to have legal process for a detention that's longer than 48 hours. And so, because this legal process was necessary, what you have to show is—you prevail unless this could have been done as a warrantless arrest.

Judge: Well, I thought the evidence was that the first two of the crimes would certainly support what was done in this case without regard to the third.

Plaintiff's Lawyer: So that's correct, Your Honor. Would support in the sense that a magistrate could constitutionally have signed this arrest warrant. But, in fact, no magistrate did sign this arrest warrant based on just those two charges.

So, based on courts' case law, for instance, we cite the Eastern District of Michigan case in our opening brief, where the court says—that's the case, remember, where the attorney general says, "I can sign warrants to authorize wiretaps." This Court says, surely a magistrate would have signed off on these warrants, right? Surely, a magistrate would have caved to the national security concerns and signed these things.

Still a Fourth Amendment violation because a magistrate didn't do so. Again, I think this is all beyond the scope of the question presented. And, frankly, because there are indications from precedent and common law to support the Eleventh Circuit's rule, at the very least, this Court shouldn't go any further than it did in Thompson in defining the precise contours of the resulted-in-a-seizure element.

Judge: Are you giving up on the Solicitor General's position that—as an alternative to the Eleventh Circuit's view—that if you can show that you were retained for longer or that, as you argued below, you would have never been arrested, you would have been given a summons to appear, as the person who sold you the jewelry was, so you would have never been in jail, that that would be enough? Are you giving up on that?

Plaintiff's Lawyer: No, Your Honor.

So we think—we don't have much of a dog in this fight. We think that we win under the Eleventh Circuit's rule, we win under the Solicitor General's rule. As between the two, we think the Eleventh Circuit's rule is more consistent with this Court's precedent and with the common law. But, again, we think that you should make that decision on the basis of some briefing about the text of the Fourth Amendment, Founding-era practice, common law—none of which you have before you.

Judge: We shouldn't reach it, is what you're saying?

Plaintiff's Lawyer: Yes, that's right.

Judge: Well, what if I think it is subsumed within the question presented? What is wrong with the logic of the Solicitor General's position? Under Thompson, an element of the claim at issue is a seizure, and that's necessary to bring this within the Fourth Amendment.

So your client was seized when he was arrested, and under Thompson, he experienced a continuing seizure during the period when he was in jail. And so the question is whether either his arrest or the length of his detention was the result of the charge—the so-called money-laundering charge—this statute that doesn’t seem to have anything to do with money laundering.

But what's wrong with that logic? It seems to follow step by step.

Plaintiff's Lawyer: So, again, Your Honor, I think we win under that test, right? The police officer's affidavit in this case says we are seeking a warrant because there is a felony of the third degree.

So I don't want to push too hard on this. That’s a totally acceptable result from our point of view. The reason that we think it's not the best way to interpret that "resulted in a seizure" language is twofold.

First, what Williams explains is that the entire point of requiring a neutral and detached magistrate to weigh in is to give that neutral party the opportunity to weigh the law enforcement interests against the privacy interests. And so doing this sort of counterfactual predicting what the magistrate would have done kind of undermines that goal.

So I think the Williams rule, which basically says, look, if you could have done this as a warrantless arrest—right? If you didn't need the warrant, you didn't need to have a Gerstein hearing, and you only detained him for a few hours—then it's fine if the warrant process was totally corrupt. That was just something bonus you were doing.

But where the warrant was necessary to the detention, you could not have detained him for four days without a warrant, then we're not going to peer in and try to figure out whether the magistrate would have reached the same result if it knew the actual charges.

Judge: What if the other charges on which there was probable cause were also felonies and maybe even more serious felonies than the so-called money-laundering charge? Would you make the same argument?

Plaintiff's Lawyer: So I don't think we'd be able to make the same argument about why we win under the United States' rule.

Judge: Armand Beaudette, you are suing Emily Abel for $935 for October and November rent plus a late fee. Tell me what's going on.

I would pay for the heat and hot water. The rent was reduced by $300 from previous leases to help my granddaughter pay her student loans and help Emily save some money. The apartment was across the street from our home, providing a safe environment for both of them.

Judge: Did you know Emily before this?

Plaintiff: I knew her through my granddaughter. They were close friends.

Judge: So she wasn't a stranger to you.

Plaintiff: No, not at all.

Judge: When did they move in?

Plaintiff: January 2016.

Judge: And how was the rent payment situation?

Plaintiff: Emily would sometimes pay in installments or be late, but we let it go because it helped both of them. On August 31st, the defendant failed to pay for the month of August and disappeared. I tried contacting her, texting and calling, but she wouldn't answer.

Judge: Emily, where were you in August?

Defendant: I was staying with my boyfriend.

Judge: So you paid for August. What about September?

Judge: Emily, did you tell anyone you were moving out?

Defendant: I understand.

Judge: So what is your defense for not paying October rent?

Judge: When did you go get your things?

Defendant: The last day of October.

Judge: How does that indicate you were going to pay rent?

Judge: Did it get ruined?

Defendant: Yes.

Judge: If you didn't notify the landlord and didn't pay October rent, whose fault is it that the friendship went south?

Judge: So I just want to clarify one thing because I've seen cases in which the indictment has many counts talking about different acts of the defendant and uses the kind of language that points to "on or about" a certain date. In some of them, those counts even have overlapping dates and time frames.

And so I guess I would expect that it would be those kinds of cases in which the defendant would have a colorable argument that these things happened on the same occasion, and those would be the ones that would be more likely to go to trial, right?

I mean, it's—I guess I'm—I'm suggesting that the trial scenario seems to me to be precisely the one where you would have to bring in all the evidence related to the past crime because if it was just as easy as, you know, these things are on separate dates, the person probably wouldn't go to trial, right?

Plaintiff's Lawyer: Well, Your Honor, I—I do think that—again, we don't have a ton of experience with this.

Judge: Yes.

Plaintiff's Lawyer: But I do think that to the extent that the indictments for the prior crimes or the information for the prior crimes, the charging documents, show that they occurred on different days or at least allow a jury to infer as much, I'm not sure that the defendant, in the absence of some plausible argument—and, again, I think that's going to be the rare case, and precedent suggests it's the rare case—in the absence of a plausible argument that they're part of a common scheme, not just a common motivation like "I'm an inveterate gambler and I need to rob stores to make my money," but an actual part of a common scheme, that the defendant's actually going to want to go to trial on that.

Because, you know, among other things, like, lots of cases plead, and the defendant may not—for—for—may not wish to kind of try the district court's patience with holding separate proceedings on something—

Judge: Yeah.

Plaintiff's Lawyer: —that's not going to benefit him.

Judge: All right. Let me ask you another question that comes from a colloquy that you had with the court about harmless error.

So any ruling that this court made—let's say we decided to address harmlessness in this context—you would anticipate that that rule would then be incorporated into jury instructions if these cases should happen in the future?

Plaintiff's Lawyer: It would depend on what the court said, Your Honor.

Our—our current proposed model jury instruction, which, again, we haven't really had to use very often because the courts of appeals—

Judge: Yeah.

Plaintiff's Lawyer: —haven't gone in our favor, largely tracks what was said earlier.

Judge: No, I know.

But any future ruling that courts issue about harmlessness in a situation—right? If we look at the facts here and we say this is harmless because, fill in the blank, that would then become a rule that I would assume would have to be incorporated into future jury instructions in order to make sure we have some sort of uniformity coming out of this, right?

Plaintiff's Lawyer: It would depend on what— it would depend on what the court said.

I—I don't know that we would invariably, even under the current precedent, insist that the jury be instructed that, for example, different days almost always mean separate occasions.

I think we're comfortable enough with kind of a description of the general inquiry—

Judge: But it doesn't bother the government that you could have a jury that is—you could have different defendants who basically got the same rap sheets coming out differently, unless we have a rule about when it's going to be treated as a different occasion?

Plaintiff's Lawyer: That does bother us, Your Honor.

We always want like defendants to be treated alike.

That's a basic—a basic animating principle of sentencing law and sentencing in general.

And to the extent we can, we would want jury instructions that would tend to reach that conclusion. However, as this court has noted, for example, in past cases, different juries, even instructed the exact same way—

Judge: Yeah.

Plaintiff's Lawyer: —can come out different ways on similar facts. That's just—

Judge: Thank you.

Plaintiff's Lawyer: —the nature of the system.

Judge: Thank you, counsel.

Plaintiff's Lawyer: Thank you.

Judge: Defense, you may proceed.

Defendant's Lawyer: Thank you, Your Honor, and may it please the court: The legal standard requires judges to make a classic recidivism determination, a finding about the separateness of prior offenses.

Under this court's precedents, that legislative choice is consistent with the Constitution.

This court held, based on centuries-old sentencing practices, that judges can impose sentencing enhancements based on recidivism.

For decades, the federal courts of appeals have unanimously upheld judicial fact-finding under this standard, and states also have relied on it to enact and enforce similar state recidivism schemes.

Both the plaintiff and the government seek to upend this practice, but they don't offer a principled basis for doing so.

Their front-line position is that judges can find only the elements of prior offenses.

But they concede that precedent authorizes judges to find various non-elemental facts as well.

So they're forced to make exception after exception to their elements-only principle, and they ultimately land on standards that are inconsistent with one another and divorced from any constitutional principle or precedent of this court.

I think what this court's precedents show is that judges can find facts about prior offenses, whereas juries must find facts about present offenses.

But, even if the other side's approaches were correct, the court should still affirm because at least the government agrees that judges applying the law can find facts about the dates and locations of prior offenses, and those very same facts are going to resolve most of these questions, as this case illustrates.

It would make no sense to allow judges to find those facts under one clause of the law but not the other.

This court should not set aside decades of consensus and impose on all federal and state courts an untested recidivism regime that would gravely prejudice defendants.

I welcome the court's questions.

Judge: Counsel, what's your best historical evidence that judges have performed inquiries like the different occasions inquiry here?

Defendant's Lawyer: Sure, Your Honor.

I—I want to flag upfront that I don't think the—the right question is to ask whether there are direct historical analogues.

But, to answer your question directly first, I do think that the statutes that have been mentioned about sequencing, going back to the early 1800s, finding that an offense occurred after a prior conviction, that an offense occurred after a defendant escaped or was released from prison, I think those are quite analogous to this standard.

I think, at bottom, in most cases, this standard is essentially asking judges to make a decision about sequencing—about how prior offenses occurred, whether they occurred one after another.

And I do think these statutes that, again, go back to the early 1800s are quite similar in that regard.

I do want to say, though, I don't think that that's the right historical question.

I think the way the court should think about the historical inquiry here is to ask, at the time of the founding, was there a settled practice that legislatures had to treat recidivism as an element of the offense?

And I think the answer to that question is no, as precedent has recognized.

Judge: Suppose the (a)(1) notice didn't have the right to counsel in it.  

Defendant’s Lawyer: So the answer, I think, is the same as before. We view the right to counsel information as an important piece of information, but the lack of that information does not affect the validity of the proceedings.  

Judge: Right. So it does suggest—you’re not informed of your right to counsel. Nonetheless, you have to show up. And I don’t see that any of your safeguards actually protect the non-citizen in that situation. What’s going to prevent the non-citizen from being ordered removed, not being able to reopen, notwithstanding that he’s never been told that he has a right to a lawyer?  

Defendant’s Lawyer: What prevents that result, Your Honor, is the regulations that I was referring to. If the notice to appear does not contain the information about the right to counsel, that is an incomplete notice to appear that would be rejected by the immigration court.  

Judge: Well—  

Defendant’s Lawyer: I also want to emphasize that—  

Judge: Counsel, let me pause there. Your regulations are interesting because they suggest that a lot of things are required in a notice to appear, except stuff that the government finds inconvenient, like the hearing date, and try to resuscitate the pre-statutory regime that existed before where the government could issue as many notices as it wanted. So the regulations themselves may or may not comply with the statute. As another judge pointed out, they may or may not change. The only statutory hook I think you’ve identified to save the problem is the clear and convincing evidence requirement, but that’s just a clear and convincing evidence requirement that the notice was given. And here, the notice would be the Section 2 notice, the notice of change.  

So none of that means that the Section 1 notice to appear has to be complete or, in fact, anything other than a blank document, right?  

Defendant’s Lawyer: So, Your Honor, I think, if we were in a world where we had repealed all our regulations and somehow—  

Judge: I’m not—  

Defendant’s Lawyer: —convinced the immigration judge to—  

Judge: Forget about your regulations. The law. Your interpretation of the law has to hang together and make sense of the law. Otherwise, it is a “trust us” argument. “Trust us. We will have our own internal operating procedures.” I’m asking you about statutory interpretation.  

And we normally think statutes are coherent, sensible, not ridiculous. You invoked common sense in your opening argument. And here, one consequence of your argument, I think, is that the notice to appear can be a blank document and that you can remove someone in absentia based on a notice of change that simply says “show up on a date certain.”  

The non-citizen may or may not even know that this is really the government. It’s just a date to show up somewhere. It doesn’t contain notice of charges or the right to a lawyer. And the government wins, right?  

Defendant’s Lawyer: Your Honor, I have several responses to that question. First, this is not a case where we are simply asking you to trust us. This is a statute that has been on the books for nearly 30 years—  

Judge: So let’s talk—  

Defendant’s Lawyer: —and has been used—  

Judge: —about the statute. Let’s—  

Defendant’s Lawyer: —hundreds of thousands of times.  

Judge: Am I correct that the clear and convincing evidence rule that you’re relying on as a matter of statutory interpretation would allow the government to remove somebody based on a blank notice to appear if they failed to appear on a notice of change that the government can prove was properly issued?  

Defendant’s Lawyer: Our position is that if a non-citizen receives a notice of hearing saying “show up at immigration proceedings at this date and time,” then, yes, the non-citizen needs to comply with that.  

Judge: Okay.  

Defendant’s Lawyer: And if the non-citizen doesn’t attend, they could be removed.  

Judge: And so if that happens—  

Defendant’s Lawyer: That’s very far away from the facts of these cases.  

Judge: If that happens, if that’s the consequence of your statutory interpretation, we have to ask whether that fits with common sense, as you say.  

One common-sense consequence might be this: If the government can issue blank notices to appear, which it has found inconvenient in the past to comply with, need only file notice of changes, and then remove people who fail to show up, why wouldn’t it proceed in exactly that fashion going forward?  

As I understand it, about a third of cases in immigration proceedings result in in absentia removals—failures to appear. So why not issue a blank notice to appear because they’re difficult, then issue a compliant notice of change, require appearance on a date certain, and then remove about a third of the non-citizens in this country without any notice of the charges against them or their right to counsel or anything else? Then, in the remaining cases, file compliant notices to appear.  

Defendant’s Lawyer: Your Honor, we do not do that and have never done that—  

Judge: I’m not asking you—  

Defendant’s Lawyer: —for several reasons.  

Judge: Let’s put aside the “trust us” arguments. What would prevent the government from following where the incentives of that decision might lead as a matter of law?  

Defendant’s Lawyer: And, Your Honor, what I’m disputing is that we would have any incentive ever to do that because, if we used a blank document, there would not be a removal proceeding under our own regulations in which to order the non-citizen removed.

Judge: This is the case of Michael Evans Sr. versus Demetrius Callaway. Mr. Evans, you are suing Mr. Callaway for $1,000—$200 of it being money you paid him and $800 for inconveniencing you and threatening you. What's going on here?

Judge: All right, so what date do you buy the parts?

Plaintiff: That date.

Judge: Just so—you buy all the parts. Now it’s January 3rd. You have all the parts. And then do you bring them to him?

Plaintiff: No.

Judge: Why?

Plaintiff: The parts had to be ordered. So I texted him back to tell him that the parts wouldn’t be in until 2:00. He tells me that 2:00 is too late, that he wouldn’t be able to do it until Saturday.

Judge: This is on January 3rd, which is a what?

Plaintiff: A Thursday.

Judge: A Thursday. Okay. So that doesn’t sound so unreasonable. So what happened?

Plaintiff: Because I had an interview—a job interview—that Friday. So me not being able to drive to my interview, I ended up missing it because—

Judge: Take public transportation to the interview.

Plaintiff: Because there’s—uh—Cleveland public transportation is terrible.

Judge: People use it.

Plaintiff: Yeah, people use it, but the bus drivers are real nasty with people, and I’ve been in alter—

Judge: You really had to miss the interview? You could have made it, you just—All right, so what happens then?

Plaintiff: I told him Saturday wasn’t going to work. He was like, “Well, you know, I don’t know what to tell you.” I’m like, “Well, just give me my money back.” We set up a date for Tuesday the next week.

Judge: So Tuesday comes—wait, what’s the Tuesday date for?

Plaintiff: For him to give me my money back.

Judge: So then Tuesday comes.

Plaintiff: He tells me, “Oh, you would have been better off getting the work done by me and paying for my parts because I don’t have the money to return to you.” At that point, I said, “Look, I’m not going to argue with you. I’m just going to file a small claims case against you.”

Judge: So what’s your version of how things went down?

Defendant: I don’t remember all these dates, but I can tell you exactly what went on. I would never turn him down from doing the work. I always was telling him we could do the work, but his times didn’t match up with mine. So he wanted his money back, and I couldn’t give him the money back. I said, “You’ll be better off getting the work done from me ‘cause I don’t have the money.” I just had Christmas and all this, so I don’t have the money. All my money went to my kids.

Judge: Right, but you were taking—that was money that was for labor, for working. So that’s kind of on you that you spent the money.

Defendant: Yeah, it was labor that I thought I was going to do.

Judge: Right. The issue is about parts and who was going to pay for the parts. Your pricing on the 3rd was X and his was X minus what?

Plaintiff: He was $27 cheaper.

Judge: Okay, so he wants to save $27. Who cares? Whether it’s $27 or $127, it’s his money. So he decides he’s going to buy the parts. But what was the problem then with the schedule?

Judge: After reviewing the evidence and considering both arguments, I find in favor of the plaintiff. The defendant is ordered to return the $200 that was originally taken. However, the additional $800 claim for inconvenience and alleged threats is denied, as there is insufficient evidence to substantiate these claims.

Plaintiff: Your Honor, but the threats—

Judge: I understand your concerns, but based on the evidence presented, there is no concrete proof of any damages or threats beyond your statements. Unless you can provide further documentation or witness testimony, I cannot rule in your favor on that claim.

Defendant: So, I only have to pay back the $200?

Judge: Correct. You must return the $200 to the plaintiff within 14 days. Failure to comply may result in further legal consequences.

Plaintiff: This is unfair! I was harassed for days.

Judge: I have made my ruling based on the legal standards required. If you wish to pursue additional damages, you may seek further legal action. But for today, this case is concluded.

Judge: Well, let's say six months before she retired, she says: Look, I've got Parkinson's.

It's getting progressively worse.

I can still do the job now, but I can see that I'm not going to be able to do the job for very much longer, and I look ahead to what's going to happen after I retire, and I'm going to be subjected to this retirement structure that gives me only 24 months, and I think that's discriminatory.

Putting aside the question of whether that's a valid claim under the ADA, why could she not sue at that point?

Lawyer of Defendant: Because she would be seeking to enjoin conduct that is not unlawful under the ADA, because she would be seeking the employer to not do something to an unqualified individual which it otherwise would not be prohibited by the ADA from doing.

It might be prohibited under another statute.

It would be no different if --

Judge: Well, she claims it's doing some -- she's qualified at that point, and she's claimed -- she claims it's doing something to her at that point.

So is she not aggrieved? Does she not have Article III standing because there's a -- an imminent threat of what she claims is unlawful conduct in the future?

Lawyer of Defendant: No.

It would be no different if she knew that her disease would render her totally disabled and she wanted to enjoin the City from terminating her employment before it did so, when, of course, under Title I, employers are allowed to terminate employees the moment they become unqualified and they can't do the job with or without a reasonable accommodation.

She would be seeking to enjoin the City from doing something that is not unlawful under the ADA.

It might be an equal protection violation.

It might be a breach of contract. It might be all of these other things, but it would not be a violation of Title I of the ADA.

Judge: Well, given her allegations, do you think she has a facially valid breach-of-contract claim?

Lawyer of Defendant: Absolutely not.

She -- her -- the -- there is no breach of contract because the policy was changed in 2003, 15 years before she retired.

And, under Florida law, governmental employees are permitted to change retirement policies before the rights under them vest.

So, if we had changed -- we would not have been allowed to change it after she retired.

She would have had a vested right. But 15 years before she retired, we changed the policy to not treat her worse but to treat her slightly less preferentially than she was already receiving.

Before the policy change, disability retirees, even if they retired with five or 10 years, were given the same health insurance subsidy as people who worked for 25 years.

So they were receiving preferential treatment over similarly situated non-disabled employees who had the same amount of years.

And then the City changed it to start treating disability retirees more equally with everyone else and said you -- now you also have to work 25 years to get the full subsidy, but, out of compassion, because you were forced to retire due to a disability, we will give you 24 months of the subsidy, whereas we would otherwise give you nothing if you were a non-disabled person.

And, uncoincidentally, 24 months is exactly how long it takes for a totally disabled person to then become Medicare-eligible and get Medicare insurance.

So the City bridged that gap between when a disability retiree retires early and the two years that it would take to start getting --

Judge: Can -- can you explain --

Lawyer of Defendant: --health insurance under Medicare.

Judge: -- the Medicare insurance and -- and how that works? So, after the two years, someone in this position gets the health insurance benefits that you're giving them for the two years in the interim, correct? Or it's similar.

Lawyer of Defendant: If they -- if they apply -- if they --

Judge: I mean, I don't know how Medicare matches up with your health insurance benefits.

Lawyer of Defendant: So a person like the Petitioner who -- who alleges they are totally disabled --

Judge: Or admitted.

Lawyer of Defendant: -- could qualify under Social Security disability.

And the Medicare Act says, once you become eligible for Social Security disability because you're permanently disabled, you then become eligible for Medicare Parts A and B.

So that bridges that gap.

We pay -- the City paid for her --

Judge: And -- and --

Lawyer of Defendant: --health insurance until --

Judge: -- I think --

Lawyer of Defendant: -- those 24 months.

Judge: I'm sorry to interrupt.

I think you were saying the City did that precisely to bridge that gap so that someone who's totally disabled is not left without health insurance --I'm not sure of the "because" here, but --

Lawyer of Defendant: Mm-hmm.

Judge: -- that's what you're representing, I think -- to bridge the gap so that someone's not left without health insurance in that two years.

Lawyer of Defendant: Right.

Because, if a non-disabled person were in Petitioner's shoes and retired with only 20 years, they would receive absolutely no health insurance subsidy.

And they also, because they're not disabled or totally disabled, would not be Medicare-eligible if they weren't 65.

So the City continued to treat its disability retirees with preferential treatment over non-disabled employees who were similarly situated with an equivalent amount of years of service.

Judge: The problem with this argument in my mind is who bears the cost. You're saying the public fisc should bear the cost because, in two years, Medicare will pay what we used to pay, correct? Because, under the Medicare Act, they don't --they exhaust private remedies first, and then the public fisc pays?

Lawyer of Defendant: Well, there is no requirement that any employer provide health insurance subsidies.

Judge: That we're putting aside.

That's the nature of the claim here, which is you promised me you would.

And assuming that were true, which I know you fight on every level, but assuming you made an explicit promise, I'm going to pay you this amount of money, and then took it away so the public fisc could pick it up, that's what you're doing, is you're saying we're -- this is just always a matter of who's going to pay, us --

Lawyer of Defendant: No, because, if -- if --

Judge:
Okay, Elias Samas.

Plaintiff:
That's me.

You are suing Speo Anopolis for $1,500 in fees that you say he owes you for testimony and expert work that you provided many moons ago—10 years ago—on a case for him. Tell me what’s going on.

Mr. Anopolis called me, and he asked me to testify and identify certain signatures.

I am a certified graphologist and analyst.

Yeah.

10 years ago.

Okay.

I sent him letters, and I visited him at his home, but still, he never paid anything.

Why did you wait so long to file a lawsuit?

Plaintiff:
[Pauses] Okay… that’s it. I have no explanation.

Sometimes that's the best answer because it's the honest answer.

From property and overseas properties.

Overseas?

So, she was denying she had signed something.

I have some letters from my ex-wife, so he put it together and said, "Yes, it's the same person." I asked him to give me a report for the court.

So, if I remember well—because it's 10 years ago—I think I paid him $1,000.

Case dismissed in favor of the defendant.

Judge: So you're fighting with saying that it caused an unreasonable seizure?

Plaintiff's Lawyer: Yes, with the malicious prosecution caused an unreasonable seizure.

Judge: Are you accepting that language or are you fighting that language?

Plaintiff's Lawyer: So I don't mean to quibble. Thompson has resulted—

Judge: It is important.

Plaintiff's Lawyer: It is important. So I think the Thompson formulation is correct. The other elements get you to the unreasonable part. "Resulted in a seizure."

Judge: What—what are the other elements? If it doesn't cause an unreasonable seizure, how—you're not making—

Plaintiff's Lawyer: So what's caused the—

Judge: Why don't you just list what you think the elements are?

Plaintiff's Lawyer: Right. So the appropriate mens rea, which this Court hasn't resolved, favorable termination; initiation of legal process; lacking probable cause—so that's where the unreasonable part comes in—resulted in a seizure. All we're saying is don't double-count the unreasonable. You don't have to prove something separate from the mens rea plus lack of probable cause.

Judge: So, Counsel, I have a question about the element that brings us here today—

Plaintiff's Lawyer: Sure.

Judge: —which is the initiation of legal process lacking probable cause, right? Am I—

Plaintiff's Lawyer: That's correct, yes.

Judge: Okay. And that's separate from the causation—

Plaintiff's Lawyer: Exactly.

Judge: —that we've been discussing. I guess you're now saying that everybody's on the same page that the lower court was wrong about that.

Plaintiff's Lawyer: Yep.

Judge: Is that—

Plaintiff's Lawyer: That's correct.

Judge: Okay. And I'm trying to understand why the lower court was wrong, and I think I have a theory, and I'm hoping you can help me determine whether or not I'm right about this.

Plaintiff's Lawyer: Sure.

Judge: I'm wondering whether the lower court—whether they were just taking what is a basic principle related to probable cause in another context. The other kind of Fourth Amendment claim that one might bring is the unlawful seizure, false arrest, etc. There, you have no probable cause. And we would say that if there are three or four different charges being brought and your claim was unlawful arrest, when you proved lack of probable cause with respect to only one of those three or four charges, we would say: Too bad, you still lose, right? So it's sort of like the same idea that the lower court was latching onto because it—there are cases that say that, but it seems like they're in a different context. They're not malicious prosecution theory. It's the unlawful arrest theory. So it's like probable cause is doing different work depending upon the theory. Am I—does that make any sense?

Plaintiff's Lawyer: That's exactly right.

Judge: Okay.

Plaintiff's Lawyer: So that's exactly how the lower court justified this rule.

Judge: Yes.

Plaintiff's Lawyer: It said the same rules apply to false arrest and malicious prosecution. But both at common law and in this Court's Fourth Amendment cases, there has always been a distinction between the warrantless arrest context, where, as Your Honor notes, it doesn't matter if the officer had one charge or ten charges or zero charges in mind, what matters is if there's probable cause out there somewhere, and the legal process cases like Gerstein and Franks and County of Riverside, where you're required to specify the charges, and that's for a couple of good reasons that I'm happy to explain.

Judge: And, I mean, is one of them because the malicious prosecution context is really about the culpability of the process server? It's about the person who is initiating process maliciously? It's not really about whether you were committing bad behavior—so, in other words, you could have a person who actually is a criminal, right, he's committing crimes, but for Charges 2, 3, and 4, but if Charge 1, there was no basis for it, baseless, and maliciously added on to the thing, that person would still—added on to the indictment, that person would still have at least theoretically a claim for malicious prosecution with respect to that charge?

Plaintiff's Lawyer: That's exactly right. And that's reflected, again, in this Court's Fourth Amendment cases. So, to take an extreme example, Gerstein says that if you don't have process, right—no warrant, no hearing—it doesn't matter if you're actually guilty of absolutely everything. It's still a Fourth Amendment violation because we care about the process. The Fourth Amendment says what makes a seizure of any duration reasonable is that someone neutral and detached weighed in and signed off on this. It's not just the police officer's discretion. And what the "any-crime" rule would do would basically move this back into a police officer's discretion if they could just lie to the magistrate about the set of charges they have on that side.

Judge: So what are we disputing here? Both sides agree that the lower court got it wrong. I guess I'm trying to understand why we're continuing on.

Plaintiff's Lawyer: I don't think—

Judge: Like, what is—what is left of this case if the fundamental issue has been essentially resolved? Should we have appointed someone on the other side of the question that was presented here?

Plaintiff's Lawyer: So I don't think so because, you know, I do think Respondents are arguing for an affirmance on a sort of waiver plus this other element theory. I think that this just means it's an easy opinion to write. So just last week, in Sheetz, this Court said, when there's radical agreement that the court below erred in applying a categorical premise, we leave all the kind of additional nuanced arguments for remand. I think this Court should follow the same course here.

Judge: Well, radical agreement is a good thing if it exists, but I'm not quite sure there is radical agreement here. I thought the argument on the other side, which is presented in Respondents' brief and also in one of the amicus briefs, which I don't have with me, is that the lower court had a prior decision in which they essentially adopted the Solicitor General's position, and what they did here should be understood in light of that. Isn't that the argument on the other side?

Plaintiff's Lawyer: So I think the other side is making two arguments. One is the one that Your Honor articulated, which is that somehow the lower court secretly was applying a different rule. But, as the United States says at page 23, that description conflicts with the language of the decision below.

Judge: Yeah, okay, so there's a dispute about what they did here, whether they were following—I’ve forgotten the name of the earlier lower court decision.

Plaintiff's Lawyer: Howse.

Judge: Okay. Whether they were following that or not. So there's not really radical agreement as much as one might like to have it.

Plaintiff's Lawyer: So I think that there is disagreement about how the "caused a seizure," "resulted in a seizure" element works in the nuances, right? What counterfactual you run, how exactly—whether it's the legal process could or the bogus charge would have or the bogus charge could have. But that's a separate element from lack of probable cause. And I think everyone agrees that the decision below, which said, because probable cause existed on at least one charge, his malicious prosecution claim failed, full stop, right? That's the holding of the decision below. Everyone agrees that's not correct. That's not the rule.

Judge: And just to clarify on the "caused the seizure" point, your position—say we don't reach it—on remand would be it doesn't matter if it actually caused the seizure, it doesn't matter if these misdemeanor offenses would have led to the same amount of the three days in detention? That would be your position, right?

Plaintiff's Lawyer: So I'd articulate it slightly differently.

Judge: Okay.

Plaintiff's Lawyer: I would say that the tainted legal process surely caused the seizure.

Judge: Well, you never really got a chance to answer the second part of my question about standing. Could you address that? What sense does it make to say the plaintiff wins on voluntary cessation because you can't prove that it's very unlikely that he will be put back on if he does some additional thing, but on standing, he will be unable to show, as required by precedent, that he has standing for purposes of injunctive relief because he can't show that he's under an imminent threat?

Defendant's Lawyer: I agree completely. He's not going to be able to show what's required to get injunctive relief. And, as we point out in our opening brief, the plaintiff seems to have abandoned the argument. He's not going to be able to get a declaration either because a declaration has to be forward-looking, and at the moment, there is no live case or controversy. So even if you were to say that the case isn't moot, I don't think he would be entitled to any relief at the end of the day, which I think just underscores why the case is moot. I mean, mootness and standing are both attempts to implement Article III's case or controversy requirement. I understand that mootness is a little more relaxed than standing, but both of the exceptions that make it a little more relaxed are all about evading review, either because the challenged action by its nature is so evanescent that ordinary judicial review won't complete or because of docket manipulation and gamesmanship.

Judge: Well, is the upshot of what you've said that Article III can't be satisfied in this case unless the basis for putting him on in the first place and taking him off later is disclosed at least to the judge? And so that's what this is all about. I'll ask opposing counsel whether that's his understanding. Are we going to say that this sensitive information that you don't want to disclose has to be disclosed in any case in which somebody who has been on the No Fly List wants to contest that?

Defendant's Lawyer: Yeah, and that's precisely what we're trying to avoid here. And we think this is exactly the kind of circumstance where the presumption of regularity in national security does play a role in that evaluation of facts. Now a court could look at all the classified evidence and then determine whether it's likely to recur or not, or can just hold as a matter of law that the government is fulfilling its national security responsibilities in good faith, it's evaluating people against the standard correctly, and even if the allegation is that it didn't do that at time one, we cannot reasonably expect the government to fall down and act in bad faith at time two.

Judge: Justice, do you have any questions?

Judge: I think you're right that he's given up an injunction, but he wants a declaration. You don't think a declaration that said he attended a mosque and that wasn't enough to put him on the list would help so that he could go back to the same mosque?

Defendant's Lawyer: No. A declaration wouldn't presumably reveal classified information, but—

Judge: It wouldn't. But it would basically say whatever ground that the court has looked at wasn't sufficient to put him on the list, so if he repeats that conduct, he won't be put at risk.

Defendant's Lawyer: So, again, we think the declaration actually tells him that he's not going to be put back on the list—

Judge: No, because—

Defendant's Lawyer: —based on the currently available information.

Judge: —we can go back to the fundraising. But, anyway.

Defendant's Lawyer: Well, but—

Judge: Okay. Thank you, counsel.

Judge: Is the government able to say anything about how this relisting occurs or, more particularly, about how often it occurs? In other words, are you capable of telling us anything about when you're taken off a list, how often is somebody put back on a list?

Defendant's Lawyer: Unfortunately, I can't tell you that information. I don't have it. I would have to imagine that in the 20-some years the No Fly List has been in existence, surely there have been some individuals who were at one time listed, removed, and then listed again. I'm unaware of any claim for the litigated cases, and there are several dozen of them. I'm unaware of any claim there of someone who was on the list, taken off the list, and then put back on. That is just not something that we have seen in, again, years and years of this kind of litigation.

Judge: And the possibility of just going before a judge in camera—has the government ever thought that that's a possible way to figure this difficult problem out? You know, you're not disclosing it to the world. You tell the judge, here's what got him on the list. Here's why we think he's not going back on the list.

Defendant's Lawyer: Yeah. So, obviously, we've done that in the cases we've taken to judgment, but that's been on the merits. So I gather your question is, well, what if it's just for the limited purpose of mootness.

Judge: Yeah. In other words, you're saying that he hasn't satisfied the standard. It's not going to recur again. He's saying, I don't know. It seems like it might recur again because I don't know why you took me off the list. You go to the judge and you say, judge, here's why he was on the list, here's why he's not on the list anymore, here's why we don't think he's going back on the list.

Defendant's Lawyer: Yeah.

Judge: Thank you, counsel.

Defendant's Lawyer: That that's just unlikely to happen.

Judge: All parties, please raise your right hand.

Welcome back to The People’s Court. Next case in the docket: The plaintiff rented his car to the defendant so he could drive for Uber, and the car came back with a urine stench and tickets. But the defendant says the plaintiff called the cops and falsely claimed the car was stolen. It’s the case of You’re in Uber Trouble Now.

Thank you, Douglas.

Judge: Mr. Go, you are suing Mr. Zanada for $5,000 for damages stemming from a car that you rented to him. And you have a counterclaim against him for $55,000 as well, for making false allegations to the police, for lost wages, and some other miscellaneous items. What is going on, gentlemen?

Plaintiff: Can I start?

Judge: Yes, go ahead.

Plaintiff: The thing is, I rented him the car on March 6, 2018.

Judge: What kind of car is it?

Plaintiff: It’s a Toyota Camry.

Judge: A Toyota Camry. Okay. You rented it to him for what?

Plaintiff: For driving, like he was going to drive for Uber and all that stuff.

Judge: Okay. And so you used the car, Mr. Zanada? You didn’t have your own car to be an Uber driver?

Defendant: No.

Judge: Okay. And so you rented the car for how long?

Plaintiff: Almost six months, Your Honor.

Judge: Okay. And what goes wrong?

Plaintiff: The thing is, at the beginning, I told him the rent was $350 a week. So, uh, first two weeks, he was negotiating. Then I said, "Okay, for the first two weeks, I’ll give it to you for $300, but after that, you have to pay me $350." But in this time, within these two weeks, he had an accident. And when the two weeks passed, he never paid me $350.

Judge: But you accepted $300?

Plaintiff: Yeah, I accepted $300.

Judge: Okay. And then the week after that, you also accepted $300. And the week after that. And the week after that. And the week after that. Do you have a contract with him where it says he’s supposed to pay $350?

Plaintiff: No, I don’t have any.

Judge: And what were you supposed to pay, Mr. Zanada? $350 or $300?

Defendant: Your Honor, the deal was $300. You know, the car is a 2014 model. The average rent for this car from any car dealer is $300.

Judge: When do you decide and why that you’re going to take the car back?

Plaintiff: In July. What happened was, when I told him, he said he was going to raise the fee, but he only went up to $325. He said he was going to give me $350, but then he only gave me $325 a week. And that’s when I decided, no, I’m going to take my car back because I can’t do that.

Judge: So then, on August 21st, you told him what?

Plaintiff: I told him, "You have one week."

Judge: So on August 21st, you tell him he has until August 28th?

Plaintiff: Yeah, and then he says, "Okay, I’m going to give you the car back." But after two days, I noticed that he had a ticket pending. He didn’t pay that, and he didn’t even tell me. So the ticket came by mail, and I texted him that he had to pay it.

Judge: Did you pay the ticket?

Defendant: Your Honor, I was going to go for a hearing because it wasn’t my fault, and I wanted to contest it. But he put pressure on me to return the car. I said the deal wasn’t over, and he had to give me one week's notice.

Judge: He did tell you one week in advance.

Defendant: No, Your Honor, I have all the documents with me—texts, everything. I have proof of all my payments being $300. He never told me $350.

Judge: Don’t worry about that. He’s not going to get $350 unless he has a contract that says $350 when he spent a year accepting $300. Now, you end up calling the police and telling them that he is not authorized to have your car?

Plaintiff: Yes.

Judge: Why didn’t you just call him and say, "I told you to return the car"?

Plaintiff: I was calling him, but he kept telling me that he had another two tickets. He said if he returned my car, he wouldn’t pay those tickets. I have proof in the texts.

[The judge reviews the texts and finds that the plaintiff repeatedly asked for the car back, while the defendant insisted on keeping it longer.]

Judge: Did you pay for the week of August 28th?

Plaintiff: No.

Judge: When did you get the car back?

Plaintiff: On August 31st.

Judge: How did you get the car back?

Plaintiff: He left it five miles away from my home and left the key there.

Judge: Why didn’t you bring it back to him?

Defendant: He was threatening me. I didn’t want any violence.

Judge: All right. Now let’s talk about damages.

Judge :
Ms. Dubin, and -- and you -- you say this actual creditor requirement, which everyone acknowledges exists in 544(b), is there for a reason.

And I've been struggling with that, and I want your reaction to -- to this. Is it because Congress was making a policy choice related to its concern about the potential disruptive nature of avoidance? So the general trustee avoidance power has a two-year statute of limitations because, when you come in and you void a previously existing transaction involving innocent third parties, that's like a big deal.

That's causing a lot of disruption in the market. And so, ordinarily, a trustee can only do that for two years after that transaction has occurred.

There's no such limitation in the 544(b) world in terms of timing, but maybe -- maybe -- this is my theory -- the -- the relevant limitation is this actual creditor requirement, that what's happening there is the trustee gets the avoidance power but only to the extent that an actual creditor could have affected the same kind of disruption in the market by bringing this kind of action on his own.

What do you think about that?

I think you have it exactly right, but I would add one piece to it, and it's why does Congress care that an actual creditor has that right in the real world? And it's because that transfer could be invalidated in the real world as to that transferee.

So all Congress is doing is saying that the trustee, instead of leaving that right to one creditor alone, he is going to vindicate the principle of equality among creditors, equality of distribution, bring it into the bankruptcy and that transfer can be avoided for the benefit of all creditors.

But, absent that situation where this transfer is already vulnerable, Congress's policy judgments about repose, about who should be able to avoid a transfer govern, and 548 governs, other than in that situation.

-- intermediate -- intermediate scrutiny applies by -- by the terms of what you're arguing.

Didn't we already dispose of that kind of reasoning with our equal protection cases that looked at things like interracial marriage, where we said, even though it applies to both, it's still making a racial classification? Even though whites can't married -- marry non-whites and non-whites can't marry whites in the statute, right, so both are equally disadvantaged, we said that's not an argument for why you shouldn't have a heightened scrutiny or why the statute is not making a race-based classification.

Judge:
Okay.

Lawyer of Plaintiff:
We are not arguing that -- that you can discriminate and draw lines so long as you do so both against boys and against girls.

PLAINTIFF: Yes, Your Honor, another one.

Lawyer of Plaintiff: Thank you, Your Honor, and may it please the Court. On the first question presented, the 30-day filing deadline in Section 1252(b)(1) is not jurisdictional.

That provision fails this Court's clear statement test, and Stone is not a binding jurisdictional ruling.

On the second question, the petition here was timely because it was filed within 30 days of the Board's CAT order.

In Section 1252(a)(4), Congress provided for judicial review of CAT claims, and precedent confirms that courts may review CAT orders together with removal orders.

It follows that a CAT order becomes final at the same time as a removal order so that challenges to both orders can be raised together in the same petition for review.

Under amicus's position, many CAT claims would be unreviewable, but when Congress wanted to preclude judicial review in the INA, it did so expressly.

It is unlikely that Congress would have precluded review of many claims indirectly by way of a generic 30-day filing deadline.

I welcome the Court's questions.

Judge: Can you give us an example of an instance in which a claim, claim B can be considered with -- or appeal B can be considered with appeal A, but appeal B actually expands the time limit for appeal A?

Lawyer of Plaintiff: I think the best analogy is the one that was pointed to, which is that a conviction cannot be appealed until after the sentence, even though the sentence doesn't affect the validity of the conviction or merge into the conviction.

And --

Judge: Okay.

Let's try the civil context.

Can you think of any other provision?

Lawyer of Plaintiff: I suppose there the way it would work in a -- in a remedial scheme, a remedial decision as opposed to a liability decision, the -- the remedial decision wouldn't affect the liability decision, but we would still under the final judgment principle not allow an appeal of the liability ruling until after the remedial decision is done.

And I think the same basic logic applies here, because as precedent points out, a CAT order does not affect the validity of a removal order, but our position is that the removal order doesn't become final until the end of CAT proceedings because that's when the agency has finished its work on the case and disassociated itself from the case, which is the basic final judgment principle that always applies in judicial review in the courts of appeals.

Judge: Mr. McDowell, can you give us some examples of -- you say that adopting amicus's view would leave certain CAT orders unreviewable.

Can you give us some examples?

Lawyer of Plaintiff: So I think under his position, I think the CAT order in this case would be unreviewable because Petitioner didn't file the petition for review within 30 days of the Section 1228 removal.

Judge: I think your friend, though, in this case would say, well, he could have done.

Lawyer of Plaintiff: So our point is that if you're referring to the premature protective petition scheme idea, I think that that -- there are two potential legal problems there.

One is the zipper clause, which contemplates a single petition for review at the end of the case; whereas the premature filing of a petition supplemented by a later filing related to the CAT claim, I think, runs counter to that idea. I also think that if the Court were to adopt amicus's reading of the statute, one potential upshot of that is that Congress intended to foreclose review of these claims. And if Congress wanted to foreclose review of these claims, we don't see how courts and litigants could adopt a work-around scheme to get around that.

But obviously our front-line position is that Congress did want review of these claims.

Judge: I think your friend on the other side would say, well, they can file these protective appeals and courts of appeals can hold onto them and -- and then when the CAT order matures, you're good to go.

Would courts of appeals have to keep those notices of appeal on their docket? Could they --could they -- is there a risk they might dismiss them? I'm just not sure -- I -- I've not held a notice of appeal on my docket as an appellate court judge on the Tenth Circuit for, what, a year, two years while the CAT order is ongoing.

Lawyer of Plaintiff: And I think that's --

Judge: I just don't know. I haven't encountered that.

Lawyer of Plaintiff: I think this points up another problem with this idea, which is that I don't actually think it's a particularly effective way of preserving judicial review because these procedures lie within the discretion of the courts of appeals.

And so some courts of appeals may deny a motion to hold a case in abeyance.

So I think we would end up with a patchwork system where some circuits are generally preserving review of these claims; others are not.

And there might be even variation within circuits.

We also see some other practical problems with the system.

One is that it leads to a flood of meritless prophylactic petitions which really aren't challenging the removal order itself.

They're just a placeholder for a later decision that may actually go in the alien's favor.

The second problem is that it creates a trap for the unwary because many people in this system are unrepresented and won't know that they have to file this sort of counterintuitive protective petition.

And then, finally, it does create administrative burdens for the courts and for the government.

And what we've seen in practice is that many of these protective petitions end up being abandoned at the end of the day because the person gets withholding relief or because they just don't want to pursue the petition.

So it really is just a waste of resources in many cases.

Judge: Could I speculate and say you're right, if we accept amicus's argument, basically CAT orders won't be reviewable because -- in those places where the courts don't want to stay the appeal of a petition for removal.

Lawyer of Plaintiff: Yes.

Lawyer of Plaintiff: That's right.

I think the Spencer v. Texas case from the '60s was one in which the Court, I think, gently endorsed bifurcation, and that was a state case.

Defendant's Lawyer: Right.

Defendant's Lawyer: I—

What the tribes are arguing here is that ISDA also obligates IHS to subsidize the tribes' expenditures of funds that they don't receive from IHS under the contract but, rather, collect from third parties as supplemental revenue.

Judge: Yeah, I have a question about the phrase in (c)(1), the specific intent. Do you agree it's specific intent with the intent to impair the object's integrity? Okay.

Defendant’s Lawyer: Well, the last thing I would point out about Employees before I turn to federal versus state is that a critical part of Employees was that the federal government could still enforce the FLSA against the states. The government's argument here takes us a step further and says no one, not even the federal government or the state governments, can enforce Employees against any governmental furnishers because the term "person" does not apply—the definition of "person" does not apply to 1681s, which is the administrative enforcement provision. So this would be a step beyond Employees.

On the federal versus state issue, Congress—I don't think there's a different textual standard in this Court's cases between waiving sovereign immunity and abrogating state immunity. It's simply that what Seminole Tribe and its progeny hold is that where there's a conflict between what Congress wants and what the state wants, the state wins absent—unless it's in a few discrete areas where the Constitution abrogates state sovereign immunity or it's a Fourteenth Amendment case.

But if this statute were in the Fourteenth Amendment context, I think this language would be sufficient to abrogate state sovereign immunity just as it waives federal sovereign immunity.

Judge: Counsel, I think it's an unavoidable consequence of your interpretation that the statute authorizes criminal prosecution of the United States.

Now, if there were such a prosecution and the United States were convicted, what would the pre-sentencing report look like?

(Laughter.)

Defendant’s Lawyer: So, as a technical matter, I would say the proper defendant in an FCRA action would be an agency, not the United States itself. That said, I don't think this Court has squarely held how far the federal—the absence of criminal liability for governmental entities extends. Last year, in Halk Bank, I think you said that criminal law can apply to foreign states and their arms. I don't think you've said that in the context of domestic agencies.

In Bennett v. Spear, you suggested that perhaps agencies could be criminally or civilly liable for failing to adhere to a biological opinion.

Judge: So you agree that your reading of the statute leads to that result, that a criminal prosecution can be brought against the United States, whether it's through a United States agency or the nation as a whole?

Defendant’s Lawyer: That—that is one reading. I can't say it's absurd since this Court has not said it's absurd previously. That said, the easier path for this Court would be to follow what Judge Katsas said in the D.C. Circuit in the Mowrer decision, what Judge Krause said below, which is that any contextual reason you might have for disregarding an otherwise controlling statutory definition in the criminal context is unique to the criminal context.

Judge: Well, but how—

Judge: Why is that?

Judge: —does that work? I mean, n and o and q are all added at the same time. We can add s to that too because s raises its own anomalies. They're all enacted at the same time. And they're all different kinds of liability provisions.

And you're essentially saying—and you're right that Judge Krause said this below, Judge Katsas said it—but you're saying, well, you know, it fits with n and o, so we'll use one interpretation there. It doesn't fit with q, so we'll use a different interpretation there.

Defendant’s Lawyer: So—so one correction. Q stems from the original 1970 act that enacted the definition of "person." The 1996 Act, Congress amended the civil liability provision and amended the administrative enforcement provision to extend—authorize enforcement against persons. But it didn't add in the term "person" in 1681q in 1996. That's—

Judge: Okay.

Defendant’s Lawyer: It enhanced the penalties in the definition. With that said, each—there's no—I think, well, q rests as sort of a stand-alone self-contained provision. It doesn't interact with the rest of the FCRA in any way.

It contains its own substantive prohibition and its own criminal penalties, whereas you have the liability provisions and the enforcement provisions, which are designed to enforce the substantive—

Judge: But you're—

Defendant’s Lawyer: —provisions of FCRA.

Judge: —to pick up on the Chief Justice's question, it's not just criminal prosecution against the United States, it's punitive damages against the United States, very unusual. State and federal enforcement against the United States would be contemplated if we took your interpretation.

There's an express waiver in another provision that was enacted months earlier. The government says that's an anomaly. The Privacy Act is a carefully reticulated scheme for imposing liability on the government for Privacy Act violations. They're saying that you would create this anomaly.

So there's a string of anomalies that the government says would be created by your position.

Defendant’s Lawyer: Well, I think the only one that qualifies as even a debatable anomaly would be 1681q. The government—it just doesn't—I don't think they've made any—

Judge: I thought your—

Defendant’s Lawyer: —case about what—

Judge: —I thought the answer was that it's not that the definition is shrinking or expanding provision by provision, the definition of "person," that you can have this group of entities that are defined statutorily as persons and that carries throughout the whole statute.

But there may be various provisions in which subsets of persons are carved out because they have other defenses. I mean, just because "person" is there doesn't mean that, you know, every person will automatically and always be subject to the entirety of that separate provision.

As the government said, they could have separate defenses. They could have other reasons why they're not subject to criminal liability even though they're still persons for the purpose of the statute.

So, if that's happening, then the government's observation that in certain parts of the statute persons are not going to—or governments, even though they're persons, are not going to be subject to that part of the statute, it seems to me, doesn't really help their argument because, as another judge said, we don't see even that happening in n and o, which is really all that is at issue here.

Defendant’s Lawyer: Right. I think the government argument really tries to focus on provisions away from n and o because there is no textual basis in looking at n and o for not applying the definition as written.

Judge: And there's no real basis for suggesting that—

That notwithstanding the statute saying that "persons" is defined in this way throughout the entirety of the provision, sometimes it's not really defined this way.

It seems to me that is an implausible reading of the text of the statute that very clearly defines the term and says it applies everywhere.

So, if it's not actually operative in certain places, it's not because the definition has changed. It's because something else is going on that would prevent that consequence occurring in that particular circumstance.

Defendant’s Lawyer: I think that's right. For example, in 1681g, subsection g, Congress has an expressed alternative definition of "person" for purposes of that provision. That doesn't mean somehow n and o become ambiguous in terms of where the definition applies.

Judge: Well, it's not only—

Judge: —that you think that it's right, but under our sovereign immunity precedent, that has to be the only way of reading it?

In other words, there's no other way and all these other areas where "person" doesn't actually mean "person" the way it's defined in the statute but means much less, and there's not just one, there's two, there's three, and you have to say that changing the reading of "person" is the only way you could read that because, if it isn't, then sovereign immunity, it seems, is implicit in the statute in a way that other provisions may not be.

Defendant’s Lawyer: Well, I—assuming the hypothetical, which is that there are variations in what "person" means throughout the statute—

Judge: It's not my hypothetical, but go ahead.

Defendant’s Lawyer: Yes. The fact remains, if the Court's going to depart from the otherwise controlling definition, it looks to something else in the statute that provides a countervailing argument.

For example, in Utility Air, the Court looked to provisions of the statute that said, if we apply the definition of "air pollutant" to these provisions, we have an unworkable statute.

And to the extent you conclude that "person" doesn't apply to 1681q because that's unworkable or improbable that Congress intended to extend criminal liability this far, that is an argument, an interpretation that would be limited to 1681q.

Defendant’s Lawyer: Well, the previous case didn't cost the bank money in the sense of forcing it to make outlays, but it cost the bank money in the sense of making it more difficult to attract customers and thereby earn money. Defendant’s Lawyer: One thing you would want to look at is to what extent the bank could earn money on the escrow account and what relationship that potential earning has to the interest it was required to pay out. Instead, they argue that escrow accounts protect the bank’s collateral against risks like failure to pay taxes or failure to maintain insurance.

Defendant’s Lawyer: That would be right.

Defendant’s Lawyer: Yes, then you’re at least trying to determine whether that mandatory outlay is significant.

Judge: Okay.

Judge: Next question. This significance test—it's not common in preemption inquiries. Usually, we need an answer that applies everywhere and for all time. If economic conditions change, does that mean a law could be preempted one day and not the next? Would different states with the same law have different preemption outcomes depending on their economic circumstances? That seems like an odd way to approach preemption.

Defendant’s Lawyer: I would point to prior cases that discuss similar preemption standards. If a state law explicitly regulates core aspects of an industry, like rates or services, it’s preempted outright. But when a law regulates something else that indirectly affects an industry, courts have had to engage in pragmatic inquiries.

Judge: But you’re not giving much reassurance about the concern that this could lead to different rules in different states or different rulings depending on when a case is brought.

Defendant’s Lawyer: That is something Congress signed up for. But it's actually a benefit to the banks. If Congress had prioritized administrative ease above all else, it could have just relied on the antidiscrimination standard. Instead, Congress provided an additional standard that allows banks to argue that a state law significantly interferes with their operations. They can choose to invoke it or not.

Judge: Moving on. The statute references prior cases. It states that "where Congress does not expressly condition the grant of power upon state permission, the Court has ordinarily found no such condition applies." It then references another case and states that the federal statute explicitly grants a national bank an authorization, permission, or power, and contains no indication that Congress intended to subject that power to local restriction. How do you interpret that?

Defendant’s Lawyer: That was in response to an argument made by the state in that case. The conflict was clear—the federal statute said national banks may sell insurance in small towns, but the state statute said they cannot. The state argued that the federal law only allowed banks to do this if state law permitted it. The Court rejected that, saying that’s not how federal authorizations usually work. If the federal law says banks can do something and state law says they can’t, federal law prevails.

Judge: One more question. You said earlier that banks could do this without material impairment. Is that still your position?

Defendant’s Lawyer: Yes. We have not seen any evidence suggesting that a bank could not comply.

Judge: If compliance results in higher costs for banks, leading to decreased availability of credit or higher interest rates, is that a material impairment?

Defendant’s Lawyer: Out-of-pocket expenses alone wouldn’t be enough. The banks would have to prove that the burden meaningfully deters them from offering their services.

Judge: Thank you.

Judge: Okay. So, if a willing buyer shows up—who owns the life insurance policy?

Defendant's Lawyer: The company is entitled to the proceeds of the life insurance. And that is hugely important when you're applying this test because the fundamental problem with the government's view—our view is that 77 percent of $3.86 million is $3 million.
The government's view is that 77 percent of $6.86 million is $5.3 million.
A willing buyer would never, at that moment, if buying Michael, the decedent's, shares, pay $5.3 million.
Why? Because a willing buyer would not be able to capture those life insurance proceeds by swooping in before the redemption.
Those life insurance proceeds belong to the company.

Judge: Well, the value has to go someplace. The 3 million goes someplace. Does it go into the value of the remaining stocks? And if it is there, why isn't the appropriate valuation 6.86 million?

Now, as a practical matter, the problem here and the fundamental issue that all of us are wrestling with is that what we know is that you can't use the $3 million as simply the valuation.
Why? Because, as I noted at the outset, we didn't satisfy the requirements of Section 2703.
And, therefore, you have to engage in this counterfactual inquiry, and the problem with the counterfactual inquiry that the government wants this Court to engage in is, again, that it requires you to disregard the redemption obligation.
Now it is true that one consequence of our interpretation is that, as to Thomas, the surviving stockholder, Thomas in some very real sense benefits from the increase in value by virtue of this transaction.
Why? Because Thomas goes from having 22 percent of the company to a hundred percent of the company.
But, under our approach, as under the government's approach, that is taken care of by the eventual application of the capital gains tax.

What the government wants you to do is to effectively take those life insurance proceeds into account twice—once when calculating the estate tax because the government wants you to tax this higher amount, $5.3 million—and, again, no hypothetical willing buyer would ever have paid that.

Crown would never have redeemed the shares for $5.3 million.
And I'm happy to explain why.

But then the government also will subject Thomas eventually to the capital gains tax on the increase in the value of his shares. And that, in our view, is the fundamental problem with the government's approach here, and that is why this is effectively double taxation.

And to just spell out for another sentence or two why the $5.3 million valuation—

Judge: But, if I can just stop you there because it seems that the fundamental problem with your approach is that Thomas's asset has quadrupled in value, and it's quadrupled in value without him putting a single cent more into the company.
And there might be some taxation effect in the end of all that, but—not sufficient to make up for the fact that it's a tell that your way of calculating the thing is wrong that somebody can come away with four times the value without putting a single cent into the company.

Defendant's Lawyer: So, with respect, I completely disagree with that, and let me explain why.

It is true that Thomas is in a very real sense practically the beneficiary of the life insurance proceeds.
Why? Because those proceeds extinguish the offsetting liability on the books, the offsetting redemption obligation.
And so this is a context in which Thomas does come away with the benefit of those proceeds because he is the sole owner of a company that is worth $3.86 million.

Now the government complains around the edges about the fact that it's the capital gains tax—the capital gains tax only operates upon realization, there is a stepped-up basis when someone dies and passes the stock along, and so forth.
But those are all features of the capital gains tax system.
That is not a bug with our position.

Again, our position is the rational one precisely because the tax system captures that increase.
And, of course, under our approach, Michael's heir is still, of course, paying the estate tax.
Michael's heir is paying the estate tax on stock at around $3 million, coincidentally roughly the amount that was contained in the buy-sell agreement, which I think confirms that that amount was a rational amount here.

But the problem with the $5.3 million, again, the government's view is—let's take Crown.
The government's view is presumably that if there had been a proper arm's-length agreement here, Crown would have been willing to pay $5.3 million to redeem this stock.

That would have required Crown to use all of the life insurance proceeds here, the entire $3.5 million, and also to dip into its operating assets in order to redeem those shares.
That illustrates why the government's position here is irrational.

Now, to be sure, I think there is a—
