Defendant’s Lawyer: I disagree with that. I think this Court definitively interpreted the phrase "first accrues" in Gabelli versus SEC just 11 years ago, and it said, in common parlance, a right first accrues when it comes into existence.

Plaintiff’s Lawyer: We made very clear that that was a default rule, a general rule that could be, of course, countermanded by Congress but that also could be countermanded by different circumstances. If you look at the Crown Coat opinion, for example, there’s a very explicit recognition by this Court of the hazards inherent in attempting to define for all purposes when a cause of action first accrues. A word like that should be interpreted in the light of the general purposes of the statute, with due regard to those practical ends which are to be served by any limitation of the time within which an action may be brought. You couldn’t find better language to point to. This is a really different context with really different interests, including reliance interests of many, many parties who are not before the Court. And courts have responded to that and created a different rule which has lasted—this is kind of a revolutionary ask.

Defendant’s Lawyer: We disagree. On Crown Coat, the top-line holding from Crown Coat as we read it is that Congress would not want the statute of limitations to start running until the plaintiff had the opportunity to sue. The language you just quoted, we see that as a recognition that obviously the underlying cause of action accrues differently depending on the type of claim. Torts accrue at different times than breaches of contract. Different torts can accrue at different times. And as far as the purposes of the statute, this Court said in Abbott Laboratories that the purpose of the APA was to embody a presumption of judicial review. So, if we're going to look at the underlying statute and its purposes, I think that cuts our way, not the government's way.

Judge: I thought that we had sort of basic first principles governing statutes of limitations. Ordinarily, we say that a cause of action arises—that is, accrues—when all of the facts necessary to establish the elements of that cause of action have occurred. In a tort situation, when there's a duty, if there's a breach and injury as a result of the breach, those facts have occurred, the cause of action has arisen, and we say the clock starts running at that point because a claim against the defendant can be sustained in court when those facts exist. If that’s right as a first principle, I don’t understand your argument that the cause of action arises when the plaintiff can bring the claim. The law regarding when a plaintiff can bring a claim is something different. But here, we have a cause of action arising out of the final agency action, because that is the point at which a person can sustain a claim against the agency under the APA. Why am I wrong about that?

Defendant’s Lawyer: This Court has identified the elements of an APA claim, and it requires a plaintiff who is injured and injured by agency action.

Judge: Where have we said that that was an element? I thought that was just a statement of the statute as to who can bring the claim, not the element of the claim itself—like when the defendant has violated the law.

Defendant’s Lawyer: I would point the Court to the Lujan decision, 1990, where it outlines the elements of an APA claim.

Judge: Right. Who has the cause of action? I'm talking about what are the elements of the cause of action. I thought it was that the agency has enacted a final rule that you claim is arbitrary and capricious or not in accordance with the law. Once the agency has done that, we have a cause of action—it has arisen—and then these other elements or aspects of the statute say who is the person who can bring such a claim.

Defendant’s Lawyer: I disagree with that. This Court has said that certainly final agency action is an element of an APA claim, but the other element, as this Court noted in Lujan and Newport News, is that someone must be actually harmed by it—a plaintiff who was harmed by it. Accrual-based statutes are necessarily plaintiff-specific. That’s in their nature. We’re not aware of a statute that uses accrual language or accrual-like language where the statute of limitations starts and ends on the same day for everyone. If that were the case, it wouldn’t be an accrual statute—it would be a statute of repose. Congress certainly knows how to pass statutes of repose, and it didn’t do so when it passed the APA. In 1934, Congress passed a Hobbs Act-like statute for the SEC. It did so again in 1938 for the FTC. And in 1950, it actually passed the Hobbs Act itself. But in between those years, it passed the APA and did not subject it to a repose-based statute of limitations, and it passed Section 2401 two years later. That must have been an intentional choice—APA claims are not subject to the kind of repose that applies to other types of causes of action.

Plaintiff's Lawyer:
Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court: As the questions this morning make clear, this is an unusual case.
At the time we filed our brief, we flagged serious concerns about whether the case was moot. Now, as I take my friend to agree, it's clearly moot.
We have not only a plaintiff who no longer seeks to litigate her claims, a defendant who no longer owns the hotel, but also a website that is no longer lacking the relevant information. Rather than decide the more difficult standing question in this highly artificial posture, the Court should exercise its discretion to vacate the First Circuit's decision and dismiss the case as moot under Munsingwear. At a minimum, if the Court exercises its discretion to address standing, it should hold that Respondent lacks standing for a narrow reason.
Her claim to standing depends on her view that Title III and the Reservation Rule give her a freestanding right to information, akin to the right to information about housing in Havens. But the ADA and the Reservation Rule are narrower.
They give individuals with disabilities a right to information in connection with the equal enjoyment of a hotel's reservation service. Because Ms. Laufer has not alleged that she would use that service in the future, she lacks standing. I welcome the Court's questions.

Judge:
You're not saying that we don't have jurisdiction over the case as I understand you. You're simply saying that we should exercise our discretion not to decide the standing issue?

Plaintiff's Lawyer:
That's correct, Justice Thomas.
When the government was on the receiving end of this type of a dismissal of a complaint in district court after the Court had granted certiorari in Carnahan, we specifically laid out our thoughts on this issue, specifically, that the Court does have discretion to decide either the standing or the mootness question first, but there, we said, you know, it makes sense, given the artificial nature of the case once the plaintiff has dismissed her claims, to decide mootness while leaving on the table, you know, the possibility that in a future case you might choose to decide standing. But I think for the reasons I was just explaining that this is not that future case because it's not just sort of artificial in the way that any case in which a plaintiff who has dismissed her claims will be artificial.
It's — it's sort of extra-artificial for the reasons that Justice Kagan was pointing out.

Judge:
You're confident that the mootness question is easy. Is the government at all concerned about the manipulation of this Court's jurisdiction when you have — because Ms. Laufer, I gather, is not the only person doing this, bringing hundreds of cases around the country, and then, when the Court gets interested in her case, it's all gone. It's moot. It doesn't stop any of the other dozens of people, however many there are, who are doing the same thing.
So we may have to come up with another case as soon as this — this one, if it's — if it's not addressed, and then they will — that one will be mooted. And, you know, we can't sort of keep granting cert and having it constantly being mooted with never a determination of whether there's standing in those variety of cases. I mean, you may think that that's not necessarily easier or harder than the standing question, but it's certainly not one that we can just, you know, toss off with the back of our hand, is it?

Plaintiff's Lawyer:
So I certainly understand the institutional concern, and that is why, among other reasons, we think the — the Court can and should leave on the table this option of actually deciding the standing question rather than the mootness question in a future case should this actually become a pattern.
I just think, again, that this is a particularly poor case to do it in. And I think, beyond that, you know, on the more specific question of whether this case will — or a case like this will come to the Court again, I think this has already been covered, but the circuit split actually looks quite different now than it did when the Court granted certiorari.
It's not just that the parties agree that under Munsingwear the First Circuit decision would have to be vacated in this case, but it's also that the Eleventh Circuit decision has since been vacated, and so now there's only one circuit decision on Petitioner's side — excuse me, Respondent's side of the split. And so I think parties may — may adjust their behavior in — in light of this, but, again, we, for the institutional reasons you note, think the Court can reserve this option in future cases.

Judge:
How — reserve the option. How many times does this — do we have to do this? The next case that comes up in the same posture as this, should we let that one go too because maybe it'll be the last one, or should we address it here?

Plaintiff's Lawyer:
So I certainly understand the concern, Mr. Chief Justice. Again, you know, I think it's not just that this is the first case. It's really that this is sort of moot to the third.
I mean, it has a plaintiff who has dismissed her claims, a defendant who no longer owns the hotel in question, and a website that everyone agrees has been updated. And if you look at the First Circuit's decision, the reason why the First Circuit thought this case was not moot was this third-party issue with respect to hotels.com and other third-party websites.
But, under the regulation, it's the hotel owner whose job it is to inform those third parties about accessibility information, and we don't even have that party before the Court to tell us — whether that has happened.

Judge:
I think — I think the Chief's question was, next time, we shouldn't do this, do you agree?

Plaintiff's Lawyer:
You know, I think certainly I would have to know more about what it looks like next time, but I think the whole point, obviously, of the Court reserving this ability would be — or recognizing this ability, really, because they're both jurisdictional questions, would be to allow you to say yes, we're going to go to standing in the next case.

Judge:
Do you think it's relevant that the reason the split looks different too is also perhaps strategic behavior on the part of the Respondent? Because didn't she go to those other circuits and then dismiss her claims and ask for the decisions to be vacated?

Plaintiff's Lawyer:
So, you know, I don't want to sort of import — or impart a motive to any of the parties on — on either side of this case.
I think the Eleventh Circuit decision is actually a little bit more complicated than that because it was actually that the defendant not only — or had not just sold the hotel at the time of the decision, it actually had dissolved and had not told the court that at the time, and so it wasn't sort of just on Ms. Laufer's side of the v.

Judge:
It was at her request, is all I'm saying.

Plaintiff's Lawyer:
Yes.

Judge:
Yeah.

Plaintiff's Lawyer:
I think it was at her request.
You know, again, I — I think the question really is, given how much more difficult at least we think the standing question is than the mootness question, whether it makes sense in this case, where we have these additional complications, to go ahead and decide that.

Judge:
I'm wondering, just to turn to the standing question —

Plaintiff's Lawyer:
Certainly.

Judge:
— how you would distinguish your position from Mr. Unikowsky's views and why it matters.

Plaintiff's Lawyer:
Sure.
So I — I think there are sort of many differences, but I guess I'll hit three main ones. The first one is that Mr. Unikowsky, in his brief and then again this morning, expressed the view that the Reservation Rule is really about access to the facility under the ADA. We — we understand it a little bit differently.
There's, of course, some element of access to the facility in there, but the statute says —

Judge:
When you say "the facility," meaning the hotel?

Plaintiff's Lawyer:
The hotel, the physical place of public accommodation.
The — the statute also guarantees people with disabilities the equal enjoyment of the services of a place of public accommodation, and so I think how this cashes out is that in — on Mr. Unikowsky's view, I think, if you have a hotel like this one that has no accessible — no accessible rooms, then a plaintiff can never have standing based on the fact that they didn't tell you that on their website because you couldn't go there anyway. We think that actually is an injury. If — if you're trying to figure it out and you can't because they don't have the information, you're not having the equal enjoyment of the service in the way that the ADA provides.

Judge:
Is your argument really a standing argument, an Article III argument, or is it a merits argument? You're saying that when Title III and the Reservation Rule are properly interpreted, then Ms. Laufer doesn't have a claim. That's a merits argument.

Plaintiff's Lawyer:
So, Justice Alito, I appreciate that the — the analyses sort of overlap in this area, and I think the reason why that is so is that this Court has specifically recognized that congressional — what Congress does in terms of creating rights and causes of action is relevant. I think the — the phrase this Court used in TransUnion was, you know, due respect to obviously Havens, other cases.
And so I do think there's some overlap.
But we do think the way this comes to the Court is that discrimination is an Article III injury when somebody personally experiences it with respect to her rights.

Judge:
Well, let — let me ask — let me give you a hypothetical of the sort that our former colleague, Justice Breyer, might have asked.
So — (Laughter.)
Let's say I am — I am driving to a dog show and I am transporting my champion Saint Bernard and I want to check in to a hotel with my dog, and they ask me: Is it a service dog? I say no, it's not a service dog. Is it an emotional support dog? No, it's not an emotional support dog.
Well, they say, you can't check into the hotel because either we don't allow dogs at all or we don't allow dogs that weigh more than a hundred pounds. And I say, well, you're violating the ADA.
And I sue under the ADA. Now do I lack standing? Have I not suffered an injury in fact because I can't get this hotel room and I can't have my dog, my champion Saint Bernard, with me in my room? Or do I just lose because the dog isn't covered — having the dog without it being a service dog or an emotional support dog is not covered by the ADA?

Plaintiff's Lawyer:
So I think you might just have a merits problem there if I'm following it correctly, but I think it's a little bit different because the harm that you're alleging in that case is the — just the — the harm from — you know, maybe the person was mean to you, you just really wanted to bring your dog, whatever it is. I think the way that this case is pitched is that the right that — that Ms. Laufer claims gives her standing is the violation of the statutory right to information or the — the violation of the regulation.
And so we sort of have to know what the statute or the regulation provide to decide whether she is — if I could just finish the — the sentence —

Judge:
Sure.

Plaintiff's Lawyer:
— in an analogy to sort of an Allen versus Wright case, you know, we have to — we have to know whether she is the person who's personally experiencing the discrimination or she's the person who's sort of across the country hearing about it, and to know that, we have to know what the right to the non-discrimination right is.

Judge:
Thank you.

Judge: Charia Preston?

Plaintiff:
Yes, ma’am.

Judge:
You are suing your former landlord, Selwin Wilford, for $2,500 that you say he owes you in the return of a security deposit?

Plaintiff:
Yes, ma’am.

Judge:
Okay, what happened here?

Plaintiff:
Okay, um, after renewing my lease, we decided towards the ending of that term that we were going to go ahead and move out.

Judge:
Okay, so your lease was going to end when?

Plaintiff:
My lease would have ended at the end of May.

Judge:
Do you have your lease?

Plaintiff:
Yes, I do.

Judge:
May I see it?

Plaintiff:
Sure.

Judge:
When did you let the landlord know that you weren't going to renew?

Plaintiff:
I let him know back in March.

Judge:
Did you do it in writing?

Plaintiff:
I did it in writing, and I also informed him and his wife that we were moving because when I'm on Section 8, we have to let the landlord know that we're not going to renew the lease.

Judge:
Okay.

Plaintiff:
So, he asked me to make sure everything was in order so that way he could start bringing people in to try to rent out the place.

Judge:
Okay.

Plaintiff:
We said okay.

Judge:
Okay, I got it. So, you move out on what day?

Plaintiff:
We moved out on May 31st.

Judge:
All right. Did you do a walkthrough with him or no?

Plaintiff:
Yes, we did a walkthrough.

Judge:
Did anybody say anything?

Plaintiff:
The only thing he said to me was that there were toys and clothes.

Judge:
Where?

Plaintiff:
In front of the house.

Judge:
Okay.

Plaintiff:
I give away furniture a lot. I give it away to Salvation Army.

Judge:
Okay, and?

Plaintiff:
There were clothes and toys on the porch.

Judge:
Okay.

Plaintiff:
And one of the stoves—he wanted the trays to be replaced.

Judge:
The burner trays?

Plaintiff:
Yes, the burner trays on the stove.

Judge:
Okay.

Plaintiff:
And that was it.

Judge:
Okay, so now you replace the burner trays, except for one that you couldn’t get loose?

Plaintiff:
Correct.

Judge:
And you left that there for him to replace because you couldn't figure it out?

Plaintiff:
Yes.

Judge:
And what about all the stuff that was outside that you were expecting to donate to charity?

Plaintiff:
We had my fiancé’s brother come and help us remove the stuff, and we took it to the junkyard.

Judge:
Okay, I got it. So now you wait and wait, and you don't get your security deposit. When do you find out he doesn’t intend to give you the security deposit?

Plaintiff:
We were overseas on vacation because he had told me that he was going to send the money.

Judge:
Okay, and then when did you realize he wasn’t going to send the money?

Plaintiff:
When we came back, and I kept texting him.

Judge:
And what did he do? Ignore you or tell you, "I'm not sending you money?"

Plaintiff:
He ignored me. He never told me he wasn’t.

Judge:
You filed the lawsuit?

Plaintiff:
Yes.

Judge:
All right, what’s going on, sir?

Defendant:
I have text messages concerning the date and time that she actually left.

Judge:
When did she leave?

Defendant:
She left on the 14th of June.

Judge:
But when she handed you back the key, did she keep just the key for two extra weeks, or did she move everything out on the 31st?

Defendant:
I don’t know what she moved out on the 31st because I was out of town.

Judge:
Okay.

Defendant:
I have pictures of everything from the 14th through the 15th and 16th of June of what took place.

Judge:
Are you trying to charge her half a month’s rent because she had stuff on the porch and a burner?

Defendant:
I'm not trying to charge her any half a month’s rent. That came up because of the lawsuit that she filed against me.

Judge:
Well, yeah, she’s trying to get—how is it you're justifying keeping $2,500 of her security deposit?

Defendant:
If you look at my response, there were several damages at the property.

Judge:
Okay.

Defendant:
The stove cost me over $130 to fix. There were walls broken, parts of the walls that had to be replaced, painted, that had to be repainted. The ceilings that were all stained up with paint that they used had to be repainted. It cost me over $3,000 to bring this to where it needed to be to be rented again.      Judge:
Okay, so here’s what I need to see. I need to see the pictures of what you are saying needed to be repaired.

Defendant:
Yes, ma’am. I have them right here.

(Defendant hands over photos to the judge.)

Judge:
Okay. So, this is a picture of what exactly?

Defendant:
That is one of the walls in the living room that had to be repaired and repainted. The plaintiff had painted it a dark color and didn’t return it to white.

Judge:
Do you have a clause in the lease stating that the tenant must return the walls to the original color?

Defendant:
Yes, it’s in the lease agreement.

Judge:
(Flipping through lease agreement) Yes, I see that here. The tenant is responsible for returning walls to their original color upon vacating.

Plaintiff:
I wasn’t made aware that I had to repaint.

Judge:
Well, it’s in the lease, so it’s your responsibility to read it. But let’s move on. What else do we have?

Defendant:
There were holes in the wall from where things were hung up. I had to patch those up.

Judge:
Let me see. (Examines photos) Okay, some minor nail holes and a few larger ones.

Plaintiff:
We only put up picture frames.

Judge:
Okay. Normally, minor nail holes are considered normal wear and tear, but some of these are larger than expected.

Defendant:
Also, there were two ceiling fans that were broken.

Judge:
How do you know she broke them and it wasn’t normal wear and tear?

Defendant:
They were functioning before she moved in, and when she moved out, they weren’t.

Judge:
(Looking at receipts) You have an invoice for the repair of the fans, which was $250.

Plaintiff:
They were old fans. I didn’t break them.

Judge:
All right. Then we have the issue of the stove. You said you had to replace burner trays?

Defendant:
Yes, but also, one of the burners was damaged.

Judge:
(Looking at invoice) $130 for that repair.

Plaintiff:
I replaced the trays. He never told me anything about a burner being broken.

Judge:
Okay, here’s where I stand. First, you cannot charge her additional rent for the days after she moved out if she was not living there and only had some items left behind temporarily.

Second, normal wear and tear, like minor nail holes, is not something you can charge a tenant for. However, the larger holes and the failure to repaint the walls back to the original color are reasonable deductions.

The stove repair is questionable since she did attempt to fix it herself, but I will allow a partial deduction for that.

The ceiling fans—there’s no clear evidence that she broke them beyond normal use, so I will not allow that deduction.

I am ordering you to return $1,500 of the $2,500 security deposit to the plaintiff.

Plaintiff:
Thank you, Your Honor.

Defendant:
Understood.

Judge:
Good luck, folks. Court is adjourned.

Judge: Alysa, you are suing Carl Kik for $5,000 in medical bills, surgery, and future medical expenses for your dog because, according to you, he viciously attacked him. Tell me what happened.

Plaintiff (Alysa): Okay, so on May 11th, 2014, I had some friends over. We were all in my backyard, which was fenced in. My small dog, Sasha, was outside with us, and she saw someone going by with another dog. She ran and snuck through a little hole in the fence.

Judge: How was the hole there?

Plaintiff (Alysa): It didn’t really look like a dog would actually fit through there. We were renting the house.

Judge: How many dogs did you have there?

Plaintiff (Alysa): My friend had her dog there as well, a Beagle.

Judge: How many dogs would normally live there?

Plaintiff (Alysa): Just my dog, one, Sasha.

Judge: Had Sasha ever gotten out before?

Plaintiff (Alysa): No.

Judge: So Sasha gets out, out into the open, to the street?

Plaintiff (Alysa): Right, to where they were.

Judge: Okay, and you're walking your— and Sasha's what kind of dog?

Plaintiff (Alysa): Pomeranian.

Judge: How big is Sasha? Show me with your hands.

Plaintiff (Alysa): Okay.

Judge: What kind of dog do you have?

Defendant (Carl): A Border Collie mix.

Judge: How big is your dog? Show me with your hands.

Defendant (Carl): Probably about 55 lbs.

Judge: And had you walked your dog in that area before?

Defendant (Carl): Yes, we don’t live too far apart, or we didn’t.

Judge: Was your dog on a leash?

Defendant (Carl): Yes.

Judge: Right. So go on.

Plaintiff (Alysa): I got up to get Sasha, but she had run, and then all of a sudden I hear a yelp, and I ran faster to get her. I picked her up on the little grass outside, which is technically my lawn, but outside of the fence, and I just picked her up, and her eye started filling up. He— you didn’t see him and his dog on...?

Judge: Wait. I haven’t asked my question yet. You didn’t see him and his dog on your lawn, right?

Plaintiff (Alysa): No.

Judge: Okay, go on.

Plaintiff (Alysa): So you see him when Sasha’s on your lawn. You see him doing what?

Plaintiff (Alysa): He was walking away, and then he swung his cane at the fence where the other dog was barking—the Beagle, my friend's dog. She was running along the fence barking, and I saw him swinging the cane. And he swung his cane up against the fence, but I was like pretty hysterical and just kind of— my dog's eye was filling up with blood, so...

Judge: So what’d you do?

Plaintiff (Alysa): Luckily, I had friends over. They drove me to the emergency vet because I was shaking, and the dog went into shock. She looked like she was basically having a seizure. We went to the emergency vet, and she had gotten a proptosed globe, which means that her eye fell out of the socket, and they had to surgically repair that.

Judge: Do you have a picture of the dog?

Plaintiff (Alysa): Yeah, I have before, before and after.

Judge: How did you find who the defendant was, since he was walking away? I mean, didn’t you— nobody ran after him when he was walking away and said, “Hey, what happened here?”

Plaintiff (Alysa): Nobody. I was concerned with my animal.

Judge: No, I know, but you had a bunch of other people there. One of them could have run over.

Plaintiff (Alysa): Right, but just...

Judge: Okay. So this is the puppy. This is Sasha before.

Plaintiff (Alysa): Right.

Judge: Poor Sasha.

Plaintiff (Alysa): And this is Sasha now. This is how the— that’s the best they could do.

Judge: Yeah. So you have to think about this every time you look at her, right?

Plaintiff (Alysa): Okay.

Judge: So how did you find him?

Plaintiff (Alysa): I just asked around the neighborhood, and anybody know an older gentleman that walks around with a cane banging fences, and everybody knew exactly who you meant?

Defendant (Carl): Yeah, actually.

Judge: All right, so what did you do?

Plaintiff (Alysa): Well, I didn’t really—I didn’t see it, so I didn’t completely know what happened. All I know is I saw him walking away swinging the cane, and I saw my dog with an eye filling up with blood. So I just went to his house to kind of try to figure out what happened, you know? Because if it was the dog that hurt Sasha or something and it was an accident, then that’s one thing. You know, but if it’s actually done by a human being that deliberately swung a cane at my animal, then I want to know about it. So I went to his house. The daughter—his daughter answered the door, and I said, you know, there was an event. My dog may lose her eye. I just want to know what happened, and she said, “Yes, my father came home, and he told me the story and everything, and he said that your dog was attacking and he hit her with the cane as protection,” and she said, “He carries the cane around to protect himself against the animals in the neighborhood.”

Judge: Are there a lot of loose animals in the neighborhood?

Plaintiff (Alysa): There was one dog, a larger dog, that the owners would leave in the front yard, but it didn’t really... It was... When you say the front yard, you mean that the dog was roaming free?

Plaintiff (Alysa): Sometimes.

Judge: So did you ever talk to him, or no?

Plaintiff (Alysa): No, I never did.

Plaintiff (Alysa): If I were to have hurt someone else’s kid or animal, I would have offered to pay or at least apologized.

Judge: And what happened?

Defendant (Carl): I’m taking my dog for a walk. Sasha jumped up in the air, bounced off the fence, and wiggled through the little opening in the fence, and then right away, like a speeding bullet, came out and she didn’t do what most dogs do. In fact, I used this defense against their Beagle very successfully. What I do...

Plaintiff (Alysa): Wait, what are you saying?

Defendant (Carl): They had the other dog—he got out through the back gate.

Plaintiff (Alysa): Oh, oh, because two—wait a second, wait a second, two dogs got out?

Defendant (Carl): Yeah.

Judge: Okay. So go ahead.

Defendant (Carl): So you... This defense being what? I’m sorry.

Plaintiff (Alysa): When you say I used this defense on the Beagle successfully, you tapped on the ground, on the ground, and put it right against...

Defendant (Carl): Yep. Okay.

Defendant (Carl): I think you got a little too close to this one because I didn’t have a choice. This is what ended up happening is I was bringing my cane down in order to try and get some distance, but the little Pomeranian just kept attacking at my dog’s throat.

Judge: Okay, when you say attacking at your dog’s throat, did the Pomeranian actually make contact with your dog, or didn’t it get to?

Defendant (Carl): No. What ended up happening is when I first— when the dog first came out...

Judge: So basically the dog is rushing towards you and your dog?

Defendant (Carl): I yanked my dog back.

Judge: You yanked your dog back?

Defendant (Carl): My dog flew by, landed on the ground, jumped right back at my dog’s throat, and that’s when— you say jumped at your dog’s throat. How?

Defendant (Carl): The dog didn’t make contact with your dog’s throat, so I’m trying to figure out where is the dog when your cane flies. Where’s the Pomeranian when your cane flies?

Defendant (Carl): About that far away from my dog’s neck. In the air.

Judge: So you literally swatted it off with the cane?

Defendant (Carl): I was trying to... come back down to try and get it to stop.

Judge: Yes. Wow, what a disaster, huh?

Defendant (Carl): Yep.

Judge: So why didn’t you ever go apologize and explain all this? You know, she came to your house, she talked to your daughter, she was distraught. Why wouldn’t you just make contact? You know, and do kind of the human thing and say, “I’m sorry, there’s so many loose animals here. It was self-defense, whatever.” Why wouldn’t you just talk to her?

Defendant (Carl): Because it’s not my responsibility to do that. My responsibility is to make sure my dog doesn’t attack other...

Judge: Why don’t you let me handle the legal parts of this? I’ll handle the legal parts. I’m talking about the human part. I’m talking about the moral part. I still don’t... you don’t understand what I’m saying. My first responsibility is to take care of my dog.

Judge: When you ended up leaving a puppy, a dog, looking like this for the rest of its life, that she has to look at it, all that... Don’t you think it would be a humane thing to do to go and speak with her and tell her it couldn’t be helped, this is what happened, so that she doesn’t have to hate the world?

Defendant (Carl): I don’t know.

Judge: I mean, it doesn’t matter to you that what she suffered. She didn’t do anything wrong other than that there’s a hole and the dog got out. That’s not an intentional act. So I understand your conduct when it happened. I don’t understand you or your conduct after it happened. I don’t know why, even while I’m standing here, when I’m suggesting to you that maybe you should feel a little bad that this happened, that you don’t turn around and tell her, “I’m sorry.” Like, aren’t you sorry a little?

Defendant (Carl): Of course.

Judge: Tell her that.

Defendant (Carl): I really am.

Judge: Welcome back to the People's Court. Har 11 here. You know, Pomeranian dogs, little tiny dogs, are they aggressive by nature?

Plaintiff (Alysa): Not really. Just a little bit yappy, but usually they’re really just really cute.

Judge: Are you suggesting small dogs are not aggressive?

Plaintiff (Alysa): Um, I think it depends on their owner.

Judge: Small dogs like Pomeranians aggressive by nature?

Plaintiff (Alysa): I think it’s definitely the owner. Napoleon complex, maybe, perhaps?

Judge: There you go.

Judge: Going inside the courtroom. He’s walking around with a weapon. Here’s the thing, and here’s the thing that’s, you know, the issue here. You say, “Well, he shouldn’t be walking around with a weapon because he may use it in self-defense.” And that’s not the issue. The issue is, was it self-defense?

Judge: Was it self-defense? Did you hit the dog in self-defense? You say yes. And now we go to his claim, the $1,500 for pain and suffering. Did you have your shot of pain and suffering?

Judge: Okay, Bernard Kelly, yes, you are suing Robert Evans, Amanda Evans, Curtis Kitchen, and what's the baby's name?

Plaintiff: Audrey.

Judge: No, you're not. So how old's your baby?

Plaintiff: Three weeks.

Judge: Oh my goodness, no wonder you wouldn’t let her go. Mr. Kelly, you and Mr. Evans work together, correct?

Plaintiff: Yes.

Judge: Alright, and tell me the nature of the transaction that gets us here.

Plaintiff: On or about May 31st, Mr. Evans had contacted my job stating that he had a laptop that he wanted me to look at, which belonged to his daughter.

Judge: You're the daughter, right?

Amanda: Yes.

Plaintiff: He explained to me the situation, that there was a laptop that his daughter had that is no longer working, and there was important data on there that they wanted me to extract from it.

Judge: How did he know that you would know how to do that?

Plaintiff: Because I do it as a hobby on the side. Everybody around knows that.

Judge: Alright, so now he asked you to do this for his daughter’s computer. Yes, and what price do you two agree upon?

Plaintiff: Well, the price was a set price for everything. That's for the laptop, the work, the labor, the software—$350 flat. I rounded it all off to just one simple number to make it easier.

Judge: So the laptop would have been $150 and the work about $200?

Plaintiff: Yes.

Judge: Alright, so you rounded it off to $350, and you told them, "Listen, your computer’s shot, and I’ve got an old computer around?"

Plaintiff: Yes, he chose from several different laptops. He then called his daughter. They discussed it. She said fine. Whatever. I’m assuming he said, "Well, I’m going to bring this one home to let you see." She said, "Okay, fine."

Judge: You discussed what you were going to do. He wanted you to extract the data and then put it into the other laptop?

Plaintiff: Yes, I had to go through an extreme process to extract the data because the initial laptop didn’t work. You can’t boot it up to see anything, so I had to find another way to extract the data.

Judge: How do you do that?

Plaintiff: I hook it up to my external computer, and then I go through the process with my software. What it does is it goes into the BIOS and reads the data. In the business I’m in, you must tell them that all data will not be extracted. We’re not responsible for lost data, so if you have corrupted files, it won’t bring it out. At that time, you can just transfer it from there to whatever piece of hardware you want.

Judge: Fascinating, isn’t it? You see your life flash before your eyes. Every picture you ever took of your kid is gone, and then someone is able to go like this and bring it back. Alright, so what goes wrong?

Defendant: It’s inexplicable.

Plaintiff: I did the work. I got your daughter’s data back.

Defendant: We had a different company transfer the files. I had to pay $50 for that.

Judge: Did you not tell him you didn’t need the broken laptop anymore?

Defendant: No. He didn’t want it. He said it was junk. I could keep it.

Defendant: You didn’t give it back!

Plaintiff: He has no proof of that.

Judge: I’m ordering them to pay you for extracting the files you successfully retrieved and for the software that was used. For your services, I’ll award you $125. As for your counterclaim, I find it baseless. I dismiss it.

Judge: This case is adjourned.

First, if our client was previously listed for attending the wrong mosque and attends that same mosque this year, the declaration would allow the government to relist him.

It gets worse. Even if he doesn’t attend again, but the government gets new suspicion-inducing information—not about him but about the mosque itself—the declaration likewise allows the government to relist him.

Any new fact not currently known to the government would allow the plaintiff to be relisted, consistent with the declaration. And if he is relisted in either of those ways, that’s recurrence of the challenged conduct.

He is disadvantaged in the same fundamental way his complaint was meant to redress. Imagine if Nike had promised Already not to bring any trademark claim based on currently available information but could sue for infringement based on new information. Under those facts, the Court couldn’t have concluded that Already could move on with its business free from suit.

Second, the government’s declaration does nothing to guarantee the plaintiff notice or a hearing if he is relisted and, therefore, does not address his procedural due process claim at all.

The government’s response is that it has no current plans to relist him, but it insists on retaining the right to do so. And that’s its prerogative, but if it does relist him, there is a hundred percent certainty that his procedural claim will recur.

Third, because the government hasn’t disclosed either to the plaintiff or to a court why he was listed, a court can’t possibly say anything clearly at all about whether recurrence is reasonably likely or not, and the plaintiff cannot move on with his life in the way the lawsuit was meant to allow.

He doesn’t know why he was listed. He doesn’t know what might cause him to be relisted. He doesn’t know if the next time he worships at a mosque or travels abroad he might be relisted, massively disrupting his life once again.

The plaintiff is peaceful, a law-abiding U.S. citizen. He has a live controversy against the government and seeks only to litigate that case on the merits. That’s it. I welcome the Court’s questions.

Judge: How would the government’s declaration have to be amended in order to satisfy your notion of mootness?

Plaintiff's Lawyer: Yes, Your Honor.

For the substantive due process claim, if the government submitted a declaration either to us or to the Court itself disclosing the reasons and made a promise that matched up with those reasons not to repeat them or invoke them or similar ones, we think that could moot the substantive due process claim.

For the procedural due process claim, if the government describes, okay, we didn’t have notice, now there’s notice, you didn’t have a meaningful opportunity to rebut the evidence against you, now here’s a meaningful opportunity to rebut the evidence against you—in doing that, they would be able to moot the procedural due process claim.

The lower court recognized a sweeping retrospective remedy that we think conflicts with both severability principles and traditional remedial equitable principles in this space. Just first on severability, Dodd-Frank itself has an express severability clause. This Court emphasized that point in a previous ruling. And here, the lower court didn't even stop to consider whether any aspect of the agency's funding mechanism could be severed or would provide a basis to therefore limit the damage to Congress's work in trying to get this agency funded. I think that was an error and that there are several candidates for severability that would be a much less disruptive remedy in this context and would not entitle the opposing party to any relief because the Payday Lending Rule they challenge isn't traceable to those features or aspects of the funding mechanism.

But even if you followed the lower court’s approach and thought that there was something about this entire funding mechanism that’s invalid, even then I don’t think a retrospective remedy is warranted. The Court would be writing on a blank slate because no court had previously found that Congress itself violated the Appropriations Clause, but under traditional remedial equitable principles, it’s necessary to take into account the public interest and the balance of the equities. And here, a prospective remedy which would prevent the agency from enforcing this rule against the opposing party until it has a valid appropriation would give them a meaningful form of relief.

Instead, the retrospective remedy that the court adopted is sweeping in its implications and would be profoundly disruptive. I would point in particular to the amicus brief that was filed by the Mortgage Bankers Association that explains how many entities in various industries have critically relied on the agency’s regulations, including, in particular, in the housing finance space. These regulations create safe harbors for lenders so that they will be deemed to be in compliance with statutory requirements on things like ability to pay and on disclosure requirements. And if the lower court is right and there’s the prospect that all of these actions should be unwound, it would create profound disruption in various economic markets that would hurt the regulated entities themselves.

So we think that provides powerful reason to reject that kind of retrospective relief and instead have a going-forward prospective remedy only.

Judge: Thank you.

But it is also possible to read a clause like this more narrowly, and an example of that was provided in a previous opinion. If you have a statute that says anyone who kills or injures or assaults someone or otherwise causes serious injury, commits a crime, you wouldn't think that that applies to defamation. Todd Angle: I believe it was at maximum 60 days.
Judge: For those 60 days, you notice it in May?
Todd Angle: Yes.
Judge: So for those 60 days, that's what you're going to show me. How many people clicked?
Todd Angle: 154 times.
Judge: Okay. Why were you thinking this was going to be okay? Just in general, that you could take somebody's work?
Todd Angle: At the time, she thought that, uh... Did you know she was doing this?
Susan J. Gordon: No.
Judge: What’s your profession?
Todd Angle: I'm a project manager.
Judge: Why isn’t she here?
Todd Angle: She’s out of the country. She's working for a nonprofit.
Judge: So did you know she was doing this?
Todd Angle: No, I didn’t.
Judge: You found out when you were found out.
Todd Angle: Yes.
Judge: Let’s talk about this. There is a concept, which I'm sure you are both extremely familiar with, and the law calls the fair use doctrine.
Judge: The rule is you can’t use other people's stuff without paying them. That’s the rule. That's why they copyright their work. You’re not allowed to use someone else’s intellectual property.
Judge: The exception is if you've used it in a way that the law looks at as fair use. There are four factors that the courts look at in order to determine if what the purported infringer did was a copyright violation or fair use.
Judge: Let’s go over them:
Judge: One, the purpose of the use.
Judge: Was it used in a non-commercial or commercial setting?
Judge: You said it was on their website. What is your website?
Todd Angle: Does she charge for membership? No.
Judge: Does she advertise on it? No.
Judge: What is the purpose of her doing it?
Todd Angle: To inform the public about weddings. She's an officiant for weddings.
Judge: Does she list her name and phone number so you can reach her to perform your wedding?
Todd Angle: Let me check.
Judge: Is that you? You're right on there. How do you not know what's on the blog?
Todd Angle: No, I know it's on there.
Judge: That does not militate toward fair use. What’s the next one?
Judge: The nature of the copyrighted work.
Judge: Is this book nonfiction or fiction?
Susan J. Gordon: Nonfiction, other than the few times I imagined what Adam and Eve's wedding day would be like.
Judge: The nature of work as nonfiction militates more towards less protection for you because it’s not your art. The stories belong to the couples, not you. That militates toward fair use.
Judge: The second part of that is: Is it published or unpublished?
Judge: This is a published work, which militates toward the defendant.
Judge: The amount of the work used.
Judge: You have 365 stories in here. There were seven stories used. That's a small percentage.
Judge: That militates toward finding this to be fair use.
Judge: The effect on the potential market or value of the work.
Judge: Does his use cut into your income?
Susan J. Gordon: This hinders it. I need to control all access to these stories.
Judge: I think if they had contacted you and said, ""We'll offer you a couple thousand, and we want to give you some advertising,"" why would you ever say no to that? It could only help you.
Judge: But they did it behind your back. I understand the frustration.
Judge: Since the law is ""you use it, you pay,"" I have to consider how and why you used it.
Judge: I find this to be fair use.
Judge: That’s my verdict.


Judge: How has this cut into your profit margin?
Todd Angle: We want to control the publicity, but we didn't mean to hurt Mrs. Gordon. We're sorry she feels this way.
Judge: The fair use doctrine is complicated, and if you end up using someone else’s stuff, make sure to check with a lawyer first. It's a technical area of the law.
Judge: That will do it for this case. Next case coming up.


Susan J. Gordon: I had hoped this would send a message to people who lift authors’ work and post them online without permission.
Susan J. Gordon: There were two issues here, but do you think you got your point across?
Susan J. Gordon: No, I don’t, because fair use is when you take a sentence or two, not between two and 3,000 words, which is what was extracted from my book.
Susan J. Gordon: I'm very disappointed.
Judge: You got a lot of exposure by bringing it into this courtroom.
Todd Angle: I've learned that copyright is very important. You have to respect these laws and educate yourself when posting things on the internet.
Judge: You were very close to losing this case. We feel bad for Mrs. Gordon, but it was fair use. Copyright is complicated. You better check with a lawyer if you're using other people's stuff for a blog or website."

Lawyer of Defendant: Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court: Section 1014 punishes only false statements, not true but misleading ones, and we know that from the text, the context in the statutory code, and this Court's precedents.

At the outset, at its most basic, the word "false" means not true.

It is, therefore, implausible to suggest that the statute that punishes false statements includes some types of true statement. "False" and "true but misleading" are different concepts.

When Congress means to prohibit both, it does so explicitly using both terms, as it has in over 100 places in the United States Code.

The government would put this all down to serial, thoughtless redundancy, but that violates the first principle of statutory interpretation: to heed the text.

The courts below erroneously held that Section 1014 punishes misleading statements in addition to false ones.

We are asking the Court to correct that legal error and to remand to the courts below for a determination of whether Mr. Thompson's statements were false or only misleading.

I welcome the Court's questions.

Judge: How would you define "false" and how would you define or distinguish -- and distinguish that from "misleading?"

Lawyer of Defendant: A false statement is one that is not true when compared to the objective facts. A misleading statement is a statement that depends on the reasonable hearer's understanding.

The term "misleading" is -- by its nature focuses on what the hearer hears.

The term "false" relates to an objective fact about the universe.

Judge: Do you think there could be overlap between the two?

Lawyer of Defendant: There definitely are -- is overlap, Your Honor.

Many, many false statements are misleading, and many misleading statements are false.

But that does not mean they're synonyms. As this Court has recognized on a number of occasions, including in the Macquarie case, where the Court dealt with Rule 10b-5 and held that 10b-5's two parts, the first penalized only express false statements and the second half-truths, which it referred to as misleading omissions.

Judge: Often, we see "false" and "misleading" paired, and you think of it -- there's a tendency to think of those two paired, false and misleading or false or misleading.

Why wouldn't we do that here as opposed to just taking a literal view of false?

Lawyer of Defendant: So, when we see them in these hundred-plus statutes, it's always in the disjunctive, false or misleading, suggesting that those are two different things.

And they do have different meanings because "false" is an objective question.

If I -- if I say the sun rises in the west, that is a false statement, and it doesn't matter what the perception of the listener is.

So I think that there is a good statutory context argument, a very good statutory context argument, for suggesting, when Congress says "false," it means false, not misleading. Otherwise, there would be a hundred statutes, from the Commodities Exchange Act to the Securities Exchange Act, to a number of labeling statutes, down to the Peanut Statistics Act and the act that penalizes false or misleading statements by an officer of the Administrative Office of the U.S. Court to either make false or misleading statements.

Congress chose not to do that here.

Judge: I take it you are not arguing that the statute requires that the statement be literally false when viewed in isolation?

Lawyer of Defendant: No.

Judge: Is that correct?

Lawyer of Defendant: I'm sorry, Your Honor.

Judge: Is that correct? Is that your --

Lawyer of Defendant: That -- that --that is correct.

Judge: -- that is your argument?

Lawyer of Defendant: That -- that is not our argument.

As this Court said in Bronston, and I would point the Court to Footnote 3, the context that's relevant is the question that's asked, not the other circumstances.

But, obviously, it would be absurd to try and judge a statement in isolation from the question that it answers.

Judge: So can I just ask you --

Judge: I -- I don't --

Judge: Oh.

Plaintiff: Yes, okay.

Judge: Tell me what’s happening.

Plaintiff: On May 22nd of 2010, I purchased a 2006 Ford Explorer from their dealership. On June 20th of this year, I traded in that same truck to another dealership, a Chevy dealership. When I traded the truck, they told me that the truck had frame damage. And I told them that the truck did not have frame damage because the truck was clean according to the dealership where I purchased the car from two years ago. That same dealership, the Chevy dealership, showed me the auto check and told me that they couldn’t give me the full value of the truck because it had been hit. Once I looked at the auto check and it showed that it was hit in December of 2019, I called up the dealership that I got the truck from and I relayed that to them while I was in the dealership on Long Island. They told me, “No, our CARFAX is clean; the truck has not been hit.” And that’s what they told me when I purchased it.

Judge: What did they tell you back then?

Plaintiff: The CARFAX is clean.

Judge: Yes, and you were now at a Chevy dealership and they were telling you, “Look, auto check,” do you have the auto check? Let me see.

Defendant: Auto check is the competing business to Carfax. Well, AutoCheck is more for auctions. They're really not used at the sales level. CARFAX is used at the sales level.

Plaintiff: They received the car from an auction in New Jersey.

Defendant: Yeah, but they use Carfax. We have AutoCheck. Do you use AutoCheck?

Defendant: When, at that point, it’s announced or it’s not announced. I actually wasn’t the one that purchased the car; I’m the sales manager, so I wasn’t the one that actually purchased the vehicle. It states that it was announced.

Plaintiff: If you had purchased the vehicle, if there's an auto check and the auto check shows damage, what does the dealership do?

Defendant: We wouldn’t purchase the car. Right? We’re a franchise dealer, we have too much to lose.

Judge: Too much to lose. You’re going to have a problem selling it.

Defendant: Absolutely.

Judge: Unless it’s completely hidden and you’re getting it at a good price.

Defendant: I don’t know.

Plaintiff: Page four, it says auction. Oh, what is this though? This says 12/17/2009, auto auction announced as unibody damage.

Judge: When did you guys buy it?

Defendant: We purchased a car 12/14/2009. I have that. We purchased the vehicle with absolutely no problems with the car.

Plaintiff: Do you have any paperwork from the auction?

Defendant: No, I don’t. What did you just look at?

Judge: This is the Carfax.

Defendant: The Carfax has when you purchased it. Absolutely, it's the same as auto check.

Plaintiff: Take a look at the auto check. What is that saying?

Defendant: No, it’s saying that it was announced, but we do check Carfax. We don’t check AutoCheck.

Plaintiff: You said that in auctions you check AutoCheck.

Defendant: Well, I personally don’t buy the cars, I heard that.

Plaintiff: But you said that your dealership at auctions checks AutoCheck. And AutoCheck is saying—but this—look, is this not saying even more than that, which is auction announced as unibody damage?

Defendant: I don’t, like I said, I’m not that well-versed in how we purchase the vehicles.

Judge: That’s what the whole case is about.

Defendant: I understand, but yeah.

Plaintiff: Why did they send you…

Defendant: I was actually the first person to get the negative review and try and work it out with them because you saw that on what site?

Plaintiff: On Carfax.

Defendant: Oh, on DealerRater?

Plaintiff: Yes.

Defendant: There is a part of your job is to constantly mine and see if anybody’s trashing you guys and try to work it out?

Plaintiff: Yes.

Defendant: So you called her and said what?

Defendant: My upper management told me that I can offer her a maintenance plan for her new car. I can offer her a few details for her new car. I can offer her a $500 gas card. These are the things that I was allowed to offer up to my authority.

Plaintiff: I don’t want that stuff either if I lost $5,000.

Judge: So it’s one way or the other. Either she has no case, or she has a case. And if she has a case, she has a $5,000 case because they gave her… Do you have the paperwork from Chevy for, to prove the amount of money that they docked you for the damage?

Plaintiff: I don’t have that.

Judge: Well, you're going to need to get it. And you are going to need to get—We’re going to take a recess now, and you're going to need to get the name, the paperwork from the auction, the purchase of this car, and we’re going to get to the bottom of this. Court, some recess.

Judge: All right, so the defendant… Let's say he didn’t really know and just was told the plaintiff of what he knew. Is he responsible for the fact that it wasn’t an accident?

Plaintiff: I would say if he didn’t have common knowledge of it, then he would not be responsible. That’s on Carfax. What do you think?

Judge: He’s not responsible for it.

Plaintiff: Especially if he got a Carfax.

Judge: No. Is anybody think he is responsible?

Judge: How about that going inside the courtroom?

Plaintiff: I understand.

Judge: That was a productive recess. So let me see your paperwork, and let me see your paperwork. You see, you have paperwork from the car dealership?

Plaintiff: But they verbally told me that I did, and I have the auto check. But the problem was I couldn’t get them to send me a document stating that what they gave me as opposed to what I should have gotten, and that’s all the paperwork that I have.

Judge: You understand though that you got 16,000?

Plaintiff: Yes.

Judge: Okay. All right. And you understand you paid 23 for it two years before you traded it in, right? So where would your loss be? Why would… And I pulled a CI Blue Book for a Ford Explorer to see what the depreciation should be in two years, and that’s just about on the money. So where would your loss be?

Plaintiff: You know, but…

Judge: And you know I’m looking at them. I see it.

Plaintiff: I understand that you’re angry because you feel like you got duped, but if you're not out anything, you’re not out of anything, and you can’t collect anything. That’s one. But two, I see that AutoCheck says that there was… This was an auction with unibody damage. I saw that. I got news for you. It wasn’t. Here’s the paperwork. And it makes sense because why would a dealership, when they have an entire yard full of cars to pick from at an auction, pick the one with unibody damage that’s going to cause them all kinds of issues later? They’re not. What they’re going to do is they’re going to pick the ones, 90% of them, that don’t have unibody damage. And those are the ones they’re going to bid on.

Defendant: We’ve spoken because I knew what we should have done.

Judge: Next case.

Judge: All right. Well, speaking of what we said, you mentioned the Anderson case. I read that case to be about whether or not state laws "impose an undue burden on the performance of the bank's functions." So, I mean, yes, you picked out some language that suggests that this is about sort of power at some level of generality. But it seemed to me that this was about whether this -- the law at issue in that case was "so burdensome" as to be inapplicable. It wasn't about the nature. It was about, as people have said, the degree.

Defendant’s Lawyer: I think you're absolutely correct. In Anderson, and when it contrasts the California case, is talking about an undue burden because it didn't affect the power. And what the Court said -- and that's why we have two tests. We have a fallback test. One is, if it affects the national banking power and controls the attribute of the product, preempted, preempted, preempted. There is a second undue burden test that looks at the practical impact, but the -- the delta between the two sides is we think that can be as a matter of law and looks -- it looks at a patchwork across 50 states. The California case said it was preempted without any factual record. In Anderson, it said it wasn't preempted with any factual record. They say with no case, not one case in 150 years of precedent, would this Court look to a factual record. They're relying on some trial court record? That's their best case? When the -- the Supreme Court didn't even talk about it? I think that pretty much tells you all you need to know whether Congress intended a factual record for banking preemption.

Now, on the OCC, I think, you know, there are three reasons why it is just simply implausible that by codifying Barnett Bank, Congress tended to overrule it or somehow upset it. And the first is what I already mentioned, the 30 words of text that says you need to follow Barnett Bank. And the second is there is a specific provision in 25b(c), we rely on it, the OCC relies on it, that says OCC must follow the legal standard of Barnett Bank, without any reference to the "prevents or significantly interferes." So they can't possibly mean two separate things. Congress told OCC to follow Barnett Bank, not to look at significant effect.

And the third reason we think it's just completely doubly bizarre and backwards that you would take Congress being mad at the OCC and imposing procedural requirements is somehow they intended to impose a new substantive standard on courts when they weren't mad at you, they weren't mad at courts, and impose a standard that no one's ever heard of or applied before, that you would go fact by fact -- fact by fact, law by law, bank by bank. And he did a little fancy footwork when you said, would this proceed bank by bank? He answered by saying, well, that would be the OCC. He never told you what would happen with the question about what would happen if Bank of America couldn't prove it, but, you know, another national bank, Citi, Citibank, could do it? There's no answer to that.

And in terms of the impact, you know, the notion that -- the representative speaking on behalf not of the OCC but the Justice Department, that you just have to look sort of at the records of the bank, the biggest problem with something like interest rates, which makes this a very easy case, is today 2 percent is four times the national savings. At the time of the case, it was 33 percent times the national savings rate. And at the -- excuse me, that's another case. At the time of the current case, it's 10 times.

I don't know what you think. Maybe you should let the courts know. Let's look at ATM fees. Four dollars sounds -- I don't know, maybe 1.50? And then we can go to credit card reward programs. We'd have to have a consumer survey. I think I'd like 2 percent back on my credit card, but maybe states say it has to be 4 percent. And I just don't even know how they would do this.

Judge: But, Counsel, I thought all the national banks were pretty much the same in terms of their powers. Like I thought we were talking about what -- what -- what a state law is doing to the national bank power. So it's not at the level of a particular bank. It is -- and any of the banks could make the argument, and once they do, it would come up to the Supreme Court and we would decide ultimately, right?

Defendant’s Lawyer: Well, that's this case. The Second Circuit said a mortgage -- mortgage escrow account is a direct assault on national banks' power.

Judge: I guess I just don’t understand why it's so hard. Like we do --

Defendant’s Lawyer: I don't think it is hard.

Judge: No, no, no. What I'm saying is you're making the argument that it is really going to be very challenging for banks if we rule against you in this case, and I don't understand why that's the case.

Defendant’s Lawyer: Well, you have, since the Reagan administration, a former OCC comptroller telling you it would create a seismic sea change and uncertainty. So that's the view of comptrollers from Reagan to -- all the way with current officials. You haven't even heard from the OCC, which regulates the national banking system. That alone should scare you tremendously, that you don't even have the OCC up here.

In terms of how hard it would be, I don't think I've heard a satisfactory answer on what interest rate would be too much and how national banks could make that showing. But take just interest rates on savings accounts. I don't even know what the -- the bank would say. They would say, well, we can do it; we'll have to --

Judge: Don't you have to say something? It's your burden. You're trying -- you have the burden in the law to show this substantially interferes. If your answer is I don't know what we would show, then I guess you lose.

Defendant’s Lawyer: Not if 150 years of case law is relevant and Barnett Bank codified it, because in no case has a bank -- the Supreme Court ever say, well, where's your facts, bank? Franklin itself is the best case on point. And both -- and I also think it's significant that the Court in Watters, that's the Supreme Court, I mean, that's -- that's actually you, you read Barnett Bank and had the most sweeping language you could possibly have about what Barnett Bank meant, and it said states cannot control banks, period. That's the Supreme Court. That interpreted Barnett Bank. So, you know -- and that's why I think OCC has always taken this position.

Judge: You're taking that quote out of context because I looked at it. It says the states can exercise no control over national banks, nor in any way affect their operation except insofar as Congress may see proper to permit.

Defendant’s Lawyer: Sure. For sure.

Judge: And that's what the whole issue is, how far did Congress permit here?

Defendant’s Lawyer: Well, two solicitor generals said in briefs before you what I said. So I'm happy standing on their view across several administrations about what Barnett Bank means.

Judge: The state statutes have to be non-discriminatory.

Defendant’s Lawyer: Correct.

Judge: So, you know, one way you could look at this is, if a state statute is non-discriminatory, how much damage could it really be doing?

Defendant’s Lawyer: And I think that's part of the problem, which is what the Franklin case illustrates and what this case illustrates, is the plaintiffs will always say, well, you applied it to your state banks, so what's the problem?

Judge: That's the question.

Defendant’s Lawyer: The problem goes much deeper --

Judge: I mean, it seems as though there should be a kind of presumption that if the state is doing it for the state banks, it's not really interfering with bank powers in a way that we should care about. There might be exceptions to that, and that’s what the language is designed to accomplish, to pick the exceptions to that where something has gone wrong such that even a non-discriminatory law does something special to national banks.

Judge: So it's about the defendant for purposes of 702 but it's not about the defendant for the purposes of 704. Help me with that.

Prosecutor: It is relevant to the defendant. Let me give you a plain language example.

Judge: Is it with respect to the defendant?

Prosecutor: If I told you that I had just read a terrific book about Julius Caesar, I think you would expect that the book at least mentions Julius Caesar somewhere in there.

Judge: No. (Laughter.)

Prosecutor: And if you learned that it's a book about ancient Romans—

Judge: No, I wouldn’t—you know, it could be about his times and his place and the Roman Empire of the era.

Prosecutor: I think that's exactly right.

Judge: Right?

Prosecutor: And that's my point.

Judge: Okay. And I'm drawing an inference about him.

Prosecutor: That's right. That's exactly right. And that's how we think the rule operates here.

Judge: You want us to draw—you want the jury to draw an inference about the mental state of this defendant, don't you?

Prosecutor: Yes. We are offering the testimony because it is relevant to the jury's assessment of the defendant’s own mental state, but that doesn't mean that it is an opinion about the defendant’s mental state.

Judge: I would think that a great many district courts would, on that theory, say that this testimony should be stricken on 702 grounds. It's just not relevant.

Prosecutor: 704(b)—

Judge: Is that what you're inviting?

Prosecutor: No, we are not inviting that.

Judge: No, of course not, right?

Prosecutor: Indeed, the district court in this case—the defense made a Rule 401 objection to the relevancy of this testimony. The district court overruled it. The defense has not renewed that contention in this Court. I think there is testimony that is relevant to the jury's assessment of the defendant’s mental state that is nonetheless not testimony that is opining directly on the defendant’s mental state.

Judge: Nor could the expert actually opine because he's not an examining expert. Isn't that right? I mean, there really isn't a world in which this expert could speak directly in a sense to what is going on in this defendant's mind. The only thing he is competent to testify about is, in his expertise, how these things work as a general matter, right?

Prosecutor: I think that's correct, and indeed in this particular case, the expert confirmed that he was not involved in the investigation of this case when he was asked that on cross-examination.

Judge: Do you want to first answer the 100 percent case?

Prosecutor: Sure. So I think the defense is misreading our brief on that point. I mean, we think that the distinction between testifying in absolute terms or conditional terms about the way that drug traffickers operate is really more of a reliability issue. I don't think at this point in time we could sponsor any testimony—

Judge: So, in fact, your argument would be the same if the expert got up and said, in my experience, 100 percent of the time drug traffickers use couriers who know that there are drugs in the car?

Prosecutor: Yeah, I think we would have the same Rule 704(b) position with respect to that testimony. And the key to the application of Rule 704(b)—

Judge: So all defendants know that they have drugs, and—and you're still saying that’s not testimony about the defendant's mental state?

Prosecutor: Well, I want to be careful and precise here. If the expert testifies that all defendants know or that all drug traffickers, including this defendant, know—

Judge: No, no, no. All mules, to use the common parlance—all mules know that there are drugs in their car.

Prosecutor: Yeah, I do—

Judge: That’s still permissible under your view? It’s not about the defendant?

Prosecutor: It is not objectionable under Rule 704(b) grounds. It doesn't mean—

Judge: The defendant just happens to be there. It just happens to be a trial in which that person is in jeopardy and—but we've got this testimony over here, and it doesn’t matter?

Judge: Yeah, I don't understand that, right? Because isn't this necessarily saying 100 percent of defendants know? Aren't you necessarily saying this defendant knows?

Prosecutor: I think the distinction between those two—

Judge: Apparently not.

Judge: Isn't the answer that—

Judge: Well, what is the answer? (Laughter.)

Prosecutor: Thank you. Look, I think the distinction between those two—the key point we're trying to get across is that Rule 704(b) forbids a particular form of opinion testimony. The expert cannot opine on the defendant's own mental state. And so we acknowledge in our brief that there are going to be circumstances in which experts testify about a class of people and put the defendant in that class. That form of expert testimony could be objectionable. But if the view is just that the expert is describing a general category of person—

Plaintiff's Lawyer: Well, Your Honor, to the extent you're suggesting that the availability of an ACCA sentence might have informed his decision to plead, he was perfectly on notice that he could receive an ACCA sentence. It turns out that it's for three -- or I think only two of the crimes are different than the original ones because of intervening decisional law that made some of the original charged predicates no longer valid. To be clear, we don't think that in the indictment we actually need to charge what the specific predicates are.

Judge: No, but you did in this information.

Plaintiff's Lawyer: We -- we did in this information, but I don't think that --

Judge: And wouldn't the --

Plaintiff's Lawyer: -- given that he had -- he has a fairly long rap sheet --

Judge: No, I understand that.

Plaintiff's Lawyer: -- I don't --

Judge: But do you think a defendant might make a reasonably different choice if he knows what -- what the -- I may be able to have a good occasions clause argument with respect to these crimes but not those crimes? And -- and the ones you chose are different than the ones you're now seeking to pursue.

Plaintiff's Lawyer: Well, to be clear, Your Honor, we charged them, as I read the information, as -- I mean, it put him on notice of the ACCA because it cited --

Judge: You did.

Plaintiff's Lawyer: It put him on notice of the ACCA, but it was also in support of the basic underlying 922(g) offense. In addition, I think he is fairly charged with knowing --

Judge: Okay.

Plaintiff's Lawyer: -- his own prior conviction history.

Judge: And then, on that, in response to earlier questioning, you admitted, I think, that there are some situations in which a jury could reasonably find that a series of crimes happened on the same occasion even though they happened over the span of some days. At least in a jury trial, you've got all the facts before you. Here, we have just the pleading documents from those prior cases. How is a judge -- how are we supposed to have a hundred percent confidence that it's harmless that these were, in fact, on separate occasions when there's been no trial and all we have before us are these pleading documents?

Plaintiff's Lawyer: Well, first of all, Your Honor, I -- I don't think we look at it quite as that there has been no trial. It's that the --

Judge: Well, there's been no trial.

Plaintiff's Lawyer: -- the entire record here would encompass the sentencing proceedings. This is the same error the Court considered in Neder, where an element was erroneously presented to a judge but not a jury. And, here, you have the record. We have the documents.

Judge: But we don't know what the defendant would say. He might say it was all part -- I -- I did this crime to commit that crime, to commit the third crime, just as you posited earlier. We don't know what he would say with respect to whether these three crimes that you wound up using are part of a single occasion or different ones.

Plaintiff's Lawyer: Well, Your Honor, now that we've expanded the different occasions inquiry into a fundamentally factual one, that's the holding of Wooden, I think looking at what the defendant precisely did, it doesn't remotely support an argument of that sort. And also, the idea that -- I -- I -- I -- I would resist the idea that it's part of a common scheme or plan simply just to undertake a string of robberies within a week. Like, clearly, he had the means to do the first one, to do the second one, and to do the third one. He had several days in between to cool off. He did not -- and on the last day, he robbed two stores, Druther's and Schnitzelbank. If you want to look at the sentencing memorandum, the government's sentencing memorandum, at page 6, those are fairly far apart from one another. I don't really think he has any viable argument, and I don't take debt, simply a debt, to be an argument. Otherwise, a gambling addict could constantly be on the same occasion.

Judge: It seems to me probably right, but we have to decide whether it's harmless beyond a reasonable doubt, and we don't have anything from the defendant here with respect to his views about why this might be a single occasion, and I'm just wondering how we're supposed to do that, but --

Plaintiff's Lawyer: Well, Your -- Your Honor, I think we do because this issue was litigated before the judge, notwithstanding his objection. And I really don't think he has anything there. If he did --

Judge: Okay.

Plaintiff's Lawyer: -- I think you would have heard it earlier this morning.

Judge: Thank you, counsel.

Plaintiff: I need the child support from him. I don't want to pull my kids from private school or, or you know, take them out of the things that they love to do because I, I can't afford it, right?

Judge: And don't say that again because you can afford it. If what he's saying is true, you're getting your nails done, you're going on trips, you're doing all of these other things. You know, especially going to the liquor store. Alcohol is not cheap. You can afford it, you're just choosing to do something other than what you should be doing, which is taking care of your children. All right, how often are you all exchanging the children?
