Defendant’s Lawyer: Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court:
I think it may be helpful for me to lay out how the federal government sees this case. In our view, the Court has already found, as indicated in $8,850 and Von Neumann, that the only process a claimant is entitled to after personal property is seized is a timely final forfeiture hearing.
If this Court is of the view that it has not yet addressed that issue, it should now. And it should hold that a claimant has no right to an interim post-seizure hearing.

History and tradition support that rule. At the time of the founding, there was no right to an early hearing when property was seized. Only a timely forfeiture hearing was required. That approach has prevailed for nearly 250 years.

The hearings that Petitioners request would upend that history, be extremely onerous, and would require more process for the pretrial deprivation of property than the pretrial detention of persons.

Finally, if the government disagrees with our front-line position, while we think that $8,850 is the better fit for Petitioners' particular claims, the government is largely ambivalent about whether Eldridge or $8,850 applies to determine whether an interim hearing is required in a given case.

But, either way, this Court's decision should emphasize that the public and government interests identified in $8,850 and Pearson Yacht will often weigh strongly against allowing such a hearing.

I welcome the Court's questions.

Judge: There have been quite a few questions suggesting that maybe something substantively goes wrong in this process. How would you address that, assuming that the forfeiture proceedings are timely?

Defendant’s Lawyer: So I think if there was some sort of question that an individual wasn't receiving due process in the course of the proceedings, some sort of procedural irregularity, there could be a due process claim for that.

It wouldn't necessarily be governed by Eldridge or even $8,850. It could be governed by just the closest civil law analogy. For example, if an inappropriate burden of proof were placed on someone, there might be due process concerns.

But that wouldn't be solved by requiring an additional layer of proceedings in all cases or in a significant category of cases. That's just a different issue.

Judge: The assessment of whether a civil forfeiture proceeding meets the requirements of due process—timeliness is a significant consideration in that, right?

Defendant’s Lawyer: Yes, it is.

Judge: And it is, of course, in retention as well?

Defendant’s Lawyer: Yes.

Judge: So there's presumably a gap between when you would have that question asked under retention and when you would have it asked under civil forfeiture. How do we look at the significance of that gap?

Defendant’s Lawyer: So I think the Court would look at that under $8,850 because $8,850 allows the Court to take into consideration the burden to a particular claimant in a particular case. I think that comes in under the first factor in $8,850.

And I think the government interests really aren't any different. This Court has talked about the government's interests in Pearson Yacht in not having a pre-seizure hearing. And I think most of those government interests continue to apply to allow the government to retain the property while the hearing is proceeding—so long as the hearing is proceeding in an appropriate amount of time and the government isn't sitting on its hands and doing nothing while holding someone's property.

And I think the history and tradition really are consistent with that. The best evidence of the history here comes from the Collection Acts, which were passed by founding-era Congresses. There was no requirement for any sort of interim hearing. The normal rule was that once property was seized, it was held until the final forfeiture hearing.

And Petitioners' counsel has suggested that once the forfeiture proceeding was filed, there was a 14-day hearing requirement. That's just not the case. Under the Collection Acts, the federal government had up to three years between seizure and initiating the forfeiture action.

Lawyer of Plaintiff: Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court: Exhaustion is the centerpiece of Congress's reforms under the Prison Litigation Reform Act.

Yet, even with this invigorated exhaustion requirement, prisoner lawsuits still account for an outsize share of filings in federal district courts.

A rule that requires a jury trial on intertwined exhaustion issues would increase this burden while incentivizing non-exhaustion and undermining the goals and structure of the PLRA.

Defendant: Respondent would have this Court cast aside the PLRA's goals and structure merely because exhaustion is an affirmative defense. Focusing on this Court's holding in Jones versus Bock, he contends that there's no principled reason for treating PLRA exhaustion differently than other affirmative defenses that are routinely sent to juries when there are facts intertwined with the merits.

Lawyer of Plaintiff: Jones does not stand for this broad proposition.

It held only that prisoners need not plead exhaustion.

And there is a principled reason for treating PLRA exhaustion differently than other affirmative defenses.

It is a mandatory prerequisite to suit.

So its intended benefits would be entirely undercut by merits discovery and a trial before its resolution.

PLRXA -- PLRA exhaustion must be resolved by a judge at the early stages of litigation.

Contrary to the Sixth Circuit, this does not run afoul of the Seventh Amendment even when there are intertwined facts.

The judge's determination on exhaustion does not interfere with the jury's ultimate fact-finding role because dismissal is typically without prejudice and the judge's determination on exhaustion would not have preclusive effect.

Defendant: Richards, like many other prisoners, can exhaust, come back, and have a jury decide the merits of any viable claims.

For this reason, this Court should reverse the Sixth Circuit's decision.

Judge: Are exhaustion determinations normally made by the judge?

Lawyer of Plaintiff: They are.

And, in fact, lower courts are pretty much in agreement that at least when there are no intertwined facts, that judges will make those determinations.

Judge: So what is it about the intertwining of facts that changed the -- changes the nature of exhaustion?

Lawyer of Plaintiff: I don't think there's anything that changes the nature of exhaustion. I think what it does is it -- it -- it makes one have to consider the Seventh Amendment now. Judge: So, historically, has there been -- do we have any analogs to -- that would suggest that this would go to a jury?

Lawyer of Plaintiff: No.

In fact, the opposite is true, that the analogs suggest that this would go to a judge.

We think that the closest analogs -- there is no precise analog. There was no exhaustion in 1791.

The doctrine hadn't been developed yet.

It came as the administrative setting was coming into force.

But we know that exhaustion has its roots in equity.

Judge: But, Ms. Sherman, do we really have to get into that? I guess what I was a little confused about from your briefing was that I took you to concede that there's intertwinement here.

Judge: There are distinctions with --with the -- the subject matter jurisdiction inquiry. The court is generally deciding it at the beginning, and the judge is doing it without a jury. It is the rare circumstance where the judge --

Plaintiff: And we said in this case, when the fact is intertwined, you had to --

Judge: When it's intertwined, this Court still has expressed that the district court has discretion to decide that but suggests that when they 're -- it's dependent, that it should go to a jury. There's no barrier at that point in terms of this Court's jurisdiction to sending it to a jury.

Judge: Ms. McGill.

Plaintiff Lawyer: Thank you, Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court: I just wanted to begin with two clarifying points. This is the first time in this five years of litigation that the State has represented that the --all of the claims might be able to be exhausted. The First Amendment claim that was the subject of the Sixth Circuit's decision here, as far as we can tell, is not protected by the PREA policy. And the Sixth Circuit held that exhaustion and the merits here were completely coterminous under Sixth Circuit First Amendment law.

The State also agrees that the historical facts at issue here are of the type that juries decide, and I think that makes this an easy case on the actual question presented because, whatever else is true, this is a 1983 action for money damages. So the jury must resolve those facts regardless of how you characterize exhaustion.

That is the point of Beacon Theatres, which is just a specific application of the general rule that even truly threshold issues must be deferred to the jury when they're intertwined with the merits. It's no answer to say that the facts could be relitigated in front of a jury maybe if and only if the case proceeds. It's simply not good enough that the jury trial right might sometimes be preserved.

And the State's suggestion that judicial findings would not be binding on a future hypothetical fact-finder really gives the game away. The only reason that would be so is because of the Seventh Amendment. That was this Court's unanimous holding in Lytle.

And there's certainly nothing in the statute or the federal rules that would suggest the non-binding factual findings procedure that the State suggests here. The State warns that having jury trials on exhaustion will undermine the goals of the PLRA. Those very same arguments were presented and rejected in Jones, and they're even less persuasive here. The State's approach would not reduce the number of trials required. We're talking about the cases that have survived judicial screening and Rule 56, the needles in the haystack as it were.

Regardless, policy arguments are no match for the Bill of Rights. I welcome the Court's questions.

Judge: Are you suggesting that the exhaustion --or your exhaustion argument has historical analogs?

Plaintiff Lawyer: I'm suggesting that this Court held in Del Monte Dunes that a 1983 action at law for damages is an action at law for Seventh Amendment purposes. And I think the State has conceded, and we submit as well, that there is no precise historical analog for the specific theory of the affirmative defense in this case. And I think Del Monte Dunes tells you that when that is so, you look to the functional considerations, the -- the divide between the judge and jury, and, predominantly, factual issues are for the jury as a default rule.

Judge: Why isn't it enough for you to get the right to a jury after an exhaustion determination if that determination is non-binding?

Plaintiff Lawyer: Two things. I'm not sure why it would be non-binding. But the second is, in this lawsuit, Mr. Richards got past summary judgment on a theory of unavailability under Ross versus Blake and a First Amendment claim that the Sixth Circuit said stated a prima facie case for --for First Amendment retaliation. He got to the point where he should have had a jury decide that. The Seventh Amendment can't turn on whether, after dismissal, a pro se plaintiff might be able to file Lawsuit 2.0 on -- on some of these claims.

Judge: Well, why not? I mean, it -- it -- if the prior determination without a jury is not binding, what exactly has been taken away? If you can proceed to litigate before a jury with respect to exhaustion, what --what's the great loss? Time, of course, but --

Plaintiff Lawyer: Other than five years of --

Judge: -- but he's got time on his hands, right?

Plaintiff Lawyer: --of effort. It's no small feat for a litigant like Mr. Richards, who was representing himself throughout these proceedings until the Sixth Circuit appointed counsel, to even get a case filed and before a court and all the way past summary judgment. So I think that's a significant consideration.

But our, you know, second point is that I think, you know, the modern Restatement of Judgments would say that once an issue is finally decided, the words "without prejudice" are not magic words that mean that nothing has actually been decided.

Judge: And we're asked to weigh -- and this discussion seems to be summing up, at least in part, two difficult circumstances. On one hand, a suggestion that it's harmless or might be absurd to require the government to fill in a TBD. Congress may have thought dates were important, but, nah, they're not that important. And on the other hand, the potential that a non-citizen might be removed from the country in absentia without any notice of the charges against them, their right to an attorney, the facts of their case, based on a compliant notice of change that just says "show up on a date certain," and they don't know where it's coming from necessarily or who. Then, clear and convincing evidence in whatever form that the government may supply in a non-adversarial proceeding, an inquisitorial proceeding before its own employee and an immigration judge. How do we weigh those two consequential arguments?

Plaintiff's Lawyer: Sure, Your Honor. So I think the short answer is that Congress has done that weighing for you. The date and time are situated no differently from the other information listed in paragraph (1). But even if we were to do the weighing, as Your Honor noted, the first downside is entirely within the government's control. The government can simply put a date and time on the notice to appear, as it acknowledged nearly 30 years ago that the statute required it to do.

On the other hand, the non-citizen has no control over whether they get adequate notice. And so, in the hypothetical you're describing, where they are never told of the charges against them and are removed in absentia without an opportunity to present their case before the immigration judge, that’s a severe sanction. Congress recognized how draconian this could be, which is why in absentia removal orders can be reopened at any time if there's inadequate notice.

Judge: Thank you.

Judge: You say inadequate notice, but they had notice.

Plaintiff's Lawyer: That's right, Your Honor. Again, Congress replaced the reasonable notice regime with notice in accordance with paragraphs (1) or (2). So rather than a case-by-case determination, Congress simply required the government to comply with these two paragraphs. It thought that this approach would make things easier for the government—just follow these precise instructions, put the required seven pieces of information on a notice, and no further case-by-case analysis is needed.

Judge: Well, Congress was concerned, correct me if I’m wrong, about people not showing up for their removal hearings, right?

Plaintiff's Lawyer: That’s exactly right. Congress believed that the prior reasonable notice system gave too much discretion to immigration judges to say, “This person was confused, this person got this document but not that document.” So Congress made it easier for the government to obtain in absentia removal orders by stating, “Government, comply with the statute, and you’ll get your removal order. The non-citizen cannot complain that the notice to appear was confusing.”

Judge: Why wouldn’t a non-citizen show up if they get a notice stating the time and place of their removal proceeding and that failure to appear results in removal?

Plaintiff's Lawyer: Your Honor, the non-citizen does have every incentive to show up. Among other things, if they don’t, ICE can arrest them and bring them to the hearing. The government can obtain an in absentia removal order fairly straightforwardly. Even if the initial NTA was defective, all it takes at that hearing is for the government to print out a new one with the correct date and time.

Judge: No, I understand there are incentives. I’m just talking about statutory language. Justice Alito pointed out the text of this provision. If a non-citizen knows the critical information—date, time, and the consequence of failing to appear—why would it be draconian for Congress to say that person could be removed in absentia if they didn’t show up?

Plaintiff's Lawyer: Congress could have structured the system that way, but it did not. Before 1996, there was a different system—

Judge: Okay, but why? Why wouldn’t it make sense? Let’s say I disagree with your argument about the NTA and believe the removal proceeding is the appropriate place to register objections. Why wouldn’t it make sense for a non-citizen, knowing the time and date, to show up? What incentive would they have not to, aside from just refusing to attend?

Plaintiff's Lawyer: In Niz-Chavez, this Court likened the NTA to an indictment in a criminal case.

Judge: Okay.

Plaintiff's Lawyer: And in a criminal case, we don’t say, “Just show up, or we’ll incarcerate you.”

Judge: But what’s the incentive? Are you saying this is just a technicality?

Plaintiff's Lawyer: I don’t think it’s a technicality, any more than an indictment in a criminal case is a technicality.

Judge: So your entire argument hinges on interpreting (a)(1) as requiring all of the information, or else the NTA is completely invalid?

Plaintiff's Lawyer: That’s right. Practically, a non-citizen needs to know what they’re defending against—what the government is alleging, on what basis, and what statutory claims they face. Congress could not have intended a system where someone is required to appear on a given date and time, under threat of in absentia removal, without even knowing what they are defending against.

Judge: But couldn’t the non-citizen just show up and say, “I don’t know what the government is alleging against me,” and the immigration judge could then determine that the government’s notice was inadequate?

Plaintiff's Lawyer: That’s possible, but the statute doesn’t leave that decision solely to an immigration judge’s discretion. The government must provide something akin to an indictment—notice of the charges—so the non-citizen knows what to expect before appearing.

Judge: If both a non-citizen with a complete NTA and one with a defective NTA receive a notice to appear on June 15, are they really different? If both show up, there’s no in absentia removal, correct?

Plaintiff's Lawyer: That’s right. If they appear, no removal in absentia occurs. However, under paragraph (1), which mandates that a proper notice to appear be issued in every removal proceeding, a person with a defective NTA could still object.

Judge: And if neither of them show up, a removal order is issued. But one can move to reopen the case while the other cannot, correct?

Plaintiff's Lawyer: Yes, with one caveat—we don’t believe an immigration judge should issue the removal order in the first place under (b)(5)(A).

Judge: But assuming they do, the person with the defective NTA can reopen the case, and the other cannot?

Plaintiff's Lawyer: Exactly.

Judge: Is there anything strange about Congress policing its own requirements in this way—allowing one person to reopen but not the other?

Plaintiff's Lawyer: No, Your Honor. Congress structured it this way to ensure that the government follows the statute’s notice requirements.

Judge: And regarding the government’s concern that people won’t show up, isn’t Congress addressing that issue differently? The law allows people with in absentia removal orders to move to rescind them under certain conditions. If someone doesn’t show up, they have the burden of proving they never received notice or that the notice was defective.

Plaintiff's Lawyer: That’s correct. Congress ensured that the government must meet its statutory obligations in order to obtain the procedural advantage of an in absentia removal.

We'll hear argument next in Case 23-997, Stanley versus the City of Sanford.

Lawyer of Plaintiff
Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court: The ADA permits former employees in Lieutenant Stanley's shoes to challenge discrimination in post-employment benefits. There are at least two paths to that conclusion here.

First, the narrow path is to recognize that former employees may sue when they allege that they were discriminated against as qualified individuals while still employed. After she was diagnosed with Parkinson's in 2016 and before she retired as a firefighter in 2018, Lieutenant Stanley was indisputably a qualified individual.

During that period, she was subject to a policy that she alleges reduced her compensation in a discriminatory manner.

Under the ADA, former employees may challenge such discrimination even if they are no longer employed by the time they bring suit.

If the Court adopts this rationale, it should make clear that it is not foreclosing the possibility that an employee may also challenge discrimination that, unlike here, occurs entirely after their last day on the job.

Second, if the Court chooses to resolve this case on a broader rationale, it should hold that former employees may challenge post-employment discrimination.

Read in context, as the City rightly concedes it must be, the "qualified individual" definition ensures that employers can make necessary job-related decisions, but it doesn't license discrimination unrelated to job performance or impose a temporal limitation on the ADA's protections.

Congress made a choice to prohibit discrimination in post-employment benefits, benefits that are crucial to recruiting people to take on dangerous jobs like firefighting and policing.

Yet, under the City's reading, the ADA's protections for these benefits mean the least precisely when they matter most.

Congress did not enact such a self-defeating scheme. I welcome the Court's questions.

Judge: There has been some discussion in the briefs about the exact scope of the Almendarez-Torres exception. Are these questions litigated, or are they merely academic? Does the exact scope matter for this case?

Defendant's Lawyer: It does not matter for this case because the Court only needs to apply the rule from Descamps and Mathis, which states that any offense-related conduct beyond the elements falls under Apprendi, not Almendarez-Torres. That is enough to resolve this case.

There are a few additional facts, but candidly, there are not many cases addressing them because few recidivist statutes deal with aspects like the date of the offense. There is little case law on these matters because, in most situations, the defendant may not have a legitimate argument regarding such facts.

Judge: I would like to explore what happens on remand—not that we need to decide it now, but just to understand it better.

Defendant's Lawyer: Yes.

Judge: The distinction between what qualifies for harmless error review and what is considered structural error is sometimes unclear. For example, it is structural error if a defendant does not receive a reasonable doubt instruction or is denied their choice of counsel. However, it is subject to harmless error review if a jury is not instructed on an element of the crime or if there is a variance.

Here, we are dealing with a plea bargaining situation, meaning there is no trial record to analyze for harmless error review. That raises a concern—how can harmless error review be conducted when there is no trial record? Wouldn’t the review process involve examining the very records the defendant wants to challenge before a jury?

Defendant's Lawyer: Right.

Judge: How does that work? How does harmless error review proceed when there is no trial record? Also, in this case, the defendant pleaded guilty to an information that listed different crimes as ACCA predicate offenses. He did not receive notice that the government would use crimes from when he was 18 to enhance his sentence by 15 years. Now, in his mid-40s, he will remain in prison until his 60s. How can we analyze this situation and conclude that it was harmless that he did not even know what he was pleading guilty to?

Defendant's Lawyer: There are challenges even in conducting a Neder-type harmless error analysis after a jury verdict. Justice Scalia pointed this out in dissent in that case, arguing that it becomes a highly speculative exercise.

Of course, the majority of the Court disagreed. The Court has also disagreed in cases related to indictments. I do not want to argue too strongly against precedent, but your point is valid—when dealing with plea bargains, determining what a jury might have found is difficult. It requires an unusually strong showing from the government that the outcome would have been the same based on the kind of documents permitted under the Almendarez-Torres exception.

There will be cases where factors like widely spaced conviction dates make the harmless error argument stronger. However, in this case, the concerns raised reinforce the argument that we have a serious basis for a non-harmless error claim on remand.

Judge: Courts have nearly always treated offenses as occurring on separate occasions when they happened a day or more apart or at a significant distance. That is still good law, correct?

Defendant's Lawyer: Of course.

Judge: The phrase used was "nearly always."

Defendant's Lawyer: Yes, that is correct.

Judge: A ruling here could require states to change their recidivism practices. That is something we did not contemplate in Descamps and Mathis. You argue that those cases were based on constitutional concerns, but they did not explicitly address the constitutional question, correct?

Defendant's Lawyer: I believe they did address the constitutional question, although they also included statutory analysis.

Regarding the effect on states, only a small number of states have a requirement similar to the "different occasions" finding. Overruling Almendarez-Torres would have a much greater impact. But in this case, the number of affected states is minimal.

Furthermore, the fact that there is no amicus brief from any state suggests that this would not be a significant burden. States could easily adjust their existing procedures to include a jury determination.

Judge: One concern raised by amici is that because plea bargaining is so prevalent, making this an element of the offense could actually be harmful to criminal defendants. How do you respond to that?

Defendant's Lawyer: The brief from NAFD addresses this concern. The change would actually improve plea bargaining dynamics.

In felon-in-possession cases where ACCA enhancements apply, defendants previously had no opportunity to negotiate because probation officers could simply inform the judge that the sentence had to be increased. Defendants had no fair notice and no leverage in negotiations.

Statistics show that 14 percent of felon-in-possession cases go to trial, which is a high number for the federal system. If the different occasions clause were treated as an element of the offense, prosecutorial discretion would come into play. Defendants could then negotiate in exchange for removing the ACCA enhancement.

Additionally, when this case was litigated, the maximum sentence for a 922(g) conviction was 10 years, but now it is 15 years. That change in law strengthens the argument that this shift would actually improve the plea bargaining process.

[Judge]: Nobody wants to say yes here. Nobody wants to say yes. Going inside the courtroom, we have a lot of screws. So, you lost a screw. Nobody found the screw that popped out. In order to find the right screw, she would have had to leave the watch because the threads are all different, right?

[Defendant]: Right.

[Judge]: But nobody found the screw that actually popped out?

[Defendant]: No, even to this day, nobody’s found it. It is so tiny. It’s unbelievable.

[Judge]: How much did your Bulgari watch cost?

[Plaintiff]: This watch was in the thousands. It was a gift to me, and it was in the thousands, and I’ve had it for many years.

[Judge]: Are you an authorized Bulgari watch repair place?

[Defendant]: No.

[Judge]: Why were you taking your several thousand dollar watch to an unauthorized dealer that wasn’t authorized?

[Plaintiff]: Over the years, changing the battery was just something very basic that, and why are you opening up an incredibly expensive watch? You’re not—I mean, maybe that's like a function of that watch where you have to be very careful with it. Maybe you should have a sneeze guard, you know, so that if something goes flying, we can stop it. I don’t know.

[Defendant]: No, we fix all kinds of watches, Rolexes, Breitlings. I mean, some of them are easy, some of them are technical.

[Judge]: Okay, so at this point, you have no faith, and so you take the watch to Bulgari?

[Plaintiff]: I asked the employee to please sign something, and she wrote, “Screw lost in store by me,” because I’m sure she felt awful. I’m sure she did. And then I took the watch to Bulgari, and they looked it over, and it took several weeks.

[Defendant]: I don’t think you’ll pay if you’re taking it there. It’s not going to be a $20 screw, right?

[Plaintiff]: Well, that’s what Mr. Babayev believed, that he could just send it to whoever, and they were just going to fix it. I didn’t know whether they would put in the right parts or approved parts for that watch, seeing as what happened at the store. So, that was where they charged you then $385 because you can’t just buy a screw according to you?

[Defendant]: That’s what I said. Can you give me a screw? I asked them because he asked me to ask the Bulgari store, and they said they cannot.

[Judge]: Okay. And did you ever call the Bulgari store once you realized you were being sued to see if it was possible to just buy the screw?

[Defendant]: No, basically, they usually charge you more just to get—they charge you more for the maintenance. They’re in the business of making the $385 for the battery change. They change your gaskets. They—but that’s the only way to get the part that they lost back in there.

[Defendant]: Exactly. They needed to do their thing before they could give me back the watch whole.

[Judge]: Okay. What is your defense?

[Defendant]: Yes, we lost a screw, but it’s not a $385 screw. It doesn’t—you know, I don’t deny it. I told her I would replace it. I do have a watchmaker on the premises. He comes in Monday, Wednesday, and Thursday. I would have charged nothing for it because I know it’s my fault. To compensate for her loss and everything, I’ll take care of it, not even charging for the battery. She said, “No, I don’t trust you. I don’t want to do that.” So, I really signed saying that, you know, if you could bring me the screw, I’ll put it in. Not the $385.

[Judge]: Honestly speaking, right?

[Defendant]: Sensible.

[Judge]: So, how much does changing a battery cost? If everything had gone well, what would that cost?

[Defendant]: On her watch, it’s basically around $10-$15 depending on the size of the battery.

[Judge]: Right, but what about the fact that this is a really expensive watch, and that she has no idea what’s going to happen a year from now with the wrong screw in there? What about the fact that none of it is her fault?

[Defendant]: I mean, there are screws, and then there are screws, I guess. Yeah.

[Judge]: And that’s kind of the risk you run when you open up a several thousand-dollar watch and lose the screw. Only one screw would fit that; otherwise, the whole mechanism is gone. That’s why I have a watch guy on the premises. He fixes all kinds of watches, certified watch technician. I don’t have him on a daily basis because he has other jobs that he does Monday, Wednesday, and Friday is the only time I have him.

[Defendant]: I hear you, and I understand what you’re saying, and it seems kind of harsh. But by the same token, I hear her, and she’s blameless. And she lost faith, you know, and she doesn’t want you to compensate her by paying her $10 because she was only able to put the watch back together by taking it to Bulgari. But you’re saying, “Well, no, judge, she would have been able to put the watch together again. She just needed to leave it with me and give me a chance.”

[Judge]: And that’s where the rub is. Does she have to leave it with you? Because that’s reasonable, and that’s the only thing she should be able to do, and that’s the only thing you should be held accountable for.

[Judge]: Does she have to leave her several thousand-dollar watch with you guys when she’s already brought it there once, you lost a screw, you made her come back, and then it turns out it was the wrong screw, and she’s done with you? And I don’t think she does.

[Judge]: I don’t think she does. I’m going to deduct $15, which is what she would have paid at your place, and I’m going to order you to pay her $370. Verdict for the plaintiff.

Judge: "But that has nothing to do with you giving some sort of financial support to this man for the support of your child. Your child, not his child—your child."
Defendant: "It is my child, that is correct. But like I said, my job had me traveling, and I really didn't have the extra money."

Judge: All parties are right here. Welcome back to The People’s Court. Next case on the docket: the plaintiff says the defendant stiffed him on embroidering the defendant's company name on some T-shirts, but the defendant says the guy wrote "Newark Heart Care" instead of "Car Care," so he's got to be kidding. It's the case of brokenhearted Leon against heavy debts.

Judge: Okay, Ernest Ricka, you are suing Leon Goretski of Newark Car Care Incorporated. You’re the owner, for $1,618.15 for 90-something t-shirts that he purchased from you and never paid for. Okay, what happened?

Plaintiff: Well, first of all, I've been doing work with Newark Car Care for about 20 years. I spoke to Leon, he said come in, and he needed stuff for the staff.

Judge: What is Newark Car Care?

Plaintiff: An auto repair shop.

Judge: How much staff do you have at any given time?

Plaintiff: Fourteen guys.

Judge: Okay, go ahead.

Plaintiff: So, he gave me an order for, I believe, 97 t-shirts for the mechanics in the back, and then he ordered polos for the guys at the counter and some Nike jackets for Leon and the higher-up people. That was delivered in March. I went in and picked up a check, and the check was returned.

Judge: How was it returned? For what?

Plaintiff: Insufficient funds. So then I told Leon, "I need the money," and he gave me another check for $1,639. That check came back. So then I called the bank that he used, and they literally told me, "This check will never be good."

Plaintiff: After going back to him, he gave me a third check. That check also came back as insufficient.

Plaintiff: We went back and forth for weeks. I called him, and he kept saying, "I know I owe you the money, everything was fine, and I’m going to pay you." He said I’d been fair. But it dragged on, so I finally filed with the court.

Defendant: The first part of the story is correct. Everything was fine, about $2,600 worth of merchandise. I was trying to work something out and get a discount on the last $1,600 because when the summer hit, we started putting on the t-shirts and realized the sizing was wrong.

Judge: What do you mean the sizing was wrong?

Defendant: The triple XLs were too tight.

Judge: You’re saying the shirts were too small?

Defendant: Yes, especially for my bigger guys.

Judge: But you didn’t bring any shirts here as proof?

Defendant: No, I left them at the shop.

Judge: How can you be this relaxed about your defense? You didn’t bring a single piece of evidence.

[Laughter]

Judge: Does this guy lose?

Judge: Yes, because he doesn't have proof that there was anything wrong with the shirts.

Judge: Fair point. I’m giving you the last word. Your answer to the complaint says the shirts were mis-sized and the name was misspelled. How many shirts had a misspelling?

Defendant: Four or five.

Judge: Did you bring them?

Defendant: No.

Judge: Why not?

Defendant: I left them at the shop.

Judge: So you’re telling me you didn’t bring the shirts to prove there was a mistake?

Defendant: No.

Judge: You stop payment on the check?

Defendant: That’s not true.

Judge: No? You didn’t stop payment on the check? It bounced because you didn’t have money in your account. So why did you say you stopped payment on the check?

Defendant: I told the plaintiff about the mistakes.

Judge: Did he ever tell you anything about mistakes?

Plaintiff: No. Every time I saw him, he was fine with the order.

Judge: You haven’t paid for the Nike jackets either. Weren’t you wearing the jacket when he came in?

Plaintiff: Yes, and so was everyone else.

Judge: And you told him, "I’ll give you a credit card in a week," then in a day, and you just kept pushing him off?

Defendant: I was working on paying him.

Judge: No, you weren’t. Paying him would mean handing him $200, $300 a week. What you did—stringing him along with three bounced checks—is not working on paying him. There’s no shame in financial struggles, but there is shame in treating him like garbage. That’s what you did.

Judge: You didn’t even bring the shirts you were complaining about. If you had, we could examine them right now. Instead, you’re making vague claims and hoping I believe them.

Judge: Pay the man. Verdict for the plaintiff: $1,618.15.

Judge: "So once your ex came back, you were done with Mr. Spencer because you really wanted to be with your ex."

Defendant: "Yes."

Judge: "Yes. Any way he was able to get in contact with me... (stammering) I... I changed everything, you know."

Defendant: "And when he did contact me, or his ex contacted me, it was like, 'Oh, we already did the DNA already. You don't have to worry no more. These are Mitchell's kids.'"

Judge: "So you did tell that story."

Defendant: "Yes, I did."

Judge: "So you said it, and you knew it was a lie when you said it."

Defendant: "Yes, I did. Yes, I said it."

Plaintiff: "She just made a mistake and messed with somebody and got a baby, and now we're trying to save my grandson, let him know that he's the dad."

Judge: "Thirst is going both ways, ma'am."

Defendant: "But he called her thirsty, though."

Plaintiff: "Let me tell you. It's the fact that I knew I wasn't gonna have a long relationship with her, but when you feed somebody the lies that you know they wanna hear, that's 'thirst' to get what you want from them."

Defendant: "But yeah, now it's a baby involved, what's your thirst?"

Plaintiff: "And now I feel like she's being thirsty, trying to put the baby on me."

Judge: It is certainly a fair point that the criminal defenders are on the other side.

Defendant's Lawyer: I think they have clearly made a judgment that this rule that the Petitioner and the government are urging is a net benefit for criminal defendants. And I don't think you should take my word over theirs on that. What I think—my submission, though, is that I think it is indisputable that in some cases, like you said, the cases where this is a close question and the government refuses to bifurcate, I think it's going to prejudice defendants. I think that's what the Harrell case that we cite at page 46 shows. In some cases, this is going to prejudice defendants.

Judge: But isn't that true of Apprendi, generally? I mean, Apprendi was not justified on the basis of always helping defendants. There are any number of elements that a particular defendant might prove and might decide in a particular set of circumstances he would rather argue to a judge. I mean, you know, it just doesn't seem to me that that's a reason for denying the force of Apprendi in this situation.

Defendant's Lawyer: So, I agree the same could be said of the prejudice point on Apprendi. But this is not something I am making up. This is what courts have said going back hundreds of years—there's a reason to treat recidivism differently. So I think Apprendi recognized a tradition as to present crimes. And Apprendi made that very clear at pages 488 and 496 of the opinion in distinguishing Almendarez-Torres. What the Court said there was Almendarez-Torres was about prior crimes. It was about issues that didn't go to the defendant's guilt. They went to punishment only. This tradition we're recognizing here is about present crimes. The Court recognized it was the prejudice point and the fact this was sort of a collateral issue, more like a sentencing issue. That was why there was a different tradition as to recidivism.

Judge: So your point is it wasn't a historical accident necessarily, it was justified by a principle of not prejudicing defendants?

Defendant's Lawyer: That's right. And I think a good place to look for this is the Bishop treatise, one of the leading criminal law treatises of the 19th century, which said just that. He said that recidivism is treated differently because putting it before a jury is seriously prejudicial to defendants and because this is an issue that's more like a sentencing issue which, as noted, has a tradition for hundreds of years as being not subject to the Sixth Amendment.

Judge: On the methodological question, because I think how to think about all this is pretty important here, so start with the text. The text itself of the Constitution does not tell us the answer, just the bare words, correct?

Defendant's Lawyer: Correct.

Judge: Okay. So then we usually look to history. We might not like it, but—

Defendant's Lawyer: Agreed.

Judge: —unless we're just making it up, I don't know where else we're going to look. And the question raised was, who has the burden on that? And I think, I would like you to speak to who has the burden. Do you have the burden to show a consistent uniform practice, or does the other side have the burden to show a consistent, uniform practice going the other way in which recidivism always went to the jury?

Defendant's Lawyer: I think that is the critical question. If the government has the burden, then I see no way in which Almendarez-Torres is correctly decided. But I think that in these cases, the government does not have the burden because, as a default principle, when somebody is coming into this Court or a court saying the Constitution invalidates my sentence or invalidates a statute, typically it is upon that person to show that there is some well-established understanding that's what the Constitution means. And sometimes when the text is clear, like in the Gaudin case that was cited, then the burden flips to the government to show some historical practice that contradicts the text. But, as noted, the text here doesn't answer the question. And so we're looking to history. And I would say we're not only just looking—we're not looking to history directly interpreting the connection of the Sixth Amendment. We're looking to history—we're looking to state common law principles. And I think when the Court is that far removed from something actually interpreting the Sixth Amendment, the Court should demand a level of uniformity in those state common law principles before making the leap that the Constitution necessarily incorporated those common law principles.

Judge: Is the right year to look at 1791 or 1868? Obviously, this is a federal case but—

Defendant's Lawyer: So I think there's academic debate about that. I think, for purposes of this case, the time of the founding is obviously the most relevant time. And I guess what I would say is I think, if anything, what the history shows here is that there was an almost uniform practice that legislatures had discretion in this area. So it wasn't only the four states where judges were allowed to make findings about recidivism. It was also four states—we have Virginia, Massachusetts, and Maine, significant states, between 1818 and 1824, enacting supplemental information statutes that allowed the government to withhold recidivism allegations from an indictment, despite generally requiring all elements of an offense to be in an indictment. So we have at least eight—and then West Virginia added on a similar statute in 1868. So we have eight states. And then, on the other side, the government and Plaintiff haven't cited a single case in any relevant time period where a court struck down a statute on the ground that it assigned recidivism findings to judges or allowed the government to withhold these allegations from the indictment. And so I think, as far as I can see, there's an unrebutted tradition here of legislatures having discretion when it comes to recidivism. And I think there were good reasons for that, as we discussed.

Judge: Throughout this pregnancy, as you prepare for the birth of Giovanni, was Mr. Lee present?
Plaintiff: No. He was telling people I was just getting fat. He was like, "She just eats a lot of fast food."
Everybody was rubbing... My friends were rubbing my stomach. I'm showing ultrasounds to people.
He's like, "Don't believe her. She's just fat." The entire time. I'm talking about, "I'm going into labor."
He's like, "No, you're not."
Judge: What kind of denial is this? Mr. Lee, you've taken denial too far.
Plaintiff: Exactly.    Defendant:I am sorry

Judge: Alan Madza, you are suing Dane for $250 that the two of you agreed he would pay you after he hit you from behind. What happened?

Plaintiff: So I was, uh, going home from work. I was driving home, and I was approaching an intersection, and I had the green light. And the police officer had his car, and he had his lights flashing.

Judge: So?

Plaintiff: And he was—

Judge: What? In the intersection?

Plaintiff: He’s in front of me.

Judge: Trying to cut in front of you?

Plaintiff: Yeah.

Judge: Okay. Go on.

Plaintiff: So I come to a stop because that’s the right thing to do.

Judge: His lights are on?

Plaintiff: Yes.

Judge: Oh. Oh. Okay. Was the siren on?

Plaintiff: Uh-huh.

Judge: Okay.

Plaintiff: So about five to ten seconds later, um, I just get hit from the—from behind. The same police officer stops to see that everything is okay with us. And, uh, what he told—what the police officer told me himself is that he saw my car stopped, and then it got launched forward, like it got hit.

Judge: Okay. Did the police officer write a report?

Plaintiff: The police officer said it was optional to write a report, um, just because it’s just whether—how we want to settle it. So we just made an agreement. Well, we got out of the cars, we inspected the damage, and I saw that my car had, uh—the bumper was cracked, the paint was chipped, and my muffler was broken. It was swaying left and right.

Judge: Okay. What happened?

Defendant: Well, that’s incorrect. What actually happened was, uh, Mr. Alan was driving in front of me, and the police car was coming perpendicular to him with the lights. The lights had just turned on, and there was no siren. So they slowed down almost to a stop, and Alan slowed down, so they were letting Alan go forward. But Alan hesitated, and the police officers—they had already stopped. So when he hesitated, I had to press on my brakes—

Judge: Wait, wait. I don’t understand. If you saw everything you just said, why do you hit him? If you see everything you just said, you shouldn’t be hitting him, ‘cause your car shouldn’t be in motion.

Defendant: I was moving approximately 3 mph.

Judge: Even more reason that you could stop. If his car stopped, why wouldn’t you have time to stop before hitting him?

Defendant: Because I pressed the brakes, and the water main that was broken—my vehicle, I was driving a Mercedes, and the ABS kicked in, and the vehicle just slid and just tapped him.

Judge: Oh. So love tap?

Defendant: Yeah, it was a little love tap.

Judge: Exactly what he called it in the answer—the little tap. I don’t know what you think love is, my friend. I’m going to tell you right now: you hit me with a car, it’s not love.

Defendant: [Laughs]

Judge: All right. So it’s your fault. It’s obviously your fault. What is it, his fault? Uh, how is it his fault?

Judge (to audience member): So if the plaintiff stops abruptly because he hears a siren, and the defendant hits him, is the defendant responsible for the accident?

Audience Member: Yes. Most—most definitely.

Judge: Even if it’s an abrupt stop?

Audience Member: Abrupt stop, sure, yes.

Judge: There was sirens, so—

Audience Member: Yeah, he’d be at fault. Um, and you’re supposed to have a length, you know, there—

Judge: There you go! Ding! Going inside the courtroom.

Defendant: It—it’s—it’s the city’s fault, it’s Alan’s fault, and it’s the police’s fault.

Judge: I want to know how it’s Alan’s fault.

Defendant: It’s Alan’s fault because he had the right—right of way. There was no emergency, there was—

Judge: I’m sorry, are you telepathic? There’s a cop with the lights—

Defendant: Yeah.

Judge: —and it doesn’t matter. I know what you’re talking about. They were late for lunch. I know what you mean. But when a police officer has the sirens or the lights on, you, by law, have to give it passage. Why do you think they put it on?

Defendant: Right.

Judge: All right. So—so what did he do wrong?

Defendant: He should have kept going when they stopped because the lights will flash.

Judge: If he had kept going, they’d be giving him a ticket for not stopping for a sirening car!

Defendant: How are they going to give him a ticket if they’re on their way to a supposedly emergency?

Judge: Oh, so you think he might get away with it?

Defendant: He should have taken the chance.

Judge: Okay. Just stop. All right. So what happens out there? You guys strike a deal?

Plaintiff: Yeah. So we got out of the car, we saw the damage, and he apologized to me for hitting the car.

Judge: And you saw the damage?

Plaintiff: He said it’s not that severe, but I told him that it needs to be reworked—the body part, uh, the bumper, and it has to be repainted.

Judge: So you guys actually—what kind of car was this?

Plaintiff: It’s a BMW 328.

Judge: Okay. And so you guys actually come up with a figure. I said $250. Did you agree to pay him $250?

Defendant: No. Actually, I agreed to pay him $200.

Judge: Have you paid it?

Defendant: I gave him $41.

Judge: Did he give you $41?

Plaintiff: He gave me $31.

Judge: $31. And according to you, you agreed to pay what?

Defendant: Uh, the balance due for—on—

Judge: You agreed to pay the balance of—

Defendant: $200—

Judge: —you minus the $31. And have you paid it?

Defendant: No.

Judge: Why haven’t you paid it?

Defendant: Because after I reflected on the situation and thought about—you know, Alan, very nice guy.

Judge: Boy Next Door!

Defendant: Boy Next Door.

Judge: He called you—he looks like—exact—hold on, I’m going to read your exact words. "The plaintiff is a nice guy. He looks like Tom Cruise in Risky Business."

Defendant: That’s what I—

Judge: The accident’s not my fault. You’re dating Tom Cruise! You should be very happy.

Defendant: [Laughs]

Judge: All right, go ahead.

Plaintiff: I have a video of the agreement.

Judge: Let’s play it.

(Video plays, confirming $250 agreement.)

Judge: Verdict for the plaintiff. $219.

Judge: Mr. Stewart, that raises a question for me because, like the Chief Justice, I was wondering, what could the OCC do here? And you alluded to that. It's interesting, I'm not sure what to make of this, but in the 13 years or so since Dodd-Frank, we don't have an OCCA rule on escrow accounts, except for the one issued in 2011 immediately after Dodd-Frank, in which it reaffirmed its rule banning any regulation by states on escrow accounts under an "obstruct or impair" standard that predated Dodd-Frank. That purported to ratify what it had done before under the old law. And as I took it from a couple of cryptic footnotes in your brief, you're not asking us to defer to that regulation. In fact, you seem to suggest that it's inconsistent with the law and entitled to no respect. Why hasn't the OCC done something here under the law that actually exists?

Defendant’s Lawyer: Well, the OCC did file an amicus brief in the Second Circuit taking—

Judge: The other way.

Defendant’s Lawyer: The other way. And so that—that was what they did. Now I—I would—

Judge: But you seem to have disavowed everything the OCC has done since Dodd-Frank. What do we do with that?

Defendant’s Lawyer: Well, I think there are substantial indications in the text and history of Dodd-Frank that, although Congress intended to codify the Barnett Bank standard, it intended to revise or overturn the way that the OCC had been making preemption—

Judge: And then the OCC said maybe you thought so, but, ha, we promulgated it before Dodd-Frank, so you're stuck with it.

Defendant’s Lawyer: And—

Judge: And now you're saying, nah, that's not right. And is the OCC going to actually do some of this work at some point under the law?

Defendant’s Lawyer: Well, as far as I'm aware, the OCC has never issued a case-by-case preemption determination. And I don't know what the reason is, but I would say, if you imagine the OCC trying to do a case-by-case preemption determination with respect to the New York law at issue here, the most straightforward way to do it would simply be to say we have a regulation that says states can't regulate mortgage escrow accounts, this is a regulation of mortgage escrow accounts; therefore, it's preempted. But, if the OCC tried to do it that way, it would run into the provisions of Dodd-Frank that say, when the OCC does these determinations, it considers the impact of the state law—

Judge: In fact, we have exactly the regulation. You say if they did this. They did it. They said there are no escrow regulations that are permissible under state law. They're all preempted. But you're not defending that regulation; you're disavowing it. You've flip-flopped positions on it. And I'm asking, is the OCC ever going to get around to doing that which Dodd-Frank directs it to do?

Defendant’s Lawyer: Well, I think I would say Dodd-Frank authorizes but doesn't direct it to do this. Now, if the Petitioners' position in this case prevails and if the Court holds that some inquiry into practical impacts is necessary with respect to the individual state law, then it's very possible that the OCC will start making these case-by-case determinations because, independent of legal expertise, the OCC has expertise in the way that national banks operate and can bring that expertise to bear in determining whether—

Judge: If it has expertise, why are you disagreeing with its longstanding position?

Defendant’s Lawyer: I think the two reasons—well, two or three reasons. The first is that, as I say, we think that the text of Dodd-Frank manifests a disapproval by Congress of the way that OCC had been doing these determinations. The text says case-by-case determinations, and it's really the opposite of an OCC rule that says here are many categories of state laws that can't be enforced at all.

Judge: Even though key members said otherwise?

Defendant’s Lawyer: They were not the key members. They were two members of the Senate who had drafted the Senate version of the preempt—

Judge: I shouldn't have used "the" but key members. I shouldn't have used the word "the."

Defendant’s Lawyer: Okay. They had drafted the Senate version of the preemption provision, and the Senate version contained a general reference to the legal standard in Barnett Bank but didn't use the phrase "prevents or significantly interferes with." And then the House bill had framed the preemption standard as does the state law "prevent and"—

Judge: I interrupted you. Keep going with why you changed positions. So one is your reading of the text and history.

Defendant’s Lawyer: That they indicate that Congress wanted the OCC to redo this. I think the second thing that we would say is the way in which OCC's view is currently manifested is in the 2011 regulations, but Congress said the way that OCC is supposed to do preemption determinations going forward is through case-by-case determinations. And, historically, it's been a requirement for deference that the agency act through the procedural mechanism that Congress specified.

Judge: Do you have a view on whether this New York statute constitutes a significant interference with national banking powers?

Defendant’s Lawyer: We don't have a concluded view. Certainly, as the opposing counsel points out, this is something that state banks have been complying with, apparently, without material impairment. I think it would depend in part on evidence or a factual showing about what rate of interest can the banks use on the money in the escrow account because—

Judge: Can I interpret that as suggesting that you're skeptical that it's a significant interference?

Defendant’s Lawyer: Yes.

Judge: Thank you.

Judge:
Hamid Shree, yes ma'am, you are suing Abraham Shen Hav for $1,415 that you say is owed to you as a result of an employment contract, sort of an independent contractor thing you had going on with him as a driver. Tell me what happened.

Plaintiff (Hamid Shree):
Yes ma'am, I'm Hamid Shree. I was driving his car. I have a contract with him from February 3rd.

Judge:
What was the contract?

Plaintiff (Hamid Shree):
That's Exhibit A and B. I have a copy of this. I have a copy of it.

Judge:
What I want to know is your understanding of the agreement.

Plaintiff (Hamid Shree):
Yes, ma'am. The agreement was like I have to drive the whole week. If I drive the whole week, then I have to pay him the first two weeks. He said I have to pay like $400 a week. I have to pay now, but then I returned his car on February 7th, on Tuesday.

Judge:
Just a second. It's your car?

Plaintiff (Hamid Shree):
Yes, ma'am.

Judge:
You actually own it or lease it?

Plaintiff (Hamid Shree):
I own it.

Judge:
Okay, then you rent it out to him?

Plaintiff (Hamid Shree):
Yes.

Judge:
And according to him, he's supposed to pay you $400 a week?

Plaintiff (Hamid Shree):
450.

Judge:
450, and that's what the contract says?

Plaintiff (Hamid Shree):
Yes.

Plaintiff (Hamid Shree):
Well, now you're on it for three days. They told me that you can drive the car to see how much you can make money like that, you know, and when I started, I feel like I can't make it. JUDGE (QUESTIONING DEFENDANT)

Did you pay rent for July and August 2018?

DEFENDANT: No, because I received a notice to quit.

What happened with the ventilation issue?

DEFENDANT: My workers stood their ground because they believed they were in the right. The plaintiff came in one day, threw down her purse, and started searching through my drawers.

JUDGE (REVIEWING LANDLORD-TENANT COURT AGREEMENT)

The agreement required the defendant to pay $1,665.28 by September 5th. Did she pay on time?

DEFENDANT: Yes, I personally put the check in her mailbox, and I have a photo as proof.

JUDGE: (Reviewing evidence) The payment was made on time.

JUDGE (REVIEWING PROPERTY CONDITION UPON VACATING)

The agreement required the defendant to leave the premises "broom clean" and return the keys. Did she comply?

PLAINTIFF: No, the place was a mess. There was garbage, furniture left outside, and items abandoned inside.

JUDGE (QUESTIONING DEFENDANT)

Why did you leave trash on the sidewalk?

Photos show garbage, leftover alcohol bottles, nail polish, and debris inside the property.

The plaintiff wins the case. The defendant did not comply with the agreement and is responsible for the damages and unpaid rent.

Judge: All parties please raise your right hand. Be seated and come to order. Please. Thank you.

Judge: Tian, you’ve asked us not to use your last name?

Plaintiff: Yes, please.

Judge: Okay, Tian. You’re suing Francis Pedford Johnson, your former landlord, for $2,800 for not returning your security deposit from back when?

Plaintiff: 2010.

Judge: 2010. Tell me what happened.

Plaintiff: Well, I knew Francis for a couple of years, we worked together, and when I was looking to move into a new apartment, she told me she had a multi-family home. I moved into her apartment, I lived there five years. It was an easy agreement, month to month. I gave her two months' security deposit.

Judge: How much did you give her?

Plaintiff: $2,800.

Judge: That was the security deposit? You gave her $2,800 plus the first month's rent?

Plaintiff: Correct.

Judge: Okay.

Plaintiff: And all she asked is that I live there at least a year and give her two months' notice when I was vacating. In August, I told her I was looking to relocate to Maryland, and I would know for sure and give her the two months that she asked for. In October, I gave her—

Judge: So you were roommates, or it was a separate entrance?

Plaintiff: She has a multi-family home. I was just renting one of her units.

Judge: Got it.

Plaintiff: I gave her my official notice in October that I would be moving in December. December came, and I moved out at the end of February.

Judge: Did you pay rent in October, November, and December?

Plaintiff: No. When I gave her my notice in October, she asked me if I was going to live out my deposit. I told her I had not planned to, but I would if she wanted.

Judge: Good idea.

Plaintiff: Well, I hadn’t planned on it. She brought it up, suggested it. I said I could do that. So, I didn’t pay rent in October.

Judge: Then October 10th she called you and said?

Plaintiff: She said, "I didn’t get your rent check for the month." And I said, "I’m living it out like you requested." And she’s like, "Oh no, no, you know, you can’t do that. I need the money." And I said, "Okay." I said, "Well, I’ve already allocated the rent funds for October to my moving expenses."

Judge: I don’t know what you’re saying. Did you pay October? Yes or no.

Plaintiff: No, I did not pay.

Judge: Did you pay November?

Plaintiff: No—I'm sorry, I did pay October.

Judge: Did you?

Plaintiff: I did not pay November.

Judge: This isn’t hard. You had to know I was going to ask these questions.

Plaintiff: Apologies. You did pay October, you didn’t pay November?

Plaintiff: Correct.

Judge: And did you pay December?

Plaintiff: I gave her notice in October. I had already paid rent.

Judge: You’re confusing me because you’re suing for the entire security deposit when you’re telling me point-blank that you didn’t pay November.

Plaintiff: Correct.

Judge: So why aren’t you just suing for $1,300?

Plaintiff: Because she flat-out just didn’t have it. She asked for more time, I gave it to her, and she took that as an opportunity to not pay me.

Judge: Did she ever give you any inkling that she wasn’t going to pay you?

Plaintiff: No, she just kept saying she couldn’t figure it out. First, she didn’t have it, then she asked for more time, then she said she wanted to figure it out.

Judge: How long does it take? Two years to figure out?

Defendant: The main reason is that Tian, when she left, she had her father, she had two other guys—well, she had about five people helping her move. And when she left, she left a huge air conditioner in the living room window, a small one in the bedroom window, and a huge—48 inches or 50 inches—television set on the bedroom floor.

Judge: What reason did she give you for not taking them?

Defendant: She said she told a couple of the young men they could have them and to contact me, but she never told me this. I had no idea.

Judge: Did they contact you to try to pick it up?

Defendant: No.

Judge: Did anybody come pick up the ACs and the TV?

Defendant: Finally.

Judge: What’s "finally"?

Defendant: February.

Judge: And she moved out when?

Defendant: The end of December.

Judge: So, within 30-45 days, somebody had picked up the items?

Defendant: Yes.

Judge: Did you re-rent the place in January or February?

Defendant: No, I couldn’t rent it with that stuff in there.

Judge: You could have. You could have paid someone $50 to take the TV out. At some point, you have to hold on for a certain amount of days. Typically, judges require landlords to wait 30 days. And within 30 days, she removed it. So, why are you still holding onto $1,300?

Defendant: There were other damages. She broke a window.

Judge: Did you repair the window?

Defendant: Yes.

Judge: What date?

Defendant: It's recent.

Judge: So, you waited two years to fix the window?

Defendant: Yes.

Judge: How much did it cost?

Defendant: $70.

Judge: Are there any other reasons for keeping the $1,300?

Defendant: She put holes in the walls.

Judge: Did she patch them?

Defendant: Yes, but the paint didn’t match.

Judge: The paint you gave her?

Defendant: Yes.

Judge: Then you should’ve provided the right paint. You are not entitled to keep $1,300 for a $70 repair and a TV that was picked up in 30 days. My ruling is for the Plaintiff for $1,230.
Manipulation Techniques: gaslighting, evasion, deflection, playing the victim

Plaintiff Lawyer: -- try --try to take a crack at the --at the question itself because, you know, it may come up in other cases.

And it says, you know, "can perform the essential functions of the person that such individual holds or desires." And then the question is: Do you have to hold or desire the position? And I think the best way grammatically to understand that is that there are present-tense verbs.

You have to be able to perform the function.

But then the rest of the -- the part of the sentence after -- with the word "that" and after "that," is a restrictive clause, modifying the position.

And so the thing you have to be able to perform is the essential functions of the job that you hold or desire to the extent that you hold or desire a job.

Judge: We'll hear argument this morning in Case 23-970, NVIDIA versus Ohman J:or Fonder AB. Mr. Katyal.

Lawyer of Defendant: Thank you, Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court: The Reform Act's pleading standards are unique.

Congress looked at private securities litigation and saw big fishing expeditions in discovery and extortion at settlements.

They knew that for centuries, heightened pleading had been required, but they super-charged it. First, they required plaintiffs to "state with particularity" facts giving rise to a strong inference the defendant acted with scienter, a dramatic break with common law, and, second, they required, for allegations outside of a plaintiff's personal knowledge, plaintiffs to "state with particularity all the facts on which their belief is formed." This double-heightened pleading requirement exists in virtually no other context.

Y2K is the lone example, and that is what resolves this case. As our cert petition and the amici explain, the Ninth Circuit's decision creates an easy roadmap for plaintiffs to evade the Reform Act.

When a stock drops, all they have to do is find an expert with numbers that contradict a company's public statements, then allege the company keeps records that executives look at, and then argue those records would have matched the hired expert's numbers. That's a recipe for Judge Friendly's warning of fraud by hindsight. This complaint, which alleges that Jensen Huang made false statements, is a good example.

It's a grave accusation against a respected CEO.

Yet the complaint never shows his scienter to be as cogent and compelling as not.

It merely surmises that Huang reviewed internal reports showing a higher quantum of crypto purchases than what he disclosed, yet it never alleges the contents of those reports. And to meet falsity, the complaint relies on an expert opinion with a series of implausible assumptions and inferences, not particularized allegations of fact.

Nothing dispels the more compelling explanation that Huang offered at the time.

He knew crypto buys were increasing prices, and so he introduced a crypto-specific chip and increased the supply of gaming chips with the hopes that prices would fall.

The fact this process took longer than anticipated is not securities fraud. The Ninth Circuit slighted the Act's heightened pleading requirements, and this Court should reverse. I welcome the Court's questions.

Judge: What would this complaint look like if it had complied with the heightened pleading standards?

Lawyer of Defendant: Yeah.

So I think it would have had to allege -- this complaint is based on internal documents, and so it would have to allege the contents of those documents, and particularly, Justice Thomas, for example, it would have to answer what specific data did the CEO actually see, how clear was it, when did he see that data.

Was the data generated after his public statements or before? How much does that data deviate from the public statements? Where does that data cover? Is it one country? Is it the world? And, you know, when was that data generated? So, you know, what did the CEO know, when did he know it, I think are the most important questions. And, here, when you scratch just below the surface -- and this is what Judge Sanchez's dissent, I think, so powerfully did or the district court did -- by going allegation by allegation, it all dissolves.

It's certainly a long complaint.

I'll give -- I'll spot them that.

But it's a long complaint like cotton candy that dissolves after -- it looks like a lot of volume, but it dissolves.

**Judge:**
Let me take both parts of your question, Justice Barrett.

Let me start with your -- the -- the last part, the title clause.

And I think the -- the title clause clarifies that the False Claims Act applies even if the United States no longer has title, present tense, to the money or property when the request is made.

So a request for money that's been provided to -- by the United States to a grantee is still a claim even though the grantee and not the United States holds title to the money when the request is made.

And the -- the revised addition of the "provides for" clause strengthens that interpretation because Congress also amended that to say "provides or has provided." So it's really a timing issue.

And I would think, if Congress -- if Congress were going to sever the link between the False Claims Act and protecting the public fisc that has been its historic focus, it would not have done that -- it would not have taken that huge step through such an oblique way as to revising the title clause, which really is more about timing and doesn't sever the link between the public fisc and the FCA.

And, Justice Barrett, let me take on your second question -- your second aspect of your question about the government -- the purpose and the interest here, and that gets back to what I was talking with Justice Jackson about.

And the text of the False Claims Act really answers that because, in the revised -- in the revisions, the amendments that were made in 2009, Congress specified: If the money or property is to be spent or used on the government's behalf or to advance a government program or interest and if the United States provides or has provided any portion of the money.

So, if -- if Congress wanted to sever this historic link between the False Claims Act and protecting the public fisc, the easiest way for it to do that would have been just to eliminate the "provides" clause altogether.

And Congress didn't -- didn't do that. It adopted a very narrow amendment --

**Plaintiff's Lawyer:**
Do --

**Judge:**
-- that was focused on the timing issue, not severing the -- the False Claims Act from the --

**Plaintiff's Lawyer:**
On -- on Justice Thomas's question about the hundred million dollars -- I want to focus on that as well for a minute -- what exactly makes something part of the public fisc, to use your terms, or makes something government funds, to use your term? What are the precise indicia?

**Judge:**
Sure.

I would say public money, money that is owed to the United States. So that would take in taxes, certainly, but also fees, say, that people pay the post office or customs that go into the public fisc. So I think the key -- the key -- the key indicia is that it's simply money that the government owns.

**Plaintiff's Lawyer:**
From taxes or fees or fines? No?

**Judge:**
Yes.

If the money -- if it's -- if it's -- if it's money that's going from private pockets into the public fisc, into the government, the government owns that money and the government can do with that money what it will.

And by contrast --

**Plaintiff's Lawyer:**

**Plaintiff's Lawyer:**
Right.

Judge:

Correct.

It -- she says it relates to 544(a) and that it really can't logically apply to 544(b) when we understand what 544(b) is doing.

Lawyer of Defendant:
