**Plaintiff: Kimberly Schn**  
She says she was in a good financial situation after she got divorced and helped out the defendant, who was like family to her. However, the defendant took advantage of her generosity. She gave multiple loans exceeding $10,000, yet the defendant repaid only $25. She is heartbroken but insists that money is money, and she is here suing for the state maximum of $5,000 in an attempt to recover at least half of her losses.

**Defendant: Tanya Adler Carabo**  
She says this is crazy and claims that the plaintiff was a generous woman who liked spending on those less fortunate. Over the years, the plaintiff did many things for her, including taking her to dinners and hosting parties at her house. They were just like family. However, once the plaintiff got married, she became jealous and angry because the defendant was no longer as available. She insists that the lawsuit is motivated by jealousy and states that $5,000 is an insane amount to be suing for because she owes nothing.

---

**Judge:** All parties, please stand. Are you ready? Please be seated and come to order. Has the defendant been sworn?

**Court Clerk:** Yes, Your Honor.

**Judge:** Thank you, Douglas. You're welcome. Kimberly Schn, you are suing Frank Cabalo and Tanya Adler Carabo. You have power of attorney, so Frank will not be here. You are suing for $5,000 for multiple loans you claim you made to them and that they refuse to repay you. What’s going on?

**Plaintiff:** I met them probably around 2011. I met Frankie first, and we were friends for a while. Then, I met Tanya, and they became a couple. We spent time together, became very close with their kids and family—we were like family. They had financial difficulties, and they would ask to borrow money now and then. Sometimes they repaid a little bit, but over the years, I was essentially paying all their bills monthly.

**Judge:** But why were you doing that?

**Plaintiff:** Because I'm an idiot. They were like family, and I genuinely believed they would pay me back.

**Judge:** How many years passed without them paying you back? Didn’t that show you they weren’t going to?

**Plaintiff:** The problem was Frankie kept in contact. He would reach out and say, "I have $25 for you here, $25 there." As recently as 2017, he made a small payment. The last time I saw him was about eight months ago when we got together with some friends. I still love the kids and the family with all my heart. This is very painful.

**Judge:** What caused the rift between you?

**Plaintiff:** Around Christmas, I had gifts for the kids, but I ended up in the ER due to health issues. They wanted me to mediate an argument between them, but I told them I was at the ER. They didn’t believe me. When I got home, I found them snooping around my backyard, looking in my house to see if I was really home.

**Judge:** They wanted you to mediate an argument between them?

**Plaintiff:** Yes.

**Judge:** And what happened when you saw them?

**Plaintiff:** They ran away. Later, they accused me of lying about going to the hospital and cut off communication with me.

**Judge:** Tanya, what do you have to say?

**Defendant:** The only truth in what she said is that she is generous and kind. I never asked her for money. Her famous line was always, "I got you, darling, no problem." The only person who asked her for money was my husband—$1,200, around 2013 or 2014. He kept in contact with her after we stopped being friends.

**Judge:** When did you stop being friends?

**Defendant:** Around 2014.

**Judge:** That long ago? She says you owe her around $10,000, but the statutory maximum is $5,000. What do you say to that?

**Defendant:** I don’t owe her anything. I never asked her for money.

**Judge:** Did you accept financial help from her?

**Defendant:** She took me and my family out to dinners, paid for trips, and invited us to events. She wanted me to be with her, to live a single lifestyle like her. But I had a husband and kids and couldn’t keep up with her.

**Judge:** Did she pay your bills?

**Defendant:** No, she did not.

**Judge:** Kimberly, you claim you paid their bills?

**Plaintiff:** Yes, I still have the truck payment coupons.

**Judge:** Whose truck was it?

**Plaintiff:** The one they both had at the time. I also paid Geico, National Grid, and other bills.

**Judge:** Under whose name were those payments?

**Plaintiff:** Under Frank’s.

**Judge:** Did you get a written agreement saying this was a loan?

**Plaintiff:** No.

**Judge:** Tanya, why is the truck payment book in her hands?

**Defendant:** We didn’t have a bank account. She said it would be easier for her to pay with her checks.

**Judge:** Kimberly, do you have bank statements showing payments?

**Plaintiff:** No, that’s my fault. I should have brought them.

**Judge:** How do you come to court claiming $10,000 in loans but provide no proof?

**Plaintiff:** I know, I should have.

**Judge:** Did the defendant acknowledge any debt?

**Plaintiff:** Yes, in text messages, they admitted owing $6,000.

**Judge:** Do you have those texts?

**Plaintiff:** Only printed copies.

**Judge:** Tanya, did your husband borrow $1,200?

**Defendant:** Yes, and he made three payments of $50 and $100.

**Judge:** So he still owes $1,000?

**Defendant:** Yes.

**Judge:** I tell everyone: If you loan a friend money, be ready never to see it again or get it in writing. In this case, I have confirmation of a $1,200 loan, of which only $200 was repaid. Based on the evidence presented, I am awarding the plaintiff $1,000. Judgment for the plaintiff.

---

**Post-Trial Interviews:**

**Reporter:** Tanya, the plaintiff claims you were ungrateful.

**Defendant:** No, I am very grateful for everything she did. She was generous and kind.

**Reporter:** Kimberly, why did you wait so long to file?

**Plaintiff:** I believed they would pay me back.

**Reporter:** Why didn’t you bring evidence?

**Plaintiff:** I should have. I didn’t expect her to lie.

**Reporter:** As you said, you were an idiot?

**Plaintiff:** Yes. I was definitely an idiot.

**Judge:** If you loan money, always get it in writing. That’s it for this case. Next case coming up.

Judge: I wanted to follow up actually on the previous question. That's where I was headed earlier too. We have an American citizen here who was for years, I don't remember exactly how long, forced to live abroad and fearful about coming home because he didn't know what he was being accused of. Now, the government may very well have had good reasons. I don't for a second mean to suggest otherwise. But an American citizen normally has a right to what's been called every man's evidence against him. That's due process. That's a pillar of our democracy. And here, the government says, no, you don't get that evidence.

Judge: But an alternative was suggested, which is at least share it with the judge. At least share the facts with the judge and maybe with cleared counsel. And you can do it in a SCIF. There are a lot of them. I imagine you spent a fair amount of time in a SCIF preparing for this case. And the government does that all the time under the Foreign Intelligence Surveillance Act, under CEPA. Why is it too much to expect that the federal government do at least that when his fundamental liberty, the right to travel, is at stake?

Defendant’s Lawyer: So, a couple of responses. First, I do need to push back on the narrative that he was somehow stranded overseas or forced to live overseas. Even someone on the No Fly List can get—

Judge: That's what he alleges.

Defendant’s Lawyer: I understand. But—

Judge: And we have to take that as true at this stage.

Defendant’s Lawyer: But I just want to make it clear for the Court that the reason he came back in 2015 is because, even though he was on the No Fly List, he had a one-time waiver. These are available to any American citizen who's overseas on the No Fly List. That's exactly how he came back. I presume the reason he didn't seek one for the years—the four years he was in Sweden—is because he had a pending Swedish asylum application. I don't know how Swedish law works, but I would imagine if he voluntarily returned, that might affect it. So I just want to resist the premise that he was stranded overseas.

Defendant’s Lawyer: He could have come back with the one-time waiver, which, when he requested it, allowed him to return. The second piece of your question—I think the judge in this case, the district court, sensibly realized that he's not on the list and he's been assured he won't be put back on the list based on the currently available—

Judge: I'm not asking about the district court judge. I'm asking about the position of the executive branch. And, again, whether he might have had a one-time waiver but was thereafter barred from travel is immaterial. It's his right to travel, his right to every man's evidence.

Judge: And when those two things are at stake, is it too much to ask the federal government to share with the district court in a SCIF enough information to be able to assess the mootness question? Perhaps even share it with cleared counsel, as it does in so many other circumstances under other statutory regimes? Why is that too much to ask the executive branch?

Defendant’s Lawyer: That is exactly what we do when these cases get to the merits. If he had remained on the No Fly List, that's what would have happened. That's what happens in the other cases. There's no question. My only point is, in the mootness context, there has been no district court that has attempted to invoke those kinds of procedures.

Defendant’s Lawyer: We haven't faced that. And the reason, I think, is that mootness, the Article III inquiry, is different from the merits inquiry. For Article III, you're trying to make a predictive judgment about expectation, and when the standard is a threat of engaging in terrorism, I think district courts have sensibly realized that that is just not reasonably expected to occur.

Judge: So, if I can summarize, I think your argument is that it's not necessary here, even though it often is in the merits?

Defendant’s Lawyer: That's correct.

Judge: Thank you.

Judge: -- in that?

Lawyer of Plaintiff: I mean, that -- that -- the predicate exception begins by talking about the action in which the manufacturer knowingly violated and --and the violation was a proximate cause, including the -- the exceptions that are mentioned.

So that -- so aiding and abetting these violations of federal and state statutes pertaining to guns is exactly what this exception was built to do.

That is why it was carved out in this bipartisan legislation.

But, on your remedies question, one of the difficulties I think for all of us is that we're here so early.

This is a complaint that has asked for a number of different remedies, including a number of different types of injunctive relief.

And one of the things that you heard the Petitioners and a lot of their amici in their briefs complain about is what these remedies might do.

That is for the district judge on remand to make sure that the judge equitably crafts a remedy that is designed to limit the harm to Mexico.

Mexico is not trying to legislate gun use in the United States.

Judge: Yeah, I understand that.

I guess I'm just wondering whether the PLCAA statute itself is telling us that we don't want the courts to be the ones to be crafting remedies that amount to regulation on this industry.

Lawyer of Plaintiff: I think --

Judge: That that was really the point of the entire thing.

And so, to the extent that we're now reading an exception to allow the very thing that the statute seems to preclude, I'm concerned about that.

Lawyer of Plaintiff: Justice, if PLCAA had wanted to preclude any lawsuit against a manufacturer, including for instances where the manufacturer had committed a wrong, it could have done that, as so many different states, in fact, did.

What PLCAA did in this effort, which, as I mentioned, was joined by members of both parties, was to carefully carve out circumstances where the manufacturer or the seller was -- was alleged to have done something wrong.

The thing they were concerned about was lawful design and manufacture and sale of product and injury solely caused by others. That is replete throughout the purpose section of PLCAA.

PLCAA could have been designed quite differently.

It was designed this way for a reason, so that harmful actions by manufacturers and sellers breaking the law could continue to be remedied.

That was exactly the point.

Judge: You've mentioned about four times that it was bipartisan. What's the relevance of that to this -- to our interpretation here?

Lawyer of Plaintiff: I -- I think I -- I -- yes.

I was --I haven't gotten to -- to 13 times yet, but four will do.

The fact that it's bipartisan, I think, points out particularly in this climate that the --what was being challenged there were really unusual lawsuits that really weren't showing up in any other part of the -- the economy against the manufacturers of a lawful product, selling their product lawfully, distributing their product lawfully, where no allegations were made in those prior lawsuits about unlawful behavior.

That was what PLCAA carefully carved out.

What it left, among other things, were actions for things like negligent entrustment, product liability.

And product liability is interesting, by the way, because you probably notice this as well, product liability specifically says you can sue for product liability, but, if it was a criminal act, then that act becomes the sole proximate cause.

That, of course, is very different from the violation of which is the --or a proximate cause, which is what you see in the -Subject to Final Review predicate exception.

I think that language difference is very important.

Judge: Thank you, counsel.

I'd like to ask you pretty much the flip side of the question I asked your -- your friend on the -- the other side, which you allege that 2 percent of the guns manufactured in the United States make their way to Mexico.

I assume the volume of that is critical to your -- your argument, and I just want to know how much is enough, if it's 1 percent or a more miniscule amount.

Where -- where's the floor?

Lawyer of Plaintiff: So 2 -- 2 percent is always a question that begs the question of what.

And, here, what you have is data in the complaint that says -- this is paragraph 438 -- between 342,000 and 597,000 of defendants' guns --and, remember, this is not the entire industry we're here talking about -- so up to 600,000 of defendants' guns are likely trafficked into Mexico every year.

That's your 2 percent.

But I think the issue is not so much whether it's 2 or 10 or 70.

It's do these manufacturers know who the rogue dealers are and what they're doing.

And this complaint in all of those paragraphs that I read to you earlier and many others around them specifically says the -- these manufacturers know the trace data that show the dealers, that show the bulk sales that are being made to traffickers who come in repeatedly over a short period of time and bring the guns into Mexico, where they're found at crime scenes.

That, I think, more than percentage is important.

Judge: The -- you -- you emphasize -- you have a number of criteria or examples, you know, the -- the gun says this or it looks like a military weapon and it has an American flag, and, you know, I --Zapata's quote about better to die on your feet than live on your knees.

I mean, those are all things that are not illegal in any way.

And the idea -- I mean, there are some people who want the experience of shooting a particular type of gun because they find it more enjoyable than using a -- a BB gun.

And I just wonder exactly what the -- the defendant, the manufacturer, is supposed to --to do in that situation.

You say no, he shouldn't be marketing a particular legal firearm because they're going to go into Mexico at a higher percentage than -- than others?

Lawyer of Plaintiff: Mr. Chief Justice, I think it's not so much that the defendants are designing a particular gun.

It's that what the complaint alleges is that they are designing certain guns to target the Mexican market, including the cartels.

So, if you take the example that you gave, this is paragraph 215, Colt produces three models of guns that it specifically targets to the Mexican market: the Super El Jefe, the Super El Grito, and the Emiliano Zapata 1911. These are coveted by the cartels.

And you can see evidence of this at paragraphs 217, 218, 219, 220.

And they are smuggled into Mexico in volume, which you can also find.

Judge: Do -- do you know what the percentage of those guns that are sold in the United States compared to the ones that are found in Mexico is?

Lawyer of Plaintiff: I don't know, but, again, the percentage, I think, is less important than the allegation that they are smuggled into Mexico in volume and coveted by the cartels, including being found on the person of many cartel chiefs who have been arrested.

Plaintiff’s Lawyer: Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court: Over eight years ago, the government violated my client’s right to procedural due process by denying her husband's visa without providing a reason why. My client has a constitutional liberty interest in living with her husband.

We do not claim that this interest gives her the right to immigrate her husband regardless of his inadmissibility, but the importance of cohabitation to marriage means that the government is required to provide procedural protections when it burdens the underlying right.

The statute also informs what process is required. There’s nothing unusual about this. When government action burdens a substantive right, the Court often relies on procedural protections to balance the implications of that burden against the government's countervailing interest.

The statute likewise here shows why my client was herself deprived of that liberty interest and undermines the government’s argument that she was a mere bystander.

Under the immigration law, the citizen petitioner must initiate and advance her spouse’s application from start to finish, and the very purpose of the consular process for spouses is to benefit the American citizen spouse. Given this interest, judicial review is required.

This Court has repeatedly acknowledged that the constitutional rights of U.S. citizens can be implicated by visa denials, and when they are, that review is necessary.

Procedural due process, therefore, requires notice sufficient to allow a meaningful opportunity to respond. A mere citation to a broad statute that encompasses any other unlawful activity forced my client to guess at the reason for the denial and provided only a mere gesture at due process, which this Court said was insufficient in a prior case.

The information my client ultimately learned came after the one-year deadline to respond had passed and after the government had informed them that its inadmissibility finding was permanent.

We now ask that my client be given the chance to respond to that information now.

Judge: We will hear argument this morning in Case 23-867, Hungary versus Simon.

Lawyer of Defendant: Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court: Hungary and its national railway, MAV, are immune from suit under the Foreign Sovereign Immunities Act unless the expropriation exception applies, and a key phrase in that exception is "exchanged for." There's no dispute that "to exchange" means to give one thing in return for another.

Accordingly, domestic courts have jurisdiction over this case only if some present-day asset having a commercial nexus with the United States was given in return for items taken from 14 individuals in 1944.

Respondents have not even attempted to make that showing.

Instead, they rest their case on the theory that all fungible assets of Hungary, its agencies, and its instrumentalities were given in return for those specific items taken more than six decades before this case was filed.

That's simply inconsistent with ordinary meaning.

And while this case can be decided on text alone, history and context confirm the limited scope of the expropriation exception. It arose from congressional opposition to a particular decision of this Court, the Sabbatino decision, which concerned identifiable and traceable property.

When Congress enacted the FSIA, it intended to codify the restrictive view of foreign sovereign immunity, not to work a radical transformation of international law.

But the commingling theory would do just that.

It would undermine important limits in other provisions of the statute and would require U.S. courts to decide claims having no real connection to this country.

The D.C. Circuit substantively erred in adopting the commingling theory, and it committed two procedural errors.

It imposed a burden of production on sovereign defendants rather than the proponents of jurisdiction, and it declined to ask whether Respondents' allegations made out a valid claim to jurisdiction.

The D.C. Circuit's opinion should be reversed, and this case should be dismissed.

I welcome the Court's questions.

Judge: What was -- and -- and under your theory, what would Respondent have to show in order to make out jurisdiction using -- employing the commingling theory?

Lawyer of Defendant: I think there are several types of evidence that a plaintiff could use to establish an exchange even after commingling.

So, first, there's the type of mathematical evidence, the most obvious example being a withdrawal from a commingled account that could not have occurred but for a deposit of tainted funds given the prior balance.

Second, you can imagine direct evidence, instructions to an accountant to run illegal proceeds through the books of a company before depositing them into a personal account.

And, third, you can imagine indirect evidence.

You might have an atypical deposit of a specific amount into an account, followed shortly thereafter by a withdrawal in the same amount.

Under those circumstances, a fact finder might be able to conclude that an exchange has occurred using ordinary meaning.

But simply showing that funds entered into the general revenues of an entire nation that, you know, contain billions of dollars, followed by untold numbers of transactions following that deposit, simply isn't consistent with the plain text.

Judge: What if there's -- the country has an account that is 95 percent composed of funds from appropriated property, 5 percent isn't? Is your argument still the same? And -- and the entity in the United States spends less than 5 percent of the proceedings.

Lawyer of Defendant: I think there are close questions, but, in -- in those circumstances, maybe a fact finder could say it's at least as likely as not that tainted funds are involved.

And the details would matter, temporal proximity, as I said, whether there's either direct or indirect evidence, but, potentially, those types of claims could qualify, yes.

Judge: So it doesn't matter -- commingling doesn't mean that it's some taint from property that is -- is -- funds that are not from appropriation? That can't be the entire rule?

Lawyer of Defendant: Right.

We're not suggesting that commingling is fatal.

In the vast majority of cases, commingling will make it impossible to trace funds.

But the ultimate question is -- is simply whether, in ordinary meaning, somebody would refer to the subsequent withdrawal as being exchanged for the initial deposit.

Judge: And doesn't this provide a roadmap to any country that wants to expropriate property? In other words, just sell the property, put it into your national treasury, insulate yourself from all claims for all time?

Lawyer of Defendant: I think this Court was clear in Altmann that the FSIA was not intended to direct or incentivize other nations in the ordering of their affairs.

It's not a substantive --

Judge: Well, I was just sort of suggesting that Congress wouldn't have wanted to write a provision that has no meaning.

And, under your theory, I think that there would be precious little meaning to this because it really just, you know, gives foreign countries an easy way to expropriate property and make sure there's no accountability for that expropriation.

Lawyer of Defendant: I'd make three points in response.

First, the FSIA doesn't make expropriation unlawful.

Other forums may be available where an unlawful taking could be litigated or perhaps settled through international espousal.

Second, Congress knew that these types of claims would be rare.

It was informed when it passed the second Hickenlooper amendment that it would apply to a tiny fraction of expropriation claims around the world.

The Congress was attempting to overrule Sabbatino, not to establish a broad new type of claim that would work, again, a radical transformation of international law.

And, third, regardless of what this Court decides in terms of the commingling theory, foreign nations can avoid U.S. courts regardless.

A foreign nation could expropriate property, segregate it, and keep those proceeds out of the United States and thereby avoid the commingling theory even if it were adopted.

**Judge:** Okay, Bilal Arif, you are suing Christopher for a $1,000 loan that you say you made to him and he hasn’t repaid. You have counterclaimed against him for $2,700 in unpaid wages. Let me talk to you first. You guys worked together at a cell phone retail store?

**Plaintiff:** That is correct.

**Judge:** Were you the owner of that store?

**Plaintiff:** No.

**Judge:** What was your role there?

**Plaintiff:** I was just a manager.

**Judge:** And what was his role there?

**Plaintiff:** He was a sales associate.

**Judge:** Okay, so you were over him?

**Plaintiff:** Yes.

**Judge:** And how long did you work together there?

**Plaintiff:** About a year or so.

**Judge:** Okay. And were you friends?

**Plaintiff:** Yeah, we were pretty good friends.

**Judge:** Would you socialize outside the store?

**Plaintiff:** We met like two to three times.

**Judge:** All right. Now, at some point, you loaned him money?

**Plaintiff:** Yes.

**Judge:** How much money did you loan him?

**Plaintiff:** $1,500.

**Judge:** Did he pay you back any of it?

**Plaintiff:** Yes, Your Honor. The last week that he was working with me and the new management, he told me to keep whatever I worked for and let him know how much he owed me after that. So it was like $406, and then he gave me $100 afterward. That’s how he paid off $500 of the $1,500, and then according to you, he still owes you $1,000?

**Plaintiff:** Yes.

**Judge:** Okay. What’s going on?

**Defendant:** So basically, it was not a loan. He says that he was just an employee—he wasn’t just an employee. He was invested 50% owner with the first boss we were working for.

**Judge:** Is that accurate?

**Plaintiff:** Yes, Your Honor, it is.

**Defendant:** Then when those two partners split, he took his investment from the first boss and went with the second boss. So basically, the money he gave me was an incentive to come work with him.

**Judge:** So according to you, the $1,500 was an incentive for you to come work with him?

**Defendant:** Correct.

**Judge:** Did you let him take $500 for repayment of something that was supposed to be an incentive?

**Defendant:** He took it off himself. I never received any pay from the time that I worked for them. They kept giving me the runaround.

**Judge:** When you say ‘they,’ you mean him and the new owner?

**Defendant:** Correct. They kept telling me they were going to have a meeting later, that I could come pick up my pay at the meeting, but then the meeting ended up not happening.

**Judge:** How many days did you work at that new place?

**Defendant:** About three weeks.

**Judge:** And then what did you do?

**Defendant:** I went back to the old place because I didn’t have any income.

**Judge:** Did you guys not pay him at all?

**Plaintiff:** No, Your Honor. He was paid.

**Judge:** Do you have proof that you paid him?

**Plaintiff:** Yes, Your Honor. I have text messages.

**Judge:** That’s not proof that you paid him. Do you have a pay stub?

**Plaintiff:** No, he got paid in cash because the owner didn’t have things set up properly at that time.

**Judge:** If you’re not doing things right, don’t open a business. But in the text messages, can I see any proof of payment?

**Defendant:** Those texts just show him saying things like, “That was two days’ pay, right?”

**Judge:** There are a lot of texts here. I see you telling him you need money, saying, ‘I’ll pay you back Friday.’ What did you mean?

**Defendant:** That was for phones we purchased together and were selling.

**Judge:** Why are you saying you’ll bring him money if you don’t owe him money?

**Defendant:** Some of those texts refer to other deals we had.

**Judge:** Why do you keep promising to pay $500 one week and $500 the next?

**Defendant:** That was supposed to be for phones.

**Judge:** Did you ever pay it?

**Defendant:** No.

**Judge:** Then you owe him $1,000.

Your counterclaim for $2,700 for unpaid wages—if you have unpaid wages, you have to sue the corporation, not him directly. You have to sue the corporation. So I am dismissing your counterclaim without prejudice for you to sue the corporation.

On his claim against you—pay him the $1,000.

**Judge:** Christopher, you’ve got to pay him $1,000.

**Defendant:** Yeah. Next time, I know better about who I deal with.

**Judge:** Mr. Arif, are you happy?

**Plaintiff:** Yeah, definitely.

**Judge:** You’re not working with him still, right?

**Plaintiff:** No, it’s done.

**Judge:** Okay, good. Thank you. Case closed.

Judge: I have a similar question to the previous one. So—and I don't think it'll require a long answer. Do you agree—so, you know, in the concurrence in the Sixth Circuit, it was stated: Well, listen, Mathews doesn't apply if there is a defined historical practice on point. Do you agree with that, or do you think Mathews would always apply? I understand you think that the history isn't determinative here. And it seems to me like that's kind of the distinction between the different briefs on the history. But, just methodologically, do you agree with this reading of Mathews and precedent about history and procedure?

Plaintiff's Lawyer: I think that if there is a precise answer to the question in the history, then the history would probably govern, but I think we're very far from that.

Judge: Okay.

Plaintiff's Lawyer: Very far from that in this case.

Judge: Thank you.

Judge: And is it your position that the application of Mathews would necessarily always mean that some additional procedure would be required in a situation like this?

Plaintiff's Lawyer: No. If the application of Mathews could lead to a conclusion that the state's existing process is sufficient, then no additional process is required.

Judge: Okay. And, you know, I was surprised a little bit, I think, about your answer regarding Von Neumann and why, if at all, remission is different. I had thought that it was not because it was discretionary. I thought it was because the case seems to make clear that it is an administrative alternative to judicial forfeiture under the statute at issue in that case.

And so, given that—the owner was complaining about it not happening quickly enough. He chose the option of remission rather than forfeiture, and he wanted the remission hearing to happen quickly. And the court, I thought, in the language that has been quoted, was making a much more narrow point, which is just that remission is not a part of the forfeiture hearing. It's not required by due process. And so, therefore, you don't have a claim that it has to be faster under the Constitution, that, really, forfeiture is where due process lies in terms of your constitutional rights.

But, to the extent that this language is talking about forfeiture being the only thing, I thought it was relative to the alternative administrative remission process. Am I misreading this?

Plaintiff's Lawyer: No. And I don't think we're disagreeing. I would simply add the point that this alternative administrative process didn't create any substantive rights because it was discretionary whether the government would give you back your property or not.

Judge: True. But it's also the case, I think from this case, that the court then goes on to talk about how remission is not a part of forfeiture, how those are two different things. And so the language, I thought, was just distinguishing forfeiture from remission as opposed to telling us something about the nature of forfeiture, that it's only the forfeiture hearing and you don't get other steps or whatever, which is the way it's being read, I think, by your counterparts on the other side.

Plaintiff's Lawyer: I—I agree. I think that's—that's also a relevant distinction of Von Neumann here.

Judge: All right. Let me finally just ask you about, again, the timing and whether—you know, I think there is something to this notion that Barker is about timing, but, again, the question remains, isn't the claimant in this case making a timing kind of argument?

And I guess I see that, but can you help me with the following hypothetical and then maybe I'll also get the reaction on the other side?

So, if we have a scenario in which everyone agrees that the average time for a forfeiture proceeding is, say, six months after the seizure, and everybody agrees that that is reasonable for due process purposes, Plaintiff Number 1 doesn't receive her forfeiture hearing until 12 months after the seizure, so her claim is that the government was too slow in giving her the hearing.

Meanwhile, Plaintiff Number 2 receives her hearing in six months, but she claims that at some point within those six months there should be an opportunity for the court to consider whether she should have been allowed to keep her car during that interim period.

She's not complaining about the time. Six months is fine. She got her hearing within the six months. But what she's saying is, while you figure out during that six months who owns this car, I should keep custody of it during that period, and I think we should have that adjudicated separately from the forfeiture hearing.

Are those two different things, or are they both really about timing?

Plaintiff's Lawyer: No, I think those are two different claims. The first one is claiming I didn't have a fast enough final merits determination. The other one is claiming: Look, the final merits determination was fast enough under the circumstances, civil litigation takes time, but I shouldn't be deprived of my property during the pendency of that.

And, again, it's like the criminal analogy that I used earlier. You wouldn't say that because you have a speedy trial right and that's going to take a year that you don't also have a right to a prompt probable cause determination.

And in a situation where, hypothetically, the trial did happen within a matter of days, that might moot the probable cause hearing or probable cause determination, but it wouldn't mean that conceptually you don't have two separate rights.

Judge: And you could have two separate tests—

Plaintiff's Lawyer: Correct.

Judge: —depending upon the claims?

Plaintiff's Lawyer: Correct, with the Barker test—

Judge: Right.

Plaintiff's Lawyer: Correct.

Judge: Thank you.

Judge: Thank you, counsel.

Defendant's Lawyer: Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court:

Forfeiture has been a critical tool for deterring crime since before the framing, and both history and precedent show what post-seizure process is due to those whose property has been seized.

From historical legal precedents, the answer is clear. If the forfeiture proceeding is instituted and concluded promptly, then the forfeiture proceeding without more provides the post-seizure hearing required by due process.

Now Petitioners assert that another post-seizure hearing is required, a mini-trial mere days or weeks after seizure, and in their telling, the federal government and the states have been violating fundamental rights for centuries with no one noticing until just a few years ago.

But their view cannot be squared with history or precedent, and their own cases show why a timely forfeiture proceeding is the meaningful opportunity to be heard at a meaningful time.

Only the timeliest test embodied in precedent accounts for the striking diversity among forfeiture cases. Some will be simple and others will involve wide-ranging investigations. Some claimants will vigorously press their rights and others will default.

As long as claimants can appear before judges promptly, then judges can strike that proper balance in each fact-bound case.

Now I've heard my friend today say essentially don't trust judges to be judges. And so, instead, they invoke Mathews to have federal courts act as legislatures in handing down new Rules of Civil Procedure for all 50 states and the federal government.

But precedent best accounts for what should be the dispositive fact in these cases. The Petitioners were before state courts within two weeks of seizure, yet they ignored that process for over a year. Petitioners received all the process they were due, and this Court therefore should affirm.

Judge: But you criticize the use of Mathews, but precedent applied here is a speedy trial case, so that would seem to also be an ill fit for determining whether or not you should have an additional hearing.

Defendant's Lawyer: I don't think so, Your Honor. The Court considered multiple ways to measure speed, and the factors in that case are exactly the sorts of factors you imagine any judge would look to. How long has this taken? Why has it taken so long? Is the claimant pushing their rights? And has there been any prejudice?

Judge: It seems that Petitioner is not talking as much about a timing issue as whether or not there should be an additional right vindicated.

Defendant's Lawyer: That's right, Your Honor. But I think one advantage of precedent is that it really draws from history.

Judge: General, could I take you back to the line of questioning that you and the other judge were talking about? In your reply brief, on page 18, you discuss a standing appropriation of up to a quadrillion dollars for the President to fund as he deems fit the entire federal government, besides the Army. So just on this question of where your outer limits are and what theory we might use to say there are some limits, what do you want to say about that sort of statute? Would that be a constitutional violation? If so, why so? And why is that different from this?

Defendant's Lawyer: Yes. So that statute, as we understand it, would be completely unprecedented. It would effectively take the whole of Congress's appropriations authority and transfer it to the executive branch. Congress has never done that for the past 230-plus years, and it's hard for me to imagine Congress ever would do that.

But I think, if the Court were confronted with that issue in a future case, it could well recognize limits, and the limits would come from history. The Court, in any number of separation of powers cases, has said that the fact that a method of structuring government has no historical precedent can strongly counsel against recognizing it as a constitutional way to proceed.

That was the analysis the Court used in a previous case. It repeated it in another ruling. But that history works both ways. The Court has likewise said that the fact that a way of structuring government is well rooted in history and traces back to the founding is powerful evidence of the contemporaneous understanding of original intent, and that's the box we're in here.

So I guess I would urge the Court to save for another day whether there's some theoretical possibility that Congress could go too far with a kind of appropriation we've never seen before and instead focus on this particular appropriations law, which we think finds its roots both in text and history.

Judge: Well, I have the same question, basically, that the other judge just asked you.

Your response in your reply brief was an answer to an argument that was made by the plaintiff, where they say:

"If the Bureau is correct that there is no constitutional limit on Congress's power to pass laws providing funding to agencies, then a single Congress could allocate each year forever up to a trillion dollars to an agency like the FBI or FTC or even up to a quadrillion dollars for the President to fund as he deems fit the entire federal government besides the Army."

Now, you answered the latter part of that in your reply brief about the quadrillion dollars, and you just answered that in response to the other judge. You didn't answer the first part of that about providing a very substantial sum of money to an agency like the FBI or the FTC.

So I just want to understand what you think the limiting principle is. Let's take the FTC, which I think had a budget of $430 million.

So let's say there's a law that allocates forever up to $1 billion, adjusted for inflation, to the FTC to use as the FTC sees fit. Would that be consistent with the Appropriations Clause?

Defendant's Lawyer: So I think, at the outset, if the law said however the FTC deems fit, it's not clear that would count as an appropriation because it's not clear Congress would have specified the purpose.

But I recognize you can tweak it and say, you know, to carry out the FTC's functions. In that context, I think the hypothetical would effectively be a standing uncapped appropriation because, of course, the FTC would never reach that amount. It would be for a single agency.

And we think that that is well grounded in history and, in fact, is how many agencies are funded today, particularly in the financial regulatory space. But, if you have concerns about that principle, here, of course, we have the statutory cap.

And the plaintiff says, you know, the cap is illusory, that it's more like the hypotheticals we've just been touching on. But I don't see how they can tenably make that argument when the cap is set at $600 million, adjusted only for inflation, and many of the agencies from which the CFPB inherited its responsibilities have far larger budgets—$1.8 billion for one agency, around a billion for another, and over a billion for another financial regulator.

This is a meaningful restraint, and I think it just demonstrates that if the Court thinks it's important to have that constraint here, the CFPB is even more under Congress's control than these other agencies.

If this weren’t here, you’d be out a thousand bucks. But this is here, and this is a new contract that supersedes the agreement to rent the place. Judge: Justice Alito asked questions of your friends on the other side about—and Justice Jackson too—about what kind of proof would be used to prove this up to a jury. Do you have anything to say about that?

Defendant's Lawyer: So I think, if it's going before a jury, subject to the rules of evidence, which, admittedly, I'm not an expert on, I think anything that's admissible and relevant I would think would be able to be used to prove this question to a jury.

Judge: But it would make it harder since they don't apply to a judge, and a judge has to find these things by a preponderance if you're right?

Defendant's Lawyer: I think that it probably—I'm sure the standard would make it harder for them to prove these issues. Again, I don't think it—I agree with the government that I don't think this is going to matter in all that many cases because I think most of these cases are going to be pretty clear that the crimes were on separate occasions.

This case, for example, I think it's clear beyond a doubt, as the government said, that these crimes occurred multiple days apart. They were on separate occasions. And I think most juries—I guess the one point I would make is there is a potential nullification risk, I think, in some of these cases because of the severe mandatory minimums at issue.

And I think the Petitioner cited one case in his cert petition where a Georgia jury refused to find different occasions despite the convictions being—or the offenses being—months and years apart.

So I do think that might happen in some cases, but for the most part, I think these are going to be pretty straightforward.

Judge: Thank you.

Judge: Thank you, counsel. Justice Thomas, anything further?

Judge: There is a lot of debate on whether historically jury nullification was an okay thing.

Defendant's Lawyer: That's right, Your Honor. I don't want to wade into that debate.

Judge: No, I'm not suggesting we do. But it is an open question.

Judge: Anything further? No? Thank you, counsel. Rebuttal, Mr. Fisher.

Plaintiff's Lawyer: Thank you. I'd like to cover two topics.

First, a couple more words about bifurcation. There was some talk about state practices. Even in 1967, when the Court looked at this issue in Spencer, it noted that the majority of the states require bifurcation by statute.

It's not even a prosecutorial discretion, discretionary decision. And I think that trend has continued for all the common-sense reasons laid out in the briefs you have.

So, Justice, it's not even a constitutional question necessarily. It's just already been decided by the states.

If you had—you know, this is a federal case where you have your own supervisory powers and you can, I think, you know, make whatever suggestions you like in the opinion, and we think it would be appropriate for the Court to say that bifurcation is the accepted solution here that seems to be the right one.

I'd also like to say a couple words about the harmless error conversation that has taken place today.

We haven't briefed that issue precisely because the Court's common practice—and overwhelming practice—is to leave decisions like that, that were not addressed by the lower courts, for the lower courts to decide in the first instance.

And that's what we'd ask for the Court to do here. And forgive me, I may have even misunderstood the way the amicus appointment works in this case.

You know, of course, we are not in line with the government on harmless error in this case, but the government's top-side brief said the case should be remanded for harmless error.

And so, on the issue on which we are adverse to the government, you know, I don't know that amicus can come in and tell this Court to go ahead and address it.

But leaving that perhaps thorny issue of the Court's practice aside, in all events, we think the safest thing is to leave that for remand.

But I'll just add a couple of things about the factual conversation that took place today.

Remember, when you ask whether these crimes that are alleged to be committed on an eight-day stretch, three different crimes on an eight-day stretch, could possibly be the same occasion, you are yourselves relying on these kinds of documents that you have noted in Mathis and Descamps are highly unreliable.

And, in fact, these documents themselves, the plea documents themselves here say that Mr. Erlinger agrees to cooperate against all of his co-defendants.

There were no co-defendants in these cases.

And so, Justice, you noted that an indictment might say "on or about" certain days. And when you get into an eight-day stretch, "on or about" matters quite a lot.

So what we would say on remand in part—and this goes to Justice Gorsuch's questions about how harmless error would work here—is that the government may not have been able to prove beyond a reasonable doubt to a jury that these crimes were committed on separate occasions, and that's enough to allow a retrial or just at least renegotiations on that point.

If the Court has no further questions, I'll submit.

Judge: Thank you, counsel.

Judge: Mr. Harper, this Court appointed you to brief and argue this case as an amicus curiae in support of the judgment below.

You have ably discharged that responsibility, for which we are grateful.

The case is submitted.

Judge: Well, I wanted to ask you some of the same questions I asked earlier about how these cases will be tried if your view of the law prevails. So I asked about the admissibility of the judgment of conviction, the charging document, the jury instructions, and the plea colloquy. Do you think all of that is admissible?

Plaintiff's Lawyer: Yes, Your Honor. I mean, in addition to the hearsay exceptions that might cover those, I think, to the extent you're submitting documents that were just shown to the jury and are being used for the purpose of showing what the jury was instructed, as opposed to for the truth of the matter asserted, there isn’t a hearsay problem with those.

Judge: Now suppose the rule is that judges have usually regarded things that are separated by more than a day as having occurred on separate occasions. Suppose the documents don’t clearly specify the exact day on which the offense occurred—one says "on or about March 27th," and another says "on or about March 30th." Is that sufficient to prove beyond a reasonable doubt that they occurred on separate occasions? If not, would you need to call witnesses from those prior trials to establish the exact day?

Plaintiff's Lawyer: Well, Your Honor, I do think that would be enough for a jury to infer beyond a reasonable doubt that they occurred on different occasions, particularly if other aspects of the crimes are different.

Judge: Were judges making that determination beyond a reasonable doubt under the beyond-a-reasonable-doubt standard?

Plaintiff's Lawyer: Yes, Your Honor. I think a jury could infer that, for example, a robbery on March 28th and an assault on March 30th would be separate occasions, particularly if there is no contrary argument connecting them.

Judge: Now, regarding differences in the nature of the offenses—if the offenses are sufficiently different, that may support the conclusion that they were not committed on the same occasion. Suppose a defendant committed a robbery in the morning by grabbing a woman’s purse and running away, then committed a mugging with a knife in the evening, and the next morning stole $500 worth of merchandise from a store. Would these be considered separate occasions?

Plaintiff's Lawyer: Yes, I think they would be.

Judge: On what basis? What would the judge instruct the jury?

Plaintiff's Lawyer: Your Honor, I take separate occasions to mean that Congress intended to address cases where multiple offenses arise from a single act. But when you have three different kinds of offenses, even over a short period, as you described, I think that’s presumptively separate occasions. Of course, the jury would still need to evaluate the evidence, and the defendant could argue otherwise, but I would expect a jury to find them to be separate occasions unless substantial contrary evidence is presented.

Judge: All right, thank you.

Judge: I heard you mention that there have been four trials of this kind. What did those trials look like? Were they bifurcated?

Plaintiff's Lawyer: Yes, Your Honor.

Judge: Do you always expect to bifurcate?

Plaintiff's Lawyer: Unless there’s some unforeseen reason not to, we generally agree to bifurcation. However, in many cases, the defendant may choose to plead or enter into a stipulation instead.

Judge: And what did those trials look like?

Plaintiff's Lawyer: They looked like normal bifurcated proceedings. We proved the primary offense first, and then there was a separate jury consideration of the sentencing enhancement. We introduced evidence about the prior crimes, presented arguments, and the jury deliberated on those questions separately.

Judge: There has been some discussion about how difficult this process will be. Was it difficult in practice?

Plaintiff's Lawyer: Well, Your Honor, this was not our first-choice position. We have argued against this view for a long time. However, while this process imposes some burden on us, we believe it is manageable. ACCA cases make up less than 1 percent of the federal criminal docket, and with the availability of pleas, stipulations, and bifurcations, we are reasonably confident that we can handle this process.

Judge: We'll hear argument this morning in Case 22-585, Culley versus Marshall. Counsel, you may proceed.

Plaintiff's Lawyer: Thank you, Your Honor, and may it please the Court. The question presented is narrow: Should courts apply Mathews or Barker to assess the sufficiency of process in civil forfeiture proceedings? The answer is Mathews.

Mathews is the default due process standard for civil cases and for good reason. It assesses both the private and governmental interests to guard against unreasonable risks of error. The Court has consistently applied it to determine whether more process is due, including in Good, another civil forfeiture case.

The opposing side prefers Barker because Barker's answer is always no additional process. But their primary argument is just that $8,850 and Von Neumann already decided the question, not that Barker makes sense and Mathews doesn't. They are wrong. As the Second and Sixth Circuits have explained in adopting Mathews over Barker, $8,850 and Von Neumann concern the length of time for a final disposition rather than the need for an interim hearing.

Only Mathews can answer the sufficiency of process question. The courts of appeals and state supreme courts that have addressed the question have overwhelmingly chosen Mathews over Barker. The point of Mathews is to ensure that laws adequately protect the Constitution's fundamental due process guarantee, taking into account the private and governmental interests at stake. It's not to micromanage state legislatures.

The easiest way for a jurisdiction to ensure its laws comport with due process, as the Second and Sixth Circuits have explained, is generally to offer a reasonably prompt post-seizure hearing to allow claimants to raise an innocent owner argument. Numerous states have done just that, and their experience makes clear that retention hearings are workable and effective. I welcome the Court's questions.

Judge: Before we get to the choice between Barker and Mathews, isn’t there an antecedent question as to whether or not there’s any constitutional requirement for additional hearings in the context of forfeiture?

Plaintiff's Lawyer: Your Honor, I think that question is what Barker or Mathews, depending on which test this Court would choose, is meant to answer.

Judge: The reason I ask is because you seem to assume that an additional hearing is required.

Plaintiff's Lawyer: We’re not assuming that an additional hearing is required. We’re saying that Mathews is the way to analyze whether an additional hearing is required. Mathews is the test that the Court has applied in cases like Good, where a litigant comes forward and says the process being provided—in that case, no hearing—is insufficient.

Judge: If you had filed a motion for summary judgment a week after the property had been taken or the forfeiture proceedings began, would you be here?

Plaintiff's Lawyer: I think we would still be here.

Judge: Why? You would have your property back because you won on summary judgment, right?

Plaintiff's Lawyer: We won on summary judgment after going through discovery with the state, which, by the way, took five months to respond to our discovery requests.

Judge: But if you got your property back, what would be the due process problem?

Plaintiff's Lawyer: If we had promptly gotten our property back within days or weeks rather than months or years, then in that situation, I think we probably would not have a constitutional claim.

Judge: So, under Alabama’s procedures, you could have gotten your property back in a reasonable time?

Plaintiff's Lawyer: Hypothetically, we could have. But due process doesn’t depend on whether a court is going to exercise its discretion to expedite a case. Realistically, courts rarely do that. And, moreover, summary judgment is about proving your ultimate entitlement on the merits definitively.

Judge: Do we have any evidence about how long or how often courts in Alabama grant motions to expedite in this context?

Plaintiff's Lawyer: Your Honor, there is not a record on that. As a practical matter, it is not very common. However, in terms of applying the Mathews factors, that is something that could be considered and developed on remand to assess the value of additional procedures. The key methodological question here is how we determine whether an additional hearing is required—do we apply the Mathews factors, the traditional test for due process in civil cases, or do we take a different approach?

Judge: Can we go back to your answer? The purpose of summary judgment is to decide the ultimate question—who owns the car, correct?

Plaintiff's Lawyer: Yes.

Judge: The purpose of a post-seizure hearing is to determine who keeps custody of the car, correct?

Plaintiff's Lawyer: Yes.

Judge: And the focus is different? The hearing may involve disputed issues of fact regarding ownership, the government might claim it needs discovery, or it may argue it has evidence requiring further investigation. The court would then weigh whether those factors justify withholding the car from the owner pending a final determination, correct?

Plaintiff's Lawyer: Yes, Your Honor.

Judge: Juliet, you're suing Latoya for $1,424.29 in damages that she did to your brand spanking new Subaru. What happened?

Plaintiff: On January 9th, we were going to make a right turn onto the expressway. There's no light, but you have to wait because of the oncoming traffic.

Judge: Okay.

Plaintiff: So we're just waiting. My husband is there talking, my daughter is in the back seat, and we felt a hit. And of course, it wasn’t like a major thing. I looked in the rearview mirror, and she looked up. I don’t know what she was doing, but she looked up. I was very upset—the car was just a little over a week old. I came out and said, “You just hit my car.”

Judge: Are you telling me that you know where her eyes were before she hit you?

Plaintiff: No, I don't know where her eyes were, but when I looked in the rearview mirror, she looked up. I don’t know what she was doing.

Judge: But you don't know how the accident happened? You just know that you got hit from behind?

Plaintiff: Yes.

Judge: What happened?

Defendant: So we were coming out of the side street, and there were vehicles—a truck and a bus—parked along the service road, so you couldn't really see until you merged out.

Judge: Yeah, you have to roll out a little bit.

Defendant: Right. So she rolled out, and I was assuming she was going to keep going.

Judge: Well, that's why you can't be riding on someone's behind like that. You're supposed to be far enough back that if you make that mistake, you have time to stop without hitting them, right?

Defendant: Right.

Judge: Okay, so now go ahead.

Plaintiff: I was upset. I came out and said, “You just hit my car.”

Judge: Do you think she did that on purpose?

Plaintiff: Well, no, I don’t think so, but it was an accident.

Judge: I understand that. But what was her response?

Plaintiff: She said, “Don’t scream at me, don’t scream. Let’s move the car.” But I wanted to take pictures before moving the car.

Judge: I don’t care. I’m telling you right now, you never move the cars until you’ve taken pictures and a video. I don’t care if the law says you have to move the cars—you will in 10 seconds. You will do it after you take pictures and video because what’s going to happen is that somebody’s going to testify later that “Nah, it didn’t happen that way.” I’m not saying you will, I’m just saying that’s what you do when you’re in an accident.

Plaintiff: So, we came out, and it started getting heated because she wanted me to move the car, and I didn’t want to. I wanted to take pictures. I was fumbling with my phone, trying to call 911, but I didn’t realize it was still hooked up to the Bluetooth in my car, so I couldn’t hear them, and they couldn’t hear me.

Judge: I just don’t understand why it has to be so heated.

Plaintiff: Well, I was upset.

Judge: I didn’t say I don’t understand why you’re upset. You have a brand-new car that got hit—I understand that you are upset. Have you ever had an accident?

Plaintiff: Yes, once before, with a sanitation truck.

Judge: Have you ever had an accident that was your fault?

Plaintiff: No.

Judge: Have you ever done anything wrong in your life?

Plaintiff: Okay...

Judge: I just don’t understand how something that is an accident has to end up being so heated and unpleasant. But go on.

Plaintiff: Well...

Judge: Do you have the video of the heated argument?

Plaintiff: I did, but I didn’t want to bring it.

Judge: Of course not. It makes you look bad.

Plaintiff: No, it doesn’t make me look bad. I wasn’t even on the video.

Judge: That’s why I want to see it!

Plaintiff: The video was more of her...

Judge: Why can’t I have any fun?

Plaintiff: So I took pictures and videos, and then when the police came—

Judge: You sent them away?

Plaintiff: No, we didn’t send them away. The police came. My husband actually helped diffuse the situation because I was upset.

Judge: You have to calm down. It’s an accident. That’s putting bad juju in the world, and one day, you or your daughter or your husband will have an accident, and someone’s going to start screaming at you. It’s unnecessary. But let’s move on.

Judge: Why didn’t the police file a report?

Plaintiff: She asked them not to.

Judge: The police only walk away if both sides agree not to do a report.

Defendant: The police came and told me she wanted to take the car back to her dealer to see if there was damage underneath.

Judge: Are there visible damages?

Defendant: Based on the light tap that happened, it looks like just a print.

Judge: Is this your car?

Plaintiff: Yes.

Judge: That’s visible damage.

Defendant: The police are the ones who told me to take it in to check if there was internal damage.

Judge: I’ve been doing this all my life—police don’t walk away unless both sides agree to handle it themselves.

Judge: Now, she expects you to pay for the damage. Why don’t you?

Defendant: I filed a claim with my insurance company. They contacted hers. I also asked her to meet me at the precinct to file a report, and I offered for her to get another estimate.

Judge: What did your insurance company decide?

Defendant: They told me they weren’t taking liability.

Judge: Because your insurance lapsed.

Defendant: No, I have active insurance.

Judge: Your insurance company says otherwise.

Defendant: I just paid my insurance.

Judge: Here’s a letter from your insurance company stating, “We have completed our investigation—there is no coverage.” That sounds like a lapse.

Defendant: I still have active insurance.

Judge: You should check that. Now, the estimate says that the damage to the bumper can’t be smoothed out, so it needs to be replaced. That’s what Subaru told her.

Defendant: But she told the police she wasn’t worried about the damage!

Judge: It doesn’t matter. She can change her mind. Why would she tolerate damage on her brand-new car?

Judge: I find that the estimate of $1,424.29 is legitimate. I rule in favor of the plaintiff in that amount.

Judge: Case closed.

Because we had an argument, and she hit me in the face with a washcloth. I went to hit her back but didn’t. She called the cops and said I hit her, but the police found out it wasn’t true. After that, I shut the business down.

Judge:
How much was the insurance check?

Defendant:
$1,800.

Judge:
And why should you get it when you never registered the car in your name?

I took care of the car and spent $2,554 maintaining it.

**Judge:** Ms. Smith, you are suing your former landlord and friend Ms. Rothenberg for $3,000 that you say she owes you as a result of putting your stuff out.

**Plaintiff:** Yes, during my tenancy.

**Judge:** Tell me what happened there.

**Plaintiff:** Well, I was looking to move. I didn’t really like where I was living, I felt uncomfortable. At the time, we were really good friends.

**Judge:** Okay.

**Plaintiff:** So then, she was like, “Hey, I’m renting out the downstairs part of my house, do you know someone who wants to rent it?” And I’m like, “Oh, I’ll find someone.” But I ended up being the person to rent the downstairs part of the house.

**Judge:** How much was your rent?

**Plaintiff:** $700.

**Judge:** And how did it go at the beginning?

**Plaintiff:** It was fine, it was really fine at the beginning.

**Judge:** And the first problem you had with her was when? What happened?

**Plaintiff:** The first altercation had to be the day that we put food on the grill. This was literally—I can’t even tell you the problem. But like I said, if she needed help putting food on the grill, closed mouths don’t get fed. If you need help, you can ask for help. I don’t know how to cook on a grill, so I’m not going to say, “Oh, let me put food on a grill.” I don’t know how to cook on a grill.

**Judge:** Okay, now how does that translate to her putting your stuff out?

**Plaintiff:** That was just the first one. From there, stuff was just never right. It was just always weird after that.

**Judge:** Okay, so let’s get to when it starts to get really bad, which is by October. Did you pay the entire rent for October?

**Plaintiff:** This is what we came to—she said you can pay it weekly or you can just pay the full $700.

**Judge:** When did she say that to you? What was happening?

**Plaintiff:** My boyfriend and I were leaving the house one day, and she was like, “Hey, can I talk to you for a second?” I’m like, “Yeah.” So I go over, and she was talking to me. She said, “We all have to be out by November 1st.” This was probably like the beginning of October.

**Judge:** Why did you have to be out by November 1st?

**Plaintiff:** The lease was ending, Your Honor.

**Judge:** So you weren’t being evicted?

**Plaintiff:** No.

**Judge:** Did you pay the rent for October to the landlord?

**Plaintiff:** Yes, I did.

**Judge:** Go ahead.

**Plaintiff:** So I said, “Okay, I’ll pay it weekly,” because I was barely there. When my apartment was ready, I was going to move out. I wanted to find an apartment. So I gave her the money for two weeks and then just that one week.

**Judge:** How much did you give her?

**Plaintiff:** I gave her $300, and then I was just going to give her the rest for the other two weeks.

**Judge:** That’s not half though.

**Plaintiff:** No, not half. But I gave her some of the money, which was—I think it was just like, what, $50 off? It was supposed to be like $350.

**Judge:** But then when the time came, you also didn’t pay the rest.

**Plaintiff:** I told her—I was going to give it to her once I got my check. It wasn’t a problem before.

**Judge:** It’s a problem now because you guys aren’t getting along. So what happens? You don’t have the rent in the middle of the month. What happens?

**Plaintiff:** We got into an altercation about me washing clothes. Some of my boyfriend’s stuff was mixed in with mine.

**Judge:** She got mad that you were doing your boyfriend’s laundry in the house?

**Plaintiff:** It was my laundry and his together, and then I get a text message saying, “You have the audacity to wash clothes in my house. You didn’t pay the rest of the rent.”

**Judge:** Can I see those texts?

**Defendant:** I asked her to move her stuff to clean, and she didn’t. Later that night, I saw her washing her boyfriend’s clothes. I texted her about it.

**Judge:** But then you put her stuff outside. You can’t do that.

**Defendant:** I set it outside.

**Judge:** Where?

**Defendant:** Near the garage, outside, uncovered.

**Judge:** That’s an illegal lockout. You can’t do that.

**Plaintiff:** It rained. My stuff was ruined.

**Defendant:** It didn’t rain for eight days after I put it out.

**Judge:** That doesn’t matter. You had no right to do that. I am ordering the defendant to pay the plaintiff $500 for the furniture and $75 for the mattress, for a total of $575. Verdict for the plaintiff.

**Defendant:** I didn’t think about it being illegal.

**Judge:** Well, you should have. You broke the law. You got off easy.

**Plaintiff:** It’s not about the money, it’s the principle. My stuff should never have been touched.

**Judge:** Case closed.

Dialogue:
Judge: Can I ask you about Saxon itself and also comments in your brief that it would make no sense to adopt the opposing side's view? Because, in Saxon, at oral argument, it was repeatedly stated to us: If we're talking about a company that is shipping its own goods, those people likely wouldn’t have been railroad employees or seamen at the time.

"Not just Amazon department stores, those people are likely not exempt, and here’s why.

There was a distinction that was made between railroads that shipped things for the public, and I think that’s how we normally understand seamen and railroad employees, and say, a coal company’s internal railroads.

And there’s more.

We have seamen and railroad employees—the two classes of workers that had preexisting dispute resolution statutes at the time and were commonly understood categories.

As a class, the seamen are the people who do the work of the shipping industry.

As a class, railroad employees are people who do the work of the railroad industry.

Now, I bring that up not to bind anyone. I bring that up just because that was the common-sense understanding of counsel in Saxon, and so it seems odd that you would read the Saxon opinion to have blown through those limits that were being stressed by counsel for Saxon about the implications of the position, number one.

And it seems odd also to say the other side’s position just makes no sense when—given what was said at the oral argument in Saxon.

So I just want to give you an opportunity to respond to that.

Plaintiff’s Lawyer: Sure. A few responses to that. One is, you know, we don’t read Saxon to decide the question presented here.

I don’t think—

Judge: I certainly didn’t think that based on what happened at oral argument.

Plaintiff’s Lawyer: Sure. And I think it leaves the question presented open, although I will say I think the opposing argument is inconsistent with the reasoning of Saxon, which is: We look at what these words meant in 1925, and we also are looking for a commonality between seamen and railroad employees. And if there isn’t that commonality, we’re not going to add an additional requirement.

Now, I think you asked about some answers to the hypotheticals in Saxon.

Judge: Mm-hmm.

Plaintiff’s Lawyer: You know, and I’ll note that this question wasn’t presented either way in Saxon, and there were some hypotheticals that I do think touched on this question, but the— you know, and I apologize if the answer wasn’t as clear as it should have been.

The gravamen of that—

Judge: Well, I thought the answer was very clear, actually.

Plaintiff’s Lawyer: Well, so—so—

Judge: It was reassuring. I think the word “narrow” was used—reassuring that the holding in favor of Saxon would be narrow and would not extend to industries other than the transportation industry.

Which—that may be incorrect, but to call it like that makes no sense is a little much for me, at least.

Plaintiff’s Lawyer: Sure. And I think the gravamen— you know, there were specific predictions maybe, but the gravamen of that answer is: To know whether the Federal Arbitration Act exempts a particular class of workers, what we’d have to do is go back and look in 1925 and see what these words meant.

And we’ve now— you know, because it wasn’t the question presented in Saxon, that research hadn’t been done.

We’ve now done that.

And I think it’s very clear that in 1925, the word “seamen” did not mean somebody who was employed by a company that sold transportation.

And I’d like to turn to that briefly, if I may.

You know, every source we have— when you go back and take a look— dictionaries, case law, books, other statutes— literally any piece of evidence we have confirms that the word “seamen” included anyone who worked aboard a vessel in furtherance of its purpose.

It had nothing to do with whether an employer sold transportation or, in the Second Circuit’s words, had a particular price or revenue structure.

And I’ll note that this Court has already canvassed this history at least twice— first in Wilander and then again in Saxon— and both times it came to the same conclusion, which is that “seamen” is a longstanding, well-defined term that in 1925 plainly meant everybody who worked aboard a vessel.

Now, to its credit, I actually don’t take the opposing side to dispute this ordinary meaning of “seamen.”

Maybe they’ll get up and tell me I’m wrong about how I read their brief, but what I take them to say is: Whatever the ordinary meaning is, for purposes of the Federal Arbitration Act, the Court should give the word a different definition.

And that different definition should be something like “workers aboard a ship in a carrying trade carrying goods for trade and commerce.”

And there are two problems with this request.

The first is: Not only is this not the ordinary meaning of “seamen” in 1925, it’s not any meaning ever of “seamen” in 1925 or since then.

What that definition comes from is a definition that a single district court gave to the term “merchant vessel,” and the term “merchant vessel” is nowhere in the Federal Arbitration Act.

So the opposing side has to demonstrate a commonality between seamen and railroad employees— not between railroad employees and merchant vessels.

So that’s the first problem: It’s just not in the statute.

The second problem, though, is that even if this Court were willing to accept this definition of words that aren’t even in the statute as the definition of “seamen” for purposes of the Federal Arbitration Act and define it in accordance with what the opposing side says we should define it, their drivers meet their own definition.

There’s no question that their truck drivers are engaged in transporting goods for commerce— just like the people on lumber boats in 1925, just like the people on the barges carrying railroad tie manufacturers’ goods in this Court’s decision in Ayer.

And so, even if we were to accept every single one of their arguments on seamen, they still haven’t shown that this employer-based industry requirement has anything to do with the words of the statute.

Judge: And just to understand, what are the categories of seamen who do not work in the shipping industry?

Plaintiff’s Lawyer: There’s a vast number of them. And they’re not— you know, one thing that’s difficult is they’re not—well, so I actually—I want to take a step back and talk about the word “industry” very briefly.

Which is to say, when you say “in the shipping industry,” we can mean two different things.

One is we can mean the workers who are in the industry— as in, these are people who do shipping work. They do the work of the boat.

Or you can mean sort of an employer-based requirement, which is they work for a company that sells transportation—on the opposing side’s version.

And I think the intuition that seamen and railroad employees are definitely in the transportation industry is an intuition on the first question about industry people—

Judge: So assuming what I meant was the second.

Plaintiff’s Lawyer: Sure.

Judge: So who are the seamen who are not working for shippers?

Plaintiff’s Lawyer: There’s a bunch of them. So there are a bunch of manufacturers, for example, who employed seamen.

There is a railroad tie manufacturer, for example, in Ayer, that employed seamen.

There were lumber boats all up and down the West Coast that employed seamen. They worked for lumber companies.

They didn’t work for transportation companies.

There were coal companies that employed seamen.

The Ford Motor Company employed seamen.

There’s a host of employers that employed seamen.

And the reason is very similar to why you have a host of companies employing truckers today—

Which is that, unlike railroads, which require infrastructure, all you needed to ship your own goods is a boat—

Judge: And before—

Plaintiff’s Lawyer: —just like—

Judge: Keep going. Sorry.
