Judge: And now he's coming after you for it?

Plaintiff: Hopefully not, but he said he was going to.

Judge: I'm telling you right now, unless this landlord has a screw loose, they’re not going to go to court on a roommate situation over one person’s unpaid rent. They’ll just go after whoever is still there.

Plaintiff: We both signed the lease. It says on line 18 that we are responsible for heat and electric. My mother and the landlord were both present when we agreed to split the bills.

Judge: Why am I here? I don’t want to hear about who’s a slob, who doesn’t do dishes, or complaints about boyfriends. Why am I here?

Plaintiff: Because she didn’t pay her half of the electric bill.

Judge: And for what months?

Plaintiff: The only month she paid since we moved in was December.

Judge: Let me see your bills. At this point, how are you two getting along?

Plaintiff: We weren’t talking.

Judge: I mean, in January, February, March—when she wasn’t paying, how were you getting along?

Plaintiff: At that time, we were getting along. But about a month and a half before she moved out, we got into a big argument.

Judge: About what?

Plaintiff: The same things—heat, messiness, little things that spiraled out of control. We said hurtful things to each other.

Judge: Had you ever said hurtful things before and then gotten over it?

Plaintiff: Yes, but not like this. Her boyfriend got involved, too, saying hurtful things to my mother.

Judge: What did he say to your mother?

Plaintiff: My mom texted him because he was saying mean things to me. She told him to stay out of it and said, "They fight like sisters, they’ll probably get over it."

Judge: And what did he say?

Plaintiff: He called me "scum of the Earth," "disgusting," and said, "nobody likes you."

Judge: Let me hear your side.

Defendant: We had issues before moving in. We were best friends, but we knew it could be a problem. We agreed that if things were going well, we’d continue living together. I paid half of the RG&E bill, which was in her name. October, November, December, everything was fine. I gave her cash for my half.

Judge: And after that?

Defendant: In December, she started dating a guy who was into serious drugs. She found a spoon in his car for heroin use and forgave him. I felt unsafe. He had a criminal record, and I started staying at my boyfriend’s house six to seven days a week. Then she finally left him, filed a police report, and got an order of protection because he slashed her tires.

Judge: When was that?

Defendant: Late January. She filed the police report in February.

Judge: So what happened next?

Defendant: She invited me to breakfast, but I wasn’t feeling well. Then I started getting text messages—she was upset I wasn’t home enough. She accused me of lying, got really needy, and then things escalated. She broke into my room, trashed it, and sent me a picture of the mess.

Judge: Let me see the texts. (Reviews them.) You do sound a little needy, accusing her of lying. But that doesn’t determine a lawsuit. You’re suing her for unpaid utilities. She’s saying she wasn’t living there. What’s your response to that?

Plaintiff: She was still on the lease. She hadn’t officially moved out.

Judge: On what legal theory are you not responsible for utilities before moving out?

Defendant: I wasn’t living there for three months.

Judge: You may have chosen to stay elsewhere, but if you haven’t moved out, you’re still living there. The fact that it’s not a legal lease affects your agreement with the landlord, not your agreement with her.

Judge: I'm generally sympathetic to the idea that you don't want to disclose information, national security information, but I think you've said multiple times you already do that when the case goes to the merits in these kinds of situations.
So I'm not sure that's a particular concern if you have to do it in a few more cases to establish mootness, but correct me if --

Defendant's Lawyer: Well, if you accept Respondent's submission in this case or the Ninth Circuit's, it's not just going to be a few more cases; it's going to be literally anyone who's ever on the No Fly List, the claims could never moot out unless we --

Judge: Right.

Defendant's Lawyer: -- revealed the information, then, you know, there is a point of minimization.
Even, you know, when we have to reveal the information --

Judge: I agree.

Defendant's Lawyer: -- we do it, but we don't want to -- that doesn't mean we do it all the time.

Judge: I -- I'm very sympathetic to that, so I -- I accept that. Okay.
On the -- on -- I'm sympathetic to your mootness argument, although I might not have sounded like it, but the reason I'm having trouble is to squeeze this kind of situation into the test we use I find very -- very difficult.
So -- and there are two different kinds of scenarios someone could come off the No Fly List that I think might affect how at least I instinctively look at this.

One would be we made a mistake, it's the wrong name, person had the same name.
This happens.
And someone comes off the No Fly List because it was some -- you know, you had nothing to do with anything and your name was the same as someone who does, okay? If you said that, then mootness seems like very easy to establish under the standard that we have.
When it's this kind of, well, connecting the dots situation, it's much harder, I think, to squeeze it into reasonably expected to recur because we just have no idea.

Defendant's Lawyer: Yeah.
So two -- two responses to that. One, I -- I think you hit the nail on the head with the dynamic nature of these things.
It's not like, you know, the churches either are or aren't entitled --

Judge: Yes.

Defendant's Lawyer: -- to a grant under the First Amendment.
The union fees either are or aren't, you know, chargeable under the First Amendment. Here, it's -- it's not static like that.
It's not like, if you're an individual, you either always belong on or always belong off the No Fly List.
It's at the moment, do you pose a threat of engaging in terrorism.
And an individual might pose that threat before 2016, not pose it since 2016, and, you know, in -- in 2050 might pose it again.
Who knows? But you can never know that, right? And I think that you're right, that is a distinction here.
And so then the second part of my answer is I think we need to remember that voluntary cessation is a judge-made exception to Article III.
We're still -- we're always implementing Article III.
And I think, therefore, it's critical not to stray or become untethered from the purposes of the voluntary cessation doctrine and why this Court developed it.
It was to prevent gamesmanship and docket manipulation to avoid judicial review, post-certiorari maneuvers, other sorts of things.
That's not what's going on in this case.
We did not take him off the list in an attempt to evade judicial review.
And I think that should be the touchstone for how you apply the reasonable expectation of recurrence or whatever the language is for voluntary cessation.
Don't untether it from the purposes for which the doctrine was developed, because, at all times, it -- mootness should not stray so far from Article III that they become completely disconnected.
And, here, he's not on the list.
He hasn't been on the list in eight years.
There just simply isn't a live case or controversy about his placement on the No Fly List any longer.
And in the unlikely event he's put back on the No Fly List in the future, he can bring a challenge at that time.

Judge: Thank you.

Judge: Mr. [Defendant's Lawyer], could you address the claim made by Respondent and some of the amici that the government, in fact, is strategically mooting these cases by dismissing them?

Defendant's Lawyer: Yeah.
We strongly disagree with that, and I don't think the evidence they cite actually supports the claim.
I think the ACLU's brief is probably the most comprehensive on this front, but if you look at the ACLU's brief, what they say is -- you know, sometimes, I -- I think it's about half or something like that, roughly, of these plaintiffs are removed from the No Fly List during the litigation.
Of course, that's the -- that's how many U.S. persons are removed from the No Fly List, just based on the administrative redress process, which is what we think is happening.
So ACLU documents that, well, some of these plaintiffs are removed before any briefs are filed, and then some are removed after the briefs are filed but before a district court decision.
And then some are removed after the decision.
Some are removed on appeal.
And I look at all that and I think, well, that makes sense because they're removed as an outcome of the administrative redress process and where the agency takes a fresh look at the file and says we now no longer believe they pose a risk of engaging in the terrorist activities.
If we were strategically mooting, you might expect the removals to all come at, like, the same point in litigation or with a certain kind of litigation trigger.
But that's just not what's going on at all.
And, of course, we have litigated several No Fly List claims to judgment, which wouldn't make sense if we were trying to engage in strategic mooting.
So I think, if you look at the universe of cases, what is apparent is that in all of the cases cited by Respondent and I think in ACLU's appendix, redress proceedings were in parallel to the litigation, and I think it's natural to expect that sometimes those individuals will be removed as a result of the redress process.
I strongly dispute any notion that we're engaged in strategic mooting, and we have filed declarations from people occupying the same position as Courtright, not in this case but in follow-on cases, that say we never place someone on the No Fly List or remove them from the No Fly List because of litigation concerns.
And I think that's entitled to deference and -- and respect.

Judge: So I just want to be clear.
Are you asking for a special mootness rule for the national security context?

Defendant's Lawyer: No.

Judge: Are we doing some sort of a carveout for national security?

Defendant's Lawyer: No, quite the opposite.
I -- we rely extensively, I didn't think it was possible to rely more heavily, on a case than we do on -- than on Already.
That was a case involving private parties.
We think exactly the same rule applies.
We do think the national security context does matter for an evaluation of the facts on the ground.
I think it's Respondent who would have a heightened rule for government officials.

JUDGE: The defendant has filed a countersuit for $5,000 for rent and cat sitting. All parties, please raise your right hands. Welcome back to The People's Court. Next case on the docket, the plaintiff says the defendant locked her and her cat out of the apartment and has all of their belongings. But the defendant says she knew the plaintiff was having hard times and stupidly let her move in, and she was beyond awful. It's the case of time to feed the kitty. Thank you, Douglas. Lisa Marie Pimental?

PLAINTIFF: Yes, ma'am.

JUDGE: You are suing Mary Elizabeth Ryder for $5,000 in jewelry and miscellaneous items, including designer items, makeup bags, clothing, pearl earrings, gemstone bracelets, and pearl necklaces, that you say she is wrongfully withholding from you. And you are counterclaiming in the amount of $5,000 for rent, cat sitting, and some bills that you say she owes you. Tell me what's going on.

PLAINTIFF: Uh, July of 2011, I was evicted from my apartment. I had met Mary Elizabeth at a flea market in the neighborhood.

JUDGE: You were evicted why?

PLAINTIFF: Non-payment. Uh, lack of payment.

JUDGE: Okay.

PLAINTIFF: Uh, we had issues in the apartment as far as leaks and mold and disrepairs. Uh, I had been in court, and I lost the case.

JUDGE: So that was how many months that you lived there without paying rent?

PLAINTIFF: Uh, I'd say about five.

JUDGE: Five months rent-free. And the court found against you on the--?

PLAINTIFF: They found against me because I was not a-- I was grandfathered into the apartment.

JUDGE: What does that mean by "grandfathered in?" Then were you--?

PLAINTIFF: They found against me.

JUDGE: That you weren't really a tenant?

PLAINTIFF: For 30 years is what I--

JUDGE: You were a tenant because you were underneath your grandparents' lease?

PLAINTIFF: Yes.

JUDGE: For 30 years in that apartment. Were your grandparents still living there?

PLAINTIFF: No, they had, uh, recently been deceased.

JUDGE: Okay.

PLAINTIFF: Um, the person that I was staying with, I just did not like the environment, so Mary Elizabeth invited me to stay with her with my cats.

JUDGE: How many cats did you have?

PLAINTIFF: Three.

JUDGE: So Mary Elizabeth came with you to retrieve your animals and took you back into her apartment. So then she brings you in, and what happens? You're not paying rent? She's just doing you the favor?

PLAINTIFF: She was doing me the favor as long as I needed to.

JUDGE: Which could be what? Two years?

PLAINTIFF: Could have been two years or until I found some place that I wanted to stay.

JUDGE: You don't think that maybe there should be a limit to that?

PLAINTIFF: At that point, I had been hit by a car. I was recuperating.

JUDGE: Do you have a lawsuit pending?

PLAINTIFF: Yes, I do.

JUDGE: So go on.

PLAINTIFF: Um, I came back from an appointment on the morning of December 9th and found my locks had been changed.

JUDGE: How long had you been there?

PLAINTIFF: From July to December.

JUDGE: From August, the second week in August, to the second week in December. Okay. And you had never paid rent?

PLAINTIFF: I had never paid.

JUDGE: Had she asked you to help out?

PLAINTIFF: No, she did not.

JUDGE: What was she mad at?

PLAINTIFF: I don't know.

JUDGE: Oh, you do know. Come on.

PLAINTIFF: I don't know because she--

JUDGE: She's the same person who offered you a roof over your head for free for five months. So something turned during the five months. What was it?

PLAINTIFF: I was giving her $200 a month in food, my, uh, food stamps.

JUDGE: You were using your food stamps to supply the house with food?

PLAINTIFF: I was going to the local food pantry, bringing home produce.

JUDGE: Why don't you work?

PLAINTIFF: Because of the car accident. I was working.

JUDGE: So what was she mad at? Are you going to answer that or no?

PLAINTIFF: I don't know.

JUDGE: Okay. Tell me what you were mad at. What happened? You bring this lady into your home.

DEFENDANT: She, uh, kept getting drunk. Drunk and drunk.

JUDGE: She kept getting drunk?

DEFENDANT: Yes, Your Honor. She would come home drunk, yelling and making a scene in the apartment. It was becoming unbearable.

JUDGE: Did you ever confront her about it?

DEFENDANT: Yes, multiple times. I told her she needed to control herself, but she didn’t listen.

JUDGE: So what happened on December 9th?

DEFENDANT: I had enough. That morning, she came home late, banging on the door, completely out of it. I told her she couldn’t stay anymore, and she got aggressive.

JUDGE: Aggressive how?

DEFENDANT: Yelling, calling me names, threatening me. I was scared, so I called the police.

JUDGE: And the police came?

DEFENDANT: Yes. They told her she had to leave.

JUDGE: What about her belongings?

DEFENDANT: I packed up what I could and left it outside for her.

JUDGE: Did you keep anything?

DEFENDANT: No, I have no use for her stuff.

JUDGE: What do you say to that?

PLAINTIFF: That’s not true. My jewelry, my clothes, my mother’s things—all of it is gone.

JUDGE: Did you take anything, Ms. Ryder?

DEFENDANT: No, ma’am.

JUDGE: Do you have proof of what was left behind, Ms. Pimental?

PLAINTIFF: I took photos of what was missing after I was locked out.

JUDGE: Did you ever try to retrieve your things?

PLAINTIFF: Yes, but she refused to give them back.

JUDGE: Ms. Ryder, do you have any receipts or records showing she owed you rent or money for cat sitting?

DEFENDANT: No, it was all verbal.

JUDGE: That’s going to be a problem for your countersuit.

DEFENDANT: But she lived there rent-free!

JUDGE: You allowed that, didn’t you?

DEFENDANT: Yes, but—

JUDGE: That’s where the problem is. If there’s no written agreement or proof, I can’t just award you money.

DEFENDANT: Understood.

JUDGE: I’ll review the evidence. Court is in recess.

Judge: How do you respond to the government's suggestion that the NTA is really not central? I mean, the government says we are looking at the statute and it says, you know, we can get an in absentia removal order if we have issued (1) or (2). And so I guess the government's point is that suggests that (1) is really not pivotal in the way that you are putting forward.

Defendant's Lawyer: So I think that's wrong, Your Honor, for two reasons. The first is that the "or" in the in absentia context doesn't define the relationship between (a)(1) and (a)(2). That's done in the statute defining those two provisions. And so, again, to go back to the voter registration hypothetical, you could have a sentence saying, you know, send the voter the primary ballot for the party listed in their voter registration form or their change of party affiliation form. That wouldn't mean that somehow the voter registration form is irrelevant just because you have the word "or" in that sentence. And so I think what you have to do is look at paragraphs (1) and (2) and the relationship between them, and it's clear both from the mandatory language of paragraph (1), from the fact that, as this Court explained in Niz-Chavez, it's the one place that tells you all the information you need to defend yourself in a removal proceeding, and the fact that (a)(2) is structured as a change or a supplement, that those two documents are not interchangeable.

Judge: I want to make sure I understand your answer about change. So you're saying that, you know, just because you file a change of party affiliation form doesn't make your initial voter registration valid. I -- I get that. But, in the statute, it says, "change of time or place of proceedings." So it's not, you know, referring generally. I'm just wondering where you get the statutory hook, because that presumes that there is an NTA. But, if it's defective in some way, if, as we take the defect in time and place out of it, why couldn't it be that there is an NTA that's defective in some way, but the statute imposes an obligation on the non-citizen who knows the time and place he's supposed to show up to make an appearance at that proceeding and raise whatever objection there is to the defect?

Defendant's Lawyer: So --

Judge: And maybe -- maybe the objection -- and I'm thinking of an analogy to a Rule 55 civil default judgment proceeding. It may well be that whatever objection that the non-citizen raises at that point is fatal to the government's case. You know, maybe it's missing some vital piece of information. Maybe it can be remedied if it's omitted the right to counsel by giving him time to obtain counsel. If there's some problem in the description of the charges, the grounds for removal, then maybe the entire thing has to be dismissed and the government has to start again. But why wouldn't it be consistent with the statutory scheme for the non-citizen to have to show up to the removal proceeding and register whatever objection the non-citizen has? I'm not saying that that's right, but it seems to me -- I -- I just want to understand why that's ruled out on your understanding of the statutory language.

Defendant's Lawyer: Sure, Your Honor. So the statute doesn't ask about notice generally or notice of the time of the hearing. It asks about notice in accordance with the specific statutory provisions. And that's a contrast to the pre- --

Judge: Or, though, right?

Defendant's Lawyer: So that's correct. But our position is that there's been neither a paragraph (1) notice nor a paragraph (2) notice.

Judge: But I guess I don't understand where in the statute you can say there has not been a paragraph (1) notice if there's been a defective paragraph (1) notice? There's been a paragraph (1) notice. And, here, I'm not talking about the blank piece of paper. I'm just saying it's missing some piece of information other than date and time. Why isn't that just a defective NTA?

Defendant's Lawyer: So I think that's sort of the square holding of Pereira. So, again, Pereira reserved the question of missing other pieces of information, but the statute puts them all on par. And Pereira says it's not just that this is a deficient document. It says no notice to appear has been served, right? That's the language that Pereira was interpreting.

Judge: But stop -- well, I'm sorry. Go ahead.

Defendant's Lawyer: So -- so Pereira is interpreting has a notice to appear been served under paragraph (1). It says no notice to appear has been served if it's missing one of these pieces of information.

Judge: Counsel, can I -- I'm not cutting you off?

Judge: No, I'm done.

Judge: Then I'll jump into that. I have -- I want to follow up on the previous question because there seems to be a difference between a jurisdictional flaw and a claim processing flaw, which is what the government has been calling this. And I do think that we -- you have to address that question. It seems to me that the clear holding of our prior precedents is that it's jurisdictional, that if you don't have a proper notice of appearance, that that's a jurisdictional defect. They can't order you -- they can't invoke the stop-gap rule, but they also can't order you removed until they provide you with the proper document, correct?

Defendant's Lawyer: So I think that's right. I want to be a little bit careful because I think "jurisdictional" is a little bit of a slippery word.

Judge: It is.

Defendant's Lawyer: Right.

Judge: So is using claim processing in this kind of context.

Defendant's Lawyer: So our position is that if you show up at the hearing, you can say this was defective. And until the government cures that defect, they can't go forward. Congress also provided an express remedy for where there's a defective notice and you're removed in absentia. There is no remedy in the statute for where there's a defective notice and a removal order was entered after a hearing. So we're not saying that someone who attended their hearing, had the chance to object to the notice to appear, and was nonetheless removed gets to reopen because Congress hasn't provided that remedy.

Judge: All right. So --

Defendant's Lawyer: Congress has, however, in --

Judge: Before we end today, I -- I want to take head-on the draconian consequences that a ruling in your favor that the government is painting, and I want you to answer that.

Judge: We may as well go with that now. (Laughter.)

Defendant's Lawyer: So -- so two responses, Your Honor, right? So the first is the government came to you in Pereira and Niz-Chavez and also articulated a parade of horribles. And this Court said those sorts of raw consequentialist calculations have no place, particularly where, as noted, those consequences are a function of the government ignoring the text of the statute over many cases and many years. The second is, you know, I -- I -- I don't want to dispute that there will be an increase in the volume of these motions, but I do want to be clear about who exactly has an incentive to file them. Remember, if you win on reopening your in absentia removal order, all you get is another hearing, right, even if you're successful in clearing all the hurdles. And at that hearing, you have to prove that you're able to remain in the U.S. And so, you know, for many, many non-citizens who have no pathway to staying in the United States, it's very unlikely that they're going to come forward and file one of these motions to reopen because the best they get is another hearing.

Judge: All parties please raise your right hand.

Judge: Samuel Romero, you are suing your former girlfriend, Hilda Hernandez, for $2,500, the value of a truck that you, according to you, loaned her, let her drive, and she totaled. While you were dating. You're no longer dating, correct?

Plaintiff: No.

Judge: All right, tell me what's going on.

Plaintiff: On October 19th, I was sick with the flu in her house, and she took my truck that morning because she didn’t want to take the bus to go to work. Later on that day, she was calling my phone, but my phone was off. Then she had her friend knock at the door, and that’s when I found out my truck was totaled.

Judge: Let me just ask you in a moment how the accident happened, but how long had you two been dating?

Plaintiff: On and off for like a year.

Judge: Okay. What happened the day of the accident?

Defendant: The day of the accident, I was driving from work and I was rushing home. It was a rainy day. I was trying to make a right turn, and I lost control. I hit a pole, and the truck was totaled. And that was the end of it. We moved on with our relationship. We were having our problems.

Judge: So you broke up?

Defendant: Yeah, we broke up at the end of November 2011.

Judge: Up to this point in time, had there been any discussion by him that you owed him money for the truck?

Defendant: No, not at all.

Judge: Because according to you, the truck was yours?

Defendant: Yes, it was. He gave me the truck. I have two voicemails right here where he is saying himself clearly and specifically that it was my truck.

Judge: When do these voicemails happen?

Defendant: November 28th, 2011.

Judge: But by then, the car is totaled. So why did they happen?

Defendant: Let me explain. The reason why is because I had a feeling—there were some things that had already happened in our relationship. Since he made a statement to the officer, and I still kept that inside me, I asked him, I believe November 27th, that I needed a notarized letter stating that that was my car. What happened was that morning, supposedly he was looking for—he was going to an interview, so he couldn’t write the notified letter. When he did call me, I didn’t pick up purposely because I wanted to make sure that if he was going to leave some type of evidence, it was going to be on this voicemail. And he sure has. I have it right here.

Defendant: Plays voicemail "That truck was yours, you understand? Because you always wanted a car, right? That truck’s yours. Because it was under my name, the title had everything under my name, but I was going to switch everything to you. Because I told you that it’s your car."

Judge: Why did you leave that message for her? What did you think she wanted it for?

Plaintiff: The reason why I left that message, Your Honor, is because she had a ticket because she was unlicensed.

Judge: You didn’t have a license?

Defendant: No.

Judge: And why are you letting her take your truck?

Plaintiff: She took my truck all the time.

Judge: No, no. You said she took your truck because she didn’t want to take public transportation to work. You knew she took your truck?

Plaintiff: I was very sick.

Judge: You were very sick, so she was driving? I have affidavits from a bunch of her family members saying that she drove that truck all the time. Why were you letting her drive your truck when she had no license?

Defendant: I deserve that.

Judge: You both deserve it. What are you both thinking? It’s under your name, so she runs over a truckload of nuns, and you're going to get sued. You understand?

Plaintiff: Yes.

Judge: Who pays? Him or her, for this truck?

Defendant: He has to pay. You can’t let someone who doesn’t have a license drive your car.

Judge: Yeah, but she knew she didn’t have a license.

Defendant: No, but he’s the one with the license, and he has the insurance and stuff.

Judge: Yeah, but she was driving the car. He’s legally liable. He knew she didn’t have a license.

Defendant: But she knows it too.

Judge: Too bad. All right, so you end up breaking up when?

Defendant: End of November 2011. Things started falling apart. He got a little violent twice already. First time, he hit me.

Judge: How?

Defendant: We were in front of his grandmother’s house, and he was smoking something. I pushed it out of his hand, and he choked me and put me on the floor.

Judge: He choked you and put you on the floor?

Defendant: Yes. That was the first time.

Judge: Did he get arrested?

Defendant: No. Because we both got into it. He hit me, and I hit him. He destroyed my DVD player, my kids' DVD player, my Blackberry. He took my big screen TV and threw it on the floor.

Judge: And that was the breakup?

Defendant: Yes.

Judge: How did he take the breakup?

Defendant: He tried to be nice about it at first, then he got angry. He was in denial. I had to get a restraining order. I changed my phone number, relocated, changed my kids' school.

Judge: Do you have a criminal case pending?

Defendant: Yes, harassment and other charges.

Judge: Here’s my ruling. You have an unhealthy obsession with her. You need to move on. There is a restraining order. If you violate it, you go to Rikers. As for the truck, based on your voicemail, I cannot rule in your favor. Case dismissed.

Defendant: "Well, I still see my son."

Plaintiff: "Okay, yeah, but like maybe once a month, you know, maybe."

Defendant: "Oh, I see him more often than that. I see him more often."

Judge: "What's more often than that?"

Defendant: "Well, once a week. I mean, I have my business all day, and then I do extra catering in the evenings, and so I'm just really busy. But when I'm not, then I do go visit with my son."

Judge: Mr. Counsel, the Solicitor General suggests, as you do, that we remand this case to the lower court for a harmless error analysis. That is our usual practice.

Defendant's Lawyer: Right.

Judge: But amici want us to address it now. Do you have a viable argument at the lower court level?

Defendant's Lawyer: Oh, yes, we do. The issue in this case involves three convictions over eight days, allegedly in the same city and within the same timeframe.

Judge: Same city, but not the same place. One was a pizzeria, another—

Defendant's Lawyer: Yes, forgive me. That is what I meant to say. That’s the allegation.

So, as I explained earlier, imagine a scenario where someone needed money to pay off a gambling debt and, over a few days, committed a series of burglaries at different commercial establishments. A rational jury could find that to be a single criminal episode, rather than separate offenses, especially in light of what ACCA’s different occasions clause aims to address.

Remember, the goal is to identify career offenders—those with a long history of criminal behavior. Someone who acts on a single impulse or executes a single plan does not fit the type of offender ACCA is intended to target.

Judge: Thank you.

Judge: All parties, please raise your right hands.

Bailiff: Welcome back to The People’s Court. Next case on the docket: The plaintiff hired the defendant to provide a DJ for a party, but the DJ got into an accident, and now the defendant refuses to refund her money. The defendant claims the plaintiff canceled at the last minute. It’s the case of Can’t Stop the Music.

Judge: Thank you, Douglas. Annette Dunlap, you are suing John M., the owner of a DJ company, for $1,500. You claim that you paid $300 for DJ services that were never provided, and you're also seeking additional costs, including bank fees and pain and suffering. Let’s start from the beginning.

Plaintiff's Side
Judge: Ms. Dunlap, what happened?

Plaintiff (Annette Dunlap): I hired the DJ company to provide music for my New Year's Eve party. I found them online because they had good reviews and offered a reasonable price compared to others. They quoted me $349, and I paid a $300 deposit in advance.

Judge: Had you used their services before?

Plaintiff: No, this was my first time. I spoke to a woman named Amanda V., who was my primary contact throughout.

Judge: Did you ever speak to the defendant, Mr. John M.?

Plaintiff: No. I only spoke to Amanda V. I was told to contact Mr. M. when I started asking for my refund, but he never responded to my calls.

Judge: What happened the day before the event?

Plaintiff: I received a call from Amanda. She told me that the DJ assigned to my event had been in a car accident and could not make it.

Judge: What solution did they offer?

Plaintiff: Amanda said I had two options:

Get a refund, or
Hire a different DJ through another company, but that would cost $500 instead of $300.
Judge: Did you ask why you had to pay an extra $200?

Plaintiff: Yes, I asked why they wouldn't cover the difference, since it was their DJ who couldn’t make it. Amanda didn’t give me a clear answer. At that point, I told her I would take the refund instead.

Judge: Did you get anything in writing about the refund?

Plaintiff: Yes. I asked for an email confirmation, and she sent one. But it wasn’t from Amanda—it was from someone named Roxanne Simonetti.

Judge: Who is Roxanne Simonetti?

Defendant's Side
Defendant (John M.): She’s a secretary at my company.

Judge: Mr. M., can you explain why Ms. Dunlap was told the DJ was in a car accident?

Defendant: I don’t know anything about that.

Judge: You don’t? Because I have an email here, sent from your secretary, which clearly states, "The DJ that was scheduled to do your event was unfortunately in an accident and won't be able to attend." Did your secretary make that up?

Defendant: I have no idea why she would say that.

Judge: So you're saying there was no car accident?

Defendant: No, not that I know of.

Judge: Then why did your secretary lie to your customer?

Defendant: I don’t know.

Judge: That’s interesting because you originally claimed that the plaintiff canceled on you.

Defendant: That’s correct.

Judge: So which is it? Did she cancel, or did your DJ back out because of a supposed accident?

Defendant: She canceled.

Judge: So if she canceled, why would your secretary send an email saying your DJ was in an accident?

(Pause. The defendant appears visibly nervous.)

Defendant: I don’t have an answer for that.

Judge's Analysis
Judge: Here’s what I think happened: You overbooked yourself for New Year’s Eve. You had multiple events going on, and you didn’t have enough DJs. When you realized that you couldn’t fulfill all the contracts, you started choosing which clients to keep—probably the ones who were paying the most. Then, rather than being honest, you lied to Ms. Dunlap and tried to push the extra cost onto her by suggesting a $500 alternative.

Defendant: That’s not true.

Judge: It is true, and you know how I know? Because if Ms. Dunlap had canceled on you, there would be no need to mention a car accident at all! The fact that your secretary sent that email proves that you canceled on her.

(The defendant remains silent.)

Plaintiff’s Additional Costs
Judge: Ms. Dunlap, you’re also suing for $400 in bank fees and $500 in pain and suffering. Explain.

Plaintiff: I had to make multiple bank transfers and cover extra fees while trying to sort this out.

Judge: What about the pain and suffering?

Plaintiff: I went through a lot of stress trying to find a last-minute replacement. My boyfriend helped me find someone, but I had to negotiate, and it was a hassle.

Judge: How much did you pay for the replacement DJ?

Plaintiff: Another $300.

Judge: So, in reality, you’re only out $300, correct?

Plaintiff: Well, yes, but I also had to—

Judge: I understand it was stressful, but pain and suffering is not something I typically award in small claims court for a business contract issue.

Final Verdict
Judge: Here’s what I’m going to do. I am ordering the defendant to refund the original $300 deposit. You don’t get an additional $300 because that would mean you get a free DJ. Your bank fees are not directly tied to the defendant’s actions, so I will not award those. Pain and suffering is also denied.

My judgment is for the plaintiff in the amount of $300.

Closing Remarks
(Gavel bangs.)

Bailiff: The judge has ruled in favor of the plaintiff for $300. Court is adjourned.

(Outside the courtroom, the plaintiff smiles while the defendant shakes his head.)

Plaintiff: I’m happy I at least got my money back. They should have just been honest from the beginning.

Defendant: I still don’t think I did anything wrong. It’s just business.

Bailiff: That’s it for The People’s Court.

Judge: We will hear argument next in Case 22-1178, the Federal Bureau of Investigation versus Yonas Fikre. Counsel, you may proceed.

Defendant's Lawyer: May it please the Court. Respondent's No Fly List claims are moot. He's not on the list. He hasn't been on the list in eight years. And he won't be put back on the list in the future based on the currently available information. That makes it absolutely clear that his return to the list for the same reasons he was put on it initially can't reasonably be expected to recur.

Now the lower court thought the claims weren't moot because the government hadn't acquiesced to the righteousness of his contentions. That fundamentally confuses mootness with the merits. As this Court has explained, a case can be moot no matter how vehemently the parties dispute the conduct that gave rise to the lawsuit. Respondent's new test isn't much better because it too can be satisfied solely by repudiation of the past conduct.

And that persistent focus on repudiation and the past lays bare what's really going on in this case. The complaint isn't really concerned about Respondent's potentially being put back on the No Fly List in the future. What Respondent wants is vindication for his past placement. But that vindication could come only through an advisory opinion.

There is no prospective relief of any kind that could actually redress any cognizable injury that Respondent currently suffers or imminently will suffer. Mootness implements Article III's case or controversy requirement. The voluntary cessation exception purpose is rooted in preventing gamesmanship or docket manipulation in an attempt to avoid judicial review.

But that's clearly not what's going on in this case, and it's not what's going on in other cases either. The government has litigated many No Fly List claims to judgment. This case, however, is 11 years old, and Respondent has been off the list for the past eight of them. There simply isn't a live case or controversy any longer, and this Court should hold as much. I welcome the Court's questions.

Judge: Well, the Respondent was also concerned about the propriety of the procedures that were used to put him on the list to begin with and challenged that. So how does the declaration that you all used change the procedures that he thought were violative of his rights?

Defendant's Lawyer: So two responses. As far as a procedural challenge goes, number one, for the post-deprivation procedures, those have actually already changed since the time he was first on the list. I take the point that maybe he wants some pre-deprivation procedures. Those haven't changed.

But I think precedent pretty squarely forecloses that challenge. In a similar case, the plaintiffs complained about the procedures used to deprive them of property and the procedures to get their property back. After their property was returned to them, the procedures hadn't changed, but the Court held that the challenge was moot.

And I think that just goes back to the fundamental principle behind Article III standing and mootness, which is that you no longer have a live challenge to procedures if, substantively, you're not subjected to those procedures or can't show that it's imminent that you're going to be subjected to those procedures. Procedures in a vacuum just can't be challenged.

Judge: But how would we know, for example, and how would he know that any circumstances or any procedures are different if he's put on the list in the future when we don't know the procedures or the circumstances that got him on the list?

Defendant's Lawyer: So let me take those two separately. With the procedures, again, I'll just say procedures in a vacuum can't be challenged unless you're substantively going to be subjected to them.

Judge: Yeah, I understand that, but we know nothing. We don't—how would he know a year from now if he is on the list with the same procedures or different procedures or different facts when he doesn’t know why he was on the list in the first instance?

Defendant's Lawyer: Yes. So that's the key. He does know why he was on the list, and we've made this public. The standard to be on the No Fly List, in addition to reasonable suspicion that someone is a known or suspected terrorist, is that they must also pose a threat of conducting or engaging in one of four enumerated acts of terrorism—international terrorism, domestic terrorism, or a violent act of terrorism.

He was told that he was put on the list because he posed a threat of engaging in or conducting a violent act of terrorism and was operationally capable of doing so. Now, I appreciate the fact that the information on which that determination was based is classified and that we don't reveal that for obvious reasons. It's based on a totality of information at the time the decision is made.

But that is the threshold determination—only people who satisfy that are placed on the list, and so we know exactly what those criteria are.

Thank you, Mr. Chief Justice.

Just a few quick points.

First, Lawyer of Defendant referred to 42 U.S.C. 2239(a), which refers -- deals with the Commission adjudications.

But that provision doesn't say, if a person satisfies certain requirements, that person becomes a party or is a party.

What it says is, under certain circumstances, the Commission "shall admit any such person as a party to such proceedings." It's a directive to the Commission.

And it's indisputable here that the Commission didn't admit either Texas or ISP as a party -- I -- I'm -- I'm sorry, Fasken or Texas as a party.

Fasken's argument is simply that it should have been admitted.

Second, Lawyer of Defendant said that when spent nuclear fuel comes out of the reactor, it's too hot to handle or too hot to move.

And there is an initial period of at least five years when it has to be placed in a pool, and I'm told that it's rare, though not unprecedented, that the -- the pool is moved. But, after that time, even when the waste is stored at the site of a nuclear reactor, it's often moved into cask storage. It's in the same containers where it would be stored at ISP's facility.

Third, Lawyer of Defendant referred to the 1980 Federal Register notice.

There was a two-paragraph discussion, I think it's Heading Number 18, offsite versus onsite storage.

But it was all about policy.

Some commenters said onsite storage is better as a policy matter, some commenters said offsite storage is better as a policy matter.

No commenter at that time questioned the Commission's statutory authority to choose one or the other or both, and the Commission chose both.

The next thing I'd refer to is there was a reference to the facilities license that, Judge, I think you asked: How would the ISP facility be illegal if your view of the materials licensing provisions is correct? And the answer was: They would still need a facilities license.

That's not correct.

The facilities licensing provisions apply only to production or utilization facilities.

If you operate a nuclear reactor, you need both a facilities license to operate the reactor and a materials license to possess the -- the relevant stuff. But an ISP -- ISP's proposed facility is not either a production or utilization facility.

All it needs is the materials license.

And it's true that in determining whether to grant the materials license the Commission will examine the nature of the facility, is it safe, is it secure, but that doesn't convert it into a facilities license.

And, Judge, you laid out the sequence of events that led to the current understanding or the until-recent understanding that offsite storage is permissible.

And I'd add only one, and that's the D.C. Circuit's decision in Bullcreek, which was a little over 20 years ago.

And that was when the question whether the Policy Act had superseded the Atomic Energy Act's licensing provisions and precluded offsite storage, it was teed up then.

And the D.C. Circuit decided that, no, the Commission's offsite licensing authority remained intact. And we've been another 20 years since then.

Thank you.

Judge
Thank you, counsel.

The case is submitted.

If you don't want -- if you don't want someone to skip the hearing, just put these seven pieces of information on the notice to appear, and then they can't skip the hearing. There are other places in the scheme that allow for consideration of exactly the kind of fault analysis you're talking about.

So, for instance, you know, if you get to reopen your hearing, you may be ineligible for various forms of discretionary removal under 1229a(b)(7) if you're that person who gets, you know, notice -- oral notice of the date and time of the hearing and just doesn't show up.
As some of the Justices have alluded to, there may be some residual discretion embedded in the "may" in (b)(5) that gives the IJ some discretion. All we're talking about is the eligibility criteria to even be able to file a motion to reopen.

And at that stage, Congress didn't ask about notice generally, notice of the time of the hearing.
It asked about notice in accordance with these two provisions that prescribe a particular format for the information.

Judge: So this Court said in Meyer that the question of the cause of action and the question of the defense of sovereign immunity are analytically distinct. And that's the—

Defendant’s Lawyer: So then there's no implication on the basis of the cause of action.

Judge: No, there can be implication. I know that I'm drawing fine lines, but, logically, there is an implication that Congress intends to waive sovereign immunity if it creates a cause of action that applies only to sovereign defendants or that expressly names sovereign defendants.

Defendant’s Lawyer: So it has to be explicit with respect to—it can't be that they do so by naming all these other entities and adding in government. Is that your point? It has to either be stand-alone just the word "government" in the "person" definition, or it has to be written "government" in the actual 1618n?

Judge: Yes, because that is the only circumstance in which the statutory text that refers to "government" would have no effect if the government were able to assert sovereign immunity.

Defendant’s Lawyer: And where have we said that before?

Judge: So I read the Court's decision in Financial Oversight and Management Board to say that. I think that's the only way that you can reconcile cases like Kimel on the one hand and Employees on the other, is to say that Congress distinguishes in this way.

And if you look at statutes like RCRA and the MPRSA that we point to at pages 22 to 24 of our brief, Congress has done what we've said it could have done here. Congress has said it's authorizing suit against any person, comma, including the United States. We agree—

Defendant’s Lawyer: All right.

Judge: —that that gives rise to the implication.

Defendant’s Lawyer: Thank you.

Judge: Thank you, counsel. Plaintiff’s Lawyer.

Plaintiff’s Lawyer: Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court:

The Fair Credit Reporting Act imposes civil liability on any person that negligently or willfully fails to comply with FCRA's requirements. It expressly defines "person" to include any government agency. The term "person" is equivalent to its definition, and when FCRA's definition of "person" is plugged into FCRA's civil liability provisions, those provisions create causes of action against federal agencies that are clear and specific enough to waive sovereign immunity.

Congress was not required to state that "persons" include federal agencies a second time in the cause of action to make its intent clear. Congress knew what it was doing when it amended FCRA in 1996. When it did so, it consistently used the term "person" to describe both who would be subject to FCRA's substantive duties and who would be subject to FCRA's enforcement mechanisms.

Interpreting "person" to mean something different in the enforcement provisions would make FCRA's substantive duties completely unenforceable against governmental furnishers.

Moreover, Congress knew how to and did choose words to alter the scope of liability where it wanted to do so. Thus, where Section 1681n generally provides for damages against "any person," 1681n[a][1][B] creates a special remedy for certain violations by a natural person. That was an amendment in 1996 as well.

Congress also expressly limited the government's liability in FCRA's sister statute in the Consumer Credit Protection Act. It did not do so in FCRA, indicating that Congress intended no such limitation.

I welcome the Court's questions.

Judge: Counsel, there's much discussion about Employees, and I'd like you to address that, what the government argued, but I'd also like you to consider addressing whether or not what's necessary to—for the government to abrogate state sovereign immunity, whether that standard is the same as the standard for waiving its own sovereign immunity.

Plaintiff’s Lawyer: Sure. So, on Employees, I think the best sort of empirical evidence of its continuing force are the five courts of appeals that have addressed this very issue, the question of whether FCRA waives sovereign immunity. That—there's a 3-2 circuit split on that. The government has raised Employees in each one of those cases. Not one single court, not even the two that agree with the government's position, thought Employees was worth discussing. It's too out—I suggest that's because the analysis does not comport with how the Court reads statutes in the modern era.

Employees, as I read it, was telling Congress how to craft an amendment to make its intent clear. The modern—the sort of the current way the Court discerns congressional intent is to look at the provisions as a whole, the amending provisions, as well as the original provisions, and construe them together. And Employees is inconsistent with that.

The other thing to point—

Judge: I'm sorry. I—I—I don't understand that, really, looking at Employees, because it relies on the text of the provision and says that because the addition of public employees—employers was in a separate definitional provision, that wasn't good enough.

And the majority said—I read it as establishing a principle—well, we're going to draw the line here. If it's just in the definitional section, that's not good enough for a waiver.

Plaintiff’s Lawyer: So Employees started off by looking at the legislative history to discern what Congress wanted to do when it amended the FLSA in 1966.

Judge: The—the principle rested on the text. I mean, I acknowledge that then it went on to the legislative history. It might have had an even looser standard for waiver of sovereign immunity than we now apply. But, even under that looser standard, the Court said no, no waiver.

Plaintiff’s Lawyer: Well, the—the textual part of Employees—

Judge: And so a fortiori—

Plaintiff’s Lawyer: Yeah.

Judge: —you're in trouble if—if we take Employees seriously. At least I want you to respond to that.

Plaintiff’s Lawyer: Sure. I—I do think the text—the textual part of Employees, which indicated the Court would find it surprising, I think that's the exact quote, if Congress chose to amend the FLSA but did not amend it in this way. Now the Court says that Congress does not have to use magic words and it doesn't have to use a magic structure, I would say, doesn't have to state its intent in any particular way.

Judge: Thank you. Janina Pagley Arini, you are suing Dan, doing business as The Collector, for $3,500, the value of an Indian gold coin that you say he has kept from you. You have a counterclaim against her for $7,000 in lost wages dealing with this. Let's hear from you first. What's going on?

Plaintiff: Actually, uh, I had looked online about the cost of the coin to sell it, and I had checked with other parties, and just the weight alone on a 22-karat double-headed gold coin is $500 because I had another party check that.

Judge: All right. Let's say there was a regulation that said that only men can be admitted to one of the military academies, and after the statute of limitations has run, a woman applies, wants to be admitted to a military academy, and you would say it's too late for her to bring a facial challenge to that?

Defendant's Lawyer: We would say that if she applies to the military academy and is denied admission, that at that point there is an application of the regulation to her and that she can raise substantive challenges to the regulation in that context.

Judge: What's the difference between that and the situation of Corner Post, other than the fact that they are indirectly hurt rather than being directly hurt?

Defendant's Lawyer: I—I mean, I think the difference is that in that case, there is a subsequent final agency action that provides the focus and that is within the last six years, whereas, on their theory, there's no final agency action that they're pointing to.

Judge: In Justice Jackson's questions, I would have thought it starts running the day they open the business.

Defendant's Lawyer: So, Justice, I don't think—

Judge: Just compared to usual APA suits, which start the day the rule is adopted and you're an ongoing business.

Defendant's Lawyer: So I think that if they wanted to challenge suit before they opened business—the doors for business, what they'd say is, we have concrete plans to accept debit cards.

Judge: Okay. So they—yeah, I take that point.

Defendant's Lawyer: I mean, I think, like, I'm not saying it's impossible to figure it out.

Judge: Maybe a little bit before. Maybe a little bit before, but you'd have to make a showing there, I think, to get in the door in that context, right?

Defendant's Lawyer: Well, I—I think more problematically, it's not they who would have to make that showing. Ordinarily, in an APA case, they would come forward and say we have concrete plans to accept debit cards as of today, and so we can bring the challenge. That's easy. The problem here is that we would have to come in and say they formed concrete plans to accept debit cards sometime before they opened their doors, but how do we know when that was? Again, I'm not saying that's impossible—

Judge: Yeah.

Defendant's Lawyer: —but I'm saying—

Judge: I mean, that's a pretty in-the-weeds debate, but—and I don't think that arises—that would arise that often, but maybe I'm wrong about that. Let me ask a question about the Article III standing point that was raised, just to make sure we're on the same page on that.

My understanding is the day a rule is adopted and you're a regulated party, even if nothing has happened to you by the agency, you have standing to go in to sue. That happens all the time, right?

Defendant's Lawyer: That's my understanding too, yes.

Judge: Okay. And if you're not a regulated party but you're an affected party, which is a big swath at law, you also, if you can show you're an affected party in some way, have standing to sue in injury on the day the rule is promulgated?

Defendant's Lawyer: I agree with that too.

Judge: Okay.

Judge: What—what do you—how do those—your answers apply if it's a corporation that wasn't incorporated until seven years, you know, rather than six years? But you'd still say they have standing?

Defendant's Lawyer: Yes. We—we don't dispute that they have standing. We just think that their claim is untimely. I do, if—if I could, want to come to the final sentence—

Judge: Can I just follow up on the Chief's question? What if they were thinking about incorporating, but they haven't yet incorporated and they're still within the six-year period and part of whether they incorporate and go into business depends on the structure of the industry and whether this rule is going to help? Defendant's Lawyer: I think that's right. I mean, there—there's no case law I can point you to on this because no court in the country has applied their rule. I mean, I think adopting their rule would open the Court up to all sorts of really thorny questions, however far down in the weeds they might be. I think those questions just haven't been explored.

Judge: Those questions—I mean, those questions come up in other contexts. Where is the business really operating? Is it a phony challenge to an—I mean, I've seen that before. So, I mean, maybe.

Judge: Mr. Lawyer, would this rule have effects—Justice in his hypothetical started to ask you about a statute and then switched and was focused on rule.

So 2401 is the all-purpose statute of limitations. I'm just wondering, is your argument that we should interpret "accrue" this way because, in the administrative law context and because of the Hobbs Act and all these specialized statutes, a statute of repose-style accrual is—makes more sense? Would there be spillover effects in, say, you know, hey, I'm sure Congress would prefer all challenges to a statute to be adjudicated right away. Would there be spillover effects?

Defendant's Lawyer: I don't think there would be spillover effects. We also have an argument about the final sentence of Section 702 that we haven't discussed. But the—the last reason that I don't think it would spill over to statutes is the challenges to statutes are—are necessarily—may I finish the sentence?

Judge: Sure.

Defendant's Lawyer: —are necessarily constitutional. And so the Court has allowed claims against the validity of statutes outside of the context of a final agency action requirement as in the APA.

Judge: Thank you.

I'm intrigued by your Mt. Healthy, Hunter versus Underwood argument.

And, here, that's the only thing I need to rely on to get you to the place that they wouldn't have announced -- wouldn't have enacted this --

Well --

Plaintiff
Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court: The Chinese government's control of TikTok poses a grave threat to national security.

No one disputes that the PRC seeks to undermine U.S. interests by amassing vast quantities of sensitive data about Americans and by engaging in covert influence operations, and no one disputes that the PRC pursues those goals by compelling companies like ByteDance to secretly turn over data and carry out PRC directives.

Those realities mean that the Chinese government could weaponize TikTok at any time to harm the United States.

TikTok collects unprecedented amounts of personal data.

And, as Justice Sotomayor noted, it's not just about the 170 million American users but also about their non-user contacts, who might not even be engaging with the platform.

That data would be incredibly valuable to the PRC.

For years, the Chinese government has sought to build detailed profiles about Americans, where we live and work, who our friends and coworkers are, what our interests are, and what our vices are.

TikTok's immense data set would give the PRC a powerful tool for harassment, recruitment, and espionage.

On top of that, the Chinese government's control over TikTok gives it a potent weapon for covert influence operations.

And my friends are wrong to suggest that Congress was seeking to suppress specific types of content or specific types of viewpoints.

Instead, the national security harm arises from the very fact of a foreign adversary's capacity to secretly manipulate the platform to advance its geopolitical goals in whatever form that kind of covert operation might take.

The Act addresses the threat of foreign adversary control with laser-like focus. It requires only divestiture of TikTok to prevent Chinese government control, and that divestiture remedy follows a long tradition of barring foreign control of U.S. communications channels and other critical infrastructure.

So, no matter what level of First Amendment scrutiny applies, this Act is valid because it's narrowly tailored to address compelling national security threats.

Now my friend, Mr. Fisher, just emphasized and I acknowledge that millions of Americans enjoy expressing themselves on this platform.

But the important thing to recognize is that the Act leaves all of that speech unrestricted once TikTok is freed from foreign adversary control.

The First Amendment does not bar Congress from taking that critical and targeted step to protect our nation's security. I welcome the Court's questions.

Judge
Is there any difference between content manipulation by a non-U.S. company as opposed to a U.S. company? I didn't hear Mr. Fisher make a distinction between the two.

Plaintiff: Yes.

Judge: Okay. Now, this is—you were renting out what?

Plaintiff: I was renting out an apartment, so like a whole floor in a house, rather.

Judge: All right. And then who lives—how many floors are there to the house?

Plaintiff: Two.

Judge: So the son lives on the top?

Defendant: Yes.

Judge: Do you live there too?

Defendant: No.

Judge: Okay. The son lives on the top, and the bottom was being rented out to you?

Plaintiff: Yes, ma’am.

Judge: Okay. So you get a notice on May 25th saying what?

Plaintiff: Saying that I was going against the lease by having an overnight guest, which was not true.

Judge: And what did you do with that notice?

Plaintiff: I let her know that I didn’t have any overnight guests.

Judge: And then?

Plaintiff: On top of that, it was my roommate who had the guest.

Judge: Okay. So it was your roommate. That’s—what, do you think that what your roommate does in the apartment doesn’t matter?

Plaintiff: But what I don’t understand is—you moved out then immediately?

Plaintiff: No. So that was my first warning, the 25th. And then, three—two days later, they told me to move out. I was given three days to move out.

Judge: That was my first—that was your first warning? Was there another event?

Plaintiff: No.

Judge: Okay. So just—they didn’t like the way you communicated, I guess?

Plaintiff: Or something, and, like, my face—something. I don’t know what was going on.

Judge: So they gave you a three-day notice, and then you—you followed it?

Plaintiff: Yeah.

Judge: So you lived out the one month that you paid, and this is all about the security deposit now?

Judge: What was the problem? Why did he get a three-day notice in his first month?

Defendant: He was sharing the apartment with someone else.

Judge: Okay.

Defendant: And his roommate said that he tried to move someone into the apartment.

Judge: Wait, what? The roommate told you that he—

Defendant: —that the plaintiff tried to move someone else in, yeah. And he was not comfortable with it.

Judge: Okay.

Defendant: So I said, before it escalates, I will ask him to leave.

Judge: So you asked him to leave. And, much to your surprise, he left?

Defendant: Yeah.

Judge: No?

Defendant: I’m not surprised. I’m surprised because most times that you file a three-day notice, they go—they put it as a doily under—at least those are the cases I see—under their beard.

Judge: All right. So he leaves, and then the other guy stays?

Defendant: Yes.

Defendant: Yes.

Defendant: Yes.

Defendant: Because a lot of times, people say, “Okay, I’m having an overnight guest.” And the guest stays until a month, two months. And then they’re not leaving because they have squatters’ rights. And I didn’t want that to happen.

Judge: You kept $350 of his security deposit because he was supposed to return the key?

Defendant: Yes. I gave him five business days to return the key. He didn’t return the key, so I had to get a locksmith to change the lock.

Defendant: Yes, he got an email from my son, who was my property manager.

Defendant: Let me see if I have that copy.

Plaintiff: No.

Judge: Well, why did you take the key with you to begin with?

Plaintiff: I thought I was going to give it to somebody, and I—

Plaintiff: I didn’t think to do that.

Judge: Okay. Well, then she had to change the locks.
